[SOURCE: 3D Cross-Linked Nanoweb Architecture of Binder-Free TiO2 Electrodes for Lithium Ion Batteries.pdf]

3D Cross-Linked Nanoweb Architecture of Binder-Free TiO2
Electrodes for Lithium Ion Batteries
Sangkyu Lee,†,‡ Jaehwan Ha,†,‡ Junghyun Choi,§ Taeseup Song,† Jung Woo Lee,§ and Ungyu Paik*,§
†Division of Materials Science Engineering and §WCU Department of Energy Engineering, Hanyang University, Seoul 133-791,
Republic of Korea
*
S Supporting Information
ABSTRACT: The nanoweb structure of TiO2 anode, cross-linked
between electrospun nanoﬁbers, is directly fabricated on the current
collector by utilizing the ﬂuidity of low glass transition temperature
polymer, poly(vinyl acetate), at room temperature. This character-
istic enables us to fabricate the nanoweb structure by direct
electrospinning on the current collector, followed by uniaxial
pressing. This proposed structure facilitates electron transport
through the direct conducting pathways between TiO2 active
materials and current collector as well as provides strong adhesion
strength to the current collector without polymeric binders. Consequently, we could achieve stable cycle performance up to 100
cycles and the excellent rate capability of ∼60% at high rate charge/discharge condition of 10 C.
KEYWORDS: cross-linked, nanoweb, binder-free, electrospinning, titanium oxide (TiO2), lithium ion battery
1. INTRODUCTION
Titanium oxide (TiO2) is an attractive material for lithium ion
battery anodes with high safety and stability because Li ions can
be inserted into TiO2 matrix at higher voltage (at least 1.5 V vs.
Li/Li+) than graphite.1,2 However, its poor electronic
conductivity and lithium ion diﬀusivity lead to the limited
electrochemical performance of TiO2-based lithium ion
batteries. In general, to resolve such intrinsic drawbacks of
electrode materials, researchers have proposed the use of
nanomaterials and their structural design and the application of
additives. As regards nanomaterials, they provide short diﬀusion
length for lithium ions as well as high contact area of
electrolyte.3−5 Among various types of the nanostructures, one-
dimensional (1D) structures such as nanorods, nanowires,
nanotubes, and nanoﬁbers are considered as the promising
nanostructures for high performance electrodes.6−8 This is in
part due to their nature of a long 1D structure with a nanoscale
diameter, enabling faster electron transport without retardation
at the interfaces while maintaining the advantages of nanoma-
terials with shorter diﬀusion length of Li ion and higher contact
area facing with the electrolyte solution. The electrodes
prepared with such 1D nanostructured materials are also
fabricated by the same procedure of fabricating conventional
electrode for lithium ion batteries that is a serial process of
mixing of active materials with conducting additive and
polymeric binder, followed by casting on the current collector.
However, agglomeration phenomenon can occur during the
mixing process, leading to the segregated components and
consequently the formation of interrupted, noncontinuous
pathways for Li ions and electrons. For example, the
segregation of binders can cause severe problems like blocking
the conducting pathways because the polymeric binders are
electrically insulating.9,10 Further, the electrochemical inactivity
of conducting additive and polymeric binder decreases the
speciﬁc mass capacity of the whole energy storage system.
To overcome the aforementioned issues, an alternative
approach to construct a nanostructure of wholly active
materials that is directly connected to the current collector
without conducting additives and polymeric binders was
recently suggested.11−13 Recently, we also proposed sol−gel
nanoglues consisting of active material to adhere the electro-
spun active materials on the current collector.14 The nanoﬁber
electrode strengthened with sol−gel nanoglues exhibited
excellent cycle performance and Coulombic eﬃciency, which
is attributed to the intimate contact between individual
nanoﬁbers as well as nanoﬁbers and current collector. Despite
achieving excellent electrochemical performance, there are
some disadvantages of complexity of two step fabrication
processing and requirement of large amounts of sol−gel
nanoglues.
Here, we demonstrate a facile single annealing step
preparation of binder-free TiO2 nanoﬁber-based electrode
with a unique structure of cross-linked nanostructure of TiO2
nanoﬁbers for use in lithium ion battery anodes. A mixture of
TiO2 precursor and a low glass transition temperature polymer
is utilized to prepare TiO2 nanoﬁbers. TiO2 nanoﬁbers are
directly electrospun on the current collector, followed by
uniaxial pressing at room temperature. Low glass transition
temperature polymer of poly(vinyl acetate) (PVAc) has a
certain level of freedom of movements near the room
Received:
September 19, 2013
Accepted:
November 11, 2013
Published: November 11, 2013
Letter
www.acsami.org

dx.doi.org/10.1021/am404082h | ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 2013, 5, 11525−11529

temperature, enabling the transport of TiO2 precursor along
with the polymer during the uniaxial pressing, and consequently
leading to the cross-linked nanostructure of TiO2 nanoﬁbers.
This unique structure increases adhesion strength of nanoﬁbers
on the current collector without the use of polymeric binders
and improves electrical contact properties between individual
nanoﬁbers as well as nanoﬁbers and current collector. We refer
to this cross-linked unique structure as “nanoweb”.
2. RESULTS AND DISCUSSION
3D Cross-Linked Nanoweb Architecture of TiO2
Electrode. Figure 1 illustrates the advantages of our unique
nanoweb structure. In the case of general direct electrospinning
(Figure 1a), the underlying layers of nanoﬁbers are surmounted
by the upper layers of nanoﬁbers, producing physical and weak
connection between two layers.14 Two interfacial resistances
including the resistance between nanoﬁbers (Ri), and nano-
ﬁber/current collector (Rc) co-exist in the electrodes along with
the intrinsic resistance of TiO2. Thus, the inﬂuence of the
interfacial resistance between the nanoﬁbers (Ri) accumulates
with increasing the thickness of nanoﬁber layers. PVAc, which
is employed as a shape forming polymer for TiO2 nanoﬁbers,
has a low glass transition temperature of 28−30 oC,15 which
provides low level of ﬂuidity to the nanoﬁbers at room
temperature. TiO2 precursor can be transported along with the
movement of PVAc during the uniaxial pressing, leading to the
formation of junction between the underlying and upper layers
of nanoﬁbers (Figure 1b and Figure S1 in the Supporting
Information). On the other hand, poly(methyl methacrylate)
with high glass transition temperature of 105 °C does not lead
to the formation of cross-linked structure (Figure S2 in the
Supporting Information.) The junction guarantees intimate
contact, therefore the interfacial resistance between the
nanoﬁbers can be ignored. To verify this concept, we measured
the current−voltage (I−V) characteristics for both structures
and the results are shown in Figure 1c. Higher current ﬂows
through the TiO2 nanoweb structure, which is evidently
indicative that the nanoweb structure is favorable for the
electron transport compared to the nanoﬁber structure. On the
basis of this concept, it can be expected that electrons are
eﬀectively transferred through the TiO2 nanoweb anode
compared to the TiO2 nanoﬁber anode, leading to the high
performance lithium ion batteries.
Figure 2 shows the scanning electron microscopy (SEM)
images of TiO2 nanoﬁbers and nanoweb. After annealing for
the removal of polymeric phase, TiO2 nanoﬁbers exist
separately and their diameter is ∼100 nm (Figure 2a). In the
case of TiO2 nanoweb (Figure 2b), cross-linking at the
nanoﬁber junctions can be clearly observed. Both TiO2
nanostructures exhibit anatase phase (see Figure S3 in the
Supporting Information). However, a faint trace of rutile phase
is also observed at 27.6° in both TiO2 nanostructures
Galvanostatic charge and discharge cycle tests were
performed using TiO2 nanoﬁbers and nanoweb electrodes in
the voltage range of 1−3 V vs. Li/Li+. Figure 3a compares the
ﬁrst charge/discharge curves for TiO2 nanoﬁbers and nanoweb
electrodes at the current rate of 0.2 C (1 C = 200 mA g−1).
TiO2 nanoﬁbers electrode yields the ﬁrst charge capacity of 195
mAhg−1 and the discharge capacity of 319 mAhg−1. On the
other hand, TiO2 nanoweb electrode delivers the ﬁrst charge
capacity of 207 mAhg−1 and the discharge capacity of 255
mAhg−1. The ﬁrst Coulombic eﬃciencies of TiO2 nanoﬁbers
and nanoweb electrodes are 61.1 and 81.3%, respectively. There
are two clear plateaus at ∼1.91 and 1.76 V in the charge and
discharge curves, respectively, which corresponds to the phase
transition between anatase TiO2 and lithiated TiO2.16 This
Figure 1. Schematic illustration of electron conduction mechanism in (a) TiO2 nanoﬁbers and (b) TiO2 nanoweb anodes, and (c) the current−
voltage (I−V) characteristics of both nanostructures.
Figure 2. SEM images of (a) TiO2 nanoﬁbers and (b) TiO2 nanoweb.
ACS Applied Materials & Interfaces
Letter
dx.doi.org/10.1021/am404082h | ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 2013, 5, 11525−11529
11526

result clearly indicates the formation of electrochemically stable
TiO2 phases in both TiO2 nanoﬁbers and nanoweb. The
variations of the charge capacity of TiO2 electrodes are plotted
as a function of cycle number in Figure 3b. The charge capacity
of TiO2 nanoﬁbers electrode severely decreases during the
initial 5 cycles and then slightly decreases. After 100 cycles, the
electrode retains the charge capacity of 158 mAhg−1, which
corresponds to 81.4% of initial charge capacity. On the
contrary, TiO2 nanoweb electrode delivers the charge capacity
of 188 mAhg−1 after 100 cycles, indicating that 90.5% of initial
charge capacity retains. The Coulombic eﬃciencies of both
electrodes as a function of cycle are also compared as shown in
Figure 3c. TiO2 nanoweb electrode approaches the Coulombic
eﬃciency of >99%, whereas the eﬃciency of TiO2 nanoﬁbers
shows severe ﬂuctuation. The instability in the Coulombic
eﬃciency for TiO2 nanoﬁbers originates from the weak
adhesion of nanoﬁbers on current collector.14
Such structural beneﬁts of the nanoweb structure can be
observed in the rate capability of TiO2 nanoweb electrode. The
rate capability was tested with increasing current density from
0.2 to 10 C. The rate capability was calculated by dividing the
capacity at a given current density by the capacity at the current
rate of 0.2 C and the results are shown in Figure 4. TiO2
nanoﬁbers electrode exhibits the capacity retention of ∼40% at
the current rate of 10 C, which is higher than that of reported
values for conventional TiO2 electrodes fabricated with
conducting additives and polymeric binders.6,17−21 It can be
explained by the fact that the presence of these components can
aﬀect the electrochemical reactions between Li ions and
electrode materials. This result clearly shows that the formation
of direct conducting pathways between electrode and current
collector can be an eﬀective approach to improve the
electrochemical performance of electrode. In addition, we
verify that rate capability can be further improved by applying
the nanoweb structure. The rate capability of TiO2 nanoweb
electrode is ∼60% at the current density of 10 C, which exceeds
that of the TiO2 nanoﬁbers electrode. It indicates that the
formation of nanoweb structure is much more favorable to
fabricate high rate lithium ion batteries than as-received
nanoﬁber structure.
To interpret the improved electrochemical performance of
TiO2 nanoweb electrode, we utilized electrochemical impe-
dance spectroscopy. The results are plotted as a Nyquist plot
(Figure 5). The Nyquist plot consists of a semicircle at high-to-
medium frequencies followed by a straight line in the low
frequency region. In the semicircle, the x-intercept corresponds
to the Ohmic resistance of bulk cell including electrolyte,
separator, and electrode. The Ohmic resistance of TiO2
nanoﬁbers and nanoweb are 3.3 and 2.5 Ω, respectively. This
diﬀerence can originate from the unique structure of TiO2
nanoweb since other components of both the TiO2 electrodes
are identical. The extrapolated right most end of the semicircle,
indicating the resistance at the medium frequency (polarization
resistance), relates to the electrolyte transport resistance in the
solid electrolyte interface and the charge transfer resistance of
Li+ to the TiO2 electrode. TiO2 nanoweb electrode clearly
shows low polarization resistance (< 90 Ω) than that of TiO2
nanoﬁbers electrode. This decreased polarization resistance,
which is observed in the nanoweb electrode, is attributed to the
merged intersects that provides well connected conducting
Figure 3. Electrochemical properties of TiO2 nanoﬁbers and nanoweb
electrodes. (a) First charge−discharge voltage proﬁles at 0.2 C, (b)
cycling performance, and (c) Coulombic eﬃciency.
Figure 4. Rate capabilities of TiO2 nanoﬁbers and nanoweb electrodes.
Figure 5. Nyquist plots of TiO2 nanoﬁbers and nanoweb electrodes.

Letter
dx.doi.org/10.1021/am404082h | ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 2013, 5, 11525−11529
11527

pathways. Therefore, this Nyquist plot clearly explains the
mechanism for the improved electrochemical performance of
TiO2 nanoweb electrode.
Delamination Issue of Directly Electrospun Nano-
ﬁbers. Additionally, the proposed methodology to fabricate
nanoweb structure solves a chronic delamination issue of the
direct electrospinning process. Figure S4 in the Supporting
Information describes the delamination of TiO2 nanoﬁbers,
which were directly electrospun on the current collector and
then annealed. At low loading level, the electrospun TiO2
nanoﬁbers remain adhered on the current collector after
annealing. However, partial delamination occurs in the TiO2
nanoﬁbers with higher loading level of 0.0557 mg/cm2. Further
increase in the loading level leads to complete delamination,
leaving a trace of nanoﬁbers. Thus, we fabricated TiO2
nanoﬁbers electrode below this limitation. A serial process of
direct electrospinning, uniaxial pressing, and annealing
produces TiO2 nanoweb structure on the current collector.
Contrary to the TiO2 nanoﬁbers, TiO2 nanoweb strongly
adheres on the current collector, enabling higher loading of
electrode materials without delamination. Therefore, the
proposed methodology is eﬀective to increase the areal capacity
of nanostructured material-based electrode. The areal capacity
of TiO2 nanoweb electrode can exceed that of our recent TiO2
nanotube electrode directly grown on the current collector.7
Table S1 in the Supporting Information compares the areal
density of TiO2 nanoﬁbers and TiO2 nanoweb electrodes.
Many eﬀorts have been made to prevent the delamination of
nanoﬁbers during annealing process. Hot pressing15,22,23 and
solvent treatment24 are proposed for this reason. However, the
original nanostructure can be severely deformed during these
processes. The deformation of nanostructure can decrease
surface area, leading to the deterioration of electrochemical
performance of nanostructure-based electrode. On the contrary,
uniaxial pressing method proposed here is conducted at room
temperature, which avoids the massive deformation of TiO2
nanoﬁbers. This methodology utilizes the ﬂuidity of a polymer
with low glass transition temperature; therefore, it can be
applied to a variety of nanomaterials without the severe
deformation.
3. CONCLUSIONS
In summary, we report a methodology to fabricate high
performance electrodes for electrochemical energy storage
devices without conducting additives and polymeric binders.
This approach utilizes electrospun nanoﬁbers which were
directly electrospun on the current collector and adapts a
uniaxial pressing to provide superior mechanical and electrical
contact properties between nanoﬁbers and current collector as
well as between nanoﬁbers. The resulting nanostructure is
referred to as “nanoweb”. This methodology is applied to
fabricate TiO2 nanoﬁber electrodes for Li ion batteries. The
resulting TiO2 nanoweb anodes exhibit stable cycling perform-
ance of 90.5% up to 100 cycles with high initial Coulombic
eﬃciency of 81.3% and show the excellent capacity retention of
∼60% at 10 C. The direct contact of TiO2 nanoﬁbers on the
current collector and the cross-linking of the stacked nanoﬁbers
at the intersecting points provide a highly eﬀective electron
conducting pathways in the entire electrode system, which
results in the stable cycling performance and the formidable
high rate capacity retention. From the point of the improve-
ment, this work provides an alternative methodology of
fabricating high performance lithium ion batteries. Moreover,
we expect that it can be adapted to prepare electrodes of other
energy storage devices as well as other Li ion anodes showing
poor rate capability.

mixture of TiO2 precursor and PMMA solution, calculated areal

A Nano-Micro Hybrid Structure Composed 
of Fe7S8 Nanoparticles Embedded in Nitrogen-Doped 
Porous Carbon Framework for High-Performance  
Lithium/Sodium-Ion Batteries
Zhiming Liu, Fang Hu, Juan Xiang, Chuang Yue, Dongsoo Lee, and Taeseup Song*
Z. M. Liu, Dr. F. Hu, J. Xiang, Dr. C. Yue, D. Lee, Prof. T. Song
Department of Energy Engineering
Hanyang University
222 Wangsimni-ro, Seongdong-gu, Seoul 04763, Korea
E-mail: tssong@hanyang.ac.kr
The ORCID identification number(s) for the author(s) of this article 
can be found under https://doi.org/10.1002/ppsc.201800163.
DOI: 10.1002/ppsc.201800163
conductivity, sluggish kinetics, and large 
volume changes during the charging and 
discharging processes, which hinders 
their practical application.[15–17]
Combining with carbonaceous mate-
rials is a feasible approach to alleviate 
these issues. Carbon not only acts as a 
buffer to mitigate pulverization, but also 
works as a conductive matrix during 
cycling.[16,18] Constructing active materials 
into nanostructures has been considered 
another effective strategy.[19,20] Generally, 
materials with nanostructures can alleviate 
mechanical stress without the initiation 
of fractures and enhance the reaction 
kinetics by reducing the transport length 
for electrons and ions during cycling, 
thereby improving the electrochemical 
performance. However, nanostructured 
materials usually suffer from a low-tap 
density because of the large interparticle 
space and high surface area.[21,22] Besides, the large contact 
area with electrolyte leads to excess formation of solid electro-
lyte interface (SEI) layer.[21,23] These problems greatly compro-
mise the merits of nanostructured electrode materials over bulk 
materials. Engineering a nano-micro hierarchical structure has 
been considered a promising strategy.[24–27] These unique archi-
tectures show high cycling stability and superior rate capability 
benefiting from the nanoscale structure, while remaining the 
advantages of packing density and SEI formation provided by 
the microscale dimension.
Herein, we have successfully fabricated a unique nano-
micro hierarchical structure organized by Fe7S8 nanoparticles 
embedded in nitrogen-doped carbon porous spheres through 
a facile hydrothermal method coupled with a carbonization 
process (Fe7S8@NC-PS). The porous carbon framework was 
constructed by interconnected 2D nanowalls. This porous 
structure offered sufficient void space for volume changes 
during cycling without causing serious pulverization. Moreover, 
2D nanowalls provided a large contact area with electrolyte and 
short diffusion path for electrons and ions, which significantly 
facilitated the reaction kinetics.[28–30] In addition, the nitrogen-
doped carbon framework contributed a high conductivity and 
structural stability upon charge and discharge.[31–33] Generally, 
the well-bonded nitrogen atom could drastically change the 
Iron sulfides are attractive anode materials for lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) 
and sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) due to their high theoretical capacities, 
low cost, and eco-friendliness. However, their real application is greatly 
hindered by the rapid capacity fading caused by the large volume changes 
and sluggish kinetics of iron sulfides during the charge and discharge 
processes. Combining with carbonaceous materials and tuning the structure 
at nanoscale are essential to address this issue. Here, a facile hydrothermal 
method coupled with a carbonization process is developed to synthesize 
a nano-micro hybrid porous structure, which is composed of Fe7S8 nano­
particles embedded in nitrogen-doped carbon framework (Fe7S8@NC-PS). 
This hierarchical sphere is constructed by interconnected 2D nanowalls. The 
as-prepared Fe7S8@NC-PS electrodes reveal excellent rate capability and 
cycling stability in LIBs and SIBs. The remarkable electrochemical properties 
are attributed to the porous nano-micro hybrid architecture and the high 
conductivity and structural stability of the nitrogen-doped carbon framework.
Structure-Property Relationships
High-performance rechargeable batteries are increasingly 
demanded for applications in portable electronic devices, elec-
tric vehicles (EV), and stationary storage systems. Lithium-ion 
batteries (LIBs) have been successfully commercialized in port-
able electronics and EV owing to the high energy density.[1–3] 
As a promising alternative to LIBs, sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) 
have attracted increasing attention because of the natural abun-
dance and environmental benignity of sodium resources.[4–6] 
Finding appropriate electrode materials with high electro-
chemical performance and low cost is still a main challenge 
currently. Among all the electrode materials, iron sulfides 
have been considered as promising anode materials for LIBs 
and SIBs due to their high theoretical capacities, abundant 
resources, and eco-friendly.[7–14] However, iron sulfides show 
poor electrochemical performance owing to their intrinsic low 
Part. Part. Syst. Charact. 2018, 35, 1800163

© 2018 WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH & Co. KGaA, Weinheim
1800163  (2 of 7)
www.advancedsciencenews.com
www.particle-journal.com
electronic performance, provide more active sites, and enhance 
the interaction between the carbon structure and Li/Na ions, 
thus it is expected to improve the kinetics of Li/Na diffusion 
and transfer.[34] Besides, N-doping is capable of increasing 
the specific capacity as a result of more favorable binding of 
N-doped sites with Li/Na ions.[34,35] Benefiting from these struc-
tural and composition merits, Fe7S8@NC-PS revealed a high 
specific capacity, excellent rate capability, and long cycling sta-
bility in LIBs and SIBs.
Figure  S1a (Supporting Information) shows the X-ray 
diffraction (XRD) patterns of Fe7S8@NC-PS precursor without 
carbonization. All peaks were mainly assigned to the FeS2 
phase (JCPDS No. 42-1340). After annealing at 500 °C in Ar, 
FeS2 totally transformed into Fe7S8 phase without impuri-
ties, which was confirmed by XRD measurement (Figure S1b, 
Supporting Information, JCPDS No. 24-0220). An X-ray 
photoelectron spectra (XPS) technique was applied to investi-
gate the composition and valence states of Fe7S8@NC-PS. The 
survey spectrum indicated the presence of Fe, S, C, O, and 
N elements (Figure S2, Supporting Information). The decon-
volution analysis then focused on the Fe 2p, S 2p, and C 1s 
regions. Figure 1a shows the high-resolution Fe 2p XPS spec-
trum. The peak located at 707.3 eV was attributed to Fe0; the 
peaks centered at 710.8 and 724.3 eV indicated to Fe2+ valence 
state; whereas the peaks at 713.6 and 726.6 eV were assigned 
to Fe3+.[16,36,37] In the high-resolution S 2p XPS spectrum 
(Figure 1b), peaks at 161.5, 162.8, 163.5, 164.1, and 165.3 eV 
corresponded to S2− and Sn2−. The other peak at 168.6 eV was 
assigned to the oxidized sulfur species (e.g., SOx).[16,36] The 
high-resolution C 1s spectrum indicated the presence of CC, 
CC, CS, CN, and CO bonds in the carbon framework, 
which originated from the carbon precursor polyvinylpyrro-
lidone K30 (PVP-K30) and sulfur source thioacetamide (TAA) 
(Figure 1c).[38–40]
Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) 
images showed that Fe7S8@NC-PS precursor exhibited a 
porous hierarchical structure with an average diameter of 
6.8 µm, which was constructed by interconnected 2D nanowalls 
(Figure 1d and Figure S3, Supporting Information). The thick-
ness of the nanowall was around 47 nm (Figure 1e). After car-
bonization, the porous spherical structure was well maintained 
(Figure 1f). However, the diameter contracted to ≈5.6 µm with 
a reduced thickness around 30  nm (Figure  1g). Meanwhile, 
numerous Fe7S8 nanoparticles appeared on the surfaces of 
nanowalls, as indicated in the purple circles. The void space 
correspondingly increased owing to the shrinkage of nanow-
alls, which offered sufficient space to accommodate the large 
volume changes of Fe7S8 during cycling.
Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image con-
firmed that the whole structure of Fe7S8@NC-PS was porous 
(Figure 2a). A high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image showed 
that the edge of carbon nanowalls was amorphous phase 
(Figure  2b). Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) ele-
mental mapping results (Figure 2c) presented the distribution 
of Fe, S, and C. The color contrast in Fe and S distribution 
further revealed a porous structure. The nitrogen adsorption–
desorption isotherm exhibited that S-Fe7S8 had a Brunauer–
Emmett–Teller (BET) surface area of 27.31 m2 g−1 and total pore 
volume of 0.028 cm3 g−1 (Figure S4, Supporting Information). 
Figure  S5 (Supporting Information) shows that the pore size 
distribution centered around 2 nm with an average pore diameter 
of 3.264 nm. The large surface area provided Fe7S8@NC-PS suf-
ficient contact area with electrolyte for fast reaction kinetics. 
Raman spectrum in Figure  S6 (Supporting Information) 
further confirmed the existence of carbon. Thermogravimetric 
analysis (TGA) was carried out to determine the carbon ratio 
of Fe7S8@NC-PS (Figure  2d). The initial weight loss between 
300 and 450  °C was attributed to the combustion of carbon 
Part. Part. Syst. Charact. 2018, 35, 1800163

d,e) Fe7S8@NC-PS precursor without carbonization and f,g) Fe7S8@NC-PS.

www.advancedsciencenews.com
www.particle-journal.com
in air. The second weight loss between 500 and 610  °C was 
ascribed to the oxidation of Fe7S8 to Fe2O3.[41,42] By calculation, 
the carbon content in Fe7S8@NC-PS was 32%.
To well observe the internal morphology, Fe7S8@NC-PS was 
grinded into small fragments. Figure  2e shows that the frag-
ment was composed of Fe7S8 nanoparticles with diameters 
ranging from 30 to 80 nm (marked by red circles), which were 
embedded in the carbon matrix. The cross sections of carbon 
nanowalls in blue circles demonstrated that the nanowall net-
works penetrated the whole structure. Figure  2f exhibits that 
the cross section of the connection part for carbon nanowall 
networks was still amorphous. HRTEM image displayed a set 
of parallel fringes with a d-spacing of 0.264 nm, corresponding 
to the (203) plane of the hexagonal Fe7S8 phase (Figure  2g). 
The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern and the 
four diffraction rings corresponded to different lattice planes of 
Fe7S8 (Figure  2h). The elemental mapping images and corre-
sponding EDX results of Fe7S8@NC-PS fragment further con-
firmed the structure and composition of S-Fe7S8 (Figure 2i and 
Figure S7, Supporting Information). All the results verify that 
an elaborate Fe7S8@NC-PS hierarchical structure has been suc-
cessfully prepared.
Lithium storage properties of Fe7S8@NC-PS were evaluated 
in coin-type half-cells. Figure 3a shows the cyclic voltammetry 
(CV) curves for the first five cycles at a scan rate of 0.1 mV s−1 
between 0.02 and 2.5  V (vs Li/Li+). During the first cathodic 
scan, a large peak appeared near 1.05 V, corresponding to Li+ 
intercalation at high voltages to form LixFeS2 (x < 2).[20,37,43] The 
other reduction peak around 0.68 V was assigned to the subse-
quent conversion reaction to form Fe0 + Na2S and the formation 
of a SEI layer. In the anodic scan, one strong peak centered at 
1.79 V corresponded to the desodiation reactions of Fe0 + Na2S 
Part. Part. Syst. Charact. 2018, 35, 1800163
Figure 2.  a) TEM image of Fe7S8@NC-PS. b) HRTEM image of the edge of Fe7S8@NC-PS. c) Elemental mapping images of Fe7S8@NC-PS. d) TGA 
analysis of Fe7S8@NC-PS. e) TEM image of the fragment of Fe7S8@NC-PS. f,g) HR-TEM images, h) SAED pattern, and i) elemental mapping images 
of the fragment of Fe7S8@NC-PS.

1800163  (4 of 7)
www.advancedsciencenews.com
www.particle-journal.com
to form Na2−xFeS2 (0 < x < 2). The irreversible formation of SEI 
layer was supposed to be the reason for the difference between 
the first scan and the subsequent scans for CV curves.[44,45] 
In the following cycles, the redox peaks were consistent with 
the reversible reactions between Na2−xFeS2 and Fe0 + Na2S.[20] 
Figure  S8 (Supporting Information) shows the discharge and 
charge voltage profiles of Fe7S8@NC-PS at a current density 
of 0.5 A g−1 with a voltage window of 0.02–2.5  V (vs Li/Li+), 
which were well consistent with CV curves. The electrode gave 
rise to an initial discharge capacity of 1346 mAh g−1 with Cou-
lombic efficiencies (CE) around 60%. The irreversible capacity 
loss in the first cycle was mainly attributed to the trapped Li+ 
ions, inevitable formation of SEI layer, and electrolyte decompo-
sition.[45,46] After the first cycle, the specific capacities gradually 
stabilized around 930 mAh g−1.
The rate performance of Fe7S8@NC-PS was evaluated at 
various current densities from 0.5 to 20 A g−1 (Figure  3b,c). 
The average specific capacities were 920, 840, 790, 713, 600, 
and 440 mAh g−1 at 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10, and 20 A g−1, respectively. 
Remarkably, a higher capacity than that of graphite was still 
maintained even at a high current rate of 20 A g−1. When the 
current density went back to 0.5 A g−1, Fe7S8@NC-PS elec-
trode could quickly return to its original value, which was 
then kept very stable for extended cycles. The excellent rate 
capacities and stability benefited from the superior kinetics 
and structural stability of Fe7S8@NC-PS. The electrodes were 
further evaluated for long-term cycling stability using two 
different current densities. Figure  3d shows the cycling per-
formance and corresponding CEs at 0.5 A g−1 for 100 cycles. 
As can be seen, Fe7S8@NC-PS delivered a slightly increasing 
capacity, which was supposed to be the activation of the elec-
trode materials.[47] The reversible formation/dissolution of 
organic polymeric gel-like layers by electrolyte decomposi-
tion was supposed to be another reason for delivering an 
extra capacity through a pseudo-capacitive behavior.[48,49] 
After 100 cycles, a high specific capacity of 1130 mAh g−1 was

of Fe7S8@NC-PS at the current densities of d) 0.5 A g−1 and e) 2 and 5 A g−1, respectively, with the corresponding Coulombic efficiencies.

1800163  (5 of 7)
www.advancedsciencenews.com
www.particle-journal.com
retained. Meanwhile, the CE quickly increased to around 100% 
in the first ten cycles. In addition, Fe7S8@NC-PS demonstrated 
a long cycling stability when cycled at high current densities 
of 2 and 5 A g−1 (Figure 3e), and the specific capacities stabi-
lized around 630 and 470 mAh g−1 after 200 and 450 cycles, 
respectively. More impressively, it delivered a reversible capacity 
of 438 mAh g−1 after 700 cycles when cycled at a high current 
density of 10 A g−1 (Figure S9, Supporting Information). The 
lithium storage properties of Fe7S8@NC-PS in terms of cycling 
stability and rate capability are comparable to the reported iron 
sulfide works as shown in Table S1 (Supporting Information).
Postmortem FESEM images show that the porous spherical 
structure still well maintained even after 100 cycles (Figure S10, 
Supporting Information), confirming the superior structural 
stability of Fe7S8@NC-PS. Electrochemical impedance spectros-
copy (EIS) was applied to explore the charge transfer kinetics of 
Fe7S8@NC-PS during cycling (Figure S11, Supporting Informa-
tion). As can be seen, the charge transfer resistance (Rct) largely 
decreased after five cycles compared with the fresh cell, which 
could be attributed to the formation of a stable ion-conductive 
SEI layer and the activation of the electrode materials during 
cycling.[50–52] After 20 cycles, a well-maintained Rct further dem-
onstrated the structural stability of Fe7S8@NC-PS.
Fe7S8@NC-PS was also tested as anode material for SIBs. 
Figure  S12 (Supporting Information) shows CVs of the 
Fe7S8@NC-PS electrode at a voltage window of 0.02–2.5  V. A 
large cathodic peak centered at 0.82 V in the initial scan was 
assigned to the phase transition owing to the intercalation of 
Na+ ions.[20,53] Another two peaks at 0.34 and 0.2 V were sup-
posed to be the conversion reactions to further form Na2S 
and metallic Fe and the irreversible formation of SEI layer. In 
the following cycles, two main pairs of redox peaks at 0.8 and 
0.41 V in cathodic scans and 1.32 and 1.73 V in anodic scans 
were ascribed to the multistep sodiation and desodiation reac-
tions of Fe7S8@NC-PS, respectively. These well-overlapped CVs 
revealed a reversible sodiation and desodiation mechanism. The 
charge/discharge voltage profiles were well consistent with CV 
curves, as shown in Figure 4a. Fe7S8@NC-PS delivered a dis-
charge capacity of 754 mAh g−1 with a high initial CE of 93.2%. 
The specific capacities stabilized around 665 mAh g−1 in the fol-
lowing five cycles. The cycling performance was evaluated at a 
current density of 0.5 A g−1 (Figure 4b). As can be seen, the CEs 
quickly increased to 100% and the specific capacity remained 
at ≈650 mAh g−1 after 50 cycles at 0.5 A g−1. When evaluated 
at a high current density of 2A g−1, Fe7S8@NC-PS exhibited an 
excellent cycling stability with a high specific capacity around 
600 mAh g−1 after 150 cycles (Figure 4c).
The superior electrochemical properties of Fe7S8@NC-PS 
in LIBs and SIBs benefited from its unique nano-micro archi-
tecture. This porous structure provided sufficient void space 
to accommodate the large volume changes during cycling, 
which enabled Fe7S8@NC-PS a good cycling stability. The 
interconnected nanowalls offered a large electrolyte/elec-
trode contact area and short diffusion path for Li+/Na+ and 
electrons, which significantly improved the rate capability. 
In addition, the nitrogen-doped carbon framework contrib-
uted a high conductivity and structural stability upon charge 
and discharge, which further enhanced the cycling and rate 
performance.
In summary, a porous nano-micro hierarchical struc-
ture has been successfully prepared, which was composed 
of Fe7S8 nanoparticles embedded in nitrogen-doped carbon 
frameworks. With the merits in structure and composition, 
the Fe7S8@NC-PS electrodes demonstrated excellent electro-
chemical performance in LIBs and SIBs. Specifically, it deliv-
ered a stable reversible capacity of 630 mAh g−1 after 200 cycles 
at 2A g−1 and a high specific capacity of 440 mAh g−1 even at 
20 A g−1 in LIBs. Impressively, it demonstrated an ultralong 
cycling stability with a reversible capacity of 438 mAh g−1 after 
700 cycles when cycled at a high current density of 10 A g−1.

In addition, Fe7S8@NC-PS revealed a good cycling stability in 
SIBs with a high capacity of 650 at 0.5 A g−1 after 50 cycles 
and 600 mAh g−1 at 2 A g−1 after 150 cycles. These superior 
electrochemical properties enabled Fe7S8@NC-PS potential 
electrode materials for next-generation rechargeable batteries.
Experimental Section
Synthesis of Fe7S8@NC-PS: In a typical synthesis, FeCl2 · 4H2O 
(2 mmol) was first dissolved in distilled water (DIW, 40 mL) to form 
a transparent solution. Afterward, polyvinylpyrrolidone K30 (PVP-
K30, 0.15  g) and thioacetamide (5  mmol) were orderly added into 
the above solution under stirring for 30 min to form a homogeneous 
solution. The above solution was then transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-
lined stainless steel autoclave and then kept at 200 °C for 12 h. After 
cooling down to room temperature naturally, the obtained dark-
brown product was separated by centrifugation, washed with DIW 
and ethanol for several times before drying at 70 °C overnight under 
vacuum.
Materials Characterization: FESEM (JEOL JSM07600F) and TEM (JEOL 
JEM-2100F) were used to characterize the microscopic features of the 
samples. The composition of the samples and elemental mapping were 
analyzed by EDX spectroscopy attached to the TEM instrument. A Rigaku 
D/MAX RINT-2000 X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation at a voltage 
of 40 kV and a current of 40 mA was used to collect the XRD patterns 
of the products. XPS (VG microtech ESCA2000) was used to investigate 
the surface composition and the valence states of the as-synthesized 
samples. Raman spectroscopy (JY LABRAM, Ar+ laser excitation with a 
wavelength of 514.5 nm) was applied to check the existence of carbon. 
The specific surface areas and pore size distribution of as-synthesized 
materials were measured using BET method on Autosorb-1 at liquid-
nitrogen temperature.
Electrochemical Measurements: The battery tests were carried 
out in a half-cell configuration. The working electrode consists of 
active materials, conductivity agent (Super P), and polymer binder 
(polyvinylidene fluoride, PVDF) with a weight ratio of 70:20:10. The 
active mass loading on the electrode is about 1.2  mg cm−2. The 
electrolytes were a solution of 1.3 m LiPF6 in a mixture of ethylene 
carbonate and diethyl carbonate (EC/DEC, 1:1 by wt%) with 5% 
fluoroethylene carbonate (FEC) additive and 1 m NaSO3CF3 in 
diethylene glycol dimethyl ether (DEGDME) for LIBs and SIBs, 
respectively. Lithium and sodium discs were used as both counter 
electrodes and reference electrodes. The coin-type half cells were 
assembled in argon-filled glove box and then tested in TOSCAT 3000 
battery tester (TOSCAT 3000, Toyo Systems, Tokyo, Japan) with a 
voltage range between 0.02 and 2.5  V for LIBs and SIBs. CV curves 
were tested using AUTOLAB potentiostat/galvanostat apparatus 
(AUT85698) with a scan rate of 0.1 mV s−1. The EIS data were tested 
with a frequency range from 100 kHz to 100 mHz.

[SOURCE: A Novel High-Performance TiO2-xTiO1-yNy Coating Material for Silicon Anode in Lithium-Ion Batteries.pdf]

2200430  (1 of 7)
© 2022 Wiley-VCH GmbH
www.small-methods.com
Research Article
A Novel High-Performance TiO2-x/TiO1-yNy Coating 
Material for Silicon Anode in Lithium-Ion Batteries
Sung Eun Wang, Min Ji Kim, Jin Woong Lee, Jinyoung Chun, Junghyun Choi, 
Kwang Chul Roh, Yun Chan Kang,* and Dae Soo Jung*
DOI: 10.1002/smtd.202200430
create more SEI on the freshly exposed Si 
surface, which quickly fills the electrode 
pores and reduces the battery capacity.[6,7]
To solve the volume expansion issues of 
Si, many nanostructures and composites 
have been studied in recent decades. Var-
ious material designs such as Si nanowires, 
nanotubes, nanorods, and porous spheres 
have been demonstrated to effectively 
reduce the internal stress of Si anodes 
during lithiation and show great promise 
for extending their cycle lifetimes.[8–11] 
However, these materials are fabricated 
using toxic reagents or high-cost chemical 
vapor deposition method. Other designs 
to accommodate the volume change are 
core–shell and yolk–shell structures.[12–15] 
Surface coating with functional layers 
such as carbon-based materials and metal 
oxides can improve the electrode conduc-
tivity and avoid direct contact between the 
active material and electrolyte, resulting in 
the growth of a stable SEI layer.[16] Despite 
these advantages, these structures still 
cannot endure the volume changes of Si or have poor initial/
late cycle coulombic efficiencies (CEs), which hinder the develop-
ment of various Si-based composites for practical applications.
Herein, we report a novel multifunctional hybrid coating 
material for Si anode, TiO2-x/TiO1-yNy (denoted as TTN), 
which shows electrically and mechanically robust features. The 
hybrid coating layer offers multiple advantages. i) The TiO2-x 
phase provides electrical pathways for electrochemical reac-
tions between Li ions and Si. While white TiO2 shows an ini-
tially low electrical conductivity, black (oxygen-deficient) TiO2 
has a relatively high electrical conductivity due to its narrower 
bandgap.[17–19] During lithiation, TiO2-x forms the LizTiO2-x  
phase, which acts as an electron and Li ion pathway.[20,21] 
ii) TiO1-yNy, which is regarded as a solid solution of TiN 
and TiO, can effectively withstand the volume expansion of 
Si because of its much larger bulk modulus than TiO2.[22–25] 
Moreover, nitrogen atoms incorporated into the oxygen vacan-
cies of the TiO phase improve the electrical conductivity of 
Si.[26] iii) The hybrid coating layer can also enhance the struc-
tural stability of the Si composite because the hybrid coating 
layer acts as an interfacial barrier between the Si NPs and elec-
trolyte, reducing the creation of an unstable SEI layer. Based 
on these properties, TiO2-x/TiO1-yNy-coated Si (Si@TTN) may 
be promising in high-performance Si-based LIBs.
Protective surface coatings on Si anodes are promising for improving the 
electrochemical performance of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs). Nevertheless, 
most coating materials have severe issues, including low initial coulombic 
efficiency, structural fracture, morphology control, and complicated synthetic 
processing. In this study, a multifunctional TiO2-x/TiO1-yNy (TTN) formed via 
a facile and scalable synthetic process is applied as a coating material for Si 
anodes. A thin layer of amorphous TiO2 is uniformly coated onto Si nano-
particles by a simple sol–gel method and then converted into a two phase 
TiO2-x/TiO1-yNy via nitridation. The lithiated TiO2-x provides high ionic and 
electrical conductivity, while TiO1-yNy can improve mechanical strength that 
alleviates volume change of Si to address capacity fading issue. Owing to 
these synergetic advantages, TiO2-x/TiO1-yNy-coated Si (Si@TTN) exhibits 
excellent electrochemical properties, including a high charge capacity of 
1650 mA h g–1 at 0.1 A g–1 and 84% capacity retention after 100 cycles at 
1 A g–1. Moreover, a significantly enhanced rate performance can be achieved 
at a high current density. This investigation presents a facile and effective 
coating material to use as the high-capacity silicon anode in the emerging Si 
anode technology in LIBs.
The ORCID identification number(s) for the author(s) of this article 
can be found under https://doi.org/10.1002/smtd.202200430.
1. Introduction
Silicon (Si) is considered as a promising candidate for anodes 
of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) anodes due to its low operation 
potential (<0.5  V versus Li/Li+) and high theoretical capacity 
(3579 mA h g–1 for Li3.75Si at room temperature).[1,2] However, 
Si anodes suffer from large volume expansion, which causes 
pulverization of the active material, electrical isolation, and the 
substantial formation of unstable solid–electrolyte interphase 
(SEI) films during cycling.[3–5] This combination continues to 
S. E. Wang, M. J. Kim, J. W. Lee, J. Chun, J. Choi, K. C. Roh, D. S. Jung
Energy and Environmental Division
Korea Institute of Ceramic Engineering and Technology (KICET)
Jinju, Gyeongnam 52851, Republic of Korea
E-mail: dsjung@kicet.re.kr
S. E. Wang, Y. C. Kang
Department of Materials Science and Engineering
Korea University
Seoul 02841, Republic of Korea
E-mail: yckang@korea.ac.kr

2. Results and Discussion
The process flow for synthesizing the composite is shown in 
Figure  1A. To obtain Si NPs coated with an amorphous TiO2 
thin film, the sol–gel method was adopted with a precursor 
solution prepared using titanium isopropoxide and Si NPs. The 
coating reaction was conducted in a mixed solvent of ethanol 
and aqueous ammonia, which contributes the uniform growth 
of amorphous TiO2.[27] After a sufficient reaction, Si@TiO2  
nanocomposite can be obtained. Si@TiO2 was thermally 
annealed in a tube furnace at 650 °C for 3 h under flowing NH3 
gas, and the TiO2 converted into TTN because of the strong 
reduction ability of NH3, thereby forming Si@TTN core–shell 
nanoparticles. Figure  1B shows the phase transformation of 
TTN during the lithiation/delithiation. During the lithiation, 
TiO2-x formed LizTiO2-x phase while TiO1-yNy component 
remained in its original phase. This is because lithium ions can 
be intercalated into TiO2-x, whereas TiO1-yNy is inactive with 
lithium ions.
LizTiO2-x remains even after complete delithiation, which 
can provide electrical pathways and promote the electron/Li 
ion conductivity. Additionally, the TiO1-yNy structure has a high 
mechanical strength and electrical conductivity, which enhance 
the structural stability and rate performance of the electrode.
To investigate the phase transformation conditions of TiO2 
according to the nitridation reaction temperature, the com-
mercial TiO2 powder was used. As shown in Figure  S1A 
(Supporting Information), the color of TiO2 changed from 
white to dark blue and finally black at high temperature. This 
is related to the electronic structure and has been discussed 
in many reports.[28–30] In the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns 
(Figure  S2, Supporting Information), the ratio of TiO1-yNy 
phase was increased at the high temperature, which means that 
TiO1-yNy contributed to the color change of the powder toward 
black. The TEM images show that the TiO1-yNy had a hollow 
structure, whereas TiO2 had a filled structure (Figure  S1B, 
Supporting Information). The driving force creating the hollow 
TiO1-yNy can be explained from several perspectives. At a low 
temperature, TiO2 is reduced by NH3 gas to yield TiO2-x with 
an oxygen-deficient structure before the TiO phase can form at 
a higher temperature. The TiO phase has a large nonstoichiom-
etry, stabilizing its structure by containing randomly distributed 
vacancies ≈15% on both Ti and O sites.[31] This suggests that 
oxygen vacancies serve as appropriate sites for N atoms because 
they are thermodynamically stable for N atom incorporation. 
The Ti2+ (for TiO) species can form stable N3– by donating elec-
trons to N atoms, thereby promoting the substitution of O and 
N.[32] The O atoms diffuse outward for the anion exchange and 
the inserted N atoms reduce the energy needed to form oxygen 
vacancies that diffuse inward. Because of imbalance of the dif-
fusion rate, anion exchange occurs near the particle surface and 
oxygen vacancies merge to form voids. Additionally, density-
functional theory calculations have indicated that the incor-
porated N atoms must be stabilized from N2– to N3–, allowing 
for the Ti 3d electrons in the TiO conduction band to transfer 
to the N atoms.[24] In the XRD patterns of nitrided TiO2, shift 
of the peak toward lower angle was observed indicating that 
TiN-rich phase is formed at high temperature (Figure S2A, Sup-
porting Information). As illustrated by the plotted composition 
ratios (Figure  S2B, Supporting Information), both TiO2-x and 
TiO1-yNy phases coexisted below 800 °C, while only the TiO1-yNy 
phase was present above 800 °C. Based on the above analysis, it 
was confirmed that TiO2 nitrided at 650 °C had hybrid structure 
containing TiO2-x and TiO1-yNy in a weight ratio of ≈7:3.
The Si NPs exhibit smooth surfaces with dimensions of tens 
of nanometers (Figure S3, Supporting Information). After TiO2 
coating, Si@TiO2 particles show slightly rough surfaces but 
maintain their spherical morphology containing crystalline Si 
cores, as determined by TEM analysis (Figure  2A). The fast 
Fourier transform (FFT) pattern obtained from the TEM image 
indicates the amorphous nature of the TiO2 coating layer, which 
had a thickness of ≈3.5 nm. When Si@TiO2 was annealed at 
Figure 1.  Schematic illustrations of the A) synthesis of Si@TTN nanocomposite and B) phase transformation of the coating layer through electro-
chemical reaction.
Small Methods 2022, 6, 2200430
 23669608, 2022, 7, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/smtd.202200430 by Gachon University, Wiley Online Library on [28/01/2026]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

© 2022 Wiley-VCH GmbH
2200430  (3 of 7)
www.advancedsciencenews.com
www.small-methods.com
650 °C in an NH3 gas atmosphere, TiO2 was converted to TTN 
phase (Figure  2B). The further roughened surface than TiO2 
coating layer was derived from crystalline growth of TiO1-yNy, 
which was impregnated by TiO2-x film, and higher resolu-
tion TEM image (Figure 2B inset) shows lattice fringes in the 
shell layer with a d-spacing of 0.21  nm, corresponding to the 
TiO1-yNy (002) orientation. In the electron energy loss spec-
troscopy (EELS) elemental mapping images of Si@TTN in 
Figure  2C, red, green, yellow, and purple colors indicate the 
existence of Si, O, Ti, and N atoms, respectively. The O, Ti, 
and N signals overlap indicating that Si was embedded uni-
formly in the TTN coating layer. The photograph of Si@TiO2 
and Si@TTN powders in Figure S4 (Supporting Information) 
show the color change after nitridation demonstrating the 
phase transformation to TTN. The XPS spectra are provided for 
the Ti 2p and N 1s regions in Figure  2D,E. The spectrum of 
Si@TTN contained a wide range of peaks in the Ti 2p region, while 
Si@TiO2 showed only two sharp peaks (Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2) 
indicating the Ti4+ oxidation state (Figure  S5A, Supporting 
Information). The oxidation state of Ti3+ is attributed to the 
TiN phase, and that of the Ti2+ species indicates the TiO phase. 
We also note that Ti 2p spectrum of Si@TiO2 revealed peaks 
at 464.8 and 459.3 eV corresponding to Ti4+; however, those of 
Si@TTN were observed at 463.9 and 457.7 eV. The peak shifts 
toward lower binding energy indicating a lower oxidation state 
of Ti, which is ascribed to oxygen vacancies for TiO2-x in TTN. 
The existence of nitrogen in Si@TTN composite was evidenced 
by the N 1 s region with peaks corresponding to Ti-N (397.07 eV) 
and Ti-O-N bonds (395.4  eV), while no noticeable peaks cor-
responding to the nitrogen bonds were observed in Si@TiO2 
(Figure S5B, Supporting Information). These results can dem-
onstrate the successful formation of the hybrid coating layer.
The amorphous property of TiO2 was established by the XRD 
patterns (Figure 2F), in which only the diffraction peaks from 
crystalline Si were observed with no signals corresponding to 
TiO2. In the XRD pattern of Si@TTN composite, new peaks 
were observed at 25° corresponding to the (101) plane of anatase 
TiO2 and 37°, 43°, and 63° corresponding to the (111), (200), and 
(220) planes of rock-salt TiO, respectively. The diffraction peaks 
from Si NPs also observed at 28.4°, 47.3°, 56.1°, 69.1°, and 76.3° 
corresponding to the (111), (220), (311), (400), and (331) planes, 
respectively.
In the electrochemical testing of the synthesized compos-
ites (Figure  3A), Si@TTN exhibited a reversible capacity of 
1650 mA h g–1 at 0.1 A g–1 and an initial Coulombic efficiency 
of 76%, which exceeds that of Si@TiO2 (72%) because TiO2 
Figure 2.  HR-TEM images of A) Si@TiO2 and B) Si@TTN at low and high magnifications showing the coating layer thickness of TiO2 and TTN, with 
the FFT in the inset. C) EELS elemental maps of Si@TTN with respect to Si, O, Ti, and N. D) Ti 2p and E) N 1s XPS spectra of Si@TTN. The dotted 
lines indicate the fitted data. F) XRD patterns indicating the formation of the TiO phase after nitridation.
Small Methods 2022, 6, 2200430
 23669608, 2022, 7, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/smtd.202200430 by Gachon University, Wiley Online Library on [28/01/2026]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

© 2022 Wiley-VCH GmbH
2200430  (4 of 7)
www.advancedsciencenews.com
www.small-methods.com
irreversibly trapped Li ions whereas TiO1-yNy in TTN was 
not reactive. To compare the theoretical reversible capacity of 
Si@TTN with the above practical value, the core-shell volu-
metric ratio was calculated. The core and shell thickness were 
used from the TEM images in Figure 2A,B. The core volume 
fraction was obtained as the core volume divided by the total 
core-shell volume, and the calculated value was 67% indicating 
that the volumetric ratio of Si to TTN was 67:23 (v/v). In order 
to convert the above result into a weight fraction, the density 
of each component in Table S1 (Supporting Information) was 
used and it was obtained as 49:51 (wt/wt). Thus, the calculated 
theoretical capacity was 1670 mA h g–1, which is almost iden-
tical to the measured practical capacity of 1650 mA h g–1. These 
results indicate that the Si core can be completely activated 
owing to the short diffusion pathway of Li ions and the high 
electric conductivity of Si@TTN. The slight decrease in prac-
tical capacity compared to the calculated value might be an irre-
versible reaction of TiO2-x with Li ions.
At the cyclic voltammetry (CV) scan of Si@TTN composite, 
peaks at 1.77 V during discharging and 2.03 V during charging 
were observed (Figure  3B). These suggest that Li ions can 
insert into TiO2-x, leading to the formation of LizTiO2-x.[20] The 
LizTiO2-x phase can promote Li ion/electron pathways due to the 
existence of Ti3+, thereby improving the electrochemical perfor-
mance of Si@TTN anode. To further investigate the phase trans-
formation of TTN during the first cycling, ex situ XRD analysis 
was conducted as shown in Figure  S6 (Supporting Informa-
tion). When TTN was lithiated by 0.01  V, two peaks appeared 
at 33.9° and 44.4° compared to the fresh electrode, indicating 
phase transition of TiO2-x to orthorhombic Li0.5TiO2-x.[33,34]  
Subsequently, delithiated electrode by 1.5 V represented that the 
Li0.5TiO2-x phase is still remained due to residual Li in TiO2-x 
during first cycling that can improve ionic conductivity on the 
following rate or cycle properties.[19,35] On the other hand, the 
Si nanocomposites with TiO2 or TiO1-yNy did not exhibit any 
peaks at a high voltage (Figure S7, Supporting Information). The 
absence of oxidation/reduction peaks at a high voltage region 
in CV curves of TiO2 results from disordered structure and 
defects of amorphous TiO2 eliminating the plateaus of reaction 
with Li and TiO2 and that of TiO1-yNy means its inactive prop-
erty with Li ions.[36] For the measurement of cycle life, all sam-
ples were precycled at 0.1 A g–1 in the voltage window of 0.01–
1.5 V. Although the Si NPs initially had the highest capacity of 
2603 mA h g–1, the capacity decreased drastically to 781 mA h g–1 
after 100 cycles, corresponding to a cycle retention of only 30% 
(Figure 3C). Si@TiO2 exhibited a relatively stable cycling perfor-
mance up to 20 cycles, but the capacity then sharply dropped, 
corresponding to a retention of only 24% (348 mA h g–1). The 
capacity drop can be explained by cracking and disintegration 
of the TiO2 coating layer during repeated charging and dis-
charging cycles. In the case of Si@TTN, the charge capacity was 
1473 mA h g–1 and remained at 84% after 100 cycles. Although 
the excellent capacity retention of Si@TTN could be attrib-
uted partially to the moderate mass loading of the electrode 
(0.3 mg cm–2), the superior performance of Si@TTN compared 
to that of Si NPs and Si@TiO2 under same condition verifies 
the critical role of the novel design for hybrid coating layers 
in the cycling performance. Even when the electrode loading 
increased by 3.3 times (0.3 → 1  mg  cm–2), Si@TTN retained 
75% of the original capacity after 50 cycles at 1 A g–1 (Figure S11, 
Supporting Information), reconfirming the superior structural 
advantage of TTN.
Figure 3.  A) Galvanostatic charge/discharge voltage profiles of Si NPs, Si@TiO2, and Si@TTN measured at 0.1 A g–1. B) CV curves of Si@TTN at a 
scanning rate of 0.1 mV s–1 in the voltage range from 0.01 to 1.5 V. The inset shows an enlargement of the voltage range from 1.5 to 2.5 V. C) Cycling 
performance and CEs of the Si NPs, Si@TiO2, and Si@TTN at 1 A g–1 for 100 cycles. D) Rate capabilities of the three samples under current densities 
from 0.1 to 5 A g–1.

See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

© 2022 Wiley-VCH GmbH
2200430  (5 of 7)
www.advancedsciencenews.com
www.small-methods.com
The CE of the Si NPs increased to 95.8% after precycle 
but maintained low values for the first few cycles. Si@TiO2 
showed a relatively good CE of 97.3% at first and high average 
CEs of 98.0% between 1 and 20 cycles. However, the CE 
decreased shortly and recovered slowly because of the afore-
mentioned pulverization of Si. In comparison, Si@TTN 
exhibited an enhanced CE of 98.7% at first and showed high 
values during cycling. These results can be explained by the 
bulk modulus of TiO2, TiO, and TiN. TiO2 shows lower bulk 
modulus of 179  GPa than TiO and TiN (197 and 280  GPa, 
respectively).[24] Due to the above properties, TiO2 has rela-
tively low structural stability against volume expansion of 
Si, resulting in cracking and disintegration of coating layer 
during cycling, which deteriorate the CEs of anode. Conse-
quently, high cycling retention of Si@TTN is attributable to 
the rigid structure of TiO1-yNy, which can withstand the stress 
exerted by Si volume expansion.
The superiority of Si@TTN was also evidenced by the rate 
capability (Figure 3D). Reversible capacities of 1658, 1646, 1451, 
1282, 1070, and 824  mA  h  g–1 were achieved at current rates 
from 0.1 to 5  A  g–1, whereas Si@TiO2 showed much lower 
capacities of 1702, 1613, 1280, 917, 513, and 148 mA h g–1. This 
difference mainly arises from oxygen-deficient TiO2-x, which 
has a favorable electrical conductivity due to its narro­wer 
bandgap than TiO2 and enhances ionic conductivity after lithi-
ation. Furthermore, the presence of N atoms in the TiO1-yNy 
phase affords conductive components to improve the rate per-
formance of the anode. To further understand the effect of the 
LizTiO2-x phase on the electrochemical performance, Si@TiO1-yNy 
composite was prepared by completely converting TiO2 to 
TiO1-yNy at a high temperature (Figure S8, Supporting Infor-
mation). Si@TiO1-yNy composite shows a similar cycling per-
formance trend to Si@TTN. However, the lower reversible 
capacities of 1787, 1752, 1518, 1308, 972, and 553  mA  h  g–1 
were delivered at current densities of 0.1 to 5 A g–1. This result 
means that both the LizTiO2-x and TiO1-yNy phases have a posi-
tive effect on the rate performance, and the synergistic effect 
of the hybrid materials can improve the electrochemical perfor-
mance of Si anodes. Impedance spectroscopy (EIS) shows the 
excellent electrochemical performances of Si@TTN (Figure S9, 
Supporting Information). Si@TTN shows a smaller semicircle 
than Si@TiO2 in the high frequency region after the first cycle, 
which corresponds to a low charge transfer resistance (Rct) of 
Si@TTN. The lower Rct values of Si@TTN are attributed to the 
high ionic and electrical conductivity of the hybrid coating layer. 
After 100 cycles, decreased diameter of semicircle of Si@TTN 
demonstrates its high structural stability even after repeated 
lithiation/delithiaion while Si@TiO2 exhibits two semicircles in 
the high frequency region indicating the formation of thick SEI 
layer due to the structural failure.[37,38]
To further demonstrate the structural effect of coating, 
the surfaces of the three different electrodes were compared 
before and after cycling using field-emission scanning electron 
microscopy (FE-SEM) (Figure 4A,B). All fresh electrodes exhibit 
spherical nanoparticles as shown in Figure  4A. However, 
FE-SEM images in Figure 4B indicate that Si NPs and Si@TiO2 
show severe fracture and agglomeration after cycling. The pul-
verization of Si NPs was inevitable due to the absence of buffer 
layer at the beginning of the cycle results in the formation of 
thick SEI layer. The Si@TiO2 shows the same morphology as 
Figure 4.  FE-SEM structural assessment of the three different electrodes A) before and B) after cycling at 1 A g–1 and C) HR-TEM images showing the 
structural maintenance of bare Si NPs, Si@TiO2, and Si@TTN electrode after 50 cycles.
Small Methods 2022, 6, 2200430
 23669608, 2022, 7, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/smtd.202200430 by Gachon University, Wiley Online Library on [28/01/2026]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

© 2022 Wiley-VCH GmbH
2200430  (6 of 7)
www.advancedsciencenews.com
www.small-methods.com
Si NPs because TiO2 was collapsed after 20 cycles as previously 
described in Figure 3C. On the other hand, the spherical mor-
phology of Si@TTN particles was found to be very robust main-
taining core-shell structure after cycling (Si@TTN in Figure 4C; 
Figure S10, Supporting Information). The robust TTN structure 
of Si@TTN allows to endure the volume expansion of the inner 
Si NPs while keeping the integration of both components.
3. Conclusion
In conclusion, we have introduced a hybrid TTN coating for Si 
anodes via a simple coating method to achieve Si-based LIBs 
with high energy density and long cycle life. The nanostruc-
ture was confirmed by various analytical methods, which show 
that the core-shell composite consisted of Si NPs as the core 
and a rigid TTN thin film as the shell. Si@TTN composite can 
achieve a high reversible capacity, stable cycle life, and good 
rate capability. The improvements are attributed to the syn-
ergistic effect of the hybrid coating layer providing enhanced 
mechanical and electrical properties. In terms of efforts for the 
commercialization of Si-based anodes, Si@TTN composite rep-
resents a promising candidate for high-performance LIBs.
4. Experimental Section
Sample Preparation: Silicon nanoparticles (Si NPs, 99%, average 
diameter = 50 nm, NanoAmor) and titanium isopropoxide (TTIP, 97%, 
SIGMA ALDRICH) were used as starting materials. The amorphous 
TiO2 was deposited on the surface of the Si NPs via a sol-gel reaction. 
Typically, 0.5  g of Si NPs was added to a solution containing 666  mL 
of absolute ethanol and 8 mL of an ammonia aqueous solution (5 N). 
Afterward, 1.9 mL of TTIP was added dropwise, and the mixed solution 
was stirred at 40  °C for 40  h. The reaction products were centrifuged 
at 11  000  rpm for 30  min and washed with ethanol before drying at 
80 °C overnight. The amorphous TiO2 thin film was then converted into 
TiO2-x/TiO1-yNy by nitridation at 650  °C under ammonia gas flow for 
3 h. As the control sample, Si@TiO1-yNy was synthesized by the same 
procedure as mentioned above but was nitrided at 800 °C under NH3 
gas flow for 3  h. Meanwhile, to identify the phase transformation of 
TiO2 according to the nitidation temperature, commercial TiO2 powders 
(P25, Degussa) was used and was thermal treated for 5  h at various 
temperatures (600 to 950 °C) by NH3 gas.
Characterization: The morphologies of the Si NPs, Si@TiO2, and 
Si@TTN were characterized by FE-SEM (JSM-7001F, JEOL). The 
TEM bright-field images and EELS mappings were obtained by high-
resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, JEM-2200FS, 
JEOL). The XPS spectra were acquired using an X-ray photoelectron 
spectrometer (XPS, VersaProbe, Ulvac-PHI), and the crystal structures 
and phases of the products were characterized by XRD (D8 Advance, 
Bruker). The nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherms of the 
samples were measured using the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller method 
(BELSORP-max, MicrotracBEL) after degassing at 150  °C for 12  h. 
For ex situ XRD measurements during first cycling, the coin cell 
was disassembled and then electrodes were washed with dimethyl 
carbonate. The electrodes were dried at 80 °C for 5 h and mounted on 
the XRD holder. To characterize the morphologies of the bare Si NPs, 
Si@TiO2, and Si@TTN after 50 cycles, the electrodes were prepared in 
the same procedure as for ex situ XRD analysis and the active particles 
were transferred from the electrodes to an SEM/TEM holder.
Electrochemical Measurements: The Si@TTN composite was mixed 
with super-P and poly (acrylic acid) (Mw = 3 000 000, Sigma–Aldrich) at 
a weight ratio of 60 (active):20 (binder):20 (super-P) with distilled water. 
After mixing, the slurry was casted onto copper foil using the doctor 
blade method, and the electrode was dried in a vacuum oven at 80 °C 
overnight. The mass loading of the active material was 0.2–0.3 mg cm–2. 
Electrodes of Si NPs and the Si@TiO2 composite were also prepared 
by the same fabrication procedure. CR2032 coin cell assembly was 
performed in a drying room. A 1 m lithium hexafluorophosphate solution 
in a co-solvent of ethylene carbonate and diethyl carbonate (1:1 = v/v) 
containing 5 wt.% fluoroethylene carbonate (PANAX ETEC) was used as 
the electrolyte. The cells were assembled as half-cell type with lithium 
metal (Wellcos) as the counter electrode and separators (Tonen). 
Galvanostatic charge/discharge experiments were performed in the 
potential range of 0.01–1.5 V (vs Li/Li+). CV was performed at a scan rate 
of 0.01 mV s–1. For cycling performance testing, all samples were precycled 
once at 0.1 A g–1 within the voltage range of 0.01–1.5 V. EIS was performed 
in the frequency range of 0.01 to 100 kHz at an amplitude of 5 mV.

from the author.
