[SOURCE: A Novel Structured Si-Based Composite with 2D Structured Graphite for High-Performance Lithium-Ion Batteries.pdf]
================================================================================
RESEARCH ARTICLE
www.small-journal.com
A Novel Structured Si-Based Composite with 2D Structured
Graphite for High-Performance Lithium-Ion Batteries
Min Ji Kim, Inuk Lee, Jin Woong Lee, Dowoong Yoon, Jung Hyun Kim, Seungho Lee,
Kwanghyun Kim, Patrick Joohyun Kim, Junghyun Choi,* Yun Chan Kang,*
and Dae Soo Jung*
Silicon is a promising alternative to graphite anodes for achieving
high-energy-density in lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) because of its high
theoretical capacity (3579 mAh g−1). However, silicon anode must be
developed to address its disadvantages, such as volume expansion and low
electronic conductivity. Therefore, the use of silicon as composed with
graphite and carbon anode materials is investigated, which requires
properties such as a spherical morphology for high density and encapsulation
of silicon particles in the composite. Herein, a graphite@silicon@carbon
(Gr@Si@C) micro-sized spherical anode composite is synthesized by
mechanofusion process. This composite comprises an outer surface, middle
layer, and core pore, which are formed by the capillary force arising from 2D
structured graphite and pitch properties. This structure eﬀectively addresses
the intrinsic issues associated with Si. Gr@Si@C exhibits a high capacity of
1622 mAh g−1 and capacity retention of 72.2% after 100 cycles, with a high
areal capacity 4.2 mAh cm−2. When Gr@Si@C is blended with commercial
graphite, the composite exhibits high capacity retention and average
Coulombic eﬃciency after cycling. The Gr@Si@C blended electrode exhibits a
high energy density of 820 Wh L−1 with ≈16% metallic Si in the electrode
(40 wt.% composite), enabling the realization of practical commercial LIBs.
M. J. Kim, I. Lee, J. W. Lee, D. Yoon, J. H. Kim, D. S. Jung
Energy and Environmental Division
Korea Institute of Ceramic Engineering and Technology
Jinju, Gyeongnam 52851, Republic of Korea
E-mail: dsjung@kicet.re.kr
I.Lee, J.W.Lee, D.Yoon,Y.C.Kang
Department of Materials ScienceandEngineering
Korea University
Anam-dong, Seongbuk-gu,Seoul 136-713,Republicof Korea
E-mail: yckang@korea.ac.kr
S.Lee,K.Kim,P.J.Kim
Department of AppliedChemistry
Kyungpook National University
Daegu 41566,Republicof Korea
J.Choi
School of Chemical
Biological andBattery Engineering
Gachon University
Seongnam-si, Gyeonggi-do 13120,Republicof Korea
E-mail: junghchoi@gachon.ac.kr
The ORCID identiﬁcation number(s) for the author(s) of this article
can be found under https://doi.org/10.1002/smll.202405005
DOI: 10.1002/smll.202405005
1. Introduction
To address climate change, the demand
for electric vehicles (EVs) is increasing.[1]
However, the adoption of EVs is hin-
dered by their low driving range, which
is related to the low energy density of
lithium-ion batteries (LIBs).[2] Silicon (Si)
is considered a promising anode mate-
rial for enhancing the energy density of
LIBs due to its high theoretical capacity
and low potential.[3] Unfortunately, Si an-
odes face challenges such as signiﬁcant
volume expansion and low electronic con-
ductivity, leading to poor mechanical and
electrochemical performances.[3a,4] To over-
come these issues, tremendous progress
has been made in developing Si-based
nanomaterials and composites (e.g., Sil-
icon/carbon composite (Si/C)) with lim-
ited amounts of Si.[5] Strategies such as
the use of Si nanoparticles (NPs) can im-
prove the electrochemical performance of
LIBs by reducing mechanical stress dur-
ing cycling and facilitating faster lithium
ion movement owing to shorter diﬀusion
paths.[6] However, their high surface areas increase the forma-
tion of a solid electrolyte interface (SEI) layer, which hinders ion
transport and reduces eﬃciency.[7,8a]
Most studies have focused on the composition and/or coating
of carbonaceous materials, which buﬀer the volume expansion,
enhance the electronic conductivity, and increase the active mate-
rial loading mass, to overcome the limitations of Si NPs in prac-
tical LIB applications.[8] However, a well-designed anode com-
posite with Si NPs anode material is diﬃcult to synthesize.[9]
Three major issues concern the design of Si-based composites:
1) a large amount of Si NPs is incorporated into the particles to
achieve high energy density. 2) The Si NPs are completely em-
bedded and/or covered by a carbonaceous material to prevent di-
rect contact between the Si particles and the electrolyte. 3) The
Si NPs are homogeneously distributed in the electrode to ensure
uniform electrode swelling and stable cycle performance.
Wet processing is a simple and scalable method for fabricating
Si-based anode composites.[10] However, the wet process requires
additional ﬁltering and a drying process and relatively limits the
variety of microstructures compared with other processes used
Small 2024, 20, 2405005
© 2024 Wiley-VCH GmbH
2405005 (1 of 9)

www.advancedsciencenews.com
www.small-journal.com
Figure 1. Schematic illustration of the a) Gr@Si@C anode and b) MF process for Gr@Si@C composite fabrication.
to synthesize anode composites. Another conventional process
is ball milling, which easily produces anode composites by us-
ing high-energy rotating balls.[11] However, unfavorable side re-
actions can occur with high energy, and Si particles can be ex-
posed on the surface of the composite. Therefore, an alternative
method for fabricating Si-based composites is required.
A graphite@silicon@carbon (Gr@Si@C) composite exhibits
a novel structure characterized by a pitch-derived hard carbon
shell, a 2D structured graphite framework, and a core pore.
This unique structure is made feasible by the pitch properties
and the mechanofusion (MF) process. The MF process is a dry
process, which is facile to operate the system.[12] Additionally,
it is possible to fabricate spherical and coated anode compos-
ite eﬀectively without any solvent. The 2D structured graphite
can wrap the composite and Si NPs like a cabbage and form
a porous tangled framework owing to the high mechanical en-
ergy of the MF process. It adapts well to the volume expan-
sion of Si NPs, oﬀers an electronic conductive network, and can
encapsulate a large quantity of Si NPs with a uniform distri-
bution in the composite owing to its high surface area.[13] The
pitch is converted into a viscous liquid during carbonization and
moves to the shell layer via the capillary force of the graphite
framework.[8a,14] This prevents direct contact between the Si NPs
and electrolytes and provides a continuous electronic conductive
network by connecting the components in the composite as a
binder.[15]
This study aimed to develop a novel Gr@Si@C composite
structure based on the synergistic eﬀects of its physical and
chemical properties. When this Gr@Si@C composite was intro-
duced into the electrode, a high electrode density was achieved
with a spherical micro-sized anode composite using the MF pro-
cess, showing good cycle retention after 100 cycles, even at up
to 40 wt.% Si mass fraction in the anode composite. After incor-
poration into commercial graphite (CG-Gr@Si@C), the blended
electrode exhibited a high cycle retention and average Coulom-
bic eﬃciency (ACE) with a 16 wt.% metallic Si mass fraction
(40 wt.% composite). This was achieved through the unifor-
mity of Si NPs in the electrode using the Gr@Si@C compos-
ite. Moreover, the full cell with the CG-Gr@Si@C anode and
LiNi0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2 (NCM811) cathode exhibited stable cycle per-
formance. Therefore, the Gr@Si@C composite, applied with 2D
structured graphite and the MF process, is a promising candidate
for high-performance Si-based anode materials.
2. Results and Discussion
Figure 1a shows a schematic of the Gr@Si@C anode mate-
rial, which is composed of three parts: the coating layer of
pitch-derived carbon (outer surface), frameworks of 2D struc-
tured graphite (middle layer), and core pores. Many Si NPs were
fully embedded and uniformly distributed in the pores of the
graphite interparticles and were covered by pitch-derived carbon.
Figure 1b shows a schematic of the synthesis of the Gr@Si@C
composite using the MF process. As the rotation speed of the
chamber increased, the materials of the composite component
were pushed toward the inner wall by the centrifugal force. High
shear and compression forces were applied to the composite
components as they pass through the gap between the inner wall
and ﬁxed press head.[16] The spherical micro-sized Gr@Si@C
anode material was fabricated by a continuous process. During
the fabrication of the Gr@Si@C composite structure, large par-
ticles were coated with small particles through high shear and
compression forces during the MF process. Moreover, the mor-
phology of composite was aﬀected by mechanical properties of
the material. Relatively soft materials enable the coating and/or
binding of rigid materials via mechanical forces. Therefore, the
pitch particles eﬀectively coated and binding the Si NPs and 2D
structured graphite.[17] Additionally, the as-prepared composite,
synthesized through the MF process, contains a micro-porous
structure formed by silicon and 2D graphite, in which the pitch,
when heated above softening point during carbonization process,
migrates from the inside to the outside of the composite driven
by capillary forces. As a result, numerous porous structures are
created inside the composite, while the migration of the pitch to
the exterior leads to the formation of a relatively large amount of
pitch-derived carbon, creating a rigid outer surface. Core pore is
where the volume expansion of the Si particles is preferably di-
rected. Thus, the core pore served as a buﬀer space to suppress
the swelling of the electrode during cycling, leading to a uniform
volume expansion of the electrode.[18]
The Gr@Si@C anode composite prepared using the MF
process exhibited clear X-ray diﬀractometer (XRD) peaks
Small 2024, 20, 2405005
© 2024 Wiley-VCH GmbH
2405005 (2 of 9)
 16136829, 2024, 49, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/smll.202405005 by Gachon University, Wiley Online Library on [28/01/2026]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

www.advancedsciencenews.com
www.small-journal.com
corresponding to the graphite, pitch-derived carbon, and Si
phases (Figure S1, Supporting Information). A crystal phase re-
lated to the 2D structured graphite and Si NPs and a slight amor-
phous phase related to the pitch-derived carbon were observed
in the XRD pattern of the Gr@Si@C composite. Images of the
pristine materials are shown in Figures S2–S4 (Supporting Infor-
mation). The 2D structured graphite is plate-like and thin, with
a size of 2–3 μm and a thickness of 60–70 nm. The Si NPs were
spherical, with a uniform size distribution of ≈100 nm. The pris-
tine pitch particles have a bimodal size distribution of ≈5 and
15 μm.
Images of the Gr@Si@C composite are shown in Figure 2.
Before carbonization, the Gr@Si@Pitch composite was spher-
ical and dense, and its core consisted of a large pitch particle
(Figure 2a). After carbonization, the Gr@Si@C composite main-
tained the spherical shape of the Gr@Si@Pitch composite and
exhibited a uniform size distribution of 20–30 μm (Figure 2b).
A core pore was formed at the center of the Gr@Si@C compos-
ite owing to the capillary force and pitch properties (Figure 2c).
The structure of the Gr@Si@C composite was investigated using
transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and elemental dot map-
ping; the sample was prepared using a focused-ion beam (FIB),
as shown in Figure 2d.
TEM images and elemental mapping images corresponding to
carbon elements revealed that a dense 3 μm thick carbon outer
surface was formed and a middle layer was observed between the
carbon outer surface and a core pore. Si NPs with a size of 100 nm
were homogeneously distributed within the dense carbon outer
surface and porous middle layer. Detailed TEM images and fast
Fourier transformation (FFT) patterns of the Gr@Si@C compos-
ite are shown in Figure 2e–g. Figure 2e shows the outer surface
of the Gr@Si@C composite, which consists of a large amount
of amorphous carbon arising from pitch-derived carbon and a
limited amount of crystalline 2D structured graphite. Closer to
the porous middle layer, the amount of amorphous carbon de-
creased, and the amount of graphite increased (Figure 2f). There-
fore, the pitch-derived carbon prevented the exposure of the com-
ponents, and the 2D structured graphite enveloped the compos-
ite. The porous middle layer was formed by 2D graphite in a hor-
izontal alignment because of the centrifugal force generated dur-
ing the MF process. The Si NPs were uniformly distributed inside
the graphite frameworks. Some amorphous carbon was observed
between the graphite interparticles and/or between the graphite
and Si NPs. This results in the pitch-derived carbon connecting
the particles to form an electronic network.
To verify the properties of the pitch, the thermal treatment pro-
cess was conducted at the softening point, and thermogravimet-
ric analysis (TGA) was performed under an argon atmosphere.
The pitch powder converted to a viscous liquid at 160 °C as shown
Figure S5 (Supporting Information). Figure S6 (Supporting In-
formation) shows the pitch yield at 1000 °C, resulting in ≈54%
carbon due to the proportion of hydrogen decreasing because of
the volatilization of H2 and CH4 at high temperature. This led to
the formation of not only a porous middle layer but also a core
pore. Therefore, the cross-sectional images and other analyses re-
veal that the novel structure of the Gr@Si@C composite can be
realized by applying pitch properties and capillary force.
The contents of each components of the Gr@Si@C composite
were analyzed by TGA with derivative thermogravimetry (DTG),
which was performed under ambient atmospheric conditions in
the temperature range of 25–1000 °C (Figure 3a). The TGA plots
show a weight loss of 58% between 600 and 800 °C, indicating
the reduction of the carbon matrix of the Gr@Si@C composite.
Notably, the thermal decomposition of the Gr@Si@C compos-
ite occurred in two stages, as indicated by the DTG curve. The
ﬁrst stage proceeded with a weight loss of 30% between 600 and
700 °C, with DTG peaks observed at 670 °C. The second stage
proceeded with a weight loss of 28% between 700 and 800 °C,
with DTG peaks observed at 740 °C. These weight losses are due
to the loss of pitch-derived carbon and 2D structured graphite,
respectively, as shown in Figure S7 (Supporting Information).
Accordingly, each component of the Gr@Si@C composite con-
tained 42% Si NPs, 28% 2D structured graphite, and 30% pitch-
derived carbon. A large number of Si NPs were embedded in the
Gr@Si@C composite, facilitated by pitch-derived carbon and 2D
structured graphite. The tap density of the Gr@Si@C composite
was 1.04 g cc−1, which was dramatically higher than that of the
pristine 2D structured graphite and pristine Si NPs (Figure 3b).
The 2D structured graphite and Si NPs were completely en-
capsulated by pitch-derived carbon using the MF process. In ad-
dition, the tap densities of the Gr@Si@C composite and com-
mercial graphite are in line. This implies that the Gr@Si@C
composite can achieve a higher electrode density than com-
mercial graphite, despite the presence of a large quantity of
high-capacity Si active materials. Consequently, the energy den-
sity of the Gr@Si@C composite was signiﬁcantly enhanced.
The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface area analysis of the
Gr@Si@C composite is shown in Figure 3c. Even though the 2D
structured graphite and Si NPs have large speciﬁc surface areas
of 14.9 and 25.1 m2 g−1, respectively, the Gr@Si@C composite
and Si/C have speciﬁc surface areas of 7.9 and 9.4 m2 g−1, respec-
tively. The large speciﬁc surface area results in a high irreversible
capacity and exhaustion of the electrolyte arising from side reac-
tions with the electrolyte. However, the speciﬁc surface area of
Gr@Si@C was eﬀectively suppressed by the MF process owing
to its spherical micro-sized particles, encapsulated 2D structured
graphite, and Si NPs in a pitch-derived carbon shell layer. The
Si/C composite had a higher surface area than the Gr@Si@C
composite despite the application of pitch-derived carbon, indi-
cating that the Si NPs were exposed on the surface of the Si/C
composite. The pore-size distribution was characterized using
the Barrett–Joyner–Halenda method (Figure 3d). The 2D struc-
tured graphite had small pore sizes of <4 nm, whereas compos-
ite pore sizes of <2 nm (micropores) and <35 nm (mesopores)
were observed, indicating the pitch-derived carbon and a porous
middle layer, respectively.[8a,19] However, the Si/C composite only
exhibited micropores related to pitch-derived carbon. Thus, the
unique pore structure of Gr@Si@C formed by the porous mid-
dle layer can contribute to the encapsulation of the Si NPs.
The charge/discharge proﬁle of the ﬁrst cycle of the Gr@Si@C
composite was investigated at 0.1 C in the voltage range of 0.01–
1.5 V (Figure 4a). For comparison, the Si/C composite, which
was prepared using a planetary mixer as a simple mixture of
Si NPs and pitch carbon, was also analyzed. The Si/C compos-
ite consisted of 42 wt.% Si NPs and 58% pitch-derived carbon,
as shown in Figure S8 (Supporting Information). The Si NPs
are highly exposed on the surface of the Si/C composite and
show a non-homogenous distribution of particle size (Figure S9,

(before carbonization). b) Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) image of Gr@Si@C composite after carbonization at 1000 °C for 3 h.

of the e) outermost part and f) between outermost and middle layer area of the outer surface and g) middle layer of Gr@Si@C.

www.small-journal.com
Figure 3. a) TGA-DTG curve representing the thermal decomposition of the Gr@Si@C composite. b) Tap density comparted with pristine 2D structured
graphite, Si NPs, commercial graphite, and Gr@Si@C composite, c) BET surface area, and d) pore diameters of 2D structured graphite, Gr@Si@C
composite, and Si/C composite.
Supporting Information). The ﬁrst discharge capacities of the
Si/C and Gr@Si@C composites were 1614 and 1622 mAh g−1,
respectively, and their initial Coulombic eﬃciencies (ICE) were
85.0% and 87.9%, respectively. The initial capacities of the anode
composites were consistent with those of the raw materials. As
determined by the mass fractions obtained from the TGA results,
the theoretical discharge capacities of the Si/C and Gr@Si@C
composites were 1612 and 1627 mAh g−1, respectively (Figure
S10, Supporting Information).
For the Si/C composite, a small slope was observed, as shown
in the inset of Figure 4a, which was attributed to SEI formation,
corresponding to the BET surface area analysis.[20] However, this
was not observed for the Gr@Si@C composite electrode, indi-
cating that the structure of the completely embedded Si NPs in
the 2D structured graphite and pitch-derived carbon and the sur-
face of the composite covered by pitch-derived carbon can reduce
natural SEI formation. Because of SEI formation, the ICE of the
Gr@Si@C composite was higher than that of the Si/C compos-
ite. The cycling performance of the composites at 0.5 C is shown
in Figure 4b. The Si/C composite exhibited a rapid decline in ca-
pacity in the ﬁrst few cycles because the Si/C composite ruptured
during cycling owing to volume expansion and non-homogenous
Si NP distribution in the Si/C composite. The pulverization of
the Si/C composite caused the reformation of the SEI ﬁlm over
the Si/C particles, which continuously consumed lithium ions
and the electrolyte. The thick SEI layer lead to electrode polariza-
tion arising from limitations in lithium ion transport, resulting in
poor cycling stability.[21] Moreover, these irreversible reactions re-
duced the cycling CEs, which act as indicators of electrochemical
reversibility. By contrast, the Gr@Si@C composite shows good
capacity retention: 72.2% of the original discharge capacity (1514
mAh g−1) after 100 cycles when measured at a rate of 0.5C, as the
unique structured Si-based composite turns out to be eﬀective for
stable cycling. In a longer period of cycling measured at a higher
rate of 1C, the composite preserves excellent discharge capaci-
ties, even with a high areal capacity of 4.2 mAh cm−2 (Figure S11,
Supporting Information). However, the slight capacity fading ob-
served during long-term cycling, despite the well-designed struc-
ture of the Gr@Si@C composite, can be attributed to the con-
tinuous consumption of lithium ions due to the formation SEI
during electrochemical reactions. Additionally, increased electri-
cal resistance due to poor electrical contact within the electrode
may also contribute to the capacity fading. Therefore, to ensure
long-term cycling stability of such high-capacity silicon anodes,
advancements in electrode engineering, including the develop-
ment of binders, conductive additives, and electrolytes that can
eﬀectively control expansion within the electrode, are necessary
alongside the development of active materials.
The continuous formation of the SEI layer is shown in the
charge/discharge proﬁles of the Si/C anode (Figure 4c,d). The
voltage/capacity proﬁle of the Si/C anode at the beginning of
lithiation declined, which was related to the formation of the
SEI layer. However, the Gr@Si@C anode exhibited a stable volt-
age/capacity proﬁle, which indicates that the pitch-derived car-
bon and 2D structured graphite prevented direct contact between
the Si NPs and the electrolyte and accommodated the volume ex-
pansion of the Si NPs. Furthermore, the Nyquist plots of the Si/C
and Gr@Si@C composites after diﬀerent cycles were tested, and
Small 2024, 20, 2405005
© 2024 Wiley-VCH GmbH
2405005 (5 of 9)

See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

www.advancedsciencenews.com
www.small-journal.com
Figure 4. a) Initial charge/discharge potential proﬁles at 0.1 C (1 C = 1500 mA g−1), b) cycle stability at 0.5 C, charge/discharge potential proﬁles of c)
Si/C composite, and d) Gr@Si@C composite at 0.5 C after cycling. Nyquist plots of e) Si/C composite and f) Gr@Si@C composite, g) rate performance
of composites, h) Raman spectra of 2D structured graphite and pitch-derived carbon, and i) electronic conductivity of pitch-derived carbon, 2D structured
graphite, Si/C composite, and Gr@Si@C composite.
all the impedances of the Gr@Si@C composite were lower than
those of the Si/C composite (Figure 4e,f). The semicircles can be
attributed to the resistance of the SEI layer (RSEI) and the charge-
transfer resistance (Rct).[22] The RSEI and Rct of the Si/C compos-
ite increased signiﬁcantly during cycling; in contrast, those of the
Gr@Si@C composite showed small changes after 100 cycles. In
particular, a signiﬁcant increase in the Rct of the Si/C compos-
ite was observed during cycling. The Si/C composites were de-
stroyed by the volume expansion of the Si NPs, which exposed
the Si NPs to direct contact and led to continuous reactions with
the electrolyte. This led to increased overgrowth of the SEI layer,
which obstructed the electron transfer pathways. Therefore, the
continuous formation of the SEI layer increased the values of RSEI
and Rct. Meanwhile, the Gr@Si@C composite endured repeated
severe volume expansion because of the frameworks of 2D struc-
tured graphite and the coverage of pitch-derived carbon.
The rate capabilities of the Si/C and Gr@Si@C composites
were further investigated (Figure 4g). The discharge capacities
of the Gr@Si@C electrode were 1574, 1537, 1487, 1440, 1348,
and 1240 mAh g−1 at 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, and 3 C, respectively.
These values were much higher than those of the Si/C composite,
showing a more stable rate performance as the current densities
increased. The good rate capability is closely related to the high
electronic conductivity in the electrode. To verify high rate capa-
bility, we evaluated the volume resistivity, which was lower for
the Gr@Si@C electrode than that of the Si/C composite (Figure
S12, Supporting Information). In general, electronic conductivity
depends on the crystallinity of the carbon material. Raman spec-
troscopy showed a lower ID/IG ratio for 2D structured graphite
in comparison with the pitch-derived carbon, indicating higher
graphitization and electronic conductivity (Figure 4h).[23] The re-
sults of the powder resistivity measurements showed that the
Small 2024, 20, 2405005
© 2024 Wiley-VCH GmbH
2405005 (6 of 9)
 16136829, 2024, 49, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/smll.202405005 by Gachon University, Wiley Online Library on [28/01/2026]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

www.advancedsciencenews.com
www.small-journal.com
electronic conductivity of the 2D structured graphite was higher
than that of the pitch-derived carbon (Figure 4i). Based on these
characteristics, the Gr@Si@C composite exhibited superior con-
ductivity compared to the Si/C composite, despite containing a
large amount of low-conductivity Si nanoparticles (Si NPs). This
enhanced conductivity is attributed to the high electronic con-
ductivity provided by the 2D graphite and the high density of
the composite. Additionally, the electrochemical performance of
high loading Gr@Si@C electrodes was investigated (Figure S13,
Supporting Information). The Gr@Si@C electrode, even with
areal loadings as high as 11 mAh cm−2, still delivered a simi-
lar electrochemical performance to the 4.2 mAh cm−2 level elec-
trode, suggesting the good lithium-ions and electrons transfer ki-
netics at the thick electrode.
Subsequently, the Si-based composite blended with commer-
cial graphite was adjusted to the capacity of a commercial anode
at weight ratios of 90:10, 75:25, and 60:40 to determine the poten-
tial for commercialization of the Gr@Si@C composite-blended
electrode. The graphite for the blending electrode was 10–15 μm
and had an initial capacity and CE of 338 mAh g−1 and 91.4%,
respectively (Figure S14, Supporting Information). At a graphite
and Si-based composite weight ratio of 90:10, the Si/C-blended
electrode (CG-Si/C) and Gr@Si@C composite-blended electrode
(CG-Gr@Si@C) exhibited discharge capacities of 453 and 465
mAh g−1, respectively (Table S1, Supporting Information). The
cycle performances of both blended electrodes were stable, with
79.7% and 89.5% cycle retention after 100 cycles and average
CEs of 99.4% and 99.6%, respectively. When the weight ratio was
75:25, the CG-Si/C and CG-Gr@Si@C showed discharge capac-
ities of 604 and 630 mAh g−1, respectively. The CG-Gr@Si@C
exhibited a stable cycle performance, with 78.3% cycle retention
and 99.2% average CE. The cycle retention and average CE of
the CG-Si/C decreased signiﬁcantly as the weight ratio of the
Si-based composite increased from 10% to 25%. Figure S15a,b
(Supporting Information) show the Nyquist plots of the CG-
Si/C and CG-Gr@Si@C composites with a 75:25 weight ratio af-
ter diﬀerent cycles, respectively. The resistance of the CG-Si/C
blended electrode to both RSEI and Rct was higher than that of the
CG-Gr@Si@C blended electrode. The electrode swelling of CG-
Si/C and CG-Gr@Si@C electrodes was also investigated, where
the CG-Si/C and CG-Gr@Si@C electrodes expanded 66.0% and
27.7%, respectively, after ten cycles (Figure S16, Supporting In-
formation). This indicates that accommodating the volume ex-
pansion and homogeneous distribution of the Si active material
is important for an electrode with Si NPs, despite the metallic Si
content being 10 wt.% in the electrode (25 wt.% composite). The
long-term cycle stability of CG-Gr@Si@C electrode also showed
that it maintains the structure and electrode integrity during cy-
cle (Figure S17, Supporting Information). At a 60:40 weight ra-
tio, CG-Si/C exhibited unstable cycle performance. However, CG-
Gr@Si@C still showed a high cycle retention (70.5%) and aver-
age CE (98.9%). This suggests that the structure of the Gr@Si@C
composite accommodates the volume expansion of Si NPs, which
can retain the integrity of the electrode. Therefore, the pristine
Gr@Si@C composite can homogeneously distribute Si NPs in
the electrode, eﬀectively suppressing electrode swelling, even
with large amounts of Si particles.
Their electrochemical performance was compared with that re-
ported in the literature (Table S2, Supporting Information).[24–29]
Notably, CG-Gr@Si@C exhibited better electrochemical perfor-
mance than those reported, despite its high areal mass loading
and Si content. However, the CG-Gr@Si@C electrode with a
60:40 weight ratio did not signiﬁcantly diﬀer from the pristine
Gr@Si@C composite electrode. This indicates that the blended
electrode is a simple mixture of graphite and the Gr@Si@C com-
posite, which could lead to localized swelling of the electrode.
Thus, CG-Gr@Si@C exhibited limitations when the metallic Si
content in the electrode increased above 16% (40 wt.% compos-
ite). This suggest that not only the design of novel anode active
materials but also design of electrode, such as optimization of
binder and conductive agent, are crucial in anode consisting of a
high contents of Si-based anode material. A full cell of the CG-
Gr@Si@C electrode with a 75:25 weight ratio was assembled by
pairing it with a LiNi0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2 (NCM811) cathode. The ﬁrst
charge/discharge proﬁle of the NCM811 applied in the full cell,
measured at 0.1C and the cycling performance at 0.5C in the volt-
age range of 2.5–4.2 V are shown in Figure S18 (Supporting Infor-
mation). Figure 5c shows the cycle performance of the full cell at
a current density of 0.5 C (1 C = 200 mA g−1) in the voltage range
of 2.5–4.2 V. The full cell with the CG-Gr@Si@C electrode exhib-
ited a high areal capacity of 3.5 mAh cm−2 and a high average CE
of 99.5% over 100 cycles. Figure 5d shows the development of an
anode incorporating graphite and Si-based anode material.
Currently, the speciﬁc capacity and volumetric energy den-
sity of LIBs have been improved by increasing the content of
Si-based materials in graphite-blended electrodes. However, de-
spite the growth in anode performance, commercial LIBs are
not yet able to meet the volumetric energy density requirements
of next-generation LIBs (>800 Wh L−1).[3a] Therefore, a large
amount of Si-based material in the electrode is necessary to
meet such requirements (i.e., total Si content in the electrode
>10 wt.%).[30] The Gr@Si@C composite oﬀers a potential so-
lution to this limitation because of its unique structure, which
eﬀectively accommodates the volume expansion of Si. This en-
ables the utilization of a 16 wt.% metallic Si anode material,
thereby fulﬁlling the requirements for next-generation LIBs.
Consequently, the CG-Gr@Si@C exhibited good cycle perfor-
mance in both half- and full-cell LIBs. Thus, the adoption of
the Gr@Si@C composite facilitates the development of next-
generation LIBs by addressing the intrinsic problems of Si anode
materials.
3. Conclusion
In this study, a Gr@Si@C anode composite with a unique struc-
ture was synthesized using MF. The novel anode composite is
composed of a net framework of 2D structured graphite, a dense
pitch-derived carbon outer surface, and a core pore with uni-
formly distributed Si NPs. This composite is feasible owing to
the capillary force of 2D structured graphite and the pitch con-
version into a viscous liquid during carbonization. The proposed
Gr@Si@C composite anode can eﬀectively accommodate the
volume expansion of Si through Si NPs comprising 40 wt.% of
the composite, thereby providing stable electrochemical perfor-
mance. Moreover, owing to the uniform distribution of Si NPs
in the electrode, the Gr@Si@C blended electrode with commer-
cial graphite exhibited high cycle retention and high areal capac-
ity. Therefore, the novel Gr@Si@C composite can signiﬁcantly

Figure 5. a) First charge/discharge potential proﬁles at 0.1 C, b) cycle stability at 0.5 C, c) cycle stability at 0.5 C of the CG-Gr@Si@C full-cell paired with
an NCM811 cathode, and d) schematic illustration of the relationship between capacity and total Si content in graphite-blended electrode employing
Gr@Si@C composite. Evolution of anode in LIBs from graphite to Si-based anode material for high speciﬁc capacity.
improve the feasibility of using Si-based anode materials with
high loading levels in LIBs.
4. Experimental Section
Gr@Si@C Composite Preparation:
The Gr@Si@C composite was syn-
thesized using the MF process (AMS-Mini, Hosokawa Micron Corpora-
tion, Japan) at 6500 rpm for 5 h, with 2D structured graphite to Si NP (Sil-
icon Nanopowder, Nanostructured & Amorphous Materials Inc.) to pitch
(softening point 160 °C) weight ratios of 30:40:60. Then, under an argon
atmosphere (>99.9%), the Gr@Si@pitch composite was carbonized at
1000 °C for 3 h to obtain the Gr@Si@C composites as a function of the
weight ratios 30:40:30. The Si/C composite was prepared using a plane-
tary mixer at 1500 rpm for 2 min repeated three times for a total of 6 min.
The Si/pitch mixture was then carbonized using the same method used
for the Gr@Si@C composite.
Material Characterization:
The morphologies of the synthesized com-
posites were observed using a ﬁeld-emission scanning electron mi-
croscopy (FE-SEM, JSM-7600F, JEOL, Japan). The cross section of the an-
ode composite was characterized using focused ion beam-scanning elec-
tron microscopy (Helios G4 UC, Thermo Fisher Scientiﬁc) with energy-
dispersive X-ray spectroscopy and TEM (JEM-2200FS, JEOL, Japan). The
crystal structure properties of the materials were investigated using
an XRD (D8 Advance, Bruker, Germany, USA) with a Cu K𝛼radiation
(𝜆= 1.542 Å). The amounts of graphite and carbon in the compos-
ites were determined by TGA (SDT 650, TA Instruments). Tap density
was measured using a tap density meter (TAP-2S, Konenano). The sur-
face area was measured using the BET method (TriStar II 3020, Mi-
cromeritics). The volume resistivity of the electrode sheet was investi-
gated using a 46-multipoint probe system (RM2610, Hioki Crop.) at room
temperature.
Electrochemical Measurements:
To investigate the electrochemical per-
formance of the Si/C and Gr@Si@C composite anodes in LIBs, a slurry
composed of the active material, carbon black, and polyacrylic acid binder
(Mv = 450000, Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis) was homogeneously distributed
in distilled water at a mass fraction of 80:10:10. This was then cast onto
the copper foil and vacuum-dried at 100 °C for 3 h. The loading mass
of active materials was ≈2.5 mg cm−2 for both the Si/C and Gr@Si@C
composites. For the electrodes blended with commercial graphite (G49),
a CG-composite slurry composed of an active material, carbon black, a car-
boxymethyl cellulose (CMC) binder, and a styrene-butadiene rubber (SBR)
binder was mixed in distilled water at a mass fraction of 96:1:1:2. The ac-
tive material loading mass of each blended electrode is shown in Table
S1 (Supporting Information). Coin-type half cells (2032R type) were as-
sembled in a dry room using the as-prepared electrodes, Li metal as the
counter electrode, a polyoleﬁn membrane as the separator, and lithium
hexaﬂuorophosphate (LiPF6) (1.15 m) in ethylene carbonate-ethylmethyl
carbonate-dimethyl carbonate (2:4:4 vol%), containing ﬂuoroethylene car-
bonate (12.5 wt.%) as the electrolyte.
Electrochemical tests were performed using a multichannel potentio-
galvanostat (TOSCAT3000, Toyo Systems). The half-cells were cycled at
0.1 C between 0.01 and 1.5 V for the initial cycle, and 0.5 C for the follow-
ing 100 cycles at 25 °C. To measure the rate capability, the current density
was changed from 0.1 to 3 C. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy
measurements were conducted in the frequency range of 7–10 MHz at
20 mV after diﬀerent cycles. For the full cell, a slurry composed of the ac-
tive material, carbon black, CMC, and SBR binder was mixed in distilled
water at a mass fraction of 95:1.1:1.3:2.6. The active material was an elec-
trode blended with G49 and Gr@Si@C composites at a mass fraction of
Small 2024, 20, 2405005
© 2024 Wiley-VCH GmbH
2405005 (8 of 9)
 16136829, 2024, 49, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/smll.202405005 by Gachon University, Wiley Online Library on [28/01/2026]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

www.advancedsciencenews.com
www.small-journal.com
75:25. A coin-type full cell was assembled in a dry room using the prepared
NCM811 as the counter electrode. The N/P ratio, deﬁned as the total ca-
pacity ratio between the anode and cathode, was set to 1.1. The full-cells
were cycled at 0.1 C (1 C = 200 mA g−1) between 2.5 and 4.2 V for the
initial cycle, and 0.5 C for the following 100 cycles at 25 °C.

[SOURCE: A multifunctional network binder enables stable and high performance of silicon-based anode in lithium-ion battery.pdf]

Journal of Power Sources 574 (2023) 233159
Available online 12 May 2023
0378-7753/© 2023 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
A multifunctional network binder enables stable and high performance of 
silicon-based anode in lithium-ion battery 
Hyunjung Park a,1, Seungmin Han b,d,1, Heetae Tak a,b, Junghwan Kim b,c, Kwangchul Roh b, 
Dae Soo Jung b, Taeseup Song d,***, Patrick Joohyun Kim c,**, Junghyun Choi b,* 
a Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Chosun University, Gwangju, 61452, South Korea 
b Energy Storage Materials Center, Korea Institute of Ceramic Engineering and Technology, Jinju, 52851, South Korea 
c Department of Applied Chemistry, Kyungpook National University, Daegu, 41566, South Korea 
d Department of Energy Engineering, Hanyang University, Seoul, 133-791, South Korea   
H I G H L I G H T S  
• The introduction of MNB optimized the ability of the binder by synergistic effect. 
• The strong binding matrix with Si particles effectively inhibited volume expansion. 
• The functional bridges and recovery properties of MNB alleviated damage of electrode. 
• The enhanced dispersion stability and wettability improved electrical/ion paths. 
• Superior electrochemical performances were represented in Si-based electrode. 
A B S T R A C T   
Rechargeable lithium-ion batteries with high energy density have attracted attention as a means of solving environmental problems. Silicon (Si) has been considered 
as one of the most promising anode materials due to its high theoretical capacity of 3579 mAh g−1 (Li15Si4). However, the enormous volume change of Si occurs 
during lithiation/delithiation process, which seriously deteriorates the mechanical/electrochemical stability of Si anodes. To address these inherent problems, it is of 
importance to develop a functional binder capable of reducing the volume variation of Si anodes. In this work, we first design a new binder system by employing a 
Multifunctional Network Binder (MNB) to synergistically improve the electrochemical stability and performances of Si-based electrodes. The introduction of MNB 
into the Li-PAA-based electrode system constructs a strong binding matrix through abundant functional bridges. In addition, the MNB with high dispersion stability 
improves the ionic conductivity of Si-based electrodes. Owing to these synergistic effects of Li-PAA/MNB binder system, the volume expansion of Si-based electrodes 
was significantly suppressed, contributing to the excellent Coulombic efficiency (99.9%) and capacity retention (87% after 100 cycles).   
1. Introduction 
Environmental problems such as air pollution and global warming 
caused by CO2 emissions have significantly impacted on our lives [1,2]. 
These issues highlight the importance of utilizing renewable energy in 
technology [3]. In particular, the research and development for 
renewable energy devices (e.g., solar cells, fuel cells and rechargeable 
cells) are being actively carried out [4–6]. Among these promising de­
vices, rechargeable Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have attracted attention 
in a variety of fields such as portable devices, energy storage devices, 
electric vehicles (EVs), etc., due to their high energy density and long 
lifespan [7–9]. 
Graphite has been widely used as an anode material for LIBs due to 
its stable layered structure, which can effectively intercalate lithium 
ions (Li+) [10]. However, graphite has a limited capacity of 372 mAh 
g−1 (LiC6) which restricts the design of LIBs with high energy density 
[11,12]. 
Silicon (Si) has been considered as one of the most promising alter­
native materials to graphite, with a theoretical capacity of 3579 mAh 
g−1 (Li15Si4) roughly ten times higher than the theoretical capacity of 
* Corresponding author. 
** Corresponding author. 
*** Corresponding author. 
E-mail addresses: tssong@hanyang.ac.kr (T. Song), pjkim@knu.ac.kr (P.J. Kim), jchoi@kicet.re.kr (J. Choi).   
1 These authors contributed equally to this work. 
Contents lists available at ScienceDirect 
Journal of Power Sources 
journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/jpowsour 
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpowsour.2023.233159 
Received 27 February 2023; Received in revised form 22 April 2023; Accepted 4 May 2023   

Journal of Power Sources 574 (2023) 233159
2
conventional graphite-based electrodes [13,14]. However, Si-based 
electrodes undergo an extremely huge volume expansion (~300%) 
during the lithiation/delithiation process [15–17]. The resulting volume 
variation breaks/reforms the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer 
formed on the surface of Si electrodes during repetitive cycles, leading to 
the formation of unstable SEI, the loss of electrochemically available 
lithium ions, the electrolyte depletion, and the rapid capacity fading 
[18–20]. 
In order to resolve these problems, researchers have conducted a 
number of studies on the design of polymeric battery binders. Basically, 
battery binders play important roles in improving inter-particle cohe­
sion and strengthening mechanical matrix [21,22]. For example, Poly 
(vinylidene fluoride) (PVdF) binder, typically used in anode electrode, 
has methyl groups, i.e., (-CH2) and –CF2 groups, which are 
cross-polymerized [23]. However, it is not suitable for Si-based anodes 
due its weak van der Waals interaction and binding force with Si par­
ticles [24,25]. Poly (acrylic acid) (PAA), employed as an aqueous binder, 
has abundant carboxyl groups (-COOH) which provide strong binding 
bridges with hydroxyl groups (-OH) on the surface of Si via hydrogen 
bonding [26–28]. When PAA binders were employed in the Si-based 
anode, the pulverization of electrodes can be effectively suppressed by 
forming a polymeric network [29]. Although there is an improvement in 
terms of mechanical/chemical properties, PAA binders consume a 
number of lithium ions during the SEI formation process, which results 
in low initial Coulombic efficiency (C.E.) and serious capacity fading in 
Si-based anodes [30]. 
To further enhance the cycle reversibility of PAA binder-based 
electrodes, a lithiation strategy has been introduced to alter the chem­
ical structure of PAA. The lithiated-PAA (Li-PAA) binder can afford extra 
lithium ions to reduce the irreversible reactions occurring in the elec­
trode [31,32]. The Li-PAA binder enhances the initial C.E. by compen­
sating for the loss of lithium ions caused by the continuous 
cracking/reformation of SEI during cycling [30,33]. In this regard, 
Li-PAA has a great potential to improve the electrochemical perfor­
mances of Si-based electrodes. Despite these fascinating improvements, 
the Li-PAA binder still has material problems in that protons in carboxyl 
groups are partially replaced with lithium ions (-COOH → -COOLi) 
during the lithiation of PAA, which weakens the binding properties of 
Si-based electrodes [34]. In addition, Li-PAA binders have difficulties in 
controlling volume changes due to the mechanical defects [31]. 
In this work, we first design a new binder system by employing a 
Multifunctional Network Binder (MNB) to complement the inherent 
weaknesses of Li-PAA binder and improve the electrochemical perfor­
mances of Si-based electrode. The Li-PAA/MNB binder system form long 
polymer bridges between Si particles, which can construct a sturdy 
scaffold and be self-repaired by hydrogen bonds [35,36]. Additionally, 
the stretched functional group (-COO-) of Li-PAA/MNB provides a strong 
binding matrix via hydrogen bonding interaction with hydroxyl groups 
on the Si surface [36]. The newly designed binder system successfully 
suppressed the volume expansion through the robust binding matrix and 
thus led to the superior cycling performance of Si-based electrodes. 
2. Experimental 
2.1. Chemicals 
The Si alloy as an anode material was purchased from MK Electron. 
The Poly (acrylic acid) (PAA, average Mv = 450,000) was purchased 
from Sigma-Aldrich. The LiOH⋅H2O (Mw = 41.97 g mol−1, 99.0%, 
SAMCHUN) was chosen for binder lithiation. The carboxymethyl cel­
lulose (CMC) used as a useful additive binder was purchased from 
DAICEL. The styrene butadiene rubber (SBR) emulsion was used simply 
to increase adhesion to the copper current collector. The PAA, Li-PAA 
and Li-PAA/MNB binder solutions were prepared by dissolving the 
DIW (Fig. S1). 
2.2. Electrode preparation 
The Si alloy, conductive agent (carbon black) and aqueous binder 
solution (10 wt% PAA or 10 wt% Li-PAA) were prepared at mass ratio of 
8 : 1: 1 in order to make the slurry. The binder ratio of the Li-PAA/MNB 
slurry was adjusted to a weight ratio of 7 : 2: 1 for the Li-PAA solution, 
MNB solution, and SBR. All these slurries were mixed via planetary 
mixer (ARE-310, THINKY) at 2000 rpm for 5 min. All the slurry was 
coated on bare copper foil by using doctor blade. The coated electrodes 
primarily were dried in oven at 80 ◦C and additionally in vacuum oven 
100 ◦C for 3 h. 
2.3. Characterization 
Fourier transform infrared (FTIR, IS50, Thermo Fisher) spectra of 
binder film was carried out to investigate the organic functional groups 
in range of 400–4000 cm−1. The dispersion stability was characterized 
for 24 h using Turbiscan (AGS, Formulaction). The rheological behavior 
of the dispersions was conducted at a shear rate of 1–100 s −1 by the 
rheometer (DV2T, BROOKFIELD). Mechanical properties such as the 
modulus, hardness and elastic recovery of the binder are investigated by 
Nano indenter (HM2000, FISCHERSCOPE). Contact angle was observed 
to measure the hydrophilicity of Si-based pellet by Contact angle 
measuring instrument (Phoenix300 Touch, SEO). 
2.4. Electrochemical measurements 
The electrochemical stabilities were evaluated in Li-metal coin half- 
cells (2032 R type) assembled from a Si-based slurry used different 
binders on Cu foil and a Li-metal as a counter electrode. The Li-metal 
was physically attached to a stainless spacer and used with a LIB sepa­
rator film (Dorey, SETELA). The electrolyte was 1.15 M lithium hexa­
fluorophosphate (LiPF6) in a mixture of ethylene carbonate, ethylmethyl 
carbonate and dimethyl carbonate (EC-EMC-DMC, 2 : 4: 4 vol%) with 
vinylene carbonate (VC, 1 wt%) and fluoroethylene carbonate (FEC, 
12.5 wt%). A galvanostatic charge-discharge test was carried out in a 
potential window of 0.01–1.5 V vs. Li/Li+. Electrochemical impedance 
spectroscopy (EIS, VSP, Biologic, Seyssinet-Pariset) was investigated in 
the frequency range of 250 kHz–10 mHz. For coin full-cell evaluation, 
the Si-based electrode was paired NCM622 as a cathode where N/P ratio 
was 1.1. In the galvanostatic mode, charge-discharge measurement was 
performed at 0.3C with voltage cut-off between 2.8 V and 4.2 V. 
3. Result and discussions 
Fig. 1 illustrates how the Li-PAA/MNB binder offers synergistic ef­
fects on Si-based electrodes. The volume expansion resulted by the 
lithiation/delithiation of Si creates huge stresses in the Si-based elec­
trode. In case of the typical Si electrode with either PVdF or PAA binder, 
irreversible reaction (i.e., trapping of lithium ions) and electrode pul­
verization would seriously occur, which aggravates the stability of 
electrodes [27]. As to the Si electrode with Li-PAA/MNB, a sturdy 
scaffold established from chains of MNB binder effectively maintains a 
network between Si particles and binders [35]. Furthermore, Li-PAA can 
strengthen the bonding between expanded Si particles since functional 
bridges provide hydrogen bonding sites (-COO-) that can maintain 
strong bonds with the hydroxyl groups on the Si surface [36]. The 
binding matrix strengthened by Li-PAA/MNB not only reduces electrode 
damages but also enables fast lithium-ion diffusion. These synergistic 
contributions of Li-PAA and MNB can significantly boost the electro­
chemical performances in Si-based anodes. 
To investigate changes in chemical bonds and functional groups of 
the PAA binder after lithiation and adding the MNB, FT-IR spectra were 
obtained from as-prepared samples as shown in Fig. 2(a). The PAA 
shows a broad peak centered at ~1690 cm−1, because of the C=O 
stretching band of –COOH. After lithiation of the PAA, a new peak of 
H. Park et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    

Journal of Power Sources 574 (2023) 233159
3
–COOLi appeared at ~1547 cm−1, while the COOH peak disappeared. 
The sample of the Li-PAA/MNB exhibits the same spectra as the Li-PAA, 
which indicates that there is no such a chemical bond or cross-linking 
between the Li-PAA and the MNB. The load-displacement curves of 
three samples were achieved by nanoindentation to study mechanical 
properties (Fig. 2(b)). According to Eq. (1−3), hardness, elastic 
modulus, and elastic recovery can be calculated from the graphs.

where Vs: Poisson’s Ratio of Sample, Vi: Poisson’s Ratio of indenter, Er: 
converted elastic modulus of press-fitting contact surface, Ei: modulus of 
elasticity of indenter, C: compliance of the contact surface, Ap: contact 
projected area. All the values are summarized in Table 1. As a result, the 
hardness of the Li-PAA/MNB was ~1.449 GPa larger than those of the 
others, while the elastic modulus was ~12.837 GPa lower than ~19.066 
GPa of the Li-PAA. It should be noted that the elastic recovery of the Li- 
PAA/MNB is 52.94% much higher than those of the others. This implies 
that the Li-PAA/MNB binder system can be beneficial to the Si-based 
electrode that experiences the large volume change (~300%) upon 
charging/discharging, because it prevents a loss of electrical contacts 
between Si particles and gives improved electrode integrity [37]. 
A rheological analysis of an electrode slurry is essential for a mass 
production of the battery. It is commonly accepted that the slurry should 
possess a viscosity in the range of 1000–5000 cP at a shear rate of 1–100 
s−1 for a good dispersion of electrode components and a uniform coating 
on a current collector. Fig. 3(a) shows a logarithmic plot of apparent 
viscosity. Both dispersions of the Li-PAA and Li-PAA/MNB show a 
desirable ‘shear thinning’ behavior due to a breakage of solid materials 
under the applied shear force. But the viscosity of the Li-PAA/MNB was 
lowered from 5000 to 2000 cP at shear rate of 1 s−1. This is because well- 
dispersion of the solid components by a repulsion force between

4
carboxylate (COO−) and a swelling of the MNB molecules in water. A 
dispersion property of a suspension differs in terms of time by a desta­
bilization such as particle settlement and agglomeration. A turbiscan 
method can evaluate a dispersion stability by irradiating light into a 
suspension and detecting scattered light through solids [38]. A turbiscan 
stability index (TSI) is a sum of backscattering light as a function of time, 
which indicates the lower the TSI value is, the higher the dispersion 
stability is [39]. As shown in Fig. 3(b), the Li-PAA/MNB slurry shows a 
lower TSI value of 2.52 than 1.03 of the Li-PAA after 24 h. This means 
the formal one has a long-term dispersion stability that could prevent 
particle agglomeration, flocculation, and sedimentation during a slurry 
casting process. 
Fig. 4(a–b) shows a wetting property of aqueous Si-based dispersions 
with the Li-PAA and the Li-PAA/MNB. This is one of the important pa­
rameters for the slurry casting process, because the smaller the contact 
angle is, the better wettability the slurry has. Contact angles between 
water and electrodes are 48.1◦for the Li PAA and 41.8◦for the Li-PAA/ 
MNB. This indicates that the Li-PAA/MNB slurry has a more hydrophilic 
property due to the more polar carboxyl and hydroxyl groups of the 
MNB. Photo images of as-prepared electrodes were taken to show an 
electrode condition right after a slurry casting. The Li-PAA-based elec­
trode shows a rough surface due to the agglomeration of solid compo­
nents such as Si, carbon black, and binder (Fig. 4(c)), while a surface of 
the Li-PAA/MNB-based electrode is smooth without noticeable floccu­
lation (Fig. 4(d)). 
EIS technique was used to study an effect of different binder systems 
on electric/ironic conductivity in the electrode after 24 h aging for 
electrolyte permeation. To investigate ionic resistance (Warburg diffu­
sion, ZW) of the samples, Warburg impedance was measured in the 
frequency range of 1000–0.001 Hz using a symmetric cell as shown in 
Fig. 5(a). To calculate a Warburg diffusion coefficient (σ, unit Ω⋅s−1/2), 
the data were transformed to the real part of resistance (Zre) as a 
Fig. 3. Characterization of aqueous dispersions prepared with Si nanoparticles, and Li-PAA, and Li-PAA/MNB binders. (a) rheological test, (b) Turbiscan stabil­
ity test. 
Fig. 4. Digital photo images of (a–b) contact angles between water and electrodes and (c–d) the surface of as-prepared electrodes after slurry casting.  
H. Park et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    

Journal of Power Sources 574 (2023) 233159
5
function of ω−1/2 (Fig. 5(b)), and then the σ was obtained from the value 
of the Zre at ω = 1 based on Eq. (4). Finally, DLi+ was determined ac­
cording to Eq. (5). 
ZW = σ̅̅s
√
(4)  
DLi+ =
R2T2
2A4n4F4C2σ2
(5)  
where s is a frequency, A is the surface area of the electrode, n is the 
number of transferred electrons, C is the concentration of lithium-ion, 
and σ is the Warburg diffusion coefficient. All the measured values are 
summarized in Table 2. As a result, σ is the lowest for Li-PAA/MNB at 
243.31 Ω • s−1/2 and DLi+ is the highest for Li-PAA/MNB at 1.95 ×
10−13cm2 • s−1, indicating that Li-PAA/MNB is the best in term of ionic 
conductivity. This means that Li-PAA/MNB has the highest wettability 
and the electrolyte penetrates deep into the electrode, increasing the 
contact interface between the electrolyte and the electrode, thereby 
showing the best ionic conductivity. Also, due to the binder of MNB, the 
homogeneous electrode and dense microstructure have an effect on 
making better ionic conductivity. Fig. 5(c) shows a change of charge 
transfer resistance (Rct) of three samples. The Rct values of PAA, Li-PAA 
and Li-PAA/MNB are 2049.3 Ω, 531.6 Ω and 455.2 Ω, respectively. The 
Rct of the Si-based electrode with the Li-PAA is much smaller than that of 
one with the PAA, which is attributed to the improved Li+ ionic con­
ductivity by PAA binder lithiation. It is worth noting that the electrode 
with the Li-PAA/MNB shows the smallest Rct than those of the others. 
This might be due to the better dispersibility of the Si and carbon black 
particles in the electrode, which improves electric/ionic pathways upon 
galvanic reactions. 
Fig. 6(a) shows initial galvanostatic charge-discharge profiles of Si- 
based anodes with the PAA, the Li-PAA, and the Li-PAA/MNB in the 
voltage window of 0.01–1.5 V vs. Li/Li+. They give charge/discharge 
capacities of 1275/1083, 1331/1173, and 1359/1182 mAh g−1. To 
demonstrate lithiation/delitiation process corresponding to galvano­
static profiles, the cyclic voltammetry (CV) test of all the half-cell sam­
ples was conducted at scanning rate of 0.1 mV s−1 (Fig. S2). After the 1st 
cycle, the reduction peaks corresponding to lithiation process of Si was 
observed at 0.11 V for PAA and 0.14 V for Li-PAA and Li-PAA/MNB. 
Subsequently, the oxidation peaks of all the samples are represented 
around 0.5 V, which are attributed to delithiation process. Based on 
these results, we can confirm that there is no noticeable difference in the 
lithiation/delithiation process of each binder. Fig. 6(b) exhibits the cycle 
performance up to 100 cycles at a current density of 0.3C-rate. The coin 
half-cell using Li-PAA and Li-PAA/MNB exhibited the higher initial C.E. 
of 88.1% and 87.0% Li-PAA than half-cell using the PAA (84.9%), which 
means that the irreversible problem of PAA has been improved. Mean­
while, capacity retentions are 79.4% for the PAA, 85.0% for the Li-PAA, 
and 87.1% for the Li-PAA/MNB. The reason for the improved cyclability 
of the Li-PAA/MNB is mainly due to the existence of the MNB that en­
ables the higher hardness and elastic modulus of the electrode. Fig. 6(c) 
shows a rate capability at various current densities. Capacity retentions 
at a high 2C-rate are 559 mAh g−1 (49%) for the PAA, 780 mAh g−1 
(66%) for the Li-PAA, and 859 mAh g−1 (72%) for the Li-PAA/MNB. We 
also evaluate a coin full-cell consisting of the NCM622-based cathode 
and the Si-based anodes with three binder systems. Fig. 6(d) shows the 
first charge-discharge profiles in the voltage window of 2.8–4.2 V vs. Li/ 
Li+. The PAA, the Li-PAA, and the Li-PAA/MNB give charge/discharge 
capacities of 2.08/2.06, 2.10/2.08 and 2.13/2.17 mAh cm−2, respec­
tively. Fig. 6(e) shows a cycle performance over 200 cycles at a current 
density of a 0.3C-rate. Capacity retentions are 1.32, 1.55, and 1.65 mAh 
cm−2. Voltage profiles are normal without noticeable malfunction even 
after 200 cycles (Fig. S3). As a result, the half and full cells containing 
the Li-PAA/MNB binder system show the best electrochemical 
properties. 
The change of electrode thickness test was conducted to investigate 
swelling behavior of electrodes (Fig. 7(a)). The cross-sectional SEM 
images was also presented to clearly show the thickness change after 1st 
lithiation/delithiation (Fig. S4). After 1st lithiation, the measured elec­
trode volume changes were 51% and 119% for PAA and Li-PAA binder. 
The severe volume expansion in electrode using Li-PAA binder was 
triggered since partially exchanged functional groups like -COOLi 
weakened the binding ability of PAA. On the other hand, the electrode 
added MNB exhibited highly suppressed volume change (34%) after 1st 
lithiation. This indicates that the polymer linkage formed by the binder 
chain of MNB effectively inhibited the volume expansion of Si particles 
during lithiation process. Furthermore, the Li-PAA/MNB electrode was 
represented lowest swelling ratio of 7% after 1st delithiation. This result 
was ascribed to the excellent recovery property of Li-PAA/MNB binder 
system which is shown in Fig. 2(b) and Table 1. The hydrogen bridges of 
MNB are conducive to form a flexible and strong binding matrix between 
Si particles and binder. This matrix enables to repair the extended Si 
particles due to volume expansion. Given that MNB effectively 
controlled pulverization of Si, the improved swelling behaviors of Li- 
PAA/MNB electrode demonstrate the high cycling stability as shown 
in Fig. 6. In order to identify the degradation of electrode, the EIS was 
analyzed after cycling process (Fig. 7(b)). The electrical contact (RS) and 
SEI resistance (RSEI) of cells using binders were observed through the 
Nyquist plots. The cell using Li-PAA/MNB binder exhibited the lowest RS 
(3.1 Ω). It is inferred that the loss of electrical contact could be mini­
mized owing to its superior swelling behaviors as represented in Fig. 7 
(a). The enhanced electrical contact facilitates electron transportation 
and prevents electrode degradation. Also, the RSEI of cell showed how 
effectively the binder improved the degradation of the electrode. The Li- 
Fig. 5. EIS measurements of (a) symmetric cells, (b) a real part of resistance vs. frequency plot attributed Warburg impedance of symmetric cells, and (c) half cells 
after aging.

H. Park et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    

Journal of Power Sources 574 (2023) 233159
7
PAA/MNB binder acts as not only a SEI stabilizer compensating for 
irreversible loss of lithium ions but also effective ion-conducting 
network allowing fast lithium-ion diffusion. Therefore, the cell using 
Li-PAA/MNB binder displayed the smallest semicircle, which represents 
the lowest RSEI at the high frequency range. Besides, Fig. S5 exhibited 
the top-view SEM images of electrode surface using PAA, Li-PAA and Li- 
PAA/MNB binder. After 100 cycles, the electrode of Li-PAA/MNB had 
homogeneous surface with narrow cracks compared to other electrodes, 
which is attributed to excellent binding network ability. All these results 
suggest that stabilized properties from the synergistic effect of MNB are 
advantageous in designing highly durable electrodes. 
4. Conclusion 
In this work, we first designed a new binder system to provide syn­
ergistic effects of each Li-PAA and MNB on Si-based electrodes. The 
various functional groups and binding bridges of the Li-PAA/MNB 
binder facilitate strong bonding interaction between Si particles and 
form a robust scaffold within the Si-based electrode, which improved the 
structural integrity of Si-based electrodes (as evidenced by a lower 
swelling ratio of 7% after 1st cycle). In addition, the enhanced electro­
lyte wettability of Si-based electrodes assisted by Li-PAA/MNB binder 
resulted in excellent cycle performances and rate retention (72% at a 
high 2C-rate). These results clearly demonstrated that Li-PAA/MNB not 
only stabilizes the structure of electrodes but only improves the elec­
trochemical kinetics and performances of Si anodes. Our attempt to 
tailor the binder system using functional polymers would address the 
fundamental problems of Si anodes and initiate further research on 
battery binders in the near future.

[SOURCE: A new approach to stabilize the electrochemical performance of Li metal batteries through the structure alteration of CNT scaffolds.pdf]
