Carbon 203 (2023) 426–435
Available online 4 December 2022
0008-6223/© 2022 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
A new approach to stabilize the electrochemical performance of Li metal 
batteries through the structure alteration of CNT scaffolds 
Junghwan Kim a,b, Junghyun Choi b,*, Patrick Joohyun Kim a,** 
a Department of Applied Chemistry, Kyungpook National University, Daegu, 41566, South Korea 
b Energy Storage Materials Center, Korea Institute of Ceramic Engineering and Technology, Jinju, 52851, South Korea  
A B S T R A C T   
Recently, interest in Li-metal batteries (LMBs) has been revived because the high specific capacity and lowest operating potential of Li-metal anodes help in resolving 
the existing issues of conventional Li-ion batteries. Unfortunately, the main problems associated with poor coulombic efficiency and dendritic Li formation impede 
the use of Li-metal anodes for commercial batteries. Among various strategies to address these intrinsic challenges of Li-metal anodes, the approach to utilize 3D 
current collectors has shown attractive results in terms of electrochemical stability. Herein, we assessed two different carbon nanotubes (e.g., double-walled 
nanotubes (DWNTs) and multi-walled nanotubes (MWNTs) scaffolds to study how the structure of carbon-based current collectors affects the coulombic effi­
ciency and electrochemical stability of LMBs. As MWNTs have a more favorable structure for the reversible storage/release of Li-ion than DWNTs, they facilitate 
uniform and stable Li deposition over the entire surface of the MWNT matrix, thereby diminishing the growth of sharp dendritic Li. This resulting effect directly 
contributes to the stable operation of Li-metal cells at high current densities. Moreover, it has shown a remarkable improvement in electrochemical stability when the 
MWNT current collector was applied to the anode-free cell using a LiCoO2 cathode. The MWNT/LiCoO2 cell exhibited excellent cycle retention (97% at 50 cycles) 
with a high coulombic efficiency (99%). This work suggests that the material of the current collectors plays an apparently important role in the electrochemical 
stability of LMBs.   
1. Introduction 
To mitigate the effects of the oil crisis and climate on our daily lives, 
developing greener and more cost-efficient energy resources is essential. 
Among the candidates that show the capability to meet the worldwide 
demands, rechargeable lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are considered as the 
most viable and reliable energy storage systems for realizing net-zero 
emissions [1–3]. The current electric vehicle battery uses a 
graphite-anode and Li-containing compounds (such as NCA, NCM, and 
LiFePO4) as the cathode, both of which have limited energy density to 
travel long distance [2,4,5]. In order to deal with unavoidable chal­
lenges and improve the energy density, many battery researchers have 
aimed at developing practical battery electrodes with high capacity, 
excellent rate-capability, and long lifespan [6–11]. 
Metallic lithium (Li) has attracted much attention for its excellent 
theoretical capacity of 3860 mAh g−1 and the lowest reduction potential 
of −3.04 V (vs. standard hydrogen electrode) [12–14]. This physical 
feature enables the energy density enhancement of LIBs if conventional 
anodes are replaced with Li anode. However, several major obstacles 
associated with irreversible electrochemical reactions and safety 
hazards originated by unfavorable byproducts (e.g., Li dendrite growth) 
hinder the pragmatic use of pure Li-metal in commercial batteries [15, 
16]. These problems have been mitigated by several strategies, 
including forming a passivation layer over the anode [17–19], opti­
mizing the organic electrolyte with additives [20–22], and utilizing 
three-dimensional (3D) current collectors [23–26]. 
Basically, Li dendrites tend to grow when electrodes cannot effi­
ciently accommodate Li-ion species within their structures. This phe­
nomenon typically occurs when the electrode experiences high actual 
current densities. The representative approach for minimizing side re­
actions associated with dendritic Li formation is to control the actual 
current density of the electrodes by employing a 3D current collector on 
behalf of a typical Cu current collector. Yang et al. previously demon­
strated that 3D current collectors are capable of accommodating the 
volume expansion induced by Li deposition/dissolution as well as 
reducing the local current density applied to the electrodes, leading to 
excellent cycle stability and electrode polarization [23]. 
Among the several materials used for constructing 3D-structured 
materials, carbon nanotubes (CNTs) have been intensively studied for 
LIB systems [27,28]. The CNT-based electrodes have presented decent 
* Corresponding author. 
** Corresponding author. 
E-mail addresses: jchoi@kicet.re.kr (J. Choi), pjkim@knu.ac.kr (P.J. Kim).  
Contents lists available at ScienceDirect 
Carbon 
journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/carbon 
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2022.11.101 
Received 25 August 2022; Received in revised form 23 November 2022; Accepted 30 November 2022   

Carbon 203 (2023) 426–435
427
performances in terms of energy density and cycle stability. However, it 
is hard to replace the convention anode system, i.e., graphite, with 
CNTs, due to the technical hurdle to achieve high volumetric energy 
density of CNT-based electrodes. For this reason, many studies have 
been more focused on utilizing CNTs as electrode additives, in order to 
utilize the excellent conductivity, large surface area, and strong me­
chanical strength of CNTs [6,29,30]. Besides the approach for LIB ap­
plications, CNTs have also shown promising performances for Li-metal 
batteries (LMBs). Salvatierra et al. reported that a lithiated multi-walled 
carbon nanotube (Li-MWNT) film plays a role as a Li diffusion interface 
to regulate the intensive Li-ion flux [31]. Deng et al. constructed phys­
ical batteries that extends the lifespan of LMBs [32]. Zhao et al. also 
demonstrated that 3D-structured carbons can extend the cycle life of 
LMBs [33]. However, relatively less attention has been paid to under­
stand the structure effect of carbons on the electrochemical reversibility 
of Li deposition/dissolution. 
In this article, we assessed two different CNT scaffolds as the current 
collectors to explore how the structure of CNT affects the homogeneity 
of Li deposition and thus enhances the electrochemical stability of LMBs. 
Double-walled carbon nanotube (DWNT) and MWNT scaffolds were 
fabricated through vacuum filtration to eliminate side effects from 
polymeric binders. The main difference between DWNT and MWNT lies 
in the number of walls which typically play roles as intercalation sites. 
Since 3D-interconnected CNT scaffolds have decent mechanical and 
electrical properties, these can be fabricated as ultralight and thin cur­
rent collectors for various energy storage devices [34–37]. When each 
prepared CNT scaffold was evaluated for LIBs, the MWNT electrodes 
showed better electrochemical performances than the DWNT electrodes. 
In addition, the Li-metal cell with the MWNT current collector exhibited 
more stable cycle performances at both low and high areal current 
densities than that with the DWNT current collector. This performance is 
attributed to the reversible storage/release of Li-ions between the walls 
of MWNTs, which facilitates uniform and stable Li deposition. In addi­
tion to these favorable features, the 3D-structured MWNT scaffolds 
accommodate excess Li within the void spaces of their entangled ar­
chitecture, which significantly reduces the volume change during 
vigorous Li migration. Overall, the behavior of Li deposition/dissolution 
was found to largely depend on the structure of the carbon current 
collectors. 
2. Experiment 
2.1. Preparation of CNT scaffolds 
6 mg DWNT powder (or MWNT powder) was added into an aqueous 
solution of sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS; 0.5 wt%) and dispersed 
through the SDS solution via a direct sonication process (250 W, 1 h). 
The dispersed DWNT (or MWNT) solution was filtered through a mixed 
cellulose acetate (MCE) membrane using a vacuum filtration technique. 
The residue of SDS was then removed with sufficient deionized water. 
The filtered DWNT (or MWNT) was dried overnight at 50 ◦C in the 
convection oven and peeled from the MCE membrane. The DWNT film 
(or MWNT film) was further dried in a vacuum oven at 120 ◦C for 3 h. 
Before the battery assembly, the completely dried DWNT film (or MWNT 
film) was cut into a circular disc with 12 mm diameter using a manual 
punching cutter. The punched DWNT (or MWNT) was employed as 3D 
current collectors for LMBs. The average thickness and areal mass 
loading of both punched DWNT and punched MWNT are around 11 μm 
and 0.346 mg cm−2, respectively. 
Fig. 1. Schematic illustrating the multiple roles of 3D-structured carbon nanotube (CNT) current collectors in LMBs, in comparison with bare Cu current collectors. 
(A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.) 
J. Kim et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      

Carbon 203 (2023) 426–435
428
2.2. Characterizations 
The morphologies of DWNTs and MWNTs were identified through 
field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, MIRA II LMH, 
TESCAN). The microstructure and crystallographic structures of both 
CNTs were investigated through Cs corrected transmission electron 
microscopy (Cs-TEM, Titan Themis Z, Thermo Sicentific) and X-ray 
diffraction (XRD, D8 Advance, Bruker), respectively. The surface areas 
and pore distributions of DWNTs and MWNTs were examined using a 
surface area and pore size analyzer (BET, Quadrasorb evo, 
Quantachrome). 
2.3. Electrochemical measurements 
The electrochemical stabilities of Li deposition/dissolution were 
evaluated in Li-metal cells using bare Cu foils (or CNT films) as current 
collectors. The Li-metal foil was physically bound to a stainless spacer 
(1T) and used as a counter electrode with a polyolefin membrane (Toray 
SETELATM). To clarify the effect of each current collector on the Li 
deposition behavior, we employed an additive-free carbonate-based 
electrolyte [1.0 M lithium hexafluorophosphate (LiPF6) in a mixture of 
ethylene carbonate and diethyl carbonate (EC:DEC = 1:1 vol%)]. Cyclic 
voltammetry (CV) measurements were performed at different scan rates 
between 0 and 1.0 V. A galvanostatic charge/discharge test was per­
formed at a constant current density of 200 mA g−1 in a potential win­
dow of 0.01–2.0 V vs. Li/Li+. The Li deposition/dissolution 
measurements were performed at 1.0, 4.0, and 8.0 mA cm−2. The Li 
deposition process continuously proceeded until the areal capacity 
reached 1.0 mAh cm−2 and the cutoff voltage of Li stripping was set to 
1.0 V. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS, VSP, Biologic, 
Seyssinet-Pariset) was conducted in the frequency range of 250 kHz–10 
mHz. 
3. Results and discussion 
Fig. 1 compares the mechanism by which a 3D-structured CNT 
Fig. 2. Fabrication and characterization. (Left) TEM images showing pristine CNT skeletons (center) top-view SEM images with inset photos of each punched CNT 
film, and (right) cross-sectional view SEM images of (a–c) DWNTs and (d–f) MWNTs. (g) XRD patterns and (i) nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms of the DWNT 
and MWNT scaffolds. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.) 
J. Kim et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      

Carbon 203 (2023) 426–435
429
current collector and a bare Cu current collector affect the behavior of 
the Li deposition/dissolution process. Bare Cu is a non-intercalating 
material where Li ions are not able to insert/intercalate into its struc­
ture [38]. Thus, under an applied current, all of the Li is deposited over 
the Cu electrode. When Li ions repetitively migrate back and forth inside 
the cell, they tend to form mossy Li across the Cu electrode surface 
owing to the “hostless” feature of Li-metal [12]. This event potentially 
causes safety issues such as battery short-circuit when the dendritic Li 
punctures the polymer separator [39]. 3D-structured CNT current col­
lectors can play multiple roles in LMB applications. First, a certain 
portion of Li-ions participate in electrochemical reactions, i.e., Faradaic 
(intercalative) and non-Faradaic (capacitive) electrode reactions, with 
CNT current collectors, which reduces the actual areal current density 
applied to the counter electrode. Once CNT current collectors are elec­
trochemically reacted with Li, it forms a lithiophilic interface layer that 
facilitates uniform and stable Li deposition over the surface. Moreover, 
since the CNT current collector can efficiently confine excess Li within 
its network structure, it experiences no significant volume change dur­
ing vigorous Li migration. 
The morphologies and material characteristics of the DWNT and 
MWNT scaffolds are characterized in Fig. 2. The number of CNT walls in 
each scaffold was determined through a Cs-TEM analysis (Fig. 2a and d). 
Considering that the space between walls affords a site for Li-ion storage, 
it is likely MWNTs are capable of storing more Li-ion than DWNTs. As 
shown in the SEM images of Fig. 2b and e, both DWNT and MWNT 
scaffolds have network architectures which facilitate the efficient Li-ion 
diffusion and provide a space for accumulating excess Li. Both scaffolds 
can be easily cut into circular discs without noticeable damage as seen 
from the inset photos of Fig. 2b and e, due to the excellent mechanical 
strength of CNT entanglement. The average thickness of each prepared 
film was precisely controlled to do a fair comparison study. Both CNT 
scaffolds are 11 μm in thickness (Fig. 2c and f). The crystalline phases of 
each CNT scaffold were determined from XRD measurements (Fig. 2g). 
The two representative peaks at 26◦and 42◦correspond to (002) plane 
and (101) plane, respectively. The XRD pattern of the MWNT scaffold 
presents a narrower and more intense (002) peak than that of the DWNT 
scaffold, reflecting variations in interlayer spacing. The interlayer 
spacing of each DWNT and MWNT calculated using Bragg’s equation 
were very similar (d002 ≈0.343 nm), which are consistent with previous 
reports [40]. The specific surface areas of each CNT scaffold were 
determined from the N2 adsorption and desorption curves (Fig. 2h). The 
specific surface area of DWNTs (822 m2 g−1) is way larger than that of 
Fig. 3. Electrochemical kinetics and properties. Cyclic voltammetry curves of (a) DWNT and (b) MWNT. Log(i)/Log(v) plots of (c) DWNT and (d) MWNT at various 
scan rates from 0.2 to 1.0 mV s−1. (e) Initial charge-discharge profiles and (f) cycle performances of DWNT and MWNT scaffolds in LIBs. (A colour version of this 
figure can be viewed online.) 
J. Kim et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      

Carbon 203 (2023) 426–435
430
MWNTs (221 m2 g−1), due to the structure difference. Interestingly, no 
noticeable difference was observed in terms of pore distribution (Fig. 2h, 
inset), indicating that only the specific surface area differed between the 
CNT scaffolds. 
The Li-ion storage mechanism includes a faradaic contribution from 
Li-ion intercalations and a non-faradaic contribution from the capacitive 
reaction [41]. The ratio of faradaic to non-faradaic contribution depends 
on the structure of CNTs. In order to understand the mechanism of Li-ion 
storage within CNT scaffolds, cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements 
were carried out using DWNT and MWNT scaffolds under various scan 
rates (Fig. 3a and c). The kinetics of each electrode was characterized by 
the following power law relation.  
i = avb … … ….                                                                        
[Eq 1] 
where i and v denote the peak current and scan rate, respectively, and a 
and b are adjustable parameters. 
When b value is close to 0.5, the diffusion-controlled process relevant 
to intercalation reaction becomes more dominant during the electro­
chemical reaction. If b value is ~1.0, the Li-ion storage is dominated by a 
surface-controlled (capacitive) process [42–44]. To estimate b [Eq (1)], 
is transformed to the following log (i) versus log (v) form:  
log (i) = b log (v) + log (a) … … …                                            
[Eq 2] 
Based upon the transformed power law equation, Fig. 3b and d are 
plotted in the lithiation step (reduction state). According to the calcu­
lation, the b value was much closer to 0.5 in the MWNT scaffold than in 
the DWNT scaffold (0.74 vs. 0.85), implying a more dominant faradaic 
contribution (such as intercalation-based charge storage) in the MWNT 
scaffold than in the DWNT scaffold. The typical electrochemical tests 
were performed to see how the Li-ion storage mechanism of each CNT 
affects the discharge capacity and cycle performance in LIBs. Fig. 3e 
shows the galvanostatic discharge profiles of the DWNT and MWNT 
scaffolds. At 200 mA g−1, DWNT and MWNT scaffolds delivered initial 
discharge capacities of 139 mAh g−1 and 226 mAh g−1, respectively, 
owing to the difference of Li-ion storage ability between the DWNT and 
MWNT scaffolds. After a few cycles, the MWNT reached a stable capacity 
of 118 mAh g−1 (Fig. 3f). In contrast, the DWNT showed a relatively 
poor capacity of around 45 mAh g−1. Since MWNTs have smaller specific 
surface area than DWNTs, it was assumed that the DWNT electrode 
would have a higher capacity than the MWNT electrode. Contrary to the 
assumption, the MWNT exhibited a larger discharge capacity than the 
DWNT in LIBs. This implies that the number of graphitic layers in the 
CNT is a more dominant factor affecting Li storage ability than the 
specific surface area of CNT. Therefore, the MWNT scaffold is electro­
chemically superior to the DWNT scaffold in Li metal-based batteries, 
due to the better electrochemical reversibility of MWNTs than that of 
DWNTs. 
To ascertain whether the lithiation abilities of each CNT affect the 
uniformity of Li deposition in LMBs, the degree of Li deposition on each 
current collector was precisely controlled by limiting the areal discharge 
capacity of the cells. Fig. 4 shows the surface images of each current 
collector tested with two cut-off capacities of 0.25 mAh cm−2 and 1.0 
mAh cm−2. Since Cu is a non-intercalating substance, all of the applied 
currents are directly contributed to the Li deposition over the Cu foil [45, 
46]. As shown in Fig. 4a, short Li dendrites and small Li clusters were 
randomly formed at multiple spots and the bare Cu surface was exposed 
due to the inhomogeneous Li deposition. After further Li deposition, 
long Li dendrites are grown across the entire Cu surface (Fig. 4d). It is 
because small Li clusters formed during the initial Li deposition play a 
role as seeds for growing long Li dendrites. As for the DWNT current 
collector, a portion of Li ions are stored between two walls during the 
initial Li deposition stage. When the Li-ion storage space saturated in the 
DWNT structure, the excess Li was deposited over the DWNT film sur­
face. Fig. 4b shows a few local areas where excess Li is agglomerated. 
When the discharge capacity increased to 1.0 mAh cm−2, the area of 
agglomerated Li got much wider due to the excess Li deposition 
(Fig. 4e). The LIB tests confirmed that the MWNT scaffold can store more 
Li-ions reversibly than the DWNT scaffold through the intercalation 
reaction. This electrochemical reaction not only efficiently reduces the 
Fig. 4. Li deposition tests. SEM images of the bare Cu, DWNT, and MWNT surfaces at (a–c) low (0.25 mAh cm−2) and (d–f) high cutoff capacities (1.0 mAh cm−2). (A 
colour version of this figure can be viewed online.) 
J. Kim et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      

Carbon 203 (2023) 426–435
431
actual current density but also facilitates the homogenous Li deposi­
tion/dissolution aided by the lithiophilic interface layer of lithiated 
MWNT scaffold. According to the previous report, the lithiated graphite 
as a superlithiophilic matrix can play a role in inducing uniform Li 
deposition [47]. These synergistic effects prevented obvious agglomer­
ation of Li over the surface of the MWNT current collector and promised 
to retard the formation of Li dendrites in LMBs (Fig. 4c and f). 
The electrochemical stabilities of each cell using different current 
collectors were directly compared by running Li deposition/dissolution 
tests. Fig. 5a shows the cycle performances of the Li-metal cells with and 
without the 3D-structured CNT current collectors at 1.0 mA cm−2. The 
first cycle coulombic efficiencies (CE) of the cells with the bare Cu, 
DWNT, and MWNT current collectors were 91.7%, 87.4% and 84.6%, 
respectively. For the first few cycles, the cells with the DWNT and 
MWNT current collectors achieved relatively lower CEs than the cell 
with the bare Cu current collector, due to the formation of solid elec­
trolyte interphase (SEI) layer [48]. In the cell with the bare Cu current 
collector, the CE of Li deposition/dissolution gradually decreases with 
cycling. The CE of the cell with the bare Cu current collector reached 
~60% after 100 cycles, owing to the huge loss of electrochemically 
available Li-ion. In contrast, the CEs of the cells with the DWNT and 
MWNT current collectors remained high (88% and 90%, respectively) 
even at 150 cycles. It is because both cells formed less dendritic Li than 
the cell with the bare Cu current collector. Fig. 5b shows the initial 
voltage profiles of each Li-metal cell. Unlike the cell with the bare Cu 
current collector, the DWNT- and MWNT-based cells have discharge 
capacities contributed by the Li-ion storage within CNT scaffolds. The 
resulting electrochemical reaction reduced the overall actual current 
density of the electrodes and thus facilitated uniform distribution of Li 
nucleation in the Li-metal cells. At 200 cycles, the cycle retention of the 
cell with the MWNT current collector exceeded that of the cell with the 
DWNT current collector due to the effectively suppressed Li dendritic 
growth (Fig. 5c). 
The cells with the DWNT and MWNT current collectors presented 
much lower Li deposition overpotentials (0.085 V) than the cell with the 
bare Cu current collector (0.3V) (as shown in Fig. 5d). This result also 
indicates that the CNT current collectors have an ability to regulate the 
Li nucleation reaction and thus suppress the growth of dendritic Li [49, 
50]. In the voltage profiles at the 1st and 200 cycles, the cell with the 
MWNT current collector shows a relatively longer and clearer lithiation 
region than the cell with the DWNT current collector. It implies that the 
MWNT current collector can store more Li-ion and has a better 
Fig. 5. Electrochemical tests at a low current density. (a) Cycle performances of the Li-metal cells with the bare Cu, DWNT, and MWNT current collectors at 1 mA 
cm−2. Voltage profiles of each cell at (b and d) the 1st cycle and (c and e) 200 cycles. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.) 
J. Kim et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      

Carbon 203 (2023) 426–435
432
Fig. 6. Electrochemical Li-metal half-cell tests at higher current density. Cycle performance (left) and voltage profiles at the first (center) and the final (right) cycle at 
(a–c) 4 mA cm−2 and (d–f) 8 mA cm−2. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.) 
Fig. 7. Post-mortem examinations of each Li-metal half-cell after cycling. (a–c) Low-magnification SEM images of the bare Cu, DWNT, and MWNT current collectors 
(insets are photographs of each current collector after cycling); (d–f) high-magnification SEM images of the boxed regions in a–c. (g–i) EIS spectra of each Li-metal 
half-cell before and after cycling. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.) 
J. Kim et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      

Carbon 203 (2023) 426–435
433
electrochemical reversibility than the DWNT current collector. This 
additional storage capacity of the MWNT-based cell contributes to the 
better electrochemical stability (Fig. 5c and e). In contrast to the cells 
with the CNT current collectors, the cell with the bare Cu current col­
lector was shorted at 160 cycles. 
To check whether the DWNT and MWNT current collectors achieve 
similar electrochemical stabilities at high current levels, Li-metal cells 
with and without CNT current collectors were further evaluated at 4 and 
8 mA cm−2 (Fig. 6). When each cell was tested at 4 mA cm−2, the CE of 
cells with the bare Cu, DWNT, and MWNT current collectors were 26%, 
64%, and 73%, respectively, at 100 cycles (Fig. 6a–c). Clearly, the cell 
with the MWNT current collector delivered the most stable performance 
among the Li-metal half-cells. Even when the applied current density 
increased to 8 mA cm−2, the cell with the MWNT current collector 
exhibited the most stable Li deposition/dissolution process without 
fluctuations (Fig. 6d–f). In contrast, the CE of Li deposition/dissolution 
in the cell with the bare Cu current collector dramatically decreased 
under the high current application. These results are consistent with the 
results obtained at low current density, i.e., 1 mA cm−2. 
In order to confirm which features contribute to the difference in 
cycle stability, the cycled Li-metal half-cells tested at 1 mA cm−2 were 
disassembled for post-mortem studies (Fig. 7). The morphologies of the 
cycled current collectors are displayed in Fig. 7a–f. The cycled Cu cur­
rent collector was covered by many black spots (Fig. 7, inset), which 
were revealed as dendritic Li (Fig. 7a and d). The cycled Cu current 
collector also presented numerous surface cracks induced by the 
continuous accumulation of irreversible Li. In comparison to the cycled 
Cu current collector, the surface of the DWNT current collector dis­
played a small number of silver spots (Fig. 7b), which were confirmed as 
Li dendrites with relatively large diameter (Fig. 7e). In case of the cycled 
MWNT current collector, it has relatively fewer silver spots than the 
DWNT current collector and a smoother and denser Li layer than the 
other current collectors (see zoomed-in SEM images in Fig. 7c and f). The 
electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements are car­
ried out to analyze the cell resistance before and after cycling, which are 
presented in Fig. 7g–i. The charge transfer resistance of the cell with the 
bare Cu current collector was around 38 Ω at the 1st cycle (i.e., before 
cycling), increasing to 50 Ω after cycling. Meanwhile, the charge 
transfer resistance of the cell with the DWNT current collector was 
around 34 Ω after cycling, due to the reduced growth of sharp Li den­
drites. In the cell with the MWNT current collector, the charge transfer 
resistance exhibited the smallest charge transfer resistance of around 23 
Ω among the cells. It is attributed to the suppressed growth of dendritic 
Li and the excellent electrochemical reversibility of the MWNT current 
collector. 
To demonstrate the practical applicability of the DWNT and MWNT 
as current collectors, anode-free cells were fabricated using a LiCoO2 
(LCO) electrode, on behalf of using Li-metal. Since LCO has a limited 
amount of electrochemically available Li-ion, the electrochemical ad­
vantages of each current collector in practical Li-metal applications can 
be straightforwardly observed in these anode-free cells. All the current 
collectors were pre-activated for surface stabilization before their cell 
assembly. Each anode-free cell was evaluated at 0.5 C (1 C = 172 mA 
g−1) in the voltage range from 3 to 4.3 V (Fig. 8). Fig. 8a–c displays the 
voltage profiles of each anode-free cell with cycling. The cell with the 
DWNT and MWNT current collectors delivered higher specific capacities 
of 160 mAh g−1 than the cell with the bare Cu current collector (150 
mAh g−1) in the initial cycle, reflecting their improved electrochemical 
kinetics and reversibility. The trend of capacity fading for each cell 
shows a significant difference. The cell with the bare Cu current col­
lector reached near-zero capacity at 35 cycle due to the serious loss of 
active Li-ion. The cell with both CNT current collectors achieved much 
higher cycling stability than the cell with the bare Cu current collector; 
moreover, the cell with the MWNT current collector outperformed the 
cell with DWNT current collector in terms of cycle retention, reflecting 
the excellent electrochemical reversibility of the MWNT current col­
lector. The coulombic efficiencies of each cell are provided in Fig. S1. 
Considering all these obtained results, it can be inferred that the current 
collector with excellent Li-ion storage ability and electrochemical 
reversibility can improve the electrochemical stability of the Li metal- 
based batteries. 
4. Conclusion 
In this work, we assessed two different CNT scaffolds (e.g. DWNT and 
MWNT) to study how the structure of CNT affects Li deposition behavior 
and thus the electrochemical stability of LMBs. Both CNT current col­
lectors were prepared without binders to clarify structural effects. When 
Fig. 8. The evaluation of anode-free cells using a LiCoO2 electrode. (a–c) Voltage profiles of each anode-free cell with the bare Cu, DWNT and MWNT current 
collectors at the first, second and the final cycle; (d) cycle retention over 50 cycles. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.) 
J. Kim et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      

Carbon 203 (2023) 426–435
434
both prepared CNT current collectors were introduced in the Li-metal 
cell, the coulombic efficiency and cycle stability were significantly 
improved in comparison with the cell with the bare Cu current collector. 
In particular, the Li-metal cell with the MWNT current collector 
exhibited the most stable electrochemical performances under various 
current densities. It also delivered the excellent cycle retention (97% at 
50th cycle) when the MWNT current collector were employed in the 
anode-free cell using a LiCoO2 electrode. These results are attributed to 
the material benefits of MWNTs as a current collector: a) the efficient 
reduction of actual current density applied to counter electrodes, b) the 
reversible storage of excess Li within the 3D architecture, and c) the 
provision of a lithiophilic matrix for uniform Li nucleation. The concept 
to use the MWNT current collector for LMBs suggests that the selection 
of suitable materials for designing current collector is highly important 
to achieve highly stable electrochemical performances of LMBs. We 
believe that this study would open a new route to improve the electro­
chemical safety and performances of LMBs and advance the develop­
ment of practical current collectors for next-generation batteries.

[46] J. Liu, Z. Bao, Y. Cui, E.J. Dufek, J.B. Goodenough, P. Khalifah, Q. Li, B.Y. Liaw, 
P. Liu, A. Manthiram, Y.S. Meng, V.R. Subramanian, M.F. Toney, V. 
V. Viswanathan, M.S. Whittingham, J. Xiao, W. Xu, J. Yang, X.-Q. Yang, J.- 
G. Zhang, Pathways for practical high-energy long-cycling lithium metal batteries,

graphite lithiophobic or lithiophilic? Natl. Sci. Rev. 7 (7) (2020) 1208–1217.

Article
Achieving Cycling Stability in Anode of Lithium-Ion Batteries
with Silicon-Embedded Titanium Oxynitride Microsphere
Sung Eun Wang 1,†
, DoHoon Kim 1,2,†, Min Ji Kim 1,3, Jung Hyun Kim 1, Yun Chan Kang 3, Kwang Chul Roh 1,
Junghyun Choi 1
, Hyung Woo Lee 2,4,*
and Dae Soo Jung 1,*
1
Energy Storage Materials Center, Korea Institute of Ceramic Engineering & Technology (KICET),
Jinju-si 52851, Republic of Korea
2
Department of Nanoenergy Engineering, Pusan National University, Pusan 46241, Republic of Korea
3
Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Korea University, Seoul 02841, Republic of Korea
4
Department of Nano Fusion Engineering and Research Center of Energy Convergence Technology,
Pusan University, Pusan 46241, Republic of Korea
*
Correspondence: lhw2010@pusan.ac.kr (H.W.L.); dsjung@kicet.re.kr (D.S.J.)
†
These authors contributed equally to this work.
Abstract: Surface coating approaches for silicon (Si) have demonstrated potential for use as anodes in
lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) to address the large volume change and low conductivity of Si. However,
the practical application of these approaches remains a challenge because they do not effectively
accommodate the pulverization of Si during cycling or require complex processes. Herein, Si-
embedded titanium oxynitride (Si-TiON) was proposed and successfully fabricated using a spray-
drying process. TiON can be uniformly coated on the Si surface via self-assembly, which can enhance
the Si utilization and electrode stability. This is because TiON exhibits high mechanical strength and
electrical conductivity, allowing it to act as a rigid and electrically conductive matrix. As a result,
the Si-TiON electrodes delivered an initial reversible capacity of 1663 mA h g−1 with remarkably
enhanced capacity retention and rate performance.
Keywords: lithium-ion batteries; silicon anodes; titanium oxynitrides; spray-drying
1. Introduction
To meet the growing demand in the ﬁelds of electronic vehicles and energy storage
systems, developing high-capacity electrode materials is crucial for enhancing the energy
density of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) [1–4]. Silicon (Si) has been considered as a promising
anode material with an outstanding theoretical capacity (4200 mA h g−1, Li22Si5), which is
10 times larger than the traditional graphite anode (372 mA h g−1, LiC6) [5]. However, com-
mercialization of Si anodes is still hampered by its volume expansion problem, resulting in
severe capacity fading during lithiation/delithiation. These shortcomings are exacerbated
by the pulverization of particles and the repeated formation of solid-electrolyte-interphase
(SEI), which leads to continuous electrolyte consumption during cycling [6–13].
Generally, the simple coating on the Si surface using various functional materials is
widely used to mitigate electrochemical and mechanical degradation [14–22]. The coating
layer can help avoid direct contact between Si and the electrolyte, reducing the growth
of the unstable SEI, while it also acts as a volume expansion buffer to alleviate fracture of
the electrode [23,24]. Carbonaceous materials are the commonly used candidates for the
surface modiﬁcation of Si due to their high electrical conductivity and chemical stability
with electrolytes. However, most of them are rigid, resulting in poor durability against Si
volume expansion. Other inorganic species, including metal oxides (e.g., TiO2, MgO, and
Al2O3) and SiOx have also been explored, which can enhance the structural integrity with
good mechanical properties and act as artiﬁcial SEI layers to suppress side reactions during
Nanomaterials 2023, 13, 132. https://doi.org/10.3390/nano13010132
https://www.mdpi.com/journal/nanomaterials

Nanomaterials 2023, 13, 132
2 of 10
cycling [25,26]. Nevertheless, the poor electrical conductivity and lithium loss at the ﬁrst
cycle may cause insufﬁcient electrochemical performances.
Herein, we propose titanium oxynitride-embedded Si NPs (Si-TiON) achieving high
cycling stability in LIBs and successfully fabricated via a spray-drying process. TiO2
can be uniformly and thinly coated on the Si surface during the drying process, and
then converted into TiON under the optimized nitridation conditions. TiON exhibits
high conductivity and mechanical strength, which facilitates electron/ion migration and
enhances the structural integrity of Si-based anodes [27–29]. Additionally, spray-drying
used for Si-TiON manufacturing plays a key role in the formation of the core-shell structure
via capillary force and is widely applied to industrial ﬁelds due to its scalable and low-cost
properties [30–32]. By virtue of these properties, the Si-TiON composite can be a promising
candidate for the practical use of high-performance Si-based anodes.
2. Materials and Methods
2.1. Synthesis of TiON as a New Coating Material
To synthesize optimized TiON, TiO2 nanoparticles (TiO2 NPs, average diameter = 20 nm,
Degussa P25 (anatase 85%, rutile 15% phase), Evonik, Essen, Germany) were nitrided at
different temperatures (700 ◦C, 750 ◦C, 800 ◦C, 850 ◦C, 900 ◦C, and 950 ◦C) under ammonia
gas (NH3, 99.9%, flow rate: 1.5 L/min) for 5 h in a quartz tube furnace.
2.2. Preparation of Si-TiON Composite
One g of Si nanoparticles (Si NPs, ~50 nm, 99.9%, Nano & Amor Inc., Houston, TX,
USA) and 1 g of polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP, Mw. 200,000, Sigma-Aldrich Co., St. Louis,
MO, USA) were dispersed in 200 mL of deionized (DI) water and stirred for 30 min. Then,
1 g of TiO2 NPs was added and agitated for 30 min using a high-energy tip sonicator
(750 W, VCX 750, Sonics & Materials Inc., Newtown, CT, USA) to obtain a homogeneous
solution. To synthesize Si-TiON composite powders, the precursor was loaded into a spray
dryer (B-290, BUCHI Labortechnik AG, Flawil, Switzerland). The detailed parameters were
as follows: inlet temperature—180 ◦C, feeding speed—5 mL min−1, and air ﬂow rate—
30 L min−1. After drying, the prepared Si-TiO2 composite underwent thermal treatment
at 800 ◦C for 5 h under an atmosphere of ammonia (NH3, 99.9%) and ramping speed
maintained at 5 ◦C min−1. To optimize the amount of TiON, TiO2 was added in different
ratios in the precursor. The amount of TiO2 corresponding to 1 g of Si was 0.5 g, 1 g, and
2 g, and denoted as “Si-TiON (1:0.5)”, “Si-TiON (1:1)”, and “Si-TiON (1:2)”, respectively.
2.3. Material Characterization
The structures of Si-TiO2 and Si-TiON were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD,
D8 Advance, Bruker, Billerica, MA, USA) using Cu Kα radiation. Nitrogen adsorption and
d,esorption isotherms were obtained with a 3-Flex (Micromeritics, Norcross, GA, USA),
and the speciﬁc surface area was obtained using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method.
The chemical binding was observed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, VersaProbe,
Ulvac-PHI Inc., Yokohama, Japan). The morphology investigations were performed using a
ﬁeld emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, JSM-7610F, JEOL, Tokyo, Japan), and
high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, Themis Z, FEI Co., Hillsboro,
OG, USA). For the measurement of conductivity of synthesized TiON, a current-voltage
(I-V) source was used. Teﬂon mold was ﬁlled with the powder and loaded under the probe
of a 20 mm diameter electrode. The conductivities of the TiON were obtained from the
gradient of the I-V curves.
2.4. Electrochemical Measurements
The mixture of Si-TiON composite (60 wt%), super-P (20 wt%), and poly-acrylic acid
(20 wt%) was stirred to form a homogeneous slurry. The electrode was prepared on Cu
foil (18 µm, Hohsen Corp., Tokyo, Japan) using a doctor blade method. Then, the electrode
was dried at 80 ◦C overnight under vacuum and punched into circular discs for coin-cell

Nanomaterials 2023, 13, 132
3 of 10
fabrication. The Si NPs electrode was also prepared separately under the same conditions.
The 2032-type coin half-cell was assembled in a dry room. The electrolyte was 1M LiPF6 in
a mixture of ethylene carbonate (EC) and diethyl carbonate (DEC) (1:1 v/v%) with 5 wt%
ﬂuoroethylene carbonate (FEC, Dongwha Electrolyte Co., LTD., Nonsan-si, Korea). The cell
was charged and discharged galvanostatically in the range of 0.01–1.5 V on a Won-A-Tech
battery tester.
3. Results and Discussion
TiON was prepared by thermal treatment of TiO2 under a NH3 atmosphere at different
temperatures. The phase transformation of TiO2 after nitridation was observed by X-ray
diffraction (XRD) patterns shown in Figure 1a. The TiO2 NPs (Degussa P25) used as the
starting material for the preparation of TiON are composed of mainly the anatase phase
(85%) and partially the rutile phase (15%), as is well-known [33]. At a low nitridation
temperature at 700 ◦C, the major peak of the rock-salt TiON phase appeared at 43.2◦with
the peaks of the anatase and rutile TiO2 phases being at 25.2◦and 27.4◦, respectively [34]. As
the temperature increased, the peaks corresponding to TiO2 were no longer detected, while
those of TiON drastically increased. These results indicate that TiO2 is completely phase-
transformed into TiON at a temperature above 750 ◦C. Figure 1b shows the magniﬁed XRD
patterns for the main peak of TiON in the range from 42.0 to 44.0◦. TiON is a solid solution
of TiO and TiN, and a diffraction peak of TiON appears between the peaks corresponding
to these two phases. It can be observed that the diffraction peaks shift to a lower degree
corresponding to the TiN region at higher nitridation temperatures. It demonstrates that
more anion exchanges of O and N atoms occurred at a high temperature, resulting in a
TiN-rich phase. A formation mechanism of TiON is proposed as follows. The TiO2 phase is
converted into the TiO phase under an atmosphere of NH3 with high reduction ability. The
TiO phase contains large oxygen vacancies (~15%) owing to its large non-stoichiometry.
The oxygen vacancy is a thermodynamically stable site for incorporating N atoms, and
Ti2+ offers electrons to N atoms for the stabilization of N3−[35,36]. This could facilitate
the exchange of O and N atoms resulting in the formation of TiON phase. The electrical
conductivity of TiON powder was compared with that of super-P commonly used as a
conductor in LIBs, as shown in Figure 1c. TiON nitrided at 800 ◦C shows higher electrical
conductivity than TiON nitrided at 700 ◦C in the whole range of pressure. TiON can reach a
high electrical conductivity of 3.60 S cm−1, which is similar to that of super-P (3.68 S cm−1)
at a high pressure of 150 kgf cm−2. This result shows that the TiN phase formed at a
high nitridation temperature can enhance electrical conductivity and act as a conductive
agent for Si. Figure 1d shows the N2 adsorption/desorption isotherm and surface area of
TiON. The plots almost overlapped at the nitridation temperatures of 700, 750, and 800 ◦C,
indicating negligible change in surface area, but the surface area signiﬁcantly decreased at
temperatures above 850 ◦C. This is caused by the agglomeration of TiON particles owing
to the metallic property of TiN manifested at high temperatures. Therefore, in this study,
the nitridation temperature was ﬁxed at 800 ◦C for the synthesis of Si-TiON composite to
achieve high electrical conductivity without particle agglomeration.
A spray-drying process was used for the facile synthesis of Si-TiON composite, which
is illustrated in Figure 2a. To prepare a precursor solution, Si NPs (50 nm), TiO2 (20 nm),
and PVP were dispersed in DI water. First, the precursor droplets generated from the
spray nozzle were injected into the reactor via drying gas. The droplet underwent solvent
evaporation and drying to form micro-sized secondary particles based on the one droplet–
one particle conversion [37,38]. During the drying process, the smaller TiO2 particles can
move easily, while the relatively larger Si NPs move slowly in the droplet. This is because
the Brownian motion effect is greater on smaller particles, resulting in faster capillary ﬂow
of TiO2 colloidal particles to the interface region where solvent evaporation occurs ﬁrst.
Thus, TiO2 particles were trapped in this region and mainly located at the shell of the
particle, leaving the Si NPs in the core. Furthermore, the spaces between the Si NPs can
be ﬁlled by the TiO2 particles due to the interparticle capillary force. This self-assembly of

Nanomaterials 2023, 13, 132
4 of 10
colloidal particles leads to the uniform embedding of Si NPs in TiO2 [39,40]. Subsequently,
the Si-TiON composite can be obtained by the nitridation process. Figure 2b–d shows the
morphology of the Si-TiON composite with different TiON contents. All the secondary
particles present a spherical morphology with a particle size of about 5 µm. The high-
magniﬁcation image in the inset of Figure 2b shows that the surface is composed of irregular
nanoparticles with a size of 20–50 nm, indicating the coexistence of both TiON and Si NPs.
On the other hand, as the TiON content increases, only small particles below 20 nm can be
observed at the surface, as shown in the inset of Figure 2c,d. It can be concluded that Si
NPs can be completely embedded in TiON if the ratio of TiON to Si exceeds 1:1.
Figure 1. Physicochemical properties of TiON prepared with different nitridation temperatures.
(a) XRD patterns and (b) magniﬁed major peaks at 2-theta 42–44◦. (c) Electrical conductivity behavior
of TiON prepared at different nitridation temperatures as a function of pressure with those of a
commercial carbon conductor (super-P). (d) Nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherms; (Inset)
surface area depends on the nitridation temperature of TiO2.
Figure 2. (a) Graphical illustration for synthesis of Si-TiON composite using spray-drying and
subsequent nitridation. FE-SEM images of Si-TiON composite synthesized using different molar
ratios of Si and TiON: (b) 1:0.5 (c) 1:1 (d) 1:2. Inset represents high-resolution images.

Nanomaterials 2023, 13, 132
5 of 10
The Si-TiON (1:1) composite was further characterized using transmission electron
microscopy (TEM). Figure 3a,b shows that the secondary particles are formed of numerous
nanoparticles and the interconnected shell region. The densely packed microsphere is a
result of the ﬂow of TiO2 particles between Si particles, as mentioned above. It is noteworthy
that TiON formed from TiO2 is connected to each other due to the metallic property of TiN,
which can further enhance the mechanical stability of coating layers against the volume
expansion of Si. The high-resolution TEM images in Figure 3c indicate clear lattice fringes
with d-spacings of 0.147 and 0.157 nm corresponding to rock-salt TiON in the shell (point 1)
and crystalline Si (point 2) in the core, respectively [41,42]. In addition, energy-dispersive
X-ray spectra (EDS) from point 1 shown in Figure 3d show a higher proportion of Ti than
Si, while the opposite tendency can be observed for point 2, which decisively demonstrates
that Si NPs were well-enclosed by the TiON particles. The elemental mapping images for
Si, Ti, O, and N (Figure 3e) also indicate that Si NPs are dispersed in the TiON matrix,
demonstrating a core-shell structure.
Figure 3. HRTEM images of Si-TiON (1:1) composite at (a) low and (b,c) high magniﬁcations. Right
inset shows lattice fringes within the TiON coating layer and Si core particle along directions (004)
and (222), respectively. (d) Energy-dispersive X-ray spectra from points 1 and 2 in (c). (e) Si, Ti, O,
and N elemental mapping indicating that TiON was well-distributed in the shell region.
Figure 4a,b shows the XRD patterns of the Si-TiO2 and Si-TiON composites prepared
with different ratios of TiO2. The typical diffraction peaks of the Si and TiO2 phases are
revealed in the Si-TiO2 composite, but the peaks corresponding to TiO2 disappeared and
peaks of TiON appeared in the Si-TiON composite. These indicate that the TiO2 phase
was completely converted into the TiON phase after the subsequent nitridation. With an
increase in the proportion of TiO2 in the Si-TiO2, the intensity of the peak corresponding
to TiO2 increases, as shown in Figure 4a. It can be found that Si-TiON also shows the
same tendency, which means that the coating material content increases in the composite.
No other peaks except those corresponding to Si and TiON are observed because no side
reactions occurred during the spray-drying and nitridation process.
The XPS survey spectra also suggest the presence of TiON coating layers on the surface
of Si (Figure 4c,d). The Ti 2p peaks show three oxidation states of Ti4+, Ti3+, and Ti2+ at
457.9, 456.6, and 455.1 eV, respectively [43–45]. The oxidation state of Ti4+ is related to the
existence of TiO2 because TiON can be easily oxidized in the air. The Ti2+ component derives
from the TiO phase, whereas the Ti3+ species come from the TiN phase, demonstrating that
TiON is the solid solution of TiO and TiN. The N1s spectra can be deconvoluted into two
major peaks corresponding to Ti–N (397.4 eV) and Ti–O–N bonding (396.3 eV), which is

Nanomaterials 2023, 13, 132
6 of 10
consistent with the results of Figure 4c [46,47]. N–O (398.5 eV) can be observed when the
sample is oxidized by exposure to air.
Figure 4. XRD patterns of (a) Si-TiO2 prepared by the spray-drying process and (b) subsequently
nitrided Si-TiON composite with ratios of 1:0.5, 1:1, and 1:2. (c,d) X-ray photoelectron spectra for
Si-TiON (1:1) composite in the region of binding energies of Ti 2p and N 1s core levels.
The electrochemical properties of Si-TiON composite and Si NPs were examined using
coin-type half-cells with the Li metals as counter electrodes (Figure 5). In galvanostatic
tests, Si NPs electrode delivered an initial speciﬁc capacity of 3193 mA h g-1 at a current
density of 0.1 A g−1. On the other hand, Si-TiON (1:0.5) and (1:1) exhibited an initial
reversible capacity of 2378 and 1663 mA h g−1, respectively. It can be found that Si-TiON
(1:1) composite has a similar initial Coulombic efﬁciency of 80.1% compared to that of Si
NPs (80.9%), which demonstrates that TiON is inactive towards lithium ions, preserving
the reversibility of Si. At the subsequent cycles at 2 A g−1, bare Si NPs show severe capacity
fading due to the absence of a buffer layer; the Si-TiON (1:0.5) composite also exhibits
relatively low cycle stability because Si cannot be completely encapsulated due to the
insufﬁcient amount of TiON, making it difﬁcult to suppress the volume expansion of Si
(Figure 5b). However, the Si-TiON (1:1) composite achieved high capacity retention up
to 30 cycles because the TiON coating layer was completely deposited on the surface of
Si, improving the structural stability of the overall electrode. Si-TiON (1:2) also exhibits
excellent initial Coulombic efﬁciency and cycle life, demonstrating the superiority of TiON
as a coating material; however, it shows low reversible capacity due to the decrease in
the total active material ratio (Figure S1a). Figure S1b shows that Si-TiO2 (1:1) delivered
an initial reversible capacity of 1517 mA h g−1 with the Coulombic efﬁciency of 75.7%,
which is smaller than that of Si-TiON (1:1) because of the irreversible reaction between TiO2
and lithium ions. To further prove the structural stability of Si-TiON (1:1), cross-sectional
FE-SEM images after cycling were observed (Figure S2). A Si NP electrode shows severe
electrode swelling and collapse due to the large volume expansion of active material,
whereas the Si-TiON electrode indicates moderate swelling behaviors and maintains its
integrity. This result shows that the TiON coating layer can enhance the stability of the
electrode during cycling due to its outstanding mechanical properties.

Nanomaterials 2023, 13, 132
7 of 10
Figure 5. Voltage proﬁles for ﬁrst and selected subsequent cycles at 0.1 A g−1 and 2 A g−1, respec-
tively: (a) Si NPs, (b) Si-TiON (1:0.5), and (c) Si-TiON (1:1).
Figure 6a shows the cycling performance and Columbic efﬁciency of the Si-TiON (1:1)
composite. Samples were cycled at 1 A g−1 after pre-cycling at 0.1 A g−1 to form stable SEI.
The initial reversible capacity of Si-TiON (1:1) was 1140 mA h g−1 with a capacity retention
of 97% after 100 cycles, demonstrating the effect of the mechanical aspect of applying the
TiON coating. Impedance spectroscopy (EIS) also exhibits the outstanding electrochemical
properties of Si-TiON (1:1) (Figure S3). Si-TiON (1:1) shows a much smaller semicircle than
Si NPs in the high-frequency region, which corresponds to the charge transfer resistance
at the electrode/electrolyte interface. This implies that Si-TiON (1:1) has high structural
stability due to the existence of the rigid coating layer, while Si NPs went through structural
failure, resulting in the formation of a thick SEI layer during cycling. The rate performances
of Si NPs and Si-TiON (1:1) under different current densities were compared in Figure 6b.
As the current density increases gradually, the Si-TiON composite achieves superior rate
capabilities, as seen from the measurements after every 10 cycles. The speciﬁc discharge
capacities were determined to be 1411, 1249, 1102, 892, and 603 mA h g−1 at various current
rates from 0.1 to 5 A g−1. In contrast, Si NPs show rapid capacity degradation for the ﬁrst
30 cycles, and even inferior rate capabilities under higher current densities. This ﬁnding
further demonstrates that the TiON shell layer can improve the electrical conductivity
of the Si-based anode by facilitating electron and ion mobility, leading to enhanced rate
performance [48]. The electrochemical performances of Si-TiON (1:1) fabricated from facile
spray pyrolysis were compared to the various Si composites reported in the previous works
(Table S1); Si-TiON (1:1) shows high initial capacity, enhanced cycle life, and excellent rate
capability compared to those of other Si-based anodes. These electrochemical performances
are attributed to the TiON with excellent electrical and mechanical properties and unique Si-
TiON structure in which electronic conductivity is supported by the TiON matrix through
direct contact with Si NPs, while the structure accommodates the volume expansion of
Si NPs.
Figure 6. (a) Cycling performance of Si-TiON (1:1) measured at 1 A g−1 and (b) comparison of rate
performances of Si NPs and Si-TiON (1:1) at different current densities.

Nanomaterials 2023, 13, 132
8 of 10
4. Conclusions
In summary, the Si-TiON composite with a TiON coating material has been introduced
and completely synthesized via a simple spray-drying. The ﬁnal product shows that Si NPs
are uniformly embedded in the TiON via self-assembly of colloidal particles during the
drying process. The TiON component plays an important role in enhancing cycling stability
and rate capability because it can alleviate the volume changes of Si NPs to maintain
structural stability and support the electrical conductivity of the electrode. As a result, the
Si-TiON composite exhibited high initial Coulombic efﬁciency, as well as excellent cycling
performance. Furthermore, it showed a remarkably high rate capability in comparison
with commercial Si NPs. This study offers a promising TiON coating material for the
development of high-performance Si anodes for LIBs, and a simple fabrication method for
the core-shell composite based on a scalable synthetic process.

proﬁles for ﬁrst and selected subsequent cycles at 0.1 A g−1 and 2 A g−1, respectively: (a) Si-
TiON (1:2) and (b) Si-TiO2 (1:1).; Figure S2: Cross-sectional FE-SEM images of (a) Si NPs and
(b) Si-TiON (1:1) electrodes before and after 30 cycles at 2 A g−1.; Figure S3: EIS proﬁles of Si NPs
and Si-TiON (1:1) electrodes after 100 cycles at 1 A g−1. References [16,20,49–51] are cited in the
Supplementary Materials.

[SOURCE: Beyond the Limits of Lithium Iron Phosphate Cutting-Edge Innovations Toward High Performance and Sustainability for Next-Generation Batteries.pdf]
