the surface is much lower than the average oxidation state of
the MoO3 particle, which was conﬁrmed using the X-ray
photoelectron spectroscopy (Figure S1).
The galvanostatic voltage proﬁles and delithiation diﬀer-
ential capacity−voltage (dQ/dV) plots of the pristine, BM-5m,
and BM-30m electrodes are compared in Figure 2. It is
believed that the smaller-sized particles exhibit higher lithiation
capacity because of their smaller diﬀusion resistance and
electrolytic decomposition on a larger surface area. However,
the milled electrodes exhibited similar lithiation capacities, as
shown in Figure 2a and Table 1. This result may imply that (i)
the Li+ diﬀusion in the particle is not a rate-determining step,
and (ii) the irreversible electrolytic decomposition is similar.
Conversely, pronounced diﬀerences were observed in the
delithiation proﬁles of the electrodes. The proﬁles are almost
superimposed in the voltage range of 0−0.8 V (vs Li/Li+) and
diverge signiﬁcantly above 0.8 V (vs Li/Li+), resulting in the
capacity diﬀerence between the electrodes. This diﬀerence is
again clearly observed in the dQ/dV plot (Figure 2b). The
delithiation capacity in the voltage range of 0.8−3 V (vs Li/
Li+) was increased signiﬁcantly by ball-milling, whereas the
values in the range of 0−0.8 V (vs Li/Li+) were comparable for
all samples.
The variation in the Mo oxidation state during the
delithiation processes was investigated via the XANES and
the extended X-ray absorption ﬁne structure (EXAFS) analyses
(Figure 2c−f). The XANES spectra of the pristine MoO3
electrodes obtained after delithiation up to 0.8 and 3 V (vs Li/
Li+) are presented in Figure 2c, together with the spectra of the
open-circuit voltage (OCV) and the Mo metal for the
comparison. First, the spectrum at 0.8 V (vs Li/Li+) is
superimposed on that of the Mo metal, which indicates that
the capacity in the voltage range of 0−0.8 V (vs Li/Li+)
originates from the interfacial storage mechanisms, while the
deconversion reaction is over the range of 0.8−3 V (vs Li/Li+).
Second, the position of the absorption peak after full
delithiation (3 V vs Li/Li+) did not return to the initial state
(OCV), indicating incomplete oxidation of Mo in the
electrode. Figure 2d compares the oxidation states of the
pristine and BM-30m electrodes, after delithiation up to 3 V
(vs Li/Li+). The BM-30m electrode did not recover its original
oxidation state, even at the end of the delithiation period;
however, the higher photon energies indicate that the ball-
milled electrode underwent further oxidation compared to the
pristine electrode. In addition, the EXAFS spectra indicate that,
after delithiation, BM-30m contains a much similar crystal
structure with the initial state than the pristine electrode
(Figure 2e). The XANES spectra are consistent with the
voltage proﬁles shown in Figure 2a; the smaller delithiation
capacity was attributed to less degree of oxidation, that is,
incomplete deconversion of LixMoO3. A quantitative compar-
ison of the Mo oxidation states was conducted using the
XANES spectra (Figure 2f). By interpolating the photon
energies, the valence states of Mo were found to be +4.6 and
+5.4 for the pristine and BM-30m electrodes, respectively, after
the delithiation. In addition to the XANES results, the initial
voltage of the BM-30m electrode in the second cycle was

higher than that of the pristine MoO3 electrode (Figure S2),
providing additional evidence that BM-30m was oxidized
further than the pristine electrode in the previous delithiation
period. The improved delithiation capacity of the BM-30m
electrodes was possibly a size eﬀect because the particle size
was reduced signiﬁcantly by milling, whereas the other
properties, such as Mo valency and bulk structure, were
comparable to those of the pristine electrode (Figure 1a). To
investigate the size eﬀect on the electrochemical performance,
smaller MoO3 particles were synthesized at a lower temper-
ature of 500 °C (Figure S3). Surprisingly, the size eﬀect was
not observed, even though the particle size was signiﬁcantly
reduced to several hundreds of nanometers. However, after
ball-milling the MoO3 powder synthesized at 500 °C, the
delithiation capacity increased and was similar to that of BM-
30m (Figure S4). Therefore, it is concluded that the higher
capacity is observed only for particles smaller than tens of
nanometers (Figure S5). The nonlinear relationship between
the capacity and particle size is surprising because MoO3
experiences particle cracking and contact loss, which is
responsible for the 175% volume change during lithiation
and delithiation. Both the particle cracking and contact loss
should be mitigated by reducing the particle size. However, for
MoO3, a reduction in the particle size to hundreds of
nanometers did not result in improved performance, as
shown in Figure S4, strongly implying that the cracking or
contact loss is not the main contributor to the incomplete
delithiation and low ICE. These results suggest that another
Figure 3. Simulated concentration gradient of Li+ in the MoO3 particles with a radius of (a) 1 μm and (b) 10 nm.
Figure 4. Schematic illustration of the lithiation and delithiation processes of particles in the (a) pristine MoO3 and (b) BM-30m electrodes with
radii of approximately 1 μm and 10 nm, respectively. (c) Cyclic performance of the pristine and ball-milled MoO3 electrodes at a current density of
500 mA g−1 over a voltage range of 0−3 V (vs Li/Li+).
Nano Letters
pubs.acs.org/NanoLett
Letter
https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.nanolett.1c04290
Nano Lett. 2022, 22, 761−767
764

failure mechanism plays a crucial role in the incomplete
delithiation. We propose that the delithiation is impeded by
the formation of an electronically resistive MoO3 layer at the
outer surface of LixMoO3. As MoO3 has extremely poor
electronic conductivity (smaller than 10−6 S m−1), the highly
delithiated outer layer of the particle should hinder the further
oxidation of the particle.21,22
The formation of the resistive outer layer during the
delithiation period was simulated for particles with a radius of
1 μm and 10 nm (Figure 3). A spherical shape and ﬁxed radius
in the delithiation period, without volume contraction, were
assumed for simpliﬁcation. The Li+ ﬂux at the surface of
particles was set as 100 mA g−1, which is the same as the
experimental current density in Figure 2. The parameters used
for the modeling are listed in Table S1. Upon delithiation, the
Li+ concentration at the particle surface decreased continu-
ously and reached zero in both particle sizes, thereby forming
the resistive MoO3 phase (shown in dark blue). However, the
concentration gradient in the inner region was dependent on
the particle size. The 1-μm particle was delithiated
predominantly from the outer region, such that the
concentration gradient developed along the radial direction
in the particle (Figure 3a). At DOD 85 (85% of depth of
delithiation), the surface was fully delithiated to form MoO3;
however, the resistive outer layer restricted further delithiation,
leading to an incomplete delithiation of the particle. In
contrast, the smaller 10 nm particle was delithiated
homogeneously, without the concentration gradient in the
particle (Figure 3b). Therefore, the entire particle was
transformed into MoO3 with minimum residual content of
Li+ at the end of the delithiation process. The modeling results
imply that the particles, with approximately 10 nm radii, are
not aﬀected by the resistive outer layer. In contrast, the
concentration gradient and incomplete delithiation are
unavoidable in the large particles. It is expected that particles
smaller than a speciﬁc radius would be free from the failure
driven by the resistive outer surface. This mechanism is
consistent with the experimental observation that only the
MoO3 nanoparticles, with sizes less than 50 nm, showed
improved delithiation capacity, whereas larger particles under-
went incomplete delithiation.
Figure 4 illustrates the proposed lithiation and delithiation
processes of the micrometer- and nanometer-sized MoO3
particles, which represent the bigger and smaller particles
than the critical size, respectively, and their experimental cycle
performances. The resistive outer shell is formed in the
electrons and results in earlier termination of the delithiation
process. However, the small particle is delithiated homoge-
neously, without the concentration gradient along the radial
direction, thereby realizing a higher delithiation capacity. In
addition, the Galvanostatic Intermittent Titration Technique
(GITT) experiments also indicated that the resistance of
pristine MoO3 increased after approximately 1.0 V (vs Li/Li+)
during discharge, which conﬁrmed that the outer resistive
MoO3 layer in the micrometer-sized particle impeded further
oxidation of the inner parts, whereas consistent resistance was
observed in the nanometer-sized sample (Figure S6). As can be
seen in Figure 4c, the ball-milled electrodes, BM-5m and BM-
30m, exhibited an improved ICE and capacity retention
compared to the pristine electrode. However, the BM-2h and
BM-4h electrodes showed poor cycle performance. The small
speciﬁc capacity and the capacity retention of the BM-2h and
BM-4h electrodes are likely due to the physical properties of
the partially reduced MoO3.23,24 The reduced MoO3−x is
soluble in water, such that the strong interaction between the
binder and particle surfaces cannot be established during the
electrode preparation using a water-based binder that we used
in this study. In Figure S7, the FE-SEM images of the prepared
electrode show that the BM-2h and BM-4h electrodes
developed severe cracks, even before the electrochemical
cycling. Therefore, even though the MoO3 particles, milled for
2 or 4 h, were broken down to nanometer-sized particles, a
poor electrochemical performance was obtained with a low
delithiation capacity and ICE (Table S2). In addition to the
ball-milling time, additional factors, such as ball-milling speed
and ball-to-powder ratio, also aﬀected the electrochemical
performance. Table S3 shows the ICE values of the MoO3
electrodes that were ball-milled at various speeds and ball-to-
powder ratios. In addition to the ball-milling time, the optimal
ball-milling speed (300 rpm) and ball-to-powder ratio (20:1)
were conﬁrmed.
We elucidated the unique charge and discharge reaction
mechanisms of the high-capacity MoO3 transition metal oxide
with conversion reaction using electrochemical and spectro-
scopic analyses and computer modeling. The improvement in
the discharge capacity when the particle size was controlled via
the ball-milling was explained by monitoring the change in the
oxidation state during the discharge process through the ex situ
X-ray absorption spectroscopy analysis, particularly focused on
the valence of molybdenum. Further in situ analysis of the
electrochemical reaction in the cell will help to elucidate the
underlying processes in the molybdenum and lithium oxide
parts during discharge with the particle size from the micro-
and nanoscales. Computational modeling used in this study
examined the formation of the delithiated phase in the outer
region of the MoO3 particle, which prevented the subsequent
delithiation process. The nanoscale particles were more
eﬃcient in mitigating the Li+ concentration gradient and
thus in minimizing the residual Li+ in the particles at the end of
the delithiation process. We believe that the advanced
computation of the multiparticles system can predict and
explain the concentration gradient in the composite electrode
more accurately. In conclusion, we introduced a simple and
fast ball-milling method to generate particles smaller than the
critical size, signiﬁcantly increasing the delithiation capacity
and improving the ICE and cycle performance. These results
provide insights into developing conversion-type electrode
materials with better performance for EVs and energy storage
systems.

0378-7753/© 2020 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
Controlled swelling behavior and stable cycling of silicon/graphite granular 
composite for high energy density in lithium ion batteries 
Dongsoo Lee a, Akira Kondo b, Seungwoo Lee a, Seungcheol Myeong a, Seho Sun a, 
Insung Hwang a, Taeseup Song a,*, Makio Naito b,**, Ungyu Paik a,*** 
a Department of Energy Engineering, Hanyang University, Seoul, 133-791, South Korea 
b Joining and Welding Research Institute, Osaka University, Osaka, 567-0047, Japan   
H I G H L I G H T S  
G R A P H I C A L  A B S T R A C T  
 C@SGG was developed by facile and 
scalable processes. 
 The C@SGG 10% electrode showed the 
long-term cycling performance over 500 
cycles. 
 The electrode swelling behavior was 
measured 
by 
in-situ 
thickness 
measurement. 
 The C@SGG 10% electrode exhibited 
stable electrode swelling behaviors.  
A R T I C L E  I N F O   
Keywords: 
Silicon graphite composite anode 
High energy density 
Long-term cycling performance 
Electrode swelling 
In-situ electrode thickness measurement 
A B S T R A C T   
The severe swelling behavior of silicon-based electrodes caused by the large volume expansion of Si associated 
with Li still prevents the practical implementation of Si. Here, we report the amorphous carbon coated silicon/ 
graphite granule (C@SGG) as an attractive anode material to control the swelling behavior and achieve improved 
electrochemical properties. With the structural uniqueness including inner buffer space, large contact area of 
silicon and graphite, and the uniform surface carbon coating layer, the C@SGG electrode exhibits stable cycling 
performance and regulated electrode swelling behavior. C@SGG shows high reversible capacity of 1150 mA h 
g␀ 1 and improved initial Coulombic efficiency of 85%. We evaluate C@SGG from a practical point of view 
through the characterization of pouch full cells prepared with LiCoO2 as a cathode with a high areal capacity of 
3.4 mA h cm␀ 2. The electrode prepared with the C@SGG of 10% and graphite of 90% (C@SGG 10% electrode) 
exhibits a stable cycling performance with a capacity retention of 71% over 500 cycles at 1 C-rate. Furthermore, 
the C@SGG 10% electrode exhibits stable electrode swelling behaviors comparable to the graphite electrode, 
indicating an initial expansion of 16.0% at a charged state and a cycling expansion of 6.7% over 50 cycles.   
* Corresponding author. 
** Corresponding author. 
*** Corresponding author. 
E-mail addresses: tssong@hanyang.ac.kr (T. Song), m-naito@jwri.osaka-u.ac.jp (M. Naito), upaik@hanyang.ac.kr (U. Paik).  
Contents lists available at ScienceDirect 
Journal of Power Sources 
journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/jpowsour 
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpowsour.2020.228021 
Received 10 December 2019; Received in revised form 21 February 2020; Accepted 10 March 2020   

Journal of Power Sources 457 (2020) 228021
2
1. Introduction 
Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have been widely used in most of elec­
tronic devices and become inseparable things in our lives. However, 
conventional LIBs already reached the limit in terms of the energy 
density of 250 Wh/kg [1–4]. To secure sufficient mileage of electric 
vehicles (EVs), it is necessary to develop the LIBs with high energy 
density. Silicon has been widely studied for the sake of its high theo­
retical specific capacity of 3572 mA h g␀ 1 and 8322 mA h cm␀ 3 (Li15Si4), 
and a relatively low working voltage (~0.5 V vs Li/Liþ) [5,6]. However, 
Si experiences the large volume changes up to 300% associated with Li, 
which leads to poor electrochemical properties caused by loss of the 
electrical contact between active materials and nagging electrolyte 
decomposition with a continuous formation of a SEI (solid electrolyte 
interphase) layer [7,8]. 
Various Si nanostructures have been explored to overcome the innate 
disadvantages of Si by mitigating the stress caused by volume changes 
during cycling [9,10]. However, nanoscale materials have the limitation 
in the increase of the volumetric energy density due to the low bulk 
density and poor Coulombic efficiency due to the large surface area for 
practical applications. In this regard, microscale materials, especially Si 
and carbon (Si/C) composite, have been significantly studied as a 
practical point of view. Microscale Si/C composite materials with the 
void space inside the composite exhibited the improved cycle perfor­
mances due to the carbon material and inner void space which can act as 
a buffer for the volume change of Si [11–13]. However, the Si/C com­
posite electrode with a high areal capacity level (>3 mA h cm␀ 2) have 
not been sufficiently studied. With increase of the areal capacity of the 
Si/C composite electrode, their electrochemical properties are dramat­
ically degraded due to the increased diffusion lengths of charge carriers 
in thicker electrode [14]. Furthermore, more severe volume expansion 
of the composite electrode causes the electrode swelling issues [15,16]. 
The battery safety is of paramount importance for applications that have 
consumers directly interacting with the device especially in consumer 
electronics and EVs [17,18]. Therefore, it is necessary to understand the 
swelling behavior as well as the electrochemical properties of the Si/C 
composite based electrode with the high areal capacity. 
Here, we report the silicon/graphite granular composite with the 
uniform amorphous carbon coating layer (C@SGG) as an anode material 
to improve the energy density and control the electrode swelling 
behavior of LIBs. C@SGG was prepared via facile and low-cost pro­
cessing route where silicon/graphite granule (SGG) was synthesized 
through the spray drying and subsequently, the amorphous carbon was 
uniformly coated on the surface of SGG through the mechanical coating 
method (Scheme 1). The homogeneously distributed Si nanoparticles 
and graphite flakes inside the particle could improve the kinetics of Li- 
ion with the large contact area. Furthermore, the uniform inner free 
space and outer amorphous carbon coating layer could buffer the vol­
ume change of Si, which enables the controlled swelling behavior of the 
C@SGG electrode during cycling. With those advantages, The C@SGG 
electrode exhibited the stable cycling performance and improved initial 
Coulombic efficiency of 85% compared to those of the SGG electrode. To 
explore the possibility of the practical implementation of the C@SGG 
active material, the electrochemical properties of the C@SGG and 
graphite composite electrode with a high areal capacity of 3.4 mA h/cm2 
were evaluated using LiCoO2 (LCO) as a cathode. The electrode 
composed of C@SGG of 10% and graphite of 90% (C@SGG 10% elec­
trode) exhibited the long-term cycling performance with a capacity 
retention of 83% over 100 cycles and 71% over 500 cycles under 1C/1C 
(charging/discharging) rate in the pouch full cell evaluation. Further­
more, the C@SGG 10% electrode showed the outstanding discharge rate 
capabilities up to 3C rate with lower interfacial resistances and higher 
Li-ion diffusion coefficient compared to those of the graphite electrode. 
The swelling behavior of the electrodes were monitored through in-situ 
electrode thickness measurement system. The C@SGG 10% electrode 
exhibited stable electrode swelling behavior over 50 cycles, showing 
initial expansion of 16.0% at charged state and cycling expansion rate of 
6.7%. 
2. Experimental 
2.1. Materials synthesis 
2.1.1. Preparation of SGG 
Before spray drying, the graphite flake was prepared through the 
planetary ball milling (FRITSCH, GERMANY), as shown in Fig. S1. The 
weight ratio of graphite flake, Si (HANBYUL), and sucrose (Aldrich) in 
the raw materials for the synthesis of SGG was designed as 55:35:10 and 
they were dispersed in deionized water. After magnetic stirring for 2 h, 
the solution was spray dried through the spray dryer (EYELA, JAPAN) to 
Scheme 1. Schematic illustration of the preparation procedure for SGG by spray drying and C@SGG by mechanical coating method.  
D. Lee et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      

Journal of Power Sources 457 (2020) 228021
3
synthesize SGG with porous structure under the following conditions: 
the rotating speed of the atomizer was 500 rpm; atomizing 190 kPa; The 
blowing speed 0.55 m2/min; the pumping speed was 200 mL/h; the inlet 
temperature of the spray dryer was 110 C. 
2.1.2. Preparation of C@SGG 
To form the uniform surface coating layer on SGG, mechanical 
coating method by using attrition type mill was employed [19]. Initially, 
the temperature of the batch was heated to 90 C using a heater, and 
then SGG and pitch (coal-tar pitch, TCK) were put into the weight ratio 
of 8:2. The input amount of pitch was determined based on the TGA 
result (Fig. S2). The pitch was uniformly coated on the surface of SGG at 
500 rpm for 30 min by the attrition type mill [20]. The as prepared 
powders were heated at 800 C for 2 h in a quartz tube furnace under an 
Ar atmosphere to form the amorphous carbon coating layer on SGG. The 
resulting composite, C@SGG, was ground for the fabrication of the 
electrode. 
2.2. Material characterization 
For analysis of the morphology of the powder and electrodes, field- 
emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, JEOL JSM07600F) 
was employed. The electrode after battery test was washed using 
dimethyl carbonate (DMC, Aldrich) as a solvent before their SEM anal­
ysis. X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku SmartLab) was used to analyze SGG 
and C@SGG. X-ray photoelectron spectrometry (XPS, VG microtech 
ESCA2000) was used for analysis of SGG and C@SGG and the binding 
energy was calibrated based on the peak of C 1s to 284.8 eV. To verify 
the surface coating layer and the elemental mapping of Si and C in the 
particle, transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100F) was 
used. Si content of SGG and C@SGG was identified through ICP-OES 
(Thermo Fisher Scientific). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was 
carried out on a TA Instruments Q500 in successive steps with the same 
specimen to verify the remaining weight of pitch. The weight loss was 
recorded upon heating to 800 C at a rate of 20 C min␀ 1. Raman 
spectroscopy was measured by LabRam Aramis (Horiba Jobin Yvon) and 
An Ar ion laser was used as an excitation source of 514 nm. Nitrogen gas 
adsorption–desorption isotherm analysis was performed on the 
graphite, SGG, and C@SGG samples at ␀ 196 C using 3Fex (Micro­
meritics). Before test, all samples were dried at 80 C for 24 h under 
vacuum. The specific surface area was calculated by Brunauer–Emmett- 
Teller (BET) equation. 
2.3. Electrochemical measurements 
For slurry preparation, the active material, conducting carbon 
(Ketjen black), carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC, Aldrich), and styrene 
butadiene rubber (SBR, 40% emulsion) were placed into the water at a 
weight ratio of 97:0.5:1:1.5 based on the solid, and then mixed with a 
thinky mixer at 2000 rpm for 5 min. Then, the prepared slurry was 
coated onto a copper current collector and dried at 110 C for 10 min. 
Before fabrication of battery, all electrodes were dried at 120 C for 4 h 
in a vacuum oven. For the half-cell test, 2032 round type coin cells were 
fabricated using lithium foil with a thickness of 200 μm as a counter 
electrode. For the full cell test, the anode was pressed by roll press 
machine to achieve a high energy density, and pouch cells were fabri­
cated using LiCoO2 (LCO) with a high areal capacity of 3.4 mA h cm␀ 2 
(21 mg of LCO cm␀ 2) as a cathode where N/P ratio was 1.08. The for­
mation of the pouch full cells were 0.1C and 0.2 C charging of CC-CV and 
discharging of CC modes, respectively between 4.2 and 2.8 V. 1.0 M 
LiPF6 in ethylene carbonate/dimethyl carbonate/diethyl carbonate with 
fluoroethylene carbonate additives (EC/DMC/DEC (1/1/2 vol%) with 
10 wt% FEC, PANAX StarLyte) was used as electrolyte, and a poly­
propylene (PP) separator was used. All of the cells were tested using a 
battery cycle tester (TOSCAT 3000, Toyo System, Tokyo, Japan). EIS 
was measured at OCV (open circuit voltage) of each cell with a 
frequency from 1 to 100,000 Hz and an amplitude of 10 mV using a 
potentiostat (Autolab, PGSTAT – 72637). The Li ion diffusion co­
efficients could be calculated by the equation of DLiþ ¼
R2T2
2A2n4F4C2σ2 where 
DLiþ is Li diffusion coefficient, R is the gas constant (8.314 J K␀ 1 mol␀ 1), 
T is the absolute temperature of 298.15 K, A is the electrode surface area 
(electrode area  surface area of active materials), n is the number of 
transferred electrons, F is the Faraday constant (96500 C mol␀ 1), C is the 
concentration of Li-ions, and σ is the Warburg factor (the slope of the 
Warburg plot). The in-situ thickness change of the pouch full cell was 
observed using a linear gauge (LGK-0110, Mitutoyo, Japan) during 
cycling. 
3. Results and discussion 
3.1. Material characterization 
Fig. 1 shows the microstructural images of SGG and C@SGG parti­
cles. The SGG particle, prepared by the spray drying, shows a granular 
structure with the homogeneous distribution of Si nanoparticles and 
graphite flakes, as shown in Fig. 1a and Fig. S3. The as prepared SGG has 
a large surface area and poor mechanical property due to the weak 
binding strength attributed to the hydrophilic surface of Si nanoparticles 
and the hydrophobic surface of graphite, which leads to the poor initial 
Coulombic efficiency and cycle performance [21,22]. The amorphous 
carbon layer was coated on the surface of SGG particle to overcome 
these problems. The pitch was conformally coated onto the surface of 
SGG particles through the mechanical coating process in a N2 atmo­
sphere, and then the pitch coated SGG particles were heated at 800 C 
for 2 h in an Ar atmosphere for the formation of the amorphous carbon 
coating layer [23]. To characterize the carbon materials Raman analysis 
was employed, as shown in Fig. S4. The pristine graphite is highly ori­
ented with a very low ID/IG ratio of 0.08, however graphite flake shows a 
higher ID/IG ratio of 0.55 due to the defect by the mechanical breakage 
[24]. Raman spectrum of C@SGG exhibits typical amorphous carbon 
with a ID/IG ratio of 0.92 [25]. The whole process is a facile, low cost, 
and scalable approach. The uniform amorphous carbon coating layer 
with a thickness of 30 nm is clearly observed, as shown in Fig. 1b and c. 
The specific surface area of C@SGG (~1.70 m2 g␀ 1) is much lower than 
that of SGG (~96.18 m2 g␀ 1), which proves the uniform amorphous 
carbon coating layer of C@SGG (Fig. S5). The SEM EDS-mapping images 
and the SEM image in backscattered electron mode clearly exhibit the 
uniform distribution of Si and graphite flake (Fig. 1d–f and Figs. S6 and 
S7). Fig. 2a shows the cross-sectional SEM image of the C@SGG particle, 
which clearly confirms the granular structure of C@SGG particle with 
the inner void space. These free space in the C@SGG particle enables the 
facile relaxation of the stress caused by the volume change of Si during 
cycling, which can improve the electrochemical performances of the 
C@SGG without the fracture of the particles. The XRD diffraction pat­
terns for SGG and C@SGG powders (Fig. 2 d) are well matched with the 
peaks of graphite corresponding to (002), (100), (101), and (004), and 
silicon corresponding to (111), (220), and (222) without any impurity 
and the formation of electrochemically inactive phase such as silicon 
carbide [26]. In addition, the intensities of Si 2p and O 1s peaks were 
reduced compared to that of C 1s peak due to the surface amorphous 
carbon coating layer of C@SGG, as shown in the XPS result of Fig. 2e. 
3.2. Electrochemical characterization 
The electrochemical properties of the SGG and C@SGG electrodes 
with an areal capacity of 3 mA h cm␀ 2 were evaluated to demonstrate 
the beneficial effect of the uniform amorphous carbon coating layer 
through the coin half-cell test. Fig. 3a exhibits the voltage profiles of the 
electrode prepared with SGG and C@SGG at the first cycle with a current 
density of 0.3 mA cm␀ 2 (0.1 C) in the voltage window of 0.01–1.5 V vs. 
Li/Liþ. The initial charging (delithiation) capacity of the SGG electrode 
D. Lee et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      

Journal of Power Sources 457 (2020) 228021
4
was 1650 mA h g␀ 1 and its initial Coulombic efficiency was 79%, while 
the C@SGG electrode showed the initial charging capacity of 1150 mA h 
g␀ 1 and the improved initial Coulombic efficiency of 85%. The uniform 
amorphous carbon coating layer of C@SGG could significantly reduce 
the surface area, which minimizes the formation of the solid electrolyte 
interphase (SEI) layer by blocking the direct contact between the 
organic electrolyte and Si/graphite active materials inside, resulting in 
the improved initial Coulombic efficiency. The initial charging capac­
ities of the SGG and C@SGG electrodes were well matched with their 
theoretical capacities based on the content of Si analyzed by ICP-OES 
Fig. 1. SEM images of (a) SGG and (b) C@SGG. (c) TEM images of C@SGG to confirm the surface coating layer. TEM images of the (d) C@SGG and the corresponding 
EDS-mapping images (e, f). 
Fig. 2. (a) SEM image of the cross-section of C@SGG and its corresponding EDS mapping images (b) and (c). (d) The XRD diffraction patterns of SGG and C@SGG. (e) 
XPS spectra of SGG and C@SGG for full survey. 
D. Lee et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      

Journal of Power Sources 457 (2020) 228021
5
measurement (Table S1). The SGG electrode exhibited the rapid capacity 
fading in earlier cycles and showed almost the same capacity as that of 
the C@SGG electrode after 20 cycles (Fig. 3b), which is attributed to the 
continuous electrolyte decomposition and the pulverization of the active 
materials during cycling. The C@SGG electrode showed the stable 
cycling performance at 0.2C rate over 50 cycles while the SGG electrode 
exhibited continuous capacity fading. 
To investigate the practical feasibility, the C@SGG and graphite 
composite electrode was prepared for the pouch full cell evaluation 
using LCO as a cathode that has an areal capacity of 3.4 mA h cm␀ 2 
(Fig. S8) [27]. In order to achieve the high energy density of LIBs, the 
anode electrode was pressed to 1.5 g cc␀ 1. For C@SGG 10% electrode 
(C@SGG 10% and graphite 90%), both the volumetric and gravimetric 
energy density can be improved by about 19% compared to those of the 
graphite electrode. Fig. S9 exhibits 19% improvement in a specific ca­
pacity of the C@SGG 10% electrode compared to the conventional 
graphite electrode. Fig. 3c shows the initial voltage profiles of the full 
cells prepared with the graphite and C@SGG 10% electrodes at 0.1C 
(0.34 mA cm␀ 2) rate. The cell prepared with the C@SGG 10% electrode 
exhibited a high initial Coulombic efficiency of 90%, which is compa­
rable to that of the conventional graphite electrode. To evaluate the 
long-term cycling performance, graphite and C@SGG 10% electrodes 
were evaluated at 1C/1C (charging/discharging) rate for 500 cycles, as 
shown in Fig. 3d. After two formation cycles at the initial 0.1 C and 0.2 
C, the cell prepared with the C@SGG 10% electrode exhibited a capacity 
fading in earlier cycles and the capacity retention of 83% at 100 cycles 
from the 3rd cycle. However, the C@SGG 10% electrode showed the 
outstandingly stable cycling performance after 100 cycles due to the 
released stress, showing the capacity retention of 71% over 500 cycles. 
The graphite electrode showed more stable cycling performance at 
earlier cycles, showing the capacity retention of 93% at 100 cycles from 
the 3rd cycle. After that, the graphite electrode exhibited continuously 
faded capacities in the long-term cycling and had lower capacities than 
those of the C@SGG electrode after 300 cycles. The capacity retention of 
the graphite electrode was 66% over 500 cycles from the 3rd cycle. To 
investigate the composite electrode with a high content of C@SGG, the 
C@SGG 20% electrodes with different electrode densities of 1.2 and 1.5 
g cc␀ 1 were evaluated in the coin half-cell test. The C@SGG 20% elec­
trode with an electrode density of 1.2 g cc␀ 1 showed the high initial 
Coulombic efficiency of 90% and the reversible capacity (delithiation) of 
510 mA h g␀ 1 at 0.1 C compared to those of the C@SGG 20% electrode 
with an electrode density of 1.5 g cc␀ 1, as shown in Fig. S10. Further­
more, the C@SGG 20% electrode with 1.2 g cc␀ 1 showed the stable 
cycling performance with the capacity retention of 80% at 0.2 C rate 
over 100 cycles. However, the cell prepared with the C@SGG 20% 
electrode with 1.5 g cc␀ 1 exhibited poor cycling performance with the 
capacity retention of 63% at 0.2 C rate for 100 cycles. As the content of 
C@SGG in the electrode increases, more void space is needed inside the 
electrode that can mitigate the volume expansion of Si [28]. These re­
sults prove that even if we apply the Si/C composite material which has 
a high capacity, the microstructure of the electrode can have significant 
influence on the Coulombic efficiency and cycling performance of LIBs. 
To evaluate the discharging rate capability of the pouch full cells, the 
discharging rate capability test was conducted up to 3.0 C discharging 
rate holding the charging rate of 0.2 C, as shown in Fig. 4a. The C@SGG 
10% electrode showed the capacity retentions of 95.2, 90.8, 77.7 and 
48.6%, respectively, at 0.5, 1.0, 2.0 and 3.0 C discharging rate. For the 
graphite electrode, the capacity retentions were 96.4, 92.9, 69.6 and 
37.9%, respectively, at 0.5, 1.0, 2.0 and 3.0 C discharging rate. The 
lower capacity retention at a low C rate might be due to the capacity 
fading behavior of the C@SGG 10% electrode in earlier cycles [29]. 
However, the C@SGG 10% electrode showed much higher capacity 
retention at a high C rate, which substantiates the high power of the 
C@SGG and graphite composite electrode due to the high reactivity and 
Li diffusivity of nanoscale Si and the uniform amorphous carbon coating 
layer [30,31]. The fast charging test of the pouch full cells was con­
ducted up to 3.0 C charging rate holding the discharging rate at 0.2 C as 
shown in Fig. 4b. The graphite and C@SGG 10% electrodes present no 
big difference in the capacity retentions up to 3.0 C because the cells 
were charged in the CC-CV (constant current and constant voltage) 
condition. However, the C@SGG 10% electrode showed higher charging 
capacities up to 3.0 C rate compared those of the graphite electrode 
when considering the charging capacity only in the constant current 
region (Fig. 4c and Fig. S11) [6]. Fig. 4d shows the electrochemical 
Fig. 3. (a) Initial charge-discharge profiles of the electrodes prepared with SGG and C@SGG at 0.1C rate between 0.01 and 1.5 V versus Li/Liþ. (b) Cycling per­
formance of the electrodes prepared with SGG and C@SGG at 0.2 C rate. (c) Initial charge-discharge profiles of the full cells prepared with LCO/C@SGG with graphite 
at 0.1C rate between 2.8 and 4.2 V versus Li/Liþ. (d) Cycling performance of the full cells prepared with LCO/C@SGG with graphite at 1C rate (1C ¼ 3.4 mA cm␀ 2). 
D. Lee et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      

Journal of Power Sources 457 (2020) 228021
6
impedance spectra (EIS) of the pouch full cells prepared with the 
graphite and C@SGG 10% electrodes measured at 50% state of charge 
(SOC) after the two formation cycles, displayed in Fig. S12, at 0.1 C and 
0.2 C [32,33]. The C@SGG 10% electrode showed the lower resistance 
of the SEI layer (RSEI, the first semicircle in the high frequency range) 
and the lower charge transfer resistance (Rct, the second semicircle in 
the medium frequency range) compared to those of the graphite elec­
trode (Fig. 4d and Table 1). Furthermore, the C@SGG 10% electrode 
exhibited higher Li-ion diffusion coefficient (DLiþ) of 7.803  10␀ 14 
compared to that of the graphite electrode (1.115  10␀ 14), as shown in 
Fig. 4e and f [34]. Even at 0% SOC, the C@SGG 10% electrode showed 
lower Rct than graphite electrode, as shown in Fig. S13. The volume 
expansion of Si associated with Li inevitably leads to the stress on the 
graphite flake and the amorphous carbon coating layer in the composite 
electrode, however, the granular structure of the C@SGG enables to 
release the stress caused by the volume expansion of Si. Therefore, Liþ
ion of the shell of LixSi can efficiently move to the inner core of Si 
nanoparticle and graphite due to the released stress [35]. The above 
results demonstrate that the charging and discharging rate capabilities 
could be improved when employing C@SGG. 
3.3. Electrode swelling characterization 
In order to monitor the swelling behavior of the C@SGG and graphite 
composite electrode, we measured the thickness change of the pouch full 
cells using in-situ electrode thickness measurement system (TMS) 
(Fig. 5a) [36]. Generally, graphite is known to have a volume expansion 
of 10% when charged [37,38]. The graphite electrode exhibited the 
initial volume expansion of 12.7% when first charged at 0.1 C and the 
volume expansion of 14.5% when third charged at 1 C after two for­
mation cycles at 0.1 C and 0.2 C. The graphite electrode showed the 
stable electrode swelling behavior during cycling, and the cycling 
expansion rate after 50 cycles at the discharged state is 5.6% based on 
the discharged state after two formation cycles. In case of the C@SGG 
10% electrode, it showed volume expansions of 13.9% when first 
charged at 0.1C and 16.0% when third charged after two formation 
cycles. Furthermore, the C@SGG 10% electrode presented the saturated 
electrode swelling behavior during cycling, showing cycling expansion 
rate of 6.7% after 50 cycles. The C@SGG electrode showed 13.9% higher 
volumetric energy density compared to the graphite electrode even at a 
charged state. The stable swelling behavior of the C@SGG 10% electrode 
can not only allow the restricted space to be used freely in the practical 
cell design, but also improve the battery safety related to the electrode 
swelling issues. However, the C@SGG 20% electrode showed very high 
initial expansion of 23% when first charged and drastically increased 
electrode swelling behavior, which is due to the loss of the internal void 
space of the granule and the electrode caused by the roll pressing of the 
electrode [39]. The increased amount of C@SGG and the reduced 
buffering space in the electrode result in more stress to the active 
Fig. 4. Electrochemical characterization of the pouch 
full cells prepared with the graphite and C@SGG 10% 
electrodes. (a) Discharge rate capability test. (b) 
Charging rate capability test. (c) The constant current 
charging capacity of the full cells. (d) Nyquist plot of 
the full cells at 50% SOC (state of charge) after for­
mation cycles. (e) Warburg plot of impedance as a 
function of ω␀ 1/2 (angular frequency) in the low fre­
quency range. (f) Li diffusion coefficient of the 
graphite and C@SGG 10% electrodes.

D. Lee et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      

Journal of Power Sources 457 (2020) 228021
7
materials and the electrode caused by the volume expansion of Si. In 
addition to the in-situ TMS evaluation, we investigated the 
cross-sectional SEM images of the composite electrodes during cycling, 
as shown in Fig. 5b. The graphite, C@SGG 10%, and C@SGG 20% 
electrodes showed the electrode thickness of 65, 53, and 46 μm, 
respectively before cycling, which is well matched with the electrode 
design with 3.4 mA h cm␀ 2 and 1.5 g cc␀ 1 (Table S2). Furthermore, the 
thickness of the composite electrodes is similar to the in-situ TMS results 
at the charged and discharged state at the 3rd cycle. The graphite 
electrode exhibited a cycling expansion of 6.2% and the C@SGG 10% 
electrode showed a cycling expansion of 9.4% at a discharged state after 
500 cycles, which is comparable to the graphite electrode. The C@SGG 
20% electrode exhibited the high cycling expansion of 30.4% at a dis­
charged state after 500 cycles. Not only did the C@SGG 10% electrode 
initially show a low expansion rate, it also showed stable electrode 
swelling behavior over 500 cycles. 
4. Conclusions 
In conclusion, we developed C@SGG through the facile, low-cost, 
and scalable process and demonstrated the practical availability of 
C@SGG in terms of the electrochemical properties and the electrode 
swelling behavior. The C@SGG electrode exhibited the high reversible 
capacity of 1150 mA h g␀ 1 and the improved initial Coulombic effi­
ciency of 85% with a high areal capacity level of 3 mA h cm␀ 2 in a coin 
half-cell test. The pouch full cell prepared with LCO as a cathode and the 
C@SGG 10% electrode as an anode showed the long-term cyclability 
with the capacity retention of 71% over 500 cycles at 1C. Furthermore, 
we elucidated the electrode swelling behavior of the C@SGG and 
graphite composite electrode through the in-situ TMS study. The 
C@SGG 10% electrode exhibited a low initial expansion of 16.0% at a 
charged state after the formation cycles and a stable and saturated 
cycling expansion of 6.7% over 50 cycles. Our findings suggest that 
C@SGG is a promising anode material that can be applied to the prac­
tical batteries with a stable cycling performance and safety with a 
Fig. 5. In-situ electrode swelling behavior of the pouch full cells prepared with graphite, C@SGG 10%, and C@SGG 20% electrodes at 1 C (1C ¼ 3.4 mA cm␀ 2) during 
50 galvanostatic cycles. (b) Cross-sectional SEM images of graphite, C@SGG 10% and C@SGG 20% electrodes before and after cycling. The thickness change (%) was 
calculated based on the anode part. 
D. Lee et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      

Journal of Power Sources 457 (2020) 228021
8
controlled swelling behavior for high energy density in LIBs.

Copper Nitride Nanowires Printed Li with Stable Cycling  
for Li Metal Batteries in Carbonate Electrolytes
Dongsoo Lee, Seho Sun, Jiseok Kwon, Hyunjung Park, Minchul Jang, Eunkyung Park, 
Byoungkuk Son, Yeongil Jung, Taeseup Song, and Ungyu Paik*
D. Lee, S. Sun, J. Kwon, Dr. H. Park, Prof. T. Song, Prof. U. Paik
Department of Energy Engineering
Hanyang University
Seoul 133–791, Republic of Korea
E-mail: upaik@hanyang.ac.kr
Dr. M. Jang, E. Park, B. Son
Future Technology Research Center
LG Chem.
Seoul 07796, Republic of Korea
Prof. Y. Jung
School of Materials Science and Engineering
Changwon National University
Changwon, Gyeongnam 641–773, Korea
The ORCID identification number(s) for the author(s) of this article 
can be found under https://doi.org/10.1002/adma.201905573.
DOI: 10.1002/adma.201905573
with high energy density for next-genera-
tion Li secondary batteries.[2] Li metal has 
been intensively studied in recent years 
as an ultimate anode material due to the 
extremely high capacity (3860 mAh g−1) 
and the lowest negative electrochemical 
potential (−3.04  V versus the standard 
hydrogen electrode).[3] Despite the advan-
tages of Li metal batteries (LMBs), its 
practical use has been hindered due to the 
poor electrochemical properties of the Li 
metal anode.[4]
A solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer 
has been known to function as a passiva-
tion layer on the surface of the Li metal 
electrode. 
However, 
physicochemically 
inhomogeneous formation of the SEI 
layer could induce the Li dendrite growth 
during electrodeposition caused by the 
nonuniform current flow and Li ion flux at 
the Li/electrolyte interface.[5] The parasitic 
reaction between newly exposed fresh Li 
and the electrolyte further promotes the Li 
dendrite growth, continuous electrolyte decomposition, and the 
dead Li generation, which leads to low Coulombic efficiency, 
capacity loss, and large volume changes of LMBs. Therefore, it 
is necessary to adopt Li protection layer that has the high Li ion 
conductivity, low electrical conductivity, and chemically/electro-
chemically stable properties to suppress Li dendrite growth and 
to enable dense Li plating behavior. Furthermore, the appro-
priate structural design of the Li protection layer is important 
for the uniform Li ion flux on the Li metal electrode. In this 
regard, various Li metal protection layers have been explored 
to improve the electrochemical properties of LMBs. The glass 
fiber cloth with large polar functional groups was reported as 
the interlayer between the Li metal and separator to uniformly 
distribute Li ions suppressing Li dendrite growth.[6] The Li 
ion affinity leaky film was introduced as a Li protection layer 
to achieve a dendrite-free Li metal anode through the electro-
kinetic surface conduction caused by the high-zeta-potential of 
the polymer layer.[7] However, for the practical use of LMBs, it 
is necessary to develop a facile, low-cost process, and scalable 
methodology as well as the reliable performances.
Here, we report copper nitride nanowires (Cu3N NWs) 
printed Li metal for the practical use in LMBs and its electro-
chemical properties, including Li plating/stripping behaviors, 
cycling stability, and rate capability using carbonate electro-
lytes. By employing one-step roll-press method, Cu3N NWs 
The practical implementation of the lithium metal anode is hindered by 
obstacles such as Li dendrite growth, large volume changes, and poor 
lifespan. Here, copper nitride nanowires (Cu3N NWs) printed Li by a 
facile and low-cost roll-press method is reported, to operate in carbonate 
electrolytes for high-voltage cathode materials. Through one-step roll 
pressing, Cu3N NWs can be conformally printed onto the Li metal surface, 
and form a Li3N@Cu NWs layer on the Li metal. The Li3N@Cu NWs layer 
can assist homogeneous Li-ion flux with the 3D channel structure, as well 
as the high Li-ion conductivity of the Li3N. With those beneficial effects, the 
Li3N@Cu NWs layer can guide Li to deposit into a dense and planar structure 
without Li-dendrite growth. Li metal with Li3N@Cu NWs protection layer 
exhibits outstanding cycling performances even at a high current density 
of 5.0 mA cm−2 with low overpotentials in Li symmetric cells. Furthermore, 
the stable cyclability and improved rate capability can be realized in a full cell 
using LiCoO2 over 300 cycles. When decoupling the irreversible reactions of 
the cathode using Li4Ti5O12, stable cycling performance over 1000 cycles can 
be achieved at a practical current density of ≈2 mA cm−2.
As the battery technology advances, lithium ion batteries (LIBs) 
have revolutionized the modern lifestyle by enabling the rise of 
mobile phones, laptops, cordless electronic gadgets, and more 
recently electric vehicles.[1] Since LIBs were commercialized in 
the 1990s, graphite has been primarily used as an anode mate-
rial due to its low cost and good electrochemical reversibility. 
However, the low gravimetric and volumetric energy densities 
of the graphite have limited the increase in energy density of 
LIBs. Therefore, it is necessary to develop new anode materials 
Adv. Mater. 2020, 32, 1905573

© 2020 WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH & Co. KGaA, Weinheim
1905573  (2 of 9)
www.advmat.de
www.advancedsciencenews.com
could be conformally transferred easily onto the surface of the 
Li metal foil. During the transfer process, Li3N was generated 
through the conversion reaction with Li and Cu3N, resulting 
in the formation of Li3N/Cu (Li3N@Cu) NWs, which was 
confirmed by the XRD patterns and XPS studies. The Li3N 
has the high Li ion conductivity (≈10−3 S cm−1), low electrical 
conductivity and the thermodynamically high stability against 
Li metal, which enhances the reversible Li plating/stripping 
behavior with the suppression of Li dendrite formation. Fur-
thermore, the Li3N@Cu NWs layer formed on the Li metal 
surface has 3D open channel structure, which could facili-
tate homogeneous Li-ion flux at the interface between the Li 
metal and the electrolyte. By the synergetic effect of chemical 
composition and structural uniqueness of the Li3N@Cu NWs 
protection layer, Li metal anode with Li3N@Cu NWs protec-
tion layer (Li3N@Cu NWs–Li) exhibits significant improve-
ments in the electrochemical properties in Li || Li symmetric 
cell, Li || LiCoO2 (LCO) and Li || Li4Ti5O12 (LTO) cells. The 
uniform and dense Li plating behavior could be achieved by 
employing Li3N@Cu NWs protection layer at the current den-
sity of 1.0 mA cm−2 with capacities of up to 20 mAh cm−2. The 
symmetric cell with Li3N@Cu NWs–Li electrode shows excel-
lent cyclability for 50 cycles even at a high current density of 
5  mA cm−2. We also demonstrated that Li3N@Cu NWs–Li 
anode could be applicable to the practical LMBs, showing out-
standing cycling performances at a practical current density 
level (2 mA cm−2) in full cells with LCO cathode over 300 cycles 
and with LTO cathode over 1000 cycles. Furthermore, the stable 
cycling performance was realized in the full cell prepared with 
LiNi0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2 (NCM–811, 3 mAh cm−2) and thin Li elec-
trode of 50  µm in lean electrolyte system over 100 cycles. It 
should be noted that the excellent electrochemical properties of 
the Li3N@Cu NWs–Li electrode could be achieved in the car-
bonate electrolyte.
Figure  1a illustrates the preparation of Cu3N NWs from 
bare Cu foil. To synthesize Cu(OH)2 NWs, a clean Cu foil 
was immersed in a solution of ammonium persulfate and 
sodium hydroxide for 10  min. During this process, Cu(OH)2 
NWs growth could be optically monitored through the color 
changes in both the solution and the Cu foil. Subsequently, the 
Cu3N NWs were synthesized by the nitridation reaction of the 
Cu(OH)2 NWs at 350 °C for 1.5 h under Ar/NH3 mixed flow.[8] 
Bare Cu has a flat surface morphology (Figure 1b). The Cu(OH)2 
NWs are uniformly grown on the Cu foil with a diameter of 
≈300 nm and a few tens of micrometers in length (Figure 1c). 
The morphology of the Cu(OH)2 NWs and color of the foils are 
different as a function of the reaction time of Cu foil and the 
solution, and large aggregated particles with the size of 5 µm 
are formed over 20 min where the Cu3N NWs shows the same 
morphologies with the Cu(OH)2 NWs (Figure S1, Supporting

f) corresponding reference XRD patterns and optical images.

Information).[9] The reaction time of 10  min was selected for 
this work considering the morphology and the electrochemical 
performance described later. The 1D structural morphology and 
the dimension was maintained after the nitridation treatment 
(Figure 1d). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns clearly reveal the 
structural changes for bare Cu, Cu(OH)2 NWs, and Cu3N NWs 
(Figure 1e). The XRD patterns of the bare Cu, Cu(OH)2 NWs, 
and Cu3N NWs are well matched with the reference peaks of Cu 
phase with cubic structure, Cu(OH)2 with orthorhombic struc-
ture, and Cu3N with cubic structure, respectively (Figure  1f). 
The clear color changes of the Cu foil could also be observed in 
the optical images (inset of Figure 1f).[10]
Figure  2a schematically illustrates the one-step Cu3N NWs 
printing process onto the Li metal through the facile roll-press 
method. After the roll-pressing process of the Li metal foil and 
Cu3N NWs foil together, the Cu3N NWs foil was peeled off 
where the Cu was clearly observed in the stripped Cu3N NWs 
foil (Figure S2, Supporting Information). The smooth surface of 
the bare Li was conformally covered with a Cu3N NWs layer with 
the thickness of ≈3 µm (Figure 2b–d). During this process, the 
color change of Li metal to dark gray is a clear indication that the 
Cu3N NWs were transferred to the bare Li surface (Figure 2b,c; 
insets). The formation of Li3N through the conversion reaction 
of Cu3N with Li was confirmed by the XRD and XPS results 
(Figure  2e–g).[11,12] The Li3N@Cu NWs–Li exhibits sharp dif-
fraction patterns corresponding to (111) and (200) of Cu with a 
cubic structure, and (001), (101), (002), and (102) of Li3N with 
a hexagonal structure, which clearly substantiates the conver-
sion reaction of Cu3N with Li to Li3N/Cu through the contact by 
roll pressing.[13,14] The formation of Li3N from the printed Cu3N 
NWs layer on Li metal was further investigated by XPS analysis. 
Cu 2p1/2 and Cu 2p3/2 peaks at 953.0 and 933.0 eV, respectively,

www.advancedsciencenews.com
can be assigned to the reduction of Cu3N that appears after 
printing of Cu3N NWs (Figure 2f). In Figure 2g, the formation 
of Li3N is demonstrated by the peak located at 398.0  eV in N 
1s.[15] The conversion of Cu3N to Li3N and Cu was also proved 
by the SAED pattern where the (001), (100), and (101) of the 
Li3N were assigned (Figure S3, Supporting Information).[16] The 
above results substantiate that when Cu3N NWs are printed 
to the Li metal surface through the roll-press process, Li3N@
Cu NWs layer is formed immediately.[11] Li3N has a high Li 
ion conductivity (≈10−3 S cm−1) and low electrical conductivity 
(<10−12 S cm−1), which suggests great potential for stabilization 
of Li metal surfaces.[14,17] Furthermore, the spontaneous conver-
sion reaction from Cu3N to Li3N can be enabled by the lithiophi-
licity of the Cu3N NWs layer. To prove the lithiophilicity of Cu3N 
NWs layer, the molten Li was dropped on the Cu and Cu3N NWs 
foils (Figure S4, Supporting Information).[18] The lithiophilic sur-
face of the Cu3N NWs layer could help the transfer of Cu3N NWs 
onto the Li surface and the conversion of Cu3N to Li3N and Cu.
It has been well known that the ether-based electrolyte shows 
better electrochemical properties compared to the carbonate-
based electrolyte for LMBs system. Li dendrite growth occurs 
more severely in carbonate electrolytes such as ethylene car-
bonate (EC) and diethyl carbonate (DEC) because of the SEI 
layer formation with an inferior elastic modulus compared 
to the oligomer SEI layer attributed to ether-based electro-
lytes such as 1,2-dimethoxyethane (DME) and 1,3-dioxolane 
(DOL).[19] However, the ether-based electrolyte is not stable 
above 4.0 V. Therefore, it limits the use of high voltage cathode 
material such as high nickel layered oxide and LCO.[20] In this 
regard, we explored the electrochemical properties of Li3N@Cu 
NWs–Li anode using carbonate electrolytes. Figure  3 shows 
the Li plating and stripping behavior of bare Li and Li3N@Cu 
NWs–Li electrode with a plating current density of 1.0 mA cm−2. 
After the Li plating of 0.5 mAh cm−2, significant Li dendrite 
growth was observed in bare Li electrode (Figure 3a). When the 
Li was further plated up to 1.0 mAh cm−2, the Li dendrite was

illustration of the Li plating and stripping behavior onto the Li3N@Cu NWs–Li electrode.

1905573  (5 of 9)
www.advmat.de
www.advancedsciencenews.com
generated more severely, and the plated Li layer had the nonu-
niform and porous structure (Figure 3b,c). The cross-sectional 
SEM image (Figure 3c) clearly shows the localized Li plating on 
the Li metal surface. The plated Li was stripped with the same 
capacity of 1.0 mAh cm−2. Needle-like Li dendrite and porous 
surface morphology were observed on the Li metal surface 
(Figure 3d), which leads to poor electrochemical properties by 
the acceleration of the Li dendrite growth, increase in the Li ion 
diffusion path with high tortuosity and the electrolyte decom-
position.[21] On the other hand, when Li of 0.5 mAh cm−2 was 
deposited on the Li3N@Cu NWs–Li electrode, Li was deposited 
from the bottom of the Li3N@Cu NWs layer (Figure 3e). Upon 
further Li plating to 1.0 mAh cm−2, the plated Li conformally 
covered the entire surface of the Li3N@Cu NWs layer without 
the Li dendrite growth (Figure 3f). The uniform and dense Li 
plating behavior was observed on the cross-sectional SEM image 
(Figure 3g). After the Li stripping process, the Li3N@Cu NWs 
layer clearly appears, which indicates the stable and reversible 
Li plating/stripping on the Li3N@Cu NWs layer (Figure 3h).[6] 
Figure  3i shows the schematic illustration of Li plating and 
stripping behavior of Li3N@Cu NWs–Li electrode. In the ini-
tial stage of Li plating, the 3D channels in Li3N@Cu NWs layer 
was filled with Li, and then the flat and uniform Li metal sur-
face is generated by the further plating. Even after the Li strip-
ping, the Li3N@Cu NWs layer could be maintained without the 
mechanical damage and detachment from the Li metal surface. 
Li plating behavior was further monitored with a capacity up to 
20 mAh cm−2 in symmetric cells (Figure S5, Supporting Infor-
mation). When Li of 20 mAh cm−2 was plated on the bare Li 
metal, the Li dendrite growth was accelerated and significant 
dead Li a porous morphology was observed. Conversely, Li3N@
Cu NWs–Li electrode presented stable Li plating behavior with 
the planar and dense Li layer formation.[22] The improvement in 
the Li plating behavior of Li3N@Cu NWs–Li electrode is attrib-
uted to the Li3N@Cu NWs layer, the synergetic effect of chem-
ical composition and structural uniqueness of the Li3N@Cu 
NWs protection layer. High Li-conducting and electrically insu-
lating Li3N and 3D channel structure generating by an NW 
structure enables the uniform Li-ion flux and reduced local 
current density.[23] To verify the chemical composition effect on 
the electrochemical properties, CuO NWs printed Li was also 
prepared. The CuO NWs printed Li electrode has similar mor-
phology to the Li3N@Cu NWs–Li electrode (Figure S6, Sup-
porting Information). During the transfer process, Li2O was 
generated through the conversion reaction with Li and CuO. 
When Li of 3.0 mAh cm−2 was deposited on the CuO NWs 
printed Li electrode, continuous Li dendrite growth occurred on 
the surface of the CuO NWs printed Li electrode. The porous 
morphology and significant dead Li formation were observed 
with further Li plating up to 20 mAh cm−2 (Figure S7, Sup-
porting Information). The different Li plating behavior is attrib-
uted to the thermodynamically high stability and low reduction 
potential of Li3N to the Li metal compared to those of Li2O.[24]
The electrochemical performances of bare Li and Li3N@Cu 
NWs–Li electrodes were evaluated in symmetric Li || Li cells. 
The Li || Li symmetric cells were tested at the same current den-
sities during the charging/discharging process. Figure  4 pre-
sents voltage profiles as a function of time for batteries with bare 
Li and Li3N@Cu NWs–Li electrodes at current densities of 1.0, 
3.0, and 5.0 mA cm−2 with a cycling capacity of 1.0 mAh cm−2. 
At a current density of 1.0 mA cm−2, the cell with the Li3N@Cu 
NWs–Li electrode exhibits stable cycling for 1000 h with a low 
voltage hysteresis (54 mV at initial cycle and 66 mV at 400 cycle, 
as shown in Figures S8 and S9, Supporting Information). In 
contrast, at a current density of 1.0 mA cm−2, the bare Li cell 
shows continuously increasing overpotential after 35 cycles 
(>200  mV) and cell short-circuiting occurred after 40 cycles 
(≈80 h). The XRD and XPS were employed to study the chem-
ical and electrochemical stability of the Li3N@Cu NWs–Li 
after 15 cycles in Li || Li symmetric cells (1.0  mA cm−2 and 
1.0 mAh cm−2). The XRD peaks corresponding to Li3N and Cu 
were clearly observed in the Li3N@Cu NWs–Li after cycling. 
The LiF formation on both bare Li and Li3N@Cu NWs–Li 
electrodes were owing to the formation of the SEI layer by the 
electrolyte decomposition (Figure S10a, Supporting Informa-
tion). The XPS results also show the presence of Cu and Li3N 
in the Li3N@Cu NWs–Li after cycling (Figure S10b–d, Sup-
porting Information).[25] To further investigate the electrochem-
ical property associated with the morphology, the Li3N@Cu 
NWs–Li electrodes prepared with the different Cu3N NWs as 
a function of on the reaction time of Cu and the etching solu-
tion were evaluated at a current density of 2 mA cm−2 with a 
capacity of 2 mAh cm−2 in Li || Li symmetric cells (Figure S11, 
Supporting Information). Li3N@Cu NWs–Li (10  min), pre-
pared with the Cu3N NWs from the 10 min reacted Cu(OH)2 
NWs, shows stable cycle performance with lower overpotentials 
compared to that of the bare Li. However, Li3N@Cu NWs–Li 
(5 min) exhibits similar overpotentials compared to the bare Li 
and the cell was short circuited over 60 h. In case of Li3N@Cu 
NWs–Li (20 min), it shows the higher overpotentials than that 
of the bare Li and the cell was short circuited with drastically 
increased overpotentials over 90 h, which could be attributed 
the large aggregated Cu3N particles (Figure S1h–j, Supporting 
Information). The appropriate coverage and the thickness of 
the Li3N@Cu NWs protection layer is important to stabilize the 
Li metal anode. Furthermore, with the increase of the current 
density, the Li3N@Cu NWs–Li cells continue to exhibit stable 
cycling performances (Figure 4b,c). The overpotential for Li3N@
Cu NWs–Li cells also remains stable at 3.0 and 5.0 mA cm−2 
within 200 h, while the bare Li cells show the dramatically 
increased overpotentials. The cell with Li3N@Cu NWs–Li 
electrode exhibits low Li nucleation overpotential (spike at the 
onset) and Li growth potential (plateau), showing low polariza-
tion potential at different current densities; this further explains 
the stable Li plating and stripping behavior on the Li3N@Cu 
NWs–Li electrode (Figure S12, Supporting Information).[26] 
In addition, the cell with the Li3N@Cu NWs–Li electrode 
shows very stable cycling with low overpotential for 650 h 
(>300 cycles) at a current density of 1.0  mA cm−2 using 1M 
LiTFSi in DME/DOL electrolyte without lithium nitrate 
(LiNO3) additives (Figure S13, Supporting Information).[27] In 
contrast, CuO NWs printed Li cells present poor cycling per-
formances and high overpotentials at current densities of 1.0, 
3.0, and 5.0  mA cm−2 (Figure S14, Supporting Information). 
These results support that uniform Li-ion flux with high sta-
bility against Li metal can enable the improved cycling perfor-
mance with low overpotentials as well as the dense and planar 
Li-plating behavior in symmetric Li || Li cells.[28] To verify the

cycling stability at a high current density with a high capacity, 
the Li || Li symmetric cells were tested at 5  mA cm−2 with 
5 mAh cm−2 using carbonate electrolytes (Figure  4d). At a 
high current density and high capacity cell test, the bare Li cell 
became short within several cycles. On the contrary, the Li3N@
Cu NWs–Li cell exhibited stable cycling performance for 100 h. 
The continuous Li plating behavior at a high current density of 
5 mA cm−2 further confirms that the homogeneous Li ion flux 
of the Li3N@Cu NWs–Li can settle inhomogeneity of the local 
current, and reduce the “tip effect” with uniform Li deposition 
(Figure 4e).[29] Following Sand’s model, the ionic concentration 
in the vicinity of the negative electrode will go to zero in which 
the time (τ) is called “Sand’s time.” The onset of the voltage pro-
file means the evolution of Li dendrite growth.[30] The Li3N@
Cu NWs–Li exhibits three times longer Sand’s time than that 
of the bare Li at a current density of 5 mA cm−2, which proves 
the stable cycling behavior of the Li3N@Cu NWs–Li without Li 
dendrite growth.[31]
To demonstrate the practical use of Li3N@Cu NWs–Li elec-
trodes for LMBs, bare Li || LCO and Li3N@Cu NWs–Li || LCO 
cells were fabricated. The SEM images and XRD patterns of the 
cathode materials (Li4Ti5O12, LiCoO2, and LiNi0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2) 
are shown in Figure S15, Supporting Information. To prove 
the beneficial effect of the Li3N@Cu NWs–Li elaborately, the 
amount of the electrolytes was limited to 8 µL per 1 mAh of 
the cathode capacity.[32] Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was carried 
out to investigate the electrochemical stability of the Li3N@
Cu NWs layer in the presence of Li metal using a Li || LCO 
full cell (Figure S16, Supporting Information). During cycling, 
the main cathodic peak at ≈3.75 V and the main anodic peak at 
≈4.1 V do not change and the profiles are almost overlapped.[33] 
This result supports that the Li3N@Cu NWs layer generated

by the conversion reaction of the Cu3N NWs with the Li metal 
has high stability during electrochemical cycling. Addition-
ally, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) confirmed 
that the cell with the Li3N@Cu NWs–Li electrode has a lower 
charge transfer resistance (34.88 Ω) (indicated by the semi-
circle at high frequencies, Rct) than that of the bare Li-cell 
(99.02 Ω) due to the Li3N@Cu NWs protection layer (Figure 5a 
and Table 1).[34] Furthermore, the calculated diffusion coefficient 
(DLi+) of Li3N@Cu NWs–Li is higher than that of the bare Li, 
which further proves the high ionic conductivity of the Li3N@
Cu NWs protection layer (Figure 5b and Table 1).[35] Figure 5d 
presents galvanostatic measurement results of bare Li || LCO 
and Li3N@Cu NWs–Li || LCO cells at 0.5C (≈1.7 mA cm−2). The 
discharge capacities of the bare Li cell continuously decreased 
and then abruptly dropped after 90 cycles despite a virtually infi-
nite Li reservoir compared to the cathode. Repeated Li dendrite 
growth and dead Li formation lead to the porous Li plating that 
could increase the Li ion diffusion length with high tortuosity 
leading to the capacity fading. On the contrary, the Li3N@Cu 
NWs–Li cell shows very stable cycling performance up to 300 
cycles at 0.5C and presents higher coulombic efficiencies 
(99.81% at 1–50 cycles) than the bare Li cell (98.58% at 1–50 
cycles). The voltage profiles of the Li3N@Cu NWs–Li || LCO 
cell further demonstrates the stable cycling performance at 
0.5C (Figure S17, Supporting Information). After 100 cycles, 
the surface morphology of bare Li and Li3N@Cu NWs–Li was

1905573  (8 of 9)
www.advmat.de
www.advancedsciencenews.com
Adv. Mater. 2020, 32, 1905573
observed to investigate the surface stability of Li (Figure S18, 
Supporting Information). The surface morphology of the bare Li 
electrode exhibited that the porous and needle-like Li was depos-
ited with a thick and porous surface layer of ≈97 µm. However, 
the Li3N@Cu NWs–Li showed a smooth and flat surface mor-
phology, which could be the cause of the improvement in the 
cycling performance of the Li || LCO full cells. Figure 5c shows 
the rate performances of bare Li || LCO and Li3N@Cu NWs–Li 
|| LCO cells. The Li3N@Cu NWs–Li cell delivered much higher 
discharge capacities of 156, 150, 140, 124, 94, and 150 mAh g−1  
at 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, and 0.2C, respectively, than did the bare 
Li cell. The increased discharge capacities of the Li3N@Cu 
NWs–Li are attributed to the lower overpotential, as shown in 
the voltage profiles of Figure S19, Supporting Information. 
After 100 cycles, the Li3N@Cu NWs–Li cell also exhibited 
lower interfacial resistance than the bare Li cell (Figure S20, 
Supporting Information). The performance of the Li3N@Cu 
NWs–Li was further investigated using zero-strain LTO as 
the cathode to verify the stability of the Li3N@Cu NWs–Li 
decoupling the side reactions form the cathode part. EIS 
results show that the Li3N@Cu NWs–Li cell has comparably 
lower interfacial resistance (28.43 Ω) and higher Li-ion diffu-
sion coefficient compared to those of bare Li (34.88 Ω) before 
cycling (Figure S21 and Table S1, Supporting Information). 
For observing long-term cycling performance, the Li || LTO and 
Li3N@Cu NWs–Li || LTO cells were evaluated over 1000 cycles 
at a high rate of 4C (≈2 mA cm−2), as shown in Figure 5e. In 
the Li || LTO cell, the discharge capacity gradually decreased and 
the cell failed after 200 cycles. In sharp contrast, the Li3N@Cu 
NWs–Li || LTO cell shows superior cycling performance, deliv-
ering a stable capacity of ≈125 mAh g−1 without noticeable 
fading. Furthermore, the Li3N@Cu NWs–Li cell shows a much 
lower interfacial resistance after 100 cycles (Figure S22, Sup-
porting Information). The differences in the surface morpholo-
gies further confirmed that the stable cycling performance 
of the Li3N@Cu NWs–Li cell was due to the stable Li plating 
and stripping behaviors (Figure S23, Supporting Information). 
Finally, the Li || NCM–811 (LiNi0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2) cells were 
evaluated over 100 cycles at a high rate of 1C (≈3  mA cm−2) 
with a thin Li electrode of 50 µm in a lean electrolyte system 
to substantiate the practical use of Li3N@Cu NWs–Li, as 
shown in Figure S24, Supporting Information).[32] In the bare 
Li || NCM–811 cell, the discharge capacity drastically decreased 
after 50 cycles due to the poor efficiency and the depletion of 
electrolytes. In contrast, the Li3N@Cu NWs–Li || NCM–811 
cell shows stable cycling performance over 100 cycles without 
noticeable fading.
In summary, we prepared the Cu3N NWs printed Li by a 
facile and low-cost roll-press method. The transferred Cu3N 
NWs can form a Li3N@Cu NWs layer, which was confirmed by 
XRD and XPS results. The Li3N@Cu NWs layer has high Li-ion 
conductivity, high thermodynamic stability against Li metal and 
good lithiophilicity with 3D ionic channel guided NW struc-
ture. It plays a crucial role in promoting homogeneous Li-ion 
flux and reducing local current density. The Li3N@Cu NWs 
layer can prevent Li dendrite growth and dead Li formation, 
supporting stable Li plating and stripping behaviors. In the 
symmetric cell test, the Li3N@Cu NWs–Li electrode presented 
very stable cycling performances even at 5.0 mA cm−2 with the 
capacity of 5.0 mAh cm−2 for 100 h. The Li3N@Cu NWs–Li || 
LCO full cell exhibited improved cycling performance at 0.5C 
up to 300 cycles using carbonate electrolytes. Furthermore, the 
Li3N@Cu NWs–Li || LTO full cell showed ultra-long cycling 
performance at a high rate of 4C over 1000 cycles. We demon-
strated that the Li3N@Cu NWs layer on the Li metal is effective 
for the improved electrochemical properties with dendrite-free 
morphologies for LMBs. To realize the practical applications of 
LMBs, we proposed the rational design of Cu3N NWs printed 
Li electrodes.

[SOURCE: Crystallinity and composition engineering of organic crystal derived 1D carbons for advanced Li-metal based batteries.pdf]
