deposition/dissolution (Figs. S9c and f). In case of the CNT current 
collector, the sharp Li deposits were considerably reduced compared to 
the Cu current collector. But nonuniform Li deposits still remained on 
the surface (Figs. S10a and d). By contrast, relatively uniform Li layer 
was observed on the ONT380 current collector (Figs. S10b and e). This 
result further demonstrates the uniform Li deposition behavior on the 
ONT380 current collector due to the improved electrolyte affinity. Also, 
the rONT380 current collector also shows the similar surface 
morphology with the ONT380 current collector after Li dissolution due 
to the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups (Figs. S10c and 
f). 
Based on the above results, Fig. 6 illustrates how the surface prop­
erties of each current collector affect the Li deposition process over the 
current collector. Bare CNTs have a poor wettability against a liquid 
electrolyte, which results in insufficient contact between the CNT and 
the electrolyte. The poor penetration of the electrolyte into the CNT 
structure leads to poor charge transfer at the CNT|electrolyte interface, 
thereby causing inhomogeneous Li deposition over the CNT scaffold 
[32]. In contrast, the ONT exhibits significantly enhanced electrolyte 
wettability owing to the presence of oxygen-containing functional 
groups on their surfaces. This leads to enhanced electrolyte penetration, 
which greatly facilitates the efficient charge transfer at the ONT| 
Fig. 4. Electrochemical performances. (a) Cycle performances of Li|CNT, Li|ONT380 and Li|rONT380 cells at a current density of 2.0 mA cm−2. Voltage profiles of 
the (b) Li|CNT, (c) Li|ONT380, and the (d) Li|rONT380 cells at the 1st cycle and 160th cycle. (e) Voltage profiles of each cell at the 1st cycle. EIS spectra of each cell 
after (f) 1 cycle and (g) 160 cycles. (h) GITT curves of each cell and (i) the values of IR drops at different points in the GITT curves. (j) Rate performances of each cell 
at different current densities varied from 0.5 mA cm−2 to 10 mA cm−2. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

SEI resistance (RSEI) and charge transfer resistance (Rct) of the Li|CNT, Li| 
ONT380, and Li|rONT380 cell after 1 cycle and after cycling.

cycle) 
Rct (initial 
cycle)

cycling)

cycling) 
CNT 
120 Ω

Short circuit 
ONT380 
40 Ω (overlapped) 
36 Ω (overlapped)

18 Ω (overlapped) 
22 Ω (overlapped)  
S. Lee et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       

Carbon 221 (2024) 118941
8
electrolyte interface, thereby contributing to homogeneous Li deposi­
tion. The improved affinity between the ONT and the electrolyte is 
preserved even after the reduction process carried out under a hydrogen 
gas atmosphere. In addition, the rONT possesses greater electrical con­
ductivity than the ONT due to the decreased surface oxygen content, 
nearly 50% of the increment in the oxidation process. These advanta­
geous features significantly contribute to the improved charge transfer 
at rONT|electrolyte interface, thereby promoting more efficient elec­
trochemical reactions and inducing homogeneous Li deposition. In light 
of all these data, it can be considered that the electrochemical perfor­
mances of the LMBs can be significantly improved by using a current 
collector with a synergistic effect of the high electrolyte affinity and the 
excellent electrical conductivity. 
4. Conclusions 
In this study, we assessed three distinct CNT-based current collectors 
to determine how the surface properties of the current collectors affect 
the Li deposition process and the overall electrochemical performance of 
the LMBs. The hydrophobic surface of the pristine CNT scaffold was 
modified by thermal treatment under an oxidizing atmosphere to obtain 
the ONT scaffold, and this was subsequently treated under a hydrogen 
gas atmosphere to obtain the rONT scaffold. When the three different 
current collectors were evaluated for the Li-metal cells, the Li|CNT cell 
exhibits a slightly better cycling stability than the Li|Cu cell, while the 
Li|ONT380 and Li|rONT380 cells show significantly improved cycling 
performances while retaining a stable CE after 160 cycles at 2.0 mA 
Fig. 5. Li deposition behavior on the CNT, ONT380, and rONT380 current collectors. Top-view SEM images of the (a, c) CNT, (e, g) ONT380, and (i, k) rONT380 
current collectors after Li deposition at 0.2 and 1.0 mAh cm−2. High-magnification SEM images of the (b, d) CNT, (f, h) ONT380, and (j, l) rONT380 current collectors 
of the boxed regions in the low-magnification SEM images on their left. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.) 
Fig. 6. Schematics illustrating the effects of the surface properties of the CNT, ONT, and rONT current collectors on the Li deposition behavior. (A colour version of 
this figure can be viewed online.) 
S. Lee et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       

Carbon 221 (2024) 118941
9
cm−2. This is attributed to the significantly enhanced electrolyte 
wettability of the ONT380 and rONT380 current collectors, which fa­
cilitates efficient charge transfer at the electrolyte|current collector 
interface and, consequently, uniform Li deposition. Under a relatively 
higher current density of 4.0 mA cm−2, the Li|rONT380 cell delivered a 
more stable cycling performance than the Li|ONT380 cell because of the 
enhanced electrical conductivity. These results suggest that the surface 
characteristics of the materials, as well as the materials selection when 
designing a current collector, are important for achieving high-rate and 
stable LMBs. We strongly believe that this work would provide a 
promising strategy for improving the electrochemical stability of LMBs 
and initiate further studies on the current collectors for next-generation 
high-energy-density batteries.

[SOURCE: Design of inorganicorganic bi-layered Li protection layer enabled.pdf]

Chemical Engineering Journal 450 (2022) 137993
Available online 9 July 2022
1385-8947/© 2022 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
Design of inorganic/organic bi-layered Li protection layer enabled 
dendrite-free practical Li metal battery 
Seho Sun 1, Seungcheol Myeong 1, Jiwoon Kim, Dongsoo Lee, Jeongheon Kim, Keemin Park, 
Jaeik Kim, Jiseok Kwon, Ungyu Paik *, Taeseup Song * 
Department of Energy Engineering, Hanyang University, Seoul 04763, Republic of Korea   
A R T I C L E  I N F O   
Keywords: 
Li metal battery 
Li protection layer 
artificial SEI layer 
PTFE 
Dendrite-free 
Rollpressing 
A B S T R A C T   
In this work, we report an inorganic/organic bi-layered, composed LiF, Li3N, Li2S, and porous polymer layer 
(LiFNS-PPL), Li protection layer for practical Li metal application toward high energy density Li metal batteries. 
The LiFNS-PPL layer, formed by a facile and scalable roll-pressing between Li metal and LiTFSI and PTFE 
complex film, is composed of an inside (lithium side) robust inorganic layer with 200 nm thick and outside 
(electrolyte side) porous polymer layer with over 20 μm thick. The robust inorganic layer prohibits dendritic Li 
growth. The flexible and porous polymer layer retains the protection layer structure with high Li-ion flux. This 
novel LiFNS-PPL protection layer enables in-plane Li growth underneath the protection layer without Li den­
drites, resulting in high electrochemical performance. Even in the carbonate-based electrolyte and high areal 
capacity, cycle retention showed over 600 h under 1 mA•cm−2 with 5 mAh•cm−2 condition in the Li||Li sym­
metric cell. Full cells paired with high-nickel contented nickel manganese oxide (NCM811) are prepared with a 
high areal capacity of 5 mA h⋅cm−2 NCM cathode and a limited Li reservoir of 4.1 mA h⋅cm−2 (20 μm-thick) with 
0.8 N/P ratios. The Li||NCM full cell with the protection layer presents stable cycle performance over 200 cycles 
with the Li anode efficiency of 99.6% calculated by average Coulombic efficiency. Our facile and scalable process 
for introducing multi-functional Li protection layer delivers good insight into the practical implementation of 
high energy density Li metal batteries.   
1. Introduction 
With the rapid development and growth of the electric vehicle 
market, consumers demands advanced batteries with high energy den­
sity and low cost. Rechargeable Li metal batteries (LMBs) have received 
significant attention as the most promising advanced batteries due to 
high capacity (~3,860 mAh g−1) and low redox potential (−3.04 V vs. 
SHE) of Li metal anode [1]. However, various challenges, including 
long-term stability and safety issues, should be addressed for the prac­
tical implementation of Li metal anode. The hurdles on LMBs signifi­
cantly depend on the interfacial electrochemical/chemical reactivity 
between the Li metal surface and electrolyte [2]. High chemical reac­
tivity between the Li metal and carbonate-based electrolytes results in 
forming a solid electrolyte interface (SEI) layer on the Li surface. A 
physicochemical inhomogeneity of the SEI layer results in the nonuni­
form current flows with the localization of current density, inducing 
dendritic Li growth [3]. Accordingly, a thick and porous SEI layer is 
formed by repeated surface reactions, which increases overall cell 
resistance and results in poor electrochemical properties. More seri­
ously, the dendritic Li growth could cause short-circuits between the 
anode and cathode, resulting in an exothermic reaction and catastrophic 
fires [4]. To address the issues, the Li protection layer and artificial SEI 
layer are promising solutions for stabilizing the reactive Li metal surface 
[5]. 
There have been explosive efforts to understand the electro/ 
mechano-chemistry of the Li metal surface and Li protection layer in 
the last few years. From the electrochemical perspective, the Li protec­
tion layer should have an insulating property with the high Li-ion con­
ductivity to enable Li deposition underneath the protection layer and 
mass transport of Li ions with uniform flux. The uniform flux releases a 
local current density which is a major reason for dendritic Li growth. 
From the mechanochemical perspective, the protection layer should 
* Corresponding authors. 
E-mail addresses: upaik@hanyang.ac.kr (U. Paik), tssong@hanyang.ac.kr (T. Song).   
1 Seho Sun and Seungcheol Myeong contributed equally to this work. 
Contents lists available at ScienceDirect 
Chemical Engineering Journal 
journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/cej 
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2022.137993 
Received 28 April 2022; Received in revised form 1 July 2022; Accepted 6 July 2022   

Chemical Engineering Journal 450 (2022) 137993
2
have a high elastic modulus with good elongation properties to physi­
cally suppress the Li dendrite growth enabling planar Li deposition [6]. 
Based on the understanding of the electro/mechano-chemistry, material 
selection for designing the Li protection layer should be carefully con­
ducted considering the above issues. 
Various organic, inorganic, and hybrid materials have been reported 
as a Li protection layer. In particular, organic materials such as poly­
ethylene oxides [7], siloxanes [8], UV-curable resins [9], poly­
acrylonitriles [10], polyvinyl alcohols [11], and polyvinylidene 
fluorides [12] have been studied. The organic protection layers have 
limits in mechanical stability with low Young’s modulus not to prevent 
the dendritic Li metal growth. In the case of the inorganic protection 
layers, some lithium compounds such as LiF [13], LiCl [14], Li2O [15], 
Li2S [16], Li3PO4 [17], Li3N [18], and their complexes [19] have been 
studied due to their high mechanical strength and high Li ion- 
conducting properties with the insulating nature. But the complexity 
and poor processability for forming dense Li protection layer on Li metal 
have inhibited its successful implantation. In the case of the organic/ 
inorganic hybrid Li protection layers, there are complementary features 
with high mechanical strength and high Li-ion conductivity [20]. To 
maximize the material utilization of such a hybrid design, design rules 
for structure and composition are required. Additionally, for the suc­
cessful commercialization of LMBs, it is necessary to develop a facile, 
cost-effective, eco-friendly, and scalable process to form a Li protection 
layer with reliable performances [21]. 
In this contribution, we demonstrate a multi-functional bi-layer 
structured Li protection layer composed of a robust inorganic layer of 
LiF, Li3N, Li2S, and a homogeneously porous polymer layer (LiFNS-PPL) 
by the facile roll-pressing process. This bi-layered Li protection layer is 
well designed considering Li-ion kinetic, electrochemical, and me­
chanical properties. The inorganic layer, positioned between Li metal 
and an organic layer, brings sufficient mechanical hardness and Li 
conducting and/or Li-ion diffusivity properties. The organic layer, 
placed on the inorganic layer, supports the inorganic layer providing 
sufficient flexibility. Moreover, the organic layer is designed as a ho­
mogeneous nano-porous structure, which brings a uniform Li conduc­
tion path. This LiFNS-PPL protection layer inhibits direct contact with 
the plated Li metal and electrolyte and retains a homogeneous SEI layer 
structure, resulting in spherical and smooth Li deposition underneath 
the protection layer. Correspondingly, the LiFNS-PPL coated Li anodes 
(LiFNS-PPL Li) delivered significantly improved cyclic stability than 
bare Li metal anodes in both symmetric cell and full cell paired with 
high-nickel contented nickel manganese oxide (NCM811) cathodes in a 
carbonate electrolyte. 
2. Results and discussion 
2.1. Concept and fabrication strategy 
For the design of the Li protection layer, design principles are 
focused on three aspects: (1) mechanical hardness to prevent the 
cracking from Li growth, (2) Li+ and e- kinetics of high Li+ conductivity, 
uniform flux, and electrical insulating properties, and (3) chemical 
stability for the undesired chemical reaction by a liquid electrolyte. To 
satisfy these three aspects, the required properties at the interface of the 
Li metal/Li protection layer and Li protection layer/electrolyte are 
considered in detail for their different required properties (Scheme (a)). 
The robust and compact layer, positioned on the Li metal surface, has 
sufficient mechanical hardness and Li conducting and/or diffusivity 
properties. The flexible and porous layer, positioned between the inor­
ganic protection layer and liquid electrolytes, brings mechanical flexi­
bility to the protection layer. Also, it should provide Li-ion conducting 
paths with the uniform Li-ion flux. Regarding the aforementioned re­
quirements, the inorganic/organic bi-layered Li protection layer is 
devised. The bi-layered structure is consist of a dense inorganic thin 
layer and a porous organic thick layer. 
As shown in scheme (b), for the formation of the bi-layered protec­
tion layer, we contrive facile and scalable two-step processing, the 
rolling and bathing. First of all, an organic and inorganic filler composite 
mixture was prepared. As an organic matrix, polytetrafluoroethylene 
(PTFE) was selected due to its chemical stability in the presence of 
carbonate/ether electrolytes, high elasticity, and insulating nature. 
Additionally, PTFE is a good source of fluorine for LiF during a reductive 
reaction of PTFE with Li metal. Lithium bis(trifluoromethanesulfonyl) 
imide (LiTFSI) is used as a filler in a polymer matrix and a source of 
fluorine, nitrogen, and sulfur during the reduction reaction. Also, the 
huge anion size of TFSI- is advantageous for solubility in the PTFE ma­
trix, resulting in a homogeneous solid-solution state. To prepare the 
composite film (LiTFSI@PTFE), we dissolved an appropriate amount of 
LiTFSI in a PTFE emulsion. After dissolving the LiTFSI, the colloidal 
emulsion of PTFE was broken by the electric double layer compression 
effect of Li+ and TFSI- ions, and LiTFSI@PTFE chunks were formed 
(Fig. S1). The chunks were dried in a vacuum chamber at 110 ◦C. The 
dried LiTFSI@PTFE chunks were kneaded, followed by roll-pressing to 
produce a compact and thin LiTFSI@PTFE film (Fig. S2). The prepared 
LiTFSI@PTFE film was roll-pressed with Li metal. As a result of pressing, 
the composites are reduced on the interface between the Li metal and 
LiTFSI@PTFE, forming a dense inorganic layer. After then, the 
remaining LiTFSI in the PTFE matrix dissolves into the electrolyte, 
providing a homogeneous nano-porous electrolyte path. 
2.2. Analysis on LiTFSI@PTFE film and porous polymer layer 
Fig. 1a-b show the chemical and crystallographic state of LiTFSI and 
PTFE in LiTFSI@PTFE film by Raman spectroscopy and X-ray diffraction 
analysis. Fig. 1a shows the Raman spectra of LiTFSI@PTFE films as a 
function of wt% of LiTFSI in PTFE matrix, bulk LiTFSI, and PTFE film. 
Bulk LiTFSI and PTFE film exhibit S-N-S bond bending vibration at 
749.2 cm−1 and symmetric F-C-F stretching at 734 cm−1, respectively. 
Interestingly, the S-N-S bending vibration peaks shifts gradually to a 
lower wavenumber (from 747.9 to 745.2) as the LiTFSI content in PTFE 
decreased. The redshift of S-N-S bending vibration peaks indicates ionic 
association regarding cation–anion coordination structure changes, 
which contributes to the difference in the TFSI- electron density in the 
PTFE matrix [22]. Fig. 1b shows XRD results obtained from bare PTFE, 
LiTFSI salt, and LiTFSI@PTFE films with varied LiTFSI concentrations. 
The black bar in Fig. 1b represents a typical PTFE reference peak. As- 
prepared PTFE from a PTFE emulsion shows an amorphous nature 
with a broad peak centered at 17.76◦[23]. Upon addition of LiTFSI salt, 
the broad peak tends to shift to a lower Bragg angle from 17.2◦to 17.1◦
slightly [24]. Moreover, no additional peaks such as 19.0◦(a main peak 
of the LiTFSI XRD) were observed, indicating dissociation of LiTFSI salt 
in the PTFE matrix. Raman spectroscopy and XRD analyses results 
indicate that the LiTFSI@PTFE complex system has a homogeneous 
nature, which suggests LiTFSI salt exists not in its bulk state but in the 
solid solution state in PTFE matrix. The prepared LiTFSI@PTFE film was 
roll-pressed with Li metal to transfer the LiTFSI@PTFE layer on Li metal 
and form an inorganic layer. As a result of pressing, corrosion reaction 
between LiTFSI@PTFE film and Li metal occurs (Fig. S3). That forms an 
interface layer consisting of the LiF, Li3N, and Li2S inorganic composites 
[20c]. The color of the protection layer became dark due to the 
defluorination of the PTFE polymer. After transferring the LiTFSI@PTFE 
film to Li metal, the LiTFSI@PTFE layer maintained its compactness well 
(Fig. S4). After the remaining LiTFSI was completely dissolved by 
bathing in dimethyl carbonate solvent (Fig. S5), pore volume and sur­
face area measurements were carried out using Brunauer-Emmett-Teller 
(BET) analysis (Fig. S6). As the amount of LiTFSI salt increases, the 
surface area of the dissolved film (porous polymer layer, PPL) tends to 
increase (Fig. S6a). In the case of pore size distribution, the PPL of 15 wt 
% LiTFSI in the PTFE showed the highest quantity of micropores and 
mesopores at 5–50 Å (Fig. S6b, c). The BET analysis results confirmed 
that the pores is well constructed in PPL layer, which could provide the 
S. Sun et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      

Chemical Engineering Journal 450 (2022) 137993
3
ion transport paths well (Fig. 1c). To identifying the Li ion transfer at the 
micropores of PPL, Li-ion transfer resistance (Rion) was evaluated by 
electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) on the symmetric cell 
(Fig. S7) [25]. As shown in Fig. 1d, the ion transport resistances at PPL 
pores tended to decrease as the concentration of LiTFSI in PTFE@LiTFSI 
increased. The micropores in PPL layer acts a Li ion transport path, and 
the relationship between the content of LiTFSI salt and the development 
of the transport path seems to correlate with the results of the BET 
analysis. Also, the micropores are constructed in PPL homogeneously for 
Li-ion paths resulting from the homogeneity of LiTFSI in PTFE (Fig. 1a- 
b). Additionally, to compare with a heterogeneous mixture of other Li 
salt and PTFE, lithium nitrate (LiNO3) and PTFE composite films were 
fabricated with the same methodology of the LiTFSI@PTFE. After the 
dissolution of LiNO3 in PTFE, bulk pores (~20 μm) were formed on the 
PTFE film resulting from the heterogeneity of LiNO3 in PTFE matrix, 
indicating LiNO3 aggregated in the LiNO3@PTFE film not LiNO3 – PTFE 
solid solution (Figs. S8 and S9). 
2.3. Analysis of the dense inorganic layer 
For the microscopic analysis of the inorganic layer between Li metal 
and PTFE@LiTFSI film, the bi-layered Li protection layer was carefully 
detached with a tweezer. A scanning electron microscope (SEM) image 
shows an inner inorganic layer, directly reduced with Li metal and 
LiTFSI@PTFE at the interface (Fig. 2a). Despite careful handling, the 
inorganic inner layer cracked during detachment. Still, it clearly showed 
that the inorganic layer formed uniformly between PPL and Li metal, 
which indicates the bi-layered structure of the Li metal protection layer 
(Fig. 2b). The thickness of the inorganic layer was about 200 nm and the 
morphology was dense and compact (Fig. 2c). X-ray photoelectron 
spectroscopy analysis was conducted for various etching times (Fig. 2d, 
pristine, 100 s, and 200 s) to investigate the chemical bonding and 
composition by depth profile. To evaluate the XPS analysis, the 
PTFE@LiTFSI layer and inorganic layer were positioned on the upper 
Fig. 1. (a) Raman spectroscopy analysis on PTFE bare, LiTFSI, and LiTFSI@PTFE films varied with LiTFSI concentration in PTFE matrix. (b) X-ray diffraction analysis 
for analysis of crystallographic information. (c) BET surface area and pore volume analysis on porous PTFE layer after dissolving remaining LiTFSI. (d) Rion analysis of 
the LiFNS-PPL layer. 
Fig. 2. (a) SEM image of LiFNS layer between Li metal and porous PTFE layer. 
(b) Schematic information of bi-layered LiFNS-PPL structure. (c) High magni­
fication SEM image of (a). (d) X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analysis via 
etching time for evaluation of decreasing LiTFSI amount and increasing 
amounts of reduced species (LiF, Li3N, Li2S). 
S. Sun et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      

Chemical Engineering Journal 450 (2022) 137993
4
part and lower part, respectively. Thus, chemical bonding changes of the 
inorganic layer in the vicinity of Li metal were observed by etching the 
sample. At the F1s, N1s, and S2p spectrums, the LiTFSI salt peaks 
decreased (pink dot line in F1s, N1s, and S2p spectrum), while the 
inorganic peaks such as LiF (blue dot line at F1s), Li3N (orange dot line at 
N1s), and Li2S (purple line at S2p) increased for increased etching time. 
By roll-pressing, LiTFSI salt reacted with Li metal actively, forming 
inorganic Li compound at the vicinity of Li metal. Additionally, the CF2 
bonds also decomposed to sp2 carbons (Fig. S10), which indicated that 
the reduction reaction occurred between Li metal and both LiTFSI and 
PTFE by roll-pressing at the Li metal and PTFE@LiTFSI interface. By 
bathing process, the un-reacted LiTFSI salts dissolved to dimethyl car­
bonate solvent. The O 1 s spectra in Fig. S10 imply that residual solvents 
trapped into the generated pores in the polymeric layer. 
2.4. Li plates underneath the Li protection layer 
The Li morphology was examined after plating on the Li anodes by 
depositing 1 mAh cm-2Li with dimethyl carbonate/fluoroethylene car­
bonate (4/1 v/v with LiPF6 1 M) electrolyte with 700 ppm LiNO3 ad­
ditive at a current of 1 mA cm−2 (Fig. 3). The Li metal plated on a bare Li 
electrode showed a porous and dendritic morphology (Fig. S11). 
Fig. 3a–c show the Li deposition behavior with a dense PTFE layer 
prepared without LiTFSI salts resulting in poorly permeable electrolyte 
paths to identify the effect of the homogeneous porous layer. During Li 
deposition, Li metal growth occurred. The overpotential for Li nucle­
ation was very high, but that of growth was low (170 mV for nucleation 
and 30 mV for growth), which indicates that as Li growth after nucle­
ation, the PTFE film cracks, and the localized current deepens in that 
part (Fig. 3a and S12). We confirmed that plated Li was located on the 
surface of the PTFE film (Fig. 3b). Considering the electronic insulating 
nature of the PTFE film, the grown Li would have been rooted on the Li 
metal and penetrated the weak areas of the PTFE film. The plated Li on 
the PTFE film has a mossy and dendritic shape (Fig. 3c and S13). In 
contrast, the Li electrode protected by LiFNS-PPL showed no Li depo­
sition on the protection layer over a large area (Fig. 3d). Deposited Li 
was located inside the LiFNS-PPL Li protection layer, and its morphology 
was sphere and rod (Fig. 3e-f). The cross-sectional view was evaluated 
for confirmation of plated Li (Fig. 3g-i). Fig. 3g shows a secondary 
electron SEM image of the cross-sectional view of LiFNS-PPL Li anode. 
The newly plated Li metal from 1 mAh cm−2 plating is shown as bright 
regions. The brightness originated from the charging effect of SEM 
evaluation due to the porosity difference between the bulk and plated Li 
metal. Both bulk Li metal and newly plated Li metal appeared dark color 
when using backscattered electron detection mode. The porous PTFE 
film appears bright due to the atomic weight difference (Fig. 3h). The 
differences between the between the secondary electron imaging and 
backscattered electrons mode confirmed that the Li was well plated in 
the LiFNS-PPL protection layer [26]. The thickness of the plated Li was 
6.4 μm, which indicates that the porosity of the plated Li is about 22% 
calculated from the theoretical thickness of the 1 mAh cm-2Li (Fig. 3i). 
2.5. Long-term stability and overpotential analysis: Symmetric cells 
Galvanostatic charge/discharge measurements were carried out to 
evaluate the long-term cycle stability and voltage hysteresis by sym­
metric Li||Li cells. The symmetric cells were cycled with two protocols of 
current density and a capacity of 1 mA cm−2 / 1 mAh cm−2 and 1 mA 
cm−2/5 mA h cm−2. As shown in Fig. 4a-b and S14, LiFNS-PPL protected 
Li metals showed stable long-term cycle performance. In particular, 
LiFNS-PPL15 Li prepared by LiTFSI@PTFE composed of a 15 wt% LiTFSI 
in PTFE matrix showed extremely durable cycle performance over 600 h 
despite its higher overpotential than bare Li at the initial stage for 250 h 
(125 cycles in Fig. 4b). The initial higher overpotential was due to the 
protection layer acting as a resistance, resulting from its dense nature. 
However, the dense structure inhibited direct contact between grown Li 
metal and electrolyte, resulting in superior cycle stability compared with 
the other Li anodes. To analyze practical operation conditions, i.e., high 
capacity, the Li metal anodes were tested at 1 mA cm−2 / 5 mAh cm−2 
(Fig. 4c-g and S15). As shown in Fig. 4c-d, the cycle stability of both 
samples is well-matched with 1 mA cm−2 / 1 mAh cm−2 conditions. 
LiFNS-PPL15 Li showed extremely high stability for 600 h. The initial 
overpotential of the LiFNS-PPL15 Li was slightly higher than that of bare 
Li (Fig. 4e). After 170 h, the trend was changed as same with the 1 mA 
cm2 / 1 mAh cm2 results (Fig. 4f-g). The overpotential trends in both 
protocols suggest that the dense but homogeneously porous structure of 
Fig. 3. Characterization of Li deposition behavior on 
protected Li metal with 1 mAh cm−2 deposition (a-c). 
Li deposition behavior on Li protected with PTFE film: 
(a) low magnification SEM images, (b) high magnifi­
cation image, and (c) dendritic growth on PTFE film. 
(d-f) Li deposition behavior on Li metal protected with 
LiFNS-PPL protection layer. (d) Low magnification 
SEM indicating underneath Li growth in the protec­
tion layer, (e) SEM image after detaching the LiFNS- 
PPL layer, and (f) high magnification SEM image 
showing smooth and planar Li growth behavior. (g-i) 
Cross-sectional SEM image of LiFNS-PPL protected Li: 
(g) secondary electron imaging, (h) backscattered 
electrons image of LiFNS-PPL protected Li after Li 
deposition, and (i) high magnification image of 
deposited Li metal.   
S. Sun et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      

Chemical Engineering Journal 450 (2022) 137993
5
the protection layer enhances the Li anode stability even the initial high 
overpotential. The higher overpotential could be originated from the 
dendrite-free Li deposition underneath the protection layer (Fig. 3). 
Moreover, the long-term stability and initial overpotential according to 
LiTFSI salt concentration suggest that the protection layer properties, 
such as appreciative electrolyte path and mechanical stability, are 
deeply related to the salt concentration (LiFNS-PPL10 and LiFNS-PPL20 
prepared by LiTFSI@PTFE with 10 and 20 wt% LiTFSI in PTFE matrix, 
respectively, Fig. S14 and 15). The LiFNS-PPL10 Li showed a higher 
overpotential and lower cycling stability at both 1 mAh cm−2 and 5 mAh 
cm−2 protocols. The LiFNS-PPL20 Li showed a lower overpotential than 
optimized LiFNS-PPL15 Li. However, there is a tendency for a soft-short 
circuit. This overpotential and long-term cycle stability of LiFNS-PPL 10 
and 20 suggest that the salt concentration affected the thickness of the 
inorganic LiFNS layer and/or pore structure in the PPL layer as a func­
tion of the LiTFSI concentration. After repeated galvanostatic cycles at 
5mAh cm−2, the post-analysis was carried out by SEM to evaluate the 
remaining Li metal, SEI layer, and dead Li layer thickness (Fig. 4h-i). The 
remaining Li metal thickness in the bare Li anode after 450-hour cycling 
was only 80 μm. Regarding pristine Li metal thickness (200 μm), 120 μm 
of Li metal was consumed, and a thick and porous SEI layer and dead Li 
were formed. The thickness of the SEI and dead Li was about 230 μm. 
The SEI layer and dead Li were thick and had high porosity, which 
causes a gradual increase in the cell overpotential (Fig. 4h, Fig. S16). A 
post cycle analysis of the LiFNS-PPL15 Li showed a high thickness of the 
remaining Li metal of 180 μm (Fig. 4i). The thick SEI layer and dead Li 
were not observed, but a solid and winkled layer was observed 
(Fig. S17), which indicates the LiFNS-PPL15 protection layer promoted 
stable Li deposition resulting in high efficiency of Li metal. 
2.6. High energy density Li metal full cell analysis: Thin Li metal (20 μm) 
|| NCM thick electrode (5 mAh cm−2) configuration (N/P = 0.8) 
In practical applications of LMBs, the energy density of the LMBs 
should be competitive with commercially available LIBs with high ca­
pacity [27]. The LiFNS-PPL15 Li metal anode was fabricated with 
extremely thin Li metal for the further evaluation of Li (20 μm, 4.1 mAh 
cm−2) || NCM (LiNi0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2, 5 mAh cm−2) full cells. Before cycle 
tests, the cells were preceded to a formation process, a 0.1C charge and 
discharge one cycle, and a 0.2C charge and discharge one cycle. Long- 
term cycle tests were conducted at 0.2C charge and 0.5C discharge. To 
eliminate the effect of degradation of the NCM cathode, more formation 
process was conducted at the 51st, 101st, and 151st cycles with constant 
charge / constant voltage (CC/CV) mode during both charge and 
discharge. Despite the higher overpotential of the LiFNS-PPL15 Li||NCM 
full cell than that of bare Li || NCM full cell during the formation process 
at an initial charge stage before 3.7 V, the overall specific capacity of 
both cells was almost the same (LiFNS-PPL15 Li||NCM was 214.0 mAh 
g−1 and 87.42% of CE, and bare Li||NCM was 213.7 mAh g−1 and 
87.40% of CE, Fig. S18). The cycle stabilities were dramatically different 
Fig. 4. Long-term stability evaluation by Li||Li sym­
metric cells. (a) Galvanostatic charge–discharge 
voltage profile of Li symmetric cells at 1 mA cm−2 and 
1 mAh cm−2. (b) Average voltage hysteresis during 
each cycle of (a). (c) Galvanostatic charge–discharge 
voltage profile of Li symmetric cells with high ca­
pacities of 1 mA cm−2 and 5 mAh cm−2. (d) Average 
voltage hysteresis during each cycle of (c). (e-g) 
voltage hysteresis curve of (c) during different cycles. 
(h-i) cross-sectional SEM evaluation after cycling of 
the cells evaluated with 5 mAh cm−2. (h) The bare Li|| 
Li symmetric cell after 450 h. (i) LiFNS-PPL15 Li 
symmetric cell after 600 h.   
S. Sun et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      

Chemical Engineering Journal 450 (2022) 137993
6
for both samples (Fig. 5). The LiFNS-PPL15 Li||NCM full cell retained 
80% of the initial capacity at the 150th cycle, significantly better than 
that of the bare Li||NCM. The CE of the bare Li||NCM full cells fluctuated 
after the 70th cycle, resulting in Li metal exhaustion due to its lower Li 
anode efficiency (Fig. 5a). The average apparent efficiency of the bare Li 
was calculated to be 98.8% [28]. On the other hand, the LiFNS-PPL15 Li 
anode showed no fluctuation in CE even at the 200th cycle. The average 
apparent efficiency of protected Li metal was 99.6% (Supplementary 
note 1.). Fig. 5b, c show the increase in overpotential with the number of 
cycles. In both samples, the tendency of the overpotential to increase 
depending on the number of cycles is the same, but the amount is quite 
different. As shown in Fig. 5b, overpotentials of the bare Li||NCM cell 
showed dramatically high both charge/discharges resulting in a 
decrease in specific capacity from 181.12 mAh g−1 to 128.24 mAh g−1 at 
the 50th cycle with a 90 mV increase in overpotential (charge: 100 mV, 
discharge: 80 mV at initial charge and discharge). 
On the other hand, specific capacities of the LiFNS-PPL15 Li||NCM 
changed from 182.52 mAh cm−2 to 162.09 mAh cm−2 with a 35 mV 
(charge: 30 mV, discharge: 40 mV for the initial charge and discharge) 
overpotential increase (Fig. 5c). To analyze the effect of the degradation 
of the cathode materials rather than the Li metal anode, the CC/CV 
charge/discharge condition was added to the cycle evaluation protocol 
at every 50th cycle (Fig. S19). Even after full lithiation and de-lithiation 
of the NCM, the specific capacities of both full cells were identical before 
and after exposure to CC/CV conditions. We confirmed that the domi­
nant factor of the cell degradation is not from the NCM cathode but the 
interfacial resistance such as the thick SEI layer by decomposition of the 
electrolyte and Li metal. 
3. Conclusion 
We have demonstrated the organic–inorganic bi-layered Li protec­
tion layer. The bi-layer is fabricated by one-pot roll-pressing process 
using LiTFE@PTFE complex film, which have unique homogeneity. 
After roll-pressing, robust inorganic LiFNS layer is formed, composed of 
LiF/Li3N/Li2S. Also, after dissolving remaining LiTFSI in unreacted 
LiTFSI@PTFE layer, the nano-pore structure is constructed. We showed 
that this organic/inorganic bi-layer structure with a significant LiTFSI 
salt (LiFNS-PPL15) concentration had extremely stable cycle perfor­
mance for both symmetric and full cells. Even after 600 h of repeated 
plating/stripping with 1 mA cm−2 / 5 mAh cm−2, only 20 μm-thick Li 
was consumed, indicating a 99.6% apparent efficiency. LiFNS-PPL15 
Li||NCM high-energy density full cell with small excess Li (n/p ≈0.8, 
5 mAh cm−2 NCM cathode and 4.1 mAh cm-2Li metal) demonstrated 
stable cycling performance over 200 cycles. This construction strategy of 
the Li protection layer presented here provides important avenues for 
protection layers of chemically and electrochemically unstable metal 
anodes for both Li metal and in other metal battery systems, even so­
dium and potassium.

[SOURCE: Directing the uniform and dense Li deposition via graphene-enhanced separators for high-stability Li metal batteries.pdf]

Electrochimica Acta 495 (2024) 144426
Available online 12 May 2024
0013-4686/© 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
Directing the uniform and dense Li deposition via graphene-enhanced 
separators for high-stability Li metal batteries 
Minsu Park a, Sujeong Woo a, Jihoon Seo b, Junghyun Choi c,*, Euigyung Jeong d,*, Patrick 
Joohyun Kim a,* 
a Department of Applied Chemistry, Kyungpook National University, Daegu 41566, Republic of Korea 
b Department of Chemical & Biomolecular Engineering, Clarkson University, Potsdam, NY, USA 
c School of Chemical, Biological and Battery Engineering, Gachon University, Seongnam-si, Gyeonggi-do 13120, Republic of Korea 
d Department of Textile System Engineering, Kyungpook National University, Daegu 41566, Republic of Korea   
A R T I C L E  I N F O   
Keywords: 
Graphene-enhanced separator 
Li dendrite growth direction control 
Dense Li layer 
Li-metal batteries 
Anode-free batteries 
A B S T R A C T   
Lithium (Li) has been widely studied as a next-generation anode material owing to its high theoretical capacity 
(3860 mAh g−1) and lowest negative potential (−3.04 V vs. SHE). However, the practical applications of Li-metal 
batteries (LMBs) are hindered by uncontrolled Li deposition during the charging/discharging process and low 
Coulombic efficiency. Among the numerous strategies, surface optimization of polymer separators with func­
tional nanomaterials, especially graphene, has achieved significant progress in both academic and industrial 
fields. In this study, we elucidate how the graphene-enhanced separator (GES) improves the electrochemical 
reversibility of Li deposition/dissolution and consequently extends the cyclability of LMBs. When the GES is 
employed in Li|Cu cells, a dense Li layer forms between the graphene layer and Cu current collector after 
multiple Li deposition/dissolution cycles. Furthermore, when the graphene-modified current collector (GMCC) is 
employed, Li dendrites predominantly grow on the graphene surface and migrate toward the separator, allevi­
ating the risk of separator puncture. The Li|Cu cell with GES demonstrates a higher cycle stability and Coulombic 
efficiency under high current conditions (~99 % at 4 mA cm−2) than the Li|Cu and Li|GMCC cells. Moreover, 
anode-free cells paired with the GES exhibited high capacities and consistent cycle performances under both low- 
and high-current conditions.   
1. Introduction 
Over the last few decades, global warming has been noticeably 
intensified by the enormous emission of greenhouse gases such as CO2, 
CH4, N2O [1-3]. Typically, the large amount of CO2 emission results 
from industrial processes and transportation. Thus, the adoption of 
renewable energy has become the most promising solution for miti­
gating the greenhouse effect and achieving the goal of zero emission [4]. 
Simultaneously, rechargeable batteries have been recognized as a so­
lution for overcoming the intermittency of renewable energy [5]. 
Among several energy storage systems, the Li-ion battery (LIB) is widely 
employed in numerous applications, owing to its decent capacity, 
excellent cycle performance, and low self-discharge rate [6,7]. However, 
the existing LIB configuration containing graphite (372 mAh g−1) as an 
anode and LiCoO2 as a cathode has a material-based limitation, pre­
venting these batteries from achieving high energy densities and 
eventually hindering the development of high-performance electronic 
applications (e.g., electric vehicles) [8,9]. 
Metallic Li is one of the most promising anode materials due to its 
high theoretical specific capacity (3860 mAh g−1), ultra-low reduction 
potential (−3.04 V vs. standard hydrogen electrode), and low density 
[10-13]. However, the practical applications of Li-metal anodes are 
limited by certain issues associated with uncontrollable Li deposition 
and low Coulombic efficiency [14]. These significant challenges 
adversely affect the reliability and safety of Li-metal batteries (LMBs). 
To address the inherent issues related to Li-metal anodes, several stra­
tegies, such as (a) the formation of a passivation layer on the Li-metal 
surface [13,15-20], (b) design of functional current collectors [21-27], 
and (c) use of a solid-state electrolyte [28-30], have been explored. The 
utilization of these strategies facilitates the effective enhancement of the 
electrochemical performance of LMBs and reduction of the Li dendrite 
growth. Among the various strategies, separator modification is the 
* Corresponding authors. 
E-mail addresses: junghchoi@gachon.ac.kr (J. Choi), wolfpack@knu.ac.kr (E. Jeong), pjkim@knu.ac.kr (P.J. Kim).  
Contents lists available at ScienceDirect 
Electrochimica Acta 
journal homepage: www.journals.elsevier.com/electrochimica-acta 
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2024.144426 
Received 13 April 2024; Received in revised form 1 May 2024; Accepted 11 May 2024   

Electrochimica Acta 495 (2024) 144426
2
simplest and most effective approach. As separators can be easily inte­
grated into the existing battery assembly lines, this approach is widely 
employed to produce commercial ceramic-coated separators [31-35]. 
Various separator-coating materials have been investigated as alterna­
tives to aluminum oxide–based ceramics to endow commercial separa­
tors with specific functionalities, such as electrical and thermal 
conductivities. For example, Kim et al. demonstrated that the cycle 
stabilities and rate-capabilities of LMBs can be effectively improved 
using graphene-coated separators [36,37]. Despite these advancements, 
the mechanism underlying the observed improvements in the electro­
chemical stability and reversibility of Li-metal deposition/dissolution 
remains unclear. 
In this study, we elucidate the mechanism by which a graphene- 
coated separator enhances the electrochemical reversibility of Li depo­
sition/dissolution and consequently extends the cycle life of LMBs. A 
graphene-modified current collector (GMCC) was prepared for com­
parison to directly investigate how the location of the graphene layer 
influences the geometry of the deposited Li metal layer. The modifica­
tion of separator with graphene has several advantages. First, it reduces 
the local current density applied to the collector, homogenizing Li 
deposition. Second, the graphene layer can store Li ions, allowing the 
accommodation of the volume changes of Li. In the Li|GMCC cells, Li is 
primarily deposited on the surface of the graphene layer. This leads to 
the growth of Li dendrites toward the separator, resulting in the short 
circuit of the battery with the progress of Li deposition/dissolution. In 
the Li|Cu cells with GES, Li is deposited on the outer surface of the 
graphene layer, inducing the growth orientation of the Li dendrites from 
the separator to the Cu current collector. As cycling progresses, a dense 
Li layer forms between the GES and Cu current collector, which prevents 
the generation of ’dead Li’, thereby facilitating more efficient electro­
chemical reactions. Owing to these characteristics, the Li|Cu cell with 
GES exhibited better electrochemical performance than the Li|GMCC 
cell. 
2. Materials and methods 
2.1. Preparation of the GES and GMCC 
Graphene powder with particle sizes in the range of 5–25 μm (Gra­
phene supermarket) was used as a surface-modifying material. First, 90 
wt.% graphene powder and 10 wt.% binder (polyvinylidene fluoride 
(PVDF) were mixed with 1-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP, 99.5 %, SAM­
CHUN) using a planetary mixer (AR-100, THINKY) to form a slurry. 
Thereafter, to obtain the GES and GMCC, the prepared slurry was 
laminated on a polypropylene (PP) separator (Celgard 2400) and a 
copper foil, respectively, via the tape casting method, followed by 
overnight drying in a convection oven at 50 ◦C. Before cell assembly, the 
GES and GMCC were further dried using a vacuum oven. After drying, 
the GES and GMCC were cut into circular disks with diameters of 18 and 
12 mm, respectively, for coin-cell assembly. The actual loading masses 
of graphene on GES and GMCC were 0.99 mg cm−2, respectively. 
2.2. Preparation of the LiFePO4 (LFP) electrode 
LFP powder was used as an active material for preparing the cathode 
of anode-free cells. First, LFP powder, Super P, and PVDF at a mass ratio 
of 8:1:1 was mixed in NMP using the planetary mixing method. There­
after, the prepared slurry was cast on an aluminum foil using a doctor 
blade and dried overnight at a convection oven at 50 ◦C. The modified 
cathode was prepared as a circular disk with a diameter of 12 mm for cell 
assembly. The actual loading masses of LFP was 3.58 mg cm−2, 
respectively. 
2.3. Characterization 
The morphologies of the GES and GMCC were analyzed using field- 
emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi, SU8220). To 
determine the thermal stabilities of the PP separator and GES, the 
samples (40 mm × 40 mm) were placed in a convection oven for 1 h at 
140 ◦C. Mechanical properties of the PP separator and GES were eval­
uated by a universal testing machine (UTM, AND, MCT-2150) at a ten­
sile speed of 30 mm min−1. Raman spectra measurement was conducted 
by Raman spectrometer (Renishaw inVia) to confirm the presence of a 
graphene layer coating. The contact angles with liquid electrolytes were 
measured (Surface Electro Optics, Phoenix-Alpha) at room temperature 
(25 ◦C). 
2.4. Characterizations and electrochemical measurements 
Electrochemical performance was assessed using standard 2032R- 
type coin cells which are fabricated in a glove box filled with Ar gas. 
Furthermore, 1 M LiPF6 in a mixture of ethylene carbonate (EC) and 
diethyl carbonate (DEC) at a 1:1 vol.% ratio (Enchem) was used as an 
electrolyte. Additionally, to compare the electrochemical stability of the 
prepared cells, experiments were conducted using 1.0 T Li foil as the 
counter electrode. The Li deposition process was conducted at an areal 
capacity of 1.0 mAh cm−2, and the cut-off voltage of the Li dissolution 
process was 1.0 V. The Li|Cu cells were evaluated at constant current 
densities of 1.0 and 4.0 mA cm−2. Electrochemical impedance spec­
troscopy (EIS, Biologic, SP-150e) was conducted at a frequency range of 
200 kHz–10 mHz, with an amplitude of 10 mV. For conducting experi­
ments using the galvanostatic intermittent titration technique (GITT), a 
constant current density of 0.2 mA cm−2 was first applied for 10 min, 
and the open-circuit mode was subsequently employed for another 10 
min within a potential window of 0.001–2 V. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) 
measurements were conducted at different scan rates between 0 and 1.0 
V. Additionally, an anode-free cell was fabricated using the combination 
of 1 M LiPF6 with EC:DEC mixture (1:1 vol.%) as an electrolyte. Cycling 
tests were performed on the anode-free cells paired with LFP within the 
voltage range of 3.0–3.8 V. 
3. Results and discussion 
Fig. 1 illustrates the effects of the graphene-layer position on the 
growth direction of Li dendrites. When current starts flowing, Li ions 
immediately diffuse across the GES (Fig. 1a). As the graphene layer 
physically touches the surface of the Cu current collector, the diffused Li 
ions directly receive electrons from the contact spots on the current 
collector. Once the graphene layer is fully lithiated, extra Li ions form Li 
nuclei on it (Fig. 1b). After multiple cycles, the Li nuclei act as seeds for 
forming a Li layer over the graphene surface (Fig. 1c). Considering the 
case with the GMCC, when the current is applied, Li ions immediately 
diffuse across the separator in the same manner as that observed for the 
case of the GES (Fig. 1d). In contrast to the GES, the graphene layer in 
the GMCC is continuously connected to the current collector throughout 
the entire area. Therefore, the electrons received from the current col­
lector can be directly transported to the graphene surface. When the 
diffused Li ions reach the outer surface of the graphene layer, Li ions 
receive electrons and subsequently initiate the formation of Li nuclei 
(Fig. 1e). After repetitive electrochemical reactions, the as-formed Li 
nuclei serve as sites for the development of sharp Li dendrites oriented 
toward the separator (Fig. 1f). These Li dendrites eventually cause a 
short circuit in LMBs, leading to significant safety issues in the battery. 
A graphene slurry was prepared by homogenizing graphene, PVDF, 
and NMP using a planetary mixer (Fig. 2a). Owing to its excellent ad­
hesive properties, the PVDF binder is widely employed to modify the 
surfaces of polymer separators with functional materials [38]. The 
prepared graphene slurry was laminated on a PP separator and a Cu foil 
via tape casting and dried using a convection oven. Fig. 2b shows a 
uniform graphene coating on the separator and current collector, 
demonstrating the scalability of this surface modification technique for 
separators and current collectors. Notably, morphological changes, such 
M. Park et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    

Electrochimica Acta 495 (2024) 144426
3
as cracking, are not observed in the GES and GMCC even after punching 
holes into them. This observation indicates that the graphene layer is 
adhered well to the separator and current collector (Fig. 2b). Surface 
morphologies of the GES and GMCC were characterized via SEM anal­
ysis. The bare separator comprises pores with sizes of ~100 nm, which is 
consistent with the previously reported results (Fig. 2c) [39,40]. The 
pores formed within the separator enable Li-ion migration through the 
liquid electrolyte. The low- and high-magnification top-view SEM im­
ages reveal a uniform distribution of the graphene powder across the 
entire separator and Cu current collector without severe agglomeration 
or film delamination. The lateral size of the graphene powder is ~3 μm 
(Fig. 2d, e, g, and h). For a comparative study, the thickness of graphene 
layers in both the GES and GMCC was precisely controlled at the same 
value of ~20 μm (Fig. 2f and i). 
To evaluate the effects of the graphene layer on the thermal stability 
of polymer separators, a thermal shrinkage test was performed using the 
PP separator and GES (Fig. 2j). The thermal shrinkage of the GES is 
smaller than that of the PP separator, owing to the enhanced thermal 
stability of the GES. Fig. 2k shows the tensile stress–strain curves of the 
PP separator and GES. The tensile strength and Young’s modulus of the 
PP separator are 16.2 and 2.50 MPa, respectively. Notably, the GES 
demonstrates a higher tensile strength and Young’s modulus of 60.2 and 
5.10 MPa, respectively, than the PP separator. The improved thermal 
and mechanical properties of the GES, compared to those of the PP 
separator, are attributed to the presence of the graphene layer in the 
GES. Fig. 2l presents the Raman spectra of PP separator and GES. The 
GES spectrum exhibits D, G, and 2D bands at 1347, 1576, and 2714 
cm−1, respectively, indicating that the GES possesses the characteristic 
structure of graphene [41,42]. Furthermore, the peaks corresponding to 
the PP separator are not observed in the GES spectrum, suggesting the 
complete coverage of the PP separator by the graphene layer. Fig. S1 and 
Supplementary videos 1 and 2 show the disparity in electrolyte wetta­
bility immediately after a liquid electrolyte is dropped onto the PP 
separator and GES, respectively. The electrolyte dropped on the GES (θ 
= 7◦) spreads more efficiently than that on the PP separator (θ = 21◦), 
suggesting that the presence of the graphene layer improves the 
wettability of the electrolyte. 
To investigate the effects of the graphene-layer position on the 
morphology of the deposited Li layer, Li-metal cells comprising the GES 
or GMCC were disassembled after Li deposition. The formation mecha­
nism of the Li layer at the interfacial gap between the graphene layer and 
Cu current collector is illustrated in Fig. 3a. Briefly, Li ions diffuse across 
the separator and reach the outer surface of the graphene layer. Sub­
sequently, the Cu current collector, which is physically in contact with 
the graphene layer, provides electrons to the diffused Li ions at the 
points of contact. Thereafter, Li deposition occurs at the interfacial gap 
between the graphene layer and Cu current collector, forming a dense Li 
layer. The graphene layer acts as an auxiliary current collector and 
effectively confines inactive Li within the region between the graphene 
layer and Cu current collector. This prevents the growth of Li dendrites 
toward the separator and improves the electrochemical reversibility of 
Li deposition/dissolution. The low-, medium-, and high-resolution SEM 
images in Fig. 3b–d demonstrate the Li deposition on the graphene 
surface in contact with the Cu current collector. Between the graphene 
layer and current collector in the Li|Cu cell with GES, the Li dendrites 
grow toward the Cu current collector. The average size of the deposited 
Li is around 1.2 μm (Fig. 3d). Fig. 3e–g reveal that Li does not accu­
mulate in the region between the graphene surface and separator. These 
findings suggest that Li is only deposited at the regions where the gra­
phene layer is in contact with the Cu current collector. Fig. 3h illustrates 
the interface between the GES and Cu current collector after Li deposi­
tion. The cross-sectional SEM images distinctly reveal that the deposited 
Li layer with protruded morphologies migrates to the Cu current col­
lector, indicative of Li accumulation within the anodic side (Fig. 3i and 
j). 
Fig. 4a illustrates the Li deposition in the cell with the GMCC. Since 
the graphene layer is electrically connected to the Cu current collector, 
the Li ions directly receive electrons when they reach outer surface of 
Fig. 1. Schematic illustrating Li deposition during the Li deposition/dissolution in the Li|Cu cell with a GES and an Li|GMCC cell.  
M. Park et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    

Electrochimica Acta 495 (2024) 144426
4
the graphene layer. Li is primarily deposited on the graphene surface 
and grows into Li dendrites, adversely affecting the separator during 
repetitive Li deposition/dissolution. The low-, medium-, and high- 
magnification SEM images presented in Fig. 4b–d display sharp Li 
structures accumulated between the graphene layer and separator. In 
contrast to the case of the Li|Cu cell with GES, the Li deposited on the 
graphene surface presents dendritic morphology, owing to the inho­
mogeneous Li deposition. The SEM image presented in Fig. 4d reveals 
the diameter of the Li accumulated between the graphene layer and 
separator in the Li|GMCC cell as 0.3 μm, which is considerably smaller 
than the Li diameter shown in Fig. 3d. These results are consistent with 
the observations of previous studies. The SEM images in Fig. 4e–g 
demonstrate the Li deposition on and within the graphene layer. It is 
highly likely Li accumulates both on the surface and the interior of the 
graphene layer, owing to the continuous electronic pathway from the 
current collector to the graphene. The SEM image in Fig. 4g displays Li 
dendrites with a diameter of 0.1 μm in the region between the graphene 
layer and current collector in the Li|GMCC cell. This is significantly 
different from the deposition characteristics observed in the area be­
tween the graphene layer and separator in the Li|Cu cell with GES. These 
results clearly reveal that the dendritic Li has grown across the entire 
graphene layer. 
To elucidate the effects of the deposition-layer morphology on the 
electrochemical stability of the LMBs, the Li deposition/dissolution 
process of cells with the GES and GMCC was evaluated. The Coulombic 
efficiency, which is defined as the ratio of the amount of Li stripped from 
Fig. 2. (a) Schematic illustrating the coating of graphene on the PP separator and Cu current collector. (b) Digital images of the GES and GMCC. Top-view SEM 
images of (c) pristine PP separator, (d, g) GES and GMCC. (e, h) High-resolution top-view SEM images and (f, i) cross-sectional SEM images of the GES and GMCC. (j) 
Photographs of the PP separator and GES captured after high-temperature exposure. (k) Tensile stress–strain curves and (l) Raman spectra of the PP separator 
and GES. 
M. Park et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    

Electrochimica Acta 495 (2024) 144426
5
the working electrode to that plated during each cycle, is an important 
parameter for assessing the cycle stability of LMBs. Prior to comparing 
the electrochemical behaviors of GES and GMCC, Fig. S2 illustrates the 
cycling performance and voltage profiles of GES when oriented towards 
current collector and Li anode. The following experiments are per­
formed with the coating layer of the GES facing the current collector, as 
the cycle life characteristics are much better (~99 % at 4 mA cm−2) than 
the GES facing the Li anode (~34.0 % at 4 mA cm−2) at the initial cycle. 
The Li deposition/dissolution efficiency for each cell was tested at a high 
areal current density of 4 mA cm−2, with an areal capacity of 1 mAh 
cm−2. Fig. 5a presents a comparison of the obtained results. The refer­
ence cell containing pristine Cu and a pristine PP separator shows a 
substantial capacity fading after 30 cycles owing to the short circuit 
caused by Li dendrite growth [43]. The Coulombic efficiency of the Li| 
GMCC cell starts to decrease after 40th cycle, reaching 30 % by 100th 
cycle. In contrast to the Li|Cu cell with GES and GMCC, the reference cell 
is short-circuited at ~60th cycle. Among the tested cells, the Li|Cu cell 
with GES exhibits the most stable cycle performance over 100 cycles, 
with a Coulombic efficiency of 80 %. The enhanced efficiency can be 
attributed to the GES’s ability to confine ’dead Li’ within the anodic side 
and facilitate its reutilization. In contrast, in the Li|GMCC cells, the 
Coulombic efficiency significantly decreases over cycles, owing to the 
loss of electrical contact between active Li and current collector. The 
electrochemical performance of the Li|Cu cells at 1 mA cm−2 is 
Fig. 3. (a) Schematic of Li deposition and locations of the reduced and deposited Li ions in the GES. SEM images of the graphene surface in the GES facing (b–d) 
current collector and (e–g) PP separator. (h) Schematic to illustrate the formation of a dense Li layer between the graphene layer and current collector. (i–j) Cross- 
sectional SEM images of the interface between the graphene layer and current collector. 
M. Park et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    

Electrochimica Acta 495 (2024) 144426
6
presented in Fig. S3a. Similar to the electrochemical results obtained at 4 
mA cm−2, the Coulombic efficiency of the Li|GMCC and reference cells 
decreases to 63 % and 35 %, respectively, after cycling. Among the 
tested cells, the Li|Cu cell with GES displays the most stable perfor­
mance, exhibiting an excellent Coulombic efficiency of 92 % even after 
100 cycles. The voltage profiles of the Li|Cu cells measured at a low 
current density (1 mA cm−2) are shown in Fig. S3b–d. 
Fig. 5b–d present voltage profiles of the reference cell, Li|GMCC cell, 
and Li|Cu cell with a GES for the 1st, 50th, and 100th cycle at 4 mA 
cm−2. As shown in Fig. 5b, the initial Coulombic efficiency of the 
reference cell is 79.8 %, which reduces to 30 % after 50 cycles. The cell is 
eventually short-circuited at the 68th cycle. The initial voltage profile of 
the Li|GMCC cell exhibits plateaus at 0.05 and 0.16 V, which are 
attributed to the lithiation of graphene (Fig. 5c). Additionally, the initial 
Coulombic efficiency of the Li|GMCC cell (86.8 %) is higher than that of 
the reference cell. After 50 cycles, the voltage plateaus disappear, and 
the Coulombic efficiency of the Li|GMCC cell decreases to <60 %. After 
100 cycles, although the Li|GMCC cell continues to function, it delivers a 
low Coulombic efficiency of 31.5 % owing to the poor utilization of the 
deposited Li. The profile of the Li|Cu cell with GES demonstrates a long 
plateau region at 0.03 V, corresponding to the graphene lithiation, and 
the cell exhibits an excellent Coulombic efficiency of 97.7 % (Fig. 5d). 
Furthermore, the initial shape of this voltage profile is retained even 
after 50 cycles, indicating the excellent reversibility of the Li deposition/ 
dissolution process. After 100 cycles, the voltage plateaus observed for 
the Li|Cu cell with GES disappear; however, the Coulombic efficiency of 
this cell (77.2 %) is higher than those of the other cells. This can be 
attributed to the effects of the confinement of the deposited Li to the 
anodic side. To check if the decreased Li deposition improves the cycle 
stability, the Li|Cu cell was cycled at 4 mA cm−2, with an areal capacity 
of 0.5 mAh cm−2 (Fig. S4). Even though the deposition capacity is 
halved, the cycle stability of the reference cell is far poorer than the Li| 
Cu cell with GES evaluated at 4 mA cm−2, with an areal capacity of 1 
mAh cm−2. In addition, the Coulombic efficiency of the reference cell is 
below 80 %, which is much lower than the Li|Cu cell with GES (close to 
99 %). These results clearly support that the GES significantly enhances 
both the electrochemical reversibility of Li deposition/dissolution and 
cycle stability of LMBs. 
To investigate the interfacial resistance as a function of the cycle 
number, EIS measurements were conducted on the as-fabricated cells. 
After a cycle, the Ohmic resistance of the Li|GMCC cell is slightly lower 
than that of the Li|Cu cell with GES (Fig. S5). This difference results from 
the position of the graphene coating. The charge-transfer resistances of 
the reference cell, Li|GMCC cell, and Li|Cu cell with GES at first cycle are 
34.5, 16.8, and 10.9 Ω, respectively (Fig. 5e–g). However, after 100 
cycles, the charge-transfer resistance of the Li|GMCC cell (163.1 Ω) in­
creases significantly compared with that of the Li|Cu cell with GES (12.9 
Ω). The charge-transfer resistance of the reference cell after 100 cycles 
Fig. 4. (a) Schematic of Li deposition and the position of reduced and deposited Li ions in the GMCC. SEM images of the graphene surface of the GMCC facing (b–d) 
PP separator and (e–g) Cu current collector. 
M. Park et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    

Electrochimica Acta 495 (2024) 144426
7
could not be measured owing to the short circuit in the battery caused by 
Li dendrites. This indicates that the resistances of the reference and Li| 
GMCC cells increase owing to the electrolyte depletion accelerated by 
the formation of Li dendrites. Conversely, the charge-transfer resistance 
of the Li|Cu cell with GES is similar before and after cycling. These re­
sults suggest that the morphology of the deposited Li varies depending 
on the location of the graphene, significantly influencing the specific 
surface area of the deposited Li. The dense Li formed between the GES 
and current collector possess a smaller surface area than the Li dendrites. 
This morphological feature affects electrolyte consumption and, in turn, 
impacts the Coulombic efficiency and cell impedance. 
To ascertain the substantial effects of the GES and GMCC on elec­
trochemical kinetics and reversibility, Li-metal cells were evaluated at 
different current densities ranging from 0.1 to 5 mA cm−2 (Fig. 6a). 
When the Li deposition/dissolution process is conducted at a low current 
density of 0.1 mA cm−2, the Li|Cu cell with GES exhibits the highest 
Coulombic efficiency of 95 %, which is 5 % higher than that of the other 
cells. In addition, the Li|Cu cell with GES demonstrates excellent 
Coulombic efficiency of 90 % even at a high current density of 5 mA 
cm−2 owing to the efficient electrochemical reaction facilitated by the 
presence of the graphene layer on the separator. As observed in the SEM 
analysis, a dense Li layer forms between the graphene layer and Cu 
current collector, significantly enhancing Li utilization and the electro­
chemical reversibility of the Li deposition/dissolution process. In the 
case of the Li|GMCC cell, dendritic Li grows on the graphene surface and 
is oriented toward the separator. Furthermore, the ’dead Li’ separated 
from the dendritic Li does not participate in the subsequent electro­
chemical reactions in LMBs, significantly reducing the efficiency of Li 
deposition/dissolution. The reference cell displays the typical voltage 
profiles of Li deposition below 0 V vs. Li/Li+ and Li dissolution above 0 V 
vs. Li/Li+ (Fig. 6b). With increasing current density, the voltage gap 
between charging and discharging gradually increases for the reference 
cell. The profiles of the Li|GMCC cell and Li|Cu cell with GES exhibit a 
relatively smaller voltage gap than that observed for the reference cell, 
suggesting that the graphene layer stabilizes the reaction kinetics 
(Fig. 6c and d). 
Furthermore, Li-ion diffusivity in the Li|Cu cell with GES and Li| 
GMCC cell was analyzed via GITT. The GITT curve of the Li|Cu cell with 
GES presented in Fig. 6e displays IR drop (0.021 V) similar to that of the 
Li|GMCC cell (0.027 V) shown in Fig. 6f. The Li-ion diffusion coefficient 
(DLi) of the cells is determined using the following equation: 
DLi = 4
πτ
(mb
ρS
)2(ΔEs
ΔEτ
)2
(1)  
where τ is the duration of the current pulse; mb is the molar mass; ρS is 
the density of the host material; and ΔEs and ΔEτ represent the steady- 
state and transient voltage variations, respectively, during a single gal­
Fig. 5. Electrochemical performances of the Li|Cu cells with PP, GES, and Li|GMCC cell. (a) Cycle performances of the cells at 4 mA cm−2. (b–d) Voltage profiles and 
(e–g) Nyquist plots of the cells at 1st, 50th, and 100th cycle. 
M. Park et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    

Electrochimica Acta 495 (2024) 144426
8
vanostatic titration step. The average diffusion coefficients of the Li|Cu 
cell with GES and the Li|GMCC cell are 9.53 × 10−11 and 9.88 × 10−11 
cm2 S−1, respectively. These results suggest that the diffusion kinetics of 
LMBs are not significantly affected by the location of the graphene 
coating. The Li|Cu cell with GES demonstrates an excellent Coulombic 
efficiency of ~90 % even at a current density of 5 mA cm−2, which is 
considerably higher than that of the Li|GMCC cell (80 %). The high 
Coulombic efficiency of the Li|Cu cell with GES is due to the efficient 
utilization of the Li layer, which is confined between the graphene layer 
and Cu current collector during the electrochemical reaction. These 
results suggest that the Coulombic efficiency of the Li deposition/ 
dissolution process can be affected by the position of the graphene layer 
in Li-metal cells. As shown in Fig. S6, Cyclic voltammetry (CV) test was 
conducted to compare the Li-ion diffusivity of Li|Cu cells with GES and 
GMCC in different scan rates from 0.2 to 1 mV s−1. 
To compare the effects of the GES and GMCC on the preservation of 
active Li in the LMB system, anode-free cells comprising an LFP elec­
trode were analyzed. As LFP has a limited amount of available Li ions, 
the electrochemical performance of cells can be directly compared. Prior 
to the cell assembly, both the GES and GMCC were pre-activated by 
placing a Li-metal foil on them for 30 min. This step facilitates the for­
mation of a stable solid electrolyte interface (SEI) layer, which mini­
mizes the consumption of active Li during initial lithiation. No additive 
was included in the tested electrolyte in order to exclude any synergistic 
effects from electrolyte additives. For achieving an effective comparison, 
the bare Cu foil in the reference cell was maintained in contact with the 
Li-metal foil for 30 min. The anode-free cells were evaluated at a low 
current density of 0.1 C (1 C = 163 mAh g−1) and voltage range of 3–3.8 
V vs. Li/Li+ (Fig. 7a). The Cu|PP|LFP cell delivers a low initial discharge 
capacity of 68 mAh g−1 and exhibits near-zero capacity at 30th cycle. 
This capacity fading is attributed to the severe depletion of the active Li 
ions because of the poor electrochemical reversibility during the Li 
deposition/dissolution process. The GMCC|PP|LFP cell delivers an 
improved initial discharge capacity of 121 mAh g−1. Notably, among the 
tested cells, the Cu|GES|LFP cell exhibits the highest initial discharge 
capacity of 144 mAh g−1 and maintains a capacity of 128 mAh g−1 over 
30 cycles, demonstrating its excellent electrochemical kinetics. The 
electrochemical reversibility trend of each anode-free cell is clearly 
manifested in the respective voltage profiles. The voltage profile of the 
Cu|PP|LFP cell demonstrates significant capacity fading with increasing 
number of cycles (Fig. 7c). Conversely, the Cu|GES|LFP and GMCC|PP| 
LFP cells exhibit enhanced capacities and stable cycle performances. 
Furthermore, the Cu|GES|LFP cell maintains a higher discharge capacity 
(128 mAh g−1) than the GMCC|PP|LFP cell (103 mAh g−1) after 30 
cycles, owing to the more efficient Li utilization during the electro­
chemical reaction. To analyze the cell performance under high-current 
conditions, the anode-free cells were cycled at a current density of 1 C 
(Fig. 7b). The Cu|PP|LFP cell exhibits low discharge capacities of <15 
Fig. 6. Evaluation of ion conductivity of Li|Cu cells with PP, GES and Li|GMCC cell. (a) Rate retention performances of each cell. (b-d) Voltage profiles of each cell at 
different rate. (e, f) GITT curves of each cell. 
M. Park et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    

Electrochimica Acta 495 (2024) 144426
9
mAh g−1 and a large voltage gap (Fig. 7f). Furthermore, owing to its 
inferior electrochemical kinetics, the Cu|PP|LFP cell demonstrates a 
significantly lower Coulombic efficiency than the other cells. Notably, 
the capacity and Coulombic efficiency of the cells considerably improve 
after the incorporation of a graphene layer into the anode-free system. 
The Cu|GES|LFP cell exhibits a substantially higher initial discharge 
capacity (125 mAh g−1) than the GMCC|PP|LFP cell (71 mAh g−1). 
Furthermore, the Cu|GES|LFP cell maintains a higher discharge capacity 
(82 mAh g−1) than the GMCC|PP|LFP cell (54 mAh g−1) after 50 cycles, 
owing to the more efficient Li utilization during the electrochemical 
reaction. The Ohmic and charge-transfer resistances of the Cu|GES|LFP 
cell are better than those of the GMCC|PPLFP cell (Fig. S6). This suggests 
that the electrolyte depletion caused by Li dendrites in the GMCC|PPLFP 
cell is more impactful than that in the Cu|GES|LFP cell. All these results 
manifestly demonstrate that the introduction of a carbon-modified 
separator, i.e., GES, in anode-free systems can enhance the electro­
chemical reactions and long-term reversibility. 
4. Conclusions 
In this study, we demonstrated the synergistic effects of a GES on the 
enhancement of the electrochemical kinetics and reversibility of Li 
deposition/dissolution in LMBs. For comparison, a GMCC was prepared 
and evaluated in LMBs. The incorporation of the GES into the Li|Cu cell 
improved the efficiency of Li deposition/dissolution under high-current 
conditions and significantly stabilized cycle performances. SEM analyses 
revealed the formation of a dense Li layer between the graphene layer 
and Cu current collector in the Li|Cu cell with GES. In contrast, in the Li| 
GMCC cell, severe Li dendrite growth was observed throughout the 
graphene layer, particularly on the surface. Owing to the morphological 
characteristics of dendritic Li, the Li|GMCC cell exhibited a relatively 
low cycle performance and a low Coulombic efficiency. These results 
confirm the significant role of the GES in the formation of a dense Li 
layer in the anodic area, which reduces the risk of short circuit caused by 
Li dendrites. Furthermore, the viability of using the GES in anode-free 
LMBs was assessed by constructing anode-free cells in which the Li 
metal was substituted with an LFP electrode. At 1 C, the Cu|GES|LFP cell 
exhibited a higher initial discharge capacity (125 mAh g−1) than the 
GMCC|PP|LFP cell (71 mAh g−1). Additionally, the Cu|GES|LFP cell 
maintained a discharge capacity of 82 mAh g−1 even after 50 cycles. The 
successful application of the GES in LMBs suggests that the geometry of 
the deposited Li layer can be regulated by adopting suitable surface- 
modified separators, which enhance electrochemical stability. We 
expect that our study would serve to promote further related research on 
next-generation battery systems.
