Chemical Engineering Journal 490 (2024) 151635
Available online 24 April 2024
1385-8947/© 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
Functionalized interfacial cover design toward pure silicon anode for high 
power density lithium–ion capacitor 
Min Ju O a,b, Junghyun Choi c,*, Jong Hyeok Park b, Kwang Chul Roh a,* 
a Energy and Environmental Division, Korea Institute of Ceramic Engineering and Technology, 101 Soho–ro, Jinju–si, Gyeongsangnam–do, 52851, Republic of Korea 
b Department of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering, Yonsei University, 50 Yonsei–ro, Seodaemun–gu, Seoul 03722, Republic of Korea 
c School of Chemical, Biological and Battery Engineering, Seongnam–si, Gyeonggi–do, 13120, Republic of Korea  
A B S T R A C T   
A lithium–ion capacitor, comprising a capacitor–type cathode and battery–type anode, exhibits high power and energy density; however, the integration of different 
charge storage mechanisms in one cell naturally leads to a kinetic mismatch between the two electrodes, reducing the power density and cycle stability. To solve this 
problem, high–capacity anode materials with thinner electrodes are required. Silicon, with its high specific capacity, is considered a promising anode material with 
high energy and power density; however, pure Si anodes undergo rapid capacity fading and exhibit increased internal resistance owing to volume changes during 
cycling. In this study, both sides of the electrode are covered with functional layers consisting of functionalized herringbone–type carbon nanofibers to protect the Si 
electrode from degradation. The polar functional groups on the nanofibers weakly interact with the native oxide layer of the Si surface and the carboxyl groups of the 
binder, ensuring stable contact between electrode materials over repeated cycles. Moreover, the conductive functional cover promotes uniform lithium ion fluxes, 
aiding the formation of a stable solid electrolyte interphase layer. This study investigates the effects of this strategy on pure Si electrodes by surface morphology 
analysis, assessment of chemical interactions, and electrochemical testing. This approach has the potential to overcome the degradation of Si electrodes and 
significantly improve the power and energy density of lithium–ion capacitors.   
1. Introduction 
Supercapacitors (SCs) have fast reactions owing to their physical 
adsorption and desorption mechanisms, and are therefore mainly used 
in applications that require high power capabilities [1–3]. Research in 
SCs has been driven by the need to increase energy density, even for 
most high–power applications [4,5]. Accordingly, Amatucci proposed 
lithium–ion capacitors (LICs) to fill the energy and power gap between 
lithium–ion batteries (LIBs) and SCs, which consist of a battery–type 
anode and a capacitor–type cathode in a lithium salts electrolyte [5]. 
Activated carbon (AC) which has a large specific surface area from 
porous structure is widely used an cathode material because of its high 
electric double–layer capacitance, electrochemical stability, and low 
fabrication cost [6]. For LIC anodes, initially Li4Ti5O12 was used [5], but 
its high Li insertion potential (1.55 V vs. Li/Li+) limits the improvement 
of full–cell voltage and results in an unsatisfactory energy density in LIC 
[7]. Since then, carbonaceous materials with lower Li+ insertion po­
tential were employed as anodes such as graphite, multi–walled carbon 
nanotube, hard carbon, N–doped carbon, graphene [8–11], LIC config­
uration of graphite//AC have been used commercially. However, the 
energy density of LICs must be increased without reducing their power 
density or cycling stability. To meet the demand for next–generation 
anodes (>1000 mAh g–1) with higher energy densities, numerous studies 
have been conducted [12,13]. However, increasing the energy density of 
LIC should not limit power density and cycling stability. 
An inherent factor limiting the electrochemical performance of LICs 
is that they consist of asymmetric electrodes with different charge 
storage mechanisms [14]. In particular, the power density of the cell is 
restricted by sluggish battery mechanisms such as intercalation, alloy­
ing, and conversion reaction, making it difficult to optimize the elec­
trochemical performance of LICs as a high–power energy storage device. 
To address this challenge, focusing on the material properties and 
electrode design becomes crucial. The thickness of the electrode 
significantly impacts the overall performance of batteries. As the elec­
trode becomes thicker, the diffusion distance of ions becomes longer, 
resulting in relatively higher internal resistance and serious concentra­
tion polarization. This can result in a lower power output compared to 
thinner electrodes, especially at high rates [15]. Therefore, reducing 
thickness of anode is an effective way to mitigate the kinetic mismatch 
between anode and cathode to optimize the electrochemical perfor­
mance of LICs, the application of Si which is the highest capacity anode 
material available for LIBs is expected to be effective. [16,17]. 
* Corresponding authors. 
E-mail addresses: junghchoi@gachon.ac.kr (J. Choi), rkc@kicet.re.kr (K. Chul Roh).  
Contents lists available at ScienceDirect 
Chemical Engineering Journal 
journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/cej 
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2024.151635 
Received 1 January 2024; Received in revised form 21 April 2024; Accepted 23 April 2024   

Chemical Engineering Journal 490 (2024) 151635
2
Silicon has a high theoretical specific capacity of 3578 mA h g−1 
(Li15Si4 phase at room temperature) and a low discharge potential 
window (~0.01–1.5 V vs. Li/Li+) [18,19]. In addition, Si is abundant 
and environmentally friendly; thus, it is considered an excellent candi­
date for next–generation anode materials [20]. However, Si suffers from 
severe volume changes of up to 300 % that can be induced by alloying 
processes during lithiation/de–lithiation [21]. Large internal stresses 
result in structural damages and mechanical fracture and delamination, 
along with loss of electrical contact. Volume changes can also form an 
unstable solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer that continuously con­
sumes lithium ion, leading to increased resistance. The failure of Si re­
sults in rapid capacity loss, impaired cycling stability, and poor rate 
performance; thus the application of pure Si anodes for achieving high 
energy and power density in energy storage devices has proved a 
considerable challenge. [22–24]. 
Numerous studies have reported significant improvements in the 
electrochemical performance of Si anode materials using microscale and 
nanoscale engineering. Among the various precisely designed protective 
layers for silicon based materials, carbon layers are common owing to 
their low cost, high conductivity, flexibility, and slight volume change 
during the charge/discharge process. Si/C composite can be categorized 
according to the way in which Si incorporated into the carbon matrix, 
including encapsulation, [25–28], layering, [29–32] and binding 
[33,34]. Although considerable improvements have been made, these 
methods are often complicated and time–consuming and do not suffi­
ciently protect pure Si anodes from accelerated electrode degradation 
because they are mainly based on the design of active Si [35]. Electrode 
failure due to volume expansion of silicon exhibits delamination, 
cracking, and the unstable SEI formation. To effectively prevent these 
problems, it is essential to consider that electtode degradation is closely 
related to the lithium ion concentration gradient. A previous study has 
reported that the Si particles closest to the separator surface undergo the 
most lithiation and experience severe fracturing, indicating that lith­
iation in the electrode is not limited by electronic conductivity but by 
the diffusion of lithium ion through the electrode layer [36]. Therefore, 
an approach that protect both sides of the electrode is needed, focusing 
on the electrode surface and the electrode-current collector interface. 
In this study, we develop a functional cover on the interfacial elec­
trode using functionalized herringbone–type carbon nanofibers (HCNF) 
to prevent volume expansion of the Si electrode. A herringbone structure 
in which the graphite basal plane is inclined with respect to the direction 
of fiber growth has many highly active edge sites for surface modifica­
tion and therefore makes a suitable coating material for a protective 
cover layer. The polar groups of the protective layer are known to have 
strong adhesive interactions owing to the difference in electronegativity 
compared to the oxygen–containing groups of the electrode material. 
The Fig. 1 shows the mechanism by which the functionalized HCNFs 
(f–CNFs) can relieve the local surface tension of the electrode through 
molecular interactions with hydrophilic groups in the electrode mate­
rials to provide maintaining electrical connections and effectively 
protecting the newly exposed active silicon from the electrolyte. 
Furthermore, the functional cover can facilitate electrolyte wettability 
and uniform lithium ion flux and alleviate the concentration gradient, 
thereby stabilizing the electrode–electrolyte interface during repeated 
cycles. Therefore, the protective cover layer can ensure that the elec­
trode integrity is maintained, endowing pure Si anode with favorable 
rate capability and cycling stability. These effects are investigated by 
tracking the interaction between the functional cover and the electrode 
surface using surface and interface cutting analysis, field–emission 
scanning electron microscopy, resistance analysis, and X–ray photo­
electron spectroscopy depth profiles. The applicability of the proposed 
cover design in the protection of a pure Si anode was confirmed through 
LIC full–cell electrochemical tests. 
2. Experimental 
2.1. Preparation of functionalized carbon nanofiber (f–CNF) 
The herringbone–type carbon nanofibers used in this study were 
provided by VINATech Co., Republic of Korea. For the oxidation treat­
ment, the as–received samples (denoted as HCNF) were impregnated 
with a mixture of sulfuric acid and nitric acid (3:1 v/v) under magnetic 
stirring for 2 h at 25 ◦C. The oxidized samples were thoroughly washed 
with excess deionized water until a neutral pH was achieved by vacuum 
filtration. Then, the washed sample was dried at 80 ◦C overnight. The 
obtained sample was denoted as f–CNF. 
2.2. Preparation of f–CNF coated current collector and separator 
The coating slurry was prepared to planetary paste mixer with f–CNF 
as active material and poly (acrylic acid) (PAA, 10 wt% dispersion in 
distilled water, Aldrich) as the binder, with a corresponding weight ratio 
of 97:3. Distilled water is added as a solvent. The slurries were spread 
onto current collector (Cu foil) and separator (polyethylene film, Toray) 
by hand coater. And then, the coated current collector and separator 
were dried in a vacuum oven at 100 ◦C for 3 h and 60 ◦C for 12 h, 
respectively. The obtained coated separator and bare separator were 
named C–PE and PE, respectively. The HCNF was coated on the Cu foil 
and separator at loading levels of 0.15–0.17 and 0.89–0.92 mg cm−2, 
respectively. 
2.3. Materials characterization 
The morphologies of the samples were examined by field–emission 
scanning electron microscopy (FE–SEM; JEOL, JSM–6700F, Japan), 
transmission electron microscopy (TEM; JEOL, JEM–2000EX, Japan), 
high angle annular dark field imaging (HAADF), and energy–dispersive 
X–ray spectroscopy (EDS). X–ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were 
collected from powder samples on a Rigaku D/Max 2500/PC spec­
trometer using a Cu Kα radiation source (λ = 1.54181 Å) operated at 40 
kV and 30 mA. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), NETZSCH, Germany) 
was performed using an analyzer in an argon atmosphere over the 
temperature range from 30 ◦C to 800 ◦C at a heating rate of 10 ◦C min−1. 
X–ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (PHI 5000 VersaProbe) was 
performed using monochromatic Al Κα radiation. The adhesion prop­
erties while cutting and peeling coating layers from 1 to 1000 μm thick 
using a surface and interface cutting analysis system (SAICAS). The 
morphology of the cross–section of the electrodes was analyzed after 
cycling through SEM and TEM combined with EDS. Prior to analysis, the 
electrodes were washed with the corresponding mother liquor (without 
salt) to remove any residual Li salts and vacuum–dried. The cross–sec­
tional samples of the prepared electrodes were produced through 
focused ion beam (FIB) (Helios G4, Thermo Fisher Scientific). XPS depth 
profiles (NEXSA, thermos fisher scientific) were obtained without 
exposure of the sample to air under conditions of 2 kV of sputter energy 
and 10 nm/min sputter rate. 
Fig. 1. Schematic of the degradation in Si electrodes in the presence and 
absence of f–CNF protective layers over multiple cycles. 
M. Ju O et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    

Chemical Engineering Journal 490 (2024) 151635
3
2.4. Electrochemical characterization 
The anode were prepared using nano silicon (amorphous structured 
and nanotechnology materials) as the active material, super–P black 
(TIMCAL Graphite & Carbon Co., Belgium) as the conducting material, 
and poly(acrylic acid) (PAA, 10 wt% dispersion in distilled water, 
Aldrich) as the binder, with a corresponding weight ratio of 6:2:2. The 
slurries were spread onto an f–CNF coated current collector, and the 
coated electrodes were dried at 100 ◦C for 3 h in a vacuum oven. The Si 
electrode obtained on the coated current collector was denoted U–Si. 
The electrodes were punched out into circular pieces measuring 12 mm 
in diameter. LIC cathodes were prepared using activated carbon (AC) 
(Power Carbon Technology). The cathode was prepared using AC as the 
active material, super–P as the conducting material, and Polytetra­
fluoroethylene preparation (PTFE; 60 wt% dispersion in H2O, Sigma 
Aldrich) as the binder, with a corresponding weight ratio of 94:3:3. The 
mixed and kneaded electrode materials were repeatedly rolled in a 
roll–press at 60 ◦C before being dried at 200 ◦C for 24 h in a vacuum 
oven and finally punched out using a 12 mm–diameter punch. Electro­
chemical tests were performed using a coin–cell (CR 2032) configura­
tion in a dry room at 25 ◦C. Half–cell tests were conducted using Si and 
U–Si as the working electrodes, along with Li metal as the counter and 
reference electrodes. A microporous polyethylene film (Toray) was used 
as the separator, and the electrolyte was 1.15 M LiPF6 dissolved in a 
mixture of ethylene carbonate (EC), dimethyl carbonate (DMC), and 
ethyl methyl carbonate (EMC) in a 2:4:4 vol ratio containing 25 vol% 
fluoroethylene carbonates (FEC) as an additive. For half–cell testing, Si 
and U–Si were as working electrodes, respectively. And Li metal was as 
the counter and reference electrode. Electrochemical impedance spec­
troscopy (VSP Potentiostat) was carried out at 1, 2, 20, 40, 100 cycles 
during lithiated (0.01 V) and de–lithiated (1.5 V) state over the fre­
quency range from 1 MHz to 10 mHz at room temperature. The 
impedance spectra were simulated by using Zfit software. LIC were also 
assembled in coin cells with pre–lithiated U–Si and Si electrodes as an­
odes, AC electrodes as cathodes, and Celgard 2400 membranes as sep­
arators. Pre–lithiation was performed before cycling to ensure stability 
during charge–discharge cycles by mitigating the capacity loss owing to 
the low initial coulombic efficiency of the anode material and to provide 
a lithium source to accommodate a wide voltage window. The ex–situ 
electrochemical pre–lithiation method was performed using a 
pre–assembled a half–cell. The half–cell maintains constant potential for 
20 h after charging with a cut–off potential of 0.4 V in the second cycle. 
At this time, the state of charge of the Si electrode was 4.7 %. The total 
weight comprises the active material, binder, conductive material, and 
any coating layers directly applied to the electrodes. Specifically, for the 
anode, this includes the underlayer coating. The anode and cathode 
materials were applied at loading levels of 0.55–0.60 mg cm−2 (loading 
level of coating layer on Cu foil: 0. 15–0.17 mg cm−2) and 6.1–6.5 mg 
cm−2, respectively. The active mass ratio of the cathode to anode was 
17.4 at an N/P ratio of 1:1.12. All electrochemical tests were performed 
at room temperature. Galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD) profiles 
were obtained using a battery test system (WonATech, Korea). Poten­
tials of 0.01–1.5 V (vs. Li/Li + ) were applied to the half–cell anode, and 
voltages of 2.6–4.1 V were applied to the full–cells, respectively. The 
specific energy density (E, Wh kg−1 and power density (P, W kg−1) were 
calculated using the following equations: 
E =.
∫t1
t2 IVdt 
P = EΔt. 
where I is the discharge current (A), m is the mass of the anode or 
cathode (kg), t is the discharge time (h), and V is the discharge voltage 
(V). 
3. Results and discussion 
Figure S1 is a SEM and TEM image of the HCNF. The diameter and 
length of the HCNF were observed to be approximately 30–50 nm and 
3–5 μm, respectively, and a few graphene layers on both sides were 
inclined along the hollowed interior as leaning against the fiber axis, and 
the angle was measured as approximately 26
◦[37]. As shown, the HCNF 
shows clear crystal lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of 0.340 nm, 
corresponding to the (002) crystal plane of the graphite phase. XRD was 
used to examine the crystal structure of the HCNFs before and after acid 
treatment, as shown Fig. 2(a). The XRD patterns of all the samples 
exhibited peaks at 25◦and 42◦, corresponding to the graphite (002) and 
(100) reflections [37]. The (002) peak of f–CNF has a lower intensity 
than that of HCNF. The decrease in the sharpness of the peak indicates 
that the graphite structure of the treated HCNF was acid–oxidized 
without significant damage because any decrease in the crystallinity 
order of the HCNF broadened the peaks and shifted the peak diffraction 
towards lower angles. Change of morphologies of HCNF after acid 
treatment is investigated by SEM and TEM. The f–CNFs have no damage 
or breaks in the nanofiber structure under the oxidation treatment, and 
their diameters are similar to those of the HCNFs. At high magnification, 
the surface of f–CNFs became an amorphous structure in Figure S2. In 
addition to a result of the slight decrease in ID/IG ratio results of Raman 
spectroscopy (Figure S3), it was confirmed that the f–CNF was oxidized 
only on the surface without significant damage to the shape of the HCNF 
under acid treatment conditions [38]. Through powder resistivity 
measurement, it was confirmed that the reduction in electrical con­
ductivity of f–CNF compared to HCNF was not significant, as shown 
Table S1. 
This transformation is attributed to the introduction of oxygen 
functional groups resulting from the induced structural defects on the 
edge sites of the relatively weak bonds due to the acid treatment. The 
decrease in the crystallinity is related to the formation of a functional 
group, and the amount attached was confirmed through TGA in nitrogen 
from 30 to 800 ◦C. (Figure S4) All samples showed an initial weight loss 
from 30 ◦C up to 140 ◦C due to the presence of moisture and the evap­
oration of physiosorbed water. When the heating temperature reaches 
200 ◦C, samples begin to gradually undergo thermal degradation 
decomposition of hydrocarbon and carbon oxides. The weight losses of 
0.38 % (HCNF) and 3.72 % (f–CNF) in the range of 200 ◦C to 500 ◦C 
correspond to the decomposition of the functional group on the surface 
[39]. XPS analysis was performed to determine the functional groups 
formed on the HCNF surface. Atomic concentration (Table S2) present 
increased oxygen of f–CNF at the surface. Fig. 2(b) shows a C 1 s narrow 
scan of the acid–treated HCNFs, which suggests the presence of multiple 
oxygen–containing surface species on the HCNFs. The C 1 s core line was 
fitted, and these binding energy peaks were assigned to sp2 C = C 
(284.3–284.4 eV), C–O at 286.8 eV, C = O at 287.1 eV, ester groups 
(C–O–R) at 288.9 eV, and p–p* at 291.3 eV [40,41]. The sp2 C = C as­
signments in the HCNF are the highest due to the fact that stacked 
graphite layers. The treated HCNF exhibited reduced quantities of sp2 C 
= C bonds, which was the main peak, and higher quantities of C = O, 
C–O, and O–C = O bonds (Table S3). This suggests that these species 
were formed from defect sites within the sp2 C = C structure. The hy­
drophilic functional groups on the surface of f–CNF, C = O (carboxyl), 
and C–O (hydroxyl, carboxyl) bonds offer adhesive interactions with the 
oxygen–containing groups of the electrode materials, facilitating robust 
contact with the electrode and ionic conductivity during volume 
expansion. The prepared f–CNF was coated on the current collector and 
separator (Figure S5), which were then analyzed through contact angle 
measurement to determine the wettability of the coating separator 
(Fig. 2(c) and (d) and S6). The contact angles of the bare, HCNF coated, 
and f–CNF coated separators were 72◦, 52◦, and 23◦, respectively. The 
results confirmed that the coated separators exhibit lower contact angles 
than the bare separator, indicating enhanced wettability. This superior 
wettability was attributed to the hydrophilic functional groups in the 
PAA binder in the functional cover [42]. Furthermore, the oxygen­
–containing functional groups introduced into the HCNF by acid treat­
ment increase the hydrophilciity of the f–CNF; thus, it exhibits the 
M. Ju O et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    

Chemical Engineering Journal 490 (2024) 151635
4
lowest observed contact angle. The enhanced wettability increases the 
affinity of the separator for the electrolyte, which increases ionic con­
ductivity (Table S4) and reduces internal resistance within the cell. 
The adhesion strength was investigated using SAICAS to determine 
whether the functional cover enhanced the binding between the current 
collector and the electrode. The measurement is carried out after precise 
cutting to the depth at which the coating layer on the electrode was 
located as shown in Fig. 2(e) [43]. The adhesion strength between the 
Fig. 2. (a) XRD patterns (b) C1s XPS spectra of HCNF and f–CNF, water contact angle images of (c) HCNF and (d) f–CNF coated separator. (e) Adhesion strength of Si 
electrode and U–Si electrode by SAICAS. 
Fig. 3. Electrochemical characterization of Si/PE and U–Si/C–PE half–cell. (a) Cycling performance at 0.5C; (b) rate performance; (c and d) selected char­
ge–discharge curves with 1, 5, 10, 15, 20, 40, 100 cycles. 
M. Ju O et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    

Chemical Engineering Journal 490 (2024) 151635
5
coating layer and the electrode was 0.106 N, which was higher than the 
adhesion strength between the current collector and the electrode of 
0.90 N. This is the result of improved adhesion owing to electrostatic 
attractions arising from the hydrophilic groups of the functional cover, 
the native oxide layer of the Si particles (Figure S7 and S8)[44], and the 
hydroxyl and carboxyl groups of the PAA binder within the electrode to 
maintain constant contact with the current collector during cycles. 
To assess the effect of the functional cover on the electrochemical 
performance of the Si–based anodes, half–cell coin cells were assembled. 
The details of the sample preparation and electrochemical tests are 
described in the experimental section. Before cycle stability test, the 
formation process of 3 cycles at 0.1, 0.2, 0.5C exhibit initial coulombic 
efficiencies (ICE) of 85 % and 81 % as shown Figure S9. The slightly 
lower ICE of the U–Si/C–PE cell is attributed to the consumption of 
lithium ion due to irreversible reactions during the 1st cycle, caused by 
the large specific surface area of the coating layer. However, from the 
2nd cycle onwards, CE quickly recovers, displaying higher values than 
Si/PE. This improvement is believed to result from the enhanced con­
ductivity of the electrode due to the conductive functional cover. The 
decrease in ICE for U–Si/C–PE is considered to have minimal impact on 
the electrochemical performance degradation of LICs, as pre–lithiation 
processes before LIC full–cell assembly can mitigate the initial lithium 
ion loss. The cycle performances of the Si/PE and U–Si/C–PE half–cell 
were evaluated using GCD tests (Fig. 3(a)). Starting with an initial ca­
pacity of 3360 mAh g−1, the U–Si/C–PE half–cell retains a high capacity 
of 2757 mAh g−1 after 100 cycles at a current density of 0.5C. In 
contrast, the Si/PE anode begins with an initial capacity of 3259 mAh 
g−1 but experiences rapid capacity fading, preserving only about 2279 
mAh g−1 after 100 cycles. This indicates a decay in the electrochemical 
properties of Si/PE. In the rate capability test (Fig. 3(b)), U–Si/C–PE 
showed a capacity retention of 88 % from 0.1C to 1C., while the Si/PE 
showed a capacity retention of 83 % from 0.1C to 1C. Finally, returning 
to 0.5C, the capacity of U–Si/C–PE approached its original level. Fig. 3 
(c) and (d) exhibit the selected charge/discharge curves of Si/PE and 
Fig. 4. (a) Cross–sectional SEM images of (a) Si and (b) U–Si electrode (containing Cu foil) at fresh (left), 5th (middle), and 100th (right) cycles at fully lithiated state. 
(c) Volume change of Si and U–Si electrode. Low magnification FIB–SEM analysis cross–section view of (d) Si and (e) U–Si electrode after 100 cycles. 
M. Ju O et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    

Chemical Engineering Journal 490 (2024) 151635
6
U–Si/C–PE over a voltage range of 0.01–1.5 V, where after 1, 5, 10, 15, 
20, 40, and 100 cycles, the potential recoveries of the U–Si/C–PE are 
much higher than those of the Si/PE. Compared with Si/PE, U–Si/C–PE 
had a lower overpotential in the 1st cycle, thereby increasing conduc­
tivity of electrode by the protective functional cover. During cycling, the 
overpotential represents the resistance of the thick SEI formed by the 
repeated formation, breaking, and detachment of the functional cover 
from the electrode. U–Si/C–PE exhibited a lower overpotential than Si/ 
PE during the overall cycle, and the difference in the overpotential of the 
two cells widened as the cycle progressed. The initial lithiation poten­
tials of Si/PE and U–Si/C–PE were 0.2 V and 1 V, respectively, at the 
100th cycle, representing an overpotential of 1.3 V and 0.5 V, respec­
tively. This difference in resistance can be explained by the covering 
layer, which continuously provides a robust and flexible electrical 
network that suppresses electrode degradation. Therefore, the electro­
chemical utilization was maintained for 100 cycles, showing an excel­
lent cycling stability of 83 % (U–Si/C–PE) compared to 70 % (Si/PE). 
During the process of electrochemical de–lithiation, silicon un­
dergoes significant volume changes. The electrode mechanical damage 
due to dynamic volume changes of silicon causes spatial rearrangement 
of silicon particles, resulting in delamination between the electrode 
materials themselves and between the electrode and the current col­
lector [45,46]. Additionally, during repeated cycles, the SEI layer on the 
particle surface cannot withstand the stress caused by silicon particle 
volume changes and undergoes destruction and regeneration processes, 
becoming non-uniform and thick [47–49]. This results in the loss of 
electrical connections and an increase in electrode resistance, leading to 
reduced cycling performance. In order to determine whether the func­
tional cover in contact with the top/bottom of the electrode suppresses 
changes in electrode structure due to silicon volume expansion, the 
surface and cross–sectional morphology of the electrode after several 
cycles were investigated through SEM analysis, as shown Fig. 4. Before 
the electrochemical experiments, the thickness of the Si electrode 
measured 8.6 µm, while the Si electrode with functional cover measured 
a total of 10.5 µm, including a coating thickness of approximately 2–3 
µm. To evaluate the volume changes of the electrodes during cycle, the 
electrode thicknesses in the fully lithiated and de–lithiated states after 5 
cycles were presented in Figure S10. For the Si electrode, a volume 
expansion of 114 % was observed in the lithiation, and it increased by 
106 % compared to the fresh electrode in the de–lithiation. On the other 
hand, the U–Si electrodes exhibited a lower volume expansion rate of 
107 % during lithiation, and the contraction rate of the electrode during 
de–lithiation was 103 %, returning to nearly the same thickness of 10.9 
µm as the fresh electrode. The functional cover of the separator has 
adsorption properties with the electrode material, limiting the spatial 
movement of silicon particles on the electrode surface, thereby miti­
gating changes in electrode volume. Moreover, by providing a uniform 
lithium ion flux on the electrode surface, the functional layer contributes 
to the formation of a stable and thin SEI layer, which is believed in 
reduced the thickness of the electrode during the charge and discharge 
cycles [50,51]. 
To verify the continuous influence of the coating layer on mitigating 
the volume changes of the electrode, the maximum thickness of the 
electrode in the state of lithium ion insertion was investigated after 5th 
and 100th cycles, as shown in Fig. 4(a) and (b). The Si and U–Si elec­
trodes exhibited volume change of 269 % and 217 %, respectively, 
compared to their fresh electrodes, the electrode with the coating layer 
demonstrating a lower rate of volume change as shown Fig. 4(c). In the 
box–shaped area at the bottom of the electrode, it was observed that the 
electrode with the functional cover maintained the contact between the 
current collector and the electrode materials, whereas the silicon par­
ticles was unable to withstand the stress caused by the dynamic changes 
in the electrode, resulting in a loss of contact. Furthermore, it is clearly 
observed in Fig. 4(d) and (e) that the electrode with the functional cover 
exhibited fewer cracks on its surface compared to the Si electrode. The 
morphological differences between Si electrode and its counterpart are 
due to the adhesion characteristics of the functional cover. The coated 
separator reduces the irreversible movement of silicon particles on the 
electrode surface, maintaining electrical connections and effectively 
protecting the newly exposed active silicon from the electrolyte. Addi­
tionally, the underlayer coating enhances the bonding between the 
current collector and the electrode. These effects prevent cracking and 
crushing of the electrode surface and delamination beneath the elec­
trode. The improved integrity of the electrode plays a crucial role in 
enhancing electrochemical performance, which can explain the reduced 
overpotential observed in the electrode with the coating layer, as shown 
in Fig. 3(c) and (d). 
In order to internal resistance of Si electrode attributed to the pro­
tective functional cover, EIS were investigated. Nyquist plots for Si/PE, 
Si/C–PE, C–Si/PE, and C–Si/C–PE half–cells in fully lithiated state (0.01 
V) (Fig. 5(a)–(d)) and de–lithiated state (Fig. 5(e)–(h)) at 1, 2, 20, 40, 
100 cycles are presented. The corresponding equivalent circuit for the 
Nyquist plots is shown in the inset, where Rs is corresponds to the bulk 
resistance of the electrolyte, the RSEI is assigned to the interfacial contact 
resistance and RCT is a scribed to the charge–transfer process. The 
Warburg impedance (ZW) is used to represent the diffusion of ions within 
the electrodes [52–54]. The RSEI (Fig. 5(i) and (j)) and RCT (Figure S11 
(a) and (b)) of the four types of half–cells with and without the coating 
layer on the separator and current collector were compared in both the 
lithiation and the de–lithiation states at 0.01 V and 1.5 V, respectively. 
All samples showed the largest RSEI/lithiation in the 1st cycle, which 
decreased after the 2nd cycle and reached a stable state after the 20th 
cycle. The elevated SEI resistance during the initial lithiated state was 
primarily attributed to the formation of an SEI layer at the electrode/ 
electrolyte interface, leading to a significant consumption of lithium ion. 
[55] the Si/C–PE showed the lowest RSEI/lithiation, followed by the U–Si/ 
C–PE, U–Si/PE, and Si/PE. The higher resistance of U–Si/C–PE 
compared to that of Si/C–PE indicated that the two coating layers 
increased the contact area between the electrolyte and the coating, 
which increased the prevalence of lithium ion consuming side reactions. 
Nonetheless, the fact that the RSEI/lithiation of Si/C–PE is lower than that 
of U–Si/PE suggests that the coated separator has a reduces RSEI/lithiation 
more significantly than the underlayer coating. The conductive func­
tional cover offered adsorption properties and ensured a uniform 
lithium ion flux at the electrode surface, thereby suppressing the irre­
versible displacement of the electrode material and preventing the for­
mation of a thick SEI film [56,57]. The RSEI/lithiation decreases from the 
2nd cycle onward because SEI film formed on the electrode surface in the 
1st cycle acts as a passivation layer that prevents contact with the elec­
trolyte in subsequent cycles and thus prevents the growth of the SEI. In 
contrast to the lithiatied state, the SEI resistance of all cells in the 
de–lithiatied state increased over 20 cycles because the passivation layer 
on silicon particles cannot endure the mechanical strain that results 
from the shrinkage of the during de–lithiatied, leading to repeated 
breakage and reformation. In this case, the U–Si/C–PE had the lowest 
RSEI/de–lithiation over the 20 cycles, and the resistance values increased in 
the following order: U–Si/PE, Si/C–PE, and Si/PE. After 40 cycles, the 
U–Si/C–PE exhibited the lowest RSEI/delithiation due to the uniform lith­
ium–ion flux at the surface and the mitigation of electrode failure 
through the interaction of the Si electrode with the functional cover on 
both sides. These factors inhibit the formation of an unstable SEI by 
preventing direct contact between new exposed active materials with 
the electrolyte during Si volume expansion. As the cycle progressed, the 
SEI layer of the Si electrode became thick and uneven owing to repeated 
breaking and reforming [58]. The difference in the RSEI of the lithiated 
and de–lithiated states serves as an indirect indicator of the extent of 
cracking/reformation in the SEI during the volume change of silicon, as 
shown in Fig. 5(k). The difference in resistance increased in the 
following order over 20–100 cycles: U–Si/C–PE, U–Si/PE, Si/C–PE, and 
Si/PE. A smaller difference between RSEI/de–lithiation and RSEI/lithiation in­
dicates a more stable SEI film. The charge transfer resistance remained 
relatively stable in the initial cycle but became the predominant source 
M. Ju O et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    

Chemical Engineering Journal 490 (2024) 151635
7
of resistance after the 20 cycle owing to the disruption of the electrical 
path between adjacent electrode materials, current collector, active 
material, and conductive agent during the lithiation and de–lithiation of 
slilicon, leading to increased resistance with an increasing number of 
cycles. Maintaining the conductive network of the electrode is therefore 
crucial in overcoming the RCT. The U–Si/C–PE cell shows the smallest 
difference in RCT after 20 cycles (Fig. 5(l)). Notably, all electrodes with 
the functional layer exhibited a substantial difference in resistance 
relative to the Si electrode. These results can be explained by the elec­
trostatic interaction between the electrode and the coating, which 
maintains electrical and structural connections during volume expan­
sion, as well as the functional coating layer of the separator, which 
weakens the solvation effect and thus promotes lithium ion transport 
[58]. EIS analysis demonstrated that the SEI resistance and charge 
transfer resistance depends on the position of the coating on the surface 
of the electrode; thus, application of the coating in the optimal position 
can significantly reduce the resistance. The functional cover is therefore 
expected to effectively suppress degradation. To verify this, the elec­
trode was analyzed after the electrochemical test. 
The effect of the protective functional cover on the surface of the 
electrode was analyzed after cycling by ex–situ TEM–EDS (Fig. 6(a) and 
(b)); the results showed the distribution of Si on the electrode surface at 
a charged state of 0.01 V after 5 cycles. The silicon particles of the 
electrode with functional cover retained their shape during cycling, 
while those of the uncoated electrode agglomerated owing to the pul­
verization of silicon particles. The de–lithiation state showed similar 
changes (Figure S12). The surface of the coated electrode had a rela­
tively lower distribution of O and a higher distribution of F than that of 
the uncoated electrode, which is indicative of the presence of LiF, an 
inorganic SEI component. The XPS F1s fitting of the electrode in the 
charged state after 5 cycles, as depicted in Figure S13, revealed a high 
LiF content in the SEI composition of the electrode featuring the 
Fig. 5. Nyquist plots and fitting lines according to equivalent circuit models for Si/PE, Si/C–PE, U–Si/PE, and U–Si/C–PE half–cells at (a)–(d) fully lithiated state 
(0.01 V) and (e)–(h) fully de–lithiated state (1.5 V) for 1st, 2nd, 20th, 40th, and 100th cycles. SEI resistance at (i) lithiated state, and (j) de–lithiated state. The difference 
of (k) SEI and (l) charge transfer resistance between lithiated and de–lithiated state. 
M. Ju O et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    

Chemical Engineering Journal 490 (2024) 151635
8
functional layer. This indicates that the functional conductive coating 
layer not only ensures a uniform flux of Li ions to the electrode surface 
but also, in collaboration with FEC additives, aids in the formation of a 
more robust and durable SEI layer [59]. The composition of the SEI is 
influenced by the structure of the electrode surface and the polarity of 
the electrolyte species. Particularly, studies from Pfaendtner group have 
shown that abstract fluorine atoms from FEC molecules are promoted on 
hydroxyl surfaces [60]. It is believed that the hydroxyl groups of the 
functional coating layer, which exhibits adsorption properties with the 
U-Si electrode surface, have influenced the formation of a LiF-rich SEI on 
the electrode surface. 
After 100 cycles, numerous cracks appeared on the surface of Si and 
U-Si electrodes as shown Fig. 6(c) and (d). The magnified FE–SEM image 
confirmed that the spherical shape of the silicon particles was not visible 
on the surface of the electrode. In contrast, the surface of the Si electrode 
with functional layer exhibited fewer cracks than the Si electrode, while 
retaining the inherent shape of the silicon particles. This is the result of 
the LiF-rich SEI layer, which has high mechanical strength, contributing 
to the integrity of the outer shape by protecting the silicon particles from 
volume changes during the cycle. In the EDS maps (Figure S14), F and P 
originating from the electrolyte were observed on the Si electrode sur­
face [61], with Si being relatively less visible owing to a high degree of 
electrolyte decomposition. This observation appears to be related to 
differences in the thickness of the SEI film resulting from the decom­
position of the electrolyte. In addition, a small amount of C is observed 
on the surface of the Si electrode when a separator with a functional 
coating layer was used, likely owing to adhesion resulting from the 
electrostatic interaction between the electrode surface and the func­
tional layer. The functional cover prevents the exposure of active silicon 
in the cracks caused by volume changes, thereby preserving the 
structure of the electrode. 
Fig. 6(e)–(h) show the XPS spectra of the electrode after 100 cycles. 
In the XPS depth profiles (Figure S15), the atomic contents of Li and F 
related in the SEI of the Si electrode with functional layer were lower 
than those in the Si electrode. Accordingly, the peak intensities in the 
spectrum of Si with functional layer at 685 and 687 eV, indicating LiF 
and LiPOFx, respectively [62], were lower than those in the spectrum of 
the Si electrode. This result indicates that the SEI formed a thin layer, 
which is consistent with the EDS maps. In the fitting of the Li 1s spectra, 
that of the Si electrode with functional layer showed a strong peak in­
tensity of approximately 55 eV [63], indicating that this electrode 
contained more Li2CO3 than the Si electrode. These results are believed 
to indicate that the hydroxyl groups on the surface of the functional 
layer facilitated the reduction reaction of FEC during the initial cycles, 
leading to the preferential reduction of FEC and a subsequent increase in 
the Li2CO3 concentration within the SEI layer [64,65]. LiF provides 
excellent electronic insulation and mechanical strength; however, it 
exhibits low ionic conductivity. Conversely, Li2CO3 exhibits high ionic 
conductivity but low mechanical strength and a narrow bandgap, 
resulting in poor electronic insulation. LiF– and Li2CO3–coated SEIs 
therefore have complementary properties that offset the drawbacks of 
each individual coating material. The synergistic effects of LiF and 
Li2CO3 as SEI components in silicon–based anodes have also been re­
ported [66]. The application of an engineered SEI containing LiF and 
Li2CO3 to a thin–film Si anode results in lower resistivity during lith­
iation and de–lithiation and prevents the reduction of the electrolyte 
owing to its superlative electronically insulating properties. Defects at 
the LiF/Li2CO3 interface result in electron depletion near Li2CO3 and 
provides a lithium ion transport pathway, thereby enhancing the elec­
tronic insulation of the material while increasing lithium ion 
Fig. 6. TEM images surface of Si electrode after 5 cycles and EDS mapping of F, O, and Si elements in (a) Si and (b) U–Si electrode. SEM images of surface of (c) Si and 
(d) U–Si electrode after 100 cycles. Li 1s XPS spectra of (e) Si and (f) U–Si electrode and F 1s XPS spectra of (g) Si and (h) U–Si electrode in the lithiated state after 
100 cycles. 
M. Ju O et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    

Chemical Engineering Journal 490 (2024) 151635
9
conductivity [63,67]. This ensures a consistent lithium ion supply and 
limits the thickening of the SEI layer, facilitating reversible electro­
chemical reactions in the anode without notable overpotentials. 
Furthermore, a deformation–adaptive SEI that accommodates anodic 
expansion and contraction cycles safeguards the Si electrode using 
minimal SEI forming compounds. The nyquist plots (Fig. 5) confirmed 
that the functional interface coating introduced on the Si electrode 
surface where relatively severe degradation occurs is effective in sup­
pressing the formation of an unstable SEI and electrode failure. 
Because the energy and power density of LIC can be improved when 
a Si anode with a high specific capacity is applied, full–cell electro­
chemical tests were conducted to evaluate their practical applicability 
[68,69]. The full–cells were assembled using U–Si as the anodes and AC 
as the cathodes with C–PE. To determine the effect of the functional 
coating, a Si anode, and AC as the cathode with PE were assembled. The 
electrochemical performance was evaluated under the following con­
ditions: 1.15 M LiPF6 electrolyte solution in 2:2:4 EC/EMC/DEC with 
FEC as the additive in the voltage range of 2.6–4.1 V. GCD profiles of Si/ 
PEllAC, and U–Si/C–PEllAC, respectively, are shown under different 
current densities (Fig. 7(a) and S16). At a low C–rate, both cells showed 
similar specific capacities; however, at high current densities, U–Si/ 
C–PEllAC than the Si/PEllAC. In the GCD plots at 10C, the overpotential 
was lower than that of the full–cell without the f–CNF coating layer, 
indicating that the electrical conductivity of the electrode was improved 
by the f–CNF layer. The Ragone plots of our U−Si/C–PEllAC full–cell 
and other silicon based and graphite based anode materials are shown in 
Fig. 7(b). Power density and energy density were calculated based on the 
total mass in both the anode and cathode. The maximum energy density 
of U–Si/C–PEllAC (156 W h kg−1) was similar to that of Si/PEllAC (132 
Wh kg−1), while the maximum power density of U–Si/C–PEllAC (4.9 kW 
kg−1 at 66 W h kg−1) was higher than that of Si/PEllAC (5.0 kW kg−1 at 
34 Wh kg−1). It exhibited superior energy density compared with the 
reported other anodes [6,8,70–73]. Furthermore, U–Si/C–PEllAC 
exhibited an energy density of 67 W h kg−1 after 2000 cycles at 5C in 
Fig. 7(c). The overall electrochemical performance of the LIC full–cell 
using the covered Si electrode as the anode was improved, with U–Si/ 
C–PEllAC showing a particularly significant improvement. This is 
thought to be the result of a reduction in the kinetic mismatch with the 
AC cathode. The electrode cover applied to the pure Si anode efficiently 
solves the problem of volume expansion and has the potential to achieve 
high energy, power density, and cycling stability LICs. 
4. Conclusion 
Si electrode degradation caused by volume expansion was alleviated 
by coating the electrode with a functional interfacial cover, allowing 
pure silicon to be utilized as a high–capacity anode for LICs with high 
power and high energy density. HCNF, which have many edge sites, 
readily form functional groups, and have robust physical properties, 
were selected as the coating materials. The polar functional groups 
formed through simple acid treatment improved the binding of the 
electrode material and the current collector, forming a stable SEI by 
connecting the cover layer of the separator and providing a continuous 
electrical path, even on the electrode surface. The results were obtained 
by SAICAS, SEM, XPS, and EIS analyses. LIC full–cell electrochemical 
tests of the pure Si electrode with a functional cover confirmed their 
high energy and power density and long cycling life, thereby demon­
strating their practical applicability. This study successfully introduced 
a functional cover on the electrode surface to effectively address the 
volume expansion problem, facilitate the utilization of pure silicon as a 
high–capacity anode for LICs with both high power and energy density.

[SOURCE: High capacity monoclinic Nb2O5 and semiconducting NbO2 composite as.pdf]

Contents lists available at ScienceDirect
Journal of Power Sources
journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/jpowsour
High capacity monoclinic Nb2O5 and semiconducting NbO2 composite as
high-power anode material for Li-Ion batteries
Hyunjung Parka,b, Dongsoo Leeb, Taeseup Songb,∗
a School of Chemical and Biomedical Engineering, Nanyang Technological University, 62 Nanyang Drive, Singapore, 637459, Singapore
b Department of Energy Engineering, Hanyang University, Seoul, 133-791, South Korea
H I G H L I G H T S
• Monoclinic Nb2O5 was prepared by
high calcination at 1100 °C.
• Semi-conducting
NbO2
was
in-
troduced to Nb2O5 by thermal reduc-
tion process.
• Nb2O5/NbO2 composite shows a cycle
performance of 81% retention over
900 cycles.
• Nb2O5/NbO2 composite shows a su-
perior rate capability at 100C.
G R A P H I C A L A B S T R A C T
A R T I C L E I N F O
Keywords:
Lithium ion batteries
Anode
Niobium oxides
Composite
A B S T R A C T
Niobium pentoxide, Nb2O5, is an intercalation-type material with a high theoretical capacity of ∼404 mAh g−1
for Li-ion batteries. However, electrochemical properties of Nb2O5 largely depend on its various polymorphs
with diﬀerent crystal structures, and their low electrical conductivity acts as the main obstacle. Here, we report
high-temperature calcined monoclinic Nb2O5 and semiconducting NbO2 composite as a high-power anode
material. Monoclinic Nb2O5 itself as a main active material shows a high capacity of ∼280 mAh g−1, and NbO2
with a small band gap of ∼0.5 eV not only improves electrical conductivity but also gives a capacity of ∼110
mAh g−1. To have a synergic eﬀect of these two materials, the Nb2O5/NbO2 composite is prepared via simple
post-calcination of as-prepared Nb2O5 under a reduction atmosphere. It shows a discharge capacity of ∼214
mAh g−1 at 0.05 C, a high initial Coulombic eﬃciency of 94.7%, a superior rate capability of ∼40 mAh g−1 at
100 C, and a robust cycle performance of 81% retention over 900 cycles.
1. Introduction
Titanium oxide-based materials such as anatase TiO2 and spinel
Li4Ti5O12 have been extensively explored as promising anode materials
owing to their high-power density and safety [1,2]. Their high working
voltage window of 1.0–3.0 V vs. Li/Li+ could prevent a signiﬁcant
formation of a solid-electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer and a lithium
dendrite, which makes them suitable for microelectronic devices [3].
However, their practical capacities of ∼150 mAh g−1 for TiO2 and
∼170 mAh g−1 for Li4Ti5O12 are much lower than that of graphite
(∼350 mAh g−1) [4–7]. Recently, titanium and niobium oxide-based
materials such as TiNb2O7 and Nb2O5 have been studied as alternatives
owing to their appealing lithium storage properties [8–11]. In com-
parison to TiO2 and Li4Ti5O12 with one possible redox couple of Ti4+/
5+, those materials have higher theoretical capacities due to multiple
redox couples of Nb3+/4+ and Nb4+/5+ [8]. Among them, niobium
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpowsour.2019.01.015
Received 11 October 2018; Received in revised form 18 December 2018; Accepted 6 January 2019
∗Corresponding author.
E-mail address: tssong@hanyang.ac.kr (T. Song).
Journal of Power Sources 414 (2019) 377–382
Available online 17 January 2019
0378-7753/ © 2019 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
T

pentoxide (Nb2O5) has attracted attention because of a highest theo-
retical capacity of ∼404 mAh g−1, corresponding to 4 lithium insertion
into the unit cell (LixNb2O5, xmax = 4). Research on controlling di-
mensions of Nb2O5 such as spheres, ﬁbers, and nanosheets has been
reported to improve the lithium storage properties [12–14]. Based on
previous studies [14,15], however, lithium storage properties of Nb2O5
more strongly depends on its polymorphs with diﬀerent crystal struc-
tures and lattice parameters; pseudo-hexagonal phase (TT–Nb2O5, P6/
mmm, a = 3.600 Å, c = 3.919 Å, T = ∼600 °C) [16], orthorhombic
phase
(T–Nb2O5,
Pbam,
a = 6.144 Å,
b = 29.194 Å,
c = 3.940 Å,
T = ∼800 °C) [17], monoclinic phase (M −Nb2O5, C2/m, a = 28.51 Å,
b = 3.830 Å,
c = 17.48 Å,
T = ∼1000 °C),
and
high-temperature
monoclinic
phase
(H–Nb2O5,
P2,
a = 21.16 Å,
b = 3.822 Å,
c = 19.35 Å, T > 1000 °C) [18]. Among them, the H–Nb2O5 speciﬁ-
cally calcined at 1100 °C showed the best electrochemical performances
due to the more favorable and larger interlayer spacing for Li+ ion
intercalation/deintercalation despite micro-sized particles caused by
crystal growth during high-temperature calcination [14]. In this re-
spect, it is highly recommended to explore monoclinic Nb2O5 as the
anode material for enhanced electrochemical performances of Li-ion
batteries.
Stoichiometric Nb2O5 is an insulator with a wide band gap energy of
3.2–4.0 eV, which cause slow electron transfer in the electrode and
eventually deteriorate electrochemical performances. To overcome this
problem, a few studies have been performed on composite materials
such as Nb2O5-carbon nanocomposite [19] and Nb2O5/graphene com-
posite [20], which results in improved rate and cycle performances
compared to those of pure Nb2O5. Unfortunately, the Nb2O5 in the
composites were pseudo-hexagonal [19] and orthorhombic phases
[20], which show the much lower speciﬁc capacity of ∼190 mAh g−1
than that of high-temperature calcined monoclinic Nb2O5 (∼240 mAh
g−1) [14]. It is worth noting that if monoclinic Nb2O5 are prepared with
electrically conductive materials, superior electrochemical perfor-
mances can be obtained from monoclinic Nb2O5-based composites. One
facile method is a reduction of Nb2O5 by thermal treatment under re-
ducing atmosphere (gaseous H2, NH3, etc.). This process can normally
cause a formation of oxygen vacancy and a reduction of transition
metal's valence states, which leads to an improvement in electrical
conductivity. It has been reported that non-stoichiometric Nb2O4.978
has
a
conductivity
of
∼3 × 103 S cm−1
much
higher
than
∼3 × 10−6 S cm−1 of the stoichiometric Nb2O5 [21]. In addition, an-
other niobium oxide (NbO2) is an n-type semiconductor with a small
band gap of ∼0.5 eV that is easily prepared by thermal reduction from
Nb2O5 [22]. In this respect, a synthesis of non-stoichiometric Nb2O5-x
and Nb2O5/NbO2 composites can be facile strategies to enhance not
only electrical conductivity but also electrochemical properties.
Here
we
ﬁrst
report
high-capacity
monoclinic
Nb2O5/semi-
conducting NbO2 composite as a high-power anode material for LIBs.
To obtain homogeneous-sized and shaped Nb2O5 particles, pelletizing
and ball milling processes are conducted, and a calcination time is
controlled. The NbO2 phase is introduced to original Nb2O5 particles via
simple thermal treatment under mixed H2/Ar gases tuned by reduction
time and temperature, which plays an important role in lithium storage
properties of the ﬁnal products. We analyze as-prepared composite
through various tools such as electron microscopes, X-ray diﬀraction, X-
ray photoelectron spectrometer, Ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy, etc.
Finally, we demonstrate a great potential of NbO2/Nb2O5 composite for
a high-power anode material through half-cell test.
2. Experimental
2.1. Synthesis of Nb2O5 polymorphs
As a starting material, Nb2O5 particles was purchased (Niobium (V)
oxide, 99.9% (metal basis), Alfa Aesar). An XRD measurement revealed
that the Nb2O5 powders are a composite of pseudo-hexagonal Nb2O5
(International Center for Diﬀraction Data (ICDD), #00-028-0317) and
monoclinic Nb2O5 (ICDD, #00-027-1312) (Fig. S1). To obtain high-
temperature monoclinic phase Nb2O5 (H–Nb2O5), the Nb2O5 powders
was annealed at a temperature at 1100 °C in air for diﬀerent times from
1 h to 10 h.
2.2. Synthesis of NbO2/Nb2O5 composite
To synthesize electrically conductive NbO2 phase, as-prepared
H–Nb2O5 powders were annealed under reducing atmosphere. In de-
tails, to ﬁnd out the starting temperature of a formation of NbO2 phase,
the Nb2O5 particles were put in an alumina crucible, and then it was
placed in the center of a horizontal tube furnace using a quartz tube.
The samples were reduced at the temperature of 800 °C for 0 min
(ramping rate of 10 °C min−1) under mixed gases, hydrogen (H2)/argon
(Ar) (100/100 sccm). Finally, the samples cooled down to room tem-
perature and were taken out from the furnace.
2.3. Material characterization
As-prepared samples were characterized using various tools as fol-
lows; Electrical microscope images were observed using a ﬁeld emission
scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, JEOL JSM07600F), ﬁeld emis-
sion transmission electron microscope (FE-TEM, JEOL JEM-2100F), and
high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, JEOL JEM-
2100F). X-ray diﬀraction patterns of samples were obtained by X-ray
diﬀraction analyzer (XRD, Rigaku D/MAX RINT-2000). Rietveld re-
ﬁnement was performed using X'Pert Highscore Plus. X-ray photoelec-
tron spectrometer (XPS, VG Microtech ESCA2000) was used for the
analysis of compositional changes. UV-VIS Impedance spectra were
gained using impedance analyzer (PARSTAT 2273, Princeton Applied
Research).
2.4. Evaluation of electrochemical performance
Coin-type half cells were fabricated to evaluate the electrochemical
performance. Working electrodes were prepared using slurries con-
sisting of Nb2O5 particles, Nb2O5/NbO2 composite, and the NbO2 par-
ticles as active materials, carbon black powders as a conducting agent
and polyvinylidene ﬂuoride (PVDF) as a binder with a weight ratio of
70:20:10. The mixtures were thoroughly mixed with Thinky mixer at a
high rotation speed of 2000 rpm. And then, as-prepared slurries were
cast on Cu foil with a thickness of ∼18 μm, followed by drying in an
oven at 100 °C for 30 min. All the working electrodes were dried in a
vacuum oven at 120 °C for 5 h before cell assembly. Coin-type half cells
(2032R type) were fabricated using working electrodes, a pure lithium
metal foil as a counter electrode, 1 M LiPF6 in ethylene carbonate/
diethylene carbonate (EC/DEC, 1:1 vol %, PANAX StarLyte, S. Korea) as
an electrolyte and a polypropylene (PP) separator. All the coin cells
were tested using a battery cycle tester (TOSCAT 3000, Toyo System,
Tokyo, Japan).
3. Results and discussion
Bare
Nb2O5
particles
with
mixed
phases
of
TT–Nb2O5
and
M −Nb2O5 show an irregular particle shape and a size (Fig. 1a). After
calcination at ∼1100 °C for 5 h, H–Nb2O5 particles were agglomerated
that leads to a large and inhomogeneous particle size in the range of
1–5 μm (Fig. 1b). To obtain homogeneous H–Nb2O5 particles, pelle-
tizing and ball milling processes were conducted for crushing and
grinding. Fig. 1 shows SEM images of pelletized and ball-milled bare
Nb2O5 particles (Fig. 1c) and H–Nb2O5 particles (Fig. 1d). Even after
post-annealing at 1100 °C for 5 h, the H–Nb2O5 shows a uniform par-
ticle size of ∼500 nm width and ∼2 μm length and a prismatic mor-
phology. The calcination time was also monitored (Fig. S2). The time
was reduced to 1 h, the particle size was in the range of 1–2 μm smaller
H. Park et al.
Journal of Power Sources 414 (2019) 377–382
378

than the size of 5 h annealing sample. In contrast, the time was in-
creased to 10 h, severe crystal growth and agglomeration occurred,
resulting in a large particle size over 4 μm.
Fig. 2 shows unit cell structures of H–Nb2O5 and NbO2. For the
H–Nb2O5, it has a layered structure consisting of NbO6 octahedra rib-
bons that provide large vacancy and 2D channels through b and c axes
for
Li+
ions.
For
tetragonal
NbO2
(Space
group
I41/a,
a = b = 13.710 Å, c = 5.985 Å, α = β = γ = 90°, V = 1124.97 Å3), it
has a rutile structure that consists of NbOx polyhedra lined to form
strings. Along the strings, Nb–Nb distances are in the range of 2.8–3.4 Å
that create an empty 1D channel along c axis (Fig. 2b). It is assumed
that the semiconducting NbO2 improve an electron transfer of overall
electrical conductivity of the H–Nb2O5 and acts as Li+ intercalation/
deintercalation material through the voids. Moreover, it is known that
the nanosized NbO2 exhibits a pseudo-capacitive property like that of
T–Nb2O5 (orthorhombic) that could be beneﬁcial for the high-power
density anode material [23,24].
To synthesize the NbO2 phase, the H–Nb2O5 particles were annealed
at diﬀerent temperatures and times under the mixed gases of H2/Ar.
The reduction temperatures varied in the range of 600–1000 °C without
holding (Fig. S3a). XRD peak patterns indicate that the reduction of
H–Nb2O5 particles did not occur at 600 °C. The XRD patterns were still
matched with the reference of H–Nb2O5 phase (ICDD, monoclinic
Nb2O5 #00-037-1468). When the temperature increased to 800 °C, they
start to be reduced to NbO2 phase that leads to a composite of H–Nb2O5
and NbO2 conﬁrmed by the patterns with two references of Nb2O5 and
NbO2 (ICDD, NbO2 #00-043-1043). As the temperature was set at
1000 °C, the H–Nb2O5 phase was transformed to the NbO2 phase. Re-
ducing times were also studied in the range of 0–60 min at the same
temperature of 800 °C to control the ratio of H–Nb2O5 and NbO2 (Fig.
S3b). As the reduction time increased, the intensities of NbO2 phase
became strong. A Rietveld reﬁnement method was used for a calcula-
tion of a percentage of two phases. H–Nb2O5 to NbO2 are 70.1%/29.9%
for 0 min, 23.4%/76.6% for 30 min, and 1.9%/98.1% for 60 min, re-
spectively. This result revealed that the H–Nb2O5 phase drastically
turned into the NbO2 phase within 30 min. Fig. 3a–c shows XRD pat-
terns of bare H–Nb2O5 particles, H–Nb2O5/NbO2 composite (reduction
for 0min), and NbO2 particles (reduction for 60 min), and their color
changes. Fig. 3d exhibits the color change of Nb2O5 particles from white
to black after the thermal reduction. As a result, three samples were
chosen for further characterizations and electrochemical performances
to investigate the eﬀect of introducing electrical conductive NbO2
phase.
A morphological change of three samples by thermal reduction was
monitored by SEM in Fig. 4a–c. No signiﬁcant changes in sizes between
samples were observed, but the prismatic structure of bare H–Nb2O5
particles became irregular and crooked after reduction. High-magniﬁ-
cation SEM images clearly show a change of a surface roughness (Fig.
S4). The more particles are reduced, the rougher their surface become
due to recrystallization and phase transformation, which is also ob-
served in low-magniﬁcation TEM images (Fig. 4d–f). A high-resolution
(HR) TEM image of H–Nb2O5 with a lattice fringe of ∼3.48 Å corre-
sponding to a (402) plane as shown in Fig. 4g. In contrast, an HR-TEM
image of H–Nb2O5/NbO2 composite in Fig. 4h shows three dis-
tinguished amorphous and crystalline regions such as lattice fringes of
Fig. 1. SEM images of (a) purchased Nb2O5 particles before calcination, (b)
purchased Nb2O5 particles after 1100 °C calcination, (c) pelletized and ball-
milled Nb2O5 particles before calcination, and (d) pelletized and ball-milled
Nb2O5 particles after 1100 °C calcination.
Fig. 2. Illustration of unit cell structures of (a) monoclinic Nb2O5 projected
along b-axis (left) and c-axis (right) and tetragonal NbO2 projected along c-axis
(left) and a-axis (right). Red spheres are oxygen, and the niobium is located at
the center of the green coloured polyhedras. (For interpretation of the refer-
ences to color in this ﬁgure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of
this article.)
Fig. 3. XRD patterns of (a) H–Nb2O5 calcined at 1100 °C, (b) H–Nb2O5/NbO2
composite after reduction at 800 °C for 0 min, and (c) NbO2 after reduction at
800 °C for 60 min. References of H–Nb2O5 (ICDD, monoclinic Nb2O5 #00-037-
1468) and NbO2 (ICDD, NbO2 #00-043-1043) were indexed at the bottom for
matching. (d) Photos on the right-side show color changes from H–Nb2O5 to
H–Nb2O5/NbO2 to NbO2 through reduction processes. (For interpretation of the
references to color in this ﬁgure legend, the reader is referred to the Web
version of this article.)
H. Park et al.
Journal of Power Sources 414 (2019) 377–382
379

∼4.50 Å and 3.40 Å that belongs to (3¯04) of H–Nb2O5 and (400) of
NbO2, respectively. It is hard to distinguish the lattice constant of the
(400) plane of NbO2 from the lattice fringe of (402) plane of H–Nb2O5
due to their very similar d-spacing values. Based on the TEM images,
the (400) plane of NbO2 with 3.40 Å d-spacing was observed particularly
near and/or in the amorphous region that newly evolved because of H2/
Ar reduction. Moreover, a low-magniﬁcation TEM image reveals that
the NbO2 phase exists as a form of islands in the particles (Fig. S5).
Fig. 4i shows a lattice fringe of ∼4.50 Å that belongs to a (222) plane of
NbO2. It is worth noting that after full reduction, the sample has high
crystallinity without noticeable amorphous regions. Fig. 4j–l conﬁrm
that the polycrystalline nature of the samples in agreement with the
XRD results.
Fig. 5 shows Nb 3d XPS spectra obtained from the three samples to
investigate the valence states. The H–Nb2O5 (black line) exhibits
doublets of the 3d3/2 and 3d5/2 at ∼210 eV and 207.3 eV, respectively.
This agrees well with the previous report on Nb2O5 calcined at a high-
temperature at 1050 °C and indicates a presence of Nb5+ oxidation
state [25]. The H–Nb2O5/NbO2 composite (blue line) shows the same
doublets with those of the H–Nb2O5 but the higher intensity of the 3d5/2
peak that is attributed to an evolution of new NbO2 phase (red sublines)
and the coexistence of Nb2O5 (black sublines). An area ratio of
H–Nb2O5 to NbO2 was determined to be 0.69 to 0.31 by peak splitting,
which corresponds to the XRD result. The NbO2, fully reduced one (red
line), has also two peaks at 208.4 and 205.9 eV that well agree with the
previous report on NbO2 and conﬁrm a presence of +4 oxidation state
[25]. The XPS result demonstrates peaks shifts to the lower angles and
the transformation of the valence state from Nb5+ to Nb4+ as a degree
of the reduction increased [26,27]. It is worth noting that the valence
transformation of niobium can result in a formation of new orbital close
to the valence band and a decrease in a band gap (Eg) [28], which
accordingly improves the surface electronic conductivity of niobium
oxide composites and electrochemical performances that makes it
suitable for the high-power anode material.
To give a further insight into the changes in colors band gaps of the
samples, UV–vis spectroscopy was used. Fig. 6a shows the UV–vis ab-
sorption spectra of H–Nb2O5, H–Nb2O5/NbO2, and NbO2. No noticeable
intensity was observed from the pristine sample in the visible light
range (450 nm ≤λ ≤700 nm) owing to the wide band gap of Nb2O5. In
contrast, as the degree of the reduction increased, the stronger ab-
sorption responses were detected for H–Nb2O5 and NbO2 samples. This
Fig. 4. Morphological and microstructural characterizations of H–Nb2O5, H–Nb2O5/NbO2 composite, and NbO2. (a–c) SEM images, (d–f) low-magniﬁcation TEM
images, (g–i) HR-TEM images, and (j–l) SAED patterns.
Fig. 5. Nb 3d XPS spectra of H–Nb2O5 (black line), H–Nb2O5/NbO2 composite
(red line), and NbO2 (blue line). (For interpretation of the references to color in
this ﬁgure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
H. Park et al.
Journal of Power Sources 414 (2019) 377–382
380

supports that the strong absorbance in the visible area is attributed to
the formation of Nb4+ ions and the new energy level lowered than that
of the conduction band of Nb2O5, which is agreement with the color
change of the samples shown in Fig. 2b. Band gaps were calculated from
the spectra. According to a Tauc's plot in Fig. 6b, the values of H–Nb2O5
and H–Nb2O5/NbO2 composite are 3.1 eV and 2.8 eV, respectively,
which indicates the band gap was decreased after reduction.
Electrochemical performances of three electrodes prepared with
H–Nb2O5, H–Nb2O5/NbO2, and NbO2 particles as the active materials
were evaluated in the in the voltage window of 1.0–3.0 V vs. Li/Li+ at
room temperature. Fig. 7a shows voltage proﬁles of three samples at a
current density of 0.05. They gave ﬁrst charge (lithiation) and dis-
charge (delithiation) capacities of 283/266, 226/214, and 115/110
mAh g−1, and show high Coulombic eﬃciencies of 93.9%, 94.7%, and
95.7%, respectively. The reason for the decrease in the capacities of
NbO2/H–Nb2O5 and NbO2 electrodes is attributed to a lower capacity of
NbO2. The oxidation state of Nb in NbO2 is +3/+4, and it leads to
possibly one electron transfer during charging/discharging. Based on
our calculation of a theoretical capacity using equation (1), the de-
termined value is ∼214 mAh g−1 that is almost half of the theoretical
capacity of Nb2O5 (∼404 mAh g−1).
=
×
−
nF
Q
3.6
M
(mAh g
)
theoretical
W
1
(1)
Where n is the number of electron transfer per formula unit of the
electrode material, F is the Faraday constant, and Mw is the molecular
weight of the electrode material. In this work, n is 1 (e–), Mw is
124.91 g/mol, and F is 96485 sA/mol.
However, it is worth noting that the speciﬁc capacity of H–Nb2O5/
NbO2 electrode obtained at 0.1 C was still much higher than those of
Nb2O5 polymorphs including amorphous Nb2O5 [15], TT–Nb2O5
[14,15,19], T–Nb2O5 [14,15,20], and M −Nb2O5 [14]. A cycling per-
formance and coulombic eﬃciency were monitored over 900 cycles in
Fig. 7b. Capacity retentions of three electrodes at a high current density
of 1 C were 62% (111 mAh g−1), 81% (123 mAh g−1), and 85% (34
mAh g−1), respectively. By introducing NbO2 to Nb2O5, the cycle life
was much improved than that of bare Nb2O5. Voltage proﬁles of three
samples were plotted in terms of cycles (Fig. S6). Except for a shift to
the left due to the capacity fading, no noticeable changes in the curve
shapes were observed among samples, which supports that there are no
such a decomposition and/or cell failure during cycling similar to those
of previous reports on niobium oxide-based materials [29–33]. A reason
for the more capacity drop of the H–Nb2O5 than others might be at-
tributed to larger intercalation-induced stress/strain to accommodate
Li+ ions into the unit cell [34]. A signiﬁcant improvement was found in
rate capability in Fig. 7c. Although the H–Nb2O5 electrode shows a
larger capacity at 1C than the others, the H–Nb2O5/NbO2 electrode
shows much better property and higher capacity retentions at high C
rates from 5 to 100 C. The NbO2 electrode also shows the superior rate
capability, but its capacity is too low. Particularly, the capacity reten-
tions of three electrodes at 100 C were 0.5%, 25%, and 13%, respec-
tively. EIS spectra were obtained to demonstrate a change in a re-
sistance of the samples. Fig. 7d clearly shows the trend of a decrease in
the semicircles after reduction, which supports the improved charge
transfer of H–Nb2O5/NbO2 and NbO2 compared to H–Nb2O5. It is worth
noting that the overall electrochemical performances of H–Nb2O5/
NbO2 are superior to those of previous reports, especially on other
Nb2O5 polymorphs. This is mainly because it has not only enhanced
electrical conductivity but also much higher capacity than those of
others (amorphous Nb2O5: 150 mAh g−1, TT–Nb2O5: 180 mAh g−1,
T–Nb2O5: 140 mAh g−1, M–Nb2O5: 200 mAh g−1), which leads to
better capacity retentions of H–Nb2O5/NbO2 at even higher current
densities [14,15,19,20].
4. Conclusion
In summary, to overcome poor electronic conductivity of high
temperature calcined monoclinic Nb2O5 with the highest capacity
among its polymorphs, semiconducting NbO2 was introduced to
H–Nb2O5 via simple post-annealing process under reducing atmosphere.
The synthetic conditions such as the calcination/reducing temperatures
and times were optimized to obtain uniform-sized and prismatic-shaped
H–Nb2O5/NbO2 particles for promising electrochemical performances.
The XRD analysis revealed that the two phases of H–Nb2O5/NbO2 co-
exist, and the weight percent was determined to be 70.1/29.9. The
electron microspore images conﬁrm that the sample has ∼500 nm
width and ∼2 μm length and consists of three diﬀerent phases in-
cluding amorphous region, H–Nb2O5, and NbO2 as a form of the iso-
lated island. As a result, the H–Nb2O5/NbO2 electrode shows the en-
hanced electrochemical performances including the high-rate capability
of the 25% retention at 100 C and the stable cycle life of the 81% re-
tention at 1 C compared to 0.5% and 62% of bare H–Nb2O5 electrode.
We believe that our experimental strategy provides a way of improve-
ments in monoclinic Nb2O5 based-material by surface modiﬁcation and
hope there will be more works on composites with unique conducting
materials such as carbon nanotube, graphene, etc. to obtain superior
lithium storage properties.

High-Energy-Density Li-Ion Batteries Employing Gradient
Porosity LiFePO4 Electrode for Enhancing Li-Ion Kinetics
and Electron Transfer
Seungmin Han, Hyungjun Lee, Subi Yang, Jaeik Kim, Jinwoo Jeong, Yeseung Lee,
Jinyoung Chun, Kwang Chul Roh, Patrick Joohyun Kim, Dongsoo Lee, Seho Sun,
Woojin Jeong, Bogeum Choi, Ungyu Paik,* Taeseup Song,* and Junghyun Choi*
1. Introduction
With the expansion of carbon-neutral poli-
cies to address climate change, the electric
vehicle (EV) market has witnessed remark-
able growth.[1] This rapid increase in EV
adoption has in turn driven a surge in
demand for Li-ion batteries (LIBs), which is
projected to rise from around 700 GWh in
2022 to 4,700 GWh by 2030.[2] As LIB
demand continues to soar, the importance
of developing stable, high-performance bat-
teries has become increasingly evident.
In addition, with many countries phasing
out or reducing subsidies for EVs, achieving
price competitiveness has become a major
challenge for manufacturers.[3] As batteries
account for 40% of the total manufactur-
ing cost of EVs, reducing battery prices is
essential to maintain market growth and
ensure consumer affordability.[4]
In this context, enhancing the energy
density, cycle life, and cost efﬁciency of
LIBs is a priority.[5] Among various cathode materials for
LIBs, lithium iron phosphate (LFP) has garnered signiﬁcant
attention due to its excellent thermal and chemical stability,
S. Han, H. Lee, J. Kim, W. Jeong, U. Paik, T. Song
Department of Energy Engineering
Hanyang University
222 Wangsimni-ro, Seoul 04763, Republic of Korea
E-mail: upaik@hanyang.ac.kr; tssong@hanyang.ac.kr
S. Yang, J. Chun, K. C. Roh
Emerging Materials R&D Division
Korea Institute of Ceramic Engineering and Technology
Jinju 52851, Republic of Korea
The ORCID identiﬁcation number(s) for the author(s) of this article
can be found under https://doi.org/10.1002/sstr.202500093.
