This is an open access article under the terms of the Creative Commons
Attribution License, which permits use, distribution and reproduction in
any medium, provided the original work is properly cited.
Correction added on 12 June 2025 after online publication: the author name
“Bogem Choi” in the online version should be changed to “Bogeum Choi”.
DOI: 10.1002/sstr.202500093
S. Yang, P. J. Kim
Department of Applied Chemistry
Kyungpook National University
Daegu 41566, Republic of Korea
J. Jeong, Y. Lee, B. Choi, T. Song
Department of Battery Engineering
Hanyang University
222 Wangsimni-ro, Seoul 04763, Republic of Korea
D. Lee, J. Choi
School of Chemical
Biological and Battery Engineering
Gachon University
Seongnam-si, Gyeonggi-do 13120, Republic of Korea
E-mail: junghchoi@gachon.ac.kr
S. Sun
School of Chemical Engineering
Yeungnam University
Gyeongsan 38541, Republic of Korea
Lithium iron phosphate (LFP) cathodes are promising materials for energy
storage device applications due to their thermal stability, chemical robustness,
cost-effectiveness, and long lifespan. However, their low electronic and ionic
conductivity, as well as challenges in achieving high packing density in thick
electrodes, limit their practical implementation. In this study, a gradient porosity
LFP electrode with a high areal capacity of 6.3 mAh cm2 and an electrode
density of 2.5 g cc1 is proposed. In electrodes with gradient porosity, binder
migration is mitigated, ensuring a uniform binder distribution that enhances
Li-ion kinetics and adhesion strength between the electrode and aluminum
current collector. Furthermore, by employing a particle with short charge carrier
pathways in the bottom layer and a particle with a high tap density in the top
layer, facile Li-ion and electron transfer and easier electrode processing can be
achieved. The resulting gradient porosity electrode with a high areal capacity
of 6.3 mAh cm2 exhibits excellent cycle stability over 100 cycles in full-cell
operation. These ﬁndings provide valuable insight into scalable strategies for
high-energy-density, cost-effective LFP-based Li-ion batteries.
RESEARCH ARTICLE
www.small-structures.com
Small Struct. 2025, 6, 2500093
2500093 (1 of 11)
© 2025 The Author(s). Small Structures published by Wiley-VCH GmbH

cost-effectiveness, and long lifespan.[6] However, LFP suffers
from intrinsic drawbacks such as low electronic and ionic con-
ductivity.[7] The low electronic conductivity of LFP stems from its
wide bandgap and the large effective mass of Fe-3d electrons.[8]
Additionally, the strong covalent bonding of oxygen within the
LFP structure leads to poor ionic diffusivity and hinders electron
mobility.[9] To address these issues, nanoscale particles with
shorter charge carrier pathways are usually introduced to com-
pensate for the low electronic and ionic conductivity of LFP.[10]
In addition, the incorporation of conductive agents to improve
the electronic conducting network within the electrode has been
widely adopted. The proportion of conductive agents in LFP-
based cathode is typically around 3–5 wt%, but it can increase
to 6–10 wt% for high-rate LIBs.[11] However, the use of nanopar-
ticles presents challenges in increasing the packing density of
electrodes, and high conductive agent content reduces the pro-
portion of active material and hinders electrode densiﬁcation.
Consequently, the electrode density of typical commercial LFP
electrodes is only about 2.0 g cc1.[12] Meanwhile, the adoption
of thick electrodes can reduce the proportion of inactive parts
such as separators and current collectors at the cell level, thereby
increasing the energy density of the electrode and enabling cost
reduction.[13] However, in thick electrodes, the upward migration
of binders occurs severely due to capillary force-driven solvent
evaporation during the drying process, leading to uneven binder
distribution within the electrode (Figure 1a). Excessive binder on
the electrode surface exacerbates Li-ion kinetics by increasing
tortuosity, thereby restricting electrolyte transport through the
pores and resulting in poor electrochemical performance.[14]
In addition, the adhesion strength between the electrode and cur-
rent collector would be low due to the lack of binder at the bottom
region of the electrode.[15] To address this issue, research on dry
processing, which does not use any solvent, has been actively
conducted on various active materials.[16] However, the fabrica-
tion of thick LFP electrodes using dry processing has only
recently been reported.[17] From an industrial perspective, there
is a need for methods that allow the fabrication of thick LFP elec-
trodes using existing wet-processing equipment.
In this study, we propose an LFP-based gradient porosity elec-
trode with a high areal capacity of 6.3 mAh cm2 and high elec-
trode density of 2.5 g cc1. To construct a tailored microstructure
that enables both high energy density and favorable Li-ion kinet-
ics, two types of particles with distinct physicochemical proper-
ties were strategically deployed. This electrode design concept is
in line with recent approaches reported in the literature that
emphasize microstructural engineering to enhance battery
performance.[18] One type of particle features short charge carrier
pathways, which can enhance the electronic and ionic conductiv-
ity of the electrode. From the perspective of electrode microstruc-
ture, the electrode employing this particle exhibits a porous
structure. Hereafter, this particle will be referred to as Psp.
The other type, with high tap density, facilitates electrode densi-
ﬁcation and will be referred to as Phd. The gradient porosity elec-
trode was fabricated by introducing Psp and Phd as shown in
Figure 1b. The bottom layer, composed of Psp, enhances elec-
tronic and ionic conduction by shortening the electron and
Li-ion diffusion pathways. It also improves electrolyte perme-
ation properties through a porous structure. The top layer,
Figure 1. Schematic illustration of microstructure evolution of a) a single-layer thick electrode and b) a gradient porosity thick electrode.
www.advancedsciencenews.com
www.small-structures.com
Small Struct. 2025, 6, 2500093
2500093 (2 of 11)
© 2025 The Author(s). Small Structures published by Wiley-VCH GmbH
 26884062, 2025, 7, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/sstr.202500093 by Gachon University Global Campus, Wiley Online Library on [28/01/2026]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

consisting of Phd, enables easier electrode processing and facil-
itates increasing the electrode density. Furthermore, the gradient
porosity electrode design could mitigate the upward migration of
binders, resulting in uniform binder distribution within the elec-
trode and improved Li-ion kinetics. Moreover, the uniform
distribution of the binder throughout the electrode, including
the bottom region, ensures well-maintained interfacial contact
between the electrode and the current collector. Consequently,
the half cell employing the gradient porosity electrode demon-
strated superior capacity retention of 70.4% at 6 mA cm2 com-
pared to the capacity at 0.6 mA cm2, along with excellent cycling
stability. In addition, the full cell employing graphite anode and
LFP gradient porosity cathode with a capacity of 6.3 mAh cm2
and high electrode density of 2.5 g cc1 exhibited stable cycling
behavior with an average Coulombic efﬁciency (CE) of 99.84%
and good cycle retention of 90.9% over 100 cycles. Further-
more, a full cell employing a silicon–graphite composite anode
and the gradient porosity electrode with a higher electrode den-
sity of 2.8 g cc1 showed stable cycling behavior over 300 cycles.
These ﬁndings offer practical guidelines for the development of
high-energy-density LFP-based LIBs.
2. Results and Discussion
Figure 2 shows how the severity of the binder migration
phenomenon affects the properties and electrochemical perfor-
mance of the electrode. Single- and dual-layer electrodes employ-
ing Psp were fabricated (Figure S1a–b, Supporting Information).
Single-layer electrode employing Psp has a porous microstructure
with localized distributions of conductive agents and/or binders
as shown in Figure S1a, Supporting Information. In contrast,
dual-layer
electrode
employing
Psp
would
have
relatively
homogeneous distributions of conductive agents and/or binders
with a porous structure (Figure S1b, Supporting Information).
Water-based contact angle measurement was conducted to com-
pare the extent of binder migration of the two electrodes
(Figure 2a,b). The single-layer electrode has a high contact angle
of 87° compared to the dual-layer electrode (76°), which means
that the hydrophobic polyvinylidene ﬂuoride (PVdF) binder is
much
more
concentrated
on
the
surface
of
the
single-
layer electrode. A similar trend was also observed in the Phd-
based electrodes, where the single-layer electrode exhibited a
higher contact angle than the dual-layer counterpart (Figure S2,
Supporting Information). A previous study reported that a cath-
ode with PVdF binder showed a high contact angle of 119°, as the
presence of ﬂuorine atoms in PVdF forms weak hydrogen bonds
and leads to hydrophobic characteristics.[19] This result suggests
that the high contact angle observed in the single-layer electrode
can be attributed to the increased hydrophobicity caused by the
accumulation of PVdF at the electrode surface. Meanwhile, the
contact angle can be inﬂuenced by electrode porosity. Mercury
porosimetry was performed to compare the porosity of the single-
and dual-layer electrodes. As shown in Figure S3, Supporting
Information, there are no signiﬁcant differences in the porosities
of the single- and dual-layer electrodes (30.4 and 28.8%, respec-
tively), and their pore size distributions are also similar. These
results conﬁrm that the difference in contact angle between
the two electrodes primarily results from differences in surface
hydrophobicity rather than porosity. The sheet resistance of the
electrodes was also measured. Figure 2c shows the sheet resis-
tance of the single- and dual-layer electrode, which can be inﬂu-
enced by the distribution of conductive agents and/or binders
within the electrode. The average sheet resistance of the single-
layer electrode (24.0 Ω) is higher than that of the dual-layer elec-
trode (21.3 Ω), indicating the uneven distribution of conductive
agents and/or binders due to aggravated binder migration in the
single-layer electrode. To further verify the severe binder
migration in the single-layer electrode, the peeling strength of
Figure 2. Comparison of single- and dual-layer electrodes. a,b) Contact angle, c) sheet resistance, and d) adhesion strength of electrode. e) dQ/dV plots
obtained from ﬁrst voltage proﬁles, and f) discharge rate capabilities of the half cell consisting of a LFP cathode and Li anode (charge 0.1 C).
www.advancedsciencenews.com
www.small-structures.com
Small Struct. 2025, 6, 2500093
2500093 (3 of 11)
© 2025 The Author(s). Small Structures published by Wiley-VCH GmbH
 26884062, 2025, 7, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/sstr.202500093 by Gachon University Global Campus, Wiley Online Library on [28/01/2026]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

the electrode was measured through a conventional 180° peeling
test (Figure 2d). The peeling strength of the single- and dual-layer
electrodes was 3.8 and 5.0 gf mm1, respectively. The lower peel-
ing strength value of the single-layer electrode denotes weakened
adhesion strength between the electrode and aluminum current
collector, which is directly related to the lack of binders in the
bottom region of the electrode. Considering these results, the
inhomogeneous microstructure caused by binder migration in
the single-layer electrode deteriorates the electrical and mechan-
ical properties of the electrode, which could lead to poor electro-
chemical performance of the electrode. Figure S4, Supporting
Information shows the ﬁrst voltage proﬁle of single- and dual-
layer electrodes. The dual-layer electrode shows low voltage hys-
teresis (ΔV) between charging and discharging, indicating low
polarization. This result is also consistent with the dQ/dV plots
derived from the ﬁrst cycle voltage proﬁles of single- and dual-
layer electrodes (Figure 2e). In voltage separation (ΔV) of the
Fe3þ/Fe2þ
redox
couple
during
charging/discharging,
the
dual-layer electrode not only has lower voltage separation of
0.11 V but also a sharper peak compared to that of single-layer
electrode. This suggests that the dual-layer electrode enables fac-
ile reaction with Li ions. The rate capability test was conducted at
diverse current densities from 0.1 C to 1.0 C as shown in
Figure 2f. The dual-layer electrode exhibited a discharge capacity
of 3.8 mAh cm2 at 0.5 C, while the single-layer electrode showed
a drastic capacity drop at the same C-rate. The results demon-
strate that dual-layer conﬁguration is an effective strategy for
improving the homogeneity of the electrode microstructure,
leading to enhanced electrochemical performance. However,
despite the improvements provided by the dual-layer conﬁgura-
tion, achieving high-rate performance at 1.0 C or above remained
a challenge.
To further enhance the electrochemical performance of the
dual-layer electrode, we designed a gradient porosity electrode
incorporating Psp and Phd. Scanning electron microscopy
(SEM) images for Psp and Phd are presented in Figure S5,
Supporting Information. Both particles exhibit a morphology
in which nanosized primary particles aggregate to form second-
ary particles, with Psp having a smaller size than Phd. The particle
size distributions (PSDs) of the Psp and Phd are also shown in
Figure S6, Supporting Information. In general, LFP is coated
with a carbon layer on its surface to compensate for its low elec-
trical conductivity, and the electrochemical performance of the
LFP can vary depending on the properties of carbon layer such
as crystallinity and thickness. Therefore, Raman and transmis-
sion electron microscopy (TEM) analyses were performed to
characterize the carbon coating layers of the Psp and Phd. In
Raman spectra, two distinct peaks were observed at around
1345 and 1600 cm1 at both samples, corresponding to the D
band and G band in carbon materials, respectively. A lower
ID/IG ratio indicates higher crystallinity of the carbon layer.
As shown in Figure 3a, the ID/IG ratios of the Psp and Phd are
nearly identical, suggesting that there is no signiﬁcant difference
in crystallinity of the carbon coating layer. Figure 3b,c show TEM
images of the Psp and Phd, respectively. Both LFPs have a uni-
form carbon coating layer with a thickness of 2–3 nm. These
results indicate that the carbon coating layers of the Psp and
Phd have similar properties. The tap densities of the Psp and
Phd are presented in Figure 3d. The Phd showed a larger tap
density of 1.1 g cc1 than that of the Psp (0.9 g cc1). These results
indicate that the Phd has better packing efﬁciency, providing an
advantage in achieving high-density electrodes. Figure 3e shows
that the electrode employing the Psp exhibits enhanced electrolyte
permeability compared to that of the electrode employing the
Phd. This implies that the electrolyte could be well penetrated into
the electrode employing the Psp due to its porous structure, facil-
itating Li-ion transfer. The electrode employing Psp exhibits a
lower contact angle than the electrode employing Phd, which
is consistent with the results of the electrolyte permeation test
(Figure S7, Supporting Information). Cross-sectional SEM
images conﬁrm the microstructure of the gradient porosity elec-
trodes with an areal capacity of 6.0 mAh cm2 (Figure 3f,g). The
electrode employing the Phd in the bottom layer exhibits reduced
porosity toward the bottom. Hereafter, this electrode will be
referred to as the reducing porosity toward bottom (RPB) elec-
trode. In contrast, the electrode employing the Psp in the bottom
layer shows increasing porosity toward the bottom and will here-
after be referred to as the increasing porosity toward bottom
(IPB) electrode. To substantiate the porosity gradient, pore size
distributions and porosities of electrodes employing Psp and Phd
were
measured
by
Mercury
porosimetry.
As
shown
in
Figure S8a, Supporting Information, the electrode employing
Psp has larger pore size than the electrode employing Phd.
Additionally, the porosity values of the electrode with Psp and
Phd are 57.8 and 34.5%, respectively (Figure S8b, Supporting
Information). These results verify that the region consisting of
Psp exhibits higher porosity. The schematic illustration of micro-
structures of the RPB and IPB electrodes is also presented in
Figure S1c,d, Supporting Information. For the RPB electrode,
the bottom layer was composed of a dense layer of Phd, as
shown in Figure 3f and Figure S9a, Supporting Information.
Considering the high areal capacity, the dense bottom layer of
the RPB electrode could hinder the Li-ion kinetics because of
its poor electrolyte permeability. In contrast, the IPB electrode
could exhibit improved Li-ion kinetics due to its porous bottom
layer (Figure 3g and Figure S9b, Supporting Information). To
investigate the impact of porosity distributions on the electrolyte
utilization, contact angle measurement was conducted on three
electrodes, which are dual-layer electrode composed solely of Phd,
RPB electrode, and IPB electrode (Figure S10, Supporting
Information). The IPB electrode exhibits lowest contact angle
value compared to the other two electrodes. This behavior can
be rationalized by considering the balance between capillary
forces and pore structure. The smaller pores in the dense top
layer of the IPB electrode generate higher capillary pressure,
which enhances the initial wetting of the electrolyte.[20] Once
the electrolyte penetrates the dense top layer, the highly porous
bottom layer facilitates further electrolyte diffusion toward the
current collector due to its larger pore volume and superior per-
meability. This capillary-assisted wetting followed by efﬁcient dif-
fusion could provide a favorable environment for Li-ion transport
throughout the electrode.
Electrochemical evaluations of the RPB and IPB electrodes
were conducted. Sheet resistance of the RPB and IPB electrodes
was evaluated via the four-point probe method. As shown in
Figure 4a, the IPB electrode has much lower resistance com-
pared to that of the RPB electrode. Introducing Psp into the bot-
tom layer could improve electrical conductivity by shortening the

electron pathway, allowing electrons transferred from the current
collector to move smoothly into the electrode. This results in low
surface resistance and interface resistance for the IPB electrode
(Figure 4b). Additionally, through-thickness electrical conductiv-
ity (σ) of RPB and IPB electrodes were measured as shown in
Figure S11, Supporting Information. A DC power supply was
used to provide the constant voltage of 1 V and the current values
were monitored. The measured average through-thickness
resistances of RPB and IPB electrodes were 22.2 Ω and 6.3 Ω,
respectively. σ was calculated using the following Equation (1)
σ ¼
L
R  A
(1)
where L, R, and A represent electrode thickness, through-
thickness resistance, and contact area, respectively. The corre-
sponding
values
were
listed
in
Table
S1,
Supporting
Information. The calculated electrical conductivity of the IPB
electrode is 3.5  103 S cm1, which is three times higher than
that of the RPB electrode (1.0  103 S cm1). The charge trans-
fer resistance (Rct) and Li-ion migration resistance (Rion) of the
RPB and IPB electrodes were also analyzed. To obtain the Rct
values, an equivalent circuit model was adopted (Figure S12,
Supporting Information). As shown in Figure 4c and Figure
S13, Supporting Information, the Rct of the IPB electrode is
44.8 Ω, which is about two times lower than the value of the
RPB electrode (75.9 Ω). The result is consistent with the surface
resistance and through-thickness electrical conductivity data,
indicating facile electron transfer within the IPB electrode.
The Rion values, representing the resistance to Li-ion migration
within the electrode, are closely related to the behavior of elec-
trolyte permeation and its migration into the electrode through
the pores. Symmetric cells were fabricated and the Rion values
were determined following the methodology reported in the pre-
vious literature.[21] The RPB and IPB electrodes exhibited Rion
values of 21.3 and 8.3 Ω, respectively (Figure 4d). To examine
electrolyte transport independently of other factors such as

Phd, respectively. d) Tap density of particles, and e) electrolyte permeation of electrodes employing Psp and Phd. Cross-sectional SEM images of f) RPB
electrode (top layer-Psp & bottom layer-Phd), and g) IPB electrode (top layer-Phd & bottom layer-Psp).

were calculated by the following Equation (2) and shown in
Figure 4e.
Nm ¼ τ
ε ¼ Rion  A  κ
d
(2)
where ε represents the porosity, Rion is the ionic resistance (Ω), A
is electrode area (cm2), κ is the conductivity of the electrolyte
(S cm1), and d is electrode thickness (cm), with the values
for these parameters listed in Table S2. The Nm and τ values
of the IPB electrode were 4.13 and 1.14, respectively, signiﬁcantly
lower than those of the RPB electrode (10.6 and 4.5, respectively),
which means that Li-ion kinetics were enhanced due to efﬁcient
penetration of the electrolyte into the porous bottom layer.
Figure 4f shows voltage proﬁles of cells employing RPB and
IPB electrodes at the ﬁrst (0.1 C) and third (0.2 C) cycles. The
cell employing the IPB electrode exhibited low overpotential dif-
ference between the ﬁrst and third cycles, whereas the cell
employing the RPB electrode showed signiﬁcantly increased
overpotential in the third cycle compared to the ﬁrst cycle. To com-
pare the voltage hysteresis of the RPB and IPB electrodes, cyclic
voltammetry (CV) measurement was conducted. The CV curves
exhibit a pair of peaks in the range of 2.5–3.9 V, indicating the
two-phase charge/discharge reactions of the Fe3þ/Fe2þ redox cou-
ple (Figure S14, Supporting Information). The potential gap
between the oxidation peak and reduction peak (ΔV) of the IPB elec-
trode was smaller than that of the RPB electrode (0.7 and 0.85 V,
respectively), representing low polarization of the IPB electrode
due to the enhanced electron and Li-ion kinetics. To determine
the diffusion coefﬁcient of Li-ion (DLi
þ) for RPB and IPB
electrodes, galvanostatic intermittent titration technique (GITT)

IPB electrode shows lower cell polarization than the cell employ-
ing the RPB electrode. DLi
þ was calculated using the following
Equation (3)

where τ, mB, MB, Vm, A, ΔEs, and ΔEt represent pulse duration,

and low tortuosity, Li-ion kinetics could be improved within the
electrode, leading to the high DLiþ. Figure 4i exhibits the rate per-

shows better rate capability compared to the cell employing
the RPB electrode due to the enhanced electrical conductivity and
Li-ion kinetics. The capacity retention of the cell employing IPB
electrode is 70.4 and 45.3% at 1 C and 1.5 C-rates, respectively,
based on the capacity at 0.1 C-rate. However, the cell employing

RPB electrode exhibits limited performance at rates above
1.0 C. These results veriﬁed that the porous bottom layer with
the Psp enables facile Li-ion and electron transfer even with a
high areal capacity and a high electrode density, leading to the
low resistance and high-rate capability of the IPB electrode. To
further validate the advantages of the IPB electrode structure,
we conducted a comprehensive comparison of Rion and rate capa-
bility across eight different electrode conﬁgurations. These
included three single-layer electrodes (Psp, Phd, and hybrid)
and ﬁve dual-layer electrodes (Psp, Phd, RPB, IPB, and hybrid).
As
shown
in
Figure
S16
and
Table
S3,
Supporting
Information, the Rion values of the dual-layer electrodes were
Figure 6. Electrochemical performance of the RPB and IPB electrodes. a,b) Voltage proﬁles of half cells during 100 cycles, c) cycle performance of half
cells during 100 cycles, and d) EIS spectra after 100 cycles. e) Cycle performance of full cells during 100 cycles employing IPB with a capacity of
6.3 mAh cm2 and electrode density of 2.5 g cc1. f) Long-term cycling stability of a full cell employing a silicon–graphite composite anode and the
IPB cathode with a high electrode density of 2.8 g cc1.

consistently lower than those of their single-layer counterparts.
Based on these results, we focused rate capability evaluations
on the dual-layer electrodes, where the IPB electrode demon-
strated
the
best
performance
(Figure
S17,
Supporting
Information). These results reinforce that the gradient porosity
structure, particularly when conﬁgured with a dense Phd top layer
and a porous Psp bottom layer, offers superior electrochemical
performance.
To compare the degree of Li-ion reactions between the RPB
and IPB electrodes, grazing incidence X-ray diffraction (GI-
XRD) analysis was conducted. In particular, the degree of lithium
insertion/extraction of the LFP in the upper and lower regions of
electrodes was examined after discharging at current densities of
0.5 and 1.5 C, while maintaining a constant charging rate of
0.1 C. Figure 5a shows the XRD patterns of the surface (near
the interface between the bulk electrolyte and electrode) of the
RPB and IPB electrodes. When discharging at 0.5 C, the RPB
and IPB electrodes exhibited nearly the same peak positions, cor-
responding to the reference pattern for LFP. In contrast, shifts in
the peak position to higher 2θ values were observed in the RPB
electrode after discharging at 1.5 C. Figure 5b shows a magniﬁed
region from 16° to 18.5°, where the RPB electrode exhibits a peak
corresponding to the (200) plane of FePO4 (FP). Similarly,
Figure 5c, which focuses on the 28° to 33° range, reveals peaks
corresponding to the (211) and (020) planes of FP at the RPB
electrode. These results indicate that the RPB electrode has lower
reactivity with Li-ion at high rates compared to the IPB electrode,
which could be attributed to the high tortuosity of the RPB elec-
trode. XRD patterns of the bottom (near the interface between
the electrode and current collector) of the RPB and IPB electro-
des are presented in Figure 5d. As shown in Figure 5e, a mag-
niﬁed view of the 16° to 18.5° range, both electrodes exhibit peaks
corresponding to the (200) plane of LFP at 0.5 C. However, the
(200) peak of FP is also observed in the RPB electrode, indicating
incomplete Li-ion insertion in the lower region of the RPB elec-
trode even at the lower current density of 0.5 C. At 1.5 C, the
peaks of both electrodes are positioned close to the FP peak posi-
tion. The nearly identical alignment with the FP peak suggests
that Li-ion reactions are not occurring effectively in the lower
regions of the electrodes at high rates. However, it should be
noted that the leftward peak shift of the IPB electrode occurs,
suggesting that Li insertion has occurred. Figure 5f shows the
magniﬁed region from 28° to 33°. Similar to the results shown
in Figure 5e, both electrodes exhibit peaks corresponding to the
LFP peaks at 0.5 C, while the peaks corresponding to the FP peak
are observed at 1.5 C. However, in the IPB electrode, the peak
highlighted with a square box is positioned slightly to the left
of the (211) plane peak of FP, indicating that reaction with Li ions
has occurred. As previously discussed, the IPB electrode exhibits
superior Li-ion kinetics compared to the RPB electrode, which is
attributed to the lower Rion value and reduced electrode tortuos-
ity, as shown in Figure 4d,e. Li-ion insertion could occur in the
bottom layer of the thick IPB electrode even at high rates, leading
to a relatively homogeneous degree of Li-ion reactions and
enhanced electrochemical performance (Figure 5g).
Additional electrochemical evaluations of cells employing the
RPB and IPB electrodes were conducted to investigate electro-
chemical performance of the gradient porosity electrodes.
Figure 6a,b shows the voltage proﬁles of half cells with RPB
and IPB electrodes during the 100 cycles. The cell employing
the RPB electrode achieves areal capacities of 5.45, 5.29, 4.74,
and
0.49 mAh cm2
at
the
10th,
20th,
50th,
and
100th
cycles, respectively. In contrast, the cell employing the IPB elec-
trode delivers the areal capacities of 5.78, 5.76, 5.70, and
5.64 mAh cm2 over the same cycles, respectively. The corre-
sponding cycle performances of half cells with RPB and IPB elec-
trodes are shown in Figure 6c. The cell employing the RPB
electrode exhibits drastic capacity fading as the cycle progresses,
while the cell employing the IPB electrode shows stable CE and
superior cycle retention of 97.5% over 100 cycles. The enhanced
cycling performance of the cell employing the IPB electrode
could be attributed to its improved electrical conductivity and
superior Li-ion kinetics within the well-designed electrode.
EIS analysis of the RPB and IPB electrodes was performed after
cycling. As shown in Figure 6d and Figure S18, Supporting
Information, the IPB electrode shows approximately six-fold
smaller Rct (157 Ω) than the RPB electrode (960 Ω), likely due
to facile electron transfer even after cycling. Furthermore,
cross-sectional SEM images of IPB and RPB electrodes after
cycling were observed to assess their structural evolution. The
microstructure of the IPB electrode remained intact, whereas
the RPB exhibited structural degradation with severe crack for-
mation after 100 cycles (Figure S19, Supporting Information).
These results conﬁrm that the IPB electrode maintains structural
integrity more effectively than the RPB electrode during
prolonged cycling. To investigate the possibility of realizing a
high-energy-density battery, a full cell was fabricated using a
6.6 mAh cm2 graphite anode and the IPB electrode with a high
areal capacity of 6.3 mAh cm2 and electrode density of
2.5 g cc1. The ﬁrst cycle voltage proﬁle of the full cell is shown
in Figure S20, Supporting Information. The full cell exhibited
stable cycling behavior with an average CE of 99.84% and good
cycle retention of 90.9% over 100 cycles (Figure 6e). Additional
long-term cycling test was conducted for a full cell employing a
silicon–graphite composite anode and the IPB cathode with a
higher electrode density of 2.8 g cc1 (Figure 6f). The full cell
showed stable cycling behavior over 300 cycles. These electro-
chemical results conﬁrmed that our electrode design with gradi-
ent porosity could be useful for developing high-energy-density
LFP-based LIBs with robust electrochemical properties.
3. Conclusion
In this study, we designed a thick electrode with an areal capacity
of 6.3 mAh cm2 and electrode density of 2.5 g cc1 with
gradient porosity structure. This gradient porosity electrode
could mitigate the severe binder migration issues (e.g., degrada-
tion in Li-ion kinetics, inhomogeneous electrode microstructure)
that
occur
in
conventional
thick
single-layer
electrodes.
Additionally, the dense top layer comprising Phd enabled easier
electrode processing and facilitated increasing the electrode den-
sity, and a porous bottom layer consisting of Psp enhanced Li-ion
and electron transfer in the electrode. This led to superior capac-
ity retention of 97.5% over 100 cycles and rate capability of 70.4%
at 1.0 C (=6 mA cm2) compared to the capacity at 0.1 C
(=0.6 mA cm2). Furthermore, the full cell employing graphite
anode and gradient porosity LFP cathode with a capacity of
www.advancedsciencenews.com
www.small-structures.com
Small Struct. 2025, 6, 2500093
2500093 (9 of 11)
© 2025 The Author(s). Small Structures published by Wiley-VCH GmbH
 26884062, 2025, 7, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/sstr.202500093 by Gachon University Global Campus, Wiley Online Library on [28/01/2026]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

6.3 mAh cm2 and electrode density of 2.5 g cc1 exhibited stable
cycling behavior over 100 cycles. Our gradient porosity electrode
design can mitigate binder migration in the thick electrode fab-
rication process and enhance the electrochemical performance of
the thick electrode by improving Li-ion kinetics and electron
transfer, providing a viable direction for the commercialization
of high-energy-density LFP-based LIBs.
4. Experimental Section
Electrode Materials and Preparations: To fabricate the wet-processed
electrode, two types of LFP (Psp and Phd), a conductive agent (carbon
black, Super P Li, Imerys), and a 6 wt% binder solution (PVdF) in
NMP with mass ratio of 94:3:3 were prepared. All these materials were
mixed through planetary mixer (ARE-310, THINKY). To fabricate a
single-layer electrode using Psp with a loading level of 39.3 mg cm2,
the slurry was tape-casted on aluminum (Al) foil with 15 μm by doctor
blade and dried in oven at 100 °C for 1 h. In the dual-layer electrode using
Psp, bottom layer slurry was casted on Al foil and dried at 100 °C for 30 min.
The top layer slurry was casted on pre-fabricated bottom layer electrode
using same process. The fabricated dual-layer electrode was composed of
two layers, each with a loading level of 19.8 mg cm2. The RPB and IPB
electrodes were fabricated using the dual-layer electrode preparation pro-
cedure described above. In the RPB electrode, Psp was employed in the top
layer and Phd in the bottom layer, whereas in the IPB electrode, Phd was
employed in the top layer and Psp in the bottom layer. All electrodes were
calendered using a roll pressing machine with rollers of 200 mm in diam-
eter (Rohtech, Korea). The process was performed in multiple steps, dur-
ing which the roll gap was gradually reduced and controlled to achieve the
target electrode density of 2.3–2.8 g cc1.
Cell Assembly: All cell components and electrodes were vacuum-dried at
110 °C overnight before cell assembly. The coin cells (2032 R type cell)
were assembled in an argon-ﬁlled glove box. In the case of half cells,
0.2 T Li-metal was employed as counter electrode and physically stitched
to a stainless spacer. For the full cells, graphite anodes were paired with
LFP electrode at an N/P ratio of 1.05. Polyethylene (PE) separators (2400,
Celgard) with diameter of 19 mm were used in both half and full cells. As
an electrolyte, 1.15 M lithium hexaﬂuorophosphate (LiPF6) was dissolved
in a mixture of ethylene carbonate (EC), ethyl methyl carbonate (EMC),
and diethyl carbonate (DEC) (EC/EMC/DEC = 2/4/4 vol%) including
1 wt% vinylene carbonate (VC), 10 wt% ﬂuoroethylene carbonate (FEC),
and 1 wt% lithium diﬂuorophosphate (LiPO2F2).
Electrochemical
Evaluation:
For
half
cells,
galvanostatic
charge–
discharge cycling tests were performed at a 0.2 C-rate within the voltage
window of 2.5–3.9 V using battery tester (TOSCAT 3000, Toyo systems). In
the case of full cells, following the formation process, cycling tests were
conducted at a 0.2 C-rate between 2.5 and 3.9 V. The rate capability assess-
ments were executed varying discharge rates of 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, and 1.5 C
with a ﬁxed charging rate of 0.1 C after one formation cycle at 0.1 C. To
conﬁrm the redox peaks in various electrolytes, CV measurements were
carried out at scan rate of 0.1 mV s1 over a potential range of
2.5–3.9 V using a potentiostat (ZIVE BP2, WonATech). Electrochemical
impedance spectroscopy (EIS, ZIVE BP2, WonATech) was performed to
evaluate cell resistance including Rion and Rct. Sheet resistance was mea-
sured by four-point probe measurement using a CMT-SR100 M-MP
(Changmin Tech, Korea) device. Four linearly aligned probes were set
on the surface of the electrode. A constant current was applied between
the two outer probes and the sheet resistance was determined by measur-
ing the potential difference between the two inner probes. GITT measure-
ments were conducted by applying a 0.1 C-rate pulse current for 10 min,
followed by a relaxation period of 2 h.
Characterization: Water-based contact angle was observed to measure
the hydrophilicity of single and dual-layer electrode by contact angle mea-
suring instrument. To investigate the surface of LFP particles, the Raman
microscope (LabRAM Aramis, Horbia Jobin Yvon) and high-resolution
transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) analyses were conducted.
Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-7600F, JEOL) was used to
observe the morphology and microstructure of the LFP electrodes. To
measure porosity of LFP electrodes, Mercury porosimetry technique
(Autopore v, Micromeritics) was performed. For postmortem analysis,
LFP electrodes were collected from the disassembled coin cells and
washed with DEC solvent. Grazing incidence X-Ray diffraction (GI-XRD,
D8 Advance) analysis was carried out to conﬁrm the degree of Li concen-
tration in the upper and lower parts of the LFP electrodes at scan speed
of 1 degree per minute.

the author.

[SOURCE: Highly reversible cycling with Dendrite-Free lithium deposition enabled by robust SEI layer with low charge transfer activation energ.pdf]
================================================================================
Applied Surface Science 572 (2022) 151439
Available online 28 September 2021
0169-4332/© 2021 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
Full Length Article 
Highly reversible cycling with Dendrite-Free lithium deposition enabled by 
robust SEI layer with low charge transfer activation energy 
Dongsoo Lee 1, Seho Sun 1, Chanho Kim , Jeongheon Kim , Keemin Park , Jiseok Kwon , 
Dowon Song , Kangchun Lee , Taeseup Song *, Ungyu Paik * 
Department of Energy Engineering, Hanyang University, Seoul 133-791, Korea   
A R T I C L E  I N F O   
Keywords: 
Dendrite-Free Lithium 
Lithium Metal Anodes 
SEI Layer 
LiNO3 
FEC 
A B S T R A C T   
Li dendrite growth and poor Coulombic efficiency still prevent the practical use of Li metal anodes. Here, we 
report a stable solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer consisting of Li3N and LiF by employing lithium nitrate 
(LiNO3) in carbonate electrolytes for the improved cycle performance with dendrite-free Li deposition for Li 
metal anodes. We demonstrate that the lower charge transfer activation energy is realized with the Li3N-rich SEI 
layer and LiF-rich SEI layer in carbonate electrolytes. With the Li3N and LiF composite SEI layer, outstanding 
improvements are achieved in that the very low charge transfer activation energy of 46.64 KJ mol−1 and stable 
electrochemical performances with dendrite-free Li deposition. With those benefits, the Li || Cu cell shows stable 
cyclability with high Coulombic efficiency of ~ 97% at a current density of 0.5 mA cm−2 with a capacity of 0.5 
mAh cm−2 over 200 cycles, and Li can be densely plated with lower porosity of 10.1 % with a plated Li of 2 mAh 
cm−2. Finally, the outstanding cyclability is demonstrated in full cells consisting of LiNi0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2 as a 
cathode and thin Li metal electrode with a thickness of 20 μm in lean electrolytes of 8 μL mAh−1.   
1. Introduction 
Although lithium metal was commercialized as an anode for Li-ion 
batteries (LIBs) in 1987, its unstable nature caused severe safety issues 
and made it fade away from the commercial market [1,2]. So far, the 
energy density of LIBs has been improved by increasing the active ma­
terial loading and density of electrodes, but it has been approaching the 
theoretical limit in the energy density [3]. With the growing demand on 
the high energy density of LIBs, Li metal is back in the spotlight as an 
ultimate anode and has been regarded as a “Holi Grail” of anodes due to 
its high specific capacity (3860 mAh g−1) and the low negative elec­
trochemical potential (–3.04 vs. SHE) [4,5]. However, two significant 
obstacles, Li dendrite growth, and poor Coulombic efficiency, still get in 
the way of the practical implementation of Li metal anodes [6]. In recent 
years, various strategies such as designing current collectors with a 
multi-dimensional structure to reduce the local current density, 
employing a Li protection layer with organic and(or) inorganic materials 
to suppress Li dendrite growth, and exploiting advanced electrolytes 
with high concentration salts and new additives for the formation of the 
stable solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer, have been introduced to 
address the issues related to Li dendrite growth and poor Coulombic 
efficiency [7–12]. 
The Li dendrite growth occurs due to the local space charge caused 
by the anion depletion near the electrode surface during Li deposition 
[13]. The parasitic reactions from Li dendrite growth deteriorate the SEI 
layer with physicochemical inhomogeneity. Accordingly, the additional 
SEI layer is continuously generated, leading to the thick SEI layer with 
large overpotentials [14]. Therefore, it is necessary to stabilize the Li 
metal anodes by engineering the interface between Li metal and the 
electrolyte to prevent the parasitic reaction from Li dendrite growth. Of 
all the approaches, employing electrolyte additives is one of the most 
viable options because electrolyte additives can have a significant effect 
on stabilizing Li metal anodes even with a very small amount in elec­
trolytes. In light of this, many reports demonstrate effective electrolyte 
additives such as lithium nitrate (LiNO3), vinyl carbonate (VC), fluoro­
ethylene carbonate (FEC), cesium cation (Cs+), and copper acetate 
[15–19]. The electrolyte additives contribute to forming a stable SEI 
layer onto the Li metal surface. Among the SEI components, Li3N and LiF 
have great attraction due to low diffusion barrier energy, thermody­
namic stability against Li metal, and mechanical stability with high 
* Corresponding authors. 
E-mail addresses: tssong@hanyang.ac.kr (T. Song), upaik@hanyang.ac.kr (U. Paik).   
1 These authors contributed equally to this work. 
Contents lists available at ScienceDirect 
Applied Surface Science 
journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/apsusc 
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2021.151439 
Received 23 August 2021; Received in revised form 23 September 2021; Accepted 25 September 2021   

Applied Surface Science 572 (2022) 151439
2
Young’s modulus [6,20,21]. The LiF-rich SEI layer is easily generated 
employing FEC as an additive [22,23]. However, the Li3N-rich SEI layer 
is not easily prepared due to the limitation of the solubility of LiNO3 in 
carbonate electrolytes [24]. 
LiNO3 has been widely employed as an additive in lithium–sulfur (Li- 
S) batteries due to improvement in reversibility by suppressing poly­
sulfide shuttling, stabilizing Li metal anodes, and high solubility in ether 
electrolytes (1,2–dimethoxyethane and 1,3–dioxolane). Although LiNO3 
can function as an effective additive in ether electrolytes, it is not 
compatible with carbonate electrolytes due to poor solubility. In this 
regard, there was a report introducing the dissolution promoter to 
dissolve LiNO3 in a common electrolyte of ethylene carbonate (EC) and 
diethylene carbonate (DEC), showing outstanding cycle performances 
[25]. Inspired by this, we have explored both LiF-rich and Li3N-rich SEI 
layers to stabilize the Li metal anodes. Furthermore, we systematically 
studied the synergetic advantages of Li3N and LiF composite SEI layer by 
employing LiNO3 in FEC-based electrolytes in more practical conditions 
in that lithium metal batteries consist of thin Li metal anodes, high 
voltage and high capacity cathodes, and lean electrolytes. 
Here, we report the stable solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer 
consisting of Li3N and LiF by employing lithium nitrate (LiNO3) for high- 
voltage batteries operated in carbonate electrolytes. LiNO3 could be 
utilized in carbonate electrolytes by introducing a trace amount of 
magnesium fluoride (MgF2). The carbonate electrolytes prepared with 
LiNO3 of 0.5 wt% and MgF2 showed good phase stability with no pre­
cipitate over 24 h. To investigate the beneficial effect of Li3N-rich SEI 
layer in carbonate electrolytes, we first evaluated Li || Cu cells. When 
LiNO3 was employed in a common carbonate electrolyte (EC/DEC), the 
cells exhibited lower Li nucleation overpotential and reduced Li plating 
overpotential. The charge transfer activation energy (Ect) through fitting 
the semicircles (Rct) in Li || Cu cells depending on temperatures was 
reduced in a LiNO3 employed common carbonate electrolyte due to the 
Li3N-rich SEI layer compared to that of the blank electrolyte. When 
LiNO3 was employed in a FEC-based electrolyte (DMC/FEC), the cells 
exhibited significantly lower overpotentials and the lowest Ect compared 
to the others due to the synergetic advantages of the Li3N and LiF 
composite SEI layer. Furthermore, stable cyclability with high 
Coulombic efficiencies (~97%) was achieved in Li || Cu cells with the 
Li3N and LiF composite SEI layer. Using the blank electrolyte, small Li 
nuclei and Li dendrite growth were observed during initial Li plating, 
resulting in porous Li plating behaviors later. However, nodular Li 
nuclei were observed in LiNO3 employed electrolytes at initial Li 
deposition, enabling dense and planar Li plating later. Especially when 
employing LiNO3 employed FEC-based electrolyte, the thickness of the 
plated Li of 2 mAh cm−2 was 10.8 μm, corresponding to the theoretical 
thickness with the low porosity of 10.1%. Finally, the advantages of the 
Li3N and LiF composite SEI layer were demonstrated in full cells using 
LiNi0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2 as a cathode with an areal capacity of 2 mAh cm−2 
and thin Li metal electrode with a thickness of 20 μm in a lean electrolyte 
condition of 8 μL mAh−2. The cell with LiNO3 employed FEC-based 
electrolyte exhibited an outstandingly stable cycle performance with a 
capacity retention of 78% over 120 cycles. 
2. Materials and methods 
Electrolyte preparation: E1 (LiPF6 in EC/DMC/DEC==1/1/2 (v/v/ 
v)) and E3 LiPF6 in DMC/FEC = 4/1(v/v) were purchased from PANAX 
StarLyte. To prepare E2 and E4, 0.2 wt% MgF2 (Sigma aldrich) and 0.5 
wt% LiNO3 (Sigma aldrich) were added in E1 and E3, respectively, and 
vigorously stirred for 72 h to be completely dissolved. EM: EC/DMC/ 
DEC = 1/1/2 (v/v/v) + MgF2 0.2 wt%, EN: EC/DMC/DEC = 1/1/2(v/v/ 
v) + 0.5 wt% LiNO3, E1: EC/DMC/DEC = 1/1/2 (v/v/v), E2: EC/DMC/ 
DEC = 1/1/2(v/v/v) + 0.2 wt% MgF2 & 0.5 wt% LiNO3, E3: DMC/FEC 
= 4/1(v/v), E4: DMC/FEC = 4/1(v/v) + 0.2 wt% MgF2 & 0.5 wt% 
LiNO3, E2′: EC/DMC/DEC = 1/1/2(v/v/v) + 0.2 wt% MgF2 & 1.0 wt% 
LiNO3, E4′: DMC/FEC = 4/1(v/v) + 0.2 wt% MgF2 & 1.0 wt% LiNO3. 
Material characterization: The morphology of Li metal anodes was 
observed by using field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE- 
SEM, JEOL JSM07600F). To observe a SEI layer, X-ray photoelectron 
spectroscopy (XPS, VG Microtech ESCA2000) was employed to obtain 
the valence state and components of the elements, and the binding en­
ergy was calibrated by referencing the C 1s peak to 284.8 eV. All samples 
were sealed in a glovebox prior to analysis by each instrument 
Electrochemical measurements: Battery performance was tested 
using a battery cycle tester (TOSCAT 3000, Toyo System, Tokyo, Japan). 
To observe the SEI formation, Li was plated at a current density of 0.05 
mA cm−2 for 1000 s in a Li || Cu cell. For the current response analysis, 
the cyclic voltammetry (CV) was measured using a Li || Cu cell at a scan 
rate of 10 mV s−1 between 1.0 and –0.3 V using a potentiostat (Autolab, 
PGSTAT – 72637). To demonstrate the charge transfer activation en­
ergy, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was observed at an 
overpotential of ~ 1.0 V with frequencies from 0.001 to 100 kHz and an 
amplitude of 10 mV using a potentiostat (Autolab, PGSTAT – 72637) 
depending on the temperature using refrigerated circulation Bath chiller 
(JSR). For the galvanostatic Li || Cu cell and Li || Li symmetric cell test, 
2032 round type coin cells were fabricated using Li metal anodes of 200 
μm (Welcos) and a polypropylene (PP) separator in the voltage window 
of −
1.0 – +1.0 V. For Li || NCM full cell tests, the NCM electrode was 
prepared by mixing NCM (LG Chem.), polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF, 
Solvay), and Super P carbon in 96:2:2 ratio with N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone 
(NMP) as a solvent. The areal mass loading of NCM in the electrode was 
~ 10 mg cm−2. EIS was performed with frequencies from 0.001 to 100 
kHz and an amplitude of 10 mV using a potentiostat (Autolab, PGSTAT – 
72637) before and after 10 cycles. 
3. Results and discussion 
To employ LiNO3 in carbonate electrolytes, we first checked the 
solubility of LiNO3 in different electrolytes (Fig. 1a). MgF2 and LiNO3 
were not dissolved in carbonate electrolytes showing precipitates. 
Hereafter, we called a common carbonate electrolyte E1, LiNO3, and 
MgF2 employed common electrolyte E2, an FEC-based electrolyte E3, 
and LiNO3 and MgF2 employed FEC-based electrolyte E4. When 
employing 0.5 wt% LiNO3 with 0.2 wt% MgF2, E2 and E4 electrolytes 
initially showed white color. When employing 1.0 wt% LiNO3 with 0.2 
wt% MgF2, the electrolytes initially exhibited opaque white color. 
However, the electrolytes showed poor stabilities, forming white LiNO3 
precipitates after 24 h (Fig. S1). Based on those results, we chose the 
ratio of LiNO3 and MgF2 optimized in carbonate electrolytes as 0.5 wt% 
and 0.2 wt%. To investigate the SEI formation on a Cu current collector, 
we evaluated Li || Cu cells at a current density of 0.05 mA cm−2 for 1000 
s (Fig. 1b) [25]. When employing LiNO3 in carbonate electrolytes, the 
cathodic decomposition of LiNO3 started from ~ 1.7 V (vs. Li+/Li), and 
lower nucleation overpotential (at the onset) and Li plating over­
potential (plateau) were observed [24]. Furthermore, the current 
response in E2 and E4 noticeably increased during Li plating/stripping 
on a Cu current collector, which means a reversible electrochemical 
reaction with low overpotentials due to the Li3N-based SEI layers 
(Fig. 1c) [26]. Generally, the activation energy for charge transfer (Ect) 
is mainly attributed to Li+ desolvation and conducting through the SEI 
layer at the interphase because of the SEI layer as charge transfer barrier 
in LIBs [27,28]. The Ect and charge transfer resistance (Rct) are in the 
Arrhenius relationship, therefore we measured Rct of Li || Cu cells as a 
function of temperature using different electrolytes (Fig. S2). To form an 
enough SEI layer on the surface of Cu substrate, Li || Cu cells were tested 
at 0.5 mA cm−2 with a capacity of 2 mA cm−2 for two Li plating/strip­
ping cycles before electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) mea­
surement [29]. All of the Rct values exist on a straight line representing 
the Arrhenius relationship (Fig. 1d). The measured Ect of E2 (64.23 KJ 
mol−1) was slightly lower than that of E1 (66.57 KJ mol−1) due to the 
Li3N-rich SEI layer. The Ect of E3 (52.96 KJ mol−1) was much lower than 
E1 and E2, which is attributed the LiF-rich SEI layer from the 
D. Lee et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      

Applied Surface Science 572 (2022) 151439
3
decomposition of FEC in E3. Surprisingly, the Ect of E4 (46.64 KJ mol−1) 
was remarkably reduced compared to the others owing to the stable SEI 
layer consisting of both Li3N and LiF from the decomposition of FEC and 
LiNO3, respectively [11,30]. 
Coulombic efficiency (CE) is calculated as a ratio of the stripped Li 
capacity to the plated Li capacity on a working electrode, which is a 
critical parameter directly related to the reversible cell operation [31]. 
To study the Coulombic efficiency depending on the SEI layers, we 
evaluated Li || Cu cells with a galvanostatic cycling test. When Li || Cu 
cells were tested at a current density of 0.5 mA cm−2 with a capacity of 
0.5 mAh cm−2, the cell with E1 exhibited continuously decreased CEs 
due to the poor SEI nature, showing the average CE of ~ 80% over 10 
cycles (Fig. 1e, Fig. S3, and Table 1). However, the cell with E2 showed 
the improved cycle performance with lower overpotentials, showing the 
average CE of ~ 85% over 10 cycles, but abruptly decreased CEs near 40 
cycles because of the small amount of LiNO3 in E2 was continuously 
exhausted during repeated cycling. The cell with E3 exhibited stable 
cycle performances at earlier cycles but showed fluctuating CEs during 
cycling with an average CE of 88% from 10 to 200 cycles. The 
outstanding cycle performance with high CEs was achieved in the cell 
with E4. The cell with E4 showed highly stable cyclability due to the 
stable Li3N and LiF composite SEI layer, showing an average CE of 97% 
from 10 to 200 cycles. When the testing current density and capacity 
were increased, more significant differences in CEs were observed in Li 
|| Cu cells. The cells with E1, E2, and E3 exhibited abruptly decreased 
CEs from the initial cycle. Among them, the cell with E3 short-circuited 
near 20 cycles, showing ~ 100% CE without onset/offset potentials 
(Fig. S4). Conversely, the cell with E4 showed significantly improved 
cyclability with a high average CE of 94% from 10 to 150 cycles. These 
beneficial advantages for adopting LiNO3 in carbonate electrolytes came 
from the different solvation structures of electrolytes. The noticeable 
chemical shifts of 13C NMR stand for the coordination of solvent and 
Mg2+ (Fig. S5 and S6) [32]. The Mg2+ has a slightly smaller ionic radius 
of 0.72 Å but a twice charge number compared to Li+ (0.76 Å). The Mg2+
can interact with solvent molecules with its stronger electron with­
drawing ability and could be coordinated with NO3
–
, which enhances 
the solubility of LiNO3. 
The initial Li nucleation behavior has a great effect on further Li 
plating, which is closely related to CE, cyclability, overall volume 
changes of the cell, and safety [33]. To monitor the Li nucleation 
behavior depending on electrolytes, Li was plated at a current density of

Applied Surface Science 572 (2022) 151439
4
0.5 mA cm−2 with a capacity of 0.1 mAh cm−2 in a Li || Cu cell [34]. The 
cell with E1 showed a typical Li dendrite structure. However, the cell 
with E2 exhibited apparent differences in Li nucleation behavior, 
showing nodular Li growth with a diameter of ~ 700 nm (Fig. 2a,b and 
Fig. S7a,b). When E3 was employed, the small Li nuclei with a diameter 
of ~ 200 nm and dendritic Li were observed (Fig. 2c and Fig. S7c). In 
case of the cell with E4, Li was deposited in a film-like form, and large Li 
nuclei with a radius of ~ 800 nm was observed (Fig. 2d and Fig. S7d). To 
demonstrate Li plating related to Li nucleation (initial plating), Li was 
further plated with a current density of 0.5 mA cm−2 with a capacity of 
2.0 mAh cm−2 in a Li || Cu cell. The plated Li with E1 had a very porous 
structure with Li dendrites due to the poor SEI layer (Fig. 2e,i). The 
plated Li with E2 showed a dense and planar structure compared to that 
with E1, but there existed Li dendrite in some parts (Fig. 2f,j). When 
using E3, the plated Li showed planar without noticeable Li dendrite due 
to the LiF-rich SEI layer (Fig. 2g,k). When Li was plated using E4, Li 
showed a very dense structure without Li dendrite due to the robust Li3N 
and LiF composite SEI layer (Fig. 2h,l). Their cross-sectional SEM images 
show apparent differences in the plated Li. The thickness of the plated Li 
was 20.8, 17.4, 17.2, and 10.8 μm when using E1, E2, E3, and E4, 
respectively, and the porosity of the plated Li was 53.4, 44.3, 43.6, 
10.1%, respectively (Fig. 2i–l and Table 2) [35]. To monitor the dead 
(electrochemically inactive) Li generation, the Li || Cu cells were tested 
at a repeated Li plating/stripping at a current density of 0.5 mA cm−2 
with a capacity of 2.0 mAh cm−2 (Fig. S8). When employing E1 without 
any additives, the whole Cu electrode was covered with Li dendrite and 
dead Li, showing the gray-colored surface in its optical image (inset). 
The Cu electrodes with E2 and E3 exhibited partially covered Li 
dendrite. However, the Cu electrode with E4 showed a clearly different 
surface with its original shiny yellow color. These results demonstrate 
that dense Li plating with dendrite-free Li can be realized when 
employing E4, which is due to the robust LiF-rich SEI layer from FEC 
with supporting Li3N from LiNO3, as shown in Fig. 4 described later 
[36,37]. 
To further demonstrate the beneficial effects of the Li3N and LiF 
composite SEI layer, Li || Li symmetric cells were evaluated with 
different current densities and capacities. When Li || Li symmetric cells 
were tested at a current density of 1 mA cm−2 with a capacity of 1 mAh 
cm−2, the cell with E1 showed the poor cycle performance with the 
largest overpotentials and short-circuited after 90 h (Fig. 3a,b). The cell 
with E2 exhibited lower overpotentials compared to those of the cells 
with E1 and E3, but the cells with E2 and E3 showed no significant 
difference in cyclability with short-circuit near 200 h. The Li3N-rich SEI 
layer enabled the lower polarization, but the LiNO3 was depleted during 
repeated cycles, resulting in the short-circuit. Contrary to the cells with 
E1, E2, and E3, the cell with E4 represented outstandingly stable cycling 
with the lowest overpotentials over 800 h in a Li || Li symmetric cell, 
which proves the robustness of the Li3N and LiF composite SEI layer. 
When increasing the current density to 2 mA cm−2 and a capacity to 5 
mAh cm−2, more significant differences in cyclability were observed in 
Li || Li symmetric cells (Fig. 3c). The cell with E1 was unstable with poor 
cyclability, showing internal short after 25 h (5 cycles). The cells with E2 
and E3 exhibited drastically increased overpotentials at earlier cycles

5
and short-circuited near 50 h. However, the cell with E4 showed an 
extremely stable cycle performance with low overpotentials over 200 h. 
A SEI layer has a great effect on the Li plating/stripping behaviors 
and overall electrochemical performances such as cyclability and rate 
capability [38]. To demonstrate the robust SEI layer attributed to LiNO3 
and FEC in carbonate electrolytes, XPS analysis was employed after 
testing Li metal anodes at a current density of 1 mA cm−2 with a capacity 
of 1 mA cm−2 for 10 cycles in Li || Li symmetric cells (Fig. 4) [39]. LiF in 
an SEI layer can be attributed to the decomposition of LiPF6 of the 
conventional Li salt and FEC. When using E1 and E2, the Li metal anodes 
showed Li2CO3-rich SEI corresponding to 55.2 eV other than LiF cor­
responding to 56.2 eV due to the absence of FEC (Fig. 4a,b,e,f) [40]. 
However, the Li metal electrodes with E3 and E4 (FEC-based electro­
lytes) had LiF-rich SEI layers due to the decomposition of FEC (Fig. 4c,d, 
g,h). The Li metal anode with E2 showed the formation of Li3N at 398.0 
eV, and the Li metal anode with E4 presented the formation of Li3N and 
LiOxNy at 398.0 and 400.0 eV, however such peaks were not observed in 
the Li metal anodes with E1 and E3 in N 1 s peaks (Fig. 4i–l) [41]. O 1s 
peaks also showed the same trend of the formation of LiOxNy in the SEI 
layer when E4 electrolyte, but not E1 and E3 (Fig. 4m–p). In brief, Li3N 
can be formed when employing LiNO3 employed carbonate electrolytes 
and the LiF-rich SEI layer can be generated when using FEC-based 
electrolytes. The SEI layer, consisting of Li3N attributed to LiNO3, en­
ables lower overpotentials, nodular Li nucleation and plating, better 
electrochemical properties due to the high Li ion conductivity, however 
it is not possible to maintain sustained performance owing to the lack of 
the amount in carbonate electrolytes. On the other hand, when LiNO3 is 
employed in FEC-based electrolytes, the LiF-rich SEI layer can effec­
tively protect the Li metal surface and reduce the amount of consump­
tion of LiNO3 in electrolytes. The synergetic advantages of Li3N and LiF 
contribute to the low charge transfer activation energy, the low nucle­
ation and plating overpotential, planar and dense Li plating with 
dendrite-free Li, stable cyclability in Li || Cu and Li || Li symmetric cells. 
Finally, we demonstrate the practical use of the Li3N and LiF com­
posite SEI layer by evaluations of the Li || LiNi0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2 (NCM) 
full cells in a practical condition. Fig. 5a shows the evaluation of Li || 
NCM full cells at 0.5C-rate using NCM with an areal capacity of 2 mAh 
cm−2 and Li with a thickness of 50 μm. The cell with E1 showed very 
poor cycle performance with abruptly decreasing capacities after 
25cycles, which is not attributed to electrolyte depletion but electro­
chemically active Li depletion (Fig. S9 and Fig. S10) because the elec­
trolyte was still wetting [42]. With employing E2, the cell showed bit 
enhanced cyclability, but also the capacities of the cell drastically 
decreased near 40 cycles because the poor SEI nature accelerated the 
fast consumption of LiNO3 and could not enable long-term cycling of the 
cell [43]. Contrastively, the cells with E3 and E4 exhibited stable cycle 
performances compared to those with E1 and E2, which supports that 
the advantages of the LiF-rich SEI layer in Li metal anodes. Especially, 
the cell with E4 that has the highly stable SEI layer among the controlled 
electrodes showed long-term cycling over 300 cycles, and the electrolyte 
was not depleted and still wet in the separator (Fig. S10a). The SEM 
images of Li metal anodes further exhibit the differences in the

Fig. 3. Galvanostatic cycle performance of Li || Li symmetric cells; a) at a current density of 1.0 mA cm−2 with a capacity of 1.0 mAh cm−2, b) corresponding 
enlarged images, and c) at a current density of 2 mA cm−2 with a capacity of 5 mAh cm−2. The Li || Li symmetric cells were tested at the same current density and

Applied Surface Science 572 (2022) 151439
6
reversible Li plating/stripping behaviors during cycling (Fig. S10b–i). To 
investigate the discharge rate performance, the 50 μm Li || NCM full 
cells were tested up to 4C-rate (Fig. 5b). The discharge capacities were 
high in the order of E4, E3, E2, and E1 at 4C-rate, which substantiates 
that the stable and unique SEI layer of E4 enables fast Li+ diffusion with 
a lower charge transfer activation barrier [44]. The EIS results demon­
strate that the charge transfer resistances (semicircle at high-frequency 
region, Rct) of the cells with E2 and E4 were lower than those with E1 
and E3 before the cycle, which is due to the lower charge transfer 
activation energy. However, the Rct values of the cells were much lower 
than those with E1 and E2 because of the stable SEI layer mainly caused 
by the decomposition of FEC. These results further prove that the Li3N- 
based SEI layer contributes the lower charge transfer activation energy, 
forming a stable SEI layer, and can produce more synergistic effects with 
FEC. Finally, we elucidate the practical use of E4 by evaluating full cells 
using the NCM electrode with an areal capacity of 2 mAh cm−2, the Li 
metal anode with a thickness of 20 μm, and lean electrolyte of 8 μL/mAh 
of NCM [45]. The cells with E1 and E2 showed slightly enhanced 
cyclability compared to those with Li with 50 μm, but their capacities 
were drastically reduced at earlier cycles, which is not due to the Li 
depletion but the electrolyte depletion caused by poor SEI layers and low 
Coulombic efficiencies (Fig. S11 and Fig. S12). The cell with E3 showed 
stable cycle performance at earlier cycles, but the capacities dropped to 
near 70 cycles due to the Li depletion, showing thick and porous dead Li 
(Fig. S11c and Fig. S12f,b). On the other hand, the cell with E4 exhibited 
a totally different behavior compared to the other with stable cyclability 
over 120 cycles (Fig. S11d). Furthermore, the Li metal anode with E4 
showed a dense and planar structure with dendrite-free morphologies 
after cycling (Fig. S12h,i). These results demonstrate that the stable SEI 
layer attributed to LiNO3 and FEC can enable reversible Li plating/ 
stripping behaviors with dendrite-free Li and stable electrochemical 
properties with lower charge transfer activation energies. 
4. Conclusions 
In conclusion, we systematically studied the electrochemical stabil­
ity of the SEI layer using four kinds of carbonate electrolytes. To build 
Li3N-based SEI layer, we employed LiNO3 in carbonate electrolytes in 
which the solubility of LiNO3 could be enhanced by employing a trace 
amount of MgF2 in carbonate electrolytes. LiNO3 can help to reduce the 
charge transfer activation energy and overpotentials by the formation of 
the Li3N-based SEI layer, enabling stable and dense Li plating with 
nodular structured Li. But the long-term cycling could not be realized 
due to the insufficient amount of LiNO3 in the common carbonate 
electrolyte. When LiNO3 was employed in the FEC-based electrolyte, 
synergetic advantages of the Li3N and LiF composite SEI layer were

Fig. 4. High resolution XPS spectra of Li metal electrodes after 10 cycles at a current density of 1 mA cm−2 with a capacity of 1 mA cm−2 in Li || Li symmetric cells to 
demonstrate SEI component of each cell; a–d) Li 1s, e–h) F 1s, i–l N 1s, and m–p) O 1s peaks.

Applied Surface Science 572 (2022) 151439
7
observed. The Li || Cu cell with the Li3N and LiF composite SEI layer 
showed very stable cycling with high CEs of ~ 97% at a current density 
of 0.5 mA cm−2 with a capacity of 0.5 mAh cm−2 over 200 cycles, and Li 
can be plated with dense and dendrite-free morphologies. The full cell 
with the Li3N and LiF composite SEI layer can be operated with stable 
cyclability over 120 cycles in a practical condition with NCM of 2 mAh 
cm−2, thin Li of 20 μm, and lean electrolyte of 8 μL/mAh of NCM. The 
beneficial effects of LiNO3 in FEC-based electrolyte is attributed to the 
stable SEI formation consisting of Li3N and LiF with high Li ion- 
conducting and insulating components.

[SOURCE: Homogeneous Li deposition guided by ultra-thin lithiophilic layer for highly stable anode-free batteries.pdf]
