Energy Storage Materials 61 (2023) 102899
Available online 28 July 2023
2405-8297/© 2023 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
Homogeneous Li deposition guided by ultra-thin lithiophilic layer for 
highly stable anode-free batteries 
Junghwan Kim a,b,1, Gyeong Ryul Lee c,1, Roy Byung Kyu Chung c,*, Patrick Joohyun Kim b,*, 
Junghyun Choi a,* 
a Energy Storage Materials Center, Korea Institute of Ceramic Engineering and Technology, Jinju 52851, Korea 
b Department of Applied Chemistry, Kyungpook National University, Daegu 41566, Korea 
c School of Materials Science and Engineering, Kyungpook National University, Daegu 41566, Korea   
A R T I C L E  I N F O   
Keywords: 
Li metal batteries 
Anode-free batteries 
Surface-modified current collector 
Uniform Li deposition 
Atomic Layer Deposition 
A B S T R A C T   
Li-metal batteries (LMBs) are intensively studied to keep up with the growing demand for sustainable and high- 
capacity energy storage devices. However, the practical implementation of LMBs is still challenging owing to the 
catastrophic side effects associated with the growth of dendritic Li and inferior Coulombic efficiency. To enhance 
the long-term electrochemical stability and high-rate performance of LMBs, it is crucial to control the 
morphology of Li deposition over the current collectors. Herein, we propose surface-modified current collectors 
to investigate how a lithiatable layer affects the morphology of Li deposition and thus contributes to the stable 
electrochemical performance of LMBs at high current densities. The lithiatable layer improves the electrolyte 
wetting ability, which efficiently diminishes the interfacial resistance between electrode and electrolyte. 
Moreover, the lithiatable layer induces homogeneous Li nucleation, consequently leading to the uniform Li 
deposition over the surface of current collectors. Due to these synergistic effects, the anode-free cells with 
surface-modified current collectors have achieved excellent cycle stability in comparison to that with conven­
tional Cu current collector.   
1. Introduction 
The electric vehicle (EV) market has grown rapidly to meet the de­
mand of carbon neutrality in the transport sector, which is one of the 
major carbon emitters (~24% of global CO2 emissions) [1]. To reach a 
critical tipping point, EVs should hit the driving range parity with in­
ternal combustion engine vehicles. Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are the 
most promising energy storage devices for EVs due to their outstanding 
electrochemical performances [2,3]. Although LIBs have shown 
impressive progress in terms of energy density and cycle stability, they 
are still not enough to be employed for advanced electronic devices 
requiring superior performance capabilities. Accordingly, developing a 
new battery system with high energy density and excellent stability has 
become the most urgent priority. 
Compared to the conventional anodes (e.g. graphite, Si/Graphite 
composite, etc.), metallic lithium (Li) has garnered huge attention as a 
next-generation anode due to its superior electrochemical performances, 
including low reduction potential (~3.04 V vs. standard hydrogen 
electrode), high gravimetric (3860 mAh g−1), and volumetric capacities 
(2060 mAh L−1) [4–7]. Among all the Li-metal batteries (LMBs), the 
LMB with anode-free configuration has regarded as an obvious solution 
to improve the energy density [8,9]. The energy density of anode-free 
LMBs is 60% higher than that of conventional LIBs [10]. Moreover, 
the structure of anode-free LMBs is relatively simple, which reduces the 
cost of cell assembly. However, the LMBs typically suffer from serious 
side effects such as the dendritic Li growth, low reversibility of the Li 
deposition/dissolution process, and instability of solid electrolyte 
interphase (SEI) [11–13]. In the case of anode-free LMBs, 
lattice-mismatched Cu current collector facilitates the irreversible side 
reactions. The absence of Li compensation from the anode side leads to 
the rapid capacity fading [14,15]. Therefore, anode-free LMBs are more 
challenging to achieve stable cycle performance compared with con­
ventional LMBs. 
In order to address all the issues mentioned above, a number of ap­
proaches have been proposed, such as designing three-dimensional 
porous scaffolds, adopting a configuration of all-solid-state battery 
* Corresponding authors. 
E-mail addresses: roy.b.chung@knu.ac.kr (R.B.K. Chung), pjkim@knu.ac.kr (P.J. Kim), jchoi@kicet.re.kr (J. Choi).   
1 These authors are equally contributed to this work. 
Contents lists available at ScienceDirect 
Energy Storage Materials 
journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/ensm 
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ensm.2023.102899 
Received 7 June 2023; Received in revised form 21 July 2023; Accepted 22 July 2023   

Energy Storage Materials 61 (2023) 102899
2
(ASSB) system, modifying conventional current collectors, etc. [12,16, 
17]. As to the design of three-dimensional (3D) scaffolds, it has shown 
promising performances in terms of cycle stability. However, most of the 
3D scaffolds designed for LMB application have not achieved desirable 
volumetric energy density owing to the significant thickness variation 
from 50 to 800 μm [18]. Another approach to allow the anode-free 
configuration is to adopt the all-solid state battery (ASSB) system 
[19]. The anode-free LMB with the ASSB system exhibited the most 
promising cycle stability and energy density. However, the ASSB system 
faces similar safety issues posed by short circuits. The growing Li den­
drites tend to spread through grain-boundaries of solid electrolytes, 
especially at high current densities above 2.0 mAh cm−2 [20]. In addi­
tion, the requirement for external pressure on the ASSB system and the 
inferior rate performance of ASSB system impede its practical applica­
tion [19–21]. Different from the aforementioned strategies, the surface 
modification of current collectors is a desirable approach to resolve a 
number of issues related to the anode-free system. The initial nucleation 
and growth of Li is typically affected by the surface properties of current 
collectors [22]. 
In this study, we propose surface-modified current collectors to study 
how a lithiatable layer affects the Li deposition behavior and thus con­
tributes to the performances of anode-free cells. An ultrathin non- 
lithiatable layer (UNL) and ultrathin lithiatable layer (ULL) are 
employed as a current collector modifying materials to improve the 
interface affinity between with current collector and electrolyte. The 
main difference between UNL and ULL lies in their Li affinity which 
typically affects the nucleation energy barrier. As opposed to the UNL, 
the ULL could transform to Li2O layer embedded with transition metal 
during lithiation process, which provides a lithiophilic property. The 
improved electrolyte wettability and lithiophilic property contributed 
by the ULL lead to the formation of uniform Li nuclei and thus facilitates 
the homogenous Li deposition over the current collector. Due to these 
synergistic assistances of the ULL-modified Cu current collector 
(ULL@Cu), stable cycle stability and high rate performance of anode- 
free cells were achieved. 
2. Experiment 
2.1. Preparation of UNL-modified and ULL-modified Cu current 
collectors 
Before the deposition of UNL and ULL over the Cu current collectors, 
a solvent cleaning process was carried out on Cu foils to remove the 
residual of organic matters and native oxides. The cleaning process was 
performed in the following sequence: acetone, isopropyl alcohol (IPA), 
and deionized (DI) water. To remove the native copper oxide on the Cu 
foil, acid etching process was performed using a solution of hydrofluoric 
acid (HF) and DI water, diluted at a 1:10 ratio. 
UNL and ULL were deposited on the Cu foil using atomic layer 
deposition (ALD) with a thickness of 10 nm. TMA (trimethylaluminium) 
was used as the precursor material for UNL, and TDMASn (Tetrakis- 
dimethylamino Tin) was used as the precursor material for ULL. The 
reactant used in the deposition process was H2O. The argon (Ar) gas was 
used throughout the entire process. The base pressure inside the 
chamber was maintained at 0.5 Torr. The growth per cycle (GPC) of UNL 
was 1.78 nm/cycle, and the GPC of ULL was 0.5 nm/cycle. 
2.2. Characterizations 
The surface analyses of bare Cu current collector, UNL@Cu and 
ULL@Cu were conducted by Field Emission Scanning Electron Micro­
scope (FE-SEM, SU8220, Hitachi, Japan). Energy dispersive X-ray 
spectroscopy (EDS, Ultim Extreme, Oxford, attached to FE-SEM) 
confirmed that UNL and ULL were uniformly deposited on the Cu cur­
rent collector. The hydrophilicity of each current collector with polar 
solvent was confirmed by contact angle measuring instrument (Phoenix 
300 Touch, SEO). To investigate the Li deposition mechanism over the 
current collectors, FE-SEM and Cs-corrected transmission electron mi­
croscope (Cs-TEM, FEI) measurements were carried out. The focused ion 
beam (FIB, Thermo Scientific, Brno, Czech Republic) was used to pre­
pare the samples for TEM measurements. X-ray photoelectron spec­
troscopy (XPS, PHI 5000 Versa Probe, ULVAC-PHI Inc, USA) was 
performed to analyze the composition of SEI layer on each current 
collector. 
2.3. Electrochemical measurements 
The standard 2032 R type coin cells were used for the electro­
chemical measurements and were assembled in the dry room with 
dewpoint below -50 ◦C. To verify the Li storage mechanism of each 
current collector, cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements were con­
ducted at a constant scan rate of 0.1 mV s−1 with a voltage window 
between 0 V to 1 V using Biologic Science instruments (VSP-300). In the 
Li-Cu cells, a polyolefin separator (SETELA, Toray Films), 1.0 M lithium 
hexafluorophosphate (LiPF6) in a mixture of ethylene carbonate and 
diethyl carbonate (EC:DEC = 1:1 vol.%) and 1.0 T Li foil (applied as 
counter electrode) were used for comparing the electrochemical stabil­
ities of each current collector. The areal capacity of the Li deposition 
process was fixed at 1.0 mAh cm−2 and the Li dissolution process had a 
cut-off voltage of 1.0 V. The Li-Cu cells were evaluated at constant 
current densities of 4.0 and 8.0 mA cm−2. Electrochemical impedance 
spectroscopy (EIS, VSP, Biologic, Seyssinet-Pariset) was carried out at 
frequency range from 250 kHZ to 10 MHz with an amplitude of 5 mV. 
The cycling tests of anode-free cells paired with LCO were performed 
within the voltage range from 3.0 to 4.3 V. In the formation step, each 
anode-free cell was cycled three times at a low current density to sta­
bilize the surface property of each current collector. [23] 
3. Results and discussion 
During the initial stage of Li deposition, the wettability between 
current collector and liquid electrolyte plays a crucial role in deter­
mining the morphology of Li nucleation. The pure Cu has relatively poor 
electrolyte wettability due to its hydrophobic properties [24]. The poor 
wettability between Cu current collector and electrolyte induces inho­
mogeneous Li nucleation over the surface of current collector, which 
eventually leads to the uncontrollable growth of sharp Li dendrites. 
Fig. 1 shows the mechanism by which the Cu current collector and 
ULL@Cu affect the formation of Li nucleation and Li dendrites. In the 
LMB with an anode-free configuration, the lithiophilicity of the current 
collector and electrochemical stability are closely related. The lith­
iophobic properties of the Cu current collector result in a lattice 
mismatch between Cu current collector and Li at the beginning of the Li 
deposition process, leading to the formation of small Li nuclei with a 
high contact angle [25]. As to the ULL@Cu, the formation of the lith­
iophilic matrix reduces the nucleation energy barrier at surface, result­
ing in the uniform Li deposition with a low contact angle [26]. The 
uniformly deposited Li not only significantly reduces the possibility of 
battery short circuit but also improves the reversibility of Li deposi­
tion/dissolution process. 
Figs. 2a-2c show the surface morphology and elemental analysis of 
each current collector. Through SEM-EDS mapping measurement, it is 
confirmed that UNL and ULL are successfully deposited on the surface of 
the current collector. The semiquantitative elemental analysis is sum­
marized in Fig. S1 and S2. To increase the validity of the deposition of 
UNL and ULL, the XPS survey scan is displayed in Fig. 2d-2f. 
Although the nano scale metal oxide layer is coated on the surface, 
there is no difference in the homogeneity of surface roughness, which 
can be found in SEM images. To investigate the surface properties of 
each current collector, the contact angle measurements related to the 
electrolyte affinity were carried out, as shown in the inset images. The 
UNL@Cu and ULL@Cu exhibit lower contact angles (70.1◦and 76.9◦, 
J. Kim et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      

Energy Storage Materials 61 (2023) 102899
3
respectively) than that of the Cu current collector (93.5◦) due to the 
hydrophilicity of metal oxides [27]. This implies that each UNL and ULL 
has a high affinity with the polar solvent (= electrolyte). 
The cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements were conducted to un­
derstand how the Li interacts with each current collector. Fig. 3a shows 
the initial CV curves of each current collector. As to the Cu current 
collector and the UNL@Cu, the reduction/oxidation peaks in the CV 
curve are very weak, with only the small SEI peak observable. In the case 
of the ULL@Cu, the strong reduction peaks corresponding to stepwise 
reactions with Li are clearly observed. The A and B peaks correspond to 
the conversion reaction of ULL and the alloy reaction of metallic seeds, 
respectively [28]. In the sequence cycles shown in Fig. 3b, only a distinct 
B-B’ pair of peaks is observed in the ULL@Cu. These results imply that 
only the ULL@Cu exhibits the reversible alloy reactions with Li. How­
ever, it should be noted that ULL serves as a nucleation layer, rather than 
as an anode material since the very small amount of ULL was deposited 
Fig. 1. Schematic to illustrate the roles of the ULL@Cu in LMBs with an anode-free configuration compared with the Cu current collector.  
Fig. 2. Characterization. (a1-c1) SEM images and (a2-c2) corresponding EDS mapping images of Cu current collector, UNL@Cu and ULL@Cu. (d-f) XPS survey scan 
of each current collector. 
J. Kim et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      

Energy Storage Materials 61 (2023) 102899
4
on the Cu current collector. 
Fig. 3c shows the schematic illustration of the Li metal deposition/ 
dissolution processes in the cell with ULL@Cu. The associated electro­
chemical reactions of the cell with ULL@Cu involve six reaction steps. 
MyO + 2xLi++2xe−→yM + Li2xO
(1)  
yM + xLi++xe−→LixMy
(2)  
Li+ + e−→Li
(3)  
Li→Li+ + e−
(4)  
LixMy→yM + xLi++xe−
(5)  
yM + Li2xO→MyO + 2xLi++2xe−
(6) 
(“M” denotes the lithiatable metal) 
During the lithiation process, the ULL transforms to a lithium oxide 
(Li2O) layer embedded with transition metals at 1.36 V. The resulting 
transition metals further react with Li to become alloy (LixMy). By the 
presence of LixMy, the uniform Li deposition takes place with a low 
contact angle, corresponding to the reaction ‘③’. From the SEM images 
in Fig. S3, the lithiophilicity derived from the LixMy could be confirmed. 
The surface of the ULL@Cu showed the film-like Li morphology after 
slight Li deposition, since the lithiophilic matrix lowers the nucleation 
energy barrier and thus facilitates the uniform Li deposition. During the 
reverse reaction from ④ to ⑥, the Li dissolution and dealloying reaction 
occur in sequence. However, the reaction ‘⑥’ associated with the for­
mation of MyO from transition metal and Li2O is not reversible within 
the voltage range between 0 and 1 V [29,30]. Based upon these reactions 
Li2O still remains in the ULL even after delithiation process, contributing 
to the formation of lithiophilic matrix over the ULL@Cu. The resulting 
lithiophilic matrix, composed of LixMy and Li2O, induces uniform Li 
deposition with low Li nucleation overpotential. Additionally, the sta­
bility of SEI layer can be enhanced by abundant Li2O, which promotes 
fast Li-ion transportation and thus induces lateral Li growth [31,32]. 
Due to these synergistic effects, dendrite-free Li metal anode can be 
constructed. 
To evaluate the electrochemical stabilities of the cell with the Cu 
current collector, UNL@Cu and ULL@Cu, repetitive Li deposition/ 
dissolution tests were carried out. The cycle performance of the Li-Cu 
cells was conducted at a high current level (4.0 mAh cm−2) (Fig. 4a). 
The Coulombic efficiency (CE) of the cell with the Cu current collector 
drastically decays to below 50% after 30 cycles due to the severe growth 
of Li dendrites. The formation of dendritic Li results in the breakdown 
and restoration of the SEI layer which consumes both Li and the elec­
trolyte. However, the cells with the UNL@Cu and ULL@Cu maintain a 
stable CE compared to the cell with the Cu current collector. From the 
XPS results in Figs S4 and S5, the UNL and ULL peaks are observed on the 
Cu current collector after cycling, confirming the stability of the UNL 
and ULL even after the repetitive electrochemical reaction. It is note­
worthy that, as shown in Fig. 2a1-2c1, electrolyte wetting ability was 
improved by depositing the UNL and ULL even though there was no 
significant distinction in terms of surface roughness. The improved 
electrolyte wettability of the UNL@Cu and ULL@Cu reduces the possi­
bility of sharp Li dendrite formation, contributing to the maintenance of 
stable CE [33,34]. Moreover, the cell with the ULL@Cu maintained 
steady and stable CE over 100 cycles. In order to identify the reason for 
the different cycle stability initial voltage profiles were compared, 
revealing different nucleation overpotentials (Fig. 4b and 4c). The cell 
with the UNL@Cu presents a slightly higher Li deposition overpotential 
(−0.46 V) than the cell with the Cu current collector (−0.43 V) since the 
metal oxide layer acts as an electron insulator. In contrast, the cell with 
the ULL@Cu exhibits the lowest nucleation overpotential, since the ULL 
was reduced to a transition metal with high electrical conductivity 
during the formation of lithiophilic matrix. Additionally, lithiophilic 
Fig. 3. Mechanism of the electrochemical Li deposition/dissolution. Cyclic voltammetry curves of the Cu current collector, UNL@Cu and ULL@Cu at (a) the first 
cycle and (b) the second cycle. (c) Schematic illustrating the mechanism of Li deposition/dissolution on the ULL@Cu. 
J. Kim et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      

Energy Storage Materials 61 (2023) 102899
5
property of ULL@Cu also contributes to its low overpotential by 
decreasing the nucleation energy barrier. As shown in the voltage pro­
file, the cell with the ULL@Cu still has a high efficiency of Li deposi­
tion/dissolution even after cycling (Fig. 4d). Similarly, Fig. S6 
demonstrates consistent cycle performances, which were evaluated at 
even higher areal current density of 8 mA cm−2. 
The cell resistance of each current collector at the initial and final 
cycles was verified by EIS measurements (Fig. 4e-4g). The initial charge 
transfer resistances (Rct) of the cells with the Cu current collector and 
UNL@Cu are 171.0 Ω and 196.0 Ω, respectively. The high Rct of the cell 
with the UNL@Cu corresponded with the Li deposition overpotential, as 
shown in Fig. 4c. In contrast, the Rct of the cell with the ULL@Cu is 75.4 
Ω owing to its lithiophilic properties. After cycling, the Rct of the cell 
with the Cu current collector increases to 320.5 Ω, indicating the un­
controlled growth of Li dendrites and the depletion of the electrolyte. 
Meanwhile, the cell with the ULL@Cu presents the smallest Rct of 107.8 
Ω after cycling. It is considered that the ULL@Cu effectively suppresses 
dendritic Li growth and achieves superior electrochemical reversibility 
during cycling. 
The morphology of Li deposition over each current collector was 
investigated at a current density of 4.0 mA cm−2 with 0.3 mAh cm−2. 
Fig. 5a-5c show the photographs of each current collector as a function 
of lithiation degree. In the partial lithiation step, the ULL@Cu shows a 
blue surface, indicating that the ULL reacted with Li (Fig. 3a). After 
partial deposition, randomly distributed Li spots are observed on the Cu 
current collector and UNL@Cu. These non-uniform Li spots are verified 
as Li dendrites with needle-shaped morphologies and compact cluster 
(Fig. 5d and 5e). In contrast, uniform metallic Li layer are detected on 
the ULL@Cu, which were induced by the uniform lithiophilic matrix 
(Fig. 5f). To precisely observe the morphology of Li deposition at the 
interface between electrolyte and current collectors, HR-TEM analyses 
were carried out using Li-deposited current collectors (Fig. 5g-5i). The Li 
deposition layer on the Cu current collector comprises void spaces and 
branching morphologies (Fig. 5g). These irregular Li depositions tend to 
form sharp Li dendrites, posing a threat to the stable operation of LMBs. 
Compared with the Cu current collector, both UNL@Cu and ULL@Cu 
have relatively smooth Li deposition layers owing to their better 
wettability against electrolyte (Fig. 5h and 5i). Interestingly, the 
ULL@Cu has the thinnest and densest Li layer in comparison with other 
two current collectors, which is attributed to the support of lithiophilic 
matrix formed during the initial stage of lithiation. The uniform depo­
sition of Li on the ULL@Cu potentially suppresses the formation of sharp 
Li dendrites, thereby reducing the excessive consumption of liquid 
electrolyte. Extrapolating from these microscopic results, the lithiophilic 
matrix formed on the current collector is capable of inducing homoge­
neous Li nucleation during the initial deposition step, thus contributing 
to the uniform Li deposition. 
To confirm the chemical composition of the initial SEI layer on each 
current collector, XPS measurements were performed after one cycle. In 
the O 1 s spectra, four different organic/inorganic complexes, derived 
from the decomposition of electrolyte, are confirmed as presented in 
Fig. 6a-6c. The oxygen peak at around 528.3 eV indicates the existence 
Fig. 4. Electrochemical performances of Li-Cu cells. (a) Cycling performances of each cell with the Cu current collector, UNL@Cu and ULL@Cu at a current density of 
4 mA cm−2. Voltage profiles of each cell at (b and c) the first cycle and (d) 100 cycles. (e-g) EIS spectra of each Li-Cu cell at the initial cycle and final cycle. 
J. Kim et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      

Energy Storage Materials 61 (2023) 102899
6
Fig. 5. Examination of the surface of each current collector after partial Li deposition. (a-c) Photographs showing the process of Li deposition on each current 
collector. (d-f) SEM and (g-i) cross-sectional TEM images of the Cu current collector, UNL@Cu and ULL@Cu after partial Li deposition. 
Fig 6. XPS Analyses of the surface of each current collector after one cycle. XPS spectra of (a-c) O 1 s and (d-f) F 1 s for the SEI layer retrieved from Li-Cu cells with Cu 
current collector, UNL@Cu, and ULL@Cu. 
J. Kim et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      

Energy Storage Materials 61 (2023) 102899
7
of Li2O. It is worth noting that the ULL@Cu exhibits the highest peak 
intensity corresponding to Li2O in comparison with other current col­
lectors. This strong intensity of the Li2O peak cannot be explained by the 
electrolyte decomposition. Instead, it can be further evidence of the 
presence of Li2O layer which was converted from the ULL. 
Li-F is a chemically and thermally stable inorganic component, 
which prevents the side reaction between the electrode and electrolyte 
and facilitates fast Li-ion transport [35,36]. The XPS F (1 s) spectra of 
each current collector are compared and deconvoluted to analyze the 
surface components, as shown in Fig. 6d-6f. The main peaks at around 
685 eV and 688 eV are assigned to Li-F and CHF, respectively. The 
ULL@Cu exhibits the highest Li-F peak ratio (81.5%) among the current 
collectors, in comparison to the Cu current collector (45.2%) and 
UNL@Cu (65.1%). As the growth of sharp Li dendrites, triggered by 
non-uniform Li nucleation, accelerates the destruction of the formed SEI, 
it significantly increases the ratio of CHF to LiF (Fig. 6d). In contrast, the 
homogeneous Li nucleation on the ULL@Cu leads to the planar Li 
growth, which supports to maintain the structural integrity of the SEI 
layer. Based upon these results, it can be inferred that the stable for­
mation of Li2O and LiF components within the SEI layer is closely related 
to the homogeneous Li nucleation and growth. 
In order to test the practical applicability of ULL@Cu as a modified 
current collector, anode-free cells were fabricated using LiCoO2 (LCO) 
cathode. LCO has been considered the most successful cathode material 
for LIBs owing to its high energy density and excellent durability. As 
LCO electrode has a limited capacity of 2.0 mAh cm−2, the cycle sta­
bilities of each current collector can be directly compared. 
The electrochemical test was performed at 0.5 C (1 C = 172 mA g−1) 
with a voltage window between 3 and 4.3 V. All the anode-free cells with 
different current collectors exhibit no noticeable change in nominal 
discharge voltage, indicating that the redox reaction in ULL does not 
affect the entire energy density (Fig. S7). In other words, there were no 
additional irreversible reactions in the anode-free cell with ULL@Cu 
since the small amount of ULL was deposited. The cycle performance of 
anode-free cells undergoes rapid degradation owing to its low CE and 
absence of Li compensation from the anode side (Fig. S8). In order to 
improve the cycle stability in anode-free configuration, pre-activation 
was carried out at low current density with multiple cycles. 
Fig 7a-7c exhibit the voltage profiles of each anode-free cell with pre- 
activation. The initial capacities of the anode-free cells with Cu current 
collector and UNL@Cu gradually decrease during cycling, due to an 
irreversible Li consumption from LCO. In contrast, the anode-free cell 
with ULL@Cu maintains a superior discharge capacity of 157 mAh g−1 
which is almost same with initial capacity. It is because that the ho­
mogeneous Li nucleation on the ULL@Cu contributes to the reversible Li 
deposition/dissolution without unwanted Li consumption. In contrast, 
the anode-free cells with Cu current collector and UNL@Cu show fluc­
tuating CE during cycling, indicating the excessive loss of Li (Fig. 7d). As 
shown in the result of cycle stability, the anode-free cell with ULL@Cu 
delivers the best capacity retention, with 98.5% after 50 cycles (Fig. 7e). 
In conclusion, these results suggest that the cycle stability and electro­
chemical reversibility of anode-free cells are significantly influenced by 
the electrolyte wettability and lithiophilicity of current collectors. 
4. Conclusion 
In summary, we proposed surface-modified current collectors to 
improve the overall electrochemical stabilities of LMBs at high current 
densities. When both UNL@Cu and ULL@Cu were introduced in the Li- 
Cu cells, the cycle performance and Coulombic efficiency were signifi­
cantly improved in comparison to the cell with the Cu current collector. 
The cell with the ULL@Cu exhibited the lowest nucleation overpotential 
and charge transfer resistance among the cells. These results are 
attributed to the advantages of the ULL: a) the enhanced wettability 
followed by reducing the interfacial resistance, b) the lithiophilic 
properties of embedded alloy particles inducing the homogeneous Li 
nucleation, and c) the provision of ionic conductive paths for stable Li 
deposition in the planar direction. Due to these synergistic benefits, the 
ULL@Cu promoted uniform Li nucleation and growth, resulting in the 
excellent cycle stability with a retention of 98.5% after 50 cycles in the 
anode-free cell paired with LCO. This study would establish a framework 
for controlling the Li nucleation and deposition and pave a new path for 
achieving highly stable anode-free cells. 
Fig. 7. The evaluation of anode-free cells using LCO cathodes. Voltage profiles of anode-free cells with (a) Cu current collector, (b) UNL@Cu, and (c) ULL@Cu. (d) 
Coulombic efficiency and (e) capacity retention of each anode-free cell. 
J. Kim et al.

================================================================================
[SOURCE: Hydroxylated carbon nanotube enhanced sulfur cathodes for improved electrochemical performance of lithium–sulfur batteries.pdf]
================================================================================
13682 | Chem. Commun., 2015, 51, 13682--13685
This journal is ©The Royal Society of Chemistry 2015
Cite this: Chem. Commun., 2015,
51, 13682
Hydroxylated carbon nanotube enhanced sulfur
cathodes for improved electrochemical
performance of lithium–sulfur batteries†
Joo Hyun Kim,a Kun Fu,b Junghyun Choi,ab Seho Sun,a Jeonghyun Kim,c
Liangbing Hu*b and Ungyu Paik*ac
Hydroxylated multi-walled carbon nanotubes were introduced into
sulfur cathodes to utilize the hydrophilic attraction between the OH
group and polysulfides as well as to increase the utilization of sulfur.
This approach shows a promising strategy for diminishing the poly-
sulfides shuttling behavior and improving the electrochemical perfor-
mance of lithium–sulfur batteries.
Battery-powered devices ranging from portable electronics to electric
vehicles have greatly dominated our lives, but the development of
lithium-ion batteries is gradually lagging behind the rapid develop-
ment of those devices.1 As a matter of fact, battery performance is
largely dependent on galvanic electrode parts, especially the cathode
component. As a promising cathode candidate, sulfur has received
great interest owing to its high theoretical capacity of 1672 mA h g1
and rich abundance.1,2 Although lithium–sulfur batteries possess
many merits as a promising energy storage solution, they still have
several challenges that remain to be solved, including the intrinsic
low electric conductivity of sulfur (5  1030 S cm1 at 25 1C) and the
polysulfides shuttling effect.3
To address these challenges, many approaches based on
carbon materials have been used to confine polysulfides physically
in the cathode in order to increase their electrical conductivity and
avoid polysulfides loss, including synthesizing the carbon–sulfur
composite via sulfur melt adsorption2,4 or inserting a carbon block-
ing layer in between the sulfur cathode and the separator.5 Besides
these approaches, chemical protection, such as use of hydroxyl
functional groups (–OH) that can chemically bind with the highly
polar polysulfides, has also proved to be a promising way to further
stabilize polysulfides in carbon materials.2,6
In this communication, we present an eﬀective method of
introducing hydroxylated multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWNTs)
into the sulfur composite in order to improve the electrochemical
performance of Li–S batteries. We prepared the sulfur–MWNT
composite via an in situ sulfur deposition method, which is favorable
to prepare the core–shell type of carbon–sulfur composites on a large
scale.7 Previous studies suggest that a hydrophilic functional group
formed on carbon materials can effectively trap the polysulfide
species due to their polarizing attraction.2,8 Fig. 1 illustrates the
hydrophilic behavior between polysulfides and OH-MWNTs. During
the discharge process, lithium ions are electrochemically reacted
with sulfur–MWNTs to form lithiated sulfur–MWNTs. In this step,
the reduced polysulfides are dissolved out of the sulfur electrode and
then attracted to the hydroxyl groups on the surface of the MWNTs
by creating a chemical gradient, which efficiently inhibits the
Fig. 1

polysulfides during the discharge.

Chem. Commun., 2015, 51, 13682--13685 | 13683
diffusion of polysulfides out to the lithium metal and conse-
quently alleviates the shuttle effect.
The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of the
MWNTs and the as-prepared sulfur–MWNTs are presented in
Fig. 2a and b, respectively. After depositing sulfur on the
surface of the MWNTs, the sulfur–MWNT composite exhibits
a core/shell structure and does not possess any agglomeration
of sulfur clusters, indicating that the sulfur was uniformly
coated onto the MWNTs (Fig. S1, ESI†). Transmission electron
microscopy (TEM) was used to examine the structure of the
sulfur–MWNT composite. As shown in the TEM images (Fig. 2c
and d), highly crystallized MWNTs and conformal coated sulfur
can be clearly observed. The thickness of sulfur coating was
around 5 nm. Additionally, electron energy loss spectroscopy
(EELS) mapping was carried out to investigate the distribution
of sulfur along with the MWNTs (Fig. 2e). The EELS mapping
shows that conformal sulfur coating was uniformly distributed
on the MWNTs. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) of the MWNTs, the
sulfur–MWNT composite, and sublimed sulfur are presented in
Fig. 2f. The sulfur–MWNTs and sublimed sulfur have a similar
pattern with no shift of peaks, indicating that sulfur is well
distributed on the surface of the MWNTs and the in situ sulfur
deposition method does not affect the crystallinity of sulfur in
the sulfur–MWNT composite.9 For further analyzing the surface
characteristic of OH-MWNTs and sulfur–MWNTs, XPS measurement
was conducted and plotted in Fig. S2 (ESI†). The peak centered at
163.75 eV corresponds to the aroma peak of the C–S bond in sulfur–
MWNTs, indicating that the C–S–C bond was formed between
OH-MWNTs and sulfur by in situ sulfur deposition.8c,10 To compare
the effect of the OH group on the electrochemical performance of
Li–S batteries, three types of samples including only sulfur–MWNTs
(S/MWNTs), sulfur–MWNTs mixed with a 25 wt% pristine MWNTs
(S/MWNT-P25), and sulfur–MWNTs mixed with 25 wt% OH-MWNTs
(S/MWNT-OH25) were evaluated based on the sulfur mass, and the
values assigned are 1.5 mg cm2, 1.18 mg cm2, and 1.19 mg cm2,
respectively. Fig. 3a shows the increased initial discharge–charge
capacity, when the MWNTs are introduced, which is due to the
improved conductivity of the electrode.11
To analyse the electrochemical characteristics of each electrode,
electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were
conducted in the frequency range of 250 kHz to 10 mHz at an
excitation amplitude of 10 mV. The Nyquist plots are composed of a
semi-circle at high frequency and a linear line at low frequency
(Fig. 3b). The intersection with the x-axis corresponds to Ohmic
resistance between the electrode and electrolyte, and the values for
the S/MWNT, S/MWNT-P25, and S/MWNT-OH25 are 2.5 O, 2.6 O
and 2.5 O, respectively. On the other hand, the diameter of the semi-
circle in the right part corresponds to the polarization resistance.12
The S/MWNTs show a high polarization resistance of 17.9 O,
whereas the S/MWNT-P25 and S/MWNT-OH25 show lower resis-
tance values of 10.1 O and 9.9 O, respectively. The similar values of
Ohmic resistance but diﬀerent values of polarization resistance
are ascribed to the eﬀect of conductive MWNTs. Fig. 3c pro-
vides the cycling performance of each electrode. The initial
specific
capacities
of
S/MWNT-P25
and
S/MWNT-OH25
increase to 1230 and 1274 mA h g1, respectively. In contrast,
the specific capacity of S/MWNT-OH25 at 100 cycles exhibits a
higher cycle retention of 56.6% than that of 49.4% for S/MWNT-
P25. Fig. 3d shows the rate retention of each electrode with an
increase in the C-rate from 0.1 C to 2 C. The S/MWNT-OH25
exhibits a higher capacity retention of 60% at 2 C than that of
S/MWNT and S/MWNT-P25. This performance is attributed to the (1)
homogenous distribution of the conductive OH-MWNTs due to
a favorable dispersibility of the OH group and (2) hydrophilic
Fig. 2

(c) MWNTs and (d) sulfur–MWNTs, (e) element mapping image using electron
energy loss spectroscopy (EELS) for carbon and sulfur, and (f) XRD patterns.
Fig. 3
Electrochemical characterizations of S/MWNT, S/MWNT-P25, and
S/MWNT-OH25. (a) Initial discharge–charge curve, (b) Nyquist plot, (c)
cycling performance, and (d) rate retention.

13684 | Chem. Commun., 2015, 51, 13682--13685
This journal is ©The Royal Society of Chemistry 2015
attraction by the OH-MWNTs. These two properties can provide
a continuous conducting pathway inside the sulfur cathode and
meanwhile suppress the polysulfide shuttle eﬀect.13 Even
though the Nyquist plots of S/MWNT-P25 and S/MWNT-OH25
show almost the same trend, the cycling performance and rate
retention exhibit diﬀerent electrochemical behavior. The discharge
curves of the S/MWNTs with diﬀerent amounts of hydroxylated
CNTs are shown in Fig. S3(a–c) (ESI†). S/MWNT-OH50 exhibited the
highest capacity retention in 100 cycles. The cycling performances of
these three samples are presented in Fig. S2d (ESI†); the initial
discharge specific capacity of S/MWNT-OH50 is 945 mA h g1 and it
retained 637 mA h g1 after 100 cycles with a retention of 67.5%,
showing higher cycle retention than S/MWNT and S/MWNT-OH25
but relatively lower discharge capacity.
To validate the substantial eﬀect of the OH-group on capturing
the polysulfides within the electrode system, we compare the
relative quantity of polysulfides in the electrolyte by conducting
ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometry (UV-Vis) absorption measure-
ments. As lithium polysulfide strongly absorbs UV radiation, the
contents of polysulfide can be demonstrated by the relative inten-
sity of UV absorption.14 In Fig. 4a, the range of wavelength that
correlates with the reaction with bi-products of sulfur and UV light
is approximately between 260 and 300 nm. The wavelength of S2
2,
elemental sulfur, and S6
2 are 265 nm, 280 nm and 300 nm,
respectively. All of these sulfur species were detected in the
electrolyte because they could not be returned back to the original
state of sulfur–MWNTs due to the irreversible electrochemical
reaction and subsidiary phenomena such as the shuttle effect.
With the increased amount of OH-MWNTs, the intensity of the
overall absorption peaks relative to the concentration of lithium
polysulfides and elemental sulfur in electrolyte decrease distinctly.
Thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) was conducted to examine the
content of sulfur within the electrode before and after 100 cycles
(Fig. S4, ESI†). The decomposition temperature of PVdF is around
375 1C. The sulfur loading percentages of each of the S/MWNT,
S/MWNT-OH25, and S/MWNT-OH50 electrodes before battery test-
ing are 46.4%, 35.8% and 25.1%, respectively. After completing
100 cycles, the content of the remaining sulfur in each of the
S/MWNT, S/MWNT-OH25, and S/MWNT-OH50 electrodes was
calculated before the temperature reached 375 1C, at which PVdF
decomposes, and found to be 20.4%, 19.3%, and 17%, respectively.
The percentage of remaining sulfur in the S/MWNT after 100 cycles
is almost similar to the numerical value of cycle retention after
100 cycles. After finishing the cycle evaluation, the percentages of
remaining sulfur in the S/MWNT, S/MWNT-OH25, and S/MWNT-
OH50 electrodes after 100 cycles are 44%, 53.9% and 67.6%
(Fig. 4b). The capacity retention values of S/MWNT, S/MWNT-
OH25, and S/MWNT-OH50 are 44.5%, 56.6% and 67.5%, which
are consistent with the sulfur remaining in the electrode after the
corresponding cycles.
In this communication, we introduced hydroxylated-MWNTs
into a sulfur cathode in order to diminish the shuttle eﬀect of
polysulfides between electrodes in a physical and chemical way
and also enhance the conductivity of the sulfur electrode. An
in situ sulfur deposition method was used to prepare the sulfur–
MWNT composite with a core–shell structure, which provides a
scalable strategy for making sulfur composites. An increase in
the ratio of OH-MWNTs to the sulfur electrode leads to a
noticeable enhancement in cycling performance and specific
capacity. The S/MWNT-OH25 exhibited a higher specific capacity
of 1274 mA h g1 and a more stable cycle retention of 56.6%
than those of the S/MWNTs. However, further addition of OH-
MWNTs (more than 25 wt%) into the electrode rather inhibits
the full activation of sulfur, thereby resulting in a decrease in the
specific capacity. The S/MWNT-OH50 showed a lower initial
specific capacity of 945 mA h g1 but a higher cycle retention
of 67.5% at 100 cycles than those of the S/MWNT-OH50.
To clarify this result, the electrochemical performance of the
S/MWNT-OH75 is additionally evaluated, exhibiting a much
lower specific capacity but stabilized cycling performance, which
is plotted in Fig. S6 (ESI†). Extrapolating from these results, the
optimal proportion of OH-MWNTs can maximize the specific
capacity and improve the cycling performance, whereas excess
addition of OH-MWNTs limits the full utilization of sulfur,
leading to poor specific capacity. In order to confirm the
immediate effect of OH-MWNTs on the electrode after the
discharge–charge process, UV-Vis absorption and TGA measure-
ment were conducted. It is proved that the OH-MWNTs are
effective in encapturing the dissolved polysulfide within the
electrode. We believe that our approach can be further enhanced
by modifying the ratio of OH-MWNTs to the sulfur–carbon
composite or using the sulfur-impregnated mesoporous carbon
to maximize the hydroxyl group effect. In addition, it can be
extended to the sulfur composite based on the transition metal
oxide (MnxOy, VxOy, TixOy, etc.) as well as the carbon material
which possesses different hydrophilic oxygen-containing groups.
