0013-4686/© 2023 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
Improved Li-ion kinetics of the anode by kneading process of binder for 
lithium-ion batteries with high energy density 
Keemin Park a, Seungcheol Myeong a, Dongsoo Lee a, Hee Eun Yoo a,b, Jaeik Kim a, Chanho Kim a, 
Jeongheon Kim a, Seho Sun a, Jiseok Kwon a, Soo Chan Kim c, Kangchun Lee d, 
Chae-Woong Cho e,*, Ungyu Paik a,*, Taeseup Song a,f,* 
a Department of Energy Engineering, Hanyang University, 222 Wangsimni-ro, Seoul 04763, Republic of Korea 
b SB) Pouch Development Team, Samsung SDI, 467 Beonyeong-ro, Cheonan 31086, Republic of Korea 
c Lab) Electrode Development Group, Samsung SDI, 130 Samsung-ro, Suwon 16678, Republic of Korea 
d Department of Electronic Engineering, Kyonggi University, Suwon 16227, Gyeonggi-Do, Republic of Korea 
e GPE) Process Development Team, Samsung SDI, 150 Gongse-ro, Giheung 17084, Republic of Korea 
f Department of Battery Engineering, Hanyang University, 222 Wangsimni-ro, Seoul 04763, Republic of Korea   
A R T I C L E  I N F O   
Keywords: 
Slurry dispersion 
Binder adsorption 
Kneading process 
Li-ion kinetics 
Lithium carboxymethyl cellulose 
A B S T R A C T   
The low Li-ion kinetics caused by the high ionic and charge transfer resistance in the dense and thick electrode 
deteriorates electrochemical properties in lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) with high energy density. Here, we report a 
kneading process of the carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) binder to improve Li-ion kinetics in the anode. The 
kneading process of Na-CMC increases the adsorption amount of Na-CMC on the graphite surface, which im­
proves the dispersibility of the anode slurry. Enhanced dispersibility enables uniform pore distribution in the 
anode, which improves Li-ion kinetics. Moreover, replacing the Na-CMC with Li-CMC in the kneading process 
further improves Li-ion kinetics in the anode by reducing the charge transfer activation energy due to the for­
mation of high Li-ion conducting LiF-rich solid-electrolyte interphase layer. The anode employing Li-CMC 
kneading process exhibits enhanced constant current charging capacity retention and cycling stability 
compared to those of the pristine anode.   
1. Introduction 
Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) with high energy density have been 
studied to satisfy the rapid growth of the electric vehicle market [1–4]. 
Although studies have been focused on the various active materials with 
high gravimetric and volumetric capacities, such as polyanion-type 
cathode and Si-based anode, their commercial use has been prevented 
due to unsatisfactory electrochemical properties [5–8]. On the other 
hand, the high energy density of LIBs has been achieved by employing 
electrode with high density and high loading level [9,10]. However, the 
decrease in pore size and volume associated with increased density and 
loading level in the electrode degrades its electrochemical performances 
due to low Li-ion kinetics [11–14]. More importantly, inhomogeneous 
microstructure in the electrode caused by the high applied stress to in­
crease density further deteriorates Li-ion kinetics associated with tor­
tuosity increase and corresponding poor electrochemical performances 
of LIBs [15,16]. Therefore, the microstructure of the electrode should be 
controlled to achieve LIBs with high energy density. 
The microstructure of the electrode is closely related to the distri­
bution of the active materials on the current collector during the coating 
and drying process of the slurry. The uniformly distributed active ma­
terials in the electrode can effectively spread the applied stress during 
the calendering process to the entire electrode, which enables formation 
of the homogeneous microstructure in the electrode [17]. As the dis­
tribution of the active materials on the current collector is attributed to 
the dispersibility of the slurry, enhancement of slurry dispersibility 
should be achieved [17–19]. Graphite, which is used as an active ma­
terial in the anode, is not easily dispersed in water solvents due to its 
hydrophobic property. Therefore, it is necessary to improve its dis­
persibility in water by interacting with a water-soluble polyelectrolyte, 
such as sodium carboxymethyl cellulose (Na-CMC) [20]. The carbox­
ymethyl group of CMC can be dissociated into a carboxylate anion 
(-CH2COO−) in an aqueous medium [21]. When CMC is adsorbed on the 
graphite through hydrophobic interactions, it creates a negative charge 
* Corresponding authors. 
E-mail addresses: cw97.cho@samsung.com (C.-W. Cho), upaik@hanyang.ac.kr (U. Paik), tssong@hanyang.ac.kr (T. Song).  
Contents lists available at ScienceDirect 
Electrochimica Acta 
journal homepage: www.journals.elsevier.com/electrochimica-acta 
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2023.142900 
Received 27 April 2023; Received in revised form 30 June 2023; Accepted 19 July 2023   

Electrochimica Acta 464 (2023) 142900
2
on the surface of the graphite, inducing an electrostatic repulsive force 
between the graphite particles [22]. As this repulsive force enhances 
dispersibility by preventing agglomeration of the graphite particles, it is 
crucial to increase the adsorption amount of CMC adsorbed on the 
graphite surface. 
On the other hand, another important factor affecting Li-ion kinetics 
is Li-ion transfer in the interphase between the active material and the 
electrolyte, which is mainly attributed to the desolvation of Li-ions and 
their conduction through the solid-electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer 
[23–25]. Accordingly, Li-ion kinetics in the electrode is influenced by 
the physicochemical properties of the SEI layer [26,27]. The SEI layer 
formed on the surface of the active material during the cell operation is 
determined not only by the electrolytes but also by a binder [28–30]. 
Lithium carboxymethyl cellulose (Li-CMC), a modified binder of com­
mercial Na-CMC, can effectively change the deposited ion from Na to Li 
on the surface of the active material, which improves Li-ion kinetics by 
promoting the formation of high Li-ion conducting SEI components 
during electrolyte decomposition [31,32]. 
Inspired by these, we report a kneading process of the CMC binder to 
improve Li-ion kinetics in the anode for LIBs with high energy density. 
When graphite and Na-CMC are mixed at once (hereafter referred to as 
“Na-CMC mixing”), the hydrophilic Na-CMC is partially adsorbed on the 
graphite surface due to its low wettability with the hydrophobic 
graphite, which makes the dispersibility of the anode slurry poor. To 
address this issue, we employed a kneading process of graphite and Na- 
CMC. The kneading process, in which half of the CMC is pre-mixed with 
graphite, enables the complete adsorption of the pre-mixed CMC on the 
graphite surface due to the large mixing force associated with high solid 
loading, which makes the graphite surface hydrophilic [33]. The hy­
drophilic graphite surface enhances wettability with Na-CMC during the 
subsequent mixing process (hereafter referred to as “Na-CMC knead­
ing”), which increases the adsorption amount of Na-CMC on the graphite 
surface and improves the dispersibility of the slurry [34]. The Na-CMC 
kneading anode exhibits better Li-ion kinetics and electrochemical 
properties compared to those of the Na-CMC mixing anode. To further 
improve Li-ion kinetics, the Na-CMC used in the kneading process is 
replaced with Li-CMC (hereafter referred to as “Li-CMC kneading”). The 
Li-CMC kneading process promotes the formation of the LiF-rich SEI 
layer during the operation of LIBs, which improves the Li-ion kinetics in 
the anode due to the low charge transfer activation energy (Ect). The 
Li-CMC kneading anode exhibited excellent cycling stability (92.3% 
capacity retention for 100 cycles at 2.0 C) compared to that of the 
Na-CMC mixing anode (78.9% capacity retention for 100 cycles at 2.0 
C). 
2. Experimental section 
2.1. Materials 
Sodium carboxymethyl cellulose (Na-CMC, Mw ~250,000, degree of 
substitution of 1.2), acetic acid (CH3COOH, >99.8%), sodium hydroxide 
(NaOH, >97%), nitric acid (HNO3, 70%), polyvinylidene fluoride 
(PVdF, 
Mw 
~180,000), 
and 
lithium 
hydroxide 
monohydrate 
(LiOH•H2O, >98%) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (USA). Lithium 
carboxymethyl cellulose (Li-CMC) was synthesized based on our previ­
ously reported paper [35]. Li- and Na-CMC was used after dissolving it in 
deionized water (DIW) at a weight ratio of 1/99 (Li- and Na-CMC/DIW). 
Graphite, LiCoO2 (LCO), 40 wt% styrene butadiene rubber (SBR) 
emulsion in DIW were provided by Samsung SDI Co., LTD (Korea). 
2.2. Graphite characterizations 
The morphology of graphite materials was observed by using a field- 
emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, JSM-7600F, JEOL, 
Japan). The graphite presented a plate shape with many protruding 
edges (Fig. S1(a)). The crystalline structure of graphite was analyzed 
with X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD, Miniflex 600, Rigaku, Japan). The 
diffraction patterns were typical graphite materials with a D-spacing of 
3.3697 Å at the (002) peak (Fig. S1(b)). Nitrogen gas adsorption- 
desorption isotherm was evaluated with a physisorption analyzer 
(3Flex, Micromeritics) at −196 ◦C. The specific surface area of graphite 
was measured by a Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) equation. The 
calculated surface area of graphite was 1.6792 m2 g −
1 (Fig. S2). 
2.3. Adsorption isotherms for na-cmc on graphite surface 
Adsorption isotherms for Na-CMC on graphite surface were investi­
gated at 25 ◦C via solution-depletion method with total organic carbon 
(TOC, TOC-VCPH, Shimadzu Corp., Japan) analyzer. The Na-CMC 
mixing was obtained by mixing graphite 10 g with a Na-CMC mass 
fraction of 0.5–1.5% compared to graphite using a thinky mixer at 2,000 
rpm for 6 min. The Na-CMC kneading was prepared by mixing graphite 
10 g with a Na-CMC mass fraction of 0.5% compared to graphite at 
2,000 rpm for 3 min and then mixing the additional Na-CMC at 2000 
rpm for 3 min again. The prepared samples were equilibrated under 
stirred conditions for 12 h. Then, each sample was centrifuged (Ultra­
centrifuge, Optima LE-80 K, BECKMAN Coulter, USA) for 30 min at 
25,000 rpm. The obtained supernatant was analyzed with a TOC 
analyzer. Results were averaged and corrected for background TOC 
using a blank solution (native graphite suspension without Na-CMC 
additives). Adsorption isotherms were derived from the difference be­
tween the added amount and the remaining amount of Na-CMC in the 
supernatant. 
2.4. Slurry characterizations 
2.4.1. Electrokinetic behavior of graphite/Na-CMC mixture 
The preparation of graphite and Na-CMC mixture followed the same 
procedure previously described for measurements of adsorption iso­
therms. The prepared mixture was diluted at a volume ratio of 3/97 
(mixture/deionized water) through deionized water. The prepared 
samples were equilibrated using roller mixing for 6 h. Before measure­
ments, the pH values of samples were adjusted to 9.5 with 1 M NaOH 
solution. The electrokinetic behavior of the samples was measured using 
an electroacoustic analyzer (ESA, ESA-9800, Matec Applied Sciences, 
USA). Furthermore, 0.1 M HNO3 solution was injected into the samples 
to evaluate zeta potential depending on suspension pH. The homoge­
neous solution was made by agitating the solution with a propeller. Zeta 
potential of the samples was obtained with an equilibration time of 60 s. 
2.4.2. Particle size distribution of graphite/Na-CMC mixture 
The prepared Na-CMC mixing and kneading consisting of graphite 
and Na-CMC mass fraction of 1.0% compared to graphite were diluted at 
a volume ratio of 0.2/99.8 (mixture/deionized water) through deion­
ized water. The particle size distribution of the diluted mixture was 
analyzed using a single particle optical sizer (AccuSizer 780, ATI Korea, 
Korea) with a flow rate of 15 ml min−1. 
2.4.3. Rheological behavior of graphite/Na-CMC/SBR slurry 
The rheological behavior of the slurry was measured with a rota­
tional rheometer (MCR 501, Anton Paar, Germany) at 25 ◦C using a cone 
and plate geometry. For making a Na-CMC mixing slurry, graphite and 
1.0% aqueous Na-CMC solution were mixed at 2,000 rpm for 6 min using 
a thinky mixer. And then, 40% SBR emulsion was mixed with prepared 
graphite and Na-CMC mixture at 2,000 rpm for 2 min. The weight ratio 
of graphite, Na-CMC, and SBR are 97.8, 1.0, and 1.2 based on the solid 
state. The solid loading of the slurry was 49.3%. For preparing a Na-CMC 
kneading slurry, graphite and half of the total Na-CMC were firstly 
mixed at 2,000 rpm for 3 min, and then additional Na-CMC with the 
obtained mixture was secondly mixed at 2,000 rpm for 3 min. SBR 
emulsion was added and mixed at 2,000 rpm for 2 min. The mixing time 
of the slurry manufacturing process and the composition of the slurry are 
K. Park et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    

Electrochimica Acta 464 (2023) 142900
3
the same as the Na-CMC mixing slurry. The rheological behavior of the 
prepared slurry was measured at a temperature of 25±0.2 ◦C using a 
rheometer (MCR 501, Paar Physica, Stuttgart, Germany). The cone and 
plate system was utilized at the shear rate of 0.01–1,000 s−1 to evaluate 
the apparent viscosity of the prepared slurry. 
2.5. Electrode characterizations 
The preparation process of the Li-CMC kneading slurry is the same as 
the Na-CMC kneading slurry, except that half of the total Na-CMC, which 
was used in the first mixing of Na-CMC kneading, was changed to Li- 
CMC. The prepared slurry was cast on Cu current collector (thickness: 
8 μm) using a doctor blade with a loading level of 9.5 mg cm−2 and dried 
at 110 ◦C for 20 min. After the drying process, to achieve a target density 
of 1.6 g cc−1 at the electrode, the prepared electrode was calendered by a 
roll calendering machine. In the case of PVdF mixing, only a binder was 
changed from Na-CMC to PVdF in the same process as Na-CMC mixing. 
The pore size distribution of the electrode was analyzed by mercury 
intrusion porosimetry (Autopore V 9600, Micromeritics). The electrical 
conductivity of the electrode was calculated via the Van der Pauw 
method using a multimeter (Multimeter 2000/E, Keithley, USA). X-ray 
photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, K-Alpha+, Thermo Fisher Scientific 
Co., USA) was employed to observe a solid-electrolyte interphase (SEI) 
layer. All XPS spectra were collected by referencing the C 1 s peak to 
284.8 eV. All samples were sealed in an argon-filled glove box before 
XPS analysis. 
2.6. Electrochemical measurements 
All electrodes were dried in a vacuum oven at 120 ◦C for 4 h to 
fabricate the LIBs. Coin half-cells (2032 round type) were assembled 
using a prepared anode as a working electrode and lithium metal 
(thickness: 200 μm) as a counter electrode. The Galvanostatic Inter­
mittent Titration Technique (GITT) experiments were conducted on the 
half-cell during the discharge (lithiation) process at 0.1 C for Na-CMC 
and Li-CMC kneading. Coin full-cells were fabricated using LiCoO2 as 
a cathode with an areal capacity of 3 mAh cm−2 and prepared anode, 
where the N/P ratio was 1.1. The two-step formation cycles were con­
ducted on charging of constant current-constant voltage (CC–CV) and 
discharging of constant current (CC) modes in a voltage window of 
2.75–4.35 V at a current density of 0.1 and 0.2 C. After conducting 
formation cycles of 0.1 and 0.2 C, the cycling performance of the full-cell 
was evaluated under CC–CV charging and CC discharging modes within 
a voltage range of 2.75–4.35 V. 1.15 M LiPF6 in ethylene carbonate/ 
diethyl carbonate/dimethyl carbonate with fluoroethylene carbonate 
additive (EC/DEC/DMC: 3/5/2 vol% with 5 wt% FEC, PANAX StarLyte) 
was selected as an electrolyte. The polypropylene (PP) separator was 
used. All the electrochemical measurements were conducted by a bat­
tery cycle tester (TOSCAT 3000, Toyo System, Japan). Electrochemical 
impedance spectroscopy (EIS) spectra were observed at an open-circuit 
voltage (OCV) of each cell in a frequency window from 100 kHz to 1 Hz 
and 20 mV amplitude by using a potentiostat (ZIVE BP2A, Won-A tech, 
Korea). The ionic resistance (Rion) was obtained through EIS measure­
ment of the anode symmetric coin cell. 
3. Results and discussion 
Fig. 1(a) shows the adsorption amount of Na-CMC on the graphite 
surface for Na-CMC mixing and kneading, respectively. Na-CMC is fully 
adsorbed on the graphite surface up to 0.5 wt% and partially adsorbed 
on the graphite surface at a concentration over 0.5 wt% for both Na- 
CMC mixing and kneading. The unadsorbed Na-CMC remains in the 
slurry, which acts as a thickening agent [36]. The adsorption amount of 
Na-CMC on the graphite surface increase by adding more Na-CMC and 
reaches the saturation value of 0.452 mg m−2 at 1.0 wt% for Na-CMC 
mixing. In comparison, Na-CMC kneading exhibits a saturated 
adsorption amount of 0.551 mg m−2 at 0.9 wt%. The carboxylic groups 
of the Na-CMC can be ionized and present a negative charge in the 
aqueous medium of neutral pH [37]. The more Na-CMC is adsorbed on 
the graphite surface, the larger the absolute value of zeta potential [38]. 
Therefore, the different adsorption behavior of Na-CMC mixing and 
kneading affects the dispersibility of the graphite slurry [18,38]. Fig. S3 
exhibited the zeta potential of the graphite and Na-CMC mixture 
depending on suspension pH at various Na-CMC concentrations. Zeta 
potential exhibited negative values, and its absolute values increased at 
the high suspension pH due to the increment in the ionization of the 
carboxylic groups [20,39]. Fig. 1(b) presents the zeta potential of the 
Na-CMC mixing and kneading according to the Na-CMC concentration at 
around pH 7.5 in Fig. S3. The absolute value of zeta potential was 
enhanced with the Na-CMC concentration increase, and the maximum 
value was shown in 1.0 and 0.9 wt% Na-CMC for Na-CMC mixing and 
kneading, respectively. As the Na-CMC concentration increases, the 
adsorption amount of Na-CMC on the graphite surface increases, which 
results in a large zeta potential value. The maximum value was shown at 
the Na-CMC concentration, where the adsorption amount of Na-CMC on 
the graphite surface was saturated. Then, the additional Na-CMC re­
duces the zeta potential of the mixture due to the screening effect of 
unadsorbed Na-CMC [40,41]. The Na-CMC kneading presents a larger 
value of zeta potential compared to that of the Na-CMC mixing due to 
the enhanced adsorption amount of Na-CMC, which affects the graphite 
dispersion in the slurry [39]. Schematic illustrations of the 
manufacturing process for Na-CMC mixing and kneading are presented 
in Fig. 1(c). Na-CMC mixing exhibits a low adsorption amount of 
Na-CMC on the graphite surface due to the low wettability between the 
hydrophobic graphite and the hydrophilic Na-CMC and small mixing 
force caused by the low solid loading associated with a large amount of 
CMC solution [33]. This low adsorption amount of Na-CMC on the 
graphite surface makes the dispersibility of slurry poor with the partial 
agglomeration state of the graphite [42–44]. For the Na-CMC kneading, 
the added Na-CMC in the kneading process, which is half of the total 
Na-CMC, is completely adsorbed on the graphite surface due to the large 
mixing force between graphite and Na-CMC caused by the high solid 
loading according to small amount of CMC solution, which makes the 
graphite surface hydrophilic [33]. The hydrophilic graphite surface 
enables improved wettability with Na-CMC during the subsequent 
mixing process, which increases adsorption amount of Na-CMC on the 
graphite surface and improves the rheological behavior of the slurry [45, 
46]. 
To elucidate the dispersibility of the graphite and Na-CMC mixture, 
single particle optical sizing was performed for the Na-CMC mixing and 
kneading. The single-particle optical sizing method can analyze the 
particle size by injecting a slurry at a constant flow rate [47]. Na-CMC 
kneading exhibited a smaller number of large particles over 5 μm 
compared to that of the Na-CMC mixing due to the superior dis­
persibility of the mixture under the 1.0 wt% CMC (Fig. 2(a)). Similarly, 
Na-CMC kneading showed fewer large particles in the slurry compared 
to Na-CMC mixing in the cumulative particle size distribution (Fig. 2 
(b)). Fig. S4 shows the rheological behavior for the final anode slurry 
with SBR added to Na-CMC mixing and kneading. Na-CMC kneading 
slurry exhibited low apparent viscosity compared to that of the Na-CMC 
mixing slurry, which might be due to the improvement of the graphite 
dispersion in the slurry [48]. This rheological behavior is in good 
agreement with the above results. 
Electrode prepared by Na-CMC mixing and kneading slurry was 
analyzed to determine the effect of slurry dispersibility on electrode 
properties. Fig. 2(c) indicates the pore size distribution of the anode. The 
average pore size of anodes with Na-CMC mixing and kneading was 1.2 
μm, exhibiting similar values. Notably, the Na-CMC kneading anode had 
a large number of pores with a size of 1.2 μm showing a sharp peak, 
while the Na-CMC mixing anode showed a broad peak with relatively 
few pores of size 1.2 μm, and a lot of pores of smaller than 1 μm and 
larger than 8 μm. This is probably because the Na-CMC kneading 
K. Park et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    

Electrochimica Acta 464 (2023) 142900
4
Fig. 1. (a) Adsorption isotherm of the Na-CMC on the graphite surface depending on Na-CMC mixing and kneading process. (b) Electrokinetic behavior of the 
graphite and Na-CMC mixture according to CMC mass fractions of 0.6–1.1 wt%. (c) Schematic illustrations of the manufacturing process for Na-CMC mixing and 
kneading, respectively. 
K. Park et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    

Electrochimica Acta 464 (2023) 142900
5
exhibits uniform pore distribution due to the superior dispersibility of 
the slurry, but Na-CMC mixing with relatively poor dispersibility shows 
uneven pore structure after the electrode manufacturing process [18,49, 
50]. The electrical conductivity and ionic resistance (Rion) are intimately 
related to the pore distribution of the electrode, which plays an impor­
tant role in electrochemical performances [10,15]. As shown in Fig. 2 
(d), the Na-CMC kneading anode presents an electrical conductivity of 
57 S cm−1, which is 30% higher than that of the Na-CMC mixing anode 
(44 S cm−1). This result is ascribed to the enhancement of the graphite 
contact structure with superior dispersibility of Na-CMC kneading slurry 
[10]. For a clearly understand the transport of the electrolyte through 
the pores in the anode, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of 
the anode symmetric cells was analyzed. In Fig. S5, Na-CMC mixing and 
kneading anode exhibited Rion of 13.1 and 12.0 Ω, respectively. To 
analyze the transport of the electrolyte regardless of other variables, 
such as anode thickness and electrolyte conductivity, the MacMullin 
number (NM) was calculated as follows [51]; 
NM = Rion⋅A ⋅σ0
d
(1)  
here Rion is the ionic resistance (Ω), A is an electrode area (cm2), σo is the 
ionic conductivity of the liquid electrolyte (S cm−1), and d is the anode 
thickness (μm). The values needed for NM calculation of Na-CMC mixing 
and kneading anode are shown in Table S1. In Fig. 2(d), the Na-CMC 
kneading anode exhibited a reduced NM of 24.1 compared to that of 
the Na-CMC mixing anode of 26.2, which means that Li-ion kinetics 
could be improved with the Na-CMC kneading process by controlling the 
adsorption behavior of Na-CMC on the graphite surface. 
Electrochemical properties of the Na-CMC mixing and kneading 
anode were measured to verify the effect of enhanced CMC adsorption 
through the coin half-cell test. Fig. S6 showed initial charge/discharge 
voltage profiles of Na-CMC mixing and kneading anode at 0.1 C in a 
voltage window of 0.01–1.2 V vs. Li/Li+. The charging (delithiation) 
capacity of the Na-CMC kneading anode was 350 mAh g−1 with 
coulombic efficiency of 94.6%, which indicated improved property 
compared to the Na-CMC mixing anode showing 347 mAh g−1 with 
coulombic efficiency of 93.9%. EIS spectra of half-cells for Na-CMC 
mixing and kneading anode were observed after two formation cycles 
at 0.1 and 0.2 C (Fig. S7). The Na-CMC kneading anode exhibited a 
lower charge transfer resistance (Rct) compared to that of the Na-CMC 
mixing anode, which result agrees well with the electrode characteris­
tics of electrical conductivity and Rion. The Na-CMC mixing and 
kneading anode was prepared for the coin full-cell test using LiCoO2 
(LCO) with an areal capacity of 3 mAh cm−2 as a cathode. The N/P ratio 
was 1.1. Na-CMC kneading presented enhanced discharge capacity and 
coulombic efficiency than Na-CMC mixing in the initial voltage profile at 
0.1 C (Fig. S8). Fig. 3(a) shows the EIS spectra of the coin full-cells after 
the two formation cycles at 0.1 and 0.2 C. The values of solution resis­
tance (Rs), solid-electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer resistance (RSEI), and 
Rct for Na-CMC mixing and Na-CMC kneading were 1.23, 2.87, 6.37 Ω 
Fig. 2. Characterizations of graphite and Na-CMC mixture: (a) Particle size distribution. (b) Cumulative particle size distribution. Electrode properties of Na-CMC 
mixing and kneading process after the calendering: (c) Pore size distribution. (d) Electrical conductivity and MacMullin number. 
K. Park et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    

Electrochimica Acta 464 (2023) 142900
6
and 0.95, 1.52, 3.61 Ω, respectively. The lower resistance values of Na- 
CMC kneading compared to Na-CMC mixing could be attributed to the 
homogeneous microstructure of the electrode associated with improved 
slurry dispersibility [52]. Fig. 3(b) indicates the voltage profiles of the 
full-cells depending on 1st and 100th cycles at the current density of 2.0 
C. The cells of Na-CMC mixing and kneading deliver the charge/di­
scharge capacities of 153/142 and 164/153 mAh g−1 in the 1st cycle, 
113/112 and 128/127 mAh g−1 in the 100th cycle, respectively. The 
Na-CMC kneading cell exhibits low polarization during charging and 
discharging at 2.0 C. The charge/discharge cycling performances of the 
full-cells were carefully monitored for 100 cycles at 2.0 C (Fig. 3(c)). The 
Na-CMC kneading cell also exhibits enhanced cycling performance 
(87.76%) compared to the Na-CMC mixing cell (78.92%). The cycling 
performance of the electrode fabricated with polyvinylidene fluoride 
(PVdF) was compared to Na-CMC mixing and kneading (Fig. S9). 
Na-CMC mixing showed low capacity retention after 100 cycles 
compared to that of the PVdF mixing. On the other hand, Na-CMC 
kneading presented enhanced capacity retention at 100 cycles 
compared to PVdF mixing, which is attributed to the improved dis­
persibility of the slurry associated with increased binder adsorption 
caused by kneading process. The enhanced electrical conductivity and 
Rion in the Na-CMC kneading anode enable the improvement in cycle 
retention. To elucidate the quick-charging properties, the full-cell was 
evaluated up to a charging rate of 3.0 C, fixing the discharging rate at 0.5 
C, as shown in Fig. S10. The charging/discharging test was conducted in 
the constant current (CC) condition. The Na-CMC mixing cells showed 
charging capacity retention of 88.22, 58.67, and 38.10%, respectively, 
at 1.0, 2.0, and 3.0 C charging rates (Fig. 3(d)). For the Na-CMC 
kneading cells, the charging capacity retentions were 91.06, 64.57, 
and 42.05%, respectively, at 1.0, 2.0, and 3.0 C charging rates. The 
above results of electrochemical properties demonstrate that the polar­
ization, cycle stability, and charging rate capabilities could be improved 
by employing the Na-CMC kneading process. 
As shown in the adsorption isotherm results (Fig. 1(a)), the CMC of 
0.5% mass fraction is completely adsorbed on the graphite surface. 
Binder adsorbed on the surface of the active material could affect the Li- 
ion transfer between the active material and the electrolyte by modi­
fying the SEI layer [29,53]. Compared to Na-CMC, as Li-CMC could 
improve battery performances through the effective formation of the SEI 
layer, the CMC initially adsorbed on the graphite surface in the kneading 
process was changed from Na-CMC to Li-CMC [31,32]. 
The charge transfer activation energy (Ect) is a useful indicator for 
evaluating Li-ion kinetics [23,53]. Ect is mainly attributed to Li-ion 
desolvation and conducting through the SEI layer because the SEI 
layer works as a charge transfer barrier of Li-ion in LIBs [24]. Ect was 
calculated through the Arrhenius equation as follows [54]; 
∂(1/Rct)
∂(1/T) = −
Ect
2.303R
(2) 
Where Rct is charge transfer resistance (Ω), T is the temperature in 
Fig. 3. Electrochemical performances of the full-cells consisting of an LCO cathode and anodes prepared with Na-CMC mixing and Na-CMC kneading process, 
respectively: (a) Nyquist plots after the formation cycles. (b) Charge-discharge voltage profiles of 1st and 100th cycle at a current density of 2.0 C. (c) Cycling 
performance over 100 cycles at 2.0 C. (d) Constant current charging capacity up to 3.0 C. 
K. Park et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    

Electrochimica Acta 464 (2023) 142900
7
Kelvin (K), Ect is charge transfer activation energy (kJ mol−1), and R is 
the gas constant (8.314 J mol−1 K−1), respectively. The EIS spectra of 
Na-CMC and Li-CMC kneading anode were measured depending on 
temperatures from −10 ◦C to 45 ◦C for calculation of Ect (Fig. S11). As 
shown in Fig. 4(a), Li-CMC kneading anode had a Ect of 52.01 kJ mol−1, 
which was a 17% lower value compared to that of the Na-CMC kneading 
anode (62.70 kJ mol−1). The low Ect reveals the improvement of Li-ion 
kinetics in the Li-CMC kneading anode [23,53]. Fig. 4(b) shows the EIS 
spectra of the anode half-cells for Na-CMC and Li-CMC kneading anode 
after the formation cycles at 25 ◦C. The values of Rs, RSEI, and Rct of 
Fig. 4. (a) Arrhenius plots (-lnRCT (Ω−1) vs 1000/T (K−1)) for calculating charge transfer activation energy of Na-CMC and Li-CMC kneading process. (b) Nyquist 
plots of the anode half-cells after the formation cycles at 25 ◦C. High-resolution XPS spectra of anode with Na-CMC and Li-CMC kneading process after 10 cycles at 1.0 
C: (c-d) Li 1 s and (e-f) F 1 s peaks. 
K. Park et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    

Electrochimica Acta 464 (2023) 142900
8
Na-CMC kneading and Li-CMC kneading were 2.35, 7, 7.57 Ω and 2.1, 
3.07, 3.22 Ω, respectively. The Li-CMC kneading anode exhibited lower 
RSEI and Rct values than the Na-CMC kneading anode, resulting from the 
enhanced Li-ion kinetics. SEI layer has a significant effect on Li-ion ki­
netics and corresponding overall electrochemical properties of cycle 
stability and rate capability in LIBs [23,26,27]. To investigate chemical 
configurations of the SEI layer, high-resolution X-ray photoelectron 
spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was conducted after 10 cycles at 1.0 C in 
coin half-cells for Na-CMC and Li-CMC kneading anode. In the Na-CMC 
kneading anode, the Li 1 s peak can be deconvoluted into LiF, Li2CO3, 
and ROCO2Li at 56.2, 55.2, and 54.7 eV, and showed the ratios of 0.21, 
0.51, and 0.28, respectively (Fig. 4(c)) [55,56]. The Li 1 s peak of the 
Li-CMC kneading anode can also be deconvoluted into LiF, Li2CO3, and 
ROCO2Li, exhibiting the ratios of 0.31, 0.40, and 0.29, respectively 
(Fig. 4(d)). LiF is widely known as an inorganic SEI layer with low Ect 
and high Li-ion kinetics [26,57]. Therefore, the superior Li-ion kinetics 
of the Li-CMC kneading anode with low Ect might be due to the high LiF 
ratio compared to the Na-CMC kneading anode. For the F 1 s spectra, 
LixPFy/LixPOyFz and LiF peaks were found at 687.0 and 684.6 eV, 
respectively, in the Na-CMC kneading anode (Fig. 4(e)) [55,58]. The 
Li-CMC kneading anode in F 1 s exhibited a LiF-richer SEI layer 
compared to that of the Na-CMC kneading anode, suggesting the cor­
responding result with Li 1 s XPS spectra (Fig. 4(f)). The C 1 s and O 1 s 
spectra of Na-CMC and Li-CMC kneading anode revealed the typical 
composition of graphite and SEI layer (Fig. S12–13) [55,59]. Fig. S14 
shows the Rion and corresponding NM. The parameters for the NM 
calculation of Na-CMC and Li-CMC kneading anode are shown in 
Table S2. The Li-CMC kneading anode exhibited reduced NM compared 
to that of the Na-CMC kneading anode. To determine the diffusion co­
efficient of Li-ion (DLi+) for Na-CMC kneading and Li-CMC kneading, 
Galvanostatic Intermittent Titration Technique (GITT) experiments 
were conducted on the half-cell during the discharge (lithiation) process 
at 0.1 C (Fig. S15). The diffusion coefficient of Li-ion can be calculated 
using the following equation [60]; 
DLi+ = 4
πτ
(mBVm
MBA
)2(ΔEs
ΔEt
)2
(3)  
here, τ represents the duration of the constant current pulse (s), mB is the 
weight of the active material (g), Vm is the molar volume of the active 
material (cm3 mol−1), MB is the molecular weight of the active material 
(g mol−1), A is the contact area between the electrode-electrolyte 
interface (cm2), ΔEs is the change in potential during the steady-state 
of the single-step GITT test (V), and ΔEt is the total change in poten­
tials during the constant current pulse duration of the sing-step GITT test 
(V). As shown in Fig. S16, Li-CMC kneading exhibited a higher DLi+ at all 
potentials during lithiation process compared to Na-CMC kneading. 
These results prove that the Li-CMC kneading process could generate a 
Fig. 5. Electrochemical performances of the full-cells consisting of an LCO cathode and anodes prepared with Na-CMC and Li-CMC kneading process, respectively: 
(a) Nyquist plots after the formation cycles. (b) Charge-discharge voltage profiles of 1st and 100th cycle at a current density of 2.0 C. (c) Cycling performance over 
100 cycles at 2.0 C. (d) Constant current charging capacity up to 3.0 C. 
K. Park et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    

Electrochimica Acta 464 (2023) 142900
9
LiF-rich SEI layer, which enhances Li-ion kinetics with reduced Ect. 
To evaluate the effect of enhanced Li-ion kinetics with Li-CMC 
kneading, coin full-cells were prepared using LCO as a cathode with 
an areal capacity of 3.0 mAh cm−2. The N/P ratio was 1.1. Fig. S17 
shows the initial charge/discharge voltage profiles of the full-cells 
consisting of an LCO cathode and anodes with Na-CMC and Li-CMC 
kneading at 0.1 C. The cell prepared with the Li-CMC kneading anode 
showed a high discharge capacity of 157 mAh g−1 and coulombic effi­
ciency of 89.4% compared to those of the Na-CMC kneading anode. 
After the 0.1 and 0.2 C formation cycles, the EIS was conducted to 
evaluate the Rct of Na-CMC and Li-CMC kneading cells. In Fig. 5(a), the 
Rct of the Li-CMC kneading cell was 3.3 Ω showing a 33% lower value 
than that of the Na-CMC kneading cell (4.4 Ω). Due to the Rct 
improvement, the Li-CMC kneading cell showed a lower polarization 
than the Na-CMC kneading cell during the 1st charge/discharge at a 
current density of 2.0 C, and the polarization difference between the two 
cells was more extensive at the 100th cycle (Fig. 5(b)). In the cycle 
performance at 2.0 C for 100 cycles, the Li-CMC kneading showed cycle 
retention of 92.34%, indicating further improvement compared to Na- 
CMC kneading of 87.76% (Fig. 5(c)). The quick-charging test of the 
full-cells was conducted up to a 3.0 C charging rate in the CC condition, 
maintaining the discharge rate at 0.5 C (Fig. S18). The Li-CMC kneading 
exhibited higher charging capacity retentions up to 3.0 C than the Na- 
CMC kneading (Fig. 5(d)). The electrochemical properties, including 
voltage profiles, cycle retention and charging rate capability clearly 
indicate that the improved Li-ion kinetics by introducing Li-CMC 
kneading play an essential role in the enhancement of the perfor­
mances of LIBs. 
4. Conclusion 
A kneading process of CMC binder was investigated to enhance Li- 
ion kinetics in the anode. In the kneading process, the pre-mixed Na- 
CMC, corresponding to half of the total Na-CMC, was fully adsorbed on 
the graphite surface, which makes graphite surface hydrophilic. The 
hydrophilic graphite surface improved wettability with Na-CMC during 
the subsequent mixing process, which enhanced the adsorption amount 
of the Na-CMC on the graphite surface and corresponding dispersibility 
of the slurry. The anode prepared with Na-CMC kneading showed 
increased Li-ion kinetics due to the improved dispersibility of the slurry. 
To further enhance Li-ion kinetics, the Na-CMC used in the kneading 
process was replaced by Li-CMC. The Li-CMC kneading enables the facile 
formation of high Li-ion conducting LiF-rich SEI layer to lower the Ect 
and increases the Li-ion kinetics, which results in the improvement of 
the electrochemical properties in the high energy density LIBs.

[SOURCE: In Situ Cross-linked Carboxymethyl Cellulose-Polyethylene Glycol.pdf]

In Situ Cross-linked Carboxymethyl Cellulose-Polyethylene Glycol
Binder for Improving the Long-Term Cycle Life of Silicon Anodes in
Li Ion Batteries
Dongsoo Lee,† Hyunjung Park,†,‡ Alan Goliaszewski,§ Yun-ki Byeun,∥Taeseup Song,*,†
and Ungyu Paik†
†Department of Energy Engineering, Hanyang University, Seoul 133-791, Korea
‡School of Chemical and Biomedical Engineering, Nanyang Technological University, 62 Nanyang Drive, Singapore 637459,
Singapore
§Ashland Specialty Ingredients, 500 Hercules Road, Wilmington, Delaware 19808, United States
∥Steelmaking Research Group, Technical Research Laboratory of POSCO, Pohang, Gyeongbuk 37859, Korea
*
S Supporting Information
ABSTRACT: To increase the energy density of Li-ion batteries
(LIBs), silicon has been widely studied due to its relative abundance
and high theoretical speciﬁc capacity (∼3572 mAh g−1). However,
silicon experiences drastic volume changes up to 300% associated
with Li. Here, we report an in situ cross-linked carboxymethyl
cellulose-polyethylene glycol (CMC-PEG) binder and its applica-
tion to the silicon anode to improve cycle life. Through in situ cross-
linking during the electrode drying process, the cross-linked CMC-
PEG binder is simply prepared without an additional process. In
particular, the cross-linked CMC-PEG binder is eﬀective in
enhancing cohesion between active materials and adhesion between
active materials and a current collector. The silicon anode with the
cross-linked CMC-PEG binder shows stable cycling performance
with a capacity of ∼2000 mAh g−1 up to 350 cycles at 0.5 C.
In terms of simplicity, this binder has potential to be used for silicon anodes and other electrodes experiencing volume
expansion during cycling.
■INTRODUCTION
Lithium ion batteries (LIBs) are used in most electronic
devices today; however, there are still concerns about cost,
energy density, cycle life, and safety issues, among which the
development of LIBs with a high energy density and long cycle
life is important for practical applications.1−4 Among the
possible candidates, silicon has been widely studied due to its
abundance and high theoretical speciﬁc capacity (∼3572 mAhg−1)
with a relatively low working voltage (∼0.5 V vs Li/Li+).5,6
However, silicon experiences drastic volume changes up to 300%
during continuous charging and discharging processes, which leads
to the loss of electrical contact between active materials and
the continuous formation of an SEI (solid-electrolyte interphase)
layer.7,8 Because of the inherent disadvantages with silicon
anodes, many researchers have introduced several concepts to
prevent pulverization of the active materials during cycling to
improve cycle life and lithium ion conductivity of the silicon
anodes.9 Nanoporous materials, nanowires, and nanotubes are
eﬀective in improving cycle retention by reducing the stress
of silicon materials attributed to the volume changes during
charging and discharging.10−13 Another approach is to modify
the polymer binders, which could provide reversible strong
bonds with the silicon materials through higher binding strength
and enhance the lithium ion conductivity with conducting
functional groups.14,15 However, in terms of the cost and cycle
life, there remain limitations to their practical use.
Binder is one of the major electrode components, despite
low content in the electrode, which has a great eﬀect on the
mechanical and electrochemical properties of LIBs.16 To improve
the cycle performance of the silicon anode, binders should
have robust bonding with active materials and current collectors,
which is related to the cohesion and adhesion properties of the
electrode.17 Conducting functional groups are also important for
stable cycling behavior in the silicon anode.18 In this respect,
many studies on robust bonding and cross-linking concepts
of polymeric binders have been reported.19 Among various
candidates, carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) has been widely
Received:
February 14, 2019
Revised:
April 22, 2019
Accepted:
April 24, 2019
Published: April 24, 2019
Article
pubs.acs.org/IECR
Cite This: Ind. Eng. Chem. Res. 2019, 58, 8123−8130
© 2019 American Chemical Society
8123
DOI: 10.1021/acs.iecr.9b00870
Ind. Eng. Chem. Res. 2019, 58, 8123−8130
Downloaded via GACHON UNIV on January 28, 2026 at 09:42:53 (UTC).
See https://pubs.acs.org/sharingguidelines for options on how to legitimately share published articles.

used in practice for anodes up to the present because of its
environmental friendliness, good adsorption property with
active materials, excellent dispersion properties in a slurry state
with carboxyl functional groups (COO−) in water, and low
cost. However, CMC has limitations in terms of the mechan-
ical property to be applied to silicon anodes. Nevertheless, with
an appropriate cross-linking agent, CMC could improve
mechanical strength to withstand the volume expansion of
silicon. In this respect, we studied the cross-linking binder of
CMC with polyethylene glycol (PEG). PEG is an environ-
mentally friendly synthetic polymer that is conducting, amphi-
philic, and water-soluble. Therefore, a novel cross-linked binder
from CMC with polyethylene glycol diglycidyl ether (PEGDE)
as a cross-linking agent could be used as an eﬀective binder to
improve the mechanical and electrochemical properties of the
electrodes.20,21 Especially, a small amount of PEGDE could
provide a large eﬀect on a variety of properties in the silicon
anode.
Here, we report an in situ cross-linked CMC-PEG binder
and its application to the silicon anode for the long-term cycle
performance in LIBs. The cross-linked CMC-PEG binder is
simply prepared through the cross-linking reaction between
CMC and PEGDE under the electrode drying process of the
coated slurry onto the current collector without an additional
process. The cross-linked CMC-PEG binder is eﬀective in
enhancing the cohesion between active materials and adhesion
between active materials and a current collector with multi-
dimensional bonding sites, inducing hydrogen bonds with the
silicon. With the cross-linked CMC-PEG binder, the electrode
peeling strength, including adhesion and cohesion, was quite
enhanced to ∼2 gf/mm. Furthermore, the silicon anode with
the cross-linked CMC-PEG binder exhibited stable cycling
performance, showing a capacity of ∼2000 mAh g−1 with high
Coulombic eﬃciencies up to 350 cycles at 0.5 C. This in situ
cross-linked CMC-PEG binder is prepared by a facile approach
and has a potential to be applied to the industry level of LIBs
with its simplicity.
■EXPERIMENTAL METHODS
Preparation of Silicon Electrodes. Silicon powder
(APS ≤50 nm, laser synthesized from the vapor phase) was
purchased from Alfa Aesar. Sodium CMC (Mw ∼250 000,
DS = 1.2) and PEGDE (Mw ∼526) were purchased from
Sigma-Aldrich. For slurry preparation of the reference elec-
trode with CMC, silicon powder, conducting agent (Ketjen black),
CMC, and SBR were placed into deionized water in a weight ratio
of 85:5:8:2. For slurry preparation of the electrode prepared with
the cross-linked CMC-PEG binder, silicon powder, conducting
agent (Ketjen black), CMC, PEGDE, and SBR were placed
into deionized water in a weight ratio of 85:5:6.4:1.6:2. The
slurry was mixed at 2000 rpm for 5 min by a Thinky mixer.
After 30 min of being aged, the prepared slurry was coated
onto a copper current collector and then dried at 110 °C for
10 min. Afterward, the prepared electrodes were dried in a
vacuum oven at 120 °C for 4 h. The areal mass loading of
silicon in the electrode was 0.4 mg cm−2.
Material Characterization. To investigate the cross-
linking reaction between CMC and PEGDE, Fourier-transform
infrared spectrometry (FTIR, Nicolet 5700, ThermoElectron,
US) was used. For the analysis, two diﬀerent binder ﬁlms were
prepared with 2 wt % binder solutions in water after drying at
25 °C, (1) CMC binder ﬁlm and (2) cross-linked CMC-PEG
binder ﬁlms. Prior to analysis, the prepared binder ﬁlms were
dried in a vacuum oven at 120 °C for 4 h. The mechanical
properties of CMC and cross-linked CMC-PEG binder ﬁlms
were measured with a universal testing machine, UTM (AGS-J,
Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan), and the specimens were prepared
using an ASTM D638-Type 5 cutter. Samples were elongated
to obtain a stress−strain curve within a standard deviation of
less than ±10%. To identify the adhesion and cohesion strength
of the silicon electrodes, the 180° peeling test was conducted
using the UTM. For nanoindentation analysis, a nanoindenter
Figure 1. FT-IR spectra of CMC and CMC-PEG binder ﬁlms in the
range of (a) 500−4000, (b) 2800−2950, and (c) 1300−1450 cm−1.
Industrial & Engineering Chemistry Research
Article
DOI: 10.1021/acs.iecr.9b00870
Ind. Eng. Chem. Res. 2019, 58, 8123−8130
8124

(Nano Instruments, MTS Systems Corp., Eden Prairie, USA)
with a Berkovich tip (radius of tip <100 nm) was used. To mini-
mize data deviation, the indentation position was changed, and
the test was conducted at least ﬁve times. For analysis of the
electrodes before and after cycle testing, ﬁeld-emission scanning
electron microscopy (FE-SEM, JEOL JSM07600F) was used.
X-ray photoelectron spectrometry (XPS, VG microtech
ESCA2000) was used for analysis of the binding eﬀect between
the cross-linked CMC-PEG binder and silicon and of electrode
stability after cycling.
Evaluation of Electrochemical Performances. Before
evaluation, all electrodes and binder ﬁlms were dried at 120 °C
for 4 h in a vacuum oven, during which CMC and PEGDE
were cross-linked by heat. For the half-cell test, 2032 round-
type coin cells were fabricated using the silicon electrode as a
working electrode, lithium foil with a thickness of ∼0.2 μm as a
counter electrode, 1.3 M LiPF6 in ethylene carbonate/diethyl
carbonate (EC/DEC (3/7 vol %) with 5 wt % FEC, PANAX
StarLyte) as electrolyte, and a polypropylene (PP) separator.
All of the coin half cells were tested using a battery cycle tester
(TOSCAT 3000, Toyo System, Tokyo, Japan) in the voltage
window of 0.01−1.5 V vs Li/Li+. EIS was performed at an
overpotential of 2.85 V with a frequency from 1 to 100 000 Hz
and an amplitude of 2 mV using a potentiostat (Autolab,
PGSTAT-72637) before the cell test.
■RESULTS AND DISCUSSION
The carboxylate group of CMC and epoxide group of PEGDE
can have a cross-linking reaction under alkaline conditions or
upon heating. A cross-linked CMC-PEG binder from CMC
with PEGDE polymers was conﬁrmed using FTIR analysis, as
shown in Figure 1. These spectra show normal adsorption
bands at 3323, 2916, 2875, 1417, 1323, and 893 cm−1, which
are assigned to CH2 stretching, CH2 scissoring bending, CH
bending, and vibrations of the α(1→4) glucopyranose ring,
respectively.20 The intensities of the bands at 2916, 2875, 1417,
and 1323 cm−1 of cross-linked CMC-PEG were noticeably
increased compared to those of CMC, which indicated that
CMC had a cross-linking reaction with PEGDE.22 This result
substantiates that the CMC and PEGDE in a slurry state became
in situ cross-linked CMC-PEG under the drying conditions
above 120 °C, which is described in Scheme 1. Generally, cross-
linked polymers have poor solubilities in solvents, so it is diﬃcult
to use in practice.23 However, this in situ CMC-PEG binder
would have advantages in processability, such as a slurry stability
and electrode preparation, because the cross-linking reaction
takes place under the electrode drying process. Furthermore,
the abundant functional groups containing carboxyl, ether, and
hydroxyl groups of the cross-linked CMC-PEG binder can
provide multidimensional bonding sites, inducing hydrogen
bonds with the surface of the silicon, which is one of the most
pivotal factors for improving the mechanical and electro-
chemical properties of the silicon anode.19,24 The conductive
functional groups of the PEG can also help improve the lithium
ion conductivity in the silicon anode.25,26 We expect that the
above positive eﬀects of the cross-linked CMC-PEG binder can
help to make the silicon anode more mechanically and elec-
trochemically stable during cycling.
To evaluate the mechanical property of the binder ﬁlms
prepared with CMC and cross-linked CMC-PEG binders,
UTM has been employed. Figure 2a shows that the mechanical
property of the cross-linked CMC-PEG binder ﬁlm was
enhanced after cross-linking. The stress and strain of the
binder ﬁlm prepared with the cross-linked CMC-PEG binder
persisted until breakdown and increased from 15.8 MPa, 4.6%,
to 32.2 MPa, 6.1%, which led to improved electrode integrity
and enhanced electrochemical properties, including Coulombic
eﬃciency and a cycling performance. Considering the mechan-
ical strength of the binder and the slurry stability (Figure S1),
we optimized the ratio of CMC to PEG to 8:2 for the further
Scheme 1. Polymer Structures of (a) CMC and PEGDE and (b) Cross-linked CMC-PEG Binder and (c) Schematic Illustration
of in Situ Cross-linking in the Silicon Electrode during the Drying Process

Ind. Eng. Chem. Res. 2019, 58, 8123−8130
8125

evaluation. The PEGDE improved mechanical properties of
the binder ﬁlms despite a small amount of 25% compared to
the CMC content.27,28 Figure S2 shows the nano-indentation
test result of a 2 mN loading using a Berkovich tip. The cross-
linked CMC-PEG binder shows improved elastic recovery
compared to that of the CMC binder. The enhanced elastic
recovery of the cross-linked CMC-PEG binder would increase
the mechanical stability of the silicon anode upon continuous
volume changes during cycling. Figure S3 shows the swelling
ratio of the binder ﬁlms after soaking in electrolyte for 24 h.
The swelling ratio of the cross-linked CMC-PEG binder is
reduced to the 2.5% compared to that of the CMC binder with
3.2%, which demonstrates that the cross-linked CMC-PEG
binder has high stability in the electrolyte soaking state. Figure 2b
reveals the electrode peeling strength, including cohesion
between the active materials and adhesion between the active
materials and copper current collector through the conven-
tional 180° peeling test by UTM. The cohesion and adhesion
strengths are extremely important parameters for reducing the
stress caused by the volume expansion of the silicon anode
during cycling. The peeling strength of the electrode prepared
with cross-linked CMC-PEG binder was quite enhanced
Figure 2. (a) Stress−strain behavior of CMC and CMC-PEG binder ﬁlms measured by UTM. (b) Adhesion and cohesion strength of silicon
electrodes from the 180° peeling test through UTM.
Figure 3. (a) Initial charge−discharge proﬁles of silicon electrodes with CMC binder and CMC-PEG binder at a 0.1 C rate between 0.01 and 1.5 V
versus Li/Li+ (b) Cycling performance of the silicon electrodes at a 0.2 C rate. (c) Charge−discharge proﬁles of a silicon electrode with CMC-PEG
binder during cycling. (d) Cycling performance of the silicon electrodes at a 0.5 C rate.
Industrial & Engineering Chemistry Research
Article
DOI: 10.1021/acs.iecr.9b00870
Ind. Eng. Chem. Res. 2019, 58, 8123−8130
8126

to ∼2 gf/mm compared to that of the electrode prepared with
CMC binder (∼1.1 gf/mm), which reveals that the in situ
cross-linked CMC-PEG binder eﬀectively increased the
adhesion and cohesion strength of the electrode.19,20,29 The
optical images after the electrode peeling test exhibited the
clear diﬀerences between the electrodes, as shown in Figure S4.
These results provide strong evidence that the in situ cross-
linked CMC-PEG binder imparts the silicon electrodes with
higher mechanical stability with multidimensional functional
groups compared to the silicon anode prepared with CMC
binder.30−33
Silicon electrodes were evaluated to demonstrate the
beneﬁcial eﬀect of the cross-linked CMC-PEG binder on the
electrochemical properties through the coin half-cell test.
In this study, we used 50 nm silicon nanoparticles, which had
high purity with a native oxide layer of ∼2 nm (Figure S5).
Figure 3a shows charge−discharge proﬁles of silicon anodes
with CMC and cross-linked CMC-PEG binders at ﬁrst cycle
(0.1 C, 357.2 mA g−1) in the voltage window of 0.01−1.5 V vs
Li/Li+. The charge (delithiation)/discharge (lithiation)
capacity and Coulombic eﬃciency at the ﬁrst cycle were
2860.4/3604.5 mAhg−1, 79%, and 3089/3816.6 mAhg−1, 81%,
for the electrodes prepared with CMC and CMC-PEG binders,
respectively, which demonstrates that the electrode prepared
with the cross-linked CMC-PEG binder had higher initial
charge/discharge capacity and Coulombic eﬃciency compared
with the electrode prepared with CMC binder. Figure 3b
exhibits the cycle performance up to 300 cycles at 0.2 C. The
electrode prepared with CMC binder showed continuous
capacity fading, and after 37 cycles, the capacities of the CMC
electrode were below 1000 mAhg−1 due to the continuous SEI
layer formation and the loss of electrical contact of the active
materials by volume changes of the silicon.34 Conversely, the
electrode prepared with the cross-linked CMC-PEG binder
exhibited highly improved cycle retention up to 300 cycles
with capacities of 2095.6, 1928.6, and 1835 mAhg−1 at 100,
200, and 300 cycles, respectively. The capacity ﬂuctuation in
the initial cycles was due to the volume changes of Si asso-
ciated with Li, which was stabilized in further cycling. The
improved physicochemical properties of adhesion and cohesion
strength with robust bonding between the binder and silicon
surface in the electrode prepared with the cross-linked CMC-
PEG binder reduce the stress and prevent the delamination of
active materials from the electrode caused by volume changes
during repeated charging and discharging.35 Figure 3c shows
voltage proﬁles of the electrode prepared with the cross-linked
CMC-PEG binder during cycling at 0.5 C. The electrode
shows nearly 2000 mAhg−1 up to 200 cycles and steadily stabi-
lized polarization curves during cycling, which demonstrates
that the cross-linked CMC-PEG binder provides the silicon
electrode with the enhanced electrochemical stability with
improved lithium ion conductivity and low overpotentials.
Figure 3d veriﬁes the improved cycle stability of the silicon
electrode prepared with the cross-linked CMC-PEG binder at
0.5 C, showing stable capacities up to 350 cycles. The capacity
retention of the electrode prepared with the cross-linked
CMC-PEG binder is ∼78% from the third cycle to the 350
cycles. By contrast, the electrode prepared with CMC shows
abrupt capacity fading at 0.5 C because of poor mechanical and
electrochemical stability of the electrode. These results derive
from the enhanced physicochemical properties of the cross-
linked CMC-PEG binder.36 Figure 4a shows the rate capability
of the electrodes prepared with CMC and cross-linked
CMC-PEG binders. The electrode with CMC showed a very
poor rate capability. However, the electrode prepared with the
cross-linked CMC-PEG binder exhibited higher capacities and
more stable performances. After the 4C rate test, the electrode
prepared with the cross-linked CMC-PEG binder shows restored
capacities, which substantiates the electrochemical reversibility of
the electrode with the CMC-PEG binder within diﬀerent C-rate
conditions. Figure 4b shows the EIS spectra of the electrodes
Figure 4. (a) Rate capability test for the silicon electrodes at a
0.2−4.0 C rate. (b) Nyquist plot of CMC and CMC-PEG electrodes
before cycling measured by potentiostat.
Figure 5. SEM images of the (a) pristine CMC electrode, (b) pristine
CMC-PEG electrode, (c) CMC electrode, and (d) CMC-PEG
electrode after discharging for 30 cycles and optical images of the Li
metal.
Industrial & Engineering Chemistry Research
Article
DOI: 10.1021/acs.iecr.9b00870
Ind. Eng. Chem. Res. 2019, 58, 8123−8130
8127

prepared with CMC and cross-linked CMC-PEG binders
before cycling, which indicates that the charge transfer resis-
tance (Rct) of the electrode prepared with the cross-linked
CMC-PEG binder was much smaller than that of the electrode
prepared with CMC. The electrical conductivity of the elec-
trode prepared with the cross-linker CMC-PEG binder was
also smaller than that of the electrode prepared with CMC
(Figure S6).
In addition to improved electrochemical performance,
Figure 5 shows that the electrode prepared with the cross-
linked CMC-PEG binder has a high stability during cycling.
After 30 cycles, the CMC electrode had a collapsed mor-
phology due to the irreversible reactions, such as the continuous
SEI layer formation and pulverization of the active materials
caused by volume changes of the silicon, while the CMC-PEG
electrode had the similar morphology with its initial state; an
increased particle size may be due to SEI layer formation and
inner pore generation at the initial cycle.37,38 Optical images of
the lithium metal provide clear evidence of irreversible reac-
tions, where the color of the lithium metal in the electrode
with CMC binder changed completely to black but not in the
electrode with cross-linked CMC-PEG binder. Figure 6 sub-
stantiates the stable SEI formation of the electrode prepared
with the cross-linked CMC-PEG binder before cycle, after ﬁrst
cycle, and after 30 cycles through XPS analysis. Before analysis,
the electrodes were washed thoroughly with acetonitrile at
least two times to eliminate residual electrolyte, so there was
no LIPF6 peak in the F 1s spectrum.39−41 The Si 2p peaks
disappeared, and an LIF peak emerged from electrolyte decom-
position after 1 cycle, i.e., formation of the stable SEI layer on
the silicon surface. The O 1s peak shift from 532.4 to 531.5 eV
indicates that functional groups of CMC-PEG binder produce
a stable SEI layer. The formation of a stable SEI layer is very
important for maintaining a reversible capacity and Coulombic
eﬃciency in the silicon anode.
■CONCLUSIONS
In summary, an in situ cross-linked CMC-PEG binder was
explored as an eﬀective binder for silicon anodes to overcome
capacity fading during cycling, resulting from the volume
changes of silicon. The pivotal eﬀects of using the cross-linked
CMC-PEG binder were conﬁrmed via physicochemical studies
on the binder and the electrode and the electrochemical prop-
erties. In particular, the improvement in mechanical properties
with the CMC-PEG binder demonstrates that the mechanical
stability of the binder can help to enhance electrochemical
stability during cycling, and the abundance of functional groups
of the binder can stabilize the electrode. According to the
electrochemical evaluations, the initial capacity and Coulombic
eﬃciency were slightly increased with the cross-linked CMC-
PEG binder, and the noteworthy improvement was observed in
the cycle performance. The silicon anode with the cross-linked
CMC-PEG binder exhibited a stable cycling behavior with
∼2000 mAhg−1 up to 350 cycles at 0.5 C. XPS analysis also
indicated stable SEI layer formation on the silicon surface. All
of these results substantiate that the mechanical and electro-
chemical properties were greatly improved by adding a small
amount of in situ cross-linker (PEGDE). The in situ cross-linked
CMC-PEG is a promising binder that could be applied to the
silicon anode to increase the energy density of the batteries.
Figure 6. High-resolution XPS spectra of the silicon electrode surface with the CMC-PEG binder for (a) C 1s, (b) F 1s, (c) C 1s, and (d) O 1s
peaks before cycle, after 1 cycle, and after 30 cycles between 0.01 and 1.5 V versus Li/Li+.

Carbon Energy
RESEARCH ARTICLE
Insights Into Improving the Li‐Ion Transference Number
and Li Deposition Uniformity Toward a
High‐Current‐Density Lithium Metal Anode
Subi Yang1,2 | Seungho Lee1,2 | Min Sung Kang3,4 | Kwang Chul Roh2
| Jihoon Seo5 | Dongsoo Lee6 | Kwanghyun Kim1,2 |
Sangkyu Lee7 | Sung Beom Cho3,4 | Patrick Joohyun Kim1 | Junghyun Choi6
1School of Chemical Engineering and Applied Chemistry, Kyungpook National University, Daehak‐ro 80, Buk‐gu, Daegu, Republic of Korea | 2Energy Storage
Materials Center, Korea Institute of Ceramic Engineering and Technology, Jinju‐si, Gyeongsangnam‐do, Republic of Korea | 3Department of Materials Science
and Engineering, Ajou University, Suwon, Gyeonggi‐do, Republic of Korea | 4Department of Energy Systems Research, Ajou University, Suwon, Gyeonggi‐do,
Republic of Korea | 5Department of Chemical & Biomolecular Engineering, Clarkson University, Potsdam, New York, United States | 6School of Chemical,
Biological and Battery Engineering, Gachon University, Seongnam‐si, Gyeonggi‐do, Republic of Korea | 7Department of Energy Science and Engineering,
Kunsan National University, Gunsan‐si, Jeonbuk‐do, Republic of Korea
Correspondence: Sung Beom Cho (csb@ajou.ac.kr) | Patrick Joohyun Kim (pjkim@knu.ac.kr) | Junghyun Choi (junghchoi@gachon.ac.kr)
Received: 20 January 2025 | Revised: 28 April 2025 | Accepted: 26 May 2025
Funding: This study was supported by Ministry of Science and ICT, South Korea (RS‐2024‐00407282) and National Research Foundation of Korea
(RS‐2024‐00408156).
Keywords: Li metal batteries | Li‐ion conductivity | separator modification | uniform Li deposition
ABSTRACT
The practical application of lithium (Li) metal batteries (LMBs) faces challenges due to the irreversible Li deposition/dissolution
process, which promotes Li dendrite growth with severe parasitic reactions during cycling. To address these issues, achieving
uniform Li‐ion flux and improving Li‐ion conductivity of the separator are the top priorities. Herein, a separator (PCELS) with
enhanced Li‐ion conductivity, composed of polymer, ceramic, and electrically conductive carbon, is proposed to facilitate fast
Li‐ion transport kinetics and increase Li deposition uniformity of the LMBs. The PCELS immobilizes PF6
– anions with high
adsorption energies, leading to a high Li‐ion transference number. Simultaneously, the PCELS shows excellent electrolyte
wettability on both its sides, promoting rapid ion transport. Moreover, the electrically conductive carbon within the PCELS
provides additional electron transport channels, enabling efficient charge transfer and uniform Li‐ion flux. With these ad-
vantages, the PCELS achieves rapid Li‐ion transport kinetics and uniform Li deposition, demonstrating excellent cycling
stability over 100 cycles at a high current density of 12.0 mA cm–2. Furthermore, the PCELS shows stable cycling performances
in Li–S cell tests and delivers an excellent capacity retention of 95.45% in the Li|LiFePO4 full‐cell test with a high areal capacity
of over 5.5 mAh cm–2.
1
|
Introduction
To mitigate rapid climate change and achieve net‐zero emis-
sions, the top priority is developing advanced batteries to
replace traditional internal combustion engines with electric
vehicles (EVs) [1–3]. Consequently, the EV market has various
requirements for rechargeable batteries, including high energy
density, fast charging, and safety [4–7]. In this regard, lithium
(Li)‐ion batteries (LIBs) are the most widely used energy storage
devices
for
EVs
due
to
their
excellent
electrochemical
This is an open access article under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution License, which permits use, distribution and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly
cited.
© 2025 The Author(s). Carbon Energy published by Wenzhou University and John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd.
Subi Yang, Seungho Lee, and Min Sung Kang contributed equally to this study.
1 of 14
Carbon Energy, 2025; 7:e70053
https://doi.org/10.1002/cey2.70053

performances [8–11]. However, as the conventional LIBs with
graphite anodes cannot meet the ever‐growing demands of the
EV markets for higher energy densities, there is an urgent need
to develop next‐generation batteries [12–14]. As a potential
alternative to conventional LIBs, Li metal batteries (LMBs) have
attracted significant attention due to their very high theoreti-
cal specific capacity (3860 mAh g–1) and low redox potential
(−3.040 V vs. standard hydrogen electrode) of the Li metal
anode [15–19]. However, several challenges hinder the practical
implementation of LMBs, such as the formation of an unstable
solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer, low Coulombic effi-
ciency (CE), and dendritic Li growth during cycling [20–26].
Furthermore, uncontrollable Li dendrite growth during repeti-
tive cycling can penetrate the separator, causing internal short‐
circuit and posing severe safety hazards [27–31].
To address these issues, an ideal separator design is required,
which can play a role in suppressing Li dendrite growth,
reducing safety concerns, and improving electrochemical
performances
of
LMBs
[32–35].
Polyolefin
separators
(polyethylene, polypropylene (PP), and their hybrids) are the
most widely used separators in LMBs due to their superior
chemical stability, uniform pore distribution, and low costs
[36, 37]. However, the poor electrolyte wettability and low
Li‐ion transference number (tLi+) of polyolefin separators
result in ununiform Li‐ion flux, limiting their ability to
support reversible electrochemical reactions and mitigate Li
dendrite growth during cycling [38–42]. In addition, due to
the poor mechanical strength and low thermal stability of
polyolefin
separators,
they
cannot
withstand
dendritic
punctures and avoid short‐circuits during cycling [43–47].
Therefore, improving the poor physicochemical properties of
polyolefin separators through modification is crucial for
achieving highly stable and high‐performance LMBs.
In this study, a polymer–ceramic–electrically conductive carbon
composite–layered separator (PCELS) is proposed to improve
the Li‐ion transport ability and uniformity of Li‐ion flux. In
addition to PCELS, a polymer–ceramic composite–layered sep-
arator
(PCS)
and
polymer–electrically
conductive
carbon
composite–layered separators (PES) were also systematically
compared to elucidate the impact of specific structural and
functional properties of separators on Li‐ion transport kinetics,
Li deposition behavior, and cycling stability in LMBs. Due to
high affinity of the ceramic–polymer composite layer to PF6
–
anions in Li salts, the PCELS immobilizes the PF6
– anions and
facilitates selective Li‐ion transport. Also, the high electrolyte
wettability on both sides of the separator significantly improves
the ionic conductivity [48]. Additionally, the electrically con-
ductive carbon within the PCELS shortens the channels for ion
transport by providing additional charge‐transfer sites, pro-
moting rapid electrochemical reactions [49–51]. Owing to these
synergistic effects, the PCELS achieves uniform Li‐ion flux and
a reversible Li deposition/dissolution process during cycling.
When the PCELS was evaluated in Li metal half‐cell tests using
conventional carbonate‐based electrolytes without additives,
the cell with the PCELS showed significantly improved cycle
performances of an average CE of ~96.5% over 200 cycles
at a current density of 4.0 mA cm–2 and stable cycling over
100 cycles even at a high current density of 12.0 mA cm–2.
Moreover, the PCELS delivered stable cycle performances in
Li‐sulfur (S) cell, and Li|LiFePO4 (LFP) full‐cell tests with a
high areal capacity of ~5.5 mAh cm–2. We believe that the
proposed separator would pave the way for the design of LMB
separators and further studies for practical implementation
of LMBs.
2
|
Experimental Section/Methods
2.1
|
Preparation of the PCS, PES, and PCELS
The slurry
for the
PCS coating
was
prepared
by
mixing
boehmite powder with 6 wt% polyvinylidene fluoride (PVdF) binder
(at a 9:1 weight ratio). This mixture was then dispersed in
N‐methylpyrrolidone (NMP) using a planetary mixer (ARE‐310,
Thinky). The slurry for the PES coating was also prepared by
mixing graphene powder with a poly(acrylic acid) (PAA) binder
(at a 9:1 weight ratio). The slurries for PCS and PES coating were
applied to the PP separator (Celgard 2400) with a doctor blade. The
coated separators were then dried in a convection oven at 50°C for
12 h. The PCELS was prepared as follows: after the PCS was fully
dried, the PES slurry was cast onto the opposite side of the
polymer–ceramic coating layer. Subsequently, the PCELS was fully
dried in the convection oven at 50°C for 12 h. Each separator was
punched into a 19 mm diameter disk for cell assembly. The
polymer–ceramic composite layer was coated at 1.14 mg cm⁻², and
the polymer–electrically conductive carbon layer was coated at
0.60 mg cm⁻² on each side of the PP separator.
2.2
|
Electrode Preparation and Cell Assembly
The Li|Cu cells were prepared using a 1 T Li metal foil (16 mm
diameter) as the anode and a Cu foil (12 mm diameter) as the
cathode. The electrolyte consisted of 1.0 M LiPF₆ dissolved in a
mixture of ethylene carbonate (EC) and diethyl carbonate (DEC)
(EC:DEC = 1:1, v/v) without any additives. The Li|Cu coin cells
(CR2032) were assembled in an argon‐filled glovebox. To prepare
the LFP electrode, a slurry was formulated by mixing LFP (LFP‐
NCO, Advanced Lithium Electrochemistry Co. Ltd.) as the active
material, carbon black (Super P) as the conductive additive, and
PVdF as the binder in NMP as the solvent. The slurry was uni-
formly coated onto a 20 μm‐thick aluminum current collector
and subsequently dried in a vacuum oven at 100°C overnight.
The LFP electrode was then punched into 12 mm diameter disks
at a loading level of ~25 mg cm–2. Li|LFP full‐cells were as-
sembled with an electrolyte composed of 1.15 M LiPF₆ in a sol-
vent mixture of EC, ethyl methyl carbonate (EMC) and DEC
(EC:EMC:DEC = 2:4:4, v/v/v). The electrolyte was supplemented
with 10 wt% fluoroethylene carbonate, 1 wt% LiPO₂F₂, and 1 wt%
vinylene carbonate (VC) as additives. The sulfur electrode slurry
was prepared by mixing 60 wt% sulfur, 30 wt% Super P, and 10 wt
% PVdF binder in an NMP solvent using a mixer. After mixing,
the sulfur slurry was cast onto an aluminum foil and dried in a
convection oven at 50°C for 24 h. After drying, the sulfur elec-
trode was punched into a 12 mm diameter disk with a loading
level of ~1.5 mg cm–2. The Li–S coin cells were assembled
in an argon‐filled glovebox using an electrolyte of 1 M LiTFSI
in a dimethoxy ethane/1,3‐dioxolane (1:1, v/v) mixture with
2 wt% LiNO3.
2 of 14
Carbon Energy, 2025

See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

2.3
|
Characterization
The surfaces of PP, PCS, PES, and PCELS were analyzed using field
emission scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi SU8230). The
chemical compositions of the PP, PCS, PES, and PCELS were
analyzed using X‐ray diffraction (XRD) on a diffractometer (D8
Advance, Bruker). The thermal shrinkage of each separator was
determined by measuring the dimensional change before and after
the thermal treatment at 100, 120, and 140°C for 1 h. Shrinkage was
calculated using the following equation:
S
S
S
Shrinkage
(%) =
−

S0 and ST represent the areas of the separator before and after
thermal treatment, respectively. The porosity of each separator
was measured using a mercury porosimeter (Autopore V 9620,
Micromeritics). The contact angle, indicating the wettability of
the separators, was determined using a contact angle mea-
surement (Phoenix 300, SEO).
2.4
|
Electrochemical Measurement
The Li deposition/dissolution tests for Li|Cu cells were conducted
with an areal capacity of 1.0 mAh cm–2, and the cutoff voltage for
Li dissolution was set to 1.0 V vs. Li+/Li. The cycling and rate
performance tests for the Li|LFP cell were performed following a
constant current–constant voltage protocol on a battery cycler
(WBCS3000L32,
WonATech).
The
ionic
resistance
of
the
electrolyte‐wetted separator was measured using a potentiostat
(VSP, Biologic, Seyssinet‐Pariset) in stainless‐steel (SUS)|separator|-
SUS symmetric cells over a frequency range from 300 kHz to 1 Hz
with an AC amplitude of 10 mV. The internal resistance of each
Li|Cu, Li–S, and Li|LFP cell after cycling was measured using
electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) over a frequency
range from 250 kHz to 10 mHz. Li|Li symmetric cells were as-
sembled to measure the tLi+ of each separator with an applied
potential of 10 mV in the frequency range from 20 kHz to 1 Hz for
20,000 s. The tLi+ of each separator was calculated using the fol-
lowing equation:

1.7–2.8 V and 2.5–4.0 V, respectively.

2.5
|
Density Functional Theory (DFT)
Calculation
DFT calculations were performed using the VASP package and
projector‐augmented wave potentials [52, 53]. The exchange–
correlation potentials were defined using the generalized gradient
approximation using Perdew–Burke–Ernzehof [54]. The electronic
wave functions were expanded in plane waves with a cutoff energy
of 520 eV to minimize Pulay stress during structural optimization.
Structural optimization was terminated when the Hellmann–
Feynman forces reached the threshold value of 0.01 eV/Å. The
Brillouin zone was sampled using (3 × 2 × 1), (2 × 2 × 2), and
(1 × 1 × 1) k‐point grids for boehmite (010), PP, and PAA, respec-
tively. The interaction between the surface and adsorbates was
described by adsorption energy (∆Eads) analysis, defined as
∆E
E
E
E
=
–
(
+
)
ads
System
Surface
adsorbate
(6)
where ESurface is the total energy of surfaces, Eadsorbate denotes
the total energy of the isolated atom, and ESystem represents the
total energy of the isolated atom adsorption system. A negative
ΔEads value indicates an attractive interaction between the
adsorbate and surfaces.
Finite element method (FEM) simulation: FEM simulation was
used to investigate the Li behavior in the Li|Cu asymmetric cell.
Li deposition modeling was performed using COMSOL Multi-
physics 6.2 software. The model incorporates two types of physics,
electrochemistry and ion transport, to simulate Li deposition
behavior on the electrode during the charging process.
2.6
|
Transportation of Species
The mass balance equations for each diluted species i in an
electrolyte are described as follows:
∂

See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

where Ji is the flux of species i. The flux is described using the
Nernst–Planck equations, which account for the diffusion, convec-
tion, and migration of the charged species. In this model, the
transport of the charged ions through diffusion and migration was
considered. Thus, the ionic flux is expressed as follows:
∇

F represents the Faraday constant, kB denotes the Boltzmann
constant, T is temperature, and ϕl is the electrolyte potential.
The first and second terms on the right‐hand side of the
equation represent diffusion driven by the concentration gra-
dient and electromigration influenced by the electric field,
respectively.
