Journal of Power Sources 541 (2022) 231728
Available online 9 June 2022
0378-7753/© 2022 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
Polyanion-assisted ionic-electronic conductive agents designed for high 
density Si-based anodes 
Jisoo Kwon a,1, Junghwan Kim a,b,1, Seong Yeon Bae a,c, Seung Pyo Jeon a, Jin Han Song d, 
Sung Eun Wang a, Dae Soo Jung a, Jihyun Jang e, Hyunjung Park c, Patrick Joohyun Kim b,**, 
Junghyun Choi a,* 
a Energy Storage Materials Center, Korea Institute of Ceramic Engineering and Technology, Jinju, 52851, South Korea 
b Department of Applied Chemistry, Kyungpook National University, Daegu, 41566, South Korea 
c Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Chosun University, Gwangju, 61452, South Korea 
d Shina T&C Co., Ltd., Seoul, 08501, South Korea 
e Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Seoul National University, Seoul, 08826, South Korea   
H I G H L I G H T S  
• A capillary-inspired conductive agent was first designed by polyanion and CNT. 
• The CNT backbone affords continuous conductive network within the Si-based anode. 
• The polyanion layer on CNTs effectively improves the electrolyte permeability. 
• The designed conductive agent provides conduction pathways for electrons and ions. 
• Excellent electrochemical performances of Si-based anodes were achieved.  
A R T I C L E  I N F O   
Keywords: 
Conductive agents 
Electrolyte permeability 
Lithium ion battery 
Si-based anode 
Electron conductivity 
Charge transfer 
A B S T R A C T   
Graphite-silicon (GS) composite electrodes have been actively studied due to the practical aspects as anodes for 
lithium ion batteries. Unfortunately, the amount of loaded Si in the GS composite electrode is required to be 
limited to avoid catastrophic issues arising from the volume change of Si anodes. To keep pace with the research 
trend and enhance the energy density of lithium ion batteries, it is inevitable to increase the density of GS 
composite electrodes in the cell. However, the permeability of organic electrolyte is typically deteriorated as the 
electrode gets denser. In addition, some relevant issues associated with the cell resistance and electrode utili­
zation seriously aggravate the cell performance. In this study, a capillary-inspired conductive agent (CCA) is 
employed as an electron/ion dual-conductive agent to address the above-mentioned challenges of high-density 
GS composite electrodes. Unlike conventional carbon blacks, the CCA is capable of providing an efficient 
conductive pathway for electrons and improving the electrolyte impregnation property due to the hydrophilic 
nature of polyanion polymer. Even when a trace amount of CCA (around 2 wt %) is introduced in the electrode 
composition, high-density GS composite electrodes show significant improvements in capacity retention (85% 
after 150 cycles) and Coulombic efficiency (99.9%).   
1. Introduction 
Electric vehicles (EVs) have emerged as one of the eco-friendly 
technologies to realize the net-zero emission, since it does not 
necessitate fossil fuels. In order for EVs to drive longer and more stable 
than fossil-fuel vehicles, it is of importance to develop energy storage 
devices with high energy density and high stability [1–3]. Lithium ion 
batteries (LIBs) are the most successful energy storage devices widely 
* Corresponding author. 
** Corresponding author. 
E-mail addresses: pjkim@knu.ac.kr (P.J. Kim), jchoi@kicet.re.kr (J. Choi).   
1 Equally contributed authors. 
Contents lists available at ScienceDirect 
Journal of Power Sources 
journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/jpowsour 
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpowsour.2022.231728 
Received 14 April 2022; Received in revised form 21 May 2022; Accepted 4 June 2022   

Journal of Power Sources 541 (2022) 231728
2
employed in a variety of fields including transportation and portable 
devices [4]. The traditional LIB adopted LiCoO2 (LCO) as a cathode and 
graphite as an anode due to its excellent electrochemical reversibility 
[5]. Although a number of electrode candidates have been intensively 
attempted to replace the graphite electrode [6–8], commercial LIBs for 
EVs still dominantly use pure graphite or graphite-based composite 
electrodes due to the economic advantages and decent performances. 
For these reasons, most of the strategies have focused on the addition of 
high-energy-density anode materials (e.g., Si, etc.) into graphite-based 
electrodes and the increase of electrode mass-loading to enhance the 
overall capacity of LIBs. The approach to mix Si and graphite together, i. 
e., forming graphite-silicon (GS) composites, has been commonly 
adopted by most of the battery industries [9,10]. Silicon (Si) has the 
highest theoretical capacity of 3600 mAh g−1 which is, at least, 9 times 
higher than graphite (382 mAh g−1) [11]. It has presented promising 
results in terms of capacity. Unfortunately, it has several bad aspects in 
terms of cycle stability, due to its huge volume change during lith­
iation/delithiation reaction [12]. Many researchers and engineers have 
aimed at improving the electrochemical kinetics and long-term cycla­
bility of GS composite electrodes via nanostructure engineering, com­
posite optimization, and surface modification [13–15]. However, GS 
composite electrodes still suffer from the electrode pulverization and 
delamination, limiting the increase of Si active materials in the com­
posite [16]. In addition, insufficient information with regard to suitable 
electrode composition limits the substantial enhancement in actual en­
ergy density of anodes. In this regard, it is necessary to design a new GS 
composite for high performance LIBs. 
Carbon black (CB) is typically employed as a conductive agent in 
LIBs. It has a high specific surface-area-to-volume ratio and an excellent 
electrical conductivity [17]. Once it is homogeneously dispersed 
throughout the electrode, it facilitates an efficient electrical contact 
between active materials [18]. However, in case that the volume of 
anode electrode significantly changes, the physical contact between CB 
particles would get loose due to the low aspect ratio of 0D-structured CB 
[19]. Consequentially, it deteriorates the efficient electron transfer and 
thus leads to the degradation of cycle performances. 
When carbon nanotubes (CNTs), in place of the CB, are introduced as 
conductive agents for LIBs, it not only provides a long and continuous 
conductive pathway within the electrode but also efficiently entangles 
active electrode materials [20–25]. These synergistic benefits would 
solve some of the issues originated from the large volume change of 
electrodes. It is reported that the CNT has a relatively low affinity for 
polar electrolyte and tends to get easily agglomerated when preparing 
slurries or dispersions [26]. Because of these intrinsic properties of 
CNTs, it is not favorable to fabricate homogeneous GS composite elec­
trodes with CNTs. Moreover, the poor electrolyte-wetting ability of 
CNTs would decrease the utilization of active materials and deteriorate 
the efficient Li+ diffusion through the GS composite electrode [27]. 
Polyanion possesses hydrophilic nature and anionic characteristics due 
to its electrolyte groups [28]. The strong hydrogen bonding between 
electrolyte group and water molecule helps to improve the dispersion 
quality of slurries for electrode coating process. Herein, we propose a 
capillary-inspired conductive agent (CCA), prepared by the complexa­
tion of polyanion and CNT, for improving the overall electrical and ionic 
conductivity of GS composite electrodes. The CNT provides a physical 
support with high electronic conductivity as well as constructs a stable 
SEI on the entire surface of active materials. The hydrophilic polyanion 
layer formed over the surface of CNTs improves the electrolyte affinity, 
contributing to an efficient charge transfer. When CCA was introduced 
into the GS composite electrode, cycle performance and rate capability 
were noticeably enhanced, even though the designed electrode contains 
a trace amount of CCA. 
2. Experiment 
2.1. Electrode preparation 
A composite of Si alloy (MK Electron) and graphite (Si alloy: 
graphite = 1 : 9 wt %) was used as the anode material. The slurry was 
prepared by mixing 96 wt % anode material, 1 wt % conductive agent 
and 3 wt % binder (CMC 1 wt %, SBR 2 wt %), as well as deionized (DI) 
water, with a planetary mixer (ARE-310, THINKY). CCA was prepared 
by mixing multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) dispersions (2.1 
wt% in H2O, Carbon T&C) and polyanion (poly (sodium 4-styrenesulfo­
nate, Mv ~70,000, 30 wt % in H2O, Sigma-Aldrich) in the same mass 
ratio in a planetary mixer at 2000 rpm for 5 min. The slurry was coated 
on copper foil via tape-casting method. The coated electrodes were dried 
in a vacuum oven at 100 ◦C for 3 h. 
2.2. Characterizations 
Cs corrected transmission electron microscope (Cs-TEM, FEI) exam­
ined that polyanion was coated on the CNT surface. The morphology of 
CNT and CCA was observed by a field emission scanning electron mi­
croscopy (FESEM, MIRA II LMH, TESCAN). The chemical composition of 
carbon and sulfur was characterized using energy-dispersive X-ray 
spectroscopy (EDX, Oxford Instruments, Abingdon, UK, attached to the 
FESEM). The cross-sectional SEM images of electrodes were observed 
using Focused ion beam (FIB, Thermo Scientific, Brno, Czech Republic). 
The zeta potentials of CNT and CCA were evaluated as a function of pH 
using zetasizer (Nano ZS, Malvern Instrument). The dispersion stability 
was monitored by Turbiscan (AGS, Formulaction). The rheological 
behavior of the dispersions was investigated by the rheometer (Physica 
MCR 501, Anton Paar). Contact angle measuring instrument (Phoenix- 
300 Touch, SEO) was characterized the hydrophilicity of CNT and CCA 
by measuring the contact angle between DIW and pellet of each solution. 
The density of all pellets was the same at 1.3 g cm−3. 
2.3. Electrochemical measurements 
2032R type half coin cells were fabricated to measure the electro­
chemical performances of GS composite electrodes with different 
conductive agents. Li metal foils (1T) were placed in counter electrode, 
and polyolefin membrane was used as separator. 1.15 M lithium hexa­
fluorophosphate (LiPF6) in a mixture of ethylene carbonate, ethylmethyl 
carbonate and dimethyl carbonate (EC-EMC-DMC, 2:4:4 vol %) with VC 
(1 wt %) and FEC (12.5 wt %) additives was used as an electrolyte. The 
galvanostatic charge and discharge measurement were conducted at 0.5 
C with voltage cut-off between 0.01 V–1.5 V versus Li/Li+. All cells 
undergo an activation process to form a stable SEI layer (0.1 C-1 cycle, 
0.2 C-1 cycle and 0.5 C-1 cycle). Electrochemical impedance spectros­
copy (EIS) (VSP, Biologic) was evaluated at frequency range between 
250 kHz and 10 mHz. Nyquist plot for EIS was calculated by equivalent 
circuit inserted in Fig. 4d–e. For the full cell test, coin full cell was 
fabricated using LiCoO2 as a cathode where N/P ratio was 1.1. The 
charging and discharging measurement were carried out under galva­
nostatic mode at 0.5 C with voltage cut-off between 3.0 V and 4.3 V. 
3. Results and discussion 
Fig. 1 depicts the roles of CCA in the GS composite electrode. In case 
of the GS composite electrode with conventional conductive agent (i.e., 
CBs), electrons are transported through contact points between CBs. 
During lithiation/delithiation, a huge volume expansion of GS com­
posite electrodes occurs, leading to the loss of electrical contact between 
GS composite and current collector. In addition, GS composite elec­
trodes with high volumetric density have a weak point for the electrolyte 
penetration since CB does not improve the electrolyte permeability due 
to the lack of polarity. This feature typically results in the poor 
J. Kwon et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    

Journal of Power Sources 541 (2022) 231728
3
utilization of active materials and the overall increase of charge transfer 
resistance. When CCA referring to “Capillary-inspired conductive agent” 
is employed as an additive inside the GS composite electrode, it effi­
ciently entangles the graphite and Si particles during the electro­
chemical reaction, providing a continuous electrical path through the 
entire electrode. Although the surface of CNTs is covered with a poly­
mer, there is no significant difference in terms of conductivity between 
CCA (σCCA = 0.26 S cm−1) and CNTs (σCNT = 0.56 S cm−1) (Fig. S1). 
Instead, the polymer coated on the surface of the CNTs is capable of 
improving the dispersibility of electrode slurry due to the presence of 
sulfonate groups. In addition, the polyanion layer on CNTs efficiently 
conveys liquid electrolyte to the whole area of the electrode like capil­
lary vessel. It would improve the Li+ diffusivity within the high-density 
GS composite electrode. 
De-bundling and dispersion of CNT in the aqueous media is one of 
the biggest challenges to use CNTs as conductive agents. Agglomerated 
CNT cannot provide an efficient electrical path to the electrode. As 
shown in Fig. 2a, CNT dispersion and CCA dispersion were fabricated by 
micro-fluidization process prior to making electrode slurry. To ascertain 
the morphology and polyanion distribution of CCA in comparison with 
pristine CNT, scanning electron microscopy and energy-dispersive X-ray 
spectroscopy (EDS) analyses were conducted using a dried CNT pellet 
Fig. 1. Concept. Schematic to illustrate the roles of capillary-inspired conductive agent, i.e., CCA, within the GS composite electrode.  
Fig. 2. Preparation and characteristics of CCA dispersion. (a) Microfluidizer process and slurry preparation process. All the GS electrode slurries are prepared by 
mixing conductive agents and active materials with a planetary mixer. SEM images of (b) CNT and (c) CCA. Sulfur element distribution of (d) CNT and (e) CCA using 
EDS mapping. TEM images of (f) CNT and (g) CCA. (h) FT-IR spectra and (i) Raman spectra of CNT and CCA. 
J. Kwon et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    

Journal of Power Sources 541 (2022) 231728
4
and a dried CCA pellet. According to Fig. 2b–c, there was no noticeable 
difference in terms of morphology before and after the surface func­
tionalization of CNTs with polyanion. In addition, no CNT agglomerate 
was observed in each specimen, indicating that each solution has a 
decent dispersibility in water. The EDS mapping identified the uniform 
sulfur distribution over the CCA, which is attributed by the sulfonate 
group of polyanion (Fig. 2d–e). In order to check if the polyanion layer 
uniformly covers the surface of CNTs, high-resolution transmission 
electron microscopy (HR-TEM) analysis was carried out. In Fig. 2e, TEM 
image clearly displays the typical feature of pristine CNT with multiple 
inner layers. After polyanion functionalization, CNT was fully encap­
sulated by the amorphous polyanion layer (Fig. 2f–g). An agglomeration 
of polyanion was not observed (Fig. S2). The chemical structures of CNT 
before and after polyanion layer formation were examined by Fourier- 
transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and Raman spectroscopy. 
Fig. 2h shows FT-IR spectra of pristine CNT and CCA. Three character­
istic peaks ranging from 1000 to 1200 cm−1, corresponding to sulfonate 
(-SO3
- ) groups in the polyanion, are observed in the CCA [29]. No sig­
nificant change in Raman spectra was observed for CNT and CCA 
(Fig. 2i). The similar G peak position (around 1595 cm−1) indicates that 
polyanion functionalization does not affect the original structure of 
CNTs. The intensity ratio of D band to G band also exhibits the analogous 
value of 0.7. These results support that polyanion is physically adsorbed 
onto CNTs, not forming a chemical bonding between polyanion and 
CNT. 
In order to check how the polyanion layer influences the dis­
persibility of CNT dispersion, three interfacial analysis tools were 
employed. Fig. 3a shows the zeta potential of CNT dispersion and CCA 
dispersion. The CCA dispersion is more negative zeta potential than 
pristine CNT dispersion through the entire pH range, due to the presence 
of sulfonate groups. Note that a large electrostatic repulsion significantly 
suppresses CNT agglomeration and precipitation, resulting in the better 
dispersion stability [30,31]. Rheology behavior is another important 
criterion to evaluate the dispersion stability of solutions. The viscosity of 
CNT dispersion and CCA dispersion was measured as a function of shear 
rate in the range from 10 s−1 to 1000 s−1 (Fig. 3b). Both dispersions 
exhibit shear thinning behavior, implying that the breakage of 
agglomerates occurs under the applied shear force [32,33]. In terms of 
viscosity, the CCA dispersion shows a lower value than the CNT 
dispersion. In other words, the CCA dispersion contains less agglomer­
ates than the CNT dispersion, indicating a better dispersion stability of 
the CCA dispersion. The other dispersion stability test was conducted as 
a function of time using turbiscan (Fig. 3c). The dispersibility of 
concentrated suspensions changes as a function of time due to destabi­
lization phenomena such as particle agglomeration or particle move­
ment [34]. Turbiscan evaluates the dispersion stability with time by 
measuring the intensity of scattered light after irradiating light to solids 
in the suspension. The turbiscan stability index (TSI) is calculated 
through the cumulative sum of all backscattering values measured. 
TSI(t) = 1
Nh
∑
tmax
ti=1
∑
zmax
zi=zmin
⃒⃒⃒⃒⃒BST(ti, zi) −BST(ti−1, zi)
⃒⃒⃒⃒⃒
(1)  
where tmax is the measurement point of time that the TSI is deduced, and 
zmin and zmax are the lower and upper selected height limits respectively. 
BST refers to the backscattering signal, Nh = (zmax - zmin)/Δh is the 
number of height positions in the selected zone of the scan [34–36]. The 
higher colloidal and physical stability, the lower the TSI value. The TSI 
values after 72 h were 0.13065 and 0.0987 for CNT dispersion and CCA 
dispersion, respectively. The CCA dispersion has a lower TSI value than 
CNT dispersion, which means that dispersion stability is improved by 
polyanion functionalization. Since it has shown a stable dispersion sta­
bility even after 72 h, long-term dispersion stability would be excellent. 
This effect would prevent some problematic phenomena such as ag­
gregation, flocculation, and sedimentation when manufacturing elec­
trode slurries. 
Fig. 4a shows voltage profiles of each electrode at 0.1 C. Graphite-Si 
with carbon black (GS-CB), Graphite-Si with CNT (GS-CNT) and 
Graphite-Si with CCA (GS-CCA) delivered similar charge capacities of 
418 mAh g−1, 426 mAh g−1 and 435 mAh g−1, respectively. No signif­
icant difference was observed between electrodes in terms of initial 
Coulombic efficiency. These results support that no side reaction occurs 
when CCA was employed as an additive. The rate performance of each 
electrode is given in Fig. 4b. The GS-CCA has exhibited the highest 
Fig. 3. Interfacial analyses. (a) Zeta potential analysis, (b) rheological test and (c) Turbiscan stability index (TSI) analysis of CNT dispersion and CCA dispersion.  
J. Kwon et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    

Journal of Power Sources 541 (2022) 231728
5
capacity retention of 91.1% at 1 C, due to the homogeneously dispersed 
CCA through the GS composite electrodes. Note that non-uniform and 
unstable SEI layer is mostly formed under high current levels, which 
boosts the loss of electrochemically available Li+ [37]. Given that the 
GS-CCA showed the best capacity recovery when the current density 
reverted to 0.1 C, the CCA can help to form a stable and uniform SEI 
layer within the GS composite and thus minimize the irreversible elec­
trochemical reactions. In order to demonstrate how CCA affects the 
electrolyte wettability, contact angle analysis was performed (Fig. 4c). 
The contact angle was measured right after dropping the same amount 
of polar solvent, i.e., water, onto a CNT pellet and a CCA pellet. The CCA 
pellet showed a lower contact angle (63◦) than that (71◦) of CNT, 
indicating a higher polar solvent affinity and wettability of CCA [38]. 
Basically higher affinity with polar solvent facilitates the efficient charge 
transfer across the electrode, leading to the reduced resistance of elec­
trodes [39]. To prove and correlate these results to the resistance of the 
battery cells, EIS measurements were carried out. The EIS spectra of 
each electrode before and after lithiation are presented in Fig. 4d–e. To 
compare the change of resistance before and after activation process, 
each Rct value was normalized based on the Rct value of CB (shown in 
Fig. 4f). The GS-CNT showed a similar normalized Rct value before and 
after lithiation. Interestingly, the GS-CCA showed a reduced normalized 
Rct after lithiation. Extrapolating from the result, it can be concluded 
that CCA is capable of offering more ionic pathway within in the GS 
composite electrode than CNT and CB, due to the better electrolyte 
wettability of CCA. 
The cycle performances of each electrode were evaluated at 0.5 C for 
180 cycles (Fig. 5a). All the electrodes have shown areal capacities 
around 3.5 mAh cm−2 in the first cycle. The voltage profiles of each 
electrode show decreased capacities after cycling (Fig. 5b). As presented 
in Fig. S3, GS-CNT and GS-CCA electrodes showed the better capacity 
retention of 82.7% and 85.4% at 180 cycles than GS-CB electrode. It is 
attributed to the binding ability and superior electrical conductivity of 
CNTs. The GS-CCA electrode achieved the most stable cycle perfor­
mance due to the improved electrolyte wettability and the well- 
distributed CCA additive within the GS composite. 
The enhanced electrolyte permeability enables the efficient Li+
diffusion inside the high-density electrode, resulting in the reduced 
charge transfer resistance. The Nyquist plots of the cycled GS-CCA and 
GS-CNT directly indicate the enhancement of the electrical contact and 
Fig. 4. (a) Initial voltage profile of GS-CB, GS-CNT and GS-CCA after activation process and (b) rate retention of GS-CB, GS-CNT and GS-CCA. (c) Contact angle 
measurements using a CNT pellet and a CCA pellet. EIS measurement (d) before and (e) after activation process. (f) Table of charge transfer resistance (Rct) and 
normalized Rct before and after lithiation. 
J. Kwon et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    

Journal of Power Sources 541 (2022) 231728
6
charge transfer efficiency, as presented in Fig. 5c and Table 1. The GS-CB 
electrode exhibited the largest contact resistance (Rs) of 6.53 Ω among 
electrodes, which is attributed by the loss of electrical contact between 
electrode materials which experience the significant volume change. It is 
reported that the inter-distance between electrode materials increases 
due to the volume change of Si particles and thus leads to the capacity 
loss and increase of electrode resistance [40]. To investigate how the 
morphology of electrodes changes after cycling and affects the resistance 
of cells, post-mortem studies were carried out. As shown in the SEM 
image of Fig. 5d, the cycled GS-CB shows a sharp cleavage between 
particles. In contrast, cycled GS-CNT and GS-CCA relatively maintained 
the original electrode structure without serious pulverization. These 
visual results corroborate that CNTs efficiently entangle electrode ma­
terials during repetitive lithiation/delithiation and reduce the overall 
resistance of cycled electrodes. Especially for the GS-CCA, it has the 
smallest charge transfer resistance among the cycle electrodes. This is 
likely due to the efficient Li+ transfer through the well-dispersed CCA 
network. 
In order to test if CCA could be applied to practical applications, full 
cell evaluation was conducted using lithium cobalt oxide (LCO) as a 
cathode electrode (Fig. 6). All the full cells were pre-cycled at 0.1 C for 
one cycle and 0.2 C for one cycle prior to cycling test. The battery cells 
were cycled at 0.5 C (1 C = 120 mAh g−1) in the voltage range from 3.0 
to 4.3 V (Fig. 6a). Fig. S4 shows voltage profiles of each full cell in the 1st 
cycle. Full cells using GS-CNT and GS-CCA exhibited higher specific 
capacities than GS-CB. The CCA makes a huge difference in the cycling 
performance of full cell. The GS-CB electrode showed the lowest average 
Coulombic efficiency during cycling as shown in Fig. 6b. It indicates that 
rapid capacity fading of GS-CB is attributed to the loss of active Li 
resulting from electrode pulverization. The GS-CCA electrode exhibited 
the most stable cycling performance, showing the retention of 89.2% 
after 50 cycles. These results strongly support that CCA can be a 
promising additive for full cell application. 
4. Conclusions 
In this work, we first proposed using capillary-inspired conductive 
agents (CCAs), prepared by the complexation of polyanion and CNT, for 
high-density GS composite electrodes. When the CCA was introduced in 
the electrode slurry it exhibited much lower viscosity and lower TSI 
Fig. 5. (a) Cycle performance of GS-CB, GS-CNT and GS-CCA at 0.5 C. To efficiently activate as-fabricated battery cells, activation process was carried out at 0.1 C for 
one cycle and at 0.2 C for one cycle. (b) Voltage profile after cycling. (c) EIS measurement after cycling. (d) Cross-sectional SEM images of electrodes after cycling. 
Table 1 
Contact resistance (Rs) and charge transfer resistance (Rct) after cycling.  
EIS after cycling 
Resistance 
GS-CB 
GS-CNT 
GS-CCA 
Rs (Ω) 
6.53 
3.83 
3.4

65.57 
35.57 
31.8  
J. Kwon et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    

Journal of Power Sources 541 (2022) 231728
7
value than pristine CNT. In addition, the CCA has shown a better elec­
trolyte affinity and more negative zeta potential than pristine CNT, due 
to the presence of well-distributed polyanion on CNTs. All these prop­
erties not only reduce the overall resistance of prepared GS composite 
electrodes but also facilitate the efficient dispersion process with slurry. 
To ascertain the practicability of CCA, electrochemical performances of 
half-cells and full-cells were evaluated under industrial conditions 
involving high areal capacity loading (≈3.5 mAh cm−2), high electrode 
density (≈1.5 g cm−3) and high actual loading mass (≈8.15 mg cm−2). 
The GS composite electrode with CCA showed an excellent cycle 
retention of 85% over 150 cycles along with high Coulombic efficiency 
of ~99.9%. In addition, the GS composite electrode with CCA exhibited 
the most promising rate retention (capacity retention was 91% at 1.0 C 
and 96.9% at 0.1 C) in comparison with GS composite electrodes with 
other additives, implying that the electrode degradation can be effi­
ciently suppressed by CCA even under the high current condition. Unlike 
the previous approaches of simply altering the composition of elec­
trodes, the use of CCA can easily improve the electrochemical kinetics 
and cycle stability of GS composite electrodes. We strongly believe that 
our approach would bring more relevant researches related to functional 
conductive agents and advance the development of practical GS com­
posite electrodes in a very near future.

Research papers
Rod shape Li4Ti5O12 additives for enhanced cycle performance of 
silicon-graphite anode in lithium-ion batteries at elevated temperature
Seungcheol Myeong a, Keemin Park b,1, Dongsoo Lee c, Insung Hwang a, Seho Sun d,  
Soo Chan Kim b, Hee Eun Yoo a,e, Chae-Woong Cho f,*, Ungyu Paik a,*, Taeseup Song a,*
a Department of Energy Engineering, Hanyang University, 222 Wangsimni-ro, Seoul 04763, Republic of Korea
b Lab) Electrode Development Group, Samsung SDI, 130 Samsung-ro, Suwon 16678, Republic of Korea
c Department of Chemical & Biological Engineering, Gachon University 1342 Seongnam-daero, Seongnam, Republic of Korea
d School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, 280 Daehak-ro, Gyeongsan, Republic of Korea
e SB) Pouch Development Team, Samsung SDI, 467 Beonyeong-ro, Cheonan 31086, Republic of Korea
f GPE) Process Development Team, Samsung SDI, 150 Gongse-ro, Giheung 17084, Republic of Korea
A R T I C L E  I N F O
Keywords:
Si-graphite anode
Rod shape Li4Ti5O12
Solid-electrolyte interphase layer
Electrical conducting path
A B S T R A C T
The severe resistance increases in the Si-based anode caused by the low Li-ion and electrical conductivity 
associated with significant volume change of Si during lithiation/delithiation process deteriorate the electro­
chemical performances in lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) at elevated temperature. Here, we report a simple and 
effective strategy to alleviate the degradation of Li-ion and electrical conductivity during cycling by introducing 
the rod shape Li4Ti5O12 (LTO) in the Si-graphite anode (SGA). The LTO rod on the surface of the SGA facilitates 
the formation of dense inorganic solid-electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer, which suppresses thickening behavior of 
SEI layer resulting in enhanced Li-ion conductivity. Moreover, the LTO rod existing in a lithiated form in the SGA 
during full-cell operation acts as electrical conducting paths between active materials, which improves electrical 
conductivity. The SGA employing LTO rod exhibits reduced resistances of SEI layer and charge transfer after 
formation and 100th cycles, and improved cycling stability compared to those of the pristine SGA.
1. Introduction
Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs), which are the most widely used energy 
storage devices, have become essential and indispensable things in our 
daily lives. However, conventional LIBs have already reached their en­
ergy density limits. This necessitates the development of LIBs with 
higher energy density [1–4]. Silicon (Si) is one of the most promising 
anode materials in LIBs due to its high theoretical specific capacity 
(3579 mAh g−1 for Li15Si4), low reaction potential (~0.5 V vs. Li/Li+), 
and low cost [5,6]. However, severe volume change of Si associated with 
Li during lithiation/delithiation process leads to the formation of thick 
solid-electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer caused by continuous decom­
position of electrolyte on the Si surface, and contact loss between the 
active materials [7–9]. This phenomenon accelerates degradation of Li- 
ion and electrical conductivity in the Si-based anode and corresponding 
poor electrochemical performances of LIBs [9,10].
When operating temperature of LIBs is increased, Li-ion conductivity 
of electrolytes and active materials increase, which intensifies the vol­
ume change of Si during lithiation/delithiation [11]. This phenomenon 
accelerates the issues caused by volume change, making them more 
serious compare to room temperature [12]. To improve the mileage of 
electric vehicles used in high temperature regions, it is necessary to 
address the degradation of Li-ion and electrical conductivity caused by 
the volume expansion of Si at elevated temperature in the Si-based 
anode.
Studies on the electrolyte engineering and surface coating on Si have 
been conducted to suppress the continuous formation of SEI layer caused 
by the reaction between the Si and the electrolyte during lithiation/ 
delithiation process for enhanced electrochemical properties of the Si- 
based anode at elevated temperature [10,13,14]. Although the electro­
lyte engineering could prevent electrolyte decomposition by forming a 
robust SEI layer on the Si surface during the initial cycle, its practical 
application is challenging due to an increase in the electrolyte viscosity 
and manufacturing cost associated with high additive concentrations 
* Corresponding authors.
E-mail addresses: cw97.cho@samsung.com (C.-W. Cho), upaik@hanyang.ac.kr (U. Paik), tssong@hanyang.ac.kr (T. Song). 
1 These authors contributed equally to this work.
Contents lists available at ScienceDirect
Journal of Energy Storage
journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/est
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.est.2025.115898
Received 1 December 2024; Received in revised form 31 January 2025; Accepted 16 February 2025  
Journal of Energy Storage 115 (2025) 115898 
Available online 28 February 2025 
2352-152X/© 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies. 

[15]. Similarly, while the coating layer on the Si surface could effec­
tively suppress electrolyte decomposition, it can decrease the produc­
tivity of Si-based anodes due to the additional processing required to 
introduce the coating layer on Si [10,14,16–19]. Studies are also being 
conducted to address the contact loss between the active materials 
caused by volume expansion of Si using electrical conducting agents 
such as single-wall carbon nanotubes and graphene [20–22]. However, 
these strategies do not prevent the capacity fading resulting from the 
continuous formation of SEI layer.
Here, we report a rod shape Li4Ti5O12 (LTO) as an additive to the Si- 
graphite anode (SGA) to reduce the degradation of Li-ion and electrical 
conductivity during cycling at elevated temperature. The LTO rod pre­
sent on the SGA surface facilitates the formation of dense inorganic SEI 
layer with high Li-ion conductivity. The dense inorganic SEI layer pro­
tects direct contact between the active materials and the electrolyte, 
which suppresses the formation of thick SEI layer caused by continuous 
electrolyte decomposition, resulting in enhancement of Li-ion conduc­
tivity. Furthermore, the LTO rod existing in a lithiated state of Li7+x­
Ti5O12 in the SGA during full-cell operation exhibits proper electrical 
conductivity and acts as electrical conducting paths between active 
materials, which improves electrical conductivity. The SGA employing 
LTO rod showed lower resistances of SEI layer and charge-transfer 
compared than the pristine SGA. Furthermore, the SGA employing 
LTO rod exhibited excellent cycling stability (73.0 % capacity retention 
for 100 cycles) compared to that of the pristine SGA (56.6 % capacity 
retention for 100 cycles) at 0.33C charging/1.0C discharging condition.
2. Experimental section
2.1. Materials
All chemicals were used as received. Ethylene glycol (C2H6O2, 
anhydrous, 99.8 %), titanium (IV) isopropoxide (Ti(OC3H7)4, 97 %), 
lithium carbonate (Li2CO3, > 99.0 %), and ethanol (EtOH, > 99.8 %) 
were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (USA). Graphite, silicon‑carbon 
composite (SCC), LiNi0.8Co0.15Al0.05O2 (NCA), carbon black, 1.0 wt% 
sodium-carboxymethyl cellulose (Na-CMC) in deionized water (DIW), 
and 40 wt% styrene-butadiene rubber (SBR) emulsion in DIW were 
provided by Samsung SDI Co., LTI (Republic of Korea).
2.2. Synthesis of LTO rod
60 ml of C2H6O2 was placed in a beaker under 200 ◦C. Then 2 ml of Ti 
(OC3H7)4 was added dropwise. After stirring the C2H6O2 and Ti(OC3H7)4 
mixture for 2 h, a mixture that was changed to milky white color was 
obtained. The precipitated material of mixture was washed several times 
with DIW and EtOH to remove residual organic impurity, and it was 
dried at 60 ◦C overnight. The product was calcined at 450 ◦C in the air 
condition for 1 h to obtain nanosized titanium dioxide (TiO2) rod 
powder. Prepared TiO2 rod were mixed with Li2CO3 at an atomic ratio of 
4.5:5 (Li:Ti). The mixture was calcined at 800 ◦C for 10 h under air 
condition to synthesize Li4Ti5O12 (LTO) rod.
2.3. Characterizations
The field-emission scanning electron microscope (SEM, JSM-7600F, 
JEOL, Japan) was used to observe the morphology of the TiO2 and LTO 
rod. The surface image of the SGA w LTO rod was also observed by SEM. 
X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained using Rigaku's SmartLab 
instrument (XRD, SmartLab, Rigaku, Japan) to analyze the crystalline 
structure of as-prepared LTO. The as-prepared LTO was analyzed by 
transmission electron microscopy (TEM) equipped with energy- 
dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (TEM, JEM-2100F, JEOL, Japan) to 
observe the shape and overall distributions of Ti and O elements. The 
LTO rod electrode was prepared with a weight ratio of LTO rod:carbon 
black:Na-CMC:SBR = 90:5:2.5:2.5. The electrode slurry was mixed in 
deionized water and cast onto a copper foil, followed by drying under 
vacuum. A lithium metal (thickness: 200 μm) was used as the counter 
electrode to assemble a half-cell. The assembled LTO rod half-cells were 
cycled at a current rate of 1.0C within a voltage range of 1.2 V to 0.01 V 
for 50 cycles. After 50 cycles, the LTO rod electrode was delithiated to 
1.2 V and subsequently analyzed using SEM, XRD, and XPS to investi­
gate its morphological, structural, and surface chemical properties.
2.4. Electrochemical measurements
For the preparation of Si-graphite anode (SGA), graphite, SCC, Na- 
CMC, and SBR were mixed with a weight ratio of 87.1:10.7:1:1.2. To 
obtain the SGA w LTO rod, graphite, SCC, LTO rod, Na-CMC, and SBR 
were prepared with a weight ratio of 86.2:10.6:1:1:1.2. The as-prepared 
slurries were coated on the Cu current collector (thickness: 6 μm) using 
doctor blade with a loading level of 12.85 mg cm−2 and dried at 110 ◦C 
for 10 min. Then, roll calendering process was conducted to the obtained 
anodes to achieve the target density of 1.63 g cc−1. The coin full-cells 
(2032 round type) were fabricated by using NCA with an areal capac­
ity of 5 mAh cm−2 and as-prepared anode. The N/P ratio was 1.1. 1.5 M 
LiPF6 in ethylene carbonate/diethyl carbonate/dimethyl carbonate with 
fluoroethylene carbonate additive (EC/DEC/DMC: 3/5/2 vol% with FEC 
5 %, PANAX StarLyte) was used as an electrolyte. The polypropylene 
(PP) separator was selected. A stable solid-electrolyte interphase (SEI) 
layer was formed through a formation cycle conducted under constant 
current-constant voltage (CC-CV) charging and constant current (CC) 
discharging conditions within a voltage range of 2.5–4.2 V. The for­
mation process involved one cycle each at 0.1C and 0.2C rates, respec­
tively, and was performed at 45 ◦C. The cycling performance was 
subsequently evaluated under CC-CV charging at a 0.33C rate and CC 
discharging at a 1.0C rate, also at 45 ◦C. All electrochemical perfor­
mances were analyzed at 45 ◦C using a battery cycle tester (TOSCAT 
3000, Toyo System, Japan). The anode after formation and 100th cycles 
were recovered after thoroughly washed with Dimethyl carbonate 
(DMC) and dried for the further analysis. For electrochemical impedance 
spectroscopy (EIS) analysis, the coin-cells were fabricated with the 
anode after formation and 100th cycles, respectively, as the working 
electrode, and lithium metal (thickness: 200 μm) as a counter electrode. 
EIS analysis was conducted at an open-circuit voltage with a frequency 
range of 1–100,000 Hz at an amplitude of 20 mV using a potentiostat 
(ZIVE BP2A, Won-A tech, Republic of Korea). The three-electrode cell 
was assembled by placing lithium metal as a reference electrode be­
tween the prepared SGA and NCA cathode. In the three-electrode cell, 
the voltage of the anode during charge/discharge process was obtained 
based on the reference electrode. To analyze the voltage profiles of LTO, 
the LTO anode was prepared by mixing LTO, carbon black, Na-CMC, and 
SBR at a weight ratio of 80:10:5:5. The coin half-cell was fabricated by 
LTO anode as a working electrode and lithium metal (thickness: 200 μm) 
as a counter electrode. After the formation cycle of 0.1 and 0.2C, the 
charge/discharge property of the three-electrode cell and LTO half-cell 
were analyzed at 0.2C with a potentiostat, respectively. The thickness 
change ratio of the pouch full-cells was measured in situ during cycling 
using a linear gauge (LGK-0110, Mitutoyo, Japan) under constant 
loading.
2.5. Post-analysis of the electrode
To observe the thickness of SEI layer, the electrodes after formation 
and 100th cycles were sonicated in DMC media. Then, the dispersed 
particles are dropped on TEM grid and dried. The dried electrodes were 
analyzed by using TEM. The components of SEI layer and distribution of 
them are observed with time-of-flight secondary-ion mass spectroscopy 
(TOF-SIMS, IONTOF GmbH, Münster, Germany). The analysis was 
conducted with Bi+ primary ions while sputtering with a 10 keV Ar gas 
gun for 30 s. All positive polarity mass spectra were calibrated using 
total intensity for comparison between samples. The analysis area of the 
S. Myeong et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 
Journal of Energy Storage 115 (2025) 115898 
2 

electrode was 200 × 200 μm2. The electrical conductivity of the elec­
trode after formation and 100th cycles was calculated via the Van der 
Pauw method using a multimeter (Multimeter 2000/E, Keithley, USA).
3. Results and discussion
3.1. Material characterization
Fig. 1 exhibits the characterizations of Li4Ti5O12 (LTO) material. 
First, TiO2 rod was synthesized through the precipitation method of 
ethylene glycol and titanium (IV) isopropoxide and post-heat treatment 
(Fig. S1(a)). Next, LTO were prepared by solid-state reaction of as- 
synthesized TiO2 rod and Li2CO3 at 800 ◦C for 10 h under air atmo­
sphere. The field-emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image 
exhibits that LTO rod with radius of 700 nm and aspect ratio of 5:1 is 
successfully prepared (Fig. 1(a)). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of 
LTO rod was well matched with the LTO planes of (111), (311), (222), 
(400), (331), (333), (440), (531), (622), and (444) (Fig. 1(b)) [23]. 
Fig. 1(c) shows a transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of LTO 
rod. TEM energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) elemental mapping images of 
LTO rod reveal the uniform distribution of Ti, and O elements. Analysis 
of morphologies and crystalline structure support the successful syn­
thesis of LTO rod. Fig. S1(b) shows the SEM image of Si-graphite anode 
(SGA) added with LTO rod 1 wt% (SGA w LTO rod). LTO rod is well 
distributed in SGA and connected to the surface of the active materials. 
Fig. S2 shows the SEM, XRD, and XPS results of the LTO rod after cycling 
within the 1.2 V–0.01 V voltage range. The results confirm that the LTO 
rod added to the SGA remain stable without significant morphological or 
structural collapse even after repeated charge/discharge cycles. How­
ever, slight changes in the XRD peaks were observed due to the lithiated 
state of LTO at 1.2 V. Additionally, XPS analysis reveals the formation of 
LiF inorganic components on the surface of the LTO rod.
3.2. Electrochemical characterization
To investigate the effectiveness of LTO rod, the SGA and SGA w LTO 
rod were prepared for the full-cells using LiNi0.8Co0.15Al0.05O2 (NCA) as 
a cathode that has a high areal capacity of 5 mAh cm−2. All electro­
chemical analyses were performed at 45 ◦C. To determine the optimal 
condition for the weight ratio (wt%) of LTO rod, electrochemical per­
formance evaluation is conducted for each ratio as shown in Fig. S3. It is 
confirmed that adding 1 wt% of LTO rod resulted in the highest 
coulombic efficiency and cycling performance. The addition of LTO rod 
enhance both Li-ion and electron kinetics, which leads to an improve­
ment in electrochemical properties. However, excessive LTO rod could 
induce a decrease in efficiency as LTO rod remains in lithiated form 
(Li7Ti5O12) even after discharge in the full cell. The addition of 1 wt% 
LTO rod is determined to be the optimal condition for SGA w LTO rod 
and used for subsequent evaluations.
Fig. 2(a) shows the voltage profiles of the full cells during 0.1C for­
mation cycle within a voltage window of 2.5–4.5 V vs. Li/Li+ at 45 ◦C. 
The SGA shows a discharging capacity of 5.03 mAh cm−2 with 
coulombic efficiency of 68.7 %. The low discharging capacity and 
coulombic efficiency of SGA are attributed to the formation of solid- 
electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer by electrolyte decomposition and 
electrical contact loss between active materials associated with large 
volume change of Si during charge/discharge process [7,8]. The SGA w 
LTO rod exhibits an improved discharging capacity of 5.24 mAh cm−2 
with coulombic efficiency of 70.8 % compared to the SGA. Similarly, the 
voltage profiles at 0.2C, conducted at 45 ◦C, showed that the SGA with 
LTO rod exhibited better electrochemical performance compared to that 
of the SGA (Fig. S4). In both 0.1 and 0.2C voltage profiles, SGA w LTO 
rod showed lower overpotentials compared to SGA during charge/ 
discharge process, indicating that the introduction of LTO rod can 
effectively reduce polarization in the SGA. After the 0.1C and 0.2C 
Fig. 1. (a) SEM image and (b) XRD pattern of LTO rod. (c) TEM image of LTO rod with low magnification and EDX elemental mapping images: Ti and O elements.
S. Myeong et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 
Journal of Energy Storage 115 (2025) 115898 
3 

formation cycles, the cycling performance at 45 ◦C was carefully 
monitored over 100 cycles under a 0.33C charging rate and a 1.0C 
discharging rate, as shown in Fig. 2(b). The SGA exhibited continuous 
capacity fading and capacity retention of 56.6 % at 100 cycles. However, 
the SGA w LTO rod showed superior cycling performance, indicating 
capacity retention of 73.0 % at 100 cycles.
The differential voltage analysis (DVA, dV/dQ vs. Q) is an effective 
tool to quantitatively specify the degradation behavior of Si and graphite 
materials in SGA, respectively [24,25]. The voltage profiles and DVA 
during discharge for the 1st and 100th cycles of SGA and SGA w LTO rod 
are shown in Fig. S5, Fig. 2(c), and (d). During the discharge process, a 
peak corresponding to Si appears at the marked point (asterisk, *) of the 
DVA (Fig. 2(c) and (d)). As most of the capacity of graphite material is 
released before the onset of Si delithiation in the SGA, the discharge 
capacity before the marked point is attributed to the graphite, while the 
discharge capacity after the marked point is attributed to the Si [26,27]. 
Therefore, it is possible to quantitatively distinguish the capacity of Si 
and graphite materials in the SGA through DVA. After 100th cycle, the 
capacity retention of Si and graphite was 53.0 and 57.8 % for SGA and 
72.3 and 73.2 % for SGA w LTO rod, respectively. The improvement in 
capacity retention depending on the introduction of LTO rod is more 
effective in Si materials than graphite, which could be attributed to the 
difference in volume expansion between Si and graphite during charge/ 
discharge process [28,29].
3.3. Understanding of enhanced electrochemical performances
To elucidate the reason for the improved electrochemical 
performances of SGA w LTO rod, the electrochemical impedance spec­
troscopy (EIS) analysis was conducted after the two-step formation cy­
cles at 0.1 and 0.2C and 100th cycles at 0.33C charge/1.0C discharge for 
the SGA and SGA w LTO rod. Fig. 3 shows Nyquist plots of the SGA and 
SGA w LTO rod with equivalent circuit. The measured resistance can be 
distinguished into three resistance, resistance of solution (Rs, high- 
frequency region), SEI layer (RSEI, the first semi-circle in high- 
frequency region), and charge transfer (Rct, the second semi-circle in 
medium-frequency region) [9,30]. The values of the resistances are 
listed in Table 1. The Rs of SGA and SGA w LTO rod showed similar 
values of 0.25 and 0.23 Ω, and 0.50 and 0.45 Ω after formation and 
100th cycles, respectively, which indicates that the addition of LTO rod 
does not affect the microstructure in the anode and corresponding 
electrolyte wettability [31]. For the SGA, RSEI and Rct after formation 
cycles were 3.07 and 18.99 Ω, and RSEI and Rct after 100th cycles were 
32.68 and 23.76 Ω, respectively (Fig. 3(a)). On the other hand, for the 
SGA w LTO rod, RSEI and Rct after formation cycles were 2.56 and 14.24 
Ω, and RSEI and Rct after 100th cycles were 5.16 and 16.68 Ω (Fig. 3(b)). 
After the cycling stability test, the resistance increases of RSEI and Rct in 
SGA w LTO rod were measured at 2.60 and 2.44 Ω, respectively, which 
corresponded to 8.8 and 51.2 % to the values of 29.61 and 4.77 Ω for 
SGA. The mitigation in resistance increase by the introduction of LTO 
rod is related to the modulated SEI layer during charge/discharge pro­
cess and the resulting changes of Li-ion and electrical conductivity in the 
anode, which will be discussed in the following sections.
Large volume change of Si associated with Li during charge/ 
discharge process leads to the continuous formation of SEI layer, which 
deteriorates Li-ion conductivity [32–35]. To investigate the SEI layer, 
Fig. 2. Electrochemical properties of the full-cells consisting of an NCA cathode and SGA and SGA w LTO rod at 45 ◦C: (a) Initial charge/discharge voltage profile at 
0.1C. (b) Cycling performance over 100 cycles at 0.33C charge/1.0C discharge. DVA curves at 1st and 100th cycles during discharge process for (c) SGA, and (d) SGA 
w LTO rod, respectively.
S. Myeong et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 
Journal of Energy Storage 115 (2025) 115898 
4 

the surfaces of the SGA and SGA w LTO rod were characterized by time- 
of-flight secondary ion mass spectrometry (TOF-SIMS) technique after 
100th cycles. TOF-SIMS are very effective technique to characterize the 
components of SEI layer due to its high sensitivity and resolution (< 1 
nm) [36,37]. Fig. 4(a) exhibits the positive TOF-SIMS mass spectra in the 
range of 10–60 m/z of 100th cycled SGA and SGA w LTO rod. The 
components of inorganic SEI layer (e.g. Li2O, LiF, and Li2CO3) formed by 
the LiPF6 salt decomposition are analyzed through Li2O+ (m/z: 30) and 
Li2F+ (m/z: 33) peaks in TOF-SIMS spectra [36]. The organic species of 
SEI layer formed by the decomposition of the electrolyte solvent are also 
detected through CH4O+ (m/z: 32) and C4H4Li+ (m/z: 59) [36]. The 
peak intensities of inorganic and organic SEI layer components for SGA 
and SGA w LTO rod after 100th cycles are presented in Table 2. The SEI 
layer is usually formed as a multi-layer structure consisting of a dense 
inorganic layer near the interface between the active materials and the 
SEI layer and a porous organic layer near the interface between the SEI 
layer and electrolyte [32,33]. To effectively alleviate the continuous 
formation of SEI layer on Si-based materials during charge/discharge 
process, the dense inorganic layer should be formed on the surface of the 
active material and prevent the electrolyte penetration [38]. The SGA w 
LTO rod exhibits higher inorganic/organic ratio of 2.37 compared to 
that of the SGA (1.72) after 100th cycles, which is attributed to the 
suppression of additional electrolyte decomposition associated with the 
formation of a relatively dense inorganic layer [39]. The dense inorganic 
layer in the SEI layer enables enhanced Li-ion conductivity due to the 
intrinsically higher Li-ion conductivity compared to the porous organic 
layer and suppressed reaction between the active material and the 
electrolyte [38]. SGA w LTO rod exhibited reduced intensities of inor­
ganic and organic SEI layer compared to those of the SGA, which in­
dicates that the introduction of LTO rod could mitigate the continuous 
formation of SEI layer during charge/discharge process [40]. TOF-SIMS 
mapping images of various SEI layer components are shown in Fig. 4(b) 
and Fig. 4(c). The distributions of Si and LTO rod are observed through 
Si+ and Ti+, respectively. For the SGA, the SEI layer components of 
Li2F+, CH4O+, and C4H4Li+ are evenly distributed around the Si material 
(Fig. 4(b)). The SEI layer components of Li2F+, CH4O+, and C4H4Li+
were intensively observed around the Si material compared to the LTO 
rod in SGA w LTO rod (Fig. 4(c)), which indicates that the LTO rod plays 
an important role in reducing SEI layer formation on the surface of the Si 
material.
Fig. 5 exhibits the thickness of SEI layer after 100th cycles on the 
surface of the graphite and SCC for SGA and SGA w LTO rod, respec­
tively. The thickness of SEI layer in each material was averaged after 
measuring five points. After 100th cycles, the SEI layer thickness on the 
graphite surface was similar with SGA of 49.1 nm and SGA w LTO rod of 
48.5 nm (Fig. 5(a) and (b)), which indicates that the LTO rod would not 
Fig. 3. Nyquist plots after formation and 100th cycles of (a) SGA and (b) SGA w LTO rod, respectively.

S. Myeong et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 
Journal of Energy Storage 115 (2025) 115898 
5 

affect the change of SEI layer thickness on the graphite surface. On the 
other hand, after 100th cycles in the SCC, the thickness of SEI layer was 
measured at 568.1 nm (Fig. 5(c)), while SGA w LTO rod showed a 
thickness of 264.5 nm (Fig. 5(d)), presenting the reduction in the SEI 
layer thickness of the SCC with the incorporated LTO rod. LTO is known 
as a “zero-strain” material in which the change of the lattice parameter 
during the charge/discharge process is virtually zero, which allows the 
SEI layer generated in the formation cycles to be maintained during 
cycling [41,42]. As shown in Fig. S7, it can be observed that the SEI layer 
thickness of the LTO rod remains extremely thin even after 100th cycles 
(after formation cycles: 20.1 nm and after 100th cycles: 25.0 nm). 
Through the analysis of the physicochemical properties of the SEI layer, 
it has been confirmed that the LTO rod in SGA effectively suppresses the 
additional formation of the SEI layer on SCC surface during cycling by 
promoting the formation of a dense inorganic SEI layer. Suppressed 
formation of the SEI layer leads to the enhancement of the RSEI and Li- 
ion conductivity, which improves the electrochemical performances of 
LIBs.
The significant volume change of Si during charge/discharge process 
not only degrades Li-ion conductivity but also electrical conductivity in 
the anode caused by inter-particle and intra-particle contact loss of Si 
[43]. The as-prepared LTO rod with radius of 700 nm and aspect ratio of 
5:1 acts as electrical conducting paths, which reduces degradation of 
electrical conductivity in the SGA during charge/discharge process. 
Fig. S8 shows working potentials of SGA in full-cell operation and LTO 
anode in half-cell operation during charge/discharge process at 0.2C, 
respectively. During full-cell operation, as the charge/discharge process 
of SGA occurs below 0.5 V, the LTO rod added to the SGA exists in the 
lithiated state of Li7+xTi5O12 [44]. The lithiated LTO rod with increased 
Li-ion contents in the lattice exhibits proper electrical conductivity (e.g. 
Li7Ti5O12: ~10−2 S cm−1), which could maintain the electrical con­
ducting paths between the active materials during cycling [45]. Fig. 6(a) 
Fig. 4. Positive TOF-SIMS analysis of the SGA and SGA w LTO rod after 100th cycles: (a) TOF-SIMS spectra. Mapping images of Si+, Ti+, Li2F+, CH4O+, and C4H4Li+
for (b) SGA, and (c) SGA w LTO rod.
Table 2

LTO rod after 100 cycles (intensity unit: 10−3).

1.69

S. Myeong et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 
Journal of Energy Storage 115 (2025) 115898 
6 

shows the electrical resistance of SGA and SGA w LTO rod after for­
mation cycles. The electrical resistance of SGA w LTO was 41.0 Ω, which 
is 4.9 % lower than that of SGA (43.1 Ω). After 100th cycles, the elec­
trical resistance of SGA w LTO rod was 104.0 Ω, which is about 20 % 
lower than that of SGA (129.8 Ω) (Fig. 6(b)). These results demonstrate 
that the introduction of LTO rod successfully forms the electrical con­
ducting paths between the active materials.
The effect of LTO rod in mitigating both additional SEI formation and 
contact loss of Si also influences the swelling behavior of SGA during 
charge/discharge cycles. As shown in Fig. S9, the SGA w LTO rod ex­
hibits a 30.7 % thickness change ratio in the charged state of the initial 
cycle, which is higher than 25.5 % for SGA. It is attributed to the in­
crease in Si charging capacity caused by the addition of LTO rod. 
However, as cycling progresses, SGA undergoes a continuous thickness 
increase of 1.1 % per cycle in the discharged state, primarily due to 
additional SEI formation and Li trapping caused by the contact loss of Si 
[46]. In contrast, SGA w LTO rod exhibits a significantly suppressed 
volumetric increase of only 0.5 % per cycle, as the LTO rod mitigate both 
additional SEI formation and Si contact loss. This demonstrates the 
effectiveness of LTO rod incorporation in SGA for controlling swelling 
behavior.
Fig. 6(c) shows the schematic illustrations of the SGA and SGA w LTO 
rod after few cycles. In SGA, the continuous formation of SEI layer and 
contact loss between active materials due to the significant volume 
change of Si result in deteriorated Li-ion and electrical conductivity. 
However, in SGA w LTO rod, the LTO rod could generate the dense 
inorganic SEI layer with high ionic conductivity, which reduces degra­
dation of Li-ion conductivity by preventing the formation of additional 
SEI layer associated with electrolyte decomposition during cycling. 
Moreover, the LTO rod existing in a lithiated state during full-cell 
operation could mitigate the degradation of electrical conductivity by 
acting as electrical conducting paths between active materials. 
Compared to SGA, SGA w LTO rod exhibits excellent Li-ion and electrical 
conductivity, which improves RSEI and Rct and corresponding electro­
chemical performances of LIBs. SGA with LTO rod demonstrates supe­
rior cycling performance, achieving 73.0 % capacity retention after 100 
cycles, compared to 68.4 % for SGA with carbon nanotube (CNT), as 
shown in Fig. S10. This enhanced performance is due to the addition of 
the LTO rod, which effectively suppresses both electrical conductivity 
and Li-ion conductivity degradation during cycling, whereas CNT miti­
gates only electrical conductivity degradation by forming an inter­
connected network [47]. This highlights the effectiveness of LTO rod in 
enhancing cycling stability of SGA.
4. Conclusions
In summary, we explored LTO rod as an effective additive to alleviate 
the degradation of Li-ion and electrical conductivity in the SGA for 
improved electrochemical performances at elevated temperature. The 
LTO rod present on the SGA forms the dense inorganic SEI layer, which 
prevents the continuous formation of SEI layer. The lithiated LTO rod in 
the SGA during full-cell operation also acts as electrical conducting 
paths. As a result, the SGA employing LTO rod exhibited reduced re­
sistances of SEI layer and charge-transfer compared to those of the 
pristine SGA after formation and 100th cycles. Furthermore, the SGA 
employing LTO rod showed outstanding cycling stability compared to 
that of the pristine SGA.
