Journal of Power Sources 580 (2023) 233326
Available online 1 July 2023
0378-7753/© 2023 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
Silicon-stabilized lithium metal powder (SLMP) composite anodes for fast 
charging by in-situ prelithiation 
Eunbin Jang a, Seokgyu Ryu a, Myeongjin Kim b, Junghyun Choi c,**, Jeeyoung Yoo a,* 
a School of Energy Engineering, Kyungpook National University, Daegu, 41566, Republic of Korea 
b Department of Hydrogen & Renewable Energy, Kyungpook National University, Daegu, 41566, Republic of Korea 
c Energy Storage Materials Center, Korea Institute of Ceramic Engineering and Technology, Jinju, 52851, South Korea   
H I G H L I G H T S  
G R A P H I C A L  A B S T R A C T  
• Si₋SLMP (SiLP) composite anode system 
for fast charging was proposed. 
• A strategy of in-situ prelithiation can 
form Li ion pathway in the SiLP anodes. 
• The in-situ LiF formation to improve the 
SEI issues of anodes was proved. 
• The LFP (NCM811)//SiLP exhibited su­
perior capacity and initial coulombic 
efficiency. 
• The DFT simulation was conducted to 
clarify the change of binding energy of 
SiLP.  
A R T I C L E  I N F O   
Keywords: 
Silicon anode 
Stabilized lithium metal powder 
Prelithiation 
Fast charging 
SEI layer 
Density functional theory calculations 
A B S T R A C T   
Advanced battery properties, such as high specific energy density and fast charging, are currently in demand 
owing to the expansion of the electric vehicle (EV) market. Si and Li metals have attracted research attention as 
anode materials because of their large theoretical capacities and fast reaction rates with Li ions. However, 
extreme volume changes and solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) issues hinder their applications in EVs. Herein, we 
mix Si with stabilized lithium metal powder (SLMP) to achieve a composite anode with complementary char­
acteristics of Si and SLMP (SiLP) that does not require carbon additives. Prelithiation is performed before 
applying an external current to contribute to a fast reaction rate because of the exclusion of the Si lithiation 
process and the plating process conducted during the initial charging state. Furthermore, a stable lithium fluoride 
layer is formed to mitigate SEI issues by adding a fluorinated polymer during electrode fabrication. Capacity 
characteristics are improved by more than double and a high SiLP anode initial Coulombic efficiency (ICE) of 
70% is attained. In addition, the diminished SiLP adsorption energy is provided via density functional theory 
(DFT) calculations.   
* Corresponding author. 
** Corresponding author. 
E-mail addresses: jchoi@kicet.re.kr (J. Choi), jyoo@knu.ac.kr (J. Yoo).  
Contents lists available at ScienceDirect 
Journal of Power Sources 
journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/jpowsour 
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpowsour.2023.233326 
Received 16 March 2023; Received in revised form 30 May 2023; Accepted 10 June 2023   

Journal of Power Sources 580 (2023) 233326
2
1. Introduction 
Lithium–ion batteries (LIBs) have been extensively studied as energy 
storage systems for portable electronic devices, such as mobile phones, 
laptop computers, and tablets as well as for electric vehicles (EVs) owing 
to their high energy density, long cycle life, and lack of memory effect 
and self-discharge. Recently, the EV market has expanded with enforced 
regulations for the use of environmentally friendly vehicles. The battery 
is the most important factor in determining EV performance. In most 
cases, EVs with LIBs compete with internal combustion engine vehicles 
(ICEVs); thus, LIBs must fulfil longer driving distances and faster 
charging times, which strongly depend on their component materials 
[1]. 
High-energy-density LIBs are required to extend the driving dis­
tances of EVs. Their energy density has been increased by adopting 
advanced cathode materials with high specific capacities and large 
operating voltage ranges [2]. Conventional cathode materials, such as 
LiMn2O4, LiFePO4, and LiCoO2, provide a low specific capacity (<250 
mAh g−1), thus, layered Li-rich and Ni-rich cathode materials have been 
investigated. Compared to LiCoO2, Li-rich cathode materials exhibit a 
different structure in which excess Li substitutes the transition metal. 
Therefore, they can achieve high specific capacity (>300 mAh g−1) and 
high energy density (>1000 Wh kg−1) [3,4]. Additionally, high oper­
ating voltage significantly increases the corresponding energy density. A 
promising candidate cathode material is spinel LiNi0.5Mn1.5O4. A high 
charge–discharge plateau of about 4.7 V was observed in this ordered 
structure, where Mn3+ fills the unoccupied spinel lattice. This led to an 
elevated energy density of about 20% and 30% compared to LiCoO2 and 
LiFePO4, respectively [5]. The most notable cathode material is the 
Ni-rich cathode, where Ni substitutes for the transition metal. The Ni 
cation undergoes Ni2+/Ni3+ and Ni3+/Ni4+ oxidation states, and the 
redox reaction of Co3+/Co4+ occurs during the deintercalation reaction. 
This process increases the operating voltage and specific capacity via the 
mechanism of charge compensation. Currently, increasing the Ni con­
tent by more than 80% has been studied for improving the gravimetric 
capacity compared to that of commercialized Ni-rich cathodes, which 
contain a Ni ratio less than 60% [6,7]. 
Anodes that can accommodate such increased cathode capacity are 
also in demand. A large amount of graphite is required to obtain a large 
cathode capacity when it is used as an anode, which increases anode 
thickness. However, thick anodes decrease the charging rate of LIBs 
owing to slow Li ion diffusion; thus, they are unsuitable for EVs. In 
addition, the battery charging time of EVs is much longer than the gas 
feeding time of ICEVs, which hinders the widespread use of EVs. 
Therefore, improving the issue of fast charging in EVs requires (1) fast 
kinetics and (2) rapid ion diffusion. Fast kinetics involves a prompt re­
action rate at the interphase between lithium ions (Li+) and anode active 
materials. Depending on the active materials, major reactions between 
Li+ and the electrode are typically divided into plating–stripping, 
alloying–dealloying, intercalation–deintercalation, and conversion re­
actions. According to kinetics studies, the reaction rates of plati­
ng–stripping and alloying–dealloying are faster than that of 
conventional intercalation–deintercalation [8,9]. For ion diffusion, a 
short diffusion length generally causes prompt ion diffusion. However, a 
tradeoff exists between electrode thickness and energy storage capacity. 
An anode material with high theoretical capacity can compensate for the 
capacity loss caused by a thin electrode and sufficiently accommodate 
cathode capacity. 
As anode materials, silicon- and lithium-based materials have been in 
the spotlight. Compared with traditional carbon materials (typical 
graphite: 372 mAh g−1), Si has the highest theoretical gravimetric ca­
pacity (4200 mAh g−1 for Li4.4Si) [10]. Si is also cheap, abundant, and 
environmentally benign compared with other battery materials, 
rendering it suitable for green EVs [11,12]. Because of these advantages, 
Si-adopted composite anodes have been recently developed for EVs [13, 
14]. However, pure Si anodes have not been commercialized because of 
intrinsic problems. Si undergoes extreme volume changes (~300%) 
during charging–discharging [15,16]. During this process, the solid 
electrolyte interphase (SEI) is continuously damaged and regenerated 
[17,18], resulting in the pulverization of active materials, abscission on 
the current collector, and low initial Coulombic efficiency (ICE) [19,20]. 
Additionally, Si is an insulator with an electrical conductivity of ~10−5 
S cm−1. Although many studies have been conducted to overcome these 
fundamental challenges, they have only been partially addressed 
through the suggestion of alternative materials [21]. Consequently, an 
innovative approach must be developed to comprehensively address 
structural and electrochemical issues. 
Li metal is a representative material used in the plating–stripping 
reaction. Therefore, the reaction rate of a Li-metal anode is faster and its 
binding energy for combination with Li+ is lower than those of other 
anode materials. In addition, a Li metal anode has superior electrical 
conductivity owing to its metallic properties and its lowest reduction 
potential (−3.04 V vs. the standard hydrogen electrode) increases the 
LIB energy density. Moreover, anode thickness can be reduced because 
of the high theoretical capacity (3862 mAh g−1) [22–24]. These merits 
endow LIBs with fast charging capability. However, Li metal has an 
exceedingly high reactivity, which causes the formation of an unstable 
SEI and spontaneous electrolyte decomposition [25–27]. As the reaction 
proceeds, dendrite growth issues cause battery degradation and sepa­
rator damage [28–30]. These disadvantages significantly reduce LIB 
reliability and safety. Thus, stabilized lithium metal powder (SLMP) has 
been developed to address the above challenges. 
SLMP maintains the properties of Li metal while having a different 
shape than that of Li foil. SLMP electrodes double the battery cycle life 
compared with that of Li-foil electrodes [31]. Additionally, SLMP elec­
trodes offer an extended surface area owing to their porosity, which 
reduces the local current density and mitigates volume changes during 
Li deposition and dissolution [32]. Therefore, SLMP is superior to Li foil 
as an anode material. However, the electrolyte can access the surface of 
the Cu substrate by penetration through SLMP voids. When two metals 
with different characteristics are in contact with each other in a corro­
sive medium, galvanic corrosion occurs. This process accompanies 
continuous Li dissolution and increases the overpotential. Consequently, 
deteriorated contact of Li with the current collector and the formation of 
dead Li decreases the reversibility of Li powder batteries. Furthermore, 
it is concerning for the use of multi-metallic composite electrodes in LIBs 
[33]. 
In this work, we propose Si–SLMP composite anodes (SiLP) as SLMP 
in powder form is advantageous for composite applications and stabi­
lizes Si. When SLMP and Si are mixed as the composite active material, 
Si anodes undergo partial lithiation without requiring further treatment 
[20]. Such prelithiated Si maximizes the contact of each Si particle with 
conductive active materials, even though the anode does not include 
conductive agents, such as carbon black, acetylene black, and nano­
carbons. This step secures adequate Li ion and electron pathways into Si 
and reduces the Li-ion binding energy with anodes compared to bare Si. 
The metallic properties of SLMP also improve Si conductivity. Therefore, 
SLMP can eliminate the need for conductive carbon additives. Addi­
tionally, using SLMP enhances the energy density of LIBs owing to the 
lowered anode potential. However, the lowered potential causes an 
easier decomposable electrolyte atmosphere during SEI formation. To 
stabilize the SEI, an additive for inducing lithium fluoride (LiF) was 
introduced. 
LiF is a highly stable SEI layer material owing to its high chemical 
stability, low solubility, and high modulus. LiF formation is naturally 
induced by the organic electrolyte and Li+. Therefore, LiF is formed 
using a fluorine (F)-rich material prior to electrolyte degradation. This 
step can minimize electrolyte and Li+ loss [34–36]. As a fluorinated 
polymer, 2-(perfluorooctyl) ethyl acrylate (PFOEA) can be used to 
fabricate an artificial protective SEI layer via ultraviolet (UV) curing. 
PFOEA has been used for stabilizing the surface of Li metal. Compared to 
bare Li foil, PFOEA-coated Li foil suppresses the volume expansion of Li 
E. Jang et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     

Journal of Power Sources 580 (2023) 233326
3
metal and obtains superior term capacity retention rates after 1000 
cycles [37]. In addition, the polymerization of PFOEA through UV 
curing acts as a binder to hold active material particles. 
In this research, we improved poor ICE and avoided capacity fading 
caused by electrolyte degradation by applying SLMP and PFOEA to Si 
anodes that typically face issues such as extreme volume change, 
incessant decay-restoration of the SEI layer, and low conductivity. 
2. Experimental 
2.1. Materials 
Si powder was received from the Korea Institute of Ceramic Engi­
neering and Technology. SLMP was obtained from FMC Specialty 
Chemicals (Zhangjiagang) Co. Ltd. 3-Aminopropyltriethoxysilane 
(APTES) and 2-Hydroxy-2-methypropiophenone (HMPP) were pur­
chased from Sigma-Aldrich. 2-(Perfluorooctyl) ethyl acrylate (PFOEA) 
was used as received. 
2.2. Optimization according to the ratio of Si and SLMP 
To optimize the composition of anodes, the experimental is con­
ducted with handling the ratio of Si and SLMP. The ratio corresponding 
to each sample is specified in Table S1. In the case of sample 1, which has 
higher ratio of Si than SLMP, the charge-discharge curves exhibit similar 
behavior to Si. This is judged that the enough prelithiation was not 
performed, which causes the low ICE of 47.4% (Fig. S1a). Also, sample 1 
presents remarkably lower capacity of 103.3 mAh g₋1 compared to the 
capacity of the original LFP. According to proceed the cycles, the 
discharge capacity increased until 10th cycle and reduced to 96 mAh g₋1. 
Therefore, the sample 1 was judged unsuitable from the aspect of cycle 
stability. Generally, the Si has four valence electrons so that a Si atom 
can bond with four Li atoms. Owing to the molecular weight of Li is 7 
and Si is 28, when the mass ratio of Si and SLMP is 1:1, a molar ratio of 
1:4 is established. Then sufficient prelithiation is possible. Conse­
quently, the sample 2, which has the same ratio of Si and SLMP, shows 
the stable charge-discharge reaction from the first cycle in Fig. 4d. When 
the lower ratio of Si than SLMP, the sample 3, the plateau appears long at 
the initial cycle and shows Li-like behavior rather than Si (Fig. S1b). The 
superior capacity of 160.2 mAh g₋1 is maintained until the 10th cycle, 
however, the capacity decreases rapidly in the subsequent cycles. While 
the concept of a composite electrode mixed with Si and Li metal powder 
can be explained as prelithiation for a Si anode, on the contrary, it can be 
explained that Si serves as a 3D matrix for stable cycle stability of Li 
metal, also. Therefore, excess Li metal occurs unstable state and exists 
the difficulty of retaining the uniform capacity. In addition, sample 3 has 
no significant difference compared to sample 2 in terms of capacity, due 
to the Si is already saturated with SLMP in a ratio of 1:1. As a result, the 
ratio of sample 2 that can perform stable charging and discharging 
through sufficient prelithiation while using less Li metal was adopted for 
economic benefits. 
2.3. Preparation of electrodes and electrochemical measurements 
Anode fabrication and CR2032 coin cell assembly were performed in 
a glove box filled with argon atmosphere to avoid air contact with 
moisture-sensitive materials. A schematic diagram of a SiLP fabrication 
is illustrated in Fig. S2. SiLP anodes were prepared as follows. First, 0.2 g 
of Si powder and 0.2 g of SLMP were premixed and then homogenized 
with 1 mol L−1 LiPF6 in ethylene carbonate (EC)/diethyl carbonate 
(DEC) (1:1 by volume) as a solvent and 5 wt% PFOEA was added to the 
mixture with 2 wt% HMPP as a photo-initiator. After adding APTES, 
which was 2 wt% of the total mixture, the slurry was stirred until 
completely dispersed while controlling polymer solidification by the 
heat generated during this step. Next, the slurry was cast onto a copper 
(Cu) substrate via tape-casting and the coated slurry was cured for 120 s 
using a UV lamp (365 nm) to crosslink the UV-curable polymer. Because 
the photo-initiator is very sensitive to UV irradiation, when the mixture 
of PFOEA and HMPP was irradiated with UV light, the photo-initiator, 
HMPP, was decomposed into free radicals. The free radicals react with 
the acrylate double bonds to initiate a photo crosslinking polymerization 
reaction. When the polymerization starts, a considerable amount of 
chain reaction proceeds by the reaction between the initiating species 
and the functional group of the monomer, and a strongly crosslinked 
polymer network is formed at the same time. In addition, the heat 
released instantaneously by the exothermic reaction and the resulting 
increase of the sample temperature contribute to more polymerization 
[38,39]. 
The preparation of LiFePO4 (LFP) and LiNi0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2 (NCM 
811) cathodes was performed in the next step. LFP (NCM 811), Super P, 
and poly(vinylidene difluoride) (PVdF) were mixed with N-methyl 
pyrrolidone (NMP) in a mass ratio of 8:1:1 (9:0.5:0.5), respectively. The 
homogeneous slurry was coated onto an aluminum (Al) substrate using a 
doctor blade and dried at 80 ◦C for 2 h in a convection oven. Then, the 
coated slurry was punched and dried at 80 ◦C overnight in a vacuum 
oven to eliminate moisture. The mass loading of the LFP is 3.26 mg cm−2 
and the NCM811 is 2.96 mg cm−2. 
For electrochemical measurements, CR2032 coin cells were assem­
bled with LFP (NCM 811) as the cathode, SiLP as the anode, and 70 μL of 
1 mol L−1 LiPF6 in EC/DEC (1:1 by volume) was used for the electrolyte. 
The applied separator was polyethylene (PE) dried at 25 ◦C overnight in 
a vacuum oven. Galvanostatic charge–discharge tests were performed at 
30 ◦C and Si/SiLP/Li vs. Li-ion overpotential measurements were con­
ducted using a BioLogic SP-200 potentiostat at 50 μA cm−2. 
2.4. Characterization 
Cross-sectional morphologies of the prelithiated Si electrodes were 
measured with a focused ion beam (FIB) and scanning electron micro­
scope (SEM) using a Hitachi NX5000 operated at a 5.0-kV accelerating 
voltage. The UV-curable polymer was coated onto electrodes to avoid 
direct contact of Li with the air. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) 
was performed to obtain elemental analysis of the Si–SLMP composite 
electrodes. The sample was quickly transferred to the observation 
chamber using a vacuum transfer holder owing to the high reactivity of 
Li. XPS spectroscopy was performed by etching an in-depth profile from 
0 to 29 min. A similar peak was observed even after 29 min, indicating 
that the deeper part of the electrode has a uniform elemental composi­
tion like that of the 29-min profile. All binding energies were referenced 
to the C–C peak at 284.6 eV. To characterize the crystal structure of SiLP, 
diffraction spectra were obtained from an X-ray diffractometer (XRD) 
using a Panalytical EMPYREAN from 10◦to 80◦. 
2.5. DFT simulation 
Density functional theory (DFT) calculations were performed with 
the VASP 5.4 package using the Projector Augment Wave (PAW) 
method. The RPBE function using generalized gradient approximation 
(GGA) was used to describe the exchange-correlation interaction. For 
structural optimization, a 4 × 4 × 1 k-point grid was used for in­
tegrations over the Brillouin zone and the supercell dimensions were 
11.6 × 25.47 × 16.41. The planewave cutoff energy was 500 eV at the 
reciprocal area, the smearing width was at 0.2 eV, and the self-consistent 
field (SCF) convergence was at 10−7 eV. Moreover, the plating and 
striping energy of Li atoms on the anode surface was obtained and suf­
ficient vacuum area on the anode surface was secured. The supercell 
used in the calculation calculated the interfacial energy of five major 
planes (111, 220, 311, 331, 400) based on XRD data. Then, the stability 
and Si-rich and Li–Si alloy surfaces were considered with the 220-plane 
applied for calculation. Finally, we investigated the plating and striping 
energy barriers of Li atoms on Li metal and Li–Si anode surfaces via 
nudged elastic band (NEB) calculations. NEB calculations were observed 
E. Jang et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     

Journal of Power Sources 580 (2023) 233326
4
by dividing 100 steps to closely observe the energy change according to 
the movement of the Li atom, which had a spring constant of 10−6 eV 
with a force-per-ion value lower than 0.02 eV with a convergence cri­
terion of 10−6 eV in energy. 
3. Results and discussion 
3.1. Configuration of Si–SLMP composite anodes 
To visually demonstrate the configuration of the SiLP composite 
anodes, a schematic representation is shown in scheme. 1a. The Si and 
SLMP particles are evenly distributed, and no carbon additive is present. 
The mechanism that forms the prelithiation of Si by SLMP and the stable 
LiF layer on the surface of the lithiated Si are described in scheme. 1b. 
Without the charging state, Si with the adjacent SLMP can propagate to 
the partially lithiated Si owing to the intrinsic potential difference be­
tween the materials. Additionally, the Si–Li composite, with a low po­
tential, is a strong electron donor and thus contributes to the breaking of 
the PFOEA C–F bond and provides sufficient F for LiF formation. 
Through these steps, a thin and stable LiF layer forms on the LixSi 
surface. 
3.2. Constitution analysis of LiF formation and prelithiation 
XPS analysis was conducted to verify the formation of prelithiated Si 
and LiF (Fig. 1). A pristine SiLP composite electrode with no external 
current was used as the analysis sample. The C 1s spectra include C–C/ 
C–H (284.6 eV), C–O (285.9 eV), C=O (288.1 eV), CF2 (290.4 eV), and 
CF3 (292.6 eV) peaks, which constitute the PFOEA (Fig. 1a) [40–42]. 
Among the peaks, the XPS spectra are calibrated based on the C–C/C–H 
peak at 284.6 eV CF2 (290.4 eV) and CF3 (292.6 eV) at 0 min indicate the 
existence of rich F donors from the PFOEA. The ingredients originating 
from the fluorinated polymer are identified in detail in the F 1s spectra 
(Fig. 1b). Although the CF3 peak largely appears at 0 min, the LiF peak 
emerges with increasing etching depth. As the electrode depth increases, 
the peak intensity of CFn gradually disappears in both C 1s and F 1s 
spectra, and LiF (685 eV) increases at the same etching time in the F 1s 
spectra [42]. These results show the evidence of the LiF formation by 
F–rich polymer. In this process, the major chemical reaction is proposed 
that the C–F bond was broken by contacting the metallic Li as a strong 
electron donor to form LiF. And then the electron transfers from another 
Li atom to the central carbon. Subsequently, the other functional groups 
are eliminated in the (CFn–CFn₋1)–Li+ and LiF is formed. This mechanism 
yields more LiF layer, due to the sufficient supplements of F and Li [37, 
43]. The Li 1s spectra in Fig. 1c also include the LiF peak at 56.49 eV 
[44]. Before the etching treatment, the largest peak is assigned to LiO 
(53.3 eV), which constitutes the native SEI; however, the peak intensity 
of LiF exceeds that of the other peaks [45]. Additionally, byproducts 
attributed to electrolyte degradation, such as lithium alkyl carbonates 
(ROCO2Li) and Li2CO3, are rarely observed. The appearance of other 
crucial peaks assigned to Li–Si (98.4 eV) and Li–Si–O (100.3, 101.5 eV) 
show that Si reacts with Li powder before inflicting any electric charge 
[46,47]. This component can be confirmed in the Si 2p spectra, and the 
Li–Si and Li–Si–O peaks prove the prelithiation of Si with application of 
SLMP [48,49]. 
In the Li 1s spectra, the intensity ratio of the lithiated peak changes 
because of the presence of other components. However, the Si 2p spectra 
have almost uniform peak intensity, regardless of etching depth. 
Therefore, the effect of fabrication by mixing Li powder with Si slurry, 
rather than treating the surface of the anode, is the appearance of 
lithiated Si throughout the anode depth. These XPS results agree with 
the process described in Scheme 1; the prelithiated Si mixed only with 
the Li powder. Furthermore, Fig. 1 shows that the F-rich polymer breaks 
CFn bonding and forms stable LiF bonding during prelithiation, without 
creating any byproducts. Fig. S3 shows the entire component ratio of the 
SiLP electrode depending on the etching time. On the surface of the 
electrode, the F component originated from an F-rich polymer is 
detected the most, and the ratio of Si and Li increases as the eching 
depth. To further clarify LiF formation in the SiLP composite anodes, 
energy dispersive X–ray spectroscopy (EDS) was conducted to distin­
guish the distribution of SiLP components (Fig. S4). Fig. S4a presents 
cross-sectional EDS images of a SiLP electrode that was cross-sectioned 
using FIB. The sample electrode is in a pristine state and without any 
charge. EDS analysis was executed in a vacuum atmosphere to avoid 
interference from other elements in the air. In the mapping image of 
specific elements, Si, oxygen (O), carbon (C), F, and phosphorus (P) are 
shown. Si and O form silicon oxide, and C and F originate from the 
fluorinated polymer. P is contained in the solvent used in manufacturing 
the electrode. The existence ratio of P is too low compared to other el­
ements in proportion at Fig. S4g. These results are consistent with the 
XPS results shown in Fig. 1. Additionally, the elements uniformly exist in 
the entire area of the electrode cross section, excluding voids generated 
Scheme 1. (a) Schematic representation showing the 
composition of Si-SLMP anodes. illustration of the 
anode surface. (b) The process of stable SEI formation 
on Si particles before charge/discharge: і) Illustrator 
of a Si particle surrounded with SLMP and PFOEA. ii) 
Before charge state, nearby Li metal particles and Si 
react to become a prelithiation state. As shown in 
image, the procedure accompanies some volume 
expansion. iii) Cross section of LixSi. It displays thin 
and stable SEI formation by fluorinated polymer.   
E. Jang et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     

Journal of Power Sources 580 (2023) 233326
5
by pores. This means that the SiLP anodes are sufficiently homogenized, 
regardless of the depth, and support the results demonstrated in Fig. 1. 
3.3. Morphological analysis of prelithiation 
To visually confirm LixSi formation, cross-sectional SEM images were 
obtained using a FIB, as shown in Fig. 2, and FIB samples were cast on a 
Cu substrate. Fig. 2a, b, and c show pristine, 1st charged, and discharged 
states of the bare Si||LFP full cell, respectively, and Fig. 2d, e, and f 
represent the pristine, 1st charged, and discharged states of the SiLP|| 
LFP full cell, respectively. The pristine sample was analyzed without 
applying charge and the 1st cycle of charged/discharged samples passed 
through the galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD) process at 0.1C (1C 
= 170 mAh g−1). GCD profiles of Si and SiLP full cells are provided as 
references (Figs. S5a and b). 
Bare Si has boundaries between each particle and signifies that the Si 
particles are in a separate state. This shows that the pathway of electrons 
and ions for fast charge transference was not formed properly (Fig. 2a). 
At the charged–discharged states of bare Si, more linking with particles 
near the pristine region is identified, however, the boundary does not 
disappear completely and retains the shape of the particle (Fig. 2b and 
c). As the anode has a much larger capacity than the cathode, a single 
charge–discharge cycle is insufficient to lithiate the whole anode. In 
contrast, in the pristine image of SiLP, the boundary between adjacent 
particles has vanished and they are observed as a connected mass 
(Fig. 2d). This mass is demonstrated in the lithiation of Si by SLMP, 
without any GCD reaction, and it facilitates the movement of charges 
because the connected active materials can make a pathway that 
transfers ions and electrons. 
The first charging step of SiLP proceeds by filling the pores that exist 
in the initial state (Fig. 2e). Thus, the discharging step of SiLP includes a 
process that regenerates the pores (Fig. 2f). The variation of lithiation 
Fig. 1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of the SiLP composite anodes in the pristine state and (a) C1s, (b) F1s, (c) Li1s and (d) Si2p spectra, 
respectively. 
Fig. 2. Visualizing the cross section of Si and SiLP anodes using SEM images. The Si images at (a) pristine, (b) first charge and (c) first discharge states. The SiLP 
images at (d) pristine, (e) first charge and (f) first discharge states. 
E. Jang et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     

Journal of Power Sources 580 (2023) 233326
6
mechanism was verified via charge/discharge profile of the electrode 
used in the analysis. When bare Si is charged for the first time, several 
stages of plateaus are observed and it follows the typical Si process with 
a steep slope, even during discharging (Fig. S5a). In the case of SiLP, 
although there are some humps, it shows an almost straight charging 
curve (Fig. S5b). In addition, the reaction plateau of SiLP is in a higher 
voltage range than that of bare Si. 
3.4. Polarization behavior of Si-SLMP composite anodes 
The importance of prelithiation is demonstrated in the voltage pro­
file during the initial Li+ deposition process on the electrode interphase. 
When Li ions bond with active materials in the electrode, lithiation 
mechanisms differentiate according to the material type. The differences 
in the reaction rates according to material properties are represented in 
Fig. 3. The samples used for polarization analysis were bare Si, SiLP 
composite, and Li metal plates acting as the working electrode (WE) and 
shown in Fig. 3 a, b, and c, respectively. The counter electrode (CE) was 
configured with Li metal as the Li+ source. The size of both electrodes is 
the same, with a radius of 14 mm, and the measurement was conducted 
at a current density of 50 μA cm−2. Li ions migrate from the CE to the WE 
and are then deposited on the surface of the WE due to the electric field. 
During this process, the different reaction properties (such as alloying or 
plating) are identified depending on the material. The lithiation process 
of Si is defined by a two-phase mechanism, which is divided as a Si 
reactant and an amorphous LixSi (a-LixSi, x ≈2.5) product. The 
boundary interface was formed between these two phases. During lith­
iation, the boundary moves down, and the visible interface disappears in 
the second step of lithiation, resulting in the final product of a-LixSi (x ≈
4.4). We define the phase of a-LixSi (x ≈2.5) as the Li-poor phase (LPP) 
and the phase of a-LixSi (x ≈4.4) as the Li-rich phase (LRP) [45,46]. 
Fig. 3a shows the lithiation profile of bare Si. The voltage decreases 
as a constant electric field from about 3.12 V (initial open circuit 
voltage) down to 0.08 V. Here, a two-phase mechanism is observed. The 
alloying reaction in which the Si reactant initially becomes LPP proceeds 
between 0 and 2 h. Then, the Li+ plating process on the LPP persists with 
a gentle slope. The continuous transformation of the LPP to the LRP 
progresses until the remaining a-Si is consumed. Contrary to Si, the 
voltage profile of a cell with Li metal plates as the WE is described in 
Fig. 3b. During the voltage-decreasing process, the extra step is not 
divided and the Li+ deposition process is maintained at −0.057 V. 
Additionally, the overpotential, which occurs after the voltage drop, 
is an important factor affecting the reaction rate. When Li+ is initially 
deposited on the surface of the WE, nucleation polarization occurs 
owing to the energy barrier from the surface. Nucleation polarization is 
a resistance component, which causes overvoltage. The larger the po­
larization, the higher the surface resistance in the reaction with Li+. In 
the Li metal plate with a flat surface, nucleation polarization occurs at 
0.060 V during the first deposition reaction. Fig. 3c shows the voltage 
profile of the SiLP composite anodes. As the SiLP was prelithiated using 
SLMP, the alloying process in which Si becomes LPP is invisible in the 
initial lithiation profile. SiLP is already in the LPP state and shows a 
sharp voltage decrease from the open-circuit voltage (OCV). Therefore, 
nucleation polarization occurs in bonding Li+ with lithiated Si. Although 
Li+ is most easily plated on Li metal plates, the overpotential of SiLP, 
which appears at 0.039 V, is lower than that of the Li metal plates (0.060 
V). This result can be explained by examining the effects of the larger 
SiLP surface area and the lithium silicide (LixSiOy) acting as an artificial 
layer on Li metal plates. The surface area of SiLP is larger than that of the 
Li metal foil because of its cavitized structure, which produces a large 
critical current density. Thus, the smaller areal current density of SiLP 
leads to lower polarization. 
As shown in Fig. 1d, lithium silicide was observed on the SiLP. In 
bare Li metal plates, surface features, such as pits, create a critical cur­
rent density at a specific point, thus, nucleation polarization becomes 
worse. When lithium silicide exists on the surface of Li metal plates, the 
overpotential is controllable because it prevents current density con­
centration in a specific spot [50]. After nucleation reaction, the region of 
not fully lithiated and the LPP forms a hump. This means a prelithiation 
process by SLMP exists, but it is not complete lithiation. Therefore, 
Fig. 3c does not show the lithiation curve observed in bare Si. Since 
forming an LRP, the electric potential of the SiLP lithiation profile is 
recuperated and stably maintained. When the graph in Fig. 3 is 
comprehensively interpreted, we deduce that SiLP exhibits similar 
behavior to that of the Li metal. In addition, the effects of Si can be 
observed in the overpotential and the hump is caused by the partly 
lithiated LPP. The prelithiation throughout the overall electrode in­
dicates the alloying process, wherein bare Si undergoes the first 
charging step, is excluded and the Li–Li plating reaction immediately 
occurs. This charging mechanism of the SiLP composite anodes enhances 
fast charging because the plating reaction is known to be faster than 
other reactions, such as intercalation, alloying, and conversion. This 
mechanism is proven in Fig. 3. 
3.5. Electrochemical performance in the full-cell test 
For application in a real battery system, the Si and SiLP anodes were 
assembled with cathodes. The mass loading of the anode electrodes used 
in this full-cell test is 1.15 mg cm−2 for Si and 1.17 mg cm−2 for SiLP. 
LiFePO4||SiLP full-cell experiments were conducted using LiFePO4 
(LFP), which is a stable cathode material, to exclude the influence of the 
cathode and test the improved performance of the composite anodes. 
Fig. 4 represents the experimental results; Fig. 4a shows the electro­
chemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) using Si and SiLP anodes, 
respectively. Compared to the Si anode, the EIS of SiLP indicates a lower 
Rct value and imaginary part. Rct refers to the impedance that occurs 
during ion exchange at the interface between the electrode and the 
electrolyte. A smaller Rct value for the SiLP electrode is interpreted as 
Fig. 3. Nucleation overpotential of the Li on Si, SiLP and Li metal at current density of 50 μA cm−2. (a) The first lithiation process of bare Si. (b) The Li plating process 
of Li metal surface. (c) The behavior of SiLP composite anodes, during the first lithiation process. 
E. Jang et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     

Journal of Power Sources 580 (2023) 233326
7
easier ion exchange. Additionally, the imaginary part of the capacitor is 
expressed by 
Z = 1
jωC  
where Z is the impedance, ω is the radial frequency, and C is the 
capacitance [51]. As Z is inversely related to C, the SiLP electrode, which 
has a smaller imaginary value, has a larger capacitance than the Si 
electrode. 
The electrochemical properties were verified in the cycle stability 
results, which were conducted at 0.1C (1C = 170 mAh g−1) and are 
shown in Fig. 4b. The initial capacities of Si and SiLP are 79 (N/P ratio of 
14.61 for Si) and 149 mAh g₋1 (N/P ratio of 14.8 for SiLP), respectively. 
These results demonstrate the effects of applying the SLMP with the Si 
electrode, such as improving the conductivity and the Li-ion affinity. 
The Si is in continuous capacity degradation; however, the SiLP main­
tains a stable capacity during the proceeding charge/discharge cycles. 
After 30 cycles, the different capacity decrease rates of the cells with the 
Si anode and SiLP composite anode are proven. Furthermore, an obvious 
ICE difference was also confirmed, with a 46% level for the Si anode and 
70% level for SiLP, which clearly shows improved ICE in the electrode 
prelithiated by SLMP. The same tendency is observed in the charge and 
discharge profiles (Fig. 4c and d). While Fig. 4c represents the lithiation/ 
delithiation curve of a typical Si anode, the behavior, especially the 
plateau voltage, of Fig. 4d wherein SILP anodes are used has a shape 
similar to that shown in Fig. S6b, which used Li metal. These results are 
the effect of Li powder on the prelithiated electrode. However, the initial 
charge–discharge curve displays a hump, which is the lithiation process 
of the remaining silicon particles owing to the incomplete prelithiation. 
Subsequently, the reaction plateau voltage of SiLP increases owing to 
the formation of an additional pathway in the electrode and has a 
gradually straight shape, like the profile of LFP/Li metal. The EIS is 
measured to compare the anodes state which was proceeded LFP full-cell 
test (Fig. S7). LFP cathode is very stable within operating voltage, thus 
variation of EIS is mainly affected by anode [52]. The case of Si anodes 
remarkably represents the difference of before and after cycle (Fig. S7a). 
Both Rct and imaginary Z values increased significantly. This result is 
interpreted that the resistance is larger due to the formation of SEI 
originated the continuous volume change during charging and dis­
charging. In contrast, SiLP anodes show a slight increase in resistance 
after the cycle progresses (Fig. S7b). These EIS data is explained that the 
SiLP anodes formed a more stable interphase than the Si anodes under 
the same cycle conditions. Because Si accompanies extreme volume 
changes during the cycles, the structure can be unstable after several 
cycles [53]. To exhibit the structure stability of SiLP anodes after several 
cycles test, the XRD analysis is performed (Fig. S8). In the pristine, the 
peak intensity of Cu foil, which is the current collector, was largely 
detected. So, the relatively low intensity peaks, such as the hump around 
10–30◦or Li2.33Si peak, which mean prelithiation, could not discovery 
[54,55]. However, more convincing hump and lithiation peaks could be 
confirmed after 30 cycles. In addition, maintaining the main peaks of the 
pristine presents that charge-discharge are proceeding with the stable 
structure in the electrode even after several cycles. Therefore, the SiLP 
anodes retain constant cycle capacities. 
These results were also observed in the experiment using a high-Ni 
cathode. A high-Ni cathode was used to increase the energy density of 
the battery and the full-cell test was conducted with LiNi0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2 
(NCM811) material at 0.1C (1C = 200 mAh g−1). The N/P ratio of Si is 
Fig. 4. Electrochemical performance of the LiFePO4 full cell batteries. EIS of the (a) Si and SiLP anodes. (b) The cycle capacity and Coulombic efficiency at 0.1C. 
Charge/discharge profiles of (c) Si, (d) SiLP. 
E. Jang et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     

Journal of Power Sources 580 (2023) 233326
8
13.7 and the SiLP is 13.87. For Si and SiLP full-cells, the GCD test was 
performed at almost the equal N/P ratio. The results are shown in Fig. 5. 
The NCM/Si is 78.1 mAh g−1 in the 5th cycle owing to continuous ca­
pacity fading. However, the NCM/SiLP maintains 185.8 mAh g−1 until 
the 30th cycle, which is more than double that with Si anodes (Fig. 5a 
and b). In the case of the NCM811/SiLP full-cell profile, the reaction 
plateau is located below at the first cycle and indicates the formation of 
the initial pathway due to the incomplete lithiation of the prelithiated 
electrode. After cycling, the plateau recovers and is maintained until 
reaching 30 cycles. Further, the NCM811 full cell using SiLP anodes 
showed a significantly higher plateau voltage range than did that with Si 
anodes. Fig. 5c shows the stability test at 0.1C. Compared to the poor ICE 
of the Si anodes, the SiLP anodes show an improved ICE and discharge 
retention rates of about 87% at 80 cycles. Therefore, the Si electrode 
with SLMP has significantly improved ICE because of prelithiation 
before the GCD reaction. It was proven through LFP and NCM full-cell 
tests that the SLMP showed lithium metal-like behavior, even after the 
first cycle, during charge–discharge. 
3.6. Binding energy calculations 
To investigate the interaction of Li+ with prelithiated Si in terms of 
binding energy, first principles calculations were employed. To obtain 
the structural models, XRD was conducted and two patterns of the 
pristine-state Si and SiLP represent lattice information were obtained 
(Fig. 6a). In the XRD analysis, the navy peaks indicate typical crystalline 
Si. The SiLP contains the crystalline peaks of Si, however, a hump is also 
observed around 20◦that indicates amorphous prelithiated Si [54]. The 
other differences in the SiLP spectrum compared to that of Si are the 
existence of Li peaks and a broad peak at about 33⁰, which manifests the 
alloying ratio of Li2.33Si. Further, the five representative peaks of Si are 
the (111), (220), (311), (400), and (331) planes, from left-to-right, in the 
spectrum. Among these planes, the major alloying reaction progresses at 
the place with the lowest interfacial energy. As a result, the energy of the 
Si single atom at the interface of the (400) plane has the lowest energy of 
4.855 eV. However, the peak intensity of the (400) plane is weak in the 
XRD data. Accordingly, (220) had the second lowest energy of 4.867 eV 
and was finally determined to be the likely Si structure, which was 
modelled for the calculation of the adsorption energy (Fig. S9). 
For the modelling of SiLP, a LixSi ratio of x = 2.33 was selected with 
reference to the XRD results and it was randomly configured. Therefore, 
the XRD analysis clarified the presence of prelithiated Si and indicated 
the appropriate simulation structure. When the Si anodes mix with Li, 
the force of Si atoms changes depending on the surrounding alloying Li 
atoms. To identify the chemical interactions between lithiated Si and Li 
atoms, the binding (adsorption) energy was computed via NEB calcu­
lations. The NEB path was designed to calculate the binding energy 
when the Li atom approaches and plates to the anode surface, and then 
includes the supercell stabilization process. The initial reaction co­
ordinates consist of a supercell that is modified to 4 × 4 × 1, the atomic 
structure introduced in Fig. 6b and c, and to the Li atom. After that, the 
energies between the host material and the Li atom increase as the Li 
atom gradually approach the anodes. The energy required for adsorption 
with the Si atom when the Li atom comes sufficiently close to the anode 
surface is called the activation energy. These steps were conducted on 
the electrode models of Si and SiLP. Consequently, the SiLP anodes 
exhibited a lower activation energy of 2.04 eV compared to that of the Si 
(2.121 eV). SiLP is more lithiophilic than bare Si, thus Li ion easily 
adsorb to SiLP comparing Si. The low chemisorption energy can be 
interpreted as the lowering of the lithiation barrier for alloying to SiLP. 
That means SiLP provide faster kinetics than Si by fast Li ion migration 
and diffusion. Thus, low binding energy can be helpful to fast redox of 
anodes. In order to prove the effect of the decreased binding energy, a 
rate capability test was performed on LFP full cells (Fig. S10). The LFP|| 
SiLP cell exhibited charge capacities of 163.5 mAh g₋1 at 0.2C, 152.1 
mAh g₋1 at 0.5C, 135.3 mAh g₋1 at 1C, 117.6 mAh g₋1 at 2C, and 98.1 
mAh g₋1 at 5C. In comparison, LFP||Si cell expressed charge capacities of 
40.0 mAh g₋1 at 0.2C, 25.6 mAh g₋1 at 0.5C, 15.1 mAh g₋1 at 1C, 7.3 mAh 
g₋1 at 2C, and 3.0 mAh g₋1 at 5C. For the charging state, higher capacity 
was constantly expressed in SiLP as an anode through all the rates tested. 
Even in terms of retention capability after high rate, SiLP shows a ca­
pacity of 161.6 mAh g₋1 at 0.2C, which is almost the same as the initial 
one, unlike Si (29.6 mAh g₋1 at 0.2C). Though the numerical binding 
energy difference does not seem large, comparing the actual rate data, 
the capacity difference is significant. Therefore, the major reaction of 
SiLP is easier than that for Si. After the highest energy step, the 
adsorption step, the energy decreases as the structure stabilizes. At the 
end of the stabilization process, the energy of SiLP remained higher than 
Si. Because the activation state of SiLP reducing the ΔE required to strip 
the Li atom, the discharge step progresses more actively than does that 
for Si anodes. The discharge capacities of the LFP||SiLP cell were 160.5 
mAh g₋1 at 0.2C, 147.9 mAh g₋1 at 0.5C, 132.3 mAh g₋1 at 1C, 113.0 mAh 
g₋1 at 2C, and 94.1 mAh g₋1 at 5C. In comparison, LFP||Si cell had 
discharge capacities of 38.0 mAh g₋1 at 0.2C, 24.7 mAh g₋1 at 0.5C, 14.4 
mAh g₋1 at 1C, 6.9 mAh g₋1 at 2C, and 2.8 mAh g₋1 at 5C. As a result, the 
DFT calculations proved that the binding energy demanded for charge- 
discharge decreases with the application of the SLMP composite to Si 
anodes. 
4. Conclusion 
In this work, we introduced a prelithiation operation for Si anodes, 
which have been recently highlighted for the development of next- 
generation LIBs. The SLMP additive in the slurry uniformly conducts 
lithiation of the overall active material in the electrode and does not 
cause locally excessive lithiation. In addition, the simple in situ process 
of applying a fluorinated polymer forms LiF in the whole electrode. This 
treatment effectively inhibited the formation of additional SEI compo­
nents during the prelithiation process. The results of full-cell tests 
demonstrated 
that 
the 
prelithiation 
affected 
charge–discharge 
Fig. 5. Electrochemical performance of the LiNi0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2 full cell batteries. Charge/discharge profiles of (a) Si and (b) SiLP anodes with LiNi0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2. 
(c) The cycle capacity and Coulombic efficiency at 0.1C. 
E. Jang et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     

Journal of Power Sources 580 (2023) 233326
9
characteristics. The reaction plateau voltage ranges were increased and 
the charge–discharge profile behavior was similar to that of Li metal in 
the cases of LFP and NCM/SiLP full cells. Moreover, a capacity retention 
rate after 80 cycles of 87% was achieved in the NCM/SiLP system. In 
particular, SiLP anodes showed a higher ICE of about 70% compared to 
Si anodes with an ICE of about 46%. Additionally, SiLP anodes were 
proven to exhibit easier chemisorption reaction than Si anodes through 
reduction of the binding energy, from 2.121 to 2.04 eV. We expect that 
this Si–SLMP composite method will contribute to the development of 
fast charging in EVs.

================================================================================
[SOURCE: Sol–gel nanoglues for an organic binder-free TiO 2 nanofiber anode for lithium ion batteries.pdf]
================================================================================
Sol–gel nanoglues for an organic binder-free TiO2
nanoﬁber anode for lithium ion batteries†
Junghyun Choi,‡a Sangkyu Lee,‡b Jaehwan Ha,b Taeseup Songb and Ungyu Paik*a
A TiO2 nanoﬁber anode is glued using a sol–gel reaction to improve
cycle performance and Coulombic eﬃciencies. The hydrolysis-
condensation process produces TiO2 nanoglues consisting of TiO2
nanoparticles, providing strong adhesion between nanoﬁbers and
the current collector as well as between nanoﬁbers, rendering
favourable transporting properties of electrons and Li ions in the TiO2
nanoﬁber electrode, and eventually leading to the excellent perfor-
mance of Li ion batteries exhibiting an excellent initial Coulombic
eﬃciency (85%), cycle performance up to 100 cycles, and high rate
capability (60% at 5 C).
TiO2 has been considered as an attractive material for use in a
lithium ion battery anode due to its low cost and excellent
structural stability during lithiation–delithiation processes.1,2
However, TiO2 has disadvantages of low Li ion diﬀusivity and
poor electrical conductivity, which limit its practical applica-
tion.3 To overcome these problems, 1D nanostructures such as
nanowires, nanobers, and nanotubes are adopted because
they provide eﬃcient electron pathways and short diﬀusion
length for lithium ions.3–6 Also, surface modication of elec-
trode materials5 and the use of hybrid electrode systems7 have
been proven to be eﬀective ways for improved Li ion diﬀusivity
and electrical conductivity. In a diﬀerent way, the structure of
electrode materials can aﬀect Li ion diﬀusivity and electrical
conductivity.8 The formation of a direct electrical contact
between electrode materials and the current collector improved
the electrochemical performance of the 1D nanostructured TiO2
electrode.8
A recent investigation showed that adhesion between elec-
trode materials and the current collector as well as between the
materials can aﬀect the electrochemical performance of Li ion
batteries.9 In general, to provide strong adhesion between these
components, an electrode is generally prepared by mixing active
materials with polymeric binders. Conducting additives such as
carbon black and graphene are also added to compensate for
low electrical conductivity of active materials.10 However, the
use of large amounts of processing additives decreases volu-
metric capacity of batteries because those are generally elec-
trochemically inactive materials.11 In addition, typical polymer
binders are insulating materials therefore they can block con-
ducting pathways between electrode materials and the current
collector as well as between electrode materials.
Here, we develop a novel method for producing Li ion
batteries with stable cycle performance without the addition of
processing additives that decrease volumetric capacity and
hinder the electrochemical reaction. We demonstrate the
fabrication of a 1D nanostructured TiO2 anode by using this
fabrication method. To fabricate the 1D nanostructure, elec-
trospinning is utilized in this study because it is an easy, cost-
eﬀective, and versatile methodology for generating 1D nano-
structures of various kinds of materials.12 The method proposed
here utilizes a general sol–gel route that is the hydrolysis–
condensation process of metal alkoxide. During the sol–gel
reaction, titanium alkoxide (Ti(OR)4) forms titanium oxide
networks (–Ti–O–Ti–) between the nanober structures.13 The
calcination process leads to the formation of particle-like metal
oxides, which binds chemically each nanober as well as
adheres nanobers on the current collector. In this work, we call
this unique structure “TiO2 sol–gel nanoglues”. The nanoglues
between nanobers provide additional electron conducting
pathways between nanobers and the current collector as well
as play a role in binding active materials and adhering them on
the current collector like polymeric binders. At the same time, it
can avoid the decrease in specic capacity and volumetric
capacity because the nanoglues consist of electrochemically
active materials, in this experiment TiO2. The glues can ll gap
spaces between the nanobers, strengthening contact proper-
ties of the whole electrode, and consequently lead to the stable
cycle performance with high initial Coulombic eﬃciency. The
aWCU Department of Energy Engineering, Hanyang University, Seoul 133-791, Korea
bDepartment of Materials Science and Engineering, Hanyang University, Seoul 133-
791, Korea. E-mail: upaik@hanyang.ac.kr

View Journal | View Issue

rate capability of a TiO2 nanober electrode with TiO2 sol–gel
nanoglues is also evaluated. The electrode exhibits highly stable
rate capability as a function of cycle number, maintaining an
excellent rate capability as high as a TiO2 nanober electrode.
These electrochemical performances prove that the nanoglue
system promotes adhesion between electrode materials and the
current collector without adding polymeric binders, and it also
provides constructive conducting pathways, leading to the
excellent stability in cycle performance. Such excellent cycle
performance is also observed even under the fast charging–
discharging conditions.
The
processing
steps
for
a
TiO2
nanober
electrode
strengthened with TiO2 sol–gel nanoglues are schematically
depicted in Fig. 1. First, 1 g of poly (vinyl acetate) (PVAc, Mw 
500 000, Sigma-Aldrich) was dissolved in 5 mL of N,N-dime-
thylformamide (DMF, 99.8%, Sigma-Aldrich). 0.75 g of titaniu-
m(IV) isopropoxide (97%, Sigma-Aldrich) and 0.3 g of acetic acid
(99.5%, Samchun Chemical) were added to the PVAc stock
solution, followed by stirring for at least 6 h. The prepared
solution was transferred to a syringe equipped with a metal
nozzle (18 G). Electrospinning was carried out on a stainless
steel foil (SUS 304, Nialco) (step 1). An electrical voltage of 15 kV
was applied at a distance of 15 cm. The electrospun nanobers
were hydrolysed for 6 h under ambient conditions. Aer
annealing at 450 C for 3 h, the TiO2 nanobers were obtained
(step 2). TiO2 sol–gel nanoglues were prepared using the same
precursor solution for TiO2 nanobers. The prepared precursor
solution was spin coated on the preformed TiO2 nanobers at
4000 rpm for 30 s (step 3). Aer hydrolysis followed by
annealing, a TiO2 nanober electrode strengthened by the
nanoglues was obtained (step 4). The resulting loading mass of
TiO2 nanobers and TiO2 nanobers strengthened with nano-
glues was 33.23 and 368.3 mg, respectively. The mass ratio of
TiO2 nanobers to nanoglues in the electrode was 0.1.
Fig. 2 shows the morphological information of the TiO2 elec-
trodes. A thin lm of the nanoglues was formed on the substrate
by spin-coating followed by annealing, and the morphology was
observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Fig. 2a shows
that TiO2 sol–gel nanoglues consist of both large nanoparticles
of 100 nm and ne ones under 50 nm. Fig. 2b shows the SEM
image of the directly electrospun TiO2 nanobers on the current
collector. The nanobers have an average diameter of 100 nm
with a continuous 1D structure. The cross-sectional SEM image
(Fig. 2c) of TiO2 nanober strengthened with nanoglues shows
that the nanoparticles are homogeneously coated on the surface
of the preformed TiO2 nanobers andll the spaces between TiO2
nanobers and the current collector. Therefore, the nanoglues
contribute to increase of the contact area of electrode materials
on the current collector as well as connecting nanobers. Fig. 2d
shows the X-ray diﬀraction (XRD) pattern of TiO2 nanobers with
TiO2 nanoglues. There are two obvious peaks at 25.25 and 43.40.
The peak observed at lower degree can be assigned to the (101)
plane of anatase TiO2 (JCPDS 21-1272). There are no any traceable
peaks corresponding to other crystal forms of TiO2 such as
brookite and rutile. The peak observed at higher degree comes
from the SUS current collector. Also, we compare the XRD
patterns of TiO2 nanostructures as shown in Fig. S1 (see ESI†) The
XRD patterns show that all TiO2 nanostructures have the same
crystal structure of anatase and the crystal structures of TiO2
nanobers were not altered by the application of TiO2 sol–gel
nanoglues.
To show the binder-like function of TiO2 sol–gel nanoglues,
the adhesion tests were conducted and the results are shown in
Fig. S2 (see ESI†). In general, the Scotch tape method is utilized
to observe the adhesion properties of a layer on a substrate.14 In
this study, a poly(dimethylsiloxane) (PDMS) elastomeric stamp
is used to apply the pressure homogeneously over the sample.
The samples were contacted with a PDMS stamp (pre-
polymer : curing agent ¼ 10 : 1 (w/w); SYLGARD 184 silicone
elastomer kit, Dow Corning) and vertical loading was applied by
placing diﬀerent balance weights of 50 g, 200 g, and 1 kg. Aer 1
min, PDMS was detached from the samples. The initial and
residual weights for TiO2 on the SUS foil were measured using a
Fig. 1
Schematic illustration of processing steps for a TiO2 nanoﬁber electrode
modiﬁed with TiO2 nanoglue.
Fig. 2
Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of (a) TiO2 glue, (b) TiO2
nanoﬁber. (c) Cross-sectional SEM image of TiO2 nanoﬁber connected with TiO2
glue, and (d) XRD patterns of the TiO2 glue and TiO2 nanoﬁber.
This journal is ª The Royal Society of Chemistry 2013
Nanoscale, 2013, 5, 3230–3234 | 3231
Communication
Nanoscale
Published on 05 March 2013. Downloaded by Gachon University on 1/28/2026 10:16:08 AM. 
View Article Online

microbalance (Sartorius SE2, resolution 0.1 mg, Sartorius,
Germany). In the case of nanobers, a great deal of nanobers is
detached, while the nanobers strengthened with nanoglues
strongly adhere to the substrate. The adhesion test reveals
that the nanoglues reinforce the adhesion strength of TiO2
nanober.
To investigate the inuence of nanoglues on the electro-
chemical performance of a TiO2 nanober electrode, two types
of TiO2 nanober electrodes were prepared according to the
presence of nanoglues. Both electrodes were assembled in an
argon lled glove box. The nanostructured TiO2 electrode was
used as a working electrode and pure lithium foil was used as
both reference and counter electrodes. 1.0 M LiPF6 in ethylene
carbonate-diethylene carbonate (EC/DEC, 1 : 1 vol%) was used
as an electrolyte. The electrochemical performance of the elec-
trodes was measured using a battery cycle tester TOSCAT 3100
(Toyo Systems). The electrodes were galvanostatically dis-
charged and charged in a voltage range 1–3 V vs. Li/Li+ at the
current rate of 0.2 C (1 C ¼ 200 mA g1).
Fig. 3a compares the curves for the rst discharge and charge
capacities of two diﬀerent TiO2 electrodes. All electrodes show
two plateaus at 1.77 and 1.89 V in the discharge and charge
curves, respectively, which correspond to the phase change
between TiO2 and lithiated TiO2 by the lithium insertion/
extraction.15–19 We conrmed these reaction voltages by cyclic
voltammetry (CV). The CV curves were obtained in the voltage
range between 3.0 V and 1.0 V at a scan rate of 0.2 mV s1.17 The
results are shown in Fig. S3 (see ESI†). Both electrodes exhibited
a cathodic peak at 1.7 V and an anodic peak at 2.0 V, which
corresponds well to the previously reported values for anatase
TiO2.18,19 The lithiation and delithiation processes for anatase
TiO2 can be expressed as the following equation:
TiO2 + xLi+ + xe 4 LixTiO2
where x is the amount of inserted Li ions (mole). Anatase TiO2
has a theoretical capacity of 335 mA h g1 (x ¼ 1).20 The TiO2
nanober electrode exhibits the rst discharge and charge
capacities of 351 (x ¼ 1.05) and 193 mA h g1 (x ¼ 0.57),
respectively. The capacity value of the rst discharge is larger
than theoretical capacity. A similar result was reported else-
where15 which may be due to the side eﬀect induced by the
interaction with an electrolyte.21 The electrode made of TiO2
nanobers strengthened with nanoglues delivers discharge and
charge capacities of 204 (x ¼ 0.61) and 174 mA h g1 (x ¼ 0.52),
respectively. Fig. 3b shows the capacity retention of both elec-
trodes as a function of cycle number up to 100 cycles; the values
of capacity retention were obtained from the cycle performance
results shown in Fig. S4 (see ESI†). For the TiO2 nanober
electrode, the capacity decreases steeply until 5 cycles and aer
that it decreases slowly, while the cycle performance is very
stable for TiO2 nanober with nanoglues over the whole
measured cycles. Also, the initial Coulombic eﬃciency for the
electrode composed of TiO2 nanobers and nanoglues is 84.7%
and the eﬃciency reaches a level >99% within 10 cycles (Fig. 3c).
In contrast , the TiO2 nanober electrode shows an initial
Coulombic eﬃciency of only 55% and the curve is uctuated.
Based on the results, it can be concluded that the presence of
TiO2 nanoglues in the nanober electrode leads to the improved
cycle performance and higher initial Coulombic eﬃciency.
The high initial Coulombic eﬃciency observed here would be
an advantage in the fabrication of a full cell that is practical
application.
The rate capability of the electrodes was also measured with
increasing current density from 0.2 C to 5 C as shown in Fig. 3d;
the values of capacity retention were obtained from the rate
capability results shown in Fig. S5 (see ESI†). The capacity
retention was calculated using each capacity value of the rst
cycle at 0.2 C. At the current rate of 5 C, TiO2 nanober-based
electrodes exhibit a capacity retention as high as 60%, irre-
spective of the presence of TiO2 nanoglues in the electrode. The
capacity of an electrode composed of TiO2 nanobers with
nanoglues is fully recovered when it was measured at 0.2 C
again. However, only 89% of initial capacity is retained for
TiO2 nanobers. This trend is similar to the results of cycle
performance that the capacity of the TiO2 nanober electrode
decreases as a function of cycle number. Conventional TiO2
anodes were prepared with 1D nanostructures, carbon con-
ducting agents, and polymer binders, which exhibited the
capacity retention of 25% at 5 C compared to the capacity
value of the initial current rate.5,8,22 This comparison clearly
shows that the nanoglue system oﬀers favorable kinetics asso-
ciated with electrons compared to the conventional electrode
system. Recently, a TiO2 anode prepared with hierarchical
spheres consisting of TiO2 nanosheets was reported to exhibit a
superior capacity retention exceeding 80% at 5 C.17 We expect
that this binder-free process utilizing sol–gel nanoglues can
produce high rate lithium ion batteries using such superior
electrode materials.
Also, we compared the volumetric capacities of both the
nanober electrode and nanober with the nanoglue electrode,
which are shown in Table S1 (see ESI†). The nanober with the
nanoglue electrode shows much higher volumetric capacity
than that of the nanober electrode since the nanoglues can ll
Fig. 3
Electrochemical performance of TiO2 batteries: (a) 1st charge–discharge
curves, (b) cycle performance, (c) Coulombic eﬃciency, and (d) rate capability.
3232 | Nanoscale, 2013, 5, 3230–3234
This journal is ª The Royal Society of Chemistry 2013
Nanoscale
Communication
Published on 05 March 2013. Downloaded by Gachon University on 1/28/2026 10:16:08 AM. 
View Article Online

the huge volume of vacancies generated by the stacked nano-
bers. The sol–gel nanoglues also have an advantage against the
conventional electrodes that were prepared by mixing electro-
chemically active materials with carbon black and the polymer
binder. The sol–gel nanoglues utilized in this demonstration
are active materials. However, polymer binders are generally
non-conductive and electrochemically inactive materials. In the
voltage range of TiO2 anode operation, carbon conducting
agents are also electrochemically inactive. As a result, the
presence of these conventional additives decreases the volu-
metric capacity of batteries.23 This result indicates that our
unique methodology using sol–gel nanoglues is benecial to
produce lithium ion batteries with high capacity density.
In order to interpret the electrochemical properties of the
electrodes, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was
conducted in the frequency range of 250 kHz to 10 mHz at an
excitation amplitude of 5 mV and the results are shown in Fig. 4.
Also, the equivalent circuit is presented to explain the electronic
interfacial parameters of the TiO2 electrode8 as shown in the
inset of Fig. 4. The Nyquist plots consist of a semicircle followed
by a linear line. In general, the x-intercept of the semicircle
corresponds to the Ohmic resistance between the electrolyte
and electrode. The semicircle corresponds to the polarization
resistance consisting of electrolyte transport resistance (RSEI) in
the solid electrolyte interface (SEI) layer and charge-transfer
resistance (Rct) of Li+ in the electrode material. The linear region
in the Nyquist plot is related to the diﬀusion of Li ions. Ohmic
resistances are 1.9 and 2.6 U for the nanober electrode and
nanober strengthened with the nanoglue electrode, respec-
tively. The TiO2 nanober electrode shows a high polarization
resistance of 400 U while the resistance signicantly decreases
below 100 U by applying TiO2 sol–gel glues. Low polarization
indicates generally the formation of a stable surface lm and an
eﬃcient charge transfer of Li ions and electrons. Also, it was
reported that the polarization resistance reects the contact
properties between electrode materials and the current collector
as well as between electrode materials.9 In this study, therefore,
the lowest polarization resistance for the nanober electrode
with nanoglues can be considered to be due to the increased
contact area of TiO2 and improved contact properties between
TiO2 nanobers and the current collector due to the presence of
nanoglues. For understanding the origin of kinetics further, the
galvanostatic
intermittent
titration
technique
(GITT)
was
utilized and the results are shown in Fig. S6 (see ESI†). Fig. S6
(see ESI†) shows the GITT voltage proles of both the TiO2
nanober electrode and TiO2 nanober with the nanoglue
electrode during the rst discharging and charging. From the
obtained results, internal resistance for both electrodes was
calculated as shown in Fig. S6c.† The nanober with the
nanoglue electrode shows nearly one order of magnitude lower
internal resistance than that of the nanober electrode, which
indicates much improved kinetics associated with lithium for
the nanober with the nanoglue electrode. This result is well
consistent with the lower polarization resistance obtained from
the EIS analysis. Therefore, it can be concluded that the utili-
zation of TiO2 sol–gel nanoglue improves the contact properties
of TiO2 nanobers and provides the additional conducting
pathways between TiO2 nanobers and the current collector,
which leads to the enhanced electrochemical performance of
TiO2 nanober-based Li ion batteries.
In summary, we investigate the application of TiO2 sol–gel
nanoglues for the high performance TiO2 nanober electrode
without
adding
processing
additives
including
polymeric
binders and conducting additives. The electrode prepared with
nanobers
strengthened
with
nanoglues
shows
excellent
cycling performance up to 100 cycles with an initial Coulombic
eﬃciency as high as 85.7%. Its electrochemical properties are
higher than those of the directly electrospun TiO2 nanober
electrode. Nanoglues compensate the low initial Coulombic
eﬃciency and unstable Coulombic eﬃciency with cycling that
were observed in the nanober electrode system. The adhesion
test reveals that the TiO2 sol–gel nanoglues strengthen the
adhesion strength of TiO2 nanobers. Thus, it can be concluded
that the TiO2 nanoglues provide enhanced contact properties of
the nanober electrode, which is key for achieving excellent
cycle performance with high initial Coulombic eﬃciency.
