Journal of Power Sources 506 (2021) 230158
Available online 13 June 2021
0378-7753/© 2021 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
Stable artificial solid electrolyte interphase with lithium selenide and 
lithium chloride for dendrite-free lithium metal anodes 
Dongsoo Lee a,1, Seho Sun a,1, Hyunjung Park b, Jeongheon Kim a, Keemin Park a, 
Insung Hwang a, Yongmin Jung a, Taeseup Song a,*, Ungyu Paik a,** 
a Department of Energy Engineering, Hanyang University, Seoul, 04763, South Korea 
b Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Chosun University, Gwangju, 61452, South Korea   
H I G H L I G H T S  
G R A P H I C A L  A B S T R A C T  
• An artificial SEI with Li2Se and LiCl was 
prepared by a solution coating method. 
• Dendrite-free Li plating and stable 
cycling were realized in Li symmetric 
cells. 
• 60% longer lifetime was realized in the 
modified Li || NCM–811 cell  
A R T I C L E  I N F O   
Keywords: 
Lithium metal anodes 
Artificial solid electrolyte interphase 
Lithium dendrite 
Lithium selenide 
Lithium chloride 
A B S T R A C T   
Li dendrite growth and corresponding parasitic reactions are inherent vulnerability of lithium metal anodes, 
limiting practical adoption. Here, we report a stable artificial solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer consisting of 
Li2Se and LiCl with high Li ion conducting and insulating properties fabricated through a facile and low-cost 
approach. The designed artificial SEI layer on Li blocks direct contact with the electrolyte and enables 
dendrite-free Li plating through the artificial SEI layer. With these benefits, Li anodes modified with an artificial 
SEI show dendrite-free Li plating behaviors with a lower porosity of 30% compared to that of bare Li (43%) at a 
high current density of 5.0 mA cm−2 and a high capacity of 40 mAh cm−2. In addition, modified anodes exhibit 
stable cyclability with lower overpotentials in Li symmetric cells operated at 1.0 mA cm−2 with 1.0 mAh cm−2 for 
400 cycles. The significant improvement in cycling performance over hundreds of cycles is achieved using 
LiCoO2 and LiFePO4 cathodes. Furthermore, higher average Coulombic efficiency of 96.4% is realized in a full 
cell consisting of a high-capacity Ni0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2 (3.5 mAh cm−2) and thin (50 μm) modified Li anodes 
compared to that of bare Li anodes (94.3%).   
1. Introduction 
Lithium metal has been regarded as the ultimate anode material for 
Li ion secondary batteries due to its high theoretical capacity of 3860 
mAh g−1 and low negative electrochemical redox potential of −3.04 V 
(vs. a standard hydrogen electrode) [1,2]. However, there are a variety 
of obstacles to practical implementation of Li metal anodes (LMAs) [3]. 
A solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) is formed on the surface of the active 
anode material and functions as a passivation layer on the electrode 
surface. However, the SEI layer is easily cracked due to large volume 
* Corresponding author. 
** Corresponding author. 
E-mail addresses: tssong@hanyang.ac.kr (T. Song), upaik@hanyang.ac.kr (U. Paik).   
1 These authors contributed equally to this work. 
Contents lists available at ScienceDirect 
Journal of Power Sources 
journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/jpowsour 
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpowsour.2021.230158 
Received 8 April 2021; Received in revised form 26 May 2021; Accepted 7 June 2021   

Journal of Power Sources 506 (2021) 230158
2
changes of the Li anode during the repeated Li plating and stripping 
process, resulting in non-uniform current flow at the Li/electrolyte 
interface that accelerates Li dendrite growth [4]. The Li dendrite with a 
branched structure induces continuous inhomogeneous Li deposition on 
the anode surface due to the “tip effect,” leading to formation of elec­
trochemically inactive dead Li [5,6]. This parasitic reaction causes 
continuous electrolyte consumption, irreversible morphology changes, 
and low Coulombic efficiency, resulting in poor cycling performance 
and serious safety issues [7]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a 
stable Li protection layer that can suppress Li dendrite growth, block 
direct contact with the electrolyte to reduce continuous electrolyte 
decomposition, and promote uniform and reversible surface reactions. 
A variety of approaches have been explored to protect the Li metal 
anode. It has been reported that Li nitride and halide compounds such as 
Li3N, LiF, and LiCl effectively act as a Li metal passivation material due 
to their high Li ion conductivity and insulating nature [8,9]. A LiF 
protection layer was formed by direct fluorination of Li using freon as a 
fluorine source, and a LiF/defluorinated polymer protection layer was 
formed by roll-pressing of polytetrafluoroethylene and Li metal, 
showing improved electrochemical performance and stable Li plating 
behavior [10–12]. A Cu/Li3N protection layer was prepared by a con­
version reaction between Cu3N and Li, and it showed enhanced cycla­
bility and rate capability for LMAs [13,14]. However, it is crucial to 
develop a facile, cost-effective, and scalable method for design of a Li 
metal protection layer as well as reliable performance for practical 
LMAs. 
To achieve high energy density of Li metal batteries (LMBs), it is 
necessary to employ layered oxide cathode materials, such as LiCoO2, 
Fig. 1. (a) Schematic illustration of the synthesis of modified Li via SeCl4 solution coating and the corresponding optical images. (b) SEM images of the top and cross- 
sectional views of modified Li. (c) XRD patterns of bare Li and modified Li. (d) XPS spectra of bare Li and modified Li. 
D. Lee et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      

Journal of Power Sources 506 (2021) 230158
3
and high-Ni layered oxide with a high capacity and high working po­
tential [15,16]. However, severe side reactions can occur in carbonate 
electrolytes compatible with intercalation-type cathode materials 
because of the increased mechanical vulnerability of the SEI layer 
compared to the oligomer type SEI attributed to DME/DOL based elec­
trolytes. Additionally, transition metal dissolution into liquid electrolyte 
could further exacerbate problems with the Li metal anode [17–19]. 
Therefore, it is crucial to develop a stable Li protection layer to operate 
in carbonate electrolytes. In this regard, we studied a novel candidate to 
build a stable Li protective layer formed via a facile chemistry route. 
Here, we report an artificial SEI layer consisting of Li2Se and LiCl by a 
facile solution coating method for dendrite-free LMA. Li2Se is a prom­
ising candidate to act as a stable Li protective layer because it is an 
insulating material and can play a role as a superionic conductor 
[20–22]. Li2Se and LiCl are conformally formed on the Li metal surface 
by a spontaneous reaction between metallic Li and selenium chloride 
solution, which is confirmed with scanning electron microscopy (SEM), 
X-ray diffraction (XRD), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) 
analyses. Li2Se and LiCl have similar chemical properties in terms of 
their high Li + ion conductivity and insulating nature, enabling homo­
geneous Li + ion transfer throughout the electrode and preventing Li 
dendrite growth. The modified Li with the Li2Se/LiCl artificial SEI layer 
exhibits uniform and dendrite-free Li plating behavior with a lower 
porosity (30%) than bare Li (43%), even when Li is plated at 5 mA cm−2 
for 8 h (~40 mAh cm−2). Furthermore, the modified Li anode shows 
excellent cycling performance at a current density of 1 mA cm−2 with a 
high capacity of 1 mAh cm−2 in a Li || Li symmetric test cell over 400 
cycles. The robustness of an artificial SEI layer was demonstrated in EIS 
results showing steady interfacial resistance different from the contin­
uously increasing interfacial resistance of bare Li. The stable electro­
chemical performances of the modified Li electrode are demonstrated in 
full cells prepared with LiCoO2 (LCO, 3.4 mAh cm−2) and LiFePO4 (LFP, 
2.5 mAh cm−2) that have practical areal capacities as cathodes. The 
modified Li || LCO cell shows stable cycle performance at a 0.5 C rate 
over 200 cycles, and the modified Li || LFP exhibits significantly 
improved cycle performance without capacity fading at a 1 C rate over 
400 cycles. More importantly, we demonstrate the feasibility of the 
modified Li using a high-capacity cathode material of Ni0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2 
(NCM–811, 3.5 mAh⋅cm−2) and a thin lithium metal electrode (50 μm) 
in a lean electrolyte condition (8 μL per 1 mAh of cathode). The modified 
Li || NCM–811 cell has a 60% longer lifetime and a higher average 
Coulombic efficiency of 96.4% at a 1 C rate compared to a bare Li || 
NCM–811 cell (94.3%). 
2. Experimental section 
Preparation of the modified Li: Modified Li was prepared in an argon- 
filled glove box. To prepare the coating solution, SeCl4 (Aldrich) was 
dissolved in tetrahydrofuran (THF, Aldrich) at a concentration of 0.2 M 
with continuous magnetic stirring in a glove box for 12 h. Then, 20 μL 
cm−2 of the coating solution was dropped onto the lithium metal foil 
(LITHSUN 200 μm, WELLCOS 50 μm) and dried in a glove box for 6 h at 
room temperature. The fast conversion reaction was verified with the 
color change. The orange-colored lithium foil was thoroughly rinsed 
with THF and turned dark gray. The modified Li foil was dried for 24 h at 
ambient temperature and punched into disks (14 mm in diameter) for 
electrochemical evaluation. 
Materials characterization: The morphology of the samples was 
observed using field-emission SEM (JEOL JSM07600F). The Li metal 
anodes after electrochemical cycling were washed using dimethyl car­
bonate (Aldrich) as a solvent before SEM analysis. XRD (Rigaku Smar­
tLab) was used to analyze the artificial SEI of the modified Li. The 
Fig. 2. Top-down and cross-sectional SEM images of (a,b) bare Li and (e,f) modified Li electrode surfaces after initial Li plating at 0.5 mA cm−2 with 1 mAh cm−2. 
Top-down and cross-sectional SEM images of (c,d) bare Li and (g,h) modified Li electrode surfaces after initial Li plating at 0.5 mA cm−2 with 1 mAh cm−2. (i) 
Schematic illustration of the Li plating behavior on the modified Li electrode. 
D. Lee et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      

Journal of Power Sources 506 (2021) 230158
4
valence state and components of the elements were determined with 
XPS (VG Microtech ESCA2000), and the binding energy was calibrated 
by referencing the C 1s peak to 284.8 eV. All samples were sealed in a 
glove box prior to analysis by each instrument. 
Electrochemical measurements: Battery performance was tested 
using a battery cycle tester (TOSCAT 3000, Toyo System, Tokyo, Japan). 
For the Li || Li symmetric cell test, 2032 round type coin cells were 
fabricated using Li and modified Li electrodes, 1.0 M LiPF6 in ethylene 
carbonate/diethyl carbonate (EC/DEC (1/1 vol%)) with 10% fluoro­
ethylene carbonate (FEC, PANAX StarLyte) as the electrolyte, and a 
polypropylene separator in the voltage window of −1.0 to +1.0 V. For 
full cell tests, cathodes were prepared by mixing active materials, 
polyvinylidene fluoride, and carbon black in a 96:2:2 ratio with N- 
methyl-2-pyrrolidone as a solvent. The areal mass loading of LiCoO2, 
LiFePO4, Li4Ti5O12, and Ni0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2 in the electrode was 21, 17, 
5, and 17 mg cm−2, respectively. To evaluate LiCoO2, LiFePO4, 
Li4Ti5O12 cells, the electrolyte of 12 μL per 1 mAh of cathode, and the Li 
electrode with the thickness of 200 μm were used. For the evaluation of 
Ni0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2 cells, the electrolyte of 8 μL per 1 mAh of cathode, and 
the Li electrode with the thickness of 50 μm were used. Electrochemical 
impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed with frequencies from 
0.01 to 100 kHz and an amplitude of 50 mV using a potentiostat 
(Autolab, PGSTAT – 72,637). 
Fig. 3. A comparison of the cycling stability of the Li || Li symmetric cells and their corresponding voltage hysteresis versus cycle number at (a,b) a current density of 
1.0 mA cm−2 with an areal capacity of 1 mAh cm−2, (c, d) a current density of 3.0 mA cm−2 with an areal capacity of 3 mAh cm−2, and (e,f) a current density of 5.0 
mA cm−2 with an areal capacity of 5 mAh cm−2. 
D. Lee et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      

Journal of Power Sources 506 (2021) 230158
5
3. Results and discussion 
The artificial SEI layer on the LMAs was formed by a simple solution 
coating method. To prepare the coating solution, SeCl4 was dissolved in 
THF at a concentration of 0.2 M with continuous magnetic stirring in a 
glove box for 12 h. The color of the solution changed from light green to 
light orange because SeCl4 forms a complex with THF (Fig. S1) [23,24]. 
The solution (20 μL cm−2) was dropped onto Li metal foil, dried for 6 h at 
room temperature, and rinsed thoroughly with THF to remove residuals 
and surface contaminants (Fig. 1a). During this process, the color change 
of the Li metal surface was optically monitored, as shown in the inset of 
Fig. 1a. The SeCl4 on the Li metal immediately undergoes a reduction 
reaction and forms a composite interphase layer of Li2Se and LiCl 
following equation (1) [25] 
6Li + SeCl4→Li2Se + 4LiCl
(1) 
An artificial SEI layer with a thickness of ~2 μm conformally covered 
the surface of the bare Li metal foil, as shown in Fig. 1b and Fig. S2. The 
XRD patterns of the artificial SEI layer proved that both Li2Se and LiCl 
were present in crystalline phases on the Li metal, with well-defined 
peaks of Li corresponding to (110), (200), and (211); peaks of Li2Se 
corresponding to (220) and (400); and peaks of LiCl corresponding to 
(111), (200), (220), and (311) (Fig. 1c) [25,26]. In addition, the 
composition of the artificial SEI was investigated with XPS analysis 
(Fig. 1d and Fig. S4). The generation of the Se and Cl related peaks was 
clearly observed, and the intensity of the O 1s peak noticeably 
decreased. The EDS analysis clearly confirmed the uniform formation of 
an artificial SEI layer composed of Li2Se and LiCl, showing uniform 
distribution of elemental Se and Cl, in which the lower intensities and 
atomic percentages of Se and Cl indicate that the thin artificial SEI layer 
was formed with the comparable amount with the native oxide. (Fig. S3) 
[27]. The above results confirmed that the composite artificial SEI layer 
consisting of Li2Se and LiCl uniformly covered the Li metal surface. The 
Li2Se and LiCl has high lattice energies, thus the artificial SEI showed no 
dissolution under aprotic carbonate electrolytes for 24 h (Fig. S5) [26, 
28] 
To verify the high energy density of the LMBs, the electrochemical 
properties of the modified Li anode were evaluated using carbonate 
electrolytes [29]. To monitor the Li plating behavior on the modified Li 
anode, Li of 1 mAh cm−2 was deposited with different current densities. 
When Li was deposited onto the bare Li anode at a current density of 0.5 
mA cm−2, severe Li dendrite growth was observed, and Li was 
non-uniformly deposited on the electrode surface (Fig. 2a and b). More 
severe irregular Li plating behavior was observed at a high current 
density of 1.0 mA cm−2 (Fig. 2c and d). The Li dendrite growth and 
non-uniform Li plating behavior caused a large volume change and 
increased the tortuosity during cycling of the LMBs [30,31]. The high 
tortuosity caused an increase in the Li ion diffusion path, which resulted 
in an increase in polarization. In contrast, when Li was deposited onto 
the modified Li anode at a current density of 0.5 mA cm−2, the plated Li 
surface was flat and smooth, and the artificial SEI was clearly observed 
on the surface of the modified Li, as shown in Fig. 2e and f. Even after Li 
plating with the high current density of 1.0 mA cm−2, the surface of the 
modified Li anode exhibited dense and planar morphologies, as shown 
in Fig. 2g and h. The clear differences in Li plating behaviors between 
the bare and modified Li anodes are shown in the cross-sectional SEM 
images, which reveal localized Li plating on the bare Li anode and 
uniform and dense Li plating on the modified Li anode. Fig. 2i sche­
matically illustrates the mechanism of dendrite-free Li plating onto the 
modified Li. Li ion transport through the electrical insulating and ionic 
conducting artificial SEI layer enables uniform deposition of Li on the Li 
metal anode. Furthermore, the conformally formed artificial SEI layer on 
the Li anode can effectively prevent direct contact between the elec­
trolyte and Li metal anode and assist homogeneous current flow [32,33] 
The electrochemical properties of the bare and modified Li elec­
trodes were evaluated in symmetric Li || Li cells using 1.0 M LiPF6 in EC/ 
DEC (1/1 vol%) with 10% FEC) as the electrolyte. Fig. 3 presents the 
voltage profiles as a function of time for the Li symmetric cells and their 
corresponding voltage hysteresis (overpotential). At a current density of 
1.0 mA cm−2 with an areal capacity of 1 mAh cm−2, the modified Li cell 
exhibits a stable cycling performance with relatively constant over­
potential of ~36.3 mV over 400 cycles (Fig. 3a and b). In contrast, the 
bare Li cell shows a drastic increase in overpotential after 50 cycles, and 
cell short-circuiting occurs near 100 cycles. The slightly higher over­
potential of the modified Li cell at earlier cycles results from the insu­
lating property of the artificial SEI layer. However, polarization of the 
modified Li cell becomes smaller than that of the bare Li cell after several 
cycles [34]. As current density and capacity increase, a more significant 
difference in the electrochemical properties of the symmetric cells is 
observed. The bare Li cell exhibits a short-circuit within several cycles at 
a current density of 3 mA cm−2 and a capacity of 3 mAh cm−2, showing 
unstable and dramatically increasing overpotentials (Fig. 3c and d). In 
contrast, the modified Li cell shows stable cycle performance over 80 
cycles with much lower overpotentials. At a high current density of 5 
mA cm−2 with a high capacity of 5 mAh cm−2 test, the bare Li cell does 
not work, but the modified Li cell exhibits stable cycling performance 
over 40 cycles with stable and relatively lower overpotentials (Fig. 3e 
and f). As the capacity is reduced to 1 mAh cm−2, the modified Li cell 
shows more stable cycling performance over 200 cycles even at high 
current densities of 3 and 5 mA cm−2, as shown in Fig. S6. To monitor 
Fig. 4. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of Li || Li symmetric 
cells using (a) bare Li and (b) modified Li before and after cycling. The cells 
were evaluated a current density of 1.0 mA cm−2 with an areal capacity of 1 
mAh cm−2. 
D. Lee et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      

Journal of Power Sources 506 (2021) 230158
6
the robustness of an artificial SEI, interfacial resistance (semicircle at 
high frequencies) of symmetric Li || Li cells was measured by EIS (Fig. 4) 
[35]. For the bare Li cell, the interfacial resistance was decreased from 
71.0 to 64.1 Ω due to the stabilization by SEI formation after 5 cycles. 
After that, it showed continuously increased interfacial resistance of 
76.4 and 85.4 Ω at 10 and 30 cycles, respectively. To clarify the different 
aspect in changes of the interfacial resistance, the surface morphologies 
were observed (Fig. S7). The increased resistance of the bare Li is 
attributed to the poor SEI nature caused by the continuous Li dendrite 
growth, dead Li formation, and thick SEI formation [36]. The modified 
Li cell showed higher interfacial resistance of 96.5 Ω compared to that of 
the bare Li cell before cycle due to the insulating nature of an artificial 
SEI layer. However, the artificial SEI layer consisting of Li2Se and LiCl 
was stabilized showing low interfacial resistance of 16.4 Ω and main­
tained the original morphology after 5 cycles, which proves the 
robustness of the artificial SEI layer of the modified Li. Furthermore, the 
modified Li cell showed consistent resistance upon cycling, which 
demonstrates the robustness of the artificial SEI layer. 
The continuous Li plating behavior was monitored at the high cur­
rent density of 5.0 mA cm−2, as shown in Fig. 5. Based on Sand’s model, 
the onset of the voltage profile suggested the evolution of Li dendrite 
growth due to the space charge caused by depletion of anions near the Li 
metal electrode surface [37,38]. The bare Li cell showed an unstable and 
fluctuating voltage profile during continuous Li plating and a clear onset 
voltage after 7 h (Fig. 5a). In contrast, the modified Li cell exhibited a 
smooth plateau during Li plating, and its onset voltage appeared after 8 
h when the other side of the Li (200 μm, ~40 mAh cm−2) was totally 
stripped. After the onset voltage appeared, we monitored the plated Li 
electrode surface through SEM analysis, as shown in Fig. 5b and c. The 
severe Li dendrite growth and porous, thick Li layer formation on the 
bare Li electrode was clearly observed. The thickness and porosity of the 
plated Li on bare Li was 350 μm and 43%, respectively, which far sur­
passed the theoretical thickness of Li (initial Li thickness of ~200 μm) 
due to the porous Li plating behavior (see the numerical calculation of 
the theoretical specific capacity and corresponding thickness in the 
Supporting information) [39]. The thick dead Li layer and porous sur­
face caused an increase in the Li diffusion path length because of the 
high tortuosity of the Li metal surface, which degraded the surface re­
action with high overpotential [40]. Furthermore, the poor Li plating 
behavior of the bare Li could cause continuous electrolyte consumption. 
Conversely, Li was densely plated with a thickness of 250 μm and 
porosity of 30% on the modified Li, showing a smooth and dense 
structure with dendrite-free morphology. The stable Li plating behavior 
on the modified Li not only improved the electrochemical properties, but 
also reduced the volume changes during continuous cycling, enabling 
stable and safe LMBs. 
To demonstrate the possibility of using the designed artificial SEI 
layer for LMBs, bare Li || LCO and modified Li || LCO full cells were 
Fig. 5. (a) Voltage profiles with continuous Li plating at a current density of 5 mA cm−2. The SEM images of the surface and cross-sectional morphologies of (b) bare 
Li and (c) modified Li electrodes after the continuous Li plating test in Li || Li symmetric cells. 
D. Lee et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      

Journal of Power Sources 506 (2021) 230158
7
Fig. 6. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy of (a) Li || LCO and (d) Li || LFP cells before cycling. Initial voltage profiles of (b) Li || LCO and (e) Li || LFP cells at 
0.1 C. Cycling performance of (c) Li || LCO cells at 0.5 C (1.7 mA cm−2) and (f) Li || LFP cells at 1 C (2.5 mA cm−2). 
D. Lee et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      

Journal of Power Sources 506 (2021) 230158
8
fabricated with cathodes with a high areal capacity of 3.4 mAh cm−2. 
The EIS results indicated that the artificial SEI with the electrical insu­
lating property resulted in an increase of the initial charge transfer 
resistance (RCT, the semicircle at high frequencies), as shown in Fig. 6a 
[41]. Bare Li || LCO and modified Li || LCO full cells show similar 
voltage profiles at the initial cycle, as shown in Fig. 6b. The cycling 
performance of both cells was evaluated at charge and discharge rates of 
0.5 C (Fig. 6c). The bare Li || LCO cell exhibited continuous capacity 
fading after 50 cycles and a drastic decrease in discharging capacity at 
~92 cycles due to the large overpotentials and poor Li plating behavior 
of the bare Li. However, the modified Li || LCO cell showed very stable 
cycle performance over 200 cycles without noticeable capacity fading. 
The bare Li || LCO cell showed a significant increase in overpotential 
with cycling (Fig. S8) [42]. To investigate the relationship between 
cycling performance and Li plating/stripping behaviors, the surface 
morphologies of the bare Li and the modified Li electrodes after cycling 
were observed (Fig. S9). A porous structure and Li dendrite growth were 
observed in the bare Li electrode (Fig. S9a,b). Furthermore, a significant 
dead Li layer was observed in the cross-sectional view of the bare Li 
(Fig. S9c). The development of a thick dead Li layer and the porous 
structure in the bare Li surface could cause a large overpotential induced 
by the long Li ion diffusion pathway and the electrolyte depletion 
resulting from continuous electrolyte decomposition during cycling 
[43]. Conversely, a flat and smooth surface morphology was observed in 
the modified Li electrode after cycling, indicating that the artificial SEI 
layer composed of Li2Se and LiCl can play a role in stabilizing the cycling 
performance due to its stable Li plating behavior with a dendrite-free 
morphology. 
The electrochemical properties of bare and modified Li || LFP full 
cells were evaluated to clarify the effect of the artificial SEI layer on the 
Li anode by minimizing the performance degradation from the cathode 
because LFP maintained a stable crystal structure during electro­
chemical reactions without structural degradation or transition metal 
dissolution (Fig. 6e and f) [44,45]. The modified Li || LFP full cell had a 
higher Rct compared to that of the bare Li || LFP full cell, which was 
attributed to the insulating nature of the artificial SEI layer before 
cycling (Fig. 6d). Initially, the bare and modified Li || LFP full cells 
exhibited similar voltage profiles at 0.1 C; however, significant differ­
ences in cycle performances were observed later, as shown in Fig. 6e and 
f. The modified Li || LFP full cell exhibited stable cycling performance 
without a noticeable increase in polarization at the high current density 
of 2 mA cm−2 (1 C) with a capacity of 2 mAh cm−2 over 400 cycles 
(Fig. 6f and Fig. S10). However, the discharge capacity of the cell 
employing the bare Li anode abruptly decreased after 50 cycles. The 
SEM images of the bare Li anode after cell testing revealed generation of 
a significant amount of dead Li with a porous structure (Fig. S11). 
Conversely, the modified Li anode after cycling had a dense and smooth 
surface morphology. When lithium titanate (Li4Ti5O12, LTO) was used as 
a cathode, the modified Li cell showed the outstandingly stable cycling 
performance at 2 mA cm−2 (4 C) for 2,000 cycles (Fig. S12 and S13) [46] 
We demonstrated the beneficial effects of the modified Li electrode 
using a variety of cathode materials with practical areal capacities. The 
stable cycling performance of the modified Li was due to the stable 
artificial SEI layer, which prevented contact with the liquid electrolyte 
and provided a uniform Li ion flux on the surface of the modified Li with 
a lower Li diffusion barrier energy. To explore the possibility of practical 
use of the designed artificial SEI layer on Li metal, the electrochemical 
properties of the full cell employing an NCM–811 cathode with a high 
Fig. 7. (a) Initial voltage profiles of Li || NCM cells at 0.1 C. (b) Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy of Li || NCM and modified Li || NCM cells before and after 
30 cycles. (c) Cycling performance of Li || NCM cells at 1.0 C (3.5 mA cm−2). Li metal foil of 50 μm and lean electrolyte of 8 μL per 1 mAh of cathode were employed 
for full cell evaluation. 
D. Lee et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      

Journal of Power Sources 506 (2021) 230158
9
energy density of 3.5 mAh cm−2 and a thin Li electrode of 50 μm were 
evaluated under a lean electrolyte condition of 8 μL per 1 mAh of 
cathode (Fig. 7). To increase the energy density of LMBs, the Li reservoir 
should be reduced. The LMBs show poor cycle performances with 
drastically decreased capacity at earlier cycles due to the finite amount 
of the Li in the anode compared to the cathode [47]. Both Li || NCM cells 
showed much higher initial discharge capacities of ~215 mAh g−1 
compared to cells with conventional cathode materials such as LCO and 
LFP, as shown in Fig. 7a. Although the cell employing the modified Li 
anode had higher RCT (43.50 Ω) due to the insulating property of the 
artificial SEI compared to that of the cell employing bare Li anode 
(34.86 Ω) before cycling (Fig. 7b and Table S1), this value decreased to 
21.91 Ω after 30 cycles, while that of the bare Li || NCM increased to 
36.87 Ω. The decrease could be attributed to the benefits of the artificial 
Li protection layer, including stable Li plating behavior with the dense 
and dendrite-free morphology. The cycling performance of both cells at 
charge and discharge current densities of 1 C was monitored (Fig. 7c). 
The bare Li || NCM cell exhibited a drastic decrease in discharge ca­
pacity due to Li depletion on the anode after 52 cycles. However, the 
modified Li || NCM cell showed longer cycling stability of more than 82 
cycles. The calculated average Coulombic efficiency of the modified Li 
was 96.4%, which was higher than that of the bare Li (94.3%) [12,48] 
4. Conclusions 
In summary, a modified Li electrode with a stable artificial SEI layer 
composed of Li2Se and LiCl was prepared by a simple solution treatment. 
The artificial SEI enabled the uniform Li ion flux at the interface between 
electrolyte and artificial SEI and blocked direct contact of Li metal with 
liquid electrolyte. With these beneficial attributes, the modified Li 
electrode exhibited stable Li plating behavior with a dense and dendrite- 
free morphology. Li symmetric cells employing the modified Li elec­
trodes presented stable cycling performances with low overpotentials. 
The robustness of the artificial SEI layer was demonstrated showing 
constant interfacial resistance in Li symmetric cell tests. The beneficial 
effects of the artificial SEI layer on the Li metal anode were demon­
strated using various cathode materials such as LCO, LFP, LTO, and NCM 
with carbonate electrolytes. The enhanced electrochemical properties of 
the cells employing the modified Li anode resulted from the stable 
artificial SEI layer, which improved the Coulombic efficiency by 
blocking direct contact of Li metal with the liquid electrolyte and 
guiding uniform Li ion flux on the surface of the modified Li without 
dendritic Li growth. Our findings open a new avenue for protecting Li 
metal anodes using an artificial SEI composed of Li2Se and LiCl to 
effectively stabilize the Li electrode for LMBs.

Styrene-butadiene rubber patterned current collector for improved Li-ion 
kinetics of the anode for high energy density lithium-ion batteries 
Keemin Park a, Hee Eun Yoo a,b, Yongmin Jung a, Myeungwoo Ryu a, Seungcheol Myeong a, 
Dongsoo Lee a, Soo Chan Kim b, Chanho Kim a, Jeongheon Kim a, Jiseok Kwon a, Kangchun Lee a, 
Chae-Woong Cho c,**, Ungyu Paik a,***, Taeseup Song a,d,* 
a Department of Energy Engineering, Hanyang University, 222 Wangsimni-ro, Seoul, 04763, Republic of Korea 
b Lab Electrode Development Group, Samsung SDI, 130 Samsung-ro, Suwon, 16678, Republic of Korea 
c GPE Process Development Team, Samsung SDI, 150 Gongse-ro, Giheung, 17084, Republic of Korea 
d Department of Battery Engineering, Hanyang University, 222 Wangsimni-ro, Seoul, 04763, Republic of Korea   
H I G H L I G H T S  
• Highly adhesive SBR patterned Cu (SPC) was prepared by facile bar-coating process. 
• SPC enables homogeneous SBR distribution in the anode. 
• SPC improves adhesion strength and Li-ion kinetics in the anode. 
• Enhanced adhesion strength reduces SBR content in the anode, improving kinetics. 
• The anode employing SPC exhibits improved electrochemical properties.  
A R T I C L E  I N F O   
Keywords: 
Styrene-butadiene rubber 
Binder migration 
Li-ion kinetics 
Adhesion strength 
High energy density 
A B S T R A C T   
The styrene-butadiene rubber (SBR) binder migration caused by the capillary force-driven solvent evaporation 
during the drying process results in the concentration gradient of SBR binder in the anode, which deteriorates the 
adhesion strength at the interface between the anode film and Cu current collector and Li-ion kinetics, respec­
tively. Here, we report SBR patterned Cu current collector (SPC) to overcome problems caused by the SBR binder 
migration behavior. Pre-patterned SBR strongly adheres to the Cu current collector, which suppresses its 
migration during the drying process. Homogeneous SBR distribution in the longitudinal direction of the anode 
improves the adhesion strength and Li-ion kinetics. Enhanced adhesion strength enables the reduction of the SBR 
content in the anode, which further improves Li-ion kinetics. The anode employing SPC exhibits improved 
constant current charging capacity retention and cycling stability compared to those of the anode with a pristine 
Cu current collector.   
1. Introduction 
With the increasing demand for electric vehicles, lithium-ion batte­
ries (LIBs) with high energy density is required [1]. Studies on LIBs with 
superior properties have been mainly focused on active materials. The 
importance of polymeric binders has been undervalued due to their low 
amounts in an electrode and their electrochemically non-reactive 
properties during cell operation [2,3]. Carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) 
and styrene-butadiene rubber (SBR) co-binder systems have been widely 
employed because of their environmental friendliness and facile engi­
neering in the rheological behavior of the slurry [4–8]. CMC adsorbed 
on active materials induces repulsive force between particles, which 
improves the dispersibility of the slurry [9]. The aromatic ring of SBR 
interacts with the Cu current collector providing adhesion strength 
* Corresponding author. Department of Energy Engineering, Hanyang University, 222 Wangsimni-ro, Seoul, 04763, Republic of Korea. 
** Corresponding author. 
*** Corresponding author. 
E-mail addresses: cw97.cho@samsung.com (C.-W. Cho), upaik@hanyang.ac.kr (U. Paik), tssong@hanyang.ac.kr (T. Song).  
Contents lists available at ScienceDirect 
Journal of Power Sources 
journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/jpowsour 
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpowsour.2023.233238 
Received 26 January 2023; Received in revised form 6 May 2023; Accepted 21 May 2023   

Journal of Power Sources 577 (2023) 233238
2
between the anode film and Cu current collector [10–12]. Therefore, 
SBR substantially acts as a binder in the CMC-SBR system, and CMC is 
the material for controlling the dispersibility and viscosity of the slurry. 
The SBR binder migrates to the surface of the anode due to the 
capillary force-driven solvent evaporation during the drying process, 
which reduces the adhesion strength between the anode film and Cu 
current collector due to the relatively small amount of SBR binder near 
the interface between the anode film and Cu current collector [13–16]. 
Moreover, the SBR binder concentrated on the surface of the anode 
degrades Li-ion kinetics associated with the tortuosity increase and 
corresponding electrochemical properties of LIBs by preventing facile 
transport of the electrolyte through the pores in the anode [17,18]. 
Significant efforts have been devoted to reducing the concentration of 
the SBR binder in the slurry as SBR binder not only degrades the elec­
trical conductivity due to its insulating property but also Li-ion kinetics 
in the anode [18–20]. However, as the areal capacity of the anode in­
creases, the SBR binder migration behavior is intensified due to a longer 
drying time for the complete evaporation of the solvent, which induces 
poor adhesion strength at the interface between the anode film and Cu 
current collector [21]. It is necessary to employ an additional SBR binder 
to meet the desired adhesion strength, which further deteriorates the 
electrochemical properties [10]. Therefore, an effective strategy is 
required to alleviate the SBR binder migration behavior during the 
drying process to achieve LIBs with high energy density. 
Various strategies for manipulating binder migration behavior dur­
ing the drying process have been explored to improve the electro­
chemical properties of LIBs [15,22,23]. Liu et al. introduced a slot-die 
coating process to fabricate the two-layered composite cathode. The 
two-layered cathode, where the bottom layer has the higher binder 
concentration than the top layer, showed superior binder distribution 
and electrochemical performances of LIBs [22]. Jaiser et al. alleviated 
binder migration and enhanced the electrochemical properties of LIBs 
by lowering the drying rate [23]. Although these approaches are effec­
tive, a facile method is necessary to realize LIBs with high energy den­
sity, maintaining productivity and reliability. 
Herein, we report a novel strategy to overcome problems caused by 
the SBR binder migration behavior by introducing SBR patterned Cu 
current collector (SPC) for LIBs with high energy density. SPC was 
prepared by a facile bar-coating process of SBR emulsion on the Cu 
current collector, which shows comparable electrical conductivity with 
a pristine Cu current collector by controlling the SBR coverage on the Cu 
current collector. Pre-patterned SBR adhered to the Cu current collector 
without migration behavior during the drying process induces homo­
geneous SBR distribution in the longitudinal direction of the anode, 
which enhances the adhesion strength and Li-ion kinetics by alleviating 
concentration gradient of SBR binder in the anode. Enhancement of 
adhesion strength with the SPC enables the reduction of the SBR content 
in the anode (Scheme 1(b)), which further improves Li-ion kinetics. The 
anode employing SPC exhibited excellent cycling stability (78.3% ca­
pacity retention for 100 cycles at 2.0C) compared to that of the pristine 
anode (64.3% capacity retention for 100 cycles at 2.0C). Furthermore, 
the anode employing SPC presented higher constant current charging 
capacity retention (61.4% at 2.0C versus 0.5C) than the pristine anode 
(54.4% at 2.0C versus 0.5C) with a high areal capacity of 5.5 mAh cm−2. 
2. Experimental 
2.1. Material characterization 
All chemicals were utilized as received without any additional pu­
rification. Sodium carboxymethyl cellulose (Na-CMC, Mw ~250,000, 
degree of substitution of 1.2), polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF, Mw 
~180,000), and N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP, 99.5%) were purchased 
from Sigma Aldrich (USA). Na-CMC dissolved in deionized water (DIW) 
at a concentration of 1.1 wt% and PVDF dissolved in NMP at a con­
centration of 8 wt% were prepared. Graphite, silicon-based nano­
composite, LiNi0.8Co0.15Al0.05O2 (NCA), carbon black and 40 wt% 
styrene-butadiene rubber (SBR) emulsion in DIW were provided by 
Samsung SDI Co., LTD (Republic of Korea). The silicon-based nano­
composite is a material in which silicon and graphite are composited at a 
weight ratio of 20:80. 
2.2. Anode fabrication with pristine Cu current collector 
Graphite, a silicon-based nanocomposite, 1.1 wt% aqueous CMC 
solution, and 40% SBR emulsion were set into the DIW with a weight 
ratio of 87.2:10.9:1.0:0.9 and 87.0:10.8:1.0:1.2 based on the solid-states 
for the slurry preparation of the anodes with areal capacities of 3.0 and 
5.5 mAh cm−2, respectively. The slurry was mixed using a thinky mixer 
at 2000 rpm for 10 min. The prepared slurry was coated on a pristine Cu 
Scheme 1. Schematic illustration of the anode configurations during the manufacturing process with (a) pristine and (b) SBR patterned Cu (SPC) anodes, 
respectively. 
K. Park et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    

Journal of Power Sources 577 (2023) 233238
3
current collector (thickness: 8 μm) using a doctor blade and dried at 
110 ◦C for 20 min. The loading weight of the anode film coated on the Cu 
current collector was 7 mg cm−2, and the amount of SBR per unit area 
was 63 μg cm−2 for the anode with 3.0 mAh cm−2 areal capacity. The 
loading weight of the anode film coated on the Cu current collector was 
13 mg cm−2, and the amount of SBR per unit area was 154 μg cm−2 for 
the anode with 5.5 mAh cm−2 areal capacity. To achieve a target density 
of 1.68 g cc−1, the prepared anode was calendered by a roll calendering 
machine after the drying process. 
2.3. Anode fabrication with SBR patterned Cu current collector (SPC) 
2.3.1. Fabrication of SPC 
The SPC was prepared by a facile bar-coating process using the SBR 
emulsion. The pristine Cu current collector was coated with an SBR 
amount of 7, 13, 27, 45, and 87 μg cm−2, respectively, and then dried in 
a 110 ◦C oven for 10 min. 
2.3.2. Anode fabrication 
The fabrication of the SPC anode was the same as that of the pristine 
anode with the areal capacity of 5.5 mAh cm−2, except that the prepared 
anode slurry was cast on the SPC, not pristine Cu current collector. For 
the preparation of the anode, depending on the amount of SBR in the 
slurry, the SBR composition in the original slurry (1.2 wt%) was varied 
at 0.23, 0.60, and 0.96 wt%. The amount of SBR per unit area was 30, 
77, and 123 μg cm−2, respectively, in the SBR composition of 0.23, 0.60, 
and 0.96 wt%. 
2.4. Cathode fabrication 
NCA, PVDF, and carbon black were mixed at 2000 rpm for 10 min in 
a thinky mixer at a weight ratio of 97.5:1.25:1.25. The prepared cathode 
slurry was coated on Al current collector (thickness: 10 μm) with a 
doctor blade and dried at 130 ◦C for 20 min. The loading weight of the 
cathode film on the Al current collector was 14 and 26 mg cm−2 for the 
cathodes with an areal capacity of 2.7 and 5.0 mAh cm−2, respectively. 
The prepared cathodes were calendered to achieve a target density of 
4.0 g cc−1. 
2.5. Characterizations of the prepared anode 
To investigate the binder distribution in the anode, the surface and 
interfacial cutting analysis system (SAICAS) was used to scratch the 
anode every 15 μm from the surface to the current collector. The 
adhesion strengths of the prepared anode were evaluated by a 180o peel 
test using a universal testing machine (AGS-J, Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). 
The source measure unit (Keithley 2400, Tektronix, United States of 
America) was used to measure the electrical resistances of the anodes 
along the thru-plane direction. The top view images of the Cu current 
collector, depending on the amount of SBR on the Cu current collector, 
were observed by a field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE- 
SEM, JSM-7600F, JEOL, Tokyo, Japan). For the tests of electrolyte 
permeability, an electrolyte 20 mg (1.5 M LiPF6 in ethylene carbonate/ 
dimethyl carbonate/diethyl carbonate with fluoroethylene carbonate 
additive (EC/DMC/DEC: 1/1/2 vol% with 10 wt% FEC, PANAX Star­
Lyte)) was used. After dropping the electrolyte on the surface of the 
anode, the time needed for the electrolyte to penetrate the anode 
completely was measured. 
2.6. Electrochemical measurements 
For the fabrication of the lithium-ion batteries (LIBs), all electrodes 
were dried at 120 ◦C for 4 h in a vacuum oven. Coin half-cells (2032 
round type) were fabricated using lithium metal (thickness: 200 μm) as a 
counter electrode and the prepared anode as a working electrode. The 
coin full-cells were fabricated using NCA as a cathode depending on 
areal capacities of 2.7 and 5.0 mAh cm−2, respectively, and the prepared 
anode ensuring that the N/P ratio was 1.1. (discharge capacity of NCA is 
200 mAh g−1, and discharge capacity of graphite and silicon-based 
nanocomposite mixture is 443 mAh g−1). The formation cycle of the 
full-cells was conducted on constant current-constant voltage (CC-CV) 
charging and constant current (CC) discharging modes with a voltage 
range of 2.5–4.2 V at a current density of 0.1C. 1.5 M LiPF6 in ethylene 
carbonate/dimethyl carbonate/diethyl carbonate with fluoroethylene 
carbonate additive (EC/DMC/DEC: 1/1/2 vol% with 10 wt% FEC, 
PANAX StarLyte) was selected as an electrolyte, and a polypropylene 
(PP) separator was used. Electrochemical measurements of the full-cells 
fabricated using NCA as a cathode depending on areal capacities of 2.7 
and 5.0 mAh cm−2 with an N/P ratio of 1.1 were conducted using a 
battery cycle tester (TOSCAT 3000, Toyo System, Tokyo, Japan). Elec­
trochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis was conducted at 
the open-circuit voltage of each cell with a frequency range from 100 
kHz to 1 Hz and a 20 mV amplitude using a potentiostat (ZIVE BP2A, 
Won-A tech, Korea). The ionic resistances of the anodes were analyzed 
through EIS measurements of the anode symmetric coin cells. 
3. Results and discussion 
To explore the relationship between the areal capacity of the elec­
trode (electrode thickness) and their physicochemical properties, the 
anodes with different areal capacities of 3.0 and 5.5 mAh cm−2 were 
prepared. The anode with the areal capacity of 3.0 mAh cm−2 exhibited 
the adhesion strength of 1.25 gf mm−1 under the SBR composition of 0.9 
wt% in the anode (Fig. 1(a)). For the anode with the areal capacity of 5.5 
mAh cm−2, 1.2 wt% of SBR in the anode was required to exhibit com­
parable adhesion strength (1.21 gf mm−1) to the anode with the areal 
capacity of 3.0 mAh cm−2. As the areal capacity of the anode increases, 
the SBR binder migration becomes more severe due to the thickness 
increment in the anode [23]. Accordingly, the adhesion strength be­
tween the anode film and Cu current collector is reduced due to the lack 
of SBR binder near the interface between the anode film and Cu current 
collector, requiring additional SBR in the anode. The increased amount 
of SBR significantly degrades the Li-ion kinetics in the anode [24]. 
Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis of the anode 
symmetric cells was conducted to elucidate the electrolyte transport 
behavior through the pores in the anode. In Fig. S1, as the areal capacity 
increased from 3.0 to 5.5 mAh cm−2, the Rion increased from 7.77 to 
16.75 Ω. To analyze the Li-ion transport behavior through the pores in 
the anode regardless of the anode thickness, the MacMullin number 
(NM) was calculated as follows [25]; 
NM = Rion • A • σ0
d
(1) 
Here, Rion is the ionic resistance (Ω), A is the electrode area (cm2), σo 
is the ionic conductivity of the electrolyte (S cm−1), and d is electrode 
thickness (μm). The large value of NM indicates the poor Li-ion kinetics 
in the anode [25]. For the anodes with areal capacities of 3.0 and 5.5 
mAh cm−2, the parameters for the calculation of NM are specified in 
Table S1. As shown in Fig. 1(b), the anode with 5.5 mAh cm−2 areal 
capacity has the NM of 27.1, which is 17% higher than that of the anode 
with 3.0 mAh cm−2 areal capacity (NM: 23.9). To analyze the effect of 
NM on the electrochemical properties depending on the areal capacity of 
the anodes, a galvanostatic charge/discharge test for the anodes with 
areal capacities of 3.0 and 5.5 mAh cm−2 was performed. Fig. 1(c) shows 
the voltage profiles of the anode half-cells at a current density of 2.0C 
after the 0.1C formation cycle. The end condition of 2.0C discharging 
(lithiation) patterns in the half-cell test was set as a voltage range of 
−0.5 V and as their areal capacities. The anode with the areal capacity of 
3.0 mAh cm−2 exhibited superior discharging capacity (235 mAh g−1 up 
to a voltage of 0 V) compared to that of the anode with the areal capacity 
of 5.5 mAh cm−2 (66 mAh g−1 up to a voltage of 0 V), which is attributed 
the low NM (Fig. 1(c)). Fig. 1(d) shows the differential capacity versus 
K. Park et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    

Journal of Power Sources 577 (2023) 233238
4
voltage (-dQ/dV vs. V) curve for the anodes with different areal ca­
pacities of 3.0 and 5.5 mAh cm−2 at discharging rate of 2.0C. The peaks 
above 0 V are related to the Li-ion intercalation into the graphite ma­
terials (Fig. 1(d)) [26,27]. The anode with the areal capacity of 3.0 mAh 
cm−2 clearly showed these peaks above 0 V, indicating successful Li-ion 
intercalation into the graphite materials. In contrast, the anode with the 
areal capacity of 5.5 mAh cm−2 exhibits no peaks above 0 V and a 
lithium precipitation peak at −0.06 V due to the poor Li-ion kinetics 
caused by the high NM. As the anode becomes thicker to increase areal 
capacity, the adhesion strength between the anode film and Cu current 
collector decreases, which is ascribed to the small amount of SBR near 
the interface between the anode film and Cu current collector, resulting 
from severe SBR binder migration in the thick anode [16]. To 
compensate for this low adhesion strength, the introduction of a large 
amount of SBR is necessary, which significantly deteriorates Li-ion ki­
netics associated with the large tortuosity and corresponding electro­
chemical properties of LIBs [28]. Therefore, a strategy to improve Li-ion 
kinetics with desirable adhesion strength in the high areal capacity 
anode is necessary for achieving LIBs with high energy density. 
Scheme 1(a) shows the drying process in the conventional 
manufacturing process of the anode. As the SBR mainly contributes to 
the adhesion strength between the anode film and Cu current collector, 
only SBR particles are described as a binder in Scheme 1. Due to the SBR 
binder migration behavior, additional SBR is required in the anode with 
the high areal capacity to compensate for binder deficiency near the 
interface between the anode film and Cu current collector. Scheme 1(b) 
exhibits a strategy to reduce the amount of SBR in the anode to improve 
Li-ion kinetics with desirable adhesion strength. Highly adhesive SPC 
was prepared by a facile bar-coating process of SBR emulsion on the Cu 
current collector. The prepared SPC shows comparable electrical con­
ductivity with a pristine Cu current collector by manipulating the SBR 
coverage on Cu current collector. The Cu current collector has a native 
oxide layer of copper (I) and (II) formed on the surface in air conditions. 
The π electrons of the aromatic benzene ring in SBR could form cation-π 
complexes by binding with copper cation, which prevents the SBR 
adhered to the Cu current collector from migrating to the anode surface 
in the subsequent electrode manufacturing process [12,29–31], 
inducing homogeneous SBR distribution in the longitudinal direction of 
the anode. Homogeneous SBR distribution improves the adhesion 
strength and Li-ion kinetics by alleviating the concentration gradient of 
SBR binder in the anode. As large SBR contents on the anode surface 
cause degradation of Li-ion kinetics due to low electrolyte wettability at 
the interface between the anode and the separator, mitigation of binder 
migration could improve Li-ion kinetics [24,28]. Moreover, enhanced 
adhesion strength enables the reduction of the insulating SBR content in 
the anode, which further improves Li-ion kinetics [28,32]. 
The optimal SBR concentration for the SPC fabrication was investi­
gated by the evaluation of the physicochemical properties of the SPC 
depending on the SBR coverage on the Cu current collector. Fig. 2(a) 
show schematic illustrations of the anodes depending on the SBR 
coverage on the Cu current collector. If SBR covers a large area of Cu 
current collector, the adhesion strength between the anode film and Cu 
current collector can be improved. However, the insulating SBR hinders 
facile electrical conduction between the anode film and Cu current 
collector, resulting in low electrical conductivity of the anode (Case III). 
On the other hand, although the small coverage of SBR on the Cu current 
Fig. 1. Study on the physical properties of the anodes in terms of areal capacity. (a) Adhesion strengths measured by a 180◦peel test. (b) MacMullin number (NM) of 
the anodes. (c) Voltage profiles of the anode half-cells at a current density of 2.0C. (d) differential capacity vs. voltage (-dQ/dV vs. V) curves from the discharging 
curves of (c). 
K. Park et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    

Journal of Power Sources 577 (2023) 233238
5
collector has good electrical conductivity, the enhancement of the 
adhesion strength is insufficient (Case I). Therefore, it is required to 
optimize the SBR concentration on the Cu current collector for the SPC 
fabrication with enhanced adhesion strength and desirable electrical 
conductivity (Case II). Fig. 2(b) presents the back scattered electron- 
scanning electron microscope (BSE-SEM) images of Cu current collec­
tor covered with different amounts of SBR. As the amount of SBR 
increased, the Cu current collector was gradually covered with SBR (the 
black regions indicate SBR). The anode was prepared depending on the 
amount of SBR on the Cu current collector from 0 to 45 μg cm−2 to 
compare the adhesion strength and electrical resistance (SBR in the 
slurry is maintained at 154 μg cm−2). As the amount of SBR on the Cu 
current collector increased, the adhesion strength was improved and 
saturated when SBR on the Cu current collector reached 27 μg cm−2 
(Fig. 2(c)). The electrical resistance sharply increased when SBR on the 
Cu current collector reached 45 μg cm−2. Because a decrease in the 
electrical conductivity (increase in the electrical resistance) eventually 
leads to the deterioration of electrochemical properties in LIBs [33], the 
condition of 27 μg cm−2, which exhibits comparable electrical resistance 
and improved adhesion strength compared to pristine Cu current col­
lector, was selected as an appropriate amount of SBR on the Cu for the 
preparation of SPC. The NM and adhesion strength were evaluated 
depending on the amount of SBR in the slurry under the condition of 27 
μg cm−2 SBR on the Cu (Fig. 2(d), Fig. S2 and Table S2). As the amount 
of SBR in the slurry decreases from 154 to 30 μg cm−2, the NM is 
improved, which is attributed to a facile movement of the Li-ion through 
the pores in the anode due to reduced SBR in the slurry [28]. On the 
other hand, the adhesion strength was degraded, caused by the lower 
amounts of SBR in the slurry. Significantly, the anode film with 30 μg 
cm−2 SBR in the slurry was pulverized from the Cu current collector 
after the calendering process resulting from the low adhesion strength 
(Fig. 2(d) and Fig. S3). Based on the above results, the anode was pre­
pared with 27 μg cm−2 SBR on the Cu and 77 μg cm−2 SBR in the slurry; 
hereafter, this anode is referred to as the “SPC anode”. The pristine 
anode was prepared using pristine Cu current collector (without SBR on 
the Cu current collector), and the amount of SBR in the slurry was 154 
Fig. 2. (a) Schematic illustrations of the anodes depending on the SBR coverage on the Cu. (b) BSE-SEM images of Cu according to the amount of SBR on the Cu 
(scale bar: 10 μm). (c) Adhesion strengths and electrical resistances of the anodes with 0–45 μg cm−2 SBR on the Cu (SBR in the slurry: 154 μg cm−2). (d) NM and 
adhesion strengths of the anodes depending on the amount of SBR in the slurry (amount of SBR on the Cu: 27 μg cm−2). 
K. Park et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    

Journal of Power Sources 577 (2023) 233238
6
μg cm−2. 
The physicochemical properties of the pristine and SPC anodes were 
evaluated to investigate the effectiveness of the SPC introduction. As 
shown in Fig. S4, the SPC anode exhibited similar electrical resistance to 
the pristine anode. Compared with the pristine anode, the SPC anode 
showed reduced total SBR in the anode (the amount of SBR on the Cu 
and in the slurry) by more than 30%, from 154 to 104 μg cm−2. 
Nevertheless, the SPC anode exhibits the comparable adhesion strength 
(1.19 gf mm−1) with that of the pristine anode (1.21 gf mm−1) due to the 
highly adhesive properties of SPC (Fig. 3(a)). To obtain the binder dis­
tribution in the longitudinal direction of the pristine and SPC anodes, the 
peel strength was measured by scratching the anode every 15 μm from 
the surface to the current collector (Fig. S5) [34]. The top layer of both 
anodes at a depth of 15 μm presented the highest peel strength, which 
can be explained by the binder migration towards the surface of the 
anode during the drying process [35]. The peel strength in the bottom 
layer of the pristine anode at a depth of 60 μm decreased by 25% 
compared to the top layer. However, in the case of the SPC anode, only a 
12% decrease was observed. The homogeneous SBR distribution with 
the introduction of SPC and improved adhesion strength enabled the 
reduction of the SBR content in the anode. The cross-sectional BSE-SEM 
images of pristine and SPC anodes with areal capacities of 3.0 and 5.5 
mAh cm−2 were observed, respectively (Fig. S6). Because of the high 
anode density (1.68 g cc−1), the significant difference in the micro­
structure between pristine and SPC anode could not be verified on the 
SEM image. Moreover, it was challenging to identify the SBR distribu­
tion due to the small content of SBR in the anode. However, despite the 
low content of SBR compared to the pristine anode, the interface be­
tween the anode film and the Cu current collector was well-retained in 
the SPC anode. Notably, the SPC anode shows improved NM compared to 
that of the pristine anode, which is ascribed to reduced total SBR in the 
anode, enhancing Li-ion kinetics through the pores (Fig. 3(b), Fig. S7 
and Table S3). Furthermore, the SPC anode has superior electrolyte 
permeability than the pristine anode, which might be attributed to the 
facile transport of electrolytes through the pores in the anode surface 
due to the reduced total amounts of SBR (Fig. S8) [18]. The electro­
chemical properties of the pristine and SPC anodes with the areal ca­
pacity of 5.5 mAh cm−2 were tested via a coin half-cell test. Fig. S9 
shows the voltage profiles of the pristine and SPC anodes for the first 
0.1C cycle in the voltage window of 0.01–1.2 V vs. Li/Li+. The charging 
(delithiation) capacity of the SPC anode was 444.0 mAh g−1 with a 
coulombic efficiency of 89.3%, which is almost the same as that of the 
pristine anode of 440.1 mAh g−1 with a coulombic efficiency of 89.3% 
due to the low current density of 0.1C. The SPC anode revealed a low 
overpotential compared to that of the pristine anode during discharging 
at a high current density of 2.0C (Fig. 3(c)). To analyze the discharging 
behaviors of the pristine and SPC anodes at a high discharge rate of 2.0C, 
-dQ/dV vs. V curves were analyzed (Fig. 3(d)). Although the peaks of 
Li-ion intercalation into the graphite materials did not appear clearly 
due to the high discharge rate of 2.0C, compared to the pristine anode, a 
low overpotential and high intensity were observed in the SPC anode, 
indicating the enhancement of the electrochemical properties during 
2.0C discharging. For the pristine and SPC anodes, the electrochemical 
impedance spectra (EIS) of the coin half-cells were evaluated after the 
0.1C formation cycle (Fig. S10). The SPC anode exhibits a lower resis­
tance of solution (Rs) and charge transfer resistance (Rct) compared to 
those of the pristine anode. As the adhesion strength and electrical 
resistance between pristine and SPC anodes are comparable, the 
Fig. 3. (a) Adhesion strengths and (b) NM values of the anodes. (c) Voltage profiles at a current density of 2.0C in the half-cell test, and (d) differential capacity vs. 
voltage (-dQ/dV vs. V) curves from the discharging curves of (c). 
K. Park et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    

Journal of Power Sources 577 (2023) 233238
7
enhanced discharging property of the SPC anode at a high current 
density of 2.0C is not due to the improvement in mechanical properties 
and the electrical conductivity of the anode. Instead, the improved 
electrochemical properties are attributed to the enhancement of Li-ion 
kinetics with reduced SBR in the anode [28]. 
To evaluate the electrochemical properties, the full-cells were 
fabricated with LiNi0.8Co0.15Al0.05O2 (NCA) cathode and prepared 
anode depending on the areal capacities of 3.0 and 5.5 mAh cm−2, 
where the N/P ratio was 1.1. The charging rate capability test was 
conducted up to 2.0C charging rate, holding the discharging rate of 0.5C 
after the 0.1C formation cycle (Fig. 4). The charging capacity was 
considered only in the constant current (CC) region because the 
graphite-based anode shows no significant difference when charged in 
the constant current and constant voltage (CC-CV) condition [2]. At the 
areal capacity of 3.0 mAh cm−2, the SPC anode showed CC charging 
capacities of 159.6, 136.6, and 107.2 mAh g−1 at 0.5, 1.0, and 2.0C 
charging rates, respectively. For the pristine anode, the CC charging 
capacities was 158.3, 134.0, and 104.5 mAh g−1, at 0.5, 1.0, and 2.0C 
charging rates, respectively (Fig. 4(a) and Fig. S11). Although the SPC 
anode exhibited slightly higher CC charging capacities compared to 
those of the pristine anode, there is no significant difference. On the 
other hand, the SPC anode showed higher CC charging capacities up to 
2.0C than those of the pristine anode. The capacity difference is more 
significant with increased charging C-rate at the high areal capacity of 
5.5 mAh cm−2 (Fig. 4(b) and Fig. S12). The SPC anode showed superior 
charging properties at the high areal capacity and C-rate due to the 
excellent Li-ion transport, which substantiates that the SPC anode is 
suitable for high energy density LIBs [36]. 
The cycling performances of the pristine and SPC anodes with 
different areal capacities were monitored at various C-rates under con­
stant current-constant voltage (CC-CV) charging and constant current 
(CC) discharging modes with a voltage range of 2.5–4.2 V after the 0.1C 
formation cycle. The pristine and SPC anodes with the areal capacity of 
3.0 mAh cm−2 showed identical capacity retention of 91.5% at a 0.5C 
rate for 50 cycles, as shown in Fig. 5(a). The pristine and SPC anodes 
with the areal capacity of 5.5 mAh cm−2 exhibited similar capacity re­
tentions of 88.2 and 89.0% at 0.5C rate for 50 cycles, respectively, due to 
the facile Li-ion transport in the low C-rate condition (Fig. 5(b)). On the 
other hand, the difference in cycling performances between pristine and 
SPC anodes could be clearly observed at the high current density of 2.0C, 
as the Li-ion transport property is more important at a high C-rate [37]. 
At a high current density of 2.0C, the SPC anode with the areal capacity 
of 3.0 mAh cm−2 exhibited improved capacity retention of 84.2% 
compared to that of the pristine anode (80%) for 100 cycles (Fig. 5(c)). 
The cycle performances for pristine and SPC anodes with the areal ca­
pacity of 5.5 mAh cm−2 were also monitored at 2.0C for 100 cycles 
(Fig. 5(d)). The SPC anode showed fast capacity fading in earlier cycles 
and capacity retention of 82.7% at 40 cycles. However, the SPC anode 
exhibited the stable cycling performance after 40 cycles and the capacity 
retention of 78.3% after 100 cycles. On the other hand, the pristine 
anode showed continuous capacity fading and capacity retention of 
69.3% at 40 cycles and 64.3% at 100 cycles. The average charge and 
discharge voltages and their hysteresis (ΔE) of the full-cells consisting of 
NCA cathode and 5.5 mAh cm−2 anode were obtained at 2.0C. The 
average charge and discharge voltages of SPC anode showed relatively 
linear behavior compared to the pristine anode, with a low average 
charge and high average discharge voltages (Fig. S13(a)). As a result, the 
average voltage differences between charge and discharge of SPC anode 
were small compared to those of the pristine anode (Fig. S13(b)). The 
introduction of SPC anode results in the reduced resistance of the cell 
after formation cycle, which influences the difference in average charge 
and discharge voltages during subsequent cycling [38–40]. Therefore, 
the low resistance associated with enhanced Li-ion kinetics by the 
introduction of SPC anode enables its superior electrochemical proper­
ties compared to the pristine anode. These results demonstrate that the 
Li-ion kinetics could be improved by both the uniform distribution of the 
SBR and the reduction in SBR content, which leads to the enhancement 
in the electrochemical properties of the anode with the high areal ca­
pacity of 5.5 mAh cm−2 even under the high current density operation. 
4. Conclusions 
The Li-ion kinetics could be significantly improved by employing 
SBR patterned Cu (SPC) current collector, which makes SBR fixed near 
the interface between the anode film and Cu current collector without 
being migrated to the surface of the anode during the drying process. 
Homogeneous SBR distribution in the longitudinal direction of the 
anode enhances the adhesion strength and Li-ion kinetics by alleviating 
the concentration gradient of SBR binder in the anode. Enhancement of 
adhesion strength with the SPC enables the reduction of the SBR content 
in the anode, which further improves Li-ion kinetics. The SPC anode 
with high areal capacity exhibited highly enhanced electrochemical 
properties, such as low overpotential during lithiation, good rate capa­
bilities in a charging state, and stable cycle performances.
