0169-4332/© 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
Full Length Article 
Synergistically enhanced LiF–rich protective layer for highly stable 
silicon anodes 
Dongsoo Lee a,1, Seungho Lee b,c,1, Dae Soo Jung c, Kwang Chul Roh c, Jihoon Seo d, 
Junghwan Kim b,c, Kwanghyun Kim b,c, Patrick Joohyun Kim b,*, Junghyun Choi a,c,* 
a School of Chemical, Biological and Battery Engineering, Gachon University, Seongnam-si, Gyeonggi-do 13120, the Republic of Korea 
b Department of Applied Chemistry, Kyungpook National University, Daegu 41566, the Republic of Korea 
c Energy Storage Materials Center, Korea Institute of Ceramic Engineering and Technology, Jinju, 52851, the Republic of Korea 
d Department of Chemical & Biomolecular Eng, Clarkson University, Potsdam, NY 13699, USA   
A R T I C L E  I N F O   
Keywords: 
Lithium fluoride 
Solid electrolyte interphase 
Silicon anode 
Lithium ion battery 
Protective layer 
A B S T R A C T   
Silicon (Si) is considered as one of the most promising anode materials with an extremely high specific capacity 
(3579 mAh/g), which is beyond the limit of conventional graphite anodes in lithium (Li) ion batteries (LIBs). 
However, large volume changes during the lithiation/delithiation process and formation of an unstable solid 
electrolyte interface (SEI) layer hinder the practical application of Si anodes. To address these issues, con­
structing a stable protective layer at the interface between anode and electrolyte is a desirable strategy. In this 
study, a LiF-rich SEI inducing protective layer (LPL) comprising aluminum fluoride (AlF3) and poly(acrylic acid) 
(PAA) is introduced onto Si anode to construct a stable LiF–rich SEI layer and mitigate the volume changes of the 
Si anodes during cycling. Owing to the synergetic effects of AlF3 and PAA, a LiF-rich SEI layer with robust 
physicochemical properties is uniformly formed on the Si anode. As a result, the LPL coated Si (LPL@Si) anodes 
exhibit outstanding electrochemical properties in Li metal cell tests. In addition, a full-cell prepared with the 
LPL@Si anode and LiNi0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2 as a cathode exhibits an excellent cycling performance and mitigated 
volume changes, demonstrating the potential of this strategy to protect the Si anodes for the development of 
high-energy–density LIBs.   
1. Introduction 
The rapid growth of the electric vehicle (EV) market has prompted an 
urgent need for lithium (Li) ion batteries (LIBs) with high energy density 
to ensure a satisfactory mileage [1–4]. Unfortunately, conventional LIBs 
employing graphite anodes have reached their theoretical energy den­
sity, which cannot meet the requirements of long-range EVs [5–8]. 
Therefore, it is necessary to develop a new battery chemistry beyond the 
limits of graphite anodes [9–11]. In this respect, silicon (Si) is regarded 
as one of the most promising anode materials for next-generation LIBs 
due to its ultrahigh theoretical specific capacity of 3579 mAh g−1 
(Li15Si4), low reaction potential (~0.4 V vs. Li/Li+), cost-effectiveness, 
and earth abundance [12–17]. However, the practical implementation 
of Si anodes is still challenging due to the severe volume change of up to 
300 % occurring during the lithiation/delithiation process, which causes 
electrode pulverization, formation of an unstable solid electrolyte 
interphase (SEI) layer, and uncontrollable electrolyte decomposition 
[18–21]. 
The SEI layer is generated via reduction of electrolyte components at 
the interface between the anode and the electrolyte during the initial 
charge process, showing a mosaic structure mainly consisting of organic 
and inorganic components [22–27]. Typically, the organic SEI compo­
nents are derived from the decomposition of carbonate electrolytes, 
showing low Li ion conductivity with a porous and undesirable structure 
[28–30]. Conversely, the inorganic SEI components, such as Li3N and 
LiF, are regarded as ideal SEI components due to their high Li ion con­
ductivity and excellent electrochemical stability against the electrolyte 
[31]. An ideal SEI layer should act a role of passivation film at the 
interface between the anode and electrolyte, preventing direct contact 
between active materials and the electrolyte. However, the substantial 
* Corresponding authors at: School of Chemical, Biological and Battery Engineering, Gachon University, Seongnam-si, Gyeonggi-do 13120, the Republic of Korea 
(J. Choi). 
E-mail addresses: pjkim@knu.ac.kr (P. Joohyun Kim), junghchoi@gachon.ac.kr (J. Choi).   
1 These authors contributed equally to this work. 
Contents lists available at ScienceDirect 
Applied Surface Science 
journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/apsusc 
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2024.160023 
Received 21 February 2024; Received in revised form 25 March 2024; Accepted 30 March 2024   

Applied Surface Science 661 (2024) 160023
2
volume fluctuations of the Si anode during the lithiation/delithiation 
process cause the pulverization of Si active materials and destruction of 
the SEI layer, which cannot play its protective role, resulting in 
continuous electrolyte decomposition and subsequent reformation of 
the SEI layer. This repetitive formation of the SEI layer increases the 
electrode resistance, which is associated with poor Li ion kinetics and 
depletion of reversible Li ions in the liquid electrolyte [32–35]. There­
fore, developing a stable and effective SEI layer is of paramount 
importance for ensuring a stable cycling performance of Si anodes. 
Research efforts toward addressing these issues have shown that LiF 
is the most important SEI component to construct a stable SEI layer on Si 
anodes [36–38]. Since LiF exhibits high Li ion conductivity and 
outstanding mechanical and electrochemical stability, achieving a LiF- 
rich SEI layer is crucial to improve the electrochemical performances 
of Si anodes. Over the past decades, various approaches have been 
proposed to construct a LiF-rich SEI layer [38,39]. In particular, fluo­
roethylene carbonate (FEC) has been widely employed as an electrolyte 
additive to form the LiF-rich SEI layer on the Si anodes [40]. However, 
the FEC is continuously consumed over extended cycling, resulting in a 
rapid capacity fading of Si anodes once the additive is exhausted [41]. 
Another approach involves forming a LiF coating layer on the Si anode 
using an electron-beam evaporator [42]. The LiF coating layer induces 
the formation of the LiF-rich SEI layer during the first lithiation process, 
thereby stabilizing the SEI. However, without sufficient mechanical 
properties, the SEI layer could be ruptured under the volumetric stress of 
the Si anode during cycling, leading to continuous electrolyte decom­
position and electrode degradation. Therefore, it is necessary to develop 
a stable LiF-rich SEI layer with robust physicochemical properties. 
Herein, we report a new strategy for the construction of a stable and 
robust LiF-rich SEI layer employing Si anodes coated with a LiF-rich SEI 
inducing protective layer (LPL) consisting of aluminum fluoride (AlF3) 
and poly(acrylic acid) (PAA) (LPL@Si). The designed LiF-rich SEI layer 
on the LPL@Si exhibits high Li ion conductivity as well as sufficient 
physicochemical and electrochemical stability. In the LPL@Si, AlF3 in­
duces the formation of the stable LiF-rich SEI layer with high Li ion 
conductivity and the polymeric network structure of PAA provides 
enhanced physicochemical properties [32,43]. Furthermore, the PAA in 
the LPL also plays a crucial role as a SEI stabilizer at the initial cycle, 
boosting the formation of the LiF-rich SEI layer due to the abundant 
carboxyl groups leading to fast reduction of Li salts in the electrolyte 
[44,45]. In addition, PAA can be partially bridged with the native oxide 
of Si by the hydrogen bonding, stabilizing the SEI layer. Due to these 
advantages, the LPL@Si anode exhibited an excellent cycle performance 
with the capacity retention of 75 % after 100 cycles at 0.5 C–rate 
compared to that of the bare Si anode (7.4 %) in Li metal cell tests. 
Additionally, the LPL@Si anode showed an outstanding electrochemical 
performance in a full-cell test with LiNi0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2 (NCM811) as a 
cathode with a practical areal capacity. These results demonstrate that 
this strategy for designing a stable LiF-rich SEI layer would pave the way 
for the practical use of Si anodes in high-energy–density LIBs. 
2. Experimental 
2.1. Preparation of bare Si and LPL@Si anodes 
To prepare the bare Si anode, Si powder (Nanostructured & Amor­
phous Materials), super P (Wellcos corporation), and 10 wt% Li–PAA 
aqueous binder were mixed at a mass ratio of 8:1:1. The Li–PAA binder 
was synthesized by mixing a PAA aqueous solution (average Mv =
450,000, Sigma-Aldrich) and a LiOH aqueous solution (Mw = 41.97 g 
mol−1, 99.0 %, SAMCHUN). The bare Si slurry was mixed using a 
planetary mixer (ARE-310, THINKY) for 5 min at 2000 rpm. The fully 
dispersed bare Si slurry was coated on a copper foil using a doctor blade. 
Then, the coated electrodes were dried in a convection oven at 80 ◦C for 
3 h. To prepare the LPL slurry for the Si electrode coating, 1.35 g of AlF3 
powder, 1.5 g of PAA, and 1.7 g of deionized water were mixed in the 
planetary mixer for 5 min at 2000 rpm. The fully dispersed LPL slurry 
was coated on the Si electrode using the spin-coating method. Then, the 
bare Si electrode and the LPL@Si electrode were further dried in a 
vacuum oven at 100 ◦C for 3 h. 
2.2. Characterization 
The morphologies of the bare Si and LPL@Si anodes were investi­
gated via field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, MIRA3 
LM, TESCAN) before and after cycling. The corresponding elemental 
mapping images were obtained using energy-dispersive X-ray spectros­
copy (EDS, Ultim Max100, OXFORD). Before observing the morphol­
ogies of the electrodes after cycling, all the cells were disassembled and 
fully rinsed with diethyl carbonate (DEC) to remove residual Li salts. To 
investigate the composition of the SEI layer, X-ray photoelectron spec­
troscopy (XPS) analyses were performed using XPS spectrometer (PHI 
5000 Versa Probe, ULVAC-PHI Inc) without exposure to the atmosphere. 
2.3. Electrochemical measurements 
CR2032-type coin cells were used for the electrochemical perfor­
mance tests. For the Li metal cell tests, the electrodes were punched into 
discs with a diameter of 12 and the areal mass loading of Si for both bare 
Si and LPL@Si anodes was 0.69 mg cm−2. 1.0 T Li metal foil (Wellcos 
corporation) with a diameter of 16 mm was used as a counter electrode. 
For the full-cell tests, an NCM811 cathode was paired with the Si anode. 
To prepare the NCM cathode for the full-cell test, 4.5 g of NCM powder, 
0.25 g of super P, 4.167 g of 6 wt% PVdF solution (Solvay, 5130), and 
0.5 g of NMP were mixed in a planetary mixer for 5 min at 2000 rpm and 
then coated on an aluminum foil using a doctor blade. The coated 
electrodes were dried in a vacuum oven at 80 ◦C for 6 h and cut into 12- 
mm-diameter discs for the cell fabrication. The N/P ratio of the full cells 
was 1.1. All cells were assembled with a polyolefin separator (SETELA, 
Toray Films). For the electrolyte, 1.0 M LiPF6 in ethylene carbonate 
(EC):DEC (1:1) (with 10 wt% FEC, Enchem) and 1.15 M LiPF6 in EC: 
EMC:DEC (2:4:4) (with 12.5 wt% FEC, 1 wt% VC, and 1 wt% LiPO2F2, 
Enchem) were used for the Li metal cell and the full-cell tests, respec­
tively. All cells were fabricated in a dry room (dewpoint below 50 ◦C). 
The galvanostatic charge/discharge tests were performed using a bat­
tery cycle tester (WonATech, WBCS3000L) in voltage ranges of 
0.01–1.5 V and 2.0–4.2 V vs. Li/Li+ for the Li metal cell and the full cell 
tests, respectively. Before the cycling test, all cells were cycled at a low 
current density for three times to stabilize the SEI layer. The Li ion 
diffusion coefficient (DLi+) was obtained by galvanostatic intermittent 
titration technique (GITT). The GITT tests were conducted for the lith­
iation process after the first cycle, with a series of pulse currents of 0.1 C- 
rate for 10 min, followed by 20 min of rest intervals. The Li ion diffusion 
coefficient (DLi+) for each electrode is measured by the following 
equation (Fick’s second law): 
DLi+ =
4
πτ
(mBVM
MBS
)2(ΔEs
ΔEt
)2  
where τ is the duration of the titration, S is the contact area of the 
electrode (=1.131 cm2), mB is the represent mass (≈1.26 g), MB is the 
molecular weight (=28.086 g/mol), and VM is the molar volume of the 
electrode materials (=12.0588 g/cm3). ΔEt and ΔEs stand for the 
changes in voltage during the single-step titration and relaxation step. 
Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS, VSP, Biologic, Seyssinet- 
Pariset) was performed in the frequency range of 250 kHz–10 MHz. 
3. Results & discussion 
Fig. 1 illustrates the effects of the LPL coating on the Si anode during 
cycling. During the first lithiation process, the bare Si anode generates 
an unstable SEI layer on its surface. After several cycles, the unstable SEI 
D. Lee et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      

Applied Surface Science 661 (2024) 160023
3
layer with low mechanical stability easily breaks, which leads to the 
continuous formation of the SEI layer from the newly exposed Si surface 
to the electrolyte. Upon cycling, this behavior causes a destruction of the 
unstable SEI layer, formation of the thick SEI layer, and electrode pul­
verization, which hinders the efficient charge transfer and, conse­
quently, results in a rapid capacity decay. On the contrary, when the LPL 
is introduced as a surface protective layer, a stable and robust LiF–rich 
SEI layer is achieved. During the first cycle, the AlF3 and PAA compo­
nents in the LPL react with Li ions and form a stable LiF-rich SEI layer, 
which exhibits excellent electrochemical stability and high Li ion con­
ductivity. In addition, PAA on the anode surface mitigates the severe 
volumetric stress during the lithiation/delithiation process via the facial 
relaxation of the stress on the electrode and the SEI layer [46,47]. Due to 
these synergistic effects, a stable SEI layer and mitigated volume 
changes are achieved on the LPL@Si anodes even after repetitive 
cycling. 
The surface morphologies of the bare Si and LPL@Si anodes were 
examined with SEM measurements. Fig. 2a shows the SEM image of the 
top-view of the bare Si anode, confirming that Si particles are uniformly 
coated on the current collector. After LPL coating, AlF3 particles are 
uniformly distributed throughout the electrode surface (Fig. 2e). Similar 
morphologies are observed in the cross-sectional SEM images of each 
electrode (Fig. 2b, f). The bare Si and LPL@Si anodes were designed with 
a thickness of 14 μm. To further verify the presence of the LPL coating, 
EDS element mapping measurements were carried out (Fig. 2c–d and 
g–h). In the EDS mapping images of the LPL@Si electrode, F atoms are 
mainly observed in the upper layer of the electrode, different from the 
bare Si electrode. Through the SEM-EDS results, it is demonstrated that 
the LPL is successfully coated on the Si electrode surface. Similarly, the 
XPS survey scan of the LPL@Si electrode exhibits the presence of Al and 
F atoms on the electrode surface, which increases the reliability of the 
SEM–EDS results (Fig. S1). 
To understand the role of the LPL coating on the Si anode, the surface 
of the LPL@Si anode was investigated with the SEM before and after the 
first cycle (Fig. S2). The LPL@Si anode after cycling shows a smooth 
surface with interconnected Si particles compared with the surface 
before cycling. This implies that the LPL is activated associated with the 
electrolyte during the first cycle, as expected. To gain a deeper under­
standing of the role of the LPL, the chemical composition of the initial 
SEI layer on the bare Si and LPL@Si anodes was analyzed via XPS after 
the first cycle. In the deconvoluted F 1s XPS spectra of the bare Si and 
LPL@Si anodes (Fig. 3a, b), a peak at 685 eV corresponding to LiF and a 
peak at 687 eV corresponding to LiPxFy/LixPOyFz are observed [48,49]. 
The relative peak area of F 1s spectrum is summarized in Fig. 3c. 
Fig. 1. Schematic illustration of the effects of the LPL coating on the Si anode during cycling.  
Fig. 2. Characterization of the bare Si and LPL@Si anodes. (a) SEM images of top–view and (b) cross–sectional SEM images for the bare Si anode. (c–d) Corre­
sponding EDS mapping images of the bare Si anode. (e) SEM images of top–view and (f) cross–sectional SEM images for the LPL@Si anode. (g–h) Corresponding EDS 
mapping images of the LPL@Si anode. 
D. Lee et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      

Applied Surface Science 661 (2024) 160023
4
Compared with the bare Si anode, it is confirmed that LiF is dominant for 
the LPL@Si anode rather than LiPxFy/LixPOyFz, which is attributed to 
the conversion reaction of AlF3 in the LPL with Li ions during the first 
cycle. Considering that the LiF has high ionic conductivity and excellent 
electrochemical stability as a SEI component, the LPL is beneficial for 
promoting the fast Li ion transport through the SEI layer and protecting 
the electrode surface from the electrolyte [24,50]. Furthermore, Li–Al–F 
compounds on the LPL@Si anode are observed at 686 eV. The Li–Al–F 
compounds such as Li3AlF6 are beneficial for the improved charge 
transfer kinetics of Si anodes in reducing the energy barrier of Li+
diffusion [32]. The stable SEI layer consisting of LiF and Li–Al–F com­
pounds can be strongly bonded to the interface of Si anodes, mitigating 
the reformation of the SEI layer during cycling [51]. To further verify 
the effect of the LPL coating on the Si anode, the C 1s spectra were also 
deconvoluted. The C 1s spectra are clearly differentiated into four sig­
nals, which are assigned to C–H (284.9 eV), C–O–C (286.6 eV), ROCO2Li 
(288.7 eV), and Li2CO3 (290.0 eV) groups (Fig. 3d, e) [49]. The relative 
peak area of C 1s spectrum is summarized in Fig. 3f. The spectrum of the 
bare Si anode exhibits a higher relative peak area of Li2CO3 owing to 
severe electrolyte decomposition [48]. The unstable SEI layer on the 
bare Si anode could accelerate the continuous electrolyte consumption, 
leading to the thick SEI layer with increasing electrode resistance. In 
contrast, the spectrum of the LPL@Si anode exhibits a relatively lower 
peak area of Li2CO3, which confirms the stability of the SEI layer asso­
ciated with the LPL. It is attributed that the AlF3 facilitates the formation 
of a stable LiF-rich SEI layer and PAA stabilizes the SEI layer with fast 
decomposition of Li salts in the electrolyte, resulting in passivation of 
the anode interface. The stable SEI layer derived from the LPL can 
mitigate the decomposition of the liquid electrolyte, enabling stable 
electrochemical performances. 
To evaluate the electrochemical performances of the bare Si and 
LPL@Si anodes, galvanostatic charge/discharge measurements were 
carried out in the Li metal cell tests. All cells were evaluated at 0.5 C-rate 
for 100 cycles. Fig. 4a, b show the charge and discharge voltage profiles 
of the bare Si and LPL@Si anodes, respectively. At the first cycle, the 
bare Si anode exhibited a specific charge capacity of 3,457 mAh g−1 and 
a discharge capacity of 2,319 mAh g−1 with a poor initial coulombic 
efficiency (CE) of 67.1 %. The low initial CE of the bare Si anode is 
attributed to the unstable SEI layer, leading to the formation of excessive 
SEI layer and irreversible capacity loss at the first cycle [52,53]. On the 
other hand, the LPL@Si anode showed a specific charge capacity of 3415 
mAh g−1 and a discharge capacity of 2659 mAh g−1, with a considerably 
improved CE of 82.1 %. The formation of SEI layers were investigated 
with differential capacity (dQ dV−1) plots (Fig. S3). The cathodic peak at 
~0.5 V is attributed to the SEI formation of bare Si and LPL@Si anodes. 
The LPL@Si anode showed higher intensity peak due to the reduction of 
AlF3, forming Li–Al–F compounds. The peaks at 0.1 and 0.08 V are 
attributed to the alloying reaction of Li and Si. These results demonstrate 
that the coated LPL can effectively protect the Si anode surface and 
minimize irreversible electrochemical reactions during the first cycle. 
After several cycles, the bare Si anode exhibited a drastic capacity fading 
due to the high electrode resistance with the cumulated SEI layer and 
consumption of the active Li ions and electrolyte (Fig. 4c). In contrast, 
the LPL@Si electrode exhibited a higher capacity retention than the bare 
Si electrode during cycling. These beneficial effects on the electro­
chemical properties are attributed to the stable SEI layer, which prevents 
irreversible reactions at the anode interface. 
To further investigate the electrochemical properties of the bare Si 
and LPL@Si anodes, EIS measurements were conducted before cycling 
and after 100 cycles (Fig. S4 and Fig. 4d). Before cycling, the charge 
transfer resistance (RCT) of the bare Si and LPL@Si anodes was 740.9 
and 775.7 Ω, respectively. The slightly higher RCT of the LPL@Si anode 
is due to the presence of the additional coating layer on the Si anode. 
Based on the equivalent circuit model, the solid electrolyte interphase 
resistance (RSEI) and RCT were investigated (Fig. S5). After cycling, the 
SEI resistance (RSEI) of the bare Si anode was 140.3 Ω due to the accu­
mulated SEI layer during cycling, resulting in the poor cycle perfor­
mance. On the contrary, the LPL@Si anode exhibited significantly lower 
RSEI of 50.9 Ω, showing outstanding cycle performance. Furthermore, 
the RCT of the bare Si and LPL@Si anodes was 174.5 and 85.2 Ω, 
respectively. Both anodes showed lower RCT values compared to those 
before cycling due to the electrochemically activated interfaces [54]. 
Fig. 3. XPS spectra for the characterization of the SEI layer on the bare Si and LPL@Si anodes. Deconvoluted F 1s spectrum of the (a) bare Si and (b) LPL@Si anodes 
after 1 cycle. (c) Corresponding relative peak area of different components derived from the XPS F 1s spectrum. Deconvoluted C 1s spectrum of the (d) bare Si and (e) 
LPL@Si anodes after 1 cycle. (f) Corresponding relative peak area of different components derived from the XPS C 1s spectrum. 
D. Lee et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      

Applied Surface Science 661 (2024) 160023
5
The higher RCT of the bare Si anode is attributed to the thick SEI layer 
caused by the continuous electrolyte decomposition on the anode sur­
face [55]. In contrast, since the LPL is beneficial for the efficient charge 
transfer of the Si anode owing to the formation of the stable LiF-rich SEI 
layer, the LPL@Si anode exhibited lower RCT than the bare Si anode. 
Fig. 4e shows the rate capability of the bare Si and LPL@Si anodes at 
various current densities from 0.05 to 5.0 C–rate. The LPL@Si anode 
exhibited significantly higher capacities than the bare Si electrode over 
the entire range of current densities. Especially, the LPL@Si anode 
showed a high capacity of 1651.4 mAh g−1 with a higher capacity 
retention of 57 % under the harsh condition (a rate of 5.0 C) compared 
with the bare Si anode (1041.3 mAh g−1, 39.3 %). These results 
demonstrate that the formation of a stable LiF-rich SEI layer from the 
homogeneous LPL coating on the Si anode can improve the electro­
chemical performances and effectively prevent undesirable side re­
actions associated with the electrolyte decomposition even at a high rate 
of 5.0 C. Moreover, the current distribution becomes localized under 
high current density, leading to irreversible and inhomogeneous elec­
trochemical reactions. Therefore, it is noteworthy that the LPL@Si 
anode fully recovered its initial capacity once the current density 
returned to 0.05 C, which differs from the behavior observed in the bare 
Si anode. The GITT measurements were employed to investigate Li+
diffusion coefficient (DLi+) of the bare Si and LPL@Si anodes in Li metal 
cell tests (Fig. 5). Overall, a lower polarization for the LPL@Si anode is 
Fig. 4. Electrochemical characterizations of the bare Si and LPL@Si anodes. Voltage profiles at the first cycle and the last cycle of the Li metal cells prepared with the 
(a) bare Si and (b) LPL@Si anodes. (c) Cycle performance of Li metal cells prepared with the bare Si and LPL@Si anodes. (d) EIS spectra of the Li metal cells with each 
anode after cycling. (e) Rate capability of the Li metal cells with each anode at different current densities varied from 0.05 C to 5.0 C. 
D. Lee et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      

Applied Surface Science 661 (2024) 160023
6
observed compared to the bare Si anode (Fig. 5a). Based on the GITT 
results, the DLi+ of the LPL@Si anode was 1.54 × 10–11 cm2 s−1, which is 
significantly greater than that of the bare Si (2.87 × 10–12 cm2 s−1) at x 
= 1 in LixSi (Fig. 5b) [56,57]. The above results demonstrate the ad­
vantages of the LPL@Si anodes for improved electrochemical properties, 
stabilizing the SEI layer and boosting Li ion kinetics for high energy 
density LIBs. 
To demonstrate the practical applicability of the LPL@Si anode, 
high-energy density full-cell tests were performed using NCM811 as a 
cathode with a practical areal capacity at a rate of 0.5 C after the pre- 
activation process (Fig. 6). VC and LiPO2F2 electrolyte additives were 
employed to improve cathodic and anodic stability of full cells. The full- 
cells prepared with the bare Si and LPL@Si anodes exhibited clear dif­
ferences in the electrochemical properties. The full-cell with the LPL@Si 
anode exhibited a much higher initial CE of 92.4 % than that with the 
bare Si anode (88.5 %) at the first cycle (Fig. 6a). The full-cell prepared 
with the bare Si anode presented a rapid capacity decay during the first 
10 cycles, attributing to the unstable SEI layer and the substantial vol­
ume change during cycling (Fig. 6b). In addition, the full-cell fabricated 
with the bare Si anode showed fluctuating CEs after 10 cycles due to the 
unstable SEI layer and large volume change, which lead to the loss of 
electrochemically available Li ions. In contrast, the full-cell prepared 
with the LPL@Si anode exhibited a stable cycle performance with a 
capacity retention of 63.3 % after 50 cycles. This result demonstrates 
that the LPL coating on the Si anode can stabilize the anode interface 
against electrolyte decomposition and mitigate the volume changes with 
the facial relaxation of the stress. 
To investigate the effect of the LPL coating on the volume change 
mitigation of the Si anode in the full-cell, cross-sectional SEM images of 
the anodes were recorded after cycling. The thickness of the bare Si and 
LPL@Si anodes was measured after 1 cycle and after 100 cycles. Fig. 7a, 
d show the thickness of the bare Si and the LPL@Si anodes after 1 cycle, 
respectively. The thickness of the bare Si anode increased from 14 μm 
(Fig. 2b) to 18 μm after 1 cycle, showing a swelling ratio of nearly 30 %. 
In contrast, the volume change in the LPL@Si anode after 1 cycle was 
considerably smaller, from 14 to 15 μm, which corresponds to a swelling 
ratio of 7 %. To determine the thickness of the LPL@Si anode after 1 
cycle, EDS mapping measurements were performed (Fig. S6). These 
results prove that the LPL coating effectively mitigates the volume 
changes in the Si anode during the first cycle. Fig. 7b displays the 
thickness of the bare Si anode after 100 cycles. A highly increased anode 
thickness of 33 μm, corresponding to a swelling ratio of nearly 236 %, 
was observed. In addition, the high-magnification SEM image of the bare 
Si anode after cycling reveals deformed and remarkably larger particles 
than those of the anode before cycling (Fig. 7c). Conversely, the increase 
in the thickness of the LPL@Si anode was relatively small compared with 
that of the bare Si anode, corresponding to a swelling ratio of 193 % 
(Fig. 7e). The electrode thickness and swelling ratio are summarized in 
Table 1. The morphology of the LPL@Si anode is relatively well pre­
served compared to that of the bare Si anode (Fig. 7f). The SEM images 
of top view of the cycled bare Si and LPL@Si anodes showed the clear 
differences in morphologies (Fig. S7). The bare Si anode showed 
agglomerated and ruptured morphologies after 100 cycles. In contrast, 
LPL@Si anode exhibited uniform and stable morphologies even after 
100 cycles. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of the LPL 
coating on alleviating the volume change of the Si anode even after 
repetitive cycling. Moreover, the capacity retention after cycling of the 
LPL@Si is outstanding compared to those in previous reports (Fig. S8). 
In light of all these results, it is proved that our strategy is successful for 
achieving the long-term stability of the Si anodes in high energy density 
Fig. 5. (a) GITT curves plotted with the voltage as a function of time and (b) 
corresponding Li+ diffusion coefficients of the bare Si and LPL@Si anodes. The 
GITT measurements were carried out with a pulse current at 0.1 C for 10 min 
and resting for 20 min after the first formation cycle in the Li metal cell tests. 
Fig. 6. Electrochemical characterizations of the full cells prepared with the bare Si and LPL@Si anodes. (a) Initial voltage profiles of the full cells at the first cycle. (b) 
Cycle performance of the full cells. NCM811 was employed as a cathode for the full cell tests. 
D. Lee et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      

Applied Surface Science 661 (2024) 160023
7
LIBs. 
4. Conclusion 
In this study, we introduced an LPL coating on the Si anode to ach­
ieve stable electrochemical performances of the Si anode. AlF3 in the LPL 
contributes to the formation of a LiF-rich SEI layer with high Li ion 
conductivity and excellent electrochemical stability, protecting the 
electrode surface against electrolyte decomposition. Additionally, PAA 
in the LPL not only acts as a SEI stabilizer but also alleviates the me­
chanical stress at the electrode interface and mitigates the volume 
changes of the Si anodes during cycling. Due to the synergistic effects of 
AlF3 and PAA in the LPL, outstanding mechanical and electrochemical 
stability was achieved on the Si anode interface. As a result, the LPL@Si 
anode showed excellent electrochemical performances in Li metal cell 
tests with a capacity retention of over 75 % after 100 cycles at 0.5 C-rate. 
Furthermore, the full-cell prepared with the LPL@Si anode presented an 
outstanding cycling performance with a relatively reduced volume 
expansion compared to the full-cell prepared with the bare Si anode. 
This study provides a new strategy for the design of an interface pro­
tective layer to stabilize the electrode surface and alleviate the volume 
change in the Si anode toward next-generation high-energy–density 
LIBs.

================================================================================
[SOURCE: Synthesis of Carboxymethyl Cellulose Lithium by Weak Acid Treatment and Its Application in High Energy-Density Graphite Anode for Li-Ion Batteries.pdf]
================================================================================
Synthesis of Carboxymethyl Cellulose Lithium by Weak Acid
Treatment and Its Application in High Energy-Density Graphite
Anode for Li-Ion Batteries
Hyunjung Park,†,‡ Dongsoo Lee,‡ and Taeseup Song*,‡
†School of Chemical and Biomedical Engineering, Nanyang Technological University, 62 Nanyang Drive, Singapore 637459,
Singapore
‡Department of Energy Engineering, Hanyang University, Seoul 133-791, Korea
ABSTRACT: Carboxymethyl cellulose lithium (CMC-Li)
has recently been explored as a promising binder for Li-ion
batteries because of enhanced Li+ ion ﬂux. CMC-Li has been
generally prepared by CMC acid form (CMC-H) as an inter-
mediate product treated with a strong acid, which consid-
erably causes a polymer degradation. Here, we report a synthesis
method of CMC-Li through the use of a weak acid (acetic acid)
and its application in a high energy-density graphite anode.
CMC-Li synthesized by acetic acid (CMC-Li (A)) exhibits enhanced physicochemical properties including an appropriate
viscosity of ∼3000 mPa·s at a shear rate of 10 s−1, good slurry stability, and strong adhesion force of 1.4 gf/mm compared to
those of CMC-Li synthesized by hydrochloric acid. The high energy-density graphite anode prepared with CMC-Li (A) shows
higher charge/discharge capacities and capacity retentions in various rates of 0.05−2 C than those of the electrode prepared
with CMC-Na that might be due to the enhanced Li+ ion ﬂux upon cycling.
1. INTRODUCTION
Li-ion batteries (LIBs) have attracted great attention as an
energy storage system in all kinds of portable electronics such as
mobile phones, laptops, tablets, etc. There have been intensive
studies to enhance battery performances by manipulating var-
ious factors including raw materials (negative/positive materials,
conductive agents, binders, and electrolytes), cell design, packing,
charging/discharging control strategy, and so on. As battery
technology has been well-established and mature nowadays, a
high degree of qualiﬁcations and standards are more required.
In this regard, research at industrial scale becomes important for
next-generation LIBs. Manufacturing processes of Li-ion
batteries are as follows: (1) slurry preparation: mixing active
materials, binders, and conductive agents in a speciﬁc ratio;
(2) casting and drying: slurry coating on a current collector by
tape casting and drying a solvent at an elevated temperature in
an oven; (3) calendaring: compressing electrodes for close
packing; (4) cutting/slitting/punching: electrodes being cut or
punched into a desired shape the same as a cell; (5) assembly:
electrodes being wounded with separator and integrated into
the housing.1 Among those multiple procedures, a dispersion
of solids including the active material and conducting agent in
an appropriate solvent is a priority in that the slurry properties
determine a homogeneity of thickness, mechanical strength, and
porosity of the electrode which are all directly relevant to cell
performances. Especially, an appropriate rheological behavior
and no sedimentation of the slurry should be achieved for a
homogeneous coating of the slurry on the current collector in
large-scale manufacturing, which is strongly governed by binders
used.
Binders as one of the major electrode components are essen-
tial for the slurry dispersibility and mechanical properties of the
positive/negative electrode. Among various candidates explored
for LIBs, polyvinylidene diﬂuoride (PVDF) and carboxymethyl
cellulose (CMC) are widely used in commercial batteries up to
date. Particularly, the CMC is considered the most promising
binder due to its water-soluble property, environmental friend-
liness, the highest mechanical strength, and low cost.2−4 Previous
studies demonstrate that various cathodes and anodes prepared
with the CMC show improved electrochemical performances
compared to those with the PVDF.5−7 Moreover, stiﬀer CMC
binder constrains electrode deformation compared to more
ductile PVDF binder, and thicker SEI layers are formed on the
PVDF electrode which results in larger electrode expansion and
irreversible deformation.8 The CMC serves as a dispersant of
active materials and/or conductive agents because of a swelling
behavior of the CMC molecule driven by a repulsion force
between carboxylate (COO−) in aqueous media, which is
achieved only by using a small amount below 5 wt %.2,9 One of
the interesting features of the CMC is diﬀerent kinds of salt
forms such as CMC-R (R = H, Li, Na, K, etc.). Studies on an
introduction of carboxymethyl cellulose lithium (CMC-Li) to
Li-ion batteries as a binder have been conducted. According to
the previous reports,10−12 the electrodes prepared with the
CMC-Li show better electrochemical performances such as
Received:
February 23, 2018
Revised:
May 29, 2018
Accepted:
June 12, 2018
Published: June 12, 2018
Article
pubs.acs.org/IECR
Cite This: Ind. Eng. Chem. Res. 2018, 57, 8895−8901
© 2018 American Chemical Society
8895
DOI: 10.1021/acs.iecr.8b00851
Ind. Eng. Chem. Res. 2018, 57, 8895−8901
Downloaded via GACHON UNIV on January 28, 2026 at 09:46:29 (UTC).
See https://pubs.acs.org/sharingguidelines for options on how to legitimately share published articles.

higher Coulombic eﬃciency and charge/discharge capacities
than those composed of the PVDF or the CMC-Na because of
enhanced Li+ ion ﬂux during charging/discharging. A similar
result has been reported in lithium polyacrylate (Li-PAA) pre-
pared with PAA by cation exchange.13
There are several synthetic methods for the CMC-Li. Among
them, a general way is a two-step method as follows: (1) prep-
aration of the CMC acid form (CMC-H) from CMC-Na under a
strong acidic condition (hydrochloric acid (HCl)); (2) prep-
aration of CMC-Li by cationic exchange with lithium sources
such as lithium hydroxide (LiOH). Previous studies have demon-
strated that the CMC-Li binder prepared in the way mentioned
above acts as an eﬃcient ion-conductive polymer with a suitable
adhesiveness for the electrode.11 However, treating CMC-Na
with the strong acid is not desirable because it risks corrosion
of processing equipment, raises a safety issue, and causes a
degradation of the CMC and a breakage of the polymer chain to
a certain extent that can potentially change its original physico-
chemical properties.14,15 In the literature of cellulose and cellulose
derivatives by Ott et al.,16 those phenomena are well explained.
In brief, the acidic treatment of the CMC can mainly cause two
kinds of reactions. First, hydrolytic degradation accompanies a
scission of glucosidic chain bonds of the cellulose that results
in diﬀering of molecular size and a degree of polymerization.
Moreover, the degradation changes the crystallinity and decreases
the viscosity as well as the strength of the CMC. Second, there is
oxidative degradation; because of its polyhydric alcohol structure,
cellulose can be easily oxidized especially in the acidity or basicity
of the medium or in the presence of the oxidizing reagents. This
oxidation cause attacks on carbon atoms and/or hydroxyl groups
in the cellulose molecule, resulting in hydrolytic cleavage of the
chain and/or a formation of carbonyl groups with/without ring
cleavage. Hence, it remains challenging to synthesize CMC-Li in
a more favorable way to minimize the polymer degradation.
In this study, we report the synthesis of CMC-Li by treating
the CMC-Na as a starting material with a weak acid such as
acetic acid for the CMC-H and an application to a graphite-
based anode for LIBs. Various physicochemical properties of the
binder, the slurry, and the electrode with the CMC-Li synthesized
by acetic acid (hereafter, CMC-Li (A)) are investigated in
comparison with CMC-Na as a reference and CMC-Li prepared
with hydrochloric acid (designated as CMC-Li (H)) from the
viewpoint of industrial qualiﬁcations. Finally, CMC-Li binders
are introduced to the high energy-density graphite anode, and
electrochemical performances are evaluated through half-cell
including charge/discharge capacity, cycle life, and rate
capability.
2. EXPERIMENTAL SECTION
2.1. Preparation of CMC-H. The acid forms of CMC-H
were prepared by treating 5 g of CMC-Na (Sigma, Mw ∼
250 000, degree of substitution of 1.2) with acidic solutions of
HCl (DAEJUNG, concentration above 35%) as a strong acid
and an acetic acid (Sigma, >99.8%) as a weak acid in 150 mL of
an ethanol/acid mixture (85:15, volume ratio) for 3 h at 35 °C.
CMC-H mixtures were ﬁltrated and washed with 500 mL of
an ethanol/water mixture (85:15, volume ratio). Two kinds
of CMC-H powders were dried at 80 °C in a vacuum oven,
separately.
2.2. Preparation of CMC-Li. CMC-Li (H) and CMC-Li
(A) binders were synthesized by treating the above two kinds of
pure CMC-H in 150 mL of an ethanol/water mixture (90:10,
volume ratio) with 9.15 g of LiOH·H2O (Sigma, >98%) for 3 h
at 50 °C. The reaction occurs at pH 11. After ﬁnishing the
reaction, the pH of the mixture is neutralized to pH ∼7 using
acetic acid to eliminate residual hydroxide (OH−) in the samples.
Finally, the pure CMC-Li was obtained by ﬁltration with 500 mL
of an ethanol/water mixture (85:15, volume ratio) several times
and drying at 70 °C in a vacuum oven.
2.3. Preparation of High Energy-Density Graphite
Electrodes. A graphite electrode with high energy density was
prepared in an aqueous system. A synthetic graphite (SAMSUNG
SDI)was used as the active material.Three types ofCMC binders,
such as CMC-Na (reference), CMC-Li (H), and CMC-Li (A),
and emulsiﬁed styrene−butadiene rubber (SBR, 40 wt % in D/I
water) as binder were used. In detail, for one batch of a slurry,
19.5 g of graphite particles, 0.2 g of CMC powders, and 0.75 g
of SBR solution (a weight ratio of 97.5:1:1.5) were put into a
50 mL vial with 20 mL of deionized water, and the mixture is
thoroughly mixed using a Thicky mixer at 2000 rpm for 5 min.
The as-prepared slurry was cast onto a Cu current collector
(7 μm thickness) using a doctor blade. Then, the electrode
was dried at 110 °C for 10 min in an oven. After drying, the
mass loading level (L/L) of the electrode was 13.8 mg cm−2.
To increase the packing density, the electrode was pressed using
a rolling press machine, and the thickness of the graphite ﬁlm
was decreased from 120 to 70 μm (not including the Cu current
collector). Therefore, the ﬁnal mass loading and the density
of the high energy-density graphite electrode were set at
13.8 mg cm−2 and 1.7 g cc−1, respectively.
2.4. Material Characterizations. Fourier-transform infra-
red spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra on KBr pellets composed of
CMC-Na, CMC-Li (H), and CMC-Li (A) were recorded by an
FTIR spectrometer (Nicolet 5700, ThermoElectron, US). X-ray
diﬀraction (XRD) patterns were obtained by an X-ray diﬀraction
analyzer (Rigaku D/MAX RINT-2000) in the range of 10° to
80° at a scan rate of 3.0° per min using a Cu Kα X-ray source.
X-ray photoelectron spectrometry (XPS, VG Microtech
ESCA2000) was used for the elemental analysis. The mechanical
properties of binder ﬁlms were measured using a universal
testing machine, UTM (AGS-J, Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan).
The specimens were prepared by using an ASTM D638-Type
5 cutter. The samples were elongated to obtain a stress−strain
curve within a standard deviation of less than ±10%. The rhe-
ological behavior of the slurry was measured at 25 °C by using a
rotational rheometer (MCR 501, Anton Paar, Germany) with a
cone and plate geometry. The stress was applied on the slurry
that contains a mass fraction of 50% solids, and the relative
viscosity was automatically calculated. To measure the adhesion
strength between the copper current collector and graphite/
CMC/SBR ﬁlm, the peeling test was conducted by a UTM
machine. In detail, the graphite electrode was cut by a strip
cutter. The size after cutting was 25 × 50 mm, and then, it was
attached to a glass plate (width: 25.4 mm; length: 50 mm).
2.5. Evaluation of Electrochemical Performances.
Before the evaluation, all the electrodes were dried at 145 °C
for 4 h in a vacuum oven to thoroughly eliminate the moisture in
the electrode. 2032 round type coin cells were fabricated using
the graphite electrodes as a working electrode, lithium foil with a
thickness of ∼1.0 mm as a counter electrode, 0.5 mL of 1.3 M
LiPF6 in ethylene carbonate/diethyl ethylene carbonate
(EC/DEC (3/7 vol %) with 5 wt % FEC, PANAX StarLyte)
as an electrolyte, and a polypropylene (PP) ﬁlm as a separator.
All the cells were tested using a battery cycle tester (TOSCAT
3000, Toyo System, Tokyo, Japan). The 1 C as the current
density is 372 mA g−1 for the electrode on the basis of the
Industrial & Engineering Chemistry Research
Article
DOI: 10.1021/acs.iecr.8b00851
Ind. Eng. Chem. Res. 2018, 57, 8895−8901
8896

theoretical capacity of graphite (372 mAh g−1). The electrodes
have a radius of 15 Φ and a mass loading level of ∼14 mg cm−2
that leads to ∼8 mA as 1 C for the electrochemical evaluations.
As a test mode, a constant current mode was used down to 0.01 V,
and then, it changed to a constant voltage mode at C/100 for
discharge (lithiation). A constant current mode was applied up
to 1.4 V for charge (delithiation). One of each formation cycle at
0.05, 0.1, and 0.2 C was performed.
3. RESULTS AND DISCUSSION

3.1. Binder Characterization. A diﬀerence in the general
way of using hydrochloric acid as a strong acid (1) and our
strategy is the use of acetic acid as a weak acid to convert the
CMC-Na to the CMC-H for the synthesis of the CMC-Li
based on eq 2. The images of as-prepared samples and their
molecular structures were shown in Figure 1. To investigate
the eﬀect of the acidic treatment on the CMC using hydro-
chloric acid and acetic acid, FT-IR spectra were obtained for
as-prepared samples of CMC-Na (black line), CMC-H (H)
(red line), and CMC-H (A) (blue line) as shown in Figure 2a.
Bands at ∼1050 cm−1 are ascribed to the ether group of
cellulose found in all the CMC samples regardless of salt
forms. Peaks at ∼1630 and ∼1418 cm−1 are attributed to the
carboxylic and methyl functional groups, respectively. Peaks
at 2923 cm−1 are originated from C−H stretching of methyl
groups that are attached to the carboxylic group. These IR
spectra basically indicate all the samples possess the cellulose
backbone with the carboxymethyl ether group. However,
a noticeable diﬀerence between them is an appearance
of absorption peaks at ∼883 and ∼1733 cm−1 that represent
hemiacetal bondsand/oraldehydiccarbonyl groups (−CHO)
typically observed in the oxidized CMC.16,17 Especially, the
peak intensity has a linear relationship with a degree of oxida-
tive degradation of the original CMC according to previous
studies on oxycellulose.17−19 In this respect, the CMC-H (H)
was more oxidized than that of the CMC-H (A) because of
the stronger intensities of the peaks. Figure 2b shows XRD
patterns of the three samples. The CMC-H (H) exhibits
much higher intensity of the broad peak in the 2θ range of
10−30° than those of the others. It has been known that the
acid treatment can cleave glucosidic linkages in the cellulose
chains, which results in a removal of the amorphous region
and/or a rearrangement of the disordered region, and thus,
the intensity of the XRD peak increases without a substantial
change in the structure conﬁrmed by the previous reports.16,20
As a result, the XRD study conﬁrms that the CMC-H (H)
experiences a more severe degree of the chain rupture than
that of the CMC-H (A) in comparison to the original CMC-Na.
To characterize the substitution of Li salt for Na salt in the
ﬁnal CMC binders, XPS spectra were obtained from three
samples. As shown in Figure 2c, the CMC-H (H) and the CMC-H
(A) do not exhibit Na 1s peaks in contrast to the CMC-Na sample
that shows the Na peak at ∼1071 eV. This conﬁrms the successful
cation exchange of Na+ with H+ using both hydrochloric acid
and acetic acid. After the lithiation process, the CMC-Li (H) and
the CMC-Li (A) show Li 1speaks at ∼54.1eV (Figure 2d). A shift
to the lower binding energy (BE) from ∼55 eV (a reference of Li
spectrum) was observed, and this could be a BE change due to
the bond with carboxylate ion (COO−).
If polymer chain and/or structure are degraded under speciﬁc
inﬂuences such as heat, light, chemicals like strong acid and
alkalis, etc., physical properties of the binder can be changed.
In this respect, a mechanical strength of the CMC-Na, CMC-Li
(H), and CMC-Li (A) was determined by the universal testing
machine as shown in Figure 2e. Specimens for measuring were
prepared using a dumbbell cutter (the inset). Stress−strain curves
of three samples demonstrate that a behavior of the CMC-Li (H)
binder far deviates from behaviors of the CMC-Na and the
CMC-Li (A). The CMC-Li (H) has the steepest slope when
∼30 N of stress was applied on the specimen, which indicates it
is the most brittle and has the lowest ﬂexibility against defor-
mation. Moreover, a rheological behavior of aqueous solutions
with 0.7 wt % of CMC-Na, CMC-Li (H), and CMC-Li (A) was
studied (Figure 2f). There is a noticeable diﬀerence that apparent
viscosities of CMC-Li (H) and CMC-Li (A) are lower than that
of CMC-Na. Particularly, CMC-Li (H) has too low of a viscosity
and shows Newtonian behavior regardless of shear rates in the
range of 1−1000 s−1. It is deduced from the results that the strong
acidic treatment can severely cause changes in original physical
and mechanical properties of the CMC-Na.
3.2. Slurry Characterization. The graphite used inthiswork
was characterized. Figure 3a shows a SEM image of graphite
particles in the range of 10−20 μm. Figure 3b exhibits XRD
patterns that conﬁrms its high crystallinity and d-spacing of
3.3572 Å from the (002) plane. Moreover, the surface area of the
graphite was measured by the BET method. The calculated
desorption value is ∼3.58 m2/g.
For a mass production, especially the coating/casting process,
a slurry is required to possess an appropriate viscosity in the
range of 2000−10 000 mPa·s at shear rates of 1−100 s−1 and no
sedimentation. Figure 4a−c shows rheological behaviors of three
slurry samples prepared with the CMC-Na, the CMC-Li (H),
and the CMC-Li (A) in terms of aging time for 5 days. It is
desirable that the slurry has a shear thinning behavior during
slurry mixing and coating processes so that the dispersed phase
appears thick and viscous but tends to ﬂow well with minimum
resistance. In this regard, the slurries prepared with the CMC-Na
and the CMC-Li (A) have appropriate viscosities of ∼3000 mPa·
s at a shear rate of 10 s−1 and show the shear thinning behavior.
In contrast, the viscosity of the slurry prepared with the CMC-Li
(H) is still very low, below ∼2000 mPa·s, in the shear rate range
of 1 to 100 s−1, and the slurry shows a Newtonian behavior
which means it is independent of shear rate and close to a liquid
phase. It is worth noting that this low viscosity can cause
problems in the coating process that results in uniformity of the
thickness and the density of the electrode ﬁlm.
Figure 5 shows a sedimentation test by aging for 5 days to
investigate the slurry stability. There were no settlement and a
separation between solid graphite particles and liquid phases in
the samples prepared with the CMC-Na and the CMC-Li (A).
This conﬁrms that those two binders are enough to disperse
the graphite particles and can provide good slurry stability in
the aqueous medium. In contrast, the slurry prepared with
the CMC-Li (H) exhibits settlement of graphite particles
and the phase separation (from a yellow line to a red line).
Despite the slurry instability and the sedimentation, it does not
mean that the use of the CMC-Li (H) is not suitable as the
binder for LIBs. The rheological behavior can be changed if the
other active materials, such as metal oxides, alloys, etc., and a
larger amount of the binder are used in the slurry system.
Industrial & Engineering Chemistry Research
Article
DOI: 10.1021/acs.iecr.8b00851
Ind. Eng. Chem. Res. 2018, 57, 8895−8901
8897

However, the result clearly conﬁrms that the treatment of the
CMC-Na with the strong acid can signiﬁcantly aﬀect dispens-
ability and slurry stability.
3.3. Electrode Characterization. The CMC-Li binders
were introduced to a high energy-density graphite electrode.
The reason to choose the graphite as the active material is
because it has been used the most in the commercial LIBs.
According to previous studies,21,22 the high energy-density
graphite suﬀers from poor Li+ ion diﬀusivity because of a long
pathway for Li+ ions from the top to the bottom of the thick
electrode and a low electrode porosity. In this respect, the use of
the CMC-Li binders for the thick and dense graphite anode can
be an eﬃcient strategy to improve Li+ ion ﬂux and electro-
chemical properties. The current LIBs generally consist of the
graphite anode with a loading level of ∼9 mg cm−2, a density of
∼1.6 g cm−3, and an electrode thickness of 50−60 μm23 that lead
to a full cell capacity of ∼3000 mAh g−1 especially for mobile
phones (Figure 6a). With a signiﬁcant development of the
portable electronics, a demand for higher energy-density
LIBs has been increased. However, the electrode thickness
increased to 80−90 μm, and the porosity decreased because of
an applied higher pressure to closely pack the graphite particles
by a roll press as shown in Figure 6b. The electrodes prepared
with the CMC-Li (H) and the CMC-Li (A) are also shown
Figure 3. (a) SEM image of graphite particles and (b) XRD patterns.
Figure 2. Binder characterization of CMC-Na, CMC-H (H), CMC-H (A), CMC-Li (H), and CMC-Li (A) binders. (a) FT-IR spectra. (b) XRD
patterns. (c, d) XPS spectra. (e) Stress−strain curves. (f) Rheological behaviors of 0.7 wt % aqueous binder solutions.
Figure 1. Molecular structures and images of carboxymethylcellulose sodium (CMC-Na), carboxymethylcellulose (CMC-H), and
carboxymethylcellulose lithium (CMC-Li).
Industrial & Engineering Chemistry Research
Article
DOI: 10.1021/acs.iecr.8b00851
Ind. Eng. Chem. Res. 2018, 57, 8895−8901
8898

in Figure 6c,d. There are no signiﬁcant diﬀerences between
samples (Figure 6c,d).
To study the mechanical property of the graphite electrodes
prepared with three binder samples, an adhesion force between a
graphite/CMC/SBR ﬁlm and a copper current collector was
measured by the UTM peeling test. From the viewpoint of
industrial manufacturing, the high adhesion force is preferable
for calendaring and cutting/slitting/punching processes. As
shown in Figure 7, the electrode prepared with CMC-Na has the
adhesion force of ∼1.89 gf/mm as a reference. However, the
adhesion force of CMC-Li (H) is ∼0.82 gf/mm lower than
the half of the CMC-Na. The CMC-Li (A) also shows a lower
value of ∼1.4 gf/mm, but it is more improved compared to that
of CMC-Li (H).
3.4. Electrochemical Evaluation. After the systematic
studies on the binder, the slurry, and the electrode, high energy-
density graphite anodes were prepared to investigate the favor-
able eﬀect of CMC-Li binders on the electrochemical perform-
ance. Figure 8 shows the electrochemical performances tested in
a voltage window of 0.01−1.4 V vs Li/Li+ (1 C = 372 mA g−1).
As shown in voltage proﬁles at 0.05 C (Figure 8a), graphite
anodes composed of CMC-Na, CMC-Li (H), and CMC-Li (A)
give Coulombic eﬃciencies of 87.7%, 92.7%, and 90.1% and
charge capacities (delithiation) of 318, 330, and 334 mAh g−1,
respectively. The anodes prepared with the CMC-Li (H) and
the CMC-Li (A) show improved the Coulombic eﬃciency and
Figure 4. Rheological behaviors of (a) graphite/CMC-Na/SBR slurry,
(b) graphite/CMC-Li (H)/SBR slurry, and (c) graphite/CMC-Li (A)/
SBR slurry in terms of aging for 1/3/5 days.
Figure 5. Sedimentation test of (a) graphite/CMC-Na/SBR slurry,
(b) graphite/CMC-Li (H)/SBR slurry, and (c) graphite/CMC-Li
(A)/SBR slurry in terms of aging for 5 days. A phase separation between
graphite particles and liquid phases is shown between a yellow line and a
red line.
Figure 6. SEM top (left) and cross-view (right) images of graphite/
CMC-Na/SBR electrodes with a mass loading level (mg cm−2) and a
density (g cm−3) of (a) 9.7/1.6 and (b) 13.8/1.7; (c) graphite/CMC-Li
(H)/SBR electrode and (d) graphite/CMC-Li (A)/SBR electrode with
13.8 mg cm−2/1.7 g cm−3.
Industrial & Engineering Chemistry Research
Article
DOI: 10.1021/acs.iecr.8b00851
Ind. Eng. Chem. Res. 2018, 57, 8895−8901
8899

capacity compared to those of the CMC-Na. Figure 8b shows a
cycle performance over 30 cycles at 0.5 C. Capacity retentions of
three graphite anodes are 98%, 96.6%, and 97.4%, respectively.
There is no signiﬁcant diﬀerence, but the anodes prepared with
the CMC-Li (H) and (A) show a slightly lower value than that of
CMC-Na which might be correlated to the adhesion force and
weaker strength between the graphite particles and the CMC-Li
binders. A noticeable change was observed in a rate capability at
current densities from 0.05 to 2 C (Figure 8c). The capacity
retentions of the graphite anodes prepared with CMC-Li (H)
and CMC-Li (A) are higher than that of the graphite anode
composed with CMC-Na through all the regions. Particularly,
the capacities at 2 C are 264, 292, and 296 mAh g−1, respectively.
Table 1 shows a summary of the electrochemical performances.
On the basis of the coin half-cell tests, the use of CMC-Li
binders improves the electrochemical performances of the high
energy-density graphite anode compared to that of CMC-Na
which could be due to the enhanced Li+ ion kinetics in the
electrode upon cycling.
4. CONCLUSIONS
In summary, the synthesis of the CMC-Li was developed using a
weak acid such as acetic acid as the alternative to the conven-
tional method by the strong acid, hydrochloric acid. The eﬀects
of treating CMC-Na with acids were investigated by various
characterizations on the physicochemical properties of binders,
slurries, and electrodes, especially from the viewpoint of the
industrial qualiﬁcations. It was revealed that the CMC-Li pre-
pared with the hydrochloric acid caused signiﬁcant changes in
the original binder properties compared to CMC-Na as the
starting material. Particularly, the low viscosity and the phase
separation between the graphite particles and the liquid phases
are not suitable for the graphite-based slurry which can poten-
tially cause the inhomogeneity of the electrode ﬁlm during
casting in large-scale manufacturing. In contrast, the CMC-Li
synthesized by the acetic acid shows the improved binder
properties compared to the CMC-Li (H). The high energy-
density graphite anode prepared with the CMC-Li (H) shows
enhanced electrochemical performances including charge/
discharge capacities and the rate capability compared to those
of the anode prepared with CMC-Na. As a result, the use of
CMC-Li synthesized by the weak acid as the binder for LIBs
can be an eﬃcient way to prevent the polymer degradation of the
CMC caused by the strong acidtreatment as well as take advantage
of the CMC-Li such as enhanced Li+ ion ﬂux upon cycling.

Electrochemistry Communications 157 (2023) 107598
