the delithiation process, resulting in a lithiation capacity of 170
mAh·g−1 during the second cycle. However, this capacity loss
faded after the second cycle. The initial columbic eﬃciency was
≈57%, which is attributed to the irreversible capacity generated
during the ﬁrst lithiation process. A comparison of the ﬁrst and
second lithiation curves revealed irreversible capacity losses at
1.5 and 1.0 V. The capacity loss at 1.5 V is attributed to the for-
mation of a solid electrolyte interface (SEI) layer on the electrode
surface.[18] In other hand, the irreversible reaction that occurred
over a wide area below 1.0 V led to a larger capacity loss than
that associated with SEI generation and does not fall under the
voltage range of SEI formation.[19]
Cyclic voltammetry (CV) proﬁles were obtained at a speciﬁc
scan rate (0.1 mV·s−1) and the same voltage range used for
galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD) measurement. Figure 2b
shows the ﬁrst and second CV curves. Similar to the GCD pro-
ﬁle, the CV proﬁle exhibited anodic peaks at 0.6 and 0.4 V and
cathodic peaks at 0.8 and 0.5 V. This conﬁrms the existence of a
redox couple at 0.8 V/0.6 V and 0.5 V/0.4 V. Furthermore, SEI for-
mation occurred at ≈1.5 V (Figure 2b; inner box, blue area), and
Li2O generation occurred below 1.0 V (Figure 2b; red area).[20]
However, Li2O does not decompose during the charge process
owing to its high binding energy, which leads to capacity loss.
Figure 2c shows the CV proﬁles of RPLLTO recorded at various
scan rates ranging from 0.1 to 2.0 mV·s−1 within a voltage range
of 0.0–2.0 V. As the scan rate increased, the current increased
and the peaks broadened. This behavior can be explained by the
Randles–Sevcik equation shown below:
ip = (2.69 × 105) n3∕2AC
√
Dv
(1)
where n is the number of electrons, A is the electrode area, D
is the diﬀusion coeﬃcient of the electrode material, C is the con-
centration of the electrode, and 𝜈is the scanning speed. The peak
current (iP) is directly proportional to the electrode concentration
(C) and the scanning speed (𝜈), without considering the number
of electrons (n) and diﬀusion coeﬃcient (D). According to Equa-
tion (1), the electrode reaction of RPLLTO is restricted by mass
transfer.
The charge/discharge process can be divided into a non-
Faradaic reaction, which stores electric charges on the electrode
surface, and a Faradaic reaction, which stores electric charges in-
side the electrode. The reaction is deﬁned by the relationship be-
tween the scan rate and current shown in the equation below:
i(V) = a𝜈b
(2)
log i(V) = b log 𝜈+ log a
(3)
The slope (b value) determines if the reaction rate is controlled
by the charge transfer or the mass transfer. A b value close to
0.5 indicates a slow electrochemical reaction occurring within
the bulk of the electrode(charge transfer), whereas a b value near
1.0 signiﬁes a fast reaction taking place on the electrode sur-
face(mass transfer). Figure 2d shows a plot of log(peak current)
versus log(scan rate) of each current peak extracted from the CV
proﬁle. The b value were 0.46, 0.47, 0.56, and 0.54, which are all
close to 0.5. This suggests that RPLLTO demonstrates a Faradaic
ion storage mechanism that mainly occurs inside the electrode
rather than on the electrode surface. Other perovskites men-
tioned in the introduction demonstrate pseudocapacitance reac-
tions, characterized by b values close to 1.0, whereas RPLLTO pre-
dominantly undergoes bulk reactions. Typically, materials that
engage in bulk reactions may exhibit lower rate capabilities
compared to those with pseudocapacitance.[21] However, bulk-
reactive materials present certain advantages, such as lower self-
discharge rates and decreased dependence on particle size and
morphology. The rate capability of RPLLTO was determined at a
current density range of 0.01–30.00 A·g−1 (Figure 2e). The rate
capability at lower currents (<2 A·g−1) decreased to 90% but de-
creased rapidly at higher currents (>5 A·g−1), which could be
due to the dominance of slow bulk reactions in the Li-ion stor-
age mechanism. The RP structure allows Li ions to diﬀuse into
the bulk due to its low APF, resulting in an extended Li diﬀusion
path. This leads to slower ionic conductivity and, consequently,
slower rate performance. In contrast, the perovskite structure ex-
hibits a sloped GCD proﬁle without a plateau, driven by pseudo-
capacitance reactions occurring on the surface, which contributes
to faster rate performance.
Figure 2f shows the long-term capacitance changes and
coulombic eﬃciencies (CE) of RPLLTO during 1000 cycles at 1
A·g−1. RPLLTO demonstrated a capacity retention of 88% and
CE of ≈100% after 1000 cycles. The crystal structures before and
after 1000 charge and discharge cycles are compared in Figure
S9 (Supporting Information). The GCD proﬁle before and after
the cycle test (Figure S9a, Supporting Information) and the XRD
pattern of the electrode (Figure S9b, Supporting Information) re-
vealed a high retention rate with no signiﬁcant capacity or struc-
tural changes.
2.3. Charge/Discharge Mechanism
Figure 3b shows the XRD pattern of the RPLLTO electrode, which
is consistent with the in situ XRD pattern. However, some strong
peaks in the latter may be attributed to the in situ XRD cell
module (Figure S8a, Supporting Information). The peak shift ob-
served in the in situ XRD patterns can be used to predict the
deformation of a speciﬁc crystal plane according to Bragg’s law,
shown below:
n𝜆= 2dsin𝜃
(4)
where n is an integer, 𝜆is the wavelength of beam, 𝜃is the an-
gle between the crystal plane and the incident beam, and d is
the interplanar distance. If n and 𝜆are assumed to be ﬁxed con-
stants, an inverse relationship exists between the measured 𝜃
and d. According to Equation (4), during the lithiation process,
most of the crystal planes in RPLLTO shift toward lower angles,
excluding the (006) and (0012) planes, resulting in an increase
in the interplanar distance. Conversely, the (001) plane family,
associated with the c-axis, shifts toward higher angles, indicat-
ing a contraction in d-spacing. Aside from the newly formed
Li2CO3 peak observed near 21°, no additional peaks were detected
within the measurement range. This suggests that RPLLTO ex-
periences straightforward contraction and expansion during the
lithiation/delithiation process without any phase transition. This

Figure 3. a) In situ X-ray diﬀraction(XRD) pattern of RPLLTO charge/discharge at a current density of 0.01 A·g−1. b) Ex situ XRD pattern of the RPLLTO
electrode before the test. c) Galvanostatic charge/discharge proﬁle during in situ XRD. Changes in the d) lattice parameter and e) cell volume during
lithiation/delithiation.
reversible structural change was consistently observed during the
second and third cycles as well (Figure S13, Supporting Informa-
tion).
Ex situ XRD was performed to predict the changes in Li-ion
storage sites and interplanar distances (Figure 3e,f). The Rietveld
reﬁnement of the XRD pattern revealed an increase in the a/b
axes parameter from 3.83 to 3.94 Å and a decrease in the c axis
parameter from 26.56 to 26.03 Å. In addition, the unit cell vol-
ume of RPLLTO was increased by 4% from 389.61 Å3 to 404.08
Å3. These results indicate excellent structural stability and preser-
vation of the crystal structure even after lithiation. Unlike layered
structure anode materials such as graphite, which have a weak
bonding strength along the c axis and expand in that direction,
resulting in low structural stability, RPLLTO maintains its stabil-
ity by redistributing the strain concentrated on the c axis to the
a/b axes.[3a,22] Moreover, the perovskite structure of RPLLTO ex-
hibits a long cation array, which possesses low symmetry. This
makes RPLLTO more ﬂexible and suppresses phase transition.
Thus, the high structural stability of RPLLTO is likely caused by
the distortion of the long array of LaO12 and TiO6, which sup-
presses phase transition upon Li-ion intercalation.[13]
Ex situ X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed
to determine the chemical state of elements during the charge
and discharge processes of RPLLTO (Figure 4a–d). The Li 1s
XPS revealed the presence of a Li2CO3 component in the OCP-
0.85 V state, which is attributed to the reaction between Li+ from
the electrolyte and carbon, a constituent of the electrode. This is
closely associated with SEI formation, as supported by the obser-
vation that the corresponding peak becomes more pronounced
below 1.3 V, a voltage where SEI formation is known to occur. At
lower voltages, a peak at 54 eV emerges, which can be assigned to
Li2O or Li+ ions. This phenomenon aligns with the Li2O forma-
tion voltage observed in Figure 2b. The continuous increase in
Li2O concentration during lithiation suggests that Li reacts with
the weakly bound oxygen distributed across the stepped surface
of RPLLTO during the initial reaction, resulting in excessive ir-
reversible capacity. Although, this may simply result from an in-
crease in lithium concentration on the electrode surface as the
voltage decreases. In the La 3d region (Figure 4b), the invariant
peak positions of the La 3d5/2 and La 3d3/2 (La 3d main peaks)
indicate that the oxidation state of La remains unchanged dur-
ing the reaction. The satellite peak at 831 eV is associated with
La-ligand bonding. In the case of RPLLTO, the ligand involved
is oxygen. As lithiation progresses, the intensity of this satellite
peak changes, suggesting a modiﬁcation in the electronic struc-
ture of La-O bonding. This observation implies alterations in the
electronic structure of the weakly bound oxygen mentioned ear-
lier.
In Figure 4c, the Ti oxidation state predominantly remains +4
until the end of the ﬁrst plateau, where it transitions almost en-
tirely to +3 at 0.45 V. This indicates that all Ti4+ in RPLLTO is
reduced to Ti3+ during the ﬁrst reaction. The presence of a Ti2+
peak at 457 eV further suggests that a portion of Ti3+ may be re-
duced to Ti2+. Below 0.2 V, after the second plateau, no detectable
peaks are observed in the Ti 2p region, likely due to the formation
of a thick SEI layer, which prevents measurement. While the SEI
Adv. Sci. 2025, 12, 2410543

www.advancedscience.com
Figure 4. Ex situ X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy during the initial lithiation by voltage state: a) Li 1s, b) La 3d, c) Ti 2p, and d) O 1s regions.
comprises components such as Li2O, LiF, Li2CO3, organic carbon
etc., it is unlikely to incorporate transition metals, resulting in no
signiﬁcant signal for Ti and La. In the O 1s region (Figure 4d), the
oxygen signal dynamically evolves during the reaction, reﬂecting
the formation of various components associated with lithiation
and SEI growth.
To conﬁrm the reversibility of bonding interactions between
chemical elements or changes in oxidation states during the
charge/discharge process of RPLLTO, in situ Raman spec-
troscopy during the ﬁrst lithiation and Ex situ XPS at the ﬁrst
and second fully lithiated/delithiated states were conducted. In
situ Raman spectroscopy (Figure S10a, Supporting Information)
was conducted to understand the change of bonding strength and
length between elements in RPLLTO. It was conducted according
to the procedure illustrated in Figure S11b (Supporting Informa-
tion). The speciﬁc state of the measurements for each spectrum
are indicated in Figure S12 (Supporting Information). A redshift
was observed during the charge process, which is attributed to
the expansion of the La–Ti bond owing to Li intercalation. This
is an irreversible structural deformation. In addition, Li2O was
generated at ≈0.5 V during the initial lithiation process, which is
consistent with the voltage observed in the CV curve (Figure 2b)
and Ex situ XPS (Figure 4a). Therefore, both the irreversible con-
traction of the La–Ti bond and the generation of Li2O during the
initial lithiation process may have contributed to the signiﬁcant
initial irreversible capacity loss of RPLLTO.
Ex situ XPS of the RPLLTO electrode during the ﬁrst and
second charge/discharge cycles at fully charged and discharged
states is presented in Figure S10 (Supporting Information). In
the Li 1s region, peaks corresponding to Li+ and Li2CO3 were
observed, consistent with the ﬁndings in Figure 4a. The inten-
sities ratio of these two peaks changes reversibly and repeatedly
during the charge/discharge process. The increase in the inten-
sity of the Li+ peak at 0 V is attributed to the rise in Li+ con-
centration on the electrode surface and the formation of Li-O
bonds, as previously discussed. During the Li+ ion conduction
process, the structural stability of these may collapse, and some
may decompose, which may decrease the intensity of the related
peak. The decrease in the Li+ peak and the increase in the in-
tensity of Li2CO3 at 2 V prove that the concentration of Li+ ions
on the surface decreases, and the SEI generated in the ﬁrst reac-
tion is not completely decomposed and remains during the sub-
sequent charge process. The O1s XPS conﬁrmed the generation
of Li2O at ≈528 eV after the ﬁrst lithiation process. This Li2O
peak either increased or decreased during the charge/discharge
cycles and did not disappear completely. These results are con-
sistent with those of in situ Raman spectroscopy. Moreover, the
peaks associated with Ovs were observed near 530 eV and shifted
slightly to the right after the ﬁrst lithiation process. Ovs have a
positive charge and can accommodate electrons. Xiao et al. devel-
oped an LTO with Ovs and analyzed its charge/discharge mech-
anism. They found that the Ovs stabilized the oxidation state
of Ti by accepting additional electrons, which could lead to in-
creased capacity and improved structural stability. Similarly, as
expected, RPLLTO contained Ovs on its surface, which led to ac-
cepted additional electrons. This stabilized bond did not return
to its original state after the ﬁrst cycle and showed irreversible
deformation.
Ti K-edge and La L3-edge ex situ X-ray absorption ﬁne struc-
ture (XAFS) spectroscopy was performed to determine the oxida-
tion states of the transition metals and the changes in the atomic
distances during the charge/discharge processes of RPLLTO. The
Ex situ XAFS spectra of the fully lithiated and delithiated states of
RPLLTO during the ﬁrst and second cycles are shown in Figure 5.
The characteristic peaks are observed in the pre-edge, absorp-
tion edge, and rising edge regions. The pre-edge mainly reﬂects
the local structure and coordination environment of Ti. In par-
ticular, it can provide information about the length, coordination
number, and symmetry of Ti–O bonds. The Ti K-edge absorption
spectra reveal that the pre-edge peaks of the pristine electrode
(4970.2 eV) and the electrode after the ﬁrst fully lithiated state (at
0 V; 4970.4 eV) were at diﬀerent positions. The rightward shift of
the pre-edge peak indicates a decrease in the Ti–O bond length.
The absorption edge is directly related to the oxidation state of Ti,
and a shift to the left suggests reduction, indicating a tendency to-
ward a lower oxidation state.[23] These results indicate the reduc-
tion of Ti4+ to Ti2+.[24] This minute alteration in the Ti K-edge
is attributed to the strong interference of La.[25] In addition, the
peak at 4988.4 eV in the rising edge region was attributed to the
interference between the adjacent bonds of Ti. This peak typically
Adv. Sci. 2025, 12, 2410543
2410543 (6 of 9)
© 2025 The Author(s). Advanced Science published by Wiley-VCH GmbH

Figure 5. Ex situ X-ray absorption ﬁne structure spectra of the fully lithiated and delithiated states of RPLLTO during the ﬁrst and second cycles. a) Ti
K-edge and b) La L3-edge X-ray absorption near edge spectra of the fully lithiated and delithiated states. c) Ti K-edge and d) La L3-edge extended X-ray
absorption ﬁne structure spectra of Ti in fully charged and discharged states.
appears when the vibrations of adjacent bonds cause destructive
interference.
Furthermore, this interference is signiﬁcantly aﬀected by the
charged and discharged states and increases after lithiation.
The formation of new bonds after Li intercalation conﬁrms
this increasing interference. The La L3-edge absorption spectra
(Figure 5b) shows negligible changes during the electrochemical
process, indicating the unchanged oxidation state of La.
Figure 5c,d show the bonding distances between each element
calculated from the Ti K-edge and La L3-edge Extended X-ray ab-
sorption ﬁne structure spectroscopy. Both O and La, which are
the neighboring elements of Ti, exhibited repeated deformation
before and after lithiation. This indicates the reversible Li inter-
calation/deintercalation around each bond, which also suggests
that Li is likely stored near each bond. Except for the Ti–O and Ti–
Ti bonds, most bonds undergo negligible, irregular deformation,
resulting in random changes. These results indicate that RPLLTO
has a highly asymmetric structure after lithiation.
To conﬁrm the changes in the Li-ion storage sites and the crys-
tal structure of RPLLTO, ex situ XRD was conducted in the fully
lithiated state (Figure 6). Li was inserted into both the LiO4 and
LaTi layers. The ﬁrst plateau observed at 0.6 V could be attributed
to Li storage in the LaTi layer. This is because Li exhibits the
highest position stability in the O4 window (2b site) of the LaO12
cuboctahedron. Hence, we conclude that Li ions were stored at
the 2b sites at the ﬁrst plateau. This suggests that two sites in the
LaTi(1) layer contain 1/2 Li ion each position, and four sites in the
LaTi(2) layer contain a 1/4 Li ion each position. As a results, stor-
age of two Li ions in LaTi layers. The second plateau, observed at
0.4 V, could be attributed to the LiO4 layer. This could be further
explained as follows: when Li ions are inserted into the 4e sites
of the LiO4(1) and LiO4(2) layers, these Li ions experience Li–Li
repulsions with the existing Li ions in the surrounding 4d sites.
The linear Li ions rearrange into a closed-packed structure, form-
ing a zigzag pattern and decreasing the lattice parameter along
the c axis.
The Li intercalation process involves the rearrangement of Li
and the Jahn–Teller distortion of the TiO6 octahedron, which
changes the crystal structure of RPLLTO. The oxidation number
of Ti changes from +4 to +2 (Figure 5a), during which the elec-
tron spin in the d-orbital changes from 3d0 to 3d2. A schematic of
the t2 g orbitals (dxy, dzx, and dyz) and their corresponding electron
spins at diﬀerent oxidation numbers of Ti is shown in Figure 6c.
Ti3+(3d1) is ﬁlled with electrons from the dxy orbital and contracts
along the z axis, and Ti2+(3d2) is same with Ti3+(3d1), but expands
along the z axis.[26] The relationship between this orbital transfor-
mation and the c-axis deformation of the unit cell is as follows:
i) the contraction of the c axis in the ﬁrst plateau is attributed to
the presence of the dxy orbital of Ti; and ii) the preservation of
the c axis length during the second plateau could be attributed
to the balance between the expansion of TiO6 and the contrac-
tion caused by the rearrangement of Li. These minimal volume
changes in the crystal structure of RPLLTO during lithiation are
unique and reﬂect improved durability.
Adv. Sci. 2025, 12, 2410543
2410543 (7 of 9)

Figure 6. Structural analysis of RPLLTO during lithiation and delithiation. a) Crystal structures of pristine and lithiated RPLLTO. b) Changes in the crystal
structure at each plateau. c) Schematic of the t2 g orbital structure based on the Ti oxidation state.
Based on the analysis of the crystal structure in Figure 3
and the observed electrochemical properties in Figure 2 and
electronic structure changes during lithiation and delithiation
in Figure 6c, we propose the following plausible Li-ion storage
mechanism of RPLLTO:
1) Initial open circuit potential at 1.0 V: SEI formation
(40mAh·g−1)
2) Initial stage at 1.0–0.6 V: Li2La2Ti3O10-𝛿+ 2x Li+ + 2x e−→
Li2La2Ti3O10 + xLi2O (x < 2)
3) First plateau (0.6 V): Li2La2Ti3O10 + y Li+ + y e−→
Li2+yLa2Ti3O10 (reduction of Ti4+ to Ti3+) (y < 3)
4) Second plateau (0.4 V): Li2+yLa2Ti3O10 + z Li+ + z e−→
Li2+y+zLa2Ti3O10 (reduction of Ti3+ to Ti2+) (z < 2)
5) Reaction during the ﬁrst lithiation process: Li2La2Ti3O10-𝛿+
(2x+y+z) Li+ →Li2+y+zLa2Ti3O10 + xLi2O
6) End of First charge/discharge reaction: Li2La2Ti3O10 + xLi2O
The overall experimental results indicate that RPLLTO demon-
strates a mechanism capable of storing approximately six
lithium ions during the initial lithiation process. In this pro-
cess, the accommodation of additional electrons by Ov, the
formation of Li2O, and reaction with a carbon material at
low cutoﬀvoltages (Figure S17, Supporting Information) con-
tribute to the initial irreversible capacity. While the reduc-
tion of Ti ions to Ti3+ would lead to the uptake of three Li
ions and a capacity of 135 mAh·g−1, the partial reduction of
some Ti ions to Ti2+ allows for the accommodation of ad-
ditional Li ions. Therefore, our ﬁndings explain the unique
electrochemical properties and charge/discharge mechanism of
RPLLTO. This study will promote the design and development
of LIB anode materials with various perovskite structures and
compositions.
3. Conclusion
In this study, we investigated the electrochemical properties and
Li-ion storage mechanism of RPLLTO to validate its applicability
as an anode material for LIBs. RPLLTO, synthesized via an ion-
exchange reaction, exhibited a capacity of 170 mAh·g−1, which
is ≈25% higher than its theoretical capacity(135mAh·g−1). The
unique two plateaus observed at 0.6 V and 0.4V(Li/Li+) were
attributed to lithium ions being stored at diﬀerent sites. XPS
and XAFS analysis revealed that while the oxidation state of Ti
changes from 4+ to 2+, La does not participate in the reaction. In
situ XRD and Rietveld reﬁnement were used to track the lithium
storage sites and the crystal structure changes during lithiation,
conﬁrming a only 4% volume change. This minor volume change
correlates with the 88% capacity retention observed after 1000 cy-
cles.This study will promote the design and development of LIB
anode materials with diﬀerent types of perovskite structures and
compositions.
4. Experimental Section
Materials Preparation:
RPLLTO was synthesized using an ion-
exchange method. Na2La2Ti3O10 (NLTO), a precursor of RPLLTO, was
synthesized through a solid-state reaction method. Na2CO3 (99.5%, SAM-
CHUN, Republic of Korea) (with an excess of 20 mol% Na2CO3 to com-
pensate for Na loss during processing), La2O3 (99.9%, Sigma Aldrich,
Germany) and TiO2 (99.9%, Sigma Aldrich, Germany) were added to
ethanol in their stoichiometric ratios and homogeneously mixed using
a planetary mill (PULVERISETTE 7 Premium, FRITSCH, Germany) at
800 rpm for 10 min. The mixture was dried to a powder in an oven at
80 °C, and the powder was pelletized for calcination. The NLTO pellet was
calcined at 1100 °C for 10 h and ground using a mortar and pestle for the
ion-exchange reaction.
For the ion-exchange reaction, NLTO and LiNO3 were mixed using a
mortar and pestle and heated at 300 °C for 12 h. To remove any remaining
LiNO3, the mixture was washed with distilled water several times and dried
in an oven at 60 °C.
Adv. Sci. 2025, 12, 2410543
2410543 (8 of 9)
© 2025 The Author(s). Advanced Science published by Wiley-VCH GmbH
 21983844, 2025, 21, Downloaded from https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/advs.202410543 by Gachon University Global Campus, Wiley Online Library on [28/01/2026]. www.advancedscience.com
Materials Characterization:
Powder XRD patterns (D8 Advance diﬀrac-
tometer, Bruker, Germany) of RPLLTO were acquired in the 2𝜃range of
5°–90° to analyze its crystal structure. The particle morphology of RPLLTO
was determined using a SEM (JSM-7600F, JEOL, Japan) at various magni-
ﬁcations. The atomic arrangement and interplanar distances were inves-
tigated using a STEM (Titan Cubed Themis G3 300, FEI Inc, USA) in the
HAADF mode. XPS (PHI50000, FEI Inc, USA) was performed to analyze
the chemical composition and oxidation state of RPLLTO.
Electrochemical Properties:
RPLLTO electrodes were fabricated using a
slurry casting method. A slurry of RPLLTO, super P (Imerys S.A, France),
and the carboxymethyl cellulose(CMC) (LANDT, USA) binder in an 8:1:1
ratio was coated on a Cu foil current collector using a doctor blade and
dried in a vacuum oven at 110 °C for 12 h. The RPLLTO electrode was
assembled into a CR2032 half cell using Li metal as the counter elec-
trode. Polypropylene was used as the separator, and 1 M LiPF6 in EC/DMC
(1:1 v/v) was used as the electrolyte. All electrochemical characterization
was performed in the voltage range of 0.0–2.0 V versus Li/Li+. Constant
current charge/discharge performance was performed using a galvanos-
tat/potentiostat battery test system (WMPG1000S, WonAtech, Republic
of Korea), and the current density was in the range of 0.01–2.00 A·g−1. CV
was performed using a potentiostat (VSP, Biologic, France) at diﬀerent
scan rates in the range of 0.1–2.0 mV·s−1.
Li-ion Storage Mechanism:
The working electrode for in situ XRD was
prepared by coating a slurry of RPLLTO and a polyvinylidene ﬂuoride
binder(PVDF) on a Be sheet. During the in situ XRD measurement, the
cells with the Be sheet electrode were subjected to a current density of
0.01 A·g−1 in the voltage range of 0.0–2.0 V versus Li/Li+. In situ XRD
patterns were obtained using the same procedure used for powder XRD
patterns.

the author.

[SOURCE: Two-dimensional Nafion nanoweb anion-shield for improved electrochemical performances of lithium–sulfur batteries.pdf]
================================================================================
Two-dimensional Naﬁon nanoweb anion-shield for
improved electrochemical performances of
lithium–sulfur batteries†
Joo Hyun Kim, Junghyun Choi, Jihoon Seo, Jiseok Kwon and Ungyu Paik*
We ﬁrst demonstrate a facile and eﬀective strategy to directly deposit
a Naﬁon nanoweb onto a sulfur cathode to address the challenges
underlying in Li–S batteries. The Naﬁon nanoweb not only blocks the
diﬀusion of polysulﬁdes by electrostatic repulsion but also allows fast
Li-ion exchange with the sulfur cathode through the selective
protection layer. The Naﬁon nanoweb-sulfur cathode retained an
excellent cycle performance over 200 cycles and delivered an
improved rate capability, as compared to a sulfur cathode and a Naﬁon
layer-sulfur cathode.
Lithium–sulfur (Li–S) batteries are one of the promising
candidates of next-generation batteries due to their various
advantages such as a high theoretical capacity of 1672 mA h g1,
environmental benignity, and cost-benets.1–4 Even though Li–S
batteries can address several drawbacks of Li-ion batteries,
some systemic challenges of Li–S batteries, associated with the
polysulde shuttling eﬀect and low coulombic eﬃciency, still
need to be overcome.
As an eﬀective approach to conne polysuldes within the
cathode side, the introduction of an interlayer in-between the
sulfur cathode and the separator has been intensively investi-
gated.5–9 The Naon polymer, as one of the promising interlayer
materials, has shown great eﬀectiveness in preventing the
diﬀusion of polysuldes from the cathode electrode.10–13 This is
attributed to the presence of SO3
 groups, which eﬃciently
bounce the negatively charged polysulde anions (Sn
2) by
electrostatic repulsion. Many studies have attempted to exploit
this electrostatic property by (a) forming a Naon layer on the
sulfur cathode12 or (b) modifying the pristine separator with the
Naon polymer.10,11 However, the dense Naon polymer layer
that is formed on the electrode or separator impedes the eﬃ-
cient permeation of Li ions toward the sulfur cathode, thereby
deteriorating the rate capability of the sulfur cathode.10–12
Herein, we rst demonstrate a facile and eﬀective strategy to
directly deposit a Naon nanoweb onto a sulfur cathode
(a Naon nanoweb-sulfur cathode) in order to eﬃciently
suppress the polysulde shuttling eﬀect and enable fast Li-ion
exchange with the sulfur cathode through the Naon selective
protection layer. The nanoweb structure is reported to have
a great advantage in terms of its electrolyte wettability, which
forms good ion migration channels and thus facilitates an
eﬃcient reaction between the active material and the electro-
lyte.14 The deposition of the Naon nanoweb was precisely
controlled to have the same loading mass (0.84 mg cm1) as
that of the Naon lm.11 Due to the synergistic contributions of
Naon as a negatively charged polymer and the structural
benets of the nanoweb, the polysulde shuttling was notice-
ably alleviated and the lithium ion permeability through the
Naon selective protection layer was dramatically improved,
which led to a superior capacity and stable cycling performance
at a high C-rate, as compared to those of a sulfur cathode and
a Naon layer-sulfur cathode.
Fig. 1a illustrates the process of directly depositing the
Naon nanoweb onto the sulfur cathode with the electro-spin-
ning method and Fig. 1b depicts the role of the Naon nanoweb
as an ion-selective blocking layer in the sulfur cathode part. The
Naon nanoweb was prepared by electro-spinning the solution
composed of a polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) polymer and a Naon
solution. PVP was used as a supporting polymer to sustain the
architecture of the Naon nanoweb. When lithium polysuldes
diﬀused out of the electrode during discharge, the Naon
nanoweb that was formed on the sulfur electrode bounced the
electrostatically negatively charged polysuldes by electrostatic
repulsion; this led to the eﬃcient suppression of the polysulde
shuttling eﬀect. The main purposes of using the Naon nano-
web are two-fold: (1) Naon, possessing SO3
 groups inside the
chain, rejects the transfer of negatively charged polysuldes by
Department of Energy Engineering, Hanyang University, Seoul, 133-791, Republic of
Korea. E-mail: upaik@hanyang.ac.kr

cathode; cross-sectional image of the Naon nanoweb-sulfur cathode; TEM
images of PVP nanobers and Naon/PVP nanobers; bar graph of average z
potentials; and the SEM image of the Naon layer-sulfur cathode. See DOI:

Coulomb repulsion and (2) the Nanoweb structure allows eﬃ-
cient electrolyte wettability, which is associated with the rate
capability, through the ion-selective blocking layer. These two
synergistic contributions eﬀectively alleviate the shuttling eﬀect
and facilitate fast electrochemical reaction between the sulfur
cathode and Li ions, which result in outstanding electro-
chemical performances of Li–S batteries.
Fig. 2a shows a photographic image of a sulfur cathode and
a Naon nanoweb-sulfur cathode. The entire electrode turned
white aer depositing the Naon nanoweb on the sulfur
cathode, demonstrating the large-area deposition of a uniform
Naon nanoweb on the electrode. Fig. 2b presents the sulfur
cathode, which is composed of sublimed sulfur, carbon black,
and a binder. The carbon particles were homogeneously
distributed throughout the electrode with no agglomerates.
Fig. 2c clearly shows that the Naon nanoweb, with a diameter
of 1–2 mm, was densely formed on the sulfur cathode. To
characterize the surface functionality of the Naon nanoweb,
Fourier-transform infrared spectrum (FT-IR) analysis was con-
ducted; the results are presented in Fig. 2d. The peak at around
1050 cm1 corresponds to the SO3
 groups present in the
polymer chain, which is clearly conrmed in both the Naon
layer and Naon nanoweb.15
To ascertain the inuence of Naon on the net electrostatic
charge in a polar solvent (DI water), the z potential was
measured and is plotted in Fig. S4.† Fig. S1a† presents a bar
graph of average z potentials for the PVP nanoweb, Naon layer,
and Naon nanoweb. The PVP nanoweb itself exhibited
a negligible electrical charge (near 0 mV) in the polar solvent,
whereas the Naon layer and Naon nanoweb exhibited nega-
tive charges of 20.9 mV and 16.5 mV, respectively. The above
results directly veried the inuence of Naon on the net
electrical charges and provided evidence that the electrically
negatively charged Naon can bounce polysulde anions by
electrostatic repulsion.11
Fig. 3 presents the electrochemical performances of the
sulfur cathode, the Naon layer-sulfur cathode, and the Naon
nanoweb-sulfur cathode. The Naon layer-sulfur cathode was
prepared as a comparison sample in order to conrm the
structural benets of the nanoweb for the electrochemical
performances. In Fig. S5,† the SEM image of the Naon layer-
sulfur cathode shows the dense and conformal coverage of the
Naon polymer over the whole area of the electrode. Fig. 3a
shows the schematics of the Naon layer-sulfur cathode and the
Naon nanoweb-sulfur cathode.
Fig. 1
(a) Schematic illustration showing deposition of the Naﬁon
nanoweb onto the sulfur cathode and (b) the role of the Naﬁon
nanoweb in the sulfur cathode during the discharge process.
Fig. 2
Characterization: (a) photograph of the sulfur cathode and the
Naﬁon nanoweb-sulfur cathode, SEM images of (b) the sulfur cathode
and (c) the Naﬁon nanoweb-sulfur cathode, and (d) the FT-IR spectra.
Fig. 3
Schematic illustrations and electrochemical performances of
a Naﬁon layer-sulfur cathode and a Naﬁon nanoweb-sulfur cathode.
(a) Schematics of each electrode, (b) initial discharge–charge curve, (c)
cycle performance and coulombic eﬃciency, (d) Nyquist plots, and (e)
rate capabilities (sulfur cathode, Naﬁon layer-sulfur cathode, and
Naﬁon nanoweb-sulfur cathode).
11204 | J. Mater. Chem. A, 2016, 4, 11203–11206
This journal is © The Royal Society of Chemistry 2016
Journal of Materials Chemistry A
Communication
Published on 23 June 2016. Downloaded by Gachon University on 1/28/2026 10:15:46 AM. 
View Article Online

The specic capacity of the sulfur cathode, the Naon layer-
sulfur cathode, and the Naon nanoweb-sulfur cathode at 0.5C
was 1210 mA g1, 1016 mA g1, and 1056 mA g1, respectively.
The initial specic capacities of the Naon layer-sulfur cathode
and the Naon nanoweb-sulfur cathode were lower than that of
the sulfur cathode; however, these results exhibited diﬀerent
properties in terms of the reversibility of the electrochemical
reaction. Comparing the peak potential values between the
lower plateau of the discharge and charge curves, the Naon
nanoweb-sulfur cathode showed the smallest gap, implying that
the Naon nanoweb can eﬃciently facilitate the reversible
electrochemical reaction (Fig. 3b).16
The cycling performance and coulombic eﬃciency of each
electrode were evaluated at a current density of 0.5C and are
presented in Fig. 3c. In the initial cycle, the capacity of the
sulfur cathode was relatively high, as compared with that of the
Naon layer-sulfur cathode and the Naon nanoweb-sulfur
cathode. However, the cycling retention of the sulfur cathode
showed a fast decay during the rst few cycles; this was due to
the severe loss of the active material and the poor coulombic
eﬃciency. Aer 200 cycles, the specic capacity reached 396
mA g1 with a retention of 32.7%. With the aid of each Naon
selective protection layer (a Naon layer and a Naon nanoweb),
the cycle performance and coulombic eﬃciency were dramati-
cally improved. In particular, the Naon nanoweb-sulfur
cathode delivered a higher capacity of 695 mA g1 with
a retention of 65.8% over 200 cycles and also a higher
coulombic eﬃciency of around 98% than the Naon layer-sulfur
cathode (582 mA g1, 54.3%). In terms of the cycling retention
aer electrochemical stabilization of the electrodes (aer three
cycles), the Naon layer-sulfur cathode showed a comparable
cycle retention performance but a lower capacity relative to the
Naon nanoweb-sulfur cathode (Fig. S7†). The two main eﬀects
(i.e., eﬃcient electrolyte permeability and Coulomb interaction)
of the Naon nanoweb synergistically facilitated better electro-
chemical reactions between the sulfur cathode and the elec-
trolyte, thus outperforming the Naon layer-sulfur cathode.
To clearly investigate the structural benets of the one-
dimensional nanoweb in terms of the rate capability, each
electrode was evaluated at diﬀerent current densities from 0.1C
to 2C (Fig. 3d). In an initial cycle of each electrode, the specic
capacities at 0.1C were almost identical. However, as the current
density was increased from 0.2C to 2C, each electrode exhibited
a diﬀerent rate capability. For the sulfur cathode, the specic
capacity abruptly decreased due to the harsh diﬀusion of poly-
suldes out of the sulfur cathode. The Naon layer-sulfur
cathode showed a better rate discharge capacity than the sulfur
cathode and a similar rate capability to the Naon nanoweb-
sulfur cathode at current densities from 0.1C to 0.2C; however,
the Naon layer-sulfur cathode showed an inferior rate capacity
from 0.5C to 2C because the dense Naon layer hindered rapid
Li-ion exchange with the sulfur cathode, thereby deteriorating
the rate capability of the electrode.
The structural benet of the nanoweb architecture, in terms
of surface reactions, can be further conrmed by carrying out
electrochemical
impedance
spectroscopy
(EIS)
studies
in
a frequency range of 100 kHz to 100 mHz with an amplitude of
10 mV (Fig. 3e). The ohmic resistances for the sulfur cathode,
the Naon layer-sulfur cathode, and the Naon nanoweb-sulfur
cathode were similar values: 1.27 U, 1.00 U, and 1.09 U,
respectively.
In
contrast,
the
charge
transfer
resistances
exhibited diﬀerent values. For the Naon layer-sulfur cathode,
the charge resistance (82.7 U) was higher than that of the sulfur
cathode (62.79 U) and the Naon nanoweb-sulfur cathode
(62.67 U). This can be due to the relatively poor electrolyte
wettability of the dense Naon layer relative to that of the
Naon nanoweb. In order to verify the structural benet of the
Naon nanoweb structure, the wettability properties were
conrmed by performing contact angle measurements of the
electrolyte on the surface of each electrode (Fig. S8†). The
contact angle of the electrolyte on the Naon layer was 15.6. In
contrast, the electrolyte spread out on the surface of the Naon
nanoweb without forming a droplet. This directly demonstrated
that the Nanoweb provides better wettability with the electrolyte
compared to the dense layer.
To visually observe the eﬀect that the Naon nanoweb has on
suppressing the migration of polysuldes, polysulde diﬀusion
tests were performed with a diﬀusion test bottle (Fig. 4). The
diﬀusion test bottle was composed of a polysulde solution,
separator, and fresh electrolyte (Fig. 4a). The polysulde solu-
tion was prepared by following the report of Dr Cui.17 Instead of
using a sulfur cathode electrode, three types of separators were
prepared and inserted into the bottle: (1) a pristine separator,
(2) a PVP nanoweb-deposited separator, and (3) a Naon
nanoweb-deposited separator. The deposition conditions are
the same as those used while preparing the Naon nanoweb-
sulfur cathode.
As shown in Fig. 4b, the three diﬀusion test bottles were
introduced into the fresh electrolyte to determine the diﬀusion
rate of polysuldes through each separator. For the pristine
separator, the polysuldes started to diﬀuse out of the bottle as
soon as the bottle was dipped into the fresh electrolyte; this was
Fig. 4
(a) Conﬁguration of a diﬀusion test bottle and (b) diﬀusion rate
tests with a pristine separator, a PVP nanoweb-deposited separator,
and a Naﬁon nanoweb-deposited separator.
This journal is © The Royal Society of Chemistry 2016
J. Mater. Chem. A, 2016, 4, 11203–11206 | 11205
Communication
Journal of Materials Chemistry A
Published on 23 June 2016. Downloaded by Gachon University on 1/28/2026 10:15:46 AM. 
View Article Online

due to the concentration gradient. In contrast, the polysuldes
slowly diﬀused out of the bottle with the PVP nanoweb-depos-
ited separator and the Naon nanoweb-deposited separator.
Aer one day, the three diﬀusion test bottles showed marked
diﬀerences in color. For the diﬀusion test bottle with the pris-
tine separator, the color of the fresh electrolyte changed to dark
brown, indicating the large migration of polysuldes out of the
separator. In contrast, the color of the fresh electrolyte became
light yellow when the Naon nanoweb-deposited separator was
applied, showing a signicant suppression of polysulde
diﬀusion out of the selective protection layer. The PVP nano-
web-deposited separator also appeared to block the migration
of polysuldes, but this eﬀect was much less than that of the
Naon nanoweb-deposited separator. These results directly
showed that the Naon nanoweb can inhibit the diﬀusion of
polysuldes in Li–S cells.
In summary, we rst demonstrated a facile and eﬃcient
methodology to deposit a Naon nanoweb onto a sulfur cathode
in order to diminish the polysulde shuttling eﬀect and
increase the coulombic eﬃciency. Compared to a sulfur
cathode, the Naon nanoweb-sulfur cathode retained more
stable
cycling
performances
over
200
cycles
with
high
coulombic eﬃciency and delivered an improved rate capability.
The excellent electrochemical performances of the Naon
nanoweb-sulfur cathode were attributed to the synergistic eﬀect
of the negatively charged Naon polymer and the structural
benets of the nanoweb architecture. Unlike the previous
concept of modifying the separator or the electrode with
a Naon solution, our proposed concept provides a new strategy
to address the inherent drawbacks of Li–S batteries with
a simple methodology. Additionally, this method can be further
extended to other types of functional polymers (conducting
polymer, hydrophilic polymers, etc.) or metal oxide-based
materials.

Uniform Li Deposition through the Graphene-Based Ion-Flux
Regulator for High-Rate Li Metal Batteries
Subi Yang, Junghwan Kim, Seungho Lee, Jihoon Seo, Junghyun Choi,* and Patrick Joohyun Kim*
Cite This: ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 2024, 16, 3416−3426
Read Online
ACCESS
Metrics & More
Article Recommendations
*
sı
Supporting Information
ABSTRACT: Lithium (Li) metal is considered an ultimate anode owing
to its high specific capacity and energy density. However, uncontrolled Li
dendrite growth and low Coulombic efficiency have limited the application
of Li metal. Among various strategies introduced to address these
limitations, the surface modification of polyolefin separators with
functional materials has been widely adopted for improving the mechanical
and thermal stabilities of polymer separators and to protect the separator
from the penetration of Li dendrites. Herein, we report a new functional
polymer separator that is surface-altered with a graphene-based Li-ion flux
regulator (GLR) to homogenize the Li-ion flux and suppress the growth of
sharp dendritic Li in Li metal batteries. The nanopores distributed through
the GLR structure serve as channels for ion transport and junctions for
electron transfer, facilitating efficient electrolyte penetration and rapid
charge transfer between graphene (Gr) sheets. Owing to these favorable features of porous GLR, a Li−Cu cell with the GLR surface-
altered polypropylene separator (GLR-PP) delivers excellent cycle and rate performances compared to a Li−Cu cell with a Gr
surface-altered polypropylene separator. In addition, among the tested cells, Li−sulfur cells with GLR-PP exhibit the most stable
cycle performance over 500 cycles. These results demonstrate that the concept of tailoring the surface of a polymer separator with
porous 2D materials is an effective strategy for improving the long-term cycle stability and electrochemical kinetics of Li metal-based
batteries and would trigger further relevant studies.
KEYWORDS: graphene, lithium-ion flux regulator, lithium metal battery, separator modification, lithium sulfur cell, Li deposition
1. INTRODUCTION
The demand for energy storage systems with high energy
density and long-term stability has increased as considerable
efforts are being devoted to achieving net-zero carbon dioxide
emissions. Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have attracted
significant attention from academic and industrial fields
owing to their satisfactory performances. Given that conven-
tional LIBs, which employ graphite as the anode and LiCoO2
as the cathode, have reached the upper limit of their energy
density, it has become increasingly challenging to meet
increasing demands for high-performance LIBs.1−3 Lithium
(Li) metal is considered a promising anode material owing to
its low density (0.534 g cm−3), low negative electrochemical
potential (−3.040 V vs SHE), and high theoretical specific
capacity (3860 mA h g−1).4,5 However, the practical use of Li
metal anodes is hindered by several issues, including low
Coulombic efficiency (CE) and safety concerns associated with
Li dendrite growth.6−8 Particularly, Li dendrites formed at high
current densities and low temperatures tend to exhibit long
and sharp morphologies, which substantially increase the risk
of separator puncture, resulting in thermal runaways.9−11
To tackle these intrinsic challenges associated with Li metal
anodes, two main approaches have been widely adopted. The
first approach involves the physical protection of the Li metal
anode from side reactions by either applying a stable
passivation layer to Li metal or introducing a ceramic-coated
separator in the battery.12−14 The second approach involves
the enhancement of the homogeneity of Li deposition by
either altering the surface property of the electrode or
regulating the Li-ion flux.15−17 It should be noted that the
dendritic Li growth is mostly initiated by nonhomogeneous Li
deposition. To achieve long-term cycling stability for Li metal
batteries (LMBs), it is crucial to regulate the Li-ion flux, which
is highly associated with the homogeneity of Li deposition,
during electrochemical reactions rather than simply blocking
the penetration of grown Li dendrites. A wide range of
strategies have been proposed with focus on the (i)
optimization of electrolyte with additives,18−20 (ii) design of
functional current collectors,21−23 and (iii) fabrication of
Received:
October 21, 2023
Revised:
December 22, 2023
Accepted:
December 28, 2023
Published: January 10, 2024
Research Article
www.acsami.org
© 2024 American Chemical Society
3416
https://doi.org/10.1021/acsami.3c15746
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 2024, 16, 3416−3426
Downloaded via GACHON UNIV on January 28, 2026 at 10:31:07 (UTC).
See https://pubs.acs.org/sharingguidelines for options on how to legitimately share published articles.

advanced separators.24−26 Among these strategies, the surface
modification of the polymer separator with functional materials
is the most attractive approach owing to its wide range of
coating material options and its applicability to the existing
separator production line.27 Therefore, several researchers have
coated polymer separators with various functional materials
(e.g., Al2O3, boehmite, and NbO2). Particularly, graphene
(Gr), which consists of two-dimensional (2D) layers of sp2-
hybridized carbon atoms, exhibits unique properties, such as
excellent electrical conductivity, thermal conductivity, mechan-
ical strength, and large specific surface areas.28 Accordingly, it
has been intensively explored as a separator coating material to
address dendritic Li growth and the volume change of the Li
metal anode during cycling.29,30 For example, Kim and Pol31
reported that the use of the Gr-coated polypropylene (PP)
separator significantly improved the cycling stability of LMBs
by reducing the actual local current density applied to the
counter electrode. However, Gr nanosheets are easily
agglomerated and restacked during the coating/drying process
owing to van der Waals forces. This results in a severe
reduction in the number of ion transport pathways, thus
deteriorating the electrochemical kinetics in LMBs.32,33
Herein, we present the design of a new functional polymer
separator whose surface was altered with a graphene-based Li-
ion flux regulator (GLR) to homogenize the Li-ion flux and
inhibit the growth of sharp dendritic Li in LMBs. To
investigate how the microstructure of the separator-coating
materials affects the homogeneity of the Li-ion flux and
electrochemical kinetics, both GLR and Gr were employed in
the fabrication of the functional separators. The results reveal
that the porous structure of GLR provides short ionic pathways
between the layers, thus significantly improving the Li-ion
diffusivity and homogenizing the Li-ion flux. Furthermore,
GLR contributes to more efficient electrochemical reactions
than Gr.34,35 Owing to the beneficial material characteristics of
GLR, a fabricated Li−Cu cell with a GLR surface-altered PP
separator (GLR-PP) exhibits exceptional electrochemical
performances even at high current densities. Moreover,
among tested Li−S cells, the Li−sulfur (Li−S) cell with a
GLR-PP demonstrates the most stable cycle performance over
500 cycles.
2. EXPERIMENTAL SECTION
2.1. Preparation of the Separator Surface-Altered with Gr
and GLR (Gr-PP and GLR-PP). First, a slurry for the separator was
prepared by mixing 0.5 g of Gr (or GLR), 1.2 g of deionized (DI)
water, and 0.926 g of a 10 wt % poly(acrylic acid) (PAA) binder in a
plastic cup, after which the mixture was dispersed using a planetary
mixer (AR100, Thinky). Subsequently, the slurry was casted on a PP
separator and dried in an oven at 50 °C for 12 h. Thereafter, the
resulting fully dried separator was punched into an 18 mm diameter
circular disc. The actual loading masses of Gr and GLR were 0.51 and
0.47 mg cm−2, respectively.
2.2. Fabrication of Li−Cu Cells and Li−S Full Cells.
Electrochemical tests for the PP, Gr-PP, and GLR-PP in Li−Cu cell
systems were conducted using a CR2032-type coin cell with a
diameter of 16 mm and 1.0 T Li metal foil and a diameter of 12 mm
and 20 μm thick Cu foil. A PP separator (Celgard 3501) was used in
the cell, and 1.0 M LiPF6 in EC:DEC with a volume ratio of 1:1 was
used as the electrolyte. Galvanostatic cycling was conducted at 1 mA h
cm−2, and the cutoff potential for Li dissolution was 1.0 V. To prepare
sulfur electrodes, 60 wt % sulfur powder, 30 wt % Super P, and a 10
wt % polyvinylidene fluoride (PVdF) binder were mixed in NMP
solvent using a mixer, after which the slurry was cast onto an Al foil.
Thereafter, the coated foil was dried in an oven at 50 °C for 24 h, after
which it was punched into a 12 mm-diameter circular electrode with
an areal mass loading of ∼1.5 mg cm−2. The Li−S full cell tests were
performed in a voltage range of 1.7−2.8 V with 1.0 M LiTFSI in
(DOL:DME) (1:1 by volume) without any additives. All the cells
were assembled in an argon-filled glovebox with O2 and H2O below
0.1 ppm.
2.3. Characterization. The surface images and post-mortem
images of PP, Gr-PP, and GLR-PP were observed using field emission
scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM; Hitachi SU8230) and field
emission transmission electron microscopy (FE-TEM; Titan G2
ChemiSTEM Cs Probe). Before observing the morphology of Li
nucleation and dendrite growth, the cells were disassembled in the
glovebox and then rinsed in DOL to remove residual Li salts. The
electrochemical performance test was conducted on a battery tester
(CT-4008Q-5 V100 mA-124, Neware). Electrochemical impedance
spectroscopy (EIS, VSP, Biologic, Seyssinet-Pariset) was performed in
the frequency range between 10 mHz and 200 kHz at an amplitude of
10 mV. The Nyquist plot for EIS was fitted using an equivalent circuit
inserted in Figures 4d,e and 7f. The Raman spectra of the separators
were measured using a Raman spectrometer (Renishaw inVia reflex),
and their Fourier transform infrared spectra were recorded on an FT-
IR spectrometer (IRXross, Shimadzu). The X-ray photoelectron
spectroscopy (XPS) profiles were obtained using an X-ray photo-
electron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher NEXSA). The pore distribu-
tions and surface area of Gr and GLR were examined using a pore size
and surface area analyzer (BET, Quadrasorb evo, Quantachrome).
The Li-ion diffusion coefficient was calculated using a galvanostatic
intermittent titration technique (GITT, WBCS3000L32, WonATech)
by applying a pulse current (0.2 mA cm−2) for 10 min, followed by a
relaxation time of 10 min in a voltage window of 0.001−1.0 V (vs Li/
Li+).
3. RESULTS AND DISCUSSION
Figure 1 illustrates the effects of the Gr- and GLR-modified
separators on the deposition of Li on a Cu current collector. As
Figure 1. Schematic illustrations of the Li deposition behavior through (a) Gr-PP and (b) GLR-PP.
ACS Applied Materials & Interfaces
www.acsami.org
Research Article
https://doi.org/10.1021/acsami.3c15746
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 2024, 16, 3416−3426
3417

Li-ions cannot cross the dense Gr sheets in a unidirectional
manner, Li-ions traverse a long diffusion path through the gaps
between Gr sheets to reach the Cu current collector (Figure
1a). Consequently, this phenomenon results in a nonuniform
Li-ion flux, leading to uneven Li deposition on the current
collector. After multiple Li deposition/dissolution process
cycles, sharp Li dendrites are formed from the unevenly
deposited Li layer. In contrast, Li-ions can migrate to the Cu
Figure 2. Characterization of Gr and GLR. (a) Thermal oxidation process for the production of GLR. SEM images of (b) Gr and (e) GLR. TEM
images of Gr at (c) low and (d) high magnification and GLR at (f) low and (g) high magnification. (h) N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms, (i)
pore size distribution, (j) Raman spectra, and (k) FT-IR spectra of Gr and GLR. XPS profiles of O 1s in (l) Gr and (m) GLR.
ACS Applied Materials & Interfaces
www.acsami.org
Research Article
https://doi.org/10.1021/acsami.3c15746
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 2024, 16, 3416−3426
3418

current collector in the GLR-PP through the holes present in
the GLR sheet (Figure 1b). Consequently, the travel distance
of Li-ions is significantly reduced, and a homogeneous Li-ion
flux is achieved owing to the short diffusion path within the
GLR layer. These advantages contributed to the improvement
in the ionic conductivity and uniform Li deposition over the
current collector.
The GLR powder was prepared via a one-step air oxidation
process (Figure 2a). To prepare the GLR powder, Gr powder
was thermally treated using an open-ended tube furnace at 430
°C for 20 h.36 The mechanism of GLR formation is divided
into two stages. The first stage is the hole formation due to the
gasification of defective carbon, which is known as the “first
transition threshold stage”. Subsequently, the “second
transition threshold stage” occurs as the oxidation time and
temperature gradually increase. In this stage, the size of the
formed holes increases owing to the gasification and oxidation
of graphitic carbons.37 To confirm the success of the thermal
oxidation process, the morphologies of Gr and GLR were
observed via SEM and TEM. Figure 2b shows the unique
planar structure of Gr: it exhibited a smooth surface without
pores (Figure 2c,d). In contrast, numerous pores were
distributed throughout the 2D-planar sheet of the GLR
(Figure 2e). In addition, similar to Gr, GLR preserves its 2D
structure. The pore size of GLR is larger than that of Gr
(Figure 2f,g), which is consistent with the plot of the pore size
distribution (Figure 2i). The specific surface areas of Gr and
GLR sheets were determined by using N2 adsorption and
Figure 3. Electrochemical evaluation of Li−Cu cells with different separators. (a) Cycling performances of Li−Cu cells at a current density of 4 mA
cm−2. The voltage profiles of Li−Cu cells with (b) PP, (c) Gr-PP, and (d) GLR-PP at 4 mA cm−2. (e) Cycling performance of Li−Cu cells at a
current density of 8 mA cm−2. The voltage profiles of Li−Cu cells with (f) PP, (g) Gr-PP, and (h) GLR-PP at 8 mA cm−2.
ACS Applied Materials & Interfaces
www.acsami.org
Research Article
https://doi.org/10.1021/acsami.3c15746
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 2024, 16, 3416−3426
3419

desorption isotherms (Figure 2h). The specific surface area of
GLR is 60.5 m2 g−1, which is larger than that of Gr (59.0 m2
g−1). To evaluate whether the thermal oxidation process
affected the crystallinity of the Gr structure, Raman spectros-
copy measurements were obtained. A defect (D) peak and a
graphite (G) peak were observed in the Raman spectra of Gr
and GLR at 1315 and 1580 cm−1, respectively (Figure 2j). The
D peak is associated with the structural defects or disorders in
the crystalline domains and the G peak is related to graphitic
carbon.38 The D/G ratios of each Gr and GLR are 0.596 and
Figure 4. Analysis of electrochemical kinetics. Contact angle measurement of a polar solvent on the surfaces of (a) Gr-PP and (b) GLR-PP. (c)
Schematic illustrations of separators after the application of the liquid electrolyte. Nyquist plots of Li−Cu cells with PP, Gr-PP, and GLR-PP: (d)
after lithiation and then delithiation and (e) after Li deposition and then Li dissolution at a current density of 1 mA cm−2. (f) GITT curves of Gr-
PP and GLR-PP. (g) IR drop values at different points in the GITT curve.
ACS Applied Materials & Interfaces
www.acsami.org
Research Article
https://doi.org/10.1021/acsami.3c15746
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 2024, 16, 3416−3426
3420

0.572, respectively, indicating that the formation of holes had
no significant effect on the overall crystallinity of Gr. In
contrast, the crystallinity of GLR is slightly improved owing to
the oxidation of the sp3 carbons in the defect region to CO and
CO2 during the thermal oxidation process.36 Figure 2k shows
the FT-IR spectra of the separators revealing some functional
groups in Gr and GLR. The intensities of the peaks assigned to
C−OH (namely, phenol) stretching (1070 cm−1), C−O
stretching (1377 cm−1), and C=O stretching (1650 cm−1)
slightly increased in the FT-IR spectrum of GLR.39,40 These
results indicate the formation of oxygen-containing groups in
GLR. To further investigate the chemical composition of Gr
and GLR, XPS analyses were conducted. Three peaks were
observed in the deconvoluted O 1s data at approximately
530.7, 532.0, and 533.4 eV, which were assigned to C=O, C−
O, and C−OH, respectively (Figure 2l,m).41 The higher
carbon oxidation state (the ratio of C=O to C−O) and
formation of phenolic groups (C−OH) can be confirmed in
Table S1. As the difference between Gr and GLR is the
formation of holes, these components are likely to be related to
the oxygen groups located at the edges of the holes in GLR.
These findings confirmed the contribution of the thermal
oxidation process to the increase in the number of oxygen
groups near the edges of pores, without compromising the
overall crystallinity of Gr.37,42,43
The electrochemical performances of Li−Cu cells with and
without surface-modified separators were investigated via a Li
deposition−dissolution process (Figure 3). The CE is defined
as the ratio of the amount of dissolved Li to that of the
deposited Li, which provides a measure for the electrochemical
stability of the Li deposition/dissolution process.44 The cycle
performances of Li−Cu cells were evaluated at a current
density of 4 mA cm−2 and an areal capacity of 1 mA h cm−2
(Figure 3a). The CE of the Li−Cu cell with PP was 76.9% in
the first cycle, and it rapidly decreased to 14.9% after 48 cycles
owing to short-circuiting caused by dendritic Li growth, which
resulted in dead Li and induced high internal resistance.45 The
Li−Cu cell with PP exhibits typical voltage profiles before and
after cycling (Figure 3b). The Li−Cu cell with Gr-PP exhibits a
high initial CE of 95.9% and maintains its CE above 80% for
80 cycles owing to the synergistic roles of the Gr layer.
However, an abrupt decay in the CE is observed after 80
cycles, and the Li−Cu cell with Gr-PP eventually short-
circuited after 160 cycles. Additionally, a lithiation region is
observed in the initial voltage profile of the Li−Cu cell with
Gr-PP, which is consistent with previous reports (Figure 3c).31
However, after 160 cycles, the lithiation region is no longer
observed, and the CE sharply decreased to <20%. In contrast,
the Li−Cu cell with GLR-PP exhibits an excellent CE over
98.1% for 200 cycles. It is important to note that the cell with
GLR-PP maintained a similar voltage profile even after 200
cycles, demonstrating that the use of GLR led to improved
electrochemical reliability (Figure 3d). To further evaluate the
cycling stability under extreme conditions, each cell was
evaluated at a high areal current density of 8 mA cm−2 and an
areal capacity of 1 mA h cm−2 (Figure 3e). The CE of the Li−
Cu cell with PP sharply decreases to 17.6% after 63 cycles,
whereas that of the Li−Cu cell with Gr-PP reduced to 12.9%
after 130 cycles. The Li−Cu cell with GLR-PP shows a high
average CE of 96.5% over 200 cycles. Even after 200 cycles, Li
deposition and dissolution are observed in the shape of the
voltage profile of the Li−Cu cell with GLR-PP, along with an
increased polarization gap (Figure 3h). In contrast, the Li−Cu
cells with PP and Gr-PP exhibit poor electrochemical
reversibility, with a steep decrease in the CE to <20% (at
the 50th and 130th cycles, respectively; Figure 3f,g). The
cycling performance of cells with different separators at low
current density is shown in Figure S1, which confirms the
functionality of GLR-PP. Compared to the Li−Cu cells with
PP and Gr-PP, the cell with GLR-PP exhibits the most stable
cycling performance over 200 cycles with a high CE of 100.1%.
This may be attributed to the facilitation of uniform Li
deposition by the GLR layer in GLR-PP even under high
current levels, contributing to improved cycling stability. In the
case of the Gr layer in the Gr-PP, the diffusion path is relatively
long as Li-ions can only traverse the Gr layer through the gaps.
Consequently, the Li-ion flux is relatively nonuniform, leading
to the accumulation of dendritic Li inside the Gr layer.
To compare the wettabilities of Gr-PP and GLR-PP with a
polar solvent, contact angle measurements were conducted.
Figure 4a,b presents a comparison of the wettability of each
separator against a polar solvent such as DI water. As soon as
DI water was dropped onto the surface of Gr-PP, it
immediately formed a large contact angle on the separator.
In contrast, a DI water droplet wetted on the GLR-PP surface
shows a small contact angle, indirectly indicating the improved
wettability of GLR-PP against polar solvents compared to that
of Gr-PP. Movies S1 and S2 show video clips confirming the
improved wettability of GLR-PP. A significant difference in the
electrolyte wettability of Gr-PP and GLR-PP can be observed
in Figure S2. The GLR-PP exhibits a significantly smaller
contact angle of 12° compared to Gr-PP (25°) when a liquid
electrolyte (1.0 M LiPF6 EC:DEC) is dropped onto the surface
of each separator, which is consistent with the results obtained
when DI water was used. The results of contact angle
measurements indicate that the holes present in GLR provide
sufficient diffusion pathways for Li-ions.36 This result can also
be attributed to the polar functional groups formed near the
holes owing to their hydrophilic nature. Figure 4c illustrates
the impact of Gr and GLR layers on electrolyte infiltration and
the associated Li diffusivity in the surface-modified separators.
To determine how the as-formed pores of GLR impact the Li-
ion diffusivity and electrochemical kinetics, EIS measurements
were performed. As shown in the Nyquist plots of the cells
after lithiation and subsequent delithiation between 0 and 2 V
at 1 mA cm−2, the Li−Cu cell with GLR-PP displays a lower
charge transfer resistance (Rct) of 41 Ω than the Li−Cu cell
with Gr-PP (60 Ω; Figure 4d). In addition, the Li−Cu cell
with GLR-PP shows a steeper slope in the Warburg region
than the cell with Gr-PP. This result is mainly attributed to the
improved electrolyte wettability of the GLR layer compared to
that of the Gr layer. Figure 4e shows the Nyquist plots of each
cell after Li deposition (that is, it proceeded until the area
capacity reached 1.0 mA h cm−2) and subsequent Li
dissolution (that is, it recharged to 1 V). The Li−Cu cell
with PP exhibits a Rct of 56 Ω, whereas the Li−Cu cell with Gr-
PP shows a relatively lower Rct of 34 Ω. This indicates that the
Gr layer significantly improves the interfacial electrochemical
reaction owing to the large surface area of Gr, enabling more
efficient electrolyte access. Particularly, the Li−Cu cell with
GLR-PP delivered the lowest Rct value owing to the combined
effects of the improved electrolyte wettability and homoge-
neous Li deposition.46,47
To assess the Li-ion diffusivity in Li−Cu cells with Gr-PP
and GLR-PP, an analysis based on the GITT was performed.
As presented in the GITT curves, the cell with GLR-PP
ACS Applied Materials & Interfaces
www.acsami.org
Research Article
https://doi.org/10.1021/acsami.3c15746
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 2024, 16, 3416−3426
3421

exhibits relatively smaller IR drops than the cell with Gr-PP
(Figure 4f). The specific values of each IR drop at different
points are presented in Figure 4g. The average IR drops of the
cells with Gr-PP and GLR-PP are 73.7 and 59.7 mV,
respectively. The Li-ion diffusion coefficient (DLi
+) of each
cell was determined using Fick’s second law based on the
following equation:

The average DLi
+ values of the cells with Gr-PP and GLR-PP
were 1.39 × 10−8 and 1.96 × 10−8 cm2 s−1, respectively,
indicating the higher Li-ion diffusivity of the cell with GLR-PP
than that with Gr-PP. Extrapolating from all these results, it
can be concluded that the cell interfacial resistance, electrolyte
uptake, and Li-ion diffusivity of cells can be controlled by the
suitable selection of separator-coating materials.
Figure 5a,b illustrates the morphologies of Gr-PP and GLR-
PP after Li deposition, with the red and blue boxes indicating
the positions at which the SEM images were obtained. To
reveal the substantial effect of GLR-PP on the homogenization
of the Li-ion flux, the morphologies of both Gr-PP and GLR-
PP were investigated after Li deposition, without the Li
dissolution process. The Li deposition process was conducted
at a current density of 4 mA cm−2 and an areal capacity of 2
mA h cm−2. The top-view SEM image of Gr-PP revealed the
presence of Li dendrites between the Gr sheets and on the
surface of the Gr layer (Figure 5c). Additionally, the cross-
sectional SEM image of Gr-PP revealed that Li dendrites were
densely packed between the gaps in the Gr layer (Figure 5e,g).
In contrast, substantially fewer Li dendrites were distributed on
the surface of GLR-PP (Figure 5d). It is interesting to note
that the shape of GLR sheets was analogous to that of a
pristine GLR sheet; however, the volume of GLR was slightly
higher owing to the intercalation of Li-ions into the sheet. The
morphology difference between Gr and GLR could be
attributed to the difference in their electrochemical reaction
efficiency. In the case of Gr-PP, many Li-ions could not
efficiently diffuse into the Gr layer owing to the relatively poor
electrolyte wettability of the Gr layer. Therefore, a large
portion of Li-ions was deposited both on the surface of Gr-PP
and within the Gr layer. In contrast, GLR facilitated efficient
and uniform Li-ion diffusion across the entire layer, leading to
a homogeneous Li deposition. As shown in Figure 5f,h,
substantially fewer Li dendrites are observed on the entire
GLR layer, compared to those on the Gr layer.
To directly compare the morphological features of the
deposited Li layers, the Li−Cu cells tested both with and
without surface-modified separators were disassembled after
cycling. Figure 6a,b shows the cross-sectional SEM images of
the Li layer deposited on Cu. The thickness of the deposited Li
Figure 5. Post-mortem analysis of the separator in the examined cell.

Cross-sectional view and top-view SEM images of (a and c) Gr-PP
and (b and d) GLR-PP. Schematics illustrating the morphologies of
Li-deposited Cu foils disassembled from the (e) Li−Cu cell with Gr-
PP and (f) Li−Cu cell with GLR-PP. The examined cells were
disassembled after cycling at a current density of 4 mA cm−2, with an
areal capacity of 1 mA h cm−2.
ACS Applied Materials & Interfaces
www.acsami.org
Research Article
https://doi.org/10.1021/acsami.3c15746
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 2024, 16, 3416−3426
3422

layer in the Li−Cu cell with Gr-PP was approximately 66 μm
(Figure 6a), whereas that of the Li−Cu cell with GLR-PP was
significantly thinner, with a thickness of 49 μm (Figure 6b). It
is hypothesized that the difference in the Li layer thickness
arose from the variation in the Li-ion flux through the
separators. A porous and thick Li layer is formed under
nonhomogeneous Li-ion flux. In contrast, a homogeneous Li-
ion flux contributes to the formation of a relatively uniform
and compact Li layer.50
To validate our hypothesis, the microstructure of the
deposited Li layer was observed by using SEM. Figure 6c,d
shows the morphology of the deposited Li layer. In the case of
the Li−Cu cell with Gr-PP, needle-like Li dendrites were
distributed within the entire Li layer, and a similar morphology
is also observed in the Li−Cu cell with PP (Figure S3). In
contrast, the deposited Li layer in the Li−Cu cell with GLR-PP
exhibits a significantly smoother and flatter morphology, which
was attributed to the uniform Li-ion flux enabled by GLR-PP
Figure 7. Evaluation of Li−S batteries. (a) Long-term cycling stability of Li−S cells with different separators at 0.5 C. Voltage profiles of Li−S cells
with (b) PP, (c) Gr-PP, and (d) GLR-PP. (e) Rate performance of Li−S cells with a variation in the current rates from 0.2 to 2 C. (f) Nyquist plots
of Li−S cells after one cycle of the activation process at 0.1 C. Voltage profiles of each Li−S cell with (g) PP, (h) Gr-PP, and (i) GLR-PP at various
current densities.
ACS Applied Materials & Interfaces
www.acsami.org
Research Article
https://doi.org/10.1021/acsami.3c15746
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 2024, 16, 3416−3426
3423

(Figure 6d). Figure 6e,f depicts the effect of variation in the Li-
ion flux on the deposition uniformity of the Li layer.
Within the Li−S battery system, the separator modified with
functional materials should possess the following properties:
(a) inhibition of the shuttling effect by physical or chemical
reaction with Li-polysulfides; (b) promotion of ion transport
through rapid ion transport pathways; and (c) sufficient
electrical conductivity to avoid a significant increase in internal
resistance. GLR-PP fulfills the above-mentioned requirements
due to the distinctive properties, such as porous 2D structures
affording cross-plane ion channels and abundant active sites.51
To demonstrate the practical application of GLR-PP, Li−S
cells were evaluated under various conditions. The cycling tests
of Li−S cells with PP, Gr-PP, and GLR-PP were performed at
a current density of 0.5 C within a potential window of 1.7−2.8
V (Figure 7a). For the Li−S cell with PP, the discharge
capacity in the first cycle is 787.0 mA h g−1, and this gradually
decreases to 202.5 mA h g−1 after 500 cycles. In contrast, the
Li−S cell with Gr-PP delivers a higher initial capacity of 993.2
mA h g−1 and retains a slightly improved capacity (271.7 mA h
g−1) compared with the cell with PP after 500 cycles. This
performance is attributed to the roles of the Gr layer as both an
additional current collector for reactivating inactive sulfur atom
and a blocking layer for soluble polysulfide species.52 The Li−S
cell with GLR-PP exhibits the highest capacity of 439.4 mA h
g−1 after long-term cycling. The significant improvement in the
cycle stability is attributed to the effective blocking of diffusing
polysulfide species by the compactly packed GLR layer.51
Figure 7b−d shows the voltage profiles of Li−S cells with
different separators. The change in the shape of the voltage
profile reflects the cycle stability of each cell. The Li−S cell
with GLR exhibits a relatively longer and flatter voltage plateau
than other cells after cycling, indicating its improved cycling
stability. Furthermore, a rate study for each of the Li−S cells
was conducted at various current densities ranging from 0.2 to
2 C (Figure 7e). The initial capacities of the Li−S cells with
Gr-PP and GLR-PP are approximately the same at 0.2 C.
However, as the current density increases to 2 C, the Li−S cell
with GLR-PP exhibits a capacity (734.7 mA h g−1) larger than
that of the cell with Gr-PP (668.8 mA h g−1). Particularly, the
cell with GLR-PP retains a capacity of 834.0 mA h g−1 when
the current density is reverted to 0.5 C, indicating its superior
electrochemical reversibility compared to those of the other
cells. These results are attributed to the conjecture that the
GLR layer promotes efficient Li-ion diffusion while suppressing
the diffusion of polysulfide species. The voltage profiles of each
cell at various current densities are plotted to illustrate the
trends in the polarization gap and capacity (Figure 7g−i). The
Li−S cell with GLR-PP displays the smallest polarization gap
and the highest capacity among the tested cells owing to the
excellent electrochemical kinetics and reversibility of Li−S cells
(Figure 7i). The overall impedance of each cell is shown in the
Nyquist plot (Figure 7f). The Li−S cell with GLR-PP exhibits
the smallest ohmic resistance (Rohm) and Rct compared to
those of other cells. This result indicates that GLR-PP
significantly reduced the interfacial impedance, thereby
improving the electrochemical reaction in Li−S cells.
4. CONCLUSIONS
In this study, we demonstrated a multifunctional separator
based on GLR-PP for enhancing Li-ion flux uniformity, thus
stabilizing the electrochemical performance of LMBs at high
current densities. When the GLR-PP separator was employed
in the Li−Cu cell, the cell not only maintained a high CE of
∼100% but also exhibited a significantly stable cycle perform-
ance at a high current density of 8 mA cm−2. In addition, the
Li−S cell with GLR-PP demonstrated excellent cycle stability
over 500 cycles. These excellent electrochemical performances
were attributed to two synergistic roles of GLR-PP: (a) the
short Li-ion diffusion distance in the cross-plane direction
within the separator-coating layer and (b) the enhanced
electrolyte wettability and homogeneous Li-ion flux enabled by
the porous structure of GLR. The use of nanoporous Gr to
modify the surface of conventional polymer separators
represents an effective strategy for improving electrochemical
kinetics and achieving uniform Li deposition/dissolution in Li
metal-based batteries. This study promotes further research to
advance the development of practical functional separators for
next-generation batteries, such as Li−S batteries.
